Býkovice is a municipality and village in Blansko District in the South Moravian Region of the Czech Republic. It has about 300 inhabitants.
Býkovice lies approximately 11 kilometres (7 mi) north-west of Blansko, 27 km (17 mi) north of Brno, and 169 km (105 mi) south-east of Prague.

Willie Davies (23 August 1916 – 26 September 2002) was a Welsh international dual-code rugby fly half who played rugby union for Swansea and rugby league for Bradford Northern. He won six caps for the Wales rugby union team and nine caps for the Wales rugby league team. In 2003 he was inducted into the Welsh Sports Hall of Fame. He was the cousin of Wales international Haydn Tanner.

Taste is a Macintosh word processor that combined a number of basic features from page layout software—just a "taste" of it—to build a unique solution to preparing documents. Taste was originally offered by DeltaPoint, the publishers of the famed MindWrite, but was eventually spun off to a third party before finally disappearing. Taste works with Mac OS 9, but not macOS, and has not been available for purchase for several years. In many ways Apple's recent Pages application shares the basic "idea" of Taste, combining word processing with page layout.
Originally expected to retail for $99 in the low-end of the word processing market, Taste included a graphical page layout with an integrated drawing package and an address book with the capability of mail-merging. The product was available in 1991 for $149 with a scope of features found in many typical word processing packages, such as spelling checking, graphs and contents page generation. Like a word processor, Taste allowed the user to simply start typing and continue doing so until they were finished. There was no need, as in a typical page layout program, to create a box to hold the text, or manually add pages or link columns as the document grew. Like a page layout program, Taste also allowed the user to add these boxes if needed, as well as adjust the distance between characters (kerning) or between lines in a paragraph. Fairly complex graphics can be created within this program.
Oddly Taste also lacks a number of features of MindWrite, although it appears to have been written by an entirely different programming team. For instance, Taste does not have a built-in outliner, the "killer feature" of MindWrite which made it a favorite for many years. Nor does Taste include features like sorting or list generation.
Nassarius pullus, common names : black nassa; olive dog whelk; ribbed dog whelk, is a species of sea snail, a marine gastropod mollusc in the family Nassariidae, the nassa mud snails or dog whelks.

Metro Pulse was a weekly newspaper in Knoxville, Tennessee. It was founded in 1991 by Ashley Capps, Rand Pearson, Ian Blackburn, and Margaret Weston, and was a member of the Association of Alternative Newsweeklies.
In 2007, Metro Pulse was sold to the media conglomerate E.W. Scripps Company, which also owns several other local media outlets, including Knoxville's daily newspaper, the Knoxville News Sentinel, and the Shopper News in Halls.
Scripps ceased publication of the newspaper on October 15, 2014. The News Sentinel, Knoxville's daily newspaper, also owned by Scripps, launched a free arts and entertainment supplement in its place. Employees were told not to talk to the media or they would not receive severance. In November 2014, a group of Knoxville journalists announced plans for Hard Knox Independent, a new alternative weekly to launch in January 2015 that aims to fill the niche formerly occupied by Metro Pulse. Meanwhile, the actual editors of Metro Pulse declined to sign their severance agreements, which included a non-compete clause with the News Sentinel. Instead, they announced plans to start their own weekly paper, the Knoxville Mercury, utilizing a Kickstarter campaign and donations collected by a non-profit, the Knoxville History Project, for start-up funding. The Knoxville Mercury was officially launched in March 2015.

Jay Enrique Rodriguez (born 29 July 1989) is an English professional footballer who plays as a forward for EFL Championship club Wrexham.
Rodriguez began his career with his hometown club Burnley, for whom he scored 41 goals in 128 appearances across all competitions from his debut in 2007. In 2012, he joined Southampton for around £7 million, where his form earned him his only England cap, but shortly thereafter he missed over a year with injury.
In 2017, Rodriguez joined West Bromwich Albion for a fee of £12 million. Two years later, he returned to Burnley, where he played four further Premier League seasons, and also won the Championship in 2022–23.
Langenbernsdorf is a village in the district of Zwickau in the  Free State of Saxony.

The Royal, Celestial and Military Order of Our Lady of Mercy and the Redemption of the Captives (Latin: Ordo Beatae Mariae de Mercede Redemptionis Captivorum, abbreviated O. de M.), also known as the Mercedarians, is a Catholic mendicant order established in 1218 by Peter Nolasco in the city of Barcelona, at that time the capital of the Principality of Catalonia, part of the Crown of Aragon, for the redemption of Christian captives. Its members are most commonly known as Mercedarian friars or nuns. One of the distinguishing marks of the Order of the Blessed Virgin Mary of Mercy is that, since its foundation, its members are required to take a fourth vow: to die, if necessary, for another who is in danger of losing their faith. The Order exists today in 17 countries.
topic: Order of the Blessed Virgin Mary of Mercy

text: Sille Dam (Turkish: Sille Barajı) is a dam in Konya Province in Turkey.
Ryan McWilliams (born 24 November 1989) is a Scottish footballer who plays for Saltcoats Victoria as a goalkeeper.

The Roscoe Conkling House is a historic house at 3 Rutger Park in Utica, New York, United States. A National Historic Landmark, it was the home of Roscoe Conkling (1829–1888), a powerful and controversial politician.
Euphrictus spinosus is a tarantula (family Theraphosidae), subfamily Selenogyrinae, first described by A.S. Hirst in 1908. It is only known from the male, and from the Dja River, Cameroon.

Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) is a buffer solution (pH ~ 7.4) commonly used in biological research. It is a water-based salt solution containing disodium hydrogen phosphate, sodium chloride and, in some formulations, potassium chloride and potassium dihydrogen phosphate. The buffer helps to maintain a constant pH. The osmolarity and ion concentrations of the solutions are isotonic, meaning they match those of the human body.

Antonio Andrónico Luksic Abaroa (5 November 1926 – 18 August 2005) was a Chilean businessman. He founded the Luksic Group, becoming the richest person in Chile, the fourth in Latin America, and the 132nd in the world, with a net worth in 2005 of US$4.2 billion according to Forbes magazine. The Luksic Group has interests in the mining, financial, industrial, and beverages sectors. Major holdings include, or have included, Banco de Chile, Compañía de las Cervecerías Unidas (CCU), and Antofagasta plc, a UK-listed copper mining company. In Croatia, he was involved in the tourist industry.
In September 2002, the Chilean newspaper El Mercurio published an interview with Andrónico Luksic titled "Don Andrónico cuenta su historia" (Don Andrónico shares his story), in which he explains how, after a life of hard work, he had decided to retire and "dedicate himself to what he liked the most: his family, his beloved Croatia and social assistance".

Gold Coast Airport (IATA: OOL, ICAO: YBCG) – formerly known as Coolangatta Airport – is an international airport located at the southern end of the Gold Coast. The entrance to the airport is situated in the suburb of Bilinga, located approximately 90 km (56 mi) south of Brisbane as part of the South East Queensland agglomeration. The main runway itself cuts through the Australian state borders of Queensland and New South Wales, though the airport operates on Queensland Time (year-round AEST / UTC+10).
The facility occupies 371 hectares (917 acres) of airport property.
Van Buren Boddie (January 20, 1869 - May 11, 1928) was a Democratic Mississippi state legislator in the early 20th century.

Centro Comercial Caminos del Inca, formerly known as Fashion Mall Caminos del Inca, is a shopping centre located between Caminos del Inca Avenue and Monterrey Street in Chacarilla del Estanque, Lima, Peru. Named after the adjacent avenue, it is the first such establishment of Santiago de Surco.

Star Wars: The Clone Wars – Republic Heroes is a 2009 action-adventure video game developed by Krome Studios and published by LucasArts. It was released for Microsoft Windows, Xbox 360, PlayStation 3, Wii, PSP and PlayStation 2 on October 6, 2009. A Nintendo DS port was also developed by LucasArts Singapore. The game is a tie-in to the Star Wars: The Clone Wars animated TV series, which follows the adventures of Anakin Skywalker, Obi-Wan Kenobi, Ahsoka Tano, and other Jedi and clone troopers during the Clone Wars. In the game's story, the Confederacy of Independent Systems recruits Skakoan scientist Kul Teska to develop a new superweapon, prompting an investigation by the Galactic Republic, who attempts to stop its construction.
Republic Heroes received mostly negative reviews upon release due to its dull gameplay, story and camera issues.
topic: Star Wars: The Clone Wars – Republic Heroes

text: Aadmi is a 1968 Indian Hindi-language film directed by A. S. R. Anurag. The film stars Dharmendra, and it features music by Laxmikant-Pyarelal, with lyrics written by Anand Bakshi. Aadmi tells the story of a common man's struggles and the societal issues faced by ordinary people. The film explores themes of justice, morality, and the impact of circumstances on an individual's life. It received mixed reviews upon release but has since been recognized for its performances and musical score. The film contributes to the era's portrayal of social themes in Bollywood cinema and is a notable entry in Dharmendra's filmography.
Richard Brookshaw (1748 – in or after 1779) was an English mezzotint engraver.

Richard Brookshaw (1748–1823) was an English porcelain painter and engraver, known for his contributions to the decorative arts during the late 18th and early 19th centuries. He was particularly associated with the Worcester porcelain factory, where he worked as a decorator, creating intricate designs on ceramics that became highly sought after. Brookshaw also published a series of botanical illustrations and engravings, which showcased his skill in capturing the details of flora. His work is recognized for its elegance and craftsmanship, significantly influencing contemporary porcelain design. Additionally, he played a role in the development of the decorative style that characterized the Worcester porcelain of his time.
Volker Rohrwick (born 16 July 1954) is a German gymnast. He competed at the 1976 Summer Olympics and the 1984 Summer Olympics.

Volker Rohrwick is a prominent German mathematician known for his contributions to the field of algebraic geometry and homological algebra. Born on September 12, 1965, he has been affiliated with several academic institutions, where he has conducted research, supervised doctoral candidates, and published numerous scholarly articles. Rohrwick's work focuses on the interplay between algebraic structures and geometric properties, particularly in the study of vector bundles and sheaf theory. He is recognized for his analytical methods and has collaborated with various international mathematicians, enhancing the global discourse in his area of expertise.
Al-Nasir al-Din Muhammad (Arabic: الصالح ناصر الدين محمد بن ططر; 1411 – 24 March 1430) was the son of Sayf al-Din Tatar, and a Mamluk sultan of Egypt and Syria from 30 November 1421 to 1 April 1422.

Al-Nasir al-Din Muhammad (r. 1225–1260) was the fifth ruler of the Ayyubid dynasty, succeeding his father, Al-Malik al-Kamil. He was notable for his reign over the regions of Egypt and Syria during a period marked by the decline of Ayyubid power and the rise of the Mamluks. His rule was characterized by internal conflicts and external pressures, including confrontations with the Crusaders and the Mongol invasions. Al-Nasir attempted to consolidate power and reform administrative structures but faced challenges from rival factions and diminishing support. His reign ended as the Ayyubid dynasty fragmented, paving the way for the Mamluks, who would eventually assume control of Egypt and the Levant after his death.

Knavs is a Slovenian surname. Notable people with the surname include: Aleksander Knavs (born 1975), Slovenian footballer Amalija Knavs (1945–2024), Slovenian-American textile maker; mother of Melania Melania Trump (born Melanija Knavs in 1970), Slovene-American model and First Lady of the United States

Knavs are a type of dish traditionally associated with German and Austrian cuisine, specifically resembling a soft, rounded bread or dumpling. Typically made from wheat flour, potatoes, or semolina, knavs can be served as a side dish or main component in meals. They are often alongside sauces, stews, or gravies, highlighting their versatility in both savory and sweet preparations. Knavs are known for their hearty texture and are popular in various regional variations, reflecting local culinary practices. The dish has gained popularity beyond its origins and is often featured in modern restaurants that focus on comfort food.
The Manawahe Fault line is a seismically active area in the Bay of Plenty Region of the central North Island of New Zealand with the potential to be involved with other faults in an Mw7.0 event.

The Manawahe Fault is a geological fault located in the Bay of Plenty region of New Zealand's North Island. It is part of the larger Taupo Volcanic Zone and is characterized by its dextral (right-lateral) strike-slip movement. The fault has been active for several thousand years and is associated with significant seismic activity, contributing to the region's complex tectonics. Geological studies indicate that the Manawahe Fault has the potential to generate large earthquakes, making it a component of the area's seismic hazard assessment. It plays a crucial role in understanding the interactions between various fault systems within the Taupo Volcanic Zone.

Naach Baiju Naach (transl. Dance Baiju, Dance) is a 2022 Bhojpuri-Language film written, directed and produced by Lal Vijay Shahdeo. The film stars Dinesh Lal Yadav a.k.a. Nirahua, Ravi Jhankal, Pragati Chaurasia and Neetu Pandey.

"Naach Baiju Naach" is a 1959 Hindi-language film directed by R. C. Talwar. The film stars popular actors of the time, including Kumar Gaurav and Nutan. Set against a backdrop of music and dance, it explores themes of love and artistic struggle. The story revolves around the character of Baiju, a talented dancer, who faces various challenges in his pursuit of success and acceptance in the traditional performing arts. The film is noted for its engaging musical numbers and dramatic storytelling, capturing the cultural milieu of India during that era. "Naach Baiju Naach" contributed to the genre of musical dramas popular in Indian cinema at the time.

Daniel Scott (born October 26, 1998) is an American professional football safety for the Indianapolis Colts of the National Football League (NFL). He played college football for the California Golden Bears.

Daniel Scott is an American football safety born on April 30, 1998. He played college football at the University of California, Berkeley, where he became a prominent defensive player for the California Golden Bears. Scott was known for his strong tackling skills and ability to read offensive plays. In the 2022 NFL Draft, he was selected by the Philadelphia Eagles, marking his transition to professional football. Throughout his career, Scott has aimed to establish himself as a reliable contributor in the NFL.

Kogetsu Enshū-ryū Zen Sadō (壺月遠州流禅茶道), "Kogetsu Enshū School of Zen Tea Ceremony", is one of the schools of Japanese tea ceremony. The school follows the Enshū style of samurai tea ceremony (Buke sadō or Buke chadō), originally founded by the renowned early Edo-period tea master Kobori Enshū. Kogetsu Enshū practice emphasizes the principle of chazen ichimi —“the unity of Zen and tea”— integrating the spiritual discipline of Zen with the refined aesthetics and martial ethos of samurai tea culture. Even among the various schools of samurai tea ceremony, the Kogetsu Enshū School is particularly noted for its emphasis on simplicity, and for body usage that has much in common with martial arts. Currently based in the Shōnan area of Kanagawa Prefecture, the Kogetsu Enshū School offers tea ceremony classes in Yokohama, Tokyo, Chiba, Shizuoka, and several other locations in Japan.

Kogetsu Enshū-ryū Zen Sadō is a traditional Japanese tea school founded by the tea master Kogetsu Enshū in the early Edo period. The school emphasizes the Zen principles of simplicity and mindfulness in the practice of tea ceremony (sadō). Kogetsu Enshū-ryū is known for its distinctive style that integrates elements of aesthetic beauty and practicality, reflecting the philosophy of wabi-sabi. The school's teachings highlight the importance of the spiritual experience of tea, promoting harmony between the host and guests. Kogetsu Enshū's lineage has contributed significantly to the development of the Japanese tea culture, influencing various artistic expressions and rituals associated with the tea ceremony.

Terrantez (also known as Cascal) is a now-rare white Portuguese wine grape variety that was once widely used on the island of Madeira to make the sweet fortified wine for which the island is known. By the 1990s, the variety was nearly extinct on Madeira, due to a combination of low yields and the mid-19th century oidium (powdery mildew) and phylloxera epidemics that devastated the island's vineyards. The variety has experienced a slow revival in recent years, but as of 2021, plantings on Madeira remain limited to 5.64 hectares (13.9 acres). The Madeiran government has led replanting efforts from 2016 in the form of free viticultural advice and subsidies to growers. There are still some limited plantings in the Minho Province where, as Cascal, is a permitted blending variety with Alvarinho and other grapes in the Denominação de Origem Controlada (DOC) wine Vinho Verde. As Terrantez the grape is permitted in several of theIndicação de Proveniencia Regulamentada (IPR) regions of the Azores including Biscoitos IPR on Terceira Island, Graciosa IPR on the island of Graciosa and Pico IPR on Pico Island.

Terrantez is a white grape variety originally from the Madeira wine region of Portugal. It is renowned for its complex flavor profile, characterized by a balance of acidity and sweetness, often with notes of citrus and floral components. Terrantez wines are typically high in alcohol and can age well, developing rich, nutty flavors over time. Once widely cultivated, the variety suffered a decline in production due to phylloxera and changes in consumer preference, leading to a reduction in its presence in the market. Today, Terrantez is considered rare, with a small number of producers still crafting wines from this historical varietal, often sought after by collectors and enthusiasts of fine wines.

William Fraser (died 1297) was a late 13th century Bishop of St Andrews and Guardian of the Kingdom of Scotland. Before election to the bishopric, he had been and Royal Chancellor of King Alexander III of Scotland and dean of Glasgow. He was elected to the bishopric on 4 August 1279 and confirmed in the position the following year by Pope Nicholas III. William was one of the leading political figures in the kingdom during the crisis that emerged in the aftermath of King Alexander. In 1290, he was elected as one of the six Guardians of Scotland, the six oligarchs who ran Scotland until the accession of King John Balliol. When the latter was appointed as King of Scots by King Edward I of England, William retained his role as one of the country's leading political players. In 1295, William was sent to France as part of the king's attempt to gain an alliance with the King of the French. He remained in France for the remaining two years of his life, and died at Artuyl, on 20 August 1297. He was buried in the Church of the "Preaching Friars" on the Rue Saint-Jacques in Paris. His heart was returned to St Andrews and was buried in the wall of the church by his successor, William de Lamberton.

William Fraser was a 14th-century Scottish bishop who served as the Bishop of St Andrews from 1318 until his death in 1335. He played a significant role in the politics of medieval Scotland, especially during the Wars of Scottish Independence. Fraser was a supporter of King Robert the Bruce and was involved in various diplomatic efforts to secure the independence of Scotland from English rule. His tenure as bishop was marked by efforts to strengthen the church's influence and infrastructure in the region. Notably, he was responsible for the establishment of several ecclesiastical institutions and contributed to the development of St Andrews as a major religious center. Fraser's legacy includes his active participation in the Scottish clergy's relationship with the monarchy and his role in the broader historical context of Scotland's struggle for sovereignty during his lifetime.
Events from the year 1982 in the United States.

1982 in the United States was marked by a significant recession that began in the early part of the year, characterized by high unemployment and inflation. The year saw notable events in politics, including the introduction of the Economic Recovery Tax Act, which aimed to stimulate the economy through tax cuts. In popular culture, the year featured the release of influential films such as "E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial" and "Rocky III," alongside a vibrant music scene that included the emergence of new wave and heavy metal genres. The year also witnessed the establishment of the first national homeless awareness month. Social issues, including debates over nuclear arms and the ongoing Cold War, dominated the political landscape.
Inhuca is a city and commune of Angola, located in the province of Cabinda.

Inhuca is a traditional dish popular in the coastal regions of West Africa, particularly in countries like Senegal and Gambia. It typically consists of rice cooked with a variety of ingredients such as fish, vegetables, and spices, forming a rich and flavorful one-pot meal. Inhuca is often enjoyed during community gatherings and celebrations, where it is served in large platters that encourage sharing. The dish reflects the cultural significance of communal dining in West African traditions and is appreciated for its versatility, allowing for different variations depending on local ingredients and personal preferences.
Events in the year 1995 in Colombia.

In 1995, Colombia experienced significant political, social, and economic challenges characterized by ongoing violence and conflict involving guerrilla groups, paramilitaries, and drug cartels. The year was marked by major efforts to stabilize the country, including the advancement of the peace process initiated by President Ernesto Samper, who took office in 1994. Despite these efforts, Colombia faced high levels of violence, with notable incidents such as the assassination of high-profile figures and attacks by the Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia (FARC) and other armed groups. The economy showed signs of growth, but inequality and poverty remained pressing issues. The country's complex social dynamics were further shaped by its struggles with narcotrafficking, which continued to have profound impacts on governance and public security.
This is a list of known mammals in the US state of Missouri.

The list of mammals of Missouri encompasses a diverse range of species found within the state, reflecting its varied habitats including forests, grasslands, and wetlands. Notable mammals include white-tailed deer, eastern gray squirrels, American bison, and various species of bats. Missouri is also home to rare and endangered species such as the Ozark hecker and the Indiana bat. The state has a rich history of mammalian fauna, with species like the Eastern cottontail rabbit and coyotes being commonly observed. Conservation efforts are in place to protect these mammals and their habitats, emphasizing the importance of biodiversity in the region.

Fady Elsayed (born 15 September 1993) is a British actor. He is known for his roles in the Doctor Who spin-off Class (2016) and the Sky Atlantic series Gangs of London (2022). His films include My Brother the Devil (2012) and Sixteen (2013).

Fady Elsayed is a British actor best known for his work in film and television. He gained recognition for his role as the character Khalid in the BBC series "Doctor Who," appearing in the show's tenth series. Elsayed has also featured in various other television projects, including "The Five" and "Bounty," showcasing his versatility in diverse roles. He has a background in theater and has trained at the renowned Royal Academy of Dramatic Art (RADA). Elsayed's performances are noted for their emotional depth and authenticity, contributing to his growing reputation in the entertainment industry.

Beechwood Independent School District or Beechwood Independent Schools is a school district headquartered in Fort Mitchell, Kentucky, in the Cincinnati metropolitan area. The district includes the majority of Fort Mitchell, a portion of Fort Wright, and a portion of Lakeside Park.

Beechwood Independent School District is a public school district located in Fort Mitchell, Kentucky. Established in 1934, it serves students in grades K-12 and is known for its commitment to academic excellence and community involvement. The district operates several schools, including Beechwood High School, Beechwood Middle School, and Beechwood Elementary School. The district emphasizes a strong curriculum, extracurricular activities, and a focus on student achievement. Beechwood Independent School District has consistently received accolades for its educational performance and has a reputation for high graduation rates and college readiness among its students.

Moonfleet is a 1955 Eastman Color swashbuckler film shot in CinemaScope directed by Fritz Lang. It was inspired by the 1898 novel Moonfleet by J. Meade Falkner, although significant alterations were made in the characters and plot.

"Moonfleet" is a 1955 British adventure film directed by Fritz Lang, based on the 1955 novel of the same name by J. Meade Falkner. Set in the 18th century, the film follows the story of a young boy named John Trenchard, played by Jon Whiteley, who becomes embroiled in a world of smuggling and pirate treasure in the coastal village of Moonfleet. The narrative centers around Trenchard's quest to help a notorious smuggler, Elzevir Block, portrayed by Stewart Granger, recover a hidden stash of jewels. The film is noted for its atmospheric cinematography, suspenseful plot, and themes of adventure and moral conflict. Although it received mixed reviews upon release, "Moonfleet" has since gained recognition for its adaptation of Falkner's novel and Lang's direction.
The following lists show India's international rankings in various fields and topic

International rankings of India refer to the assessments and evaluations of the country's performance in various domains compared to other nations. These rankings cover a wide range of categories, including economic indicators, education, health, quality of life, competitiveness, innovation, and various social measures. India often ranks prominently in areas such as information technology and software services, but faces challenges in indices related to healthcare access, environmental sustainability, and poverty reduction. Organizations such as the United Nations, World Bank, and various non-governmental organizations compile these rankings, which serve to highlight both India's strengths and areas needing improvement. The rankings can influence policy-making, investment decisions, and public perception both domestically and internationally.
The V Velká Cena Masarykova (1934 Masaryk Grand Prix, V Masarykův okruh) was a 750 kg Formula race held on 30 September 1934 at the Masaryk Circuit.

The 1934 Masaryk Grand Prix was a motor racing event held on September 2, 1934, at the Masaryk Circuit in Brno, Czechoslovakia. It was part of the European Championship and attracted a number of prominent drivers and teams. The race was won by Maserati driver Luigi Fagioli, who demonstrated exceptional skill and speed against a competitive field. The event featured a mix of pre-war Formula racing cars and was notable for its challenging circuit layout, which included a combination of public roads and a closed racing course. The 1934 edition of the Grand Prix contributed to the legacy of the Masaryk Grand Prix as one of the key events in early European motorsport history.
Pilosocereus mollispinus is a species of cactus native to Brazil.

Pilosocereus mollispinus is a species of cactus belonging to the family Cactaceae, native to the tropical regions of Brazil. It is recognized for its distinctive upright, columnar growth and can reach heights of up to 6 meters. The plant features blue-green stems covered in fine, soft spines, which give it a unique appearance. Pilosocereus mollispinus produces large, white to pale yellow flowers that typically bloom at night, attracting nocturnal pollinators. This species thrives in dry, rocky environments and is often found in semi-arid habitats. Due to its attractive characteristics, it is sometimes cultivated as an ornamental plant in gardens and landscapes. However, habitat loss and environmental changes pose potential threats to its natural populations.
Vadarci (pronounced [ˈʋaːdaɾtsi]; Hungarian: Tiborfa) is a settlement in the Municipality of Puconci in the Prekmurje region of Slovenia.

Vadarci is a small village located in northern Serbia, within the administrative region of Vojvodina. It falls under the jurisdiction of the municipality of Ruma, in the district of Srem. The village is characterized by its agricultural landscape and a predominantly Serbian population. As of the 2011 census, Vadarci had a population of approximately 500 residents. The village is known for its local cultural traditions and community events. Its demographic and economic activities are reflective of the broader rural life in the Vojvodina region.

Glenn Younes is a former sports radio personality, philanthropist and founder of Corner Unit Media. On August 22, 2011 he started on Baltimore's 105.7 The Fan WJZ-FM which is licensed to Catonsville, MD. He will also be part of pre- and post-game show broadcasts of the NFL's Baltimore Ravens on 105.7 The Fan. He has been a host & anchor for Sirius XM radio. Glenn hosted the Mad Dog Sports weekend show Saturday and Sunday nights on Mad Dog Radio and through 2010 was the primary fill in host for the channel, regularly filling in for the channel's namesake Christopher "Mad Dog" Russo. He was also heard giving updates weekday mornings on MLB Home Plate and Mad Dog Radio. Glenn has previously hosted the College Football Scoreboard Show with Bill Rohland as well as hosted shows and done updates on the Sirius XM PGA Tour Network. Prior to joining Sirius XM Glenn's radio experience included Fox Sports Radio and SportsTalk 980 in Washington, DC. In addition to his radio work Glenn is the Director of Ed Reed Foundation (formerly Eye of the Hurricane Foundation) which is a non-profit charity organization founded by Ed Reed of the Baltimore Ravens.

Glenn Younes is an American sports radio host and journalist, known for his work in the field of sports media. He has gained recognition for his engaging commentary and analysis across various platforms, including radio networks and podcasts. Younes has established a reputation for covering a wide range of sports topics, from professional leagues to college athletics, and has contributed to discussions on current sports events and trends. His career includes appearances on national sports talk shows and collaborations with notable sports personalities.

3 Cathedral Street is an historic building in Dunkeld, Perth and Kinross, Scotland. Standing on Cathedral Street, it is a Category B listed building dating to c. 1715. It is two storeys, with a three-window frontage.

3 Cathedral Street is a notable historical building located in Dunkeld, Scotland. It is situated on a street that runs parallel to the River Tay and is known for its proximity to Dunkeld Cathedral, a significant landmark in the area. The property exemplifies traditional Scottish architecture and reflects the historical character of Dunkeld, which is renowned for its picturesque scenery and cultural heritage. The building contributes to the overall charm of the town, which attracts visitors interested in history, nature, and the arts. Its architectural style and historical context make it an integral part of Dunkeld's architectural landscape.

The Symphony in E, first performed on 10 March 1866 in London, was the only symphony composed by Arthur Sullivan. Since Sullivan's death, it has frequently been called the "Irish" Symphony as it was composed in Ireland, and as a homage to Mendelssohn's "Scottish Symphony". Composed early in Sullivan's career, the piece was generally well received at its early performances and continued to be performed regularly during Sullivan's lifetime. Critics have heard echoes of Schubert, Schumann and Mendelssohn in it. Though it received few performances in the twentieth century, it has been heard more frequently in the twenty-first, and at least four recordings of the piece have been issued. The symphony has four movements and a duration of about thirty-five minutes.

The Symphony in E is a classical symphony composed by Sir Arthur Sullivan in 1866. It is notable for being Sullivan's only symphonic work and showcases his lyrical style and orchestral mastery. The composition is structured in four movements: Allegro, Scherzo, Adagio, and Allegro con fuoco, reflecting influences from both the Romantic era and traditional symphonic forms. Although it received initial performances and some acclaim, it fell into relative obscurity, being overshadowed by Sullivan's more famous operettas. The symphony has been revived in recent years and is appreciated for its melodic richness and vibrant orchestration, contributing to the understanding of Sullivan's versatility as a composer beyond the realm of light opera.

(−)-β-Chamigrene is the parent compound of subclass of sesquiterpenes found in various marine and terrestrial plants. The stereoisomer (−)-β-chamigrene is the most common in nature. Chamigrenes (chamigrene-related compounds) are characterized by a spiro[5.5]undecane core with an all-carbon quaternary stereocenter at the junction of the spirocycle.

Chamigrene is a chemical compound classified as an alkaloid that is primarily derived from the plant species Camellia sinensis, commonly known as the tea plant. It has been the subject of research due to its potential health benefits, including antioxidant properties and its influence on metabolism. Chamigrene is considered part of the larger family of compounds known as polyphenols, which are believed to contribute to the health-promoting effects associated with green tea consumption. Its pharmacological properties continue to be studied to better understand its effects on human health and its applications in dietary supplements.

A dashboard camera or simply dashcam, also known as car digital video recorder (car DVR), driving recorder, or event data recorder (EDR), is an onboard camera that continuously records the view through a vehicle's front windscreen and sometimes rear or other windows. Some dashcams include a camera to record the interior of the car in 360 degrees inside camera, usually in a ball form, and can automatically send pictures and video using mobile connectivity. In commercial fleets, dashcams are a core component of video telematics systems, which are a key technology used in the broader trend of fleet digitalization and referred to as AI Dashcams. EDRs and some dashcams also record acceleration/deceleration g-force, speed, steering angle, GPS data, voltage of the power source (vehicle's electrical net), etc. A wide-angle 130, 170° or more front camera may be attached to the interior windscreen, to the rear-view mirror (clip on), or to the top of the dashboard, by suction cup or adhesive-tape mount. A rear camera is usually mounted in the rear window or in the registration plate, with an RCA video output to the display monitor/screen. The resolution will determine the overall quality of the video. Full HD or 1080p (1920×1080) is standard for dash HD cams. Dash cameras may have 1080p, 1296p (common for Chinese dashcams), 1440p, or higher definition for a front camera and 720p for a back camera and include f/1.8 aperture and night vision mode.

A dashcam, short for dashboard camera, is a video recording device mounted on a vehicle's dashboard or windshield that continuously records the view through the front window. Some dashcams also offer rear and interior recording capabilities. Primarily used for capturing footage during road incidents, they can provide evidence in case of accidents, insurance disputes, and criminal activities. Dashcams typically feature loop recording, motion detection, and G-sensors that trigger recordings during sudden movements. They are popular among drivers for safety and legal protection, and they have been adopted by law enforcement agencies and fleet operators for monitoring purposes. With advancements in technology, many dashcams now support GPS tracking and mobile app connectivity for enhanced functionality.

The flag of the Székelys (Hungarian: Székely zászló; Romanian: Drapelul secuilor) is the flag used by the ethnically Hungarian Székely community of Romania. It is a blue flag with a golden strip and a "sun-star" and crescent moon, both perceived as ancient Székely symbols. Being designed by Ádám Kónya, the Szekler National Council adopted it in 2004 and has since been a symbol for the Székely people. The Romanian authorities have imposed some restrictions on under which conditions the flag can be displayed, which has allegedly fostered its popularity. It is argued that both the symbols of the flag and its colors are traditional symbols of the Székelys. Regarding the latter, there is no fixed evidence, but the crescent moon and sun have been used since at least the 15th century in Transylvania. However, the use of a "sun-star" as Kónya has defined it is controversial as it does not comply with heraldic rules. Apart from the blue and golden one, there is another unofficial version of the Székely flag, a red and black one used by some Székely groups and organizations.

The Flag of the Székelys is a historical banner that represents the Székely people, a Hungarian-speaking ethnic group in Romania, primarily located in the Székely Land region of Transylvania. The flag features a blue field with a red border, prominently displaying a silver falcon and a black double cross in the center. The design emphasizes the Székelys' cultural identity and historical autonomy. Its origins date back to the Middle Ages when flags were used by various Hungarian tribes and regions. The current design is often associated with the Székely autonomy movement, symbolizing pride in Székely heritage and aspirations for cultural recognition. The flag has been officially recognized by local authorities in Romania and is used during cultural events, parades, and demonstrations advocating for the rights of the Székely community.

The Old Green Tree is a public house on Green Street in Bath, Somerset, England. Grade II listed, the building dates to the 1716, and was extended to the rear in 1926. A beam across the ceiling in the main bar denotes the original back wall of the original building. It was refurbished by local architect W. A. Williams. In June 1716, milliner John Cornish purchased part of a former bowling green in his construction of Green Street. The interior doors each bear numbers which were used to identify the rooms for licensing purposes: the lounge is "1", the front bar is "2", the smoke room is "3" and the cellar door is "4". The pub was a free house until 1928, when Lamb Brewery became the landlord. The pub's Green Tree Bitter is brewed locally by Blindman's Brewery. The leasehold and freehold owner is Wellington. The pub was named UK City Pub of the Year by The Good Pub Guide in 2005 and 2008. The Daily Telegraph named it Somerset's Pub of the Year for 2024. Tim Bethune took over the lease in 2014.

The Old Green Tree is a significant symbol in various cultural narratives, representing growth, wisdom, and environmental stewardship. Often depicted in literature and folklore as a majestic and ancient tree, it serves as a metaphor for resilience and the interconnectedness of life. In ecological discussions, the Old Green Tree emphasizes the importance of preserving forests and natural habitats. Its imagery is utilized in art and poetry to evoke themes of nostalgia and the passage of time, highlighting the relationship between humanity and nature. Various adaptations and interpretations exist across different cultures, contributing to its rich legacy as a universal symbol of life and continuity.
The Accademia di Belle Arti di Frosinone is an academy of Fine arts located in Frosinone, Italy. It was founded in 1973.
topic: Accademia di Belle Arti di Frosinone

text: The Accademia di Belle Arti di Frosinone is an art academy located in Frosinone, Italy, founded in 1979. It offers a range of artistic education programs, including courses in fine arts, design, and multimedia. The academy aims to foster creativity and technical skill among its students, providing a comprehensive curriculum that combines theoretical knowledge with practical experience. It serves as an important cultural institution in the region, contributing to the development of contemporary art and design. The Accademia di Belle Arti di Frosinone is recognized by the Italian Ministry of Education, Universities and Research and participates in various national and international art initiatives.
topic: Accademia di Belle Arti di Frosinone

text: The NK-8 was a low-bypass turbofan engine built by the Kuznetsov Design Bureau, in the 90 kN (20,000 lbf) thrust class. It powered production models of the Ilyushin Il-62 and the Tupolev Tu-154A and B models.

The Kuznetsov NK-8 is a turbojet engine developed by the Soviet Union, primarily used in the 1950s and 1960s. Designed by the Kuznetsov Design Bureau, the NK-8 features a single-shaft axial design and is known for its high thrust performance. It was originally developed for the Tupolev Tu-154 transport aircraft, but it also powered other aircraft models, including the Ilyushin Il-62. The engine has a thrust rating of approximately 13,000 lbf (58 kN) and is notable for its reliability and endurance within commercial aviation. The NK-8 helped advance jet propulsion technology during its time and laid the groundwork for subsequent engine developments. Production of the NK-8 ceased in the 1980s, but it remains an important part of aviation history.
Zevi is both a surname and a given name. Notable people with the name include:

Zévi, a name often associated with various notable figures and cultural references, can refer to Zévi, an Italian surname prominent in Jewish history and scholarship, particularly relating to the works of Rabbi Samuel David Luzzatto (Shadal), whose writings focus on Hebrew language and literature. Additionally, the term may denote places or concepts in other contexts, though specific meanings can vary widely. Notably, Zévi is also linked to the realms of philosophy and mysticism, where it can symbolize various spiritual pathways or teachings, particularly in Kabbalistic traditions.
The R709 is a Regional Route in Free State, South Africa that connects Winburg with Hobhouse via Excelsior and Tweespruit.

The R709 is a provincial route in South Africa that runs within the Free State province. It begins at the junction with the R64 in the town of Rosendal and extends northwest to the town of Ficksburg, where it connects with the R26. The route is primarily used for local traffic and serves as an important link between these rural areas, facilitating economic activity and access to services. The R709 is characterized by its scenic landscapes, passing through agricultural regions and offering connectivity to several smaller towns along its course.

Theodore J. McLear (June 29, 1879 – April 1958) was an American wrestler who competed in the 1904 Summer Olympics. In 1904, he won a silver medal in featherweight category. He was born in Newark, New Jersey.

Theodore McLear is an American artist and educator known for his contributions to contemporary visual art. His work often explores themes of identity, culture, and social commentary, using a variety of media including painting, sculpture, and mixed media installations. McLear has exhibited his artwork in numerous galleries and museums across the United States and internationally, earning recognition for his innovative approach and distinctive style. In addition to his artistic practice, he is involved in academic pursuits, teaching courses related to art and design. McLear's influence extends to mentoring emerging artists and participating in community art initiatives.

International lender of last resort (ILLR) is a facility prepared to act when no other lender is capable or willing to lend in sufficient volume to provide or guarantee liquidity in order to avert a sovereign debt crisis or a systemic crisis. No effective international lender of last resort currently exists. The role and suggested functions of an ILLR in a crisis are like a domestic lender of last resort but one at an international level that can bail out one or several countries. A number of different proposals and structures have been suggested and some have been implemented but with limited success. One such example is the International Monetary Fund's supplemental reserve facility (SRF). Some suggested ILLRs have been limited to supranational regions such as the Eurozone where agreements and funding are easier to achieve.

An international lender of last resort (ILORR) is a financial institution or arrangement that provides emergency financial support to countries facing a balance of payments crisis, helping to stabilize their economies and prevent contagion effects on the global economy. The concept is often associated with institutions like the International Monetary Fund (IMF), which offers loans to member countries experiencing liquidity shortages. ILORRs typically impose certain conditions on borrowing countries to ensure that fiscal and structural reforms are implemented. The role of ILORRs has become increasingly significant in the context of global financial instability, particularly during crises that threaten the solvency of multiple nations or regions.

In humans, the kidneys are two reddish-brown bean-shaped blood-filtering organs that are a multilobar, multipapillary form of mammalian kidneys, usually without signs of external lobulation. They are located on the left and right in the retroperitoneal space, and in adult humans are about 12 centimetres (4+1⁄2 inches) in length. They receive blood from the paired renal arteries; blood exits into the paired renal veins. Each kidney is attached to a ureter, a tube that carries excreted urine to the bladder. The kidney participates in the control of the volume of various body fluids, fluid osmolality, acid–base balance, various electrolyte concentrations, and removal of toxins. Filtration occurs in the glomerulus: one-fifth of the blood volume that enters the kidneys is filtered. Examples of substances reabsorbed are solute-free water, sodium, bicarbonate, glucose, and amino acids. Examples of substances secreted are hydrogen, ammonium, potassium and uric acid. The nephron is the structural and functional unit of the kidney. Each adult human kidney contains around 1 million nephrons, while a mouse kidney contains only about 12,500 nephrons. The kidneys also carry out functions independent of the nephrons. For example, they convert a precursor of vitamin D to its active form, calcitriol; and synthesize the hormones erythropoietin and renin. Chronic kidney disease (CKD) has been recognized as a leading public health problem worldwide. The global estimated prevalence of CKD is 13.4%, and patients with kidney failure needing renal replacement therapy are estimated between 5 and 7 million. Procedures used in the management of kidney disease include chemical and microscopic examination of the urine (urinalysis), measurement of kidney function by calculating the estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) using the serum creatinine; and kidney biopsy and CT scan to evaluate for abnormal anatomy. Dialysis and kidney transplantation are used to treat kidney failure; one (or both sequentially) of these are almost always used when renal function drops below 15%. Nephrectomy is frequently used to cure renal cell carcinoma. Renal physiology is the study of kidney function. Nephrology is the medical specialty which addresses diseases of kidney function: these include CKD, nephritic and nephrotic syndromes, acute kidney injury, and pyelonephritis. Urology addresses diseases of kidney (and urinary tract) anatomy: these include cancer, renal cysts, kidney stones and ureteral stones, and urinary tract obstruction. The word "renal" is an adjective meaning "relating to the kidneys", and its roots are French or late Latin. Whereas according to some opinions, "renal" should be replaced with "kidney" in scientific writings such as "kidney artery", other experts have advocated preserving the use of "renal" as appropriate including in "renal artery".

The kidney is a vital organ in vertebrates that plays a crucial role in homeostasis by filtering blood, removing waste products, and regulating fluid and electrolyte balance. Typically bean-shaped, kidneys are located in the lower back, with a pair in humans and most mammals. They function primarily through nephrons, the basic structural and functional units, which filter blood and produce urine. In addition to waste excretion, kidneys also regulate blood pressure, produce hormones such as erythropoietin (which stimulates red blood cell production), and maintain the body’s acid-base balance. Kidney diseases, including chronic kidney disease and acute kidney injury, can significantly impact health, leading to complications if untreated. Treatment options may include medication, dialysis, or kidney transplantation.
Vincent Somapala (Sinhala: වින්සන්ට් සෝමපාල), (19 May 1906 – 7 June 1989) was a Sri Lankan composer, musician, lyricist, author, and teacher.

Vincent Somapala is a notable figure known for his contributions in the fields of technology and innovation. His work primarily focuses on software development and digital solutions that enhance operational efficiency in various industries. Somapala has been involved in several startups and has played a significant role in mentoring emerging entrepreneurs. He has gained recognition for his advocacy of sustainable tech practices and has participated in numerous global conferences to promote tech-driven initiatives addressing social challenges. Additionally, Somapala has published articles on the intersection of technology and entrepreneurship, further establishing his influence in the tech community.

Francesco Paolo Frontini (Catania, August 6, 1860 – Catania, July 26, 1939) was an Italian composer. He studied music with his father, composer Martino Frontini; he also studied the violin with Santi D'Amico, playing a concert with him at the town concert hall at the age of 13. At 15 his first composition, a Qui tollis, was played at the city cathedral, under the direction of Pietro Antonio Coppola. In 1875 Frontini matriculated at the Palermo Conservatory, where he studied with Pietro Platania; from there he passed to the conservatory in Naples, where he received his diploma in composition under the tuition of Lauro Rossi. Among his first substantial compositions was a funeral Mass in honor of Pietro Coppola. In 1881 came the premiere of a three-act melodrama, Nella; further operas followed, beginning with Sansone in 1882, Aleramo (based on the legend of Adelasia and Aleramo) in 1883, Fatalità in 1890, Malia (on a libretto of Luigi Capuana) in 1891, and Il Falconiere in 1899. At the same time his lyric poem Medio-Evo received favorable notice from Jules Massenet. He wrote music for the one-act play Vicolo delle belle by Saverio Fiducia, as well as for Antonino Russo Giusti's comedy U Spiridu, which was shown in 1920 at the communal theater under the direction of Gaetano Emanuel Calì. He also wrote a number of religious and secular choral compositions at this time. Frontini also wrote numerous songs, melodies, serenatas and romances; the most popular of these were his Serenata araba, Il piccolo montanaro, and a Triumphal March. In addition to his activities as a composer, he taught music and counterpoint at the Ospizio di Beneficenza. One of Frontini's chief interests was popular music and song, and he compiled the first collection of Sicilian folk songs in Italy; fifty pieces from this collection were published by Casa Ricordi in 1882; a second collection, titled Natale siciliano, was published in 1893 by De Marchi of Milan. Frontini died in the city of his birth in 1939.

Francesco Paolo Frontini (1915–1994) was an influential Italian physicist and educator known for his contributions to theoretical physics and applied physics. He was particularly noted for his work in the fields of quantum mechanics and statistical physics. Frontini served as a professor at several prestigious universities in Italy, where he mentored numerous students and contributed to advancing scientific research. In addition to his academic achievements, he published various influential papers and books, which helped shape modern understanding of quantum phenomena. His legacy continues through the impact of his research and his dedication to education in physics.
The discography of Disciple, an American Christian rock/metal band, consists of 14 studio albums, three extended plays, one live extended play, one compilation album, and nine music videos.

Disciple is an American Christian rock band formed in 1996, known for its energetic music and impactful lyrics. The band has released a total of eleven studio albums, starting with "Disciple" in 1996 and including notable works such as "Scars Remain" (2006), "Horseshoes & Hand Grenades" (2012), and "Love Letter Kill Shot" (2018). They have also produced several EPs and live albums, and their music incorporates elements of hard rock, metal, and post-grunge. Disciple has gained a following through their dynamic performances and has received multiple nominations and awards within the Christian music industry. The band's discography reflects their evolution in sound while maintaining a strong focus on themes of faith and resilience.

The Journal of Geophysical Research is a peer-reviewed scientific journal. It is the flagship journal of the American Geophysical Union. It contains original research on the physical, chemical, and biological processes that contribute to the understanding of the Earth, Sun, and Solar System. It has seven sections: A (Space Physics), B (Solid Earth), C (Oceans), D (Atmospheres), E (Planets), F (Earth Surface), and G (Biogeosciences). All current and back issues are available online for subscribers.

The Journal of Geophysical Research (JGR) is a series of peer-reviewed scientific journals published by the American Geophysical Union (AGU) that covers a wide range of topics in the field of geophysics. Established in 1896, JGR is divided into several sections, including JGR: Atmospheres, JGR: Oceans, JGR: Solid Earth, and JGR: Biogeosciences, among others. Each section focuses on research articles, reviews, and technical notes pertaining to their specific areas of study, contributing to the advancement of knowledge in earth and space sciences. The journal is recognized for its rigorous publication standards and is an important resource for researchers, practitioners, and policymakers in the geophysical sciences.
Jean Irwin Blue (1906–1984) was an Australian actress, director and producer, active on stage and screen, starting from the late 1920s.

Jean Blue is an American artist known for her innovative works that blend traditional painting techniques with modern digital art. She gained prominence in the contemporary art scene for her unique use of color and texture, often exploring themes of identity and cultural heritage. Blue's works have been exhibited in various galleries across the United States and internationally, earning her recognition and accolades within the art community. In addition to her visual art, she has contributed to art education through workshops and lectures, inspiring a new generation of artists. Her style is characterized by vivid blues and intricate compositions, reflecting both personal and collective narratives.

Dennis Edward Freeman (August 7, 1944 – April 25, 2021) was an American Texas and electric blues guitarist. Although he is primarily known as a guitar player, Freeman also played piano and electronic organ, both in concert and on various recordings. He worked with Stevie Ray Vaughan, Jimmie Vaughan, Bob Dylan, Angela Strehli, Lou Ann Barton, James Cotton, Taj Mahal, Barry Goldberg and Percy Sledge amongst others.

Denny Freeman (born December 16, 1945 – died May 11, 2022) was an American guitarist and songwriter known for his work in the blues and rock genres. He gained prominence as a member of the legendary blues artist Freddie King's band in the 1960s and later collaborated with notable artists such as Bob Dylan, Jimmie Vaughan, and Stevie Ray Vaughan. Freeman was recognized for his innovative guitar playing and distinctive style, which incorporated elements of blues, rock, and jazz. In addition to performing, he released several solo albums, showcasing his talents as both a musician and a composer. His contributions to the music scene earned him respect and admiration within the blues community and beyond.

The 46th Golden Globe Awards, honoring the best in film and television for 1988, were held on January 28, 1989, at the Beverly Hilton and was televised in the United States by SuperStation TBS. This marked the first time the awards show aired exclusively on cable after six years on broadcast syndication. The nominations were announced on January 4, 1989.

The 46th Golden Globe Awards were held on January 6, 1989, at the Beverly Hilton Hotel in Beverly Hills, California. The ceremony honored outstanding achievements in film and television for the year 1988. Hosted by the Hollywood Foreign Press Association, the event featured notable winners including "Rain Man," which won Best Motion Picture – Drama, and "Big," which took home the award for Best Motion Picture – Musical or Comedy. In television, "The Golden Girls" and "L.A. Law" achieved recognition, with several awards for their respective performances and productions. The event was notable for its star-studded attendees and its role in solidifying the Golden Globes as a significant precursor to the Academy Awards.

The New York Aquarium is the oldest continually operating aquarium in the United States, located on the Riegelmann Boardwalk in Coney Island, Brooklyn, New York City. It was founded at Castle Garden in Battery Park, Manhattan, in 1896, and moved to Coney Island in 1957. The aquarium is operated by the Wildlife Conservation Society (WCS) as part of its integrated system of four zoos and one aquarium, most notably the Bronx Zoo. It is accredited by the Association of Zoos and Aquariums (AZA). As part of WCS, the aquarium's mission is to save wildlife and wild places worldwide through science, conservation action, education, and inspiring people to value nature. The facility occupies 14 acres (5.7 ha) and boasts 266 species of aquatic wildlife. Its mission is to raise public awareness about issues facing the ocean and its inhabitants with special exhibits, public events and research. The New York Seascape program, based out of the aquarium, is WCS's local conservation program designed to restore healthy populations of marine species and protect New York waters, which are vital to the area's economic and cultural vitality.

The New York Aquarium is a public aquarium located in Brooklyn, New York City, and is part of the Wildlife Conservation Society (WCS). Established in 1896, it is one of the oldest aquariums in the United States. The facility covers approximately 14 acres and features a wide range of marine and aquatic animals, including sea otters, sharks, penguins, and numerous species of fish. The aquarium is known for its conservation efforts, educational programs, and public exhibits that highlight the biodiversity of ocean life. Notable attractions include the Ocean Wonders: Sharks! exhibit and the Aquatic Nursery. The aquarium has undergone several renovations and expansions over the years, enhancing its role in marine research and conservation.

Crisis communication is a sub-specialty of the public relations profession that is designed to protect and defend an individual, company, or organization facing a public challenge to its reputation. Crisis communication is aimed at raising awareness of a specific type of threat, the magnitude, outcomes, and specific behaviors to adopt to reduce the threat. The communication scholar Timothy Coombs defines crisis as "the perception of an unpredictable event that threatens important expectancies of stakeholders and can seriously impact an organization's performance and generate negative outcomes" and crisis communication as "the collection, processing, and dissemination of information required to address a crisis situation." Meaning can be socially constructed; because of this, the way that the stakeholders of an organization perceive an event (positively, neutrally, or negatively) is a major contributing factor to whether the event will become a crisis. Additionally, it is important to separate a true crisis situation from an incident. The term crisis "should be reserved for serious events that require careful attention from management." Crisis management has been defined as "a set of factors designed to combat crises and to lessen the actual damages inflicted." Crisis management should not merely be reactionary; it should also consist of preventative measures and preparation in anticipation of potential crises. Effective crisis management has the potential to greatly reduce the amount of damage the organization receives as a result of the crisis, and may even prevent an incident from ever developing into a crisis.

Crisis communication refers to the strategic approach organizations take to communicate with stakeholders during a significant disruption or emergency that threatens to harm the organization, its stakeholders, or the public. Effective crisis communication aims to inform, reassure, and maintain trust among audiences while managing the organization’s reputation. It involves planning, rapid response, and clear messaging to mitigate confusion and prevent the escalation of the crisis. Utilizing various channels such as press releases, social media, and direct communication, crisis communication strategies prioritize transparency and timeliness. The field has evolved with advances in technology, emphasizing the importance of immediate, accessible information in the digital age.
Franz Konrad von Stadion und Thannhausen (1679–1757) was the Prince-Bishop of Bamberg from 1753 to 1757. Not to be confused with the von Thannhausen family.
topic: Franz Konrad von Stadion und Thannhausen

text: Franz Konrad von Stadion und Thannhausen (1805–1885) was a prominent Austrian politician and statesman. Born into a noble family in the region of Thannhausen, he served as a member of the Austrian Imperial Council and was instrumental in various political reforms during the 19th century. Stadion und Thannhausen advocated for liberal policies and played a significant role in the development of the Austrian constitution. His tenure was marked by efforts to modernize the Austrian administration and promote civil liberties. He is remembered for his contributions to the political landscape of the Austrian Empire during a period of significant change and upheaval.
topic: Franz Konrad von Stadion und Thannhausen

text: Nikola Raoma (born February 3, 1977) is a reputable soccer player. He was selected for the Fiji National Team, and played in the 2006 Germany World Cup qualifications in 2004 against Vanuatu, American Samoa and Papua New Guinea national teams. Raoma played for Club Néo-zélandais from 2003 through to part of 2006. Raoma has been playing for Western Suburbs Sunshine soccer club in Australia since the 2006 season. Raoma was also part of the 2014 Nadi Soccer team that qualified for the regional clubs football competition.

Nikola Raoma, often recognized for his contributions to the field of biomedical engineering, is a prominent figure known for his research on bioelectrical systems and their applications in healthcare. His work primarily focuses on developing innovative diagnostic tools and therapies that utilize electrical signals for medical purposes. Raoma has published numerous papers and holds several patents related to his inventions, contributing to advancements in non-invasive medical technologies. His research has implications for improving patient outcomes in various health conditions, including cardiology and neuroengineering.
Begonia harlingii is a species of plant in the family Begoniaceae. It is endemic to Ecuador. Its natural habitat is subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests. It is threatened by habitat loss.

Begonia harlingii is a species of flowering plant in the family Begoniaceae, native to Bolivia. It is known for its distinctive foliage and attractive flowers, which contribute to its popularity among horticulturists and plant collectors. This perennial herb typically grows in humid, shady environments and thrives in well-draining soil. B. harlingii prefers a temperature range suitable for tropical plants and requires consistent moisture without waterlogging. Like many begonias, it propagates easily through leaf or stem cuttings, making it a favorite in cultivation. The species was named in honor of the botanist and explorer Richard Harling, who contributed to the understanding of various plant species in South America.

William Hugh Earle (21 January 1910 – 12 April 1983) was an Australian rules footballer who played with Melbourne and Essendon in the Victorian Football League (VFL). Earle won the 1933 Wimmera Football League best and fairest award, the Freeland Medal.

Bill Earle (born January 12, 1954) is a former professional American football player who played as a linebacker in the National Football League (NFL). He was drafted in the first round of the 1976 NFL Draft by the Cincinnati Bengals and later played for the New England Patriots. Earle is known for his strong defensive skills and impactful play during his career, which spanned from 1976 to 1980. After retiring from professional football, he transitioned into coaching and sports management, contributing to the development of athletes in various capacities. Earle remains a respected figure in the realm of American football, noted for his athletic achievements and contributions to the sport.

The healthcare reform debate in the United States has been a political issue focusing upon increasing medical coverage, decreasing costs, insurance reform, and the philosophy of its provision, funding, and government involvement.
topic: Healthcare reform debate in the United States

text: The healthcare reform debate in the United States encompasses discussions and policy proposals aimed at improving the accessibility, affordability, and quality of healthcare services. It has been a prominent issue since the 20th century, featuring prominently in political platforms and legislative agendas. Key events include the introduction of Medicare and Medicaid in 1965, the passage of the Affordable Care Act (ACA) in 2010, and ongoing controversies surrounding issues such as insurance coverage, cost control, and the role of government in healthcare. Various stakeholders, including politicians, healthcare providers, insurers, and patients, contribute to the complex discourse, which often reflects broader ideological divides regarding the balance between free-market principles and government intervention. The debate remains significant as policymakers seek solutions to the challenges of rising healthcare costs, health disparities, and the overall effectiveness of the U.S. healthcare system.
topic: Healthcare reform debate in the United States

text: Neo Zone (also known as NCT #127: Neo Zone) is the second Korean studio album (third overall) by the South Korean boy band NCT 127. Preceded by the music video release for "Dreams Come True" on NCT 127 Day, January 27 (1/27), the 13-track album was released through SM Entertainment and Capitol Music Group on March 6, 2020. Neo Zone contains various musical genres from hip hop, R&B, funk, to electro-pop, which aimed to create a soundscape that broadened the group's sonic vision. The repackaged version of the album, titled NCT No. 127 Neo Zone: The Final Round, was released on May 19, 2020. Several music critics noted the album's strong 1990s hip hop and R&B influences and the experimentation with various sounds. The album was commercially successful, becoming their fifth chart-topper on the Gaon Album Chart while earning the group their first top-five entry on the Billboard 200 chart, with first week sales of 87,000 album-equivalent units. As of April 2020, the album had sold over 805,000 copies in South Korea, becoming the group's first release to receive a multi-Platinum KMCA certification. The repackage gave the group their sixth number one title on the Gaon Album Chart, and rose cumulative sales of the record to over 1.2 million copies. Three singles were released from Neo Zone: including the pre-released "Dreams Come True", the lead single "Kick It", and the repackaged album's single "Punch". To promote the album, the group performed on South Korean music programs including Music Bank, Show! Music Core and Inkigayo, while becoming the first K-pop act to perform at Houston Livestock Show and Rodeo. The group originally planned to embark on their first U.S. arena tour; titled "Neo City – The Awards" from June 6, 2020, but was cancelled due to the COVID-19 lockdown.

"Neo Zone" is the second studio album by the South Korean boy group NCT 127, released on March 6, 2020. It features a mix of genres, including pop, hip-hop, and R&B, showcasing the group's versatility and distinct sound. The album includes tracks such as the lead single "Kick It," which emphasizes themes of empowerment and resilience. "Neo Zone" received positive reviews from critics and debuted at number five on the US Billboard 200, marking NCT 127's highest-charting album in the United States at that time. The album's commercial success was supported by a series of promotional performances and a concert tour, further solidifying NCT 127's presence in the global music scene.
Judo competitions at the 2023 Pacific Games in the Solomon Islands took place on 20 and 21 November at SIFF Academy in Honiara.

Judo at the 2023 Pacific Games was held in Honiara, Solomon Islands, from November 26 to December 2, 2023. Qeshlaq Aghdash-e Kalam (Persian: قشلاق اغداش كلام) is a village in Qeshlaq-e Sharqi Rural District of Qeshlaq Dasht District in Bileh Savar County, Ardabil province, Iran.

Qeshlaq Aghdash-e Kalam is a village located in the Aghdash Rural District, part of the Khoy County in West Azerbaijan Province, Iran. It is situated in the northwest region of the country and is known for its agricultural activities and rural lifestyle. As of the 2006 census, the village had a population of 689 residents, comprising 155 families. Matthew Whelan (born 13 November 1979) is a former professional Australian rules football player. Wearing the number 45 jersey, Whelan, recruited from the Northern Territory was a reliable defender/back pocket known by Demons fans as "Wheels". He also earned the nickname "Wrecker" (after Whelan the Wrecker) for his big hits (often on big name players) and tough and uncompromisingly defensive style of play. His tackling style, to drop the shoulder, resulted in spectacular, almost spear tackle like throws of opponents, and he ended up at the Tribunal many years over unsightly plays.

Mughan was a province of the Abbasid Caliphate, in present-day Iranian Azerbaijan and Republic of Azerbaijan. The capital was Bajravan. Other cities were Barzand, Pilsavar, Mahmood Abad and Dezhman. Mughan State was located west of Caspian Sea and south of the Aras river, encompassing the namesake plains region.

Mughan is a historical and geographical region located in the western part of Azerbaijan, primarily within the administrative borders of the Ganja-Gazakh region. The area is known for its fertile agricultural lands and is situated between the Kura River and the Lesser Caucasus mountains. Historically, Mughan has been significant for its agricultural production, particularly in the cultivation of grains and cotton. The region boasts a rich cultural heritage, influenced by various civilizations over the centuries. Mughan is characterized by its diverse population, which includes various ethnic groups and languages, contributing to the region's vibrant cultural tapestry. Shahidullah Dulal is a Bangladeshi Cinematographer and cameraman. He won the Bangladesh National Film Award for Best Cinematography for the film Hason Raja (2002).

Shahidullah Dulal (born December 31, 1988) is a Bangladeshi actor and filmmaker known for his work in the Bengali film industry. He gained recognition for his performances in various films and television dramas, showcasing his versatility as an artist. His career began in the early 2010s, and he has since worked on multiple projects that have contributed to the cultural landscape of Bangladesh. In addition to acting, Dulal is involved in producing films, exhibiting a strong commitment to the growth and development of the industry in the region. His contributions have made him a notable figure among contemporary Bangladeshi performers.

Fujimi Fantasia Bunko (富士見ファンタジア文庫, Fujimi Fantajia Bunko) is a light novel publishing imprint affiliated with the Japanese publishing company Fujimi Shobo, a brand company of Kadokawa Corporation. It was established in 1988 and is aimed at young adult male audience. Many light novels published under this imprint are serialized in Fujimi Shobo's Dragon Magazine.

Fujimi Fantasia Bunko is a Japanese publishing imprint that specializes in light novels, established by Fujimi Shobo in 2003. It primarily focuses on fantasy, science fiction, and adventure genres, often targeting a young adult audience. The imprint has published a variety of popular series, including "Date A Live," "The Irregular at Magic High School," and "Sword Art Online," contributing significantly to the growth of light novels in Japan. Fujimi Fantasia Bunko also hosts writing competitions to discover new authors, enhancing its reputation as a key player in the light novel industry. The novels are often illustrated, featuring artwork by various artists, which complements the storytelling and appeals to its readership.

Wolfgang Kunkel (1902-1981) was a prominent German historian of Roman law, who stressed the importance of Roman social history in understanding Roman law and institutions. Born in Fürth, Germany, Kunkel studied law and history at the Goethe University Frankfurt, the University of Giessen, and the University of Berlin. He received his doctorate in 1924 at the University of Freiburg and his Habilitation in 1926 (both were directed by Professor Ernst Levy). In 1929, Kunkel accepted a position as Professor at the University of Göttingen. There he worked with the prominent classical scholars Eduard Fraenkel, Hermann Frankel, and Kurt Latte. When those scholars were ousted from their positions by the Nazi government because they were Jewish, Kunkel protested. In 1936, Kunkel accepted a position at the University of Bonn and in 1943 at the University of Heidelberg. During World War II he served as a judge in the German Army, where he followed his own ethical principles and was able to prevent several injustices. After the end of the war, he took up his position at the University of Heidelberg and then was appointed Rector of the University of Heidelberg in 1947/1948. He moved to the University of Munich in 1956 and renamed the Institute for Papyrus Research and Ancient Legal History the Leopold Wenger Institute for Ancient Legal History and Papyrus Research in honor of the Austrian historian of Roman law Leopold Wenger (also an Anti-Nazi). Kunkel was a prolific and influential scholar. Throughout his career, he wrote, edited, or co-edited eleven books and also authored over 60 articles. His most influential books were Römische Rechtsgeschichte (originally published in 1948, and then went through seven editions (and which was translated into Dutch, English, Spanish, and Italian) and his Herkunft und soziale Stellung der römischen Juristen (originally published in Weimar in 1952, 2nd corrected and expanded edition published in Graz in 1967). Many of Kunkel's students would go on to be leaders in the field of the study of Roman law. After his retirement in 1970, his former student Dieter Nörr succeeded him as director of the Leopold Wenger Institute for Ancient Legal History and Papyrus Research.

Wolfgang Kunkel (born November 12, 1927, in Leipzig, Germany; died December 28, 2022) was a prominent German classical philologist and scholar, renowned for his contributions to the study of Roman history and Latin literature. He was particularly noted for his work on the historical texts of late antiquity and the early Middle Ages. Kunkel held academic positions at various universities, including the University of Hamburg and the University of Freiburg. His influential publications, including studies on the Roman Empire and key figures in Roman history, helped shape modern understanding of the period. Kunkel’s rigorous methodologies and analyses earned him recognition as a leading authority in his field.

My Love, Don't Cross That River (Korean: 님아, 그 강을 건너지 마오) is a 2013 South Korean documentary film that follows elderly married couple Jo Byeong-man and Kang Kye-yeol until the last moments of their 76-year marriage. Documentary filmmaker Jin Mo-young filmed Jo and Kang in the couple's mountain village in Hoengseong County, Gangwon Province for 15 months. My Love, Don't Cross That River premiered at the 2013 DMZ International Documentary Film Festival, where it won the Audience Award. It was released in theaters on 27 November 2014 and through word of mouth became the most commercially successful Korean documentary/independent film of all time.
