text: "My Love, Don't Cross That River" is a 2014 South Korean documentary film directed by Jin Mo-young. The film portrays the poignant life of an elderly couple, an octogenarian couple named Lee So-hee and her husband, who live in a rural village. It captures their deep love and connection as they face the realities of aging and mortality. The documentary highlights themes of love, devotion, and the inevitability of death, with an intimate look at their daily lives and the emotional struggles they endure. The film received critical acclaim for its heartfelt storytelling and cinematography, resonating with audiences and contributing to discussions about love and relationships in the context of aging.

text: Edward Francis Wells was a painter of pastoral scenes and portraits. He trained at the Royal Academy Schools and painted in a traditional ‘old master’ style. He exhibited at the Royal Academy summer exhibition and at smaller commercial galleries in London and elsewhere. The artist signed his work E. F. Wells.

Edward Francis Wells (1877–1958) was an American botanist and mycologist known for his extensive work in the field of plant ecology and his contributions to the study of fungi. Born in New Haven, Connecticut, he earned his degree from Yale University and later became a faculty member, where he focused on the classification and ecology of mushrooms. Wells is recognized for his research on the biogeography of fungi and the establishment of important fungal collections. His work significantly advanced understanding of plant-fungal interactions and contributed to the field of mycology. Throughout his career, he published numerous scientific papers and was active in various mycological societies.
The Lyceum was a live theatre in Sydney, Australia, which became the Sydney City Mission for the Methodist Church while also hosting a cinema, several times relabeled the New Lyceum.

The Lyceum Theatre, located in Sydney, Australia, is a historic venue known for its significant contributions to the performing arts. Originally opened in 1836, it has undergone various renovations and changes in purpose throughout its history. The theatre has hosted a wide range of performances, including plays, musicals, and concerts, serving as a cultural hub in the city. Its architectural style reflects the influences of 19th-century design, and it remains a notable landmark in Sydney's arts scene. The Lyceum Theatre is recognized for its role in promoting both local and international talent.

Gnathophylleptum is a genus of shrimps belonging to the family Palaemonidae. The species of this genus are found in Central America, Southeastern Asia and Australia. Species: Gnathophylloides mineri Schmitt, 1933 Gnathophylloides robustus Bruce, 1973

Gnathophylleptum is a genus of lizards in the family Carphodactylidae, native to New Guinea. Established in 2005, it is notable for its unique adaptations to arboreal life, including specialized limb structures that aid in climbing. The genus includes several species characterized by distinctive morphological traits, such as elongated bodies and prehensile tails, which facilitate their habitat in the rainforest canopy. The Gutenberg-Gymnasium Erfurt is a gymnasium (secondary school) located in Erfurt, Germany. It opened in 1991 and has approximately 750 students between the ages of 10 and 19.

Gutenberg-Gymnasium Erfurt is a co-educational secondary school located in Erfurt, Germany. Established in 1990, the school is named after Johannes Gutenberg, the inventor of the printing press. text: Panbers (short for Panjaitan Bersaudara / Panjaitan Brothers) is the name of a musical group. The band, founded in 1969, is composed of four brothers who are the sons of Drs. JMM Pandjaitan, S.H, (late) with BSO Sitompul. They are Hans Pandjaitan, Benny Pandjaitan, Doan Pandjaitan, and Asido Pandjaitan. By naming their band Panbers, they pioneered their careers in the capital, ranging from filling entertainment events at the school party to a party of young people who was then known as the 'party Dayak'. With the unanimous determination of capital and their persistent struggle to create a song and bring it to parties, one song that they always bring wherever they performed is "Akhir Cinta". Starting from this, Panbers is becoming known and creating a new era in the world of Indonesian music. Panbers career beginning with his first appearance on stage at the event Istora Bands Jamboree 1970 which makes Panbers more widely known. Especially after appearing on television opportunities already open to them. Original songs of their own work such as Bye Bye, Jakarta City Sound, "Akhir Cinta", "Hanya Semusim Bunga" and "Hanya Padamu" begin to be released. The success of their performances on television seems to attract the attention of Dick Tamimi, company managers Dimita Molding Industries, which later led Panbers into the recording studio. They were given the opportunity to record their songs in the form of LPs. As already known, their hits, entitled "Akhir Cinta" was released. This song always engraved in the hearts of music fans of Indonesia. Panbers success in the world record is the beginning of the return of the band era in Indonesia, which at that time was still dominated by solo singers. Koeswoyo Brothers, often shortened to Koes Brothers, is a pioneering band of Rock'n Roll music in the '60s. The emergence of Panbers in early 1972 was followed by a number of bands who enliven the world of Indonesian music until now. Panbers lost one of their members, Hans Pandjaitan, being replaced by bassist Maxi Pandelaki. Meanwhile, Hans Pandjaitan replaced by a musician named Hans Noya. Panbers has created more than 700 songs in dozens of albums, both the genre of pop, rock, spiritual, keroncong, even Malay. Until now, Panbers still exists to enliven the world of Indonesian music, not only active in live concerts, but also releasing albums. Some of their songs include "Gereja Tua", "Cinta dan Permata", "Kami Cinta Perdamaian", "Indonesia My Lovely Country", "Akhir Cinta", "Jakarta City Sound", "Haai", dan "Terlambat Sudah". In October 2010, Panbers again lost one of its personnel forever. Doan Panjaitan died due to illness. However Panbers keep trying to make music with a distinguished timeless masterpieces.

Panbers is an Indonesian rock band formed in 1970 in Jakarta, known for its significant contributions to the country's music scene, particularly in the 1970s and 1980s. The band originally consisted of founding members Ahmad Albar, Janitra, and Ronny, and it gained popularity with its blend of rock, pop, and traditional Indonesian influences. Panbers is notable for their hits such as "Jangan Biarkan" and "Malam Suci," which reflect themes of love, social issues, and cultural identity. The band's style has evolved over the years, and they are recognized as one of Indonesia's pioneering rock groups, influencing many subsequent artists in the region. Despite several lineup changes, Panbers continues to perform and remains a beloved figure in Indonesian music history.

In atmospheric dynamics, oceanography, asteroseismology and geophysics, the Brunt–Väisälä frequency, or buoyancy frequency, is a measure of the stability of a fluid to vertical displacements such as those caused by convection. More precisely it is the frequency at which a vertically displaced parcel will oscillate within a statically stable environment. It is named after David Brunt and Vilho Väisälä. It can be used as a measure of atmospheric stratification.

The Brunt–Väisälä frequency, denoted as N, is a measure of the stability of a stratified fluid, particularly in the context of atmospheric and oceanic sciences. It quantifies the frequency of oscillation that a displaced parcel of fluid will undergo due to vertical density stratification. The frequency is derived from the balance between buoyancy forces and inertial forces acting on the parcel. Mathematically, it is defined as \( N = \sqrt{-\frac{g}{\rho} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial z}} \), where \( g \) is the acceleration due to gravity, \( \rho \) is the density of the fluid, and \( z \) is the vertical coordinate. A higher Brunt–Väisälä frequency indicates a more stable stratification, while a lower frequency implies potential instability and the likelihood of convective motions. It is crucial for understanding phenomena such as thermocline dynamics, atmospheric stability, and internal waves in oceans and the atmosphere.

Baker Memorial Church was a Methodist church in Concord, New Hampshire, United States. It organized on October 30, 1874, by Presiding Elder Theodore L. Flood, in response to a request from 100 members of the First Church, who believed the best interests of a growing Methodism could be served best with two societies instead of one.The church was named in honor of Bishop Osmon C. Baker, a native of New Hampshire and a member of the New Hampshire Annual Conference. It was later demolished.

Baker Memorial Church is a historic church located in the city of St. Charles, Illinois. Constructed in the late 19th century, it was designed by noted architect Frank W. Smith in the Gothic Revival style. The church is named in honor of Elias Baker, a prominent local businessman and philanthropist. The structure features distinctive elements such as stained glass windows, a tall steeple, and ornate woodwork. Baker Memorial Church has served as a place of worship for various denominations over the years and is recognized for its architectural significance and community role. In 1978, it was added to the National Register of Historic Places, highlighting its cultural importance in the region.

A bourrelet is a portion of an elongated artillery projectile that is used in conjunction with the projectile's driving band, or rotating band, to stabilize its launch by keeping the fore part of the projectile precisely aligned with the gun barrel.

The bourrelet is a structural feature found in certain gastropod shells, specifically functioning as a ridge or ledge that distinguishes different whorls of the shell. It typically occurs at the junction of the body whorl and the preceding whorl, contributing to the overall morphology of the shell. Bourrelets can vary in prominence among different species, influencing classification and identification in malacology. Beyond gastropods, the term may also be applied to similar structures in other mollusks and some cephalopods, where it may serve a role in shell stability and design.

Maneesha Inamdar is a stem cell and developmental biologist conducting research at Bangalore, India. She is Director of inStem (Institute for Stem Cell Science and Regenerative Medicine), and Professor at Jawaharlal Nehru Centre for Advanced Scientific Research (JNCASR), Bangalore. She is an elected fellow of the Indian Academy of Sciences and the Indian National Science Academy and a J C Bose National Fellow with several awards and honors.

Maneesha S. Inamdar is an Indian academic and researcher known for her contributions to the fields of environmental science and sustainable development. She is currently a faculty member at a prominent university where she focuses on climate change, ecosystem management, and policy formulation for sustainability. Inamdar has published numerous research papers and articles in international journals, and she actively participates in conferences and workshops aimed at promoting environmental awareness and action. In addition to her research, she is involved in various outreach programs aimed at educating communities about environmental issues and fostering sustainable practices.

Emmanuel Joseph Sieyès (3 May 1748 – 20 June 1836), usually known as the Abbé Sieyès (traditional French pronunciation: [sijɛːs]; modern pronunciation: [sjejɛs]), was a French Catholic priest, abbé, and political writer who was a leading political theorist of the French Revolution (1789–1799); he also held offices in the governments of the French Consulate (1799–1804) and the First French Empire (1804–1815). His pamphlet What Is the Third Estate? (1789) became the political manifesto of the Revolution, which facilitated transforming the Estates-General into the National Assembly, in June 1789. He was offered and refused an office in the French Directory (1795–1799). After becoming a director in 1799, Sieyès was among the instigators of the Coup of 18 Brumaire (9 November), which installed Napoleon Bonaparte in power. In addition to his political and clerical life, Sieyès coined the term "sociologie", and contributed to the nascent social sciences.

Emmanuel Joseph Sieyès (1748–1836) was a French clergyman, political theorist, and revolutionary best known for his influential pamphlet "What Is the Third Estate?" published in 1789, which argued for the representation of the common people and the inadequacies of the privileged classes in France. A key figure in the early stages of the French Revolution, Sieyès played a significant role in the formation of the National Assembly and the drafting of the revolutionary Constitution. His ideas contributed to the rise of the concept of citizenship and popular sovereignty. Sieyès later aligned himself with the Directory and supported Napoleon Bonaparte, participating in the coup of 18 Brumaire in 1799, which led to Napoleon's rise to power. Throughout his life, Sieyès remained a prominent intellectual figure and served in various political capacities during the tumultuous periods of the French Revolutionary and Napoleonic eras.

Lolworth is a small village and civil parish in the district of South Cambridgeshire, in the county of Cambridgeshire, England, located approximately 8 miles (13 km) northwest of Cambridge city centre. The Prime Meridian passes through the village, around just 50 metres (160 ft) to the west of the crossroads in the centre.

Lolworth is a village and civil parish located in Cambridgeshire, England, approximately 9 miles northwest of Cambridge. The village is part of the South Cambridgeshire district and has a small population, which is reflected in its rural character. Lolworth is primarily residential, with a history that dates back to the Domesday Book of 1086, where it was recorded as "Luleworde." The parish features a historic church, St. Peter's, which exhibits elements from different architectural periods. Lolworth is well-connected through local road networks and is surrounded by agricultural land. The village is noted for its community spirit and local events.

The Mattei Affair (Italian: Il Caso Mattei) is a 1972 Italian drama film directed by Francesco Rosi. It depicts the life and mysterious death of Enrico Mattei, an Italian businessman who in the aftermath of World War II managed to avoid the sale of the nascent Italian oil and hydrocarbon industry to US companies and developed them in the Eni, a state-owned oil company which rivaled the "Seven Sisters" for oil and gas deals in Northern African and Middle Eastern countries. The film shared the Grand Prix with The Working Class Goes to Heaven at the 1972 Cannes Film Festival. Italian star Gian Maria Volonté was the leading actor in both films. The film is an innovative hybrid of documentary and fiction, representing Francesco Rosi's concept of cine-inchieste (film investigation). The flashback structure shows the influence of Citizen Kane and Rosi's Salvatore Giuliano (1962). Rosi remains faithful to his neo-realist roots with on-location shooting and non-professional actors. The main plot is interwoven with a fictionalized account of the director's own investigation into the death of his friend, the journalist Mauro De Mauro, who disappeared while doing research for the film. He was killed by the Sicilian Mafia, but like the death of Mattei, De Mauro's case was never solved. In 2008, the film was included among the Italian Ministry of Cultural Heritage’s 100 Italian films to be saved, a list of 100 films that "have changed the collective memory of the country between 1942 and 1978."

The Mattei Affair refers to the mysterious death of Italian industrialist Enrico Mattei, who died in a plane crash on October 27, 1962, near Pavia, Italy. Mattei was the head of the Italian state-owned oil company, ENI, and was known for his efforts to challenge the dominance of Western oil companies in the global market. His controversial dealings in the Middle East and Africa, coupled with his support for independent oil production, led to speculation that his death was not accidental. Various conspiracy theories suggest involvement by the CIA, the Mafia, or rival oil interests, although no definitive evidence has confirmed foul play. The incident remains a subject of intrigue and debate, illustrating the intersection of politics, economics, and industrial espionage during the Cold War era.

Pacuvius Labeo (died 42 BC) was a Roman jurist and senator, and one of the murderers of Julius Caesar. He was father of the more eminent jurist Marcus Antistius Labeo, who lived under the emperor Augustus. Pacuvius was one of the disciples of Servius Sulpicius Rufus, who are stated by Sextus Pomponius to have written books which were digested by Aufidius Namusa. Aulus Gellius mentioned him as the recipient of a letter by Sinnius Capito in a discussion about grammar. Pacuvius joined the conspiracy of Brutus to assassinate the dictator Julius Caesar. He was probably a senator by then, but his career up to that point is unrecorded. Pacuvius was one of the most enthusiastic among the conspirators, and took an active role in the recruiting process. He was present at the Battle of Philippi on Brutus's side. After the defeat, he was unwilling to survive Brutus, who, he was told, had pronounced his name with a sigh before his death. Having dug in his tent a hole of the length of his body, he settled his worldly affairs, and sent messages to his wife and children. Then, taking the hand of his most faithful slave, he turned him round (as was usual in the ceremony of manumission), and, giving him his sword, presented his throat to be stabbed, and was buried in his tent in the hole which he had dug. His name was formerly thought to be "Pacuvius Antistius Labeo", but Ernst Badian has shown that this is an unlikely name for a senator of his time, and noted that the name "Antistius" is only attested by a corrupt passage in the Digest. Pacuvius's son, Antistius Labeo, will then have acquired his own name through adoption. Pacuvius's entry in the DGRBM (1849) gives his name as "Quintus Antistius Labeo", but this is confirmed by no other source.

Pacuvius Labeo (c. 100–c. 50 BC) was a notable Roman jurist and legal scholar during the late Roman Republic, recognized for his contributions to juristic thought and legal interpretation. He was a leading figure in the development of Roman law, particularly in the areas of civil law and legal procedure. Labeo is often associated with the "proculiani" school, which emphasized a more systematic approach to legal reasoning compared to the "sabinian" school. His influence extended through his written works, although most of his texts have been lost. Labeo's ideas were integral to the evolution of legal theory in Rome and the subsequent influence of Roman law on Western legal systems. His legacy persists through references by later legal scholars and historians who highlighted his role in shaping the foundations of Roman jurisprudence.

Statilia maculata, common name Asian jumping mantis or "小蟷螂", ko-kamakiri (Japanese meaning "small mantis") or "좀사마귀", jom-sa-ma-gui (Korean meaning "small mantis"), is a species of mantis native to Asia that can be found in Russia, China and Japan and Korea, and Sri Lanka.

Statilia maculata is a species of spider belonging to the family Thomisidae, commonly known as crab spiders. It is found in various regions across Europe and parts of Asia. The species is characterized by its distinctive coloration, which often includes spots that help it blend into its environment, making it effective at ambushing prey. Statilia maculata primarily preys on insects, using its ability to remain motionless to catch unsuspecting victims. This spider species is typically observed in gardens, grasslands, and areas with dense vegetation, where it can utilize its camouflage for hunting. Its ecological role includes participation in the control of insect populations.
Martin Muhr (born June 17, 1971) is a strongman from Germany. He participated in the World's Strongest Man finals of 2000 and 2001.

Martin Muhr is a notable figure recognized for his contributions to the fields of technology and engineering. With a background in computer science, he has been involved in various innovative projects and research initiatives. He has collaborated with numerous organizations, contributing to advancements in automation and artificial intelligence. Muhr is also known for his publications in academic journals and for speaking at industry conferences, where he shares insights about emerging trends in technology.

Furesø is a fresh water lake in Northeastern Zealand, Denmark and the deepest lake in Denmark. It defined the former Farum municipality's southeasthern border and is the site of Nicolai Eigtved's 18th century small pleasure pavilion for Privy Councillor Johan Sigismund Schulin on the Furesø Lake called Frederiksdal Pavilion. The lake neighbours the adjacent Farum Lake, to which it is connected by a short stream, Fiskebaek. It is the namesake of Furesø municipality, which was formed January 1, 2007 as a merger of the two former municipalities Farum and Værløse. It has a popular beach on its western shore, which is within the municipality.

Furesø is a freshwater lake located in the northern part of the Greater Copenhagen area in Denmark. It is the deepest lake in the country, with a maximum depth of approximately 36 meters (118 feet) and covers an area of around 5.5 square kilometers (2.1 square miles). The lake is situated near the towns of Furesø and Værløse and is surrounded by scenic nature, including forests and recreational areas. Furesø serves as a popular destination for various activities such as swimming, sailing, and fishing. The lake is fed by several streams and is part of the water system that drains into the larger Lake Gentofte. Its shores are lined with residential neighborhoods, and it plays an essential role in local biodiversity and ecosystem. The Furesø municipality, which encompasses the lake, was established in 2007 and focuses on environmental sustainability and conservation of the lake's natural beauty.

As of December 2025, the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) lists 825 critically endangered amphibian species, including 187 which are tagged as possibly extinct. 10.2% of all evaluated amphibian species are listed as critically endangered. Additionally, 896 amphibian species (11.1% of those evaluated) are listed as data deficient, meaning there is insufficient information for a full assessment of conservation status. As these species typically have small distributions and/or populations, they are intrinsically likely to be threatened, according to the IUCN. While the category of data deficient indicates that no assessment of extinction risk has been made for the taxa, the IUCN notes that it may be appropriate to give them "the same degree of attention as threatened taxa, at least until their status can be assessed." This is a complete list of critically endangered amphibian species evaluated by the IUCN, last substantially updated January 2022. Species considered possibly extinct by the IUCN are marked as such.

The list of critically endangered amphibians comprises species that are facing an extremely high risk of extinction in the wild, as categorized by the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN). This classification is part of the IUCN Red List, which assesses the conservation status of various species globally. Factors contributing to the critical endangerment of amphibians include habitat loss, climate change, pollution, disease (notably chytridiomycosis), and invasive species. The list includes various taxa, such as frogs, toads, and salamanders, highlighting the urgent conservation needs and efforts required to protect these vulnerable species and their habitats. Organizations and governments worldwide are implementing strategies to mitigate threats, restore ecosystems, and promote amphibian conservation initiatives.

Raymond Parkinson (March 22, 1922 – June 28, 2018) was a Canadian psychiatrist and politician in British Columbia. Along with Tom Berger, he represented Vancouver-Burrard in the Legislative Assembly of British Columbia from 1966 to 1969 as a New Democratic Party (NDP) member. He served in the Royal Canadian Army Medical Corps and the Medical Services unit of the Royal Canadian Air Force during World War II. After the war, Parkinson studied at the University of British Columbia and McGill University and went on to practise at the Medical Health Centre in Burnaby. He also consulted at the Fraser Valley Health Units in Chilliwack and Mission. Parkinson was defeated when he ran for reelection to the provincial assembly in 1969. After leaving politics, he returned to private practice in Vancouver. Parkinson was married to Ella McWilliam. He was a member of the board of governors for Simon Fraser University, serving five years as board chairman. Parkinson died in June 2018 at the age of 96.

Ulrich "Ulli" Libor (born 27 March 1940) is a German sailor. He won a silver medal in the Flying Dutchman Class at the 1968 Summer Olympics and a bronze medal at the 1972 Summer Olympics.

Ullrich Libor is a prominent German mathematician known for his contributions to algebraic geometry and number theory. He was born on February 15, 1968, and has held academic positions at various prestigious institutions. Libor's research primarily focuses on the interplay between algebraic structures and geometric properties, leading to significant advancements in understanding algebraic varieties. He has published numerous articles in leading mathematical journals and has been involved in various educational initiatives to promote mathematics in higher education. His work is recognized for its impact on both theoretical constructs and practical applications in mathematics.

Benjamin Lawrence Reid (May 3, 1918 — November 30, 1990) was an American professor in English from the 1940s to 1980s. During his career, Reid primarily taught at Sweet Briar College from 1951 to 1957 and Mount Holyoke College from 1957 to 1983. Outside of academics, Reid wrote multiple books, and won a Pulitzer Prize and other honors.

Benjamin Lawrence Reid (born December 1, 1977) is an American entrepreneur and philanthropist best known for his work in the tech industry and contributions to social causes. He co-founded several successful startups focusing on innovative technology solutions and has served in various leadership roles across different organizations. Reid is also recognized for his philanthropic efforts, particularly in education and community development, and has been involved in initiatives aimed at providing access to technology for underprivileged communities. He holds a degree in Computer Science from a prominent university and is a frequent speaker at tech conferences and seminars, advocating for entrepreneurship and social responsibility in the tech sector.
Luíza Touché de Almeida (June 3, 1985 in Rio de Janeiro) is a Brazilian actress.

Luíza Curvo is a Brazilian influencer, model, and social media personality known for her presence on platforms such as Instagram and TikTok. She gained recognition for her fashion, beauty, and lifestyle content, often showcasing her style and engaging with her audience through creative posts and videos. Curvo has collaborated with various brands and participated in campaigns, enhancing her visibility within the digital influencer space. As of 2023, she continues to expand her reach and influence in the realm of social media.

KMMF (channel 17) was a television station in Missoula, Montana, United States, affiliated with the Fox network. Founded on April 25, 2000, the station was owned by Equity Broadcasting. Its signal was repeated in Kalispell on KMMF-LP (channel 34). KMMF added MyNetworkTV as a secondary affiliation in September 2006. KMMF was fed from Equity's hub in Little Rock, Arkansas, by March 2008. Technical issues dramatically increased in number after this was implemented. At auction on April 16, 2009, Max Media bought the Fox affiliations and certain programming assets, but not the broadcasting facilities, of KMMF and the rest of Equity's Montana Fox station system.

KMMF, short for Karnataka Music and Multimedia Foundation, is a cultural organization based in Karnataka, India, dedicated to the promotion and preservation of traditional and contemporary music, multimedia arts, and cultural heritage. Established in the early 2000s, KMMF organizes events, workshops, and festivals that showcase a diverse range of artistic expressions, including classical music, folk traditions, and modern multimedia performances. The foundation also collaborates with local artists and institutions to foster educational programs aimed at enriching the cultural landscape of the region. Through its initiatives, KMMF aims to support artists, enhance audience engagement, and celebrate the rich artistic heritage of Karnataka.

The Norinchukin Bank (農林中央金庫, Nōrin Chūō Kinko; lit. "Central Credit Union for Agriculture and Forestry [Cooperatives]"), also referred to as Nochu Bank, is a Japanese cooperative bank serving over 5,612 agricultural, fishing and forestry cooperatives from its headquarters in Tokyo. Nochu is one of Japan's largest institutional investors, with an investment portfolio of more than US$400 billion and assets exceeding US$840 billion. Through overseas branches located in New York City, London, and Singapore, it invests in bond, securitization products, stock, private equity, and real estate. Its large assets of US$840 billion are managed by only around 3,200 employees, as the bank is mainly engaged in asset management and large scale corporate financing. It has 41 offices throughout Japan and five overseas branches. Nochu's member cooperatives include federations such as the Japan Agricultural Cooperatives (JA) and the Japan Fishery Cooperatives (JF). Norinchukin supports political lobbying against agricultural imports and the deterioration of living standards in rural areas.

Norinchukin Bank is a cooperative bank based in Japan, primarily serving the agricultural, fishery, and forestry sectors. Established in 1923, it functions as a central bank for agricultural cooperatives and provides various financial services, including lending, investment, and asset management. The bank plays a crucial role in supporting rural economies and facilitating funding for agricultural initiatives. Its governance structure reflects its cooperative nature, with members primarily consisting of agricultural cooperatives and related organizations. Norinchukin Bank is also involved in international markets and investment activities, aiming to promote agricultural and economic development.

The list of minor planets numbered 254001 to 255000 includes various small celestial bodies in the Solar System, primarily composed of rock and metal, that orbit the Sun. These minor planets are part of the wider group of objects known as asteroids, which are located mainly in the asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter, as well as in other regions such as the Kuiper Belt and scattered disc. Üçtepe is a village in the Bulanık District of Muş Province, eastern Turkey. Its population is 247 (2021). It is 115 km from the city of Muş and 7 km from Bulanık town center.

Üçtepe is a village located in the Bulanık district of Muş Province in Turkey. Situated in the Eastern Anatolia region, Üçtepe features a predominantly agricultural economy, with residents engaged in farming and animal husbandry. The village is characterized by its rural landscape and traditional lifestyle, reflecting the cultural heritage of the region. Information about the population and specific demographic details may vary, as census data is periodically updated. The village is accessible by local roads connecting it to larger towns and cities in the area.

Safford Unified School District v. Redding, 557 U.S. 364 (2009), was a case in which the Supreme Court of the United States held that a strip search of a middle school student by school officials violated the Fourth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution, which prohibits unreasonable searches and seizures. On October 8, 2003, the assistant principal of Safford Middle School in Safford, Arizona, informed 13-year-old Savana Redding that another student had accused her of distributing prescription-strength ibuprofen, which was disallowed without prior permission by school rules. Redding denied this accusation, and after a search of her belongings did not reveal any pills, school officials instructed her to remove her outer clothing and pull out her bra and underpants, which also did not reveal any pills. Redding's mother sued the Safford Unified School District and the school officials who searched her daughter, arguing that they had violated the Fourth Amendment. On June 25, 2009, in an 8–1 decision authored by Justice David Souter, the Supreme Court held that the search failed to meet the "reasonable suspicion" standard for searches of students in a school setting established by the Court in New Jersey v. T. L. O. (1985), stating that the school lacked reasons to suspect either that the drugs presented a danger or that they were concealed in her underwear. However, the Court also found that because there was sufficient doubt as to whether the law was clearly established at the time of the search, the school officials were shielded from liability by qualified immunity. The case garnered nationwide interest and sparked debates over the extent to which school officials should enforce zero-tolerance policies in schools. Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg was the only female justice at the time and discussed the case in an interview while a decision was still pending; observers have described Ginsburg's role in the case as emphasizing the need for more diversity on the Supreme Court.
topic: Safford Unified School District v. Redding

text: Safford Unified School District v. Redding is a landmark United States Supreme Court case decided in 2009, involving the Fourth Amendment rights of students in public schools. The case arose from a 2003 incident where officials at Safford Middle School in Arizona conducted a strip search on 13-year-old student Savanna Redding, in an attempt to find prescription-strength ibuprofen, which was prohibited by the school's policies. Redding's lawsuit claimed that the search violated her constitutional rights. The Supreme Court ruled that the strip search was unconstitutional, emphasizing that such invasive searches require a reasonable suspicion of a serious threat to student safety. The decision clarified the limitations of school officials’ authority concerning student searches and reinforced the protection of students' privacy rights within the educational environment.
topic: Safford Unified School District v. Redding

text: Robbie Martin (born December 3, 1958) is an American former professional football player who was a wide receiver and kick returner in the National Football League (NFL). Martin played college football for the Cal Poly Mustangs and was selected by the Pittsburgh Steelers in the fourth round of the 1981 NFL draft. He played for the Detroit Lions from 1981 to 1984 and the Indianapolis Colts from 1985 to 1986.

Robbie Martin is an American filmmaker, musician, and political commentator, known for his critical analysis of modern media and U.S. foreign policy. He gained prominence through his documentaries, particularly "A Very Heavy Agenda," which critiques the influence of neoconservative figures in American politics. Martin is also recognized for his work in the fields of animation and music, often blending his artistic pursuits with socially and politically charged themes. He is a founding member of the band The Black Mamba and has contributed his voice to various podcasts and discussions focusing on leftist perspectives. His online presence includes a significant following for his provocative commentary on contemporary issues.

Natsuki Obama (小濱なつき, Obama Natsuki; born February 12, 1984) is a Japanese fashion model and actor. She appeared in the national "What should I do to coordinate tomorrow?" campaign for the fashion magazine Marisol in 2017 and 2018. While attending Kagoshima Girl's High School, she was a member of the volleyball team that went to the national finals. Obama was a finalist for the 3rd Cover Girl Prize given out by Magazine Summit, a national organization for magazine publishers in Japan. Obama has used her celebrity status to support products such as shōchū that are produced in Minamisatsuma. She gave birth to a daughter in 2019.

Natsuki Obama is a Japanese television personality, model, and social media influencer, known for her vibrant online presence and engaging content. Born on October 11, 1997, in Tokyo, Japan, she gained popularity through platforms such as Instagram and YouTube, where she shares lifestyle, fashion, and beauty-related content. Natsuki has collaborated with various brands and has a significant following, appealing to a young, trend-conscious audience. Her work often emphasizes themes of self-expression and creativity, as she connects with fans through relatable experiences and interactions.

Joyce Cohen Lashof (March 27, 1926 in Philadelphia – June 4, 2022) was an American physician, public health expert and advocate for health equity. She was the first woman to head a state public health department and the first woman to serve as dean of the School of Public Health at the University of California, Berkeley.

Joyce C. Lashof (born 1928) is an American epidemiologist and public health leader, noted for her contributions to the fields of epidemiology and women's health. She served as the director of the California Department of Health Services and was a professor at the University of California, Berkeley. Lashof has played a significant role in research on health disparities and health policy, actively advocating for women's health issues throughout her career. She is recognized for her leadership in various health organizations and councils, contributing to the advancement of public health practices and policies.
Karumba Airport (IATA: KRB, ICAO: YKMB) is an airport located 2 nautical miles (3.7 km; 2.3 mi) north of Karumba, Queensland, Australia. The airport received $91,500 for security upgrades in 2006.

Karumba Airport is a small regional airport located in Karumba, Queensland, Australia. It serves the community and surrounding areas with domestic flights, primarily connecting to larger regional centers. Operated by the local government, the airport features a single asphalt runway suitable for small aircraft. The facility plays a vital role in providing access for residents, tourism, and local industries such as fishing. Karumba Airport is characterized by its modest amenities and primarily serves as a gateway for visitors exploring the Gulf of Carpentaria region.

Leonard E. Lindquist (September 5, 1912 – September 10, 2004) was an American lawyer and politician. Lindquist was born in Minneapolis, Minnesota. He served in the United States Navy during World War II. Lindquist received his law degree from the University of Minnesota Law School and practiced law in Minneapolis, where he co-founded the firm later known as Lindquist & Vennum in 1946. He lived in Minneapolis with his wife and family. Lindquist served in the Minnesota House of Representatives from 1955 to 1958 and was a Republican. He unsuccessfully ran for Congress as a Republican for Minnesota's 3rd congressional district in 1958. He lost the Republican nomination for the same district in 1960. He died at Northwestern Hospital Hospital, in Minneapolis, from complications from a fall. His funeral and burial was in Minneapolis.

Leonard E. Lindquist (1920-2017) was an American mathematician known for his contributions to the field of mathematics education and curriculum development. He served as a professor at several institutions, including the University of Wisconsin-Madison, where he was influential in shaping mathematics teaching methodologies. Lindquist was an advocate for improving mathematical understanding in primary and secondary education and played a significant role in developing innovative instructional materials. He authored numerous articles and books on mathematics education and participated in notable educational reform initiatives throughout his career, emphasizing the importance of conceptual understanding in mathematics.
Vinícius Faria dos Santos (born 15 November 1999), known as Vinícius Faria, is a Brazilian footballer who plays as a midfielder for Camboriú on loan from Cianorte.

Vinícius Faria is a Brazilian professional footballer born on 18 June 1994 in São Paulo, Brazil. He primarily plays as a forward and is known for his speed, technical skills, and goal-scoring ability. Faria began his career in the youth system of several Brazilian clubs before making his professional debut in the lower divisions of Brazilian football. His performances caught the attention of scouts, leading to stints with various clubs in Brazil and abroad. Over his career, Faria has been recognized for his contributions in critical matches and has garnered a reputation as a promising talent in the footballing world.

Hamra (Arabic: الحمراء ) is a neighborhood (sector 34) in Beirut, Lebanon (quarter Ras Beirut). The heart of the neighborhood, Hamra Street, is a major commercial district, and is known for its fashion stores, restaurants, cafes, bars and hotels, and a lively nightlife. The American University of Beirut and Lebanese American University are in the neighborhood. Hamra Street used to be one of the main places where Arab poets, writers, thinkers, and philosophers met and collaborated, making it an important cultural hub. Many impacts of the Lebanese Civil War remain on the walls, but it has also seen development. Although it may not be as culturally and artistically vibrant as before, it remains rich with life and art. Graffiti has become a form of artistic expression along the central boulevard; an example is a mural of the popular singer and icon Sabah towards the beginning of the street.

Hamra is a vibrant neighborhood located in Beirut, Lebanon, known for its cultural and historical significance. Renowned for its blend of modern and traditional elements, Hamra features a diverse array of shops, restaurants, cafes, and nightlife, making it a popular destination for locals and tourists alike. The area is also home to several educational institutions, including the American University of Beirut, which contributes to its youthful atmosphere. Historically, Hamra has been a hub for intellectual and artistic movements in Lebanon, reflecting the city’s rich cultural heritage. Its streets are characterized by a mix of architectural styles, representing the neighborhood's evolution over time.
Ghost House is a 2004 South Korean horror-comedy film. It was released in South Korea on September 7, 2004, and was the fourth best selling film of the year with 2,890,000 tickets sold.

"Ghost House" is a 2004 supernatural horror film directed by Mark Roper. The plot revolves around a group of friends who unwittingly disturb a haunted house in Thailand while on vacation. The film explores themes of friendship, fear, and the consequences of meddling with the supernatural. It is characterized by traditional horror elements, including ghostly apparitions and suspenseful sequences. "Ghost House" received mixed reviews from critics but has garnered a cult following for its eerie atmosphere and unique setting. The film is distributed by 20th Century Fox and is part of the larger trend of horror films inspired by Asian cinema.
The men's 4×7.5 km classic competition of the 2015 Winter Universiade was held at the Sporting Centre FIS Štrbské Pleso on January 30.

text: The Men's 4 x 7.5 km relay in cross-country skiing at the 2015 Winter Universiade took place on February 22, 2015, in Granada, Spain. This event featured teams of four athletes, each skiing a distance of 7.5 kilometers in a relay format. The competition included participants from various countries, showcasing emerging talents in the sport. Ultimately, the team from Norway won the gold medal, while silver and bronze medals were awarded to teams from Russia and Italy, respectively. The relay highlighted the strategic teamwork and endurance required in competitive cross-country skiing.

text: The second season of Yu-Gi-Oh! Duel Monsters, based on the manga by Kazuki Takahashi, premiered in Japan on April 10, 2001, and concluded on March 5, 2002, on TV Tokyo. The English adaptation of this season aired in the United States from November 16, 2002, and concluded on November 1, 2003, on Kids' WB. The season was directed by Kunihisa Sugishima, and written by Junki Takegami, Masashi Sogo, and Shin Yoshida. This season follows Yugi and Joey as they compete in Seto Kaiba's Battle City tournament, which was organized in an attempt to gather the three Egyptian God Cards. However, Marik Ishtar, and his loyal Rare Hunter servants, also enter the tournament as part of his plan to use the power of the God cards to take over the world. Between January 24 and December 28, 2004, Funimation released eleven DVD sets for the season under the title "Yu-Gi-Oh! Battle City Duels", containing episodes from the season. The complete season package was released on March 4, 2008. All DVDs were encoded in Region 1. The series was formerly licensed by 4Kids Entertainment in North America and other English-speaking territories, and was formerly distributed by FUNimation Entertainment, Ltd. on North American home video and also formerly distributed by Warner Bros. Television Animation thru North American television rights, when it aired on Kids’ WB. It is now licensed and distributed by 4K Media.

Yu-Gi-Oh! Duel Monsters Season 2, also known as the "Doma" arc or "Virtual World" arc, is the second season of the Japanese anime series Yu-Gi-Oh!, based on Kazuki Takahashi's manga. It originally aired in Japan from September 2000 to March 2001, consisting of 48 episodes. This season follows Yugi Muto and his friends as they navigate the threats posed by the organization known as the Big Five, who have taken them to a virtual world to compete in a series of duel tournaments. The arc introduces new characters, including the Big Five and the mysterious duelist, Dartz, who serves as the primary antagonist. The season explores themes of friendship, the importance of courage, and the bond between duelists, while further developing the lore of the Duel Monsters game. The English dubbed version aired in North America from 2002 to 2003, with some alterations to the original content.

Luis Mariano de Larra y Wetoret (1830, in Madrid – 1901, in Madrid) was a Spanish writer, son of journalist Mariano José de Larra. Noted as the librettist of numerous zarzuelas such as El Toro y el Tigre, Un embuste y una boda, Todo con raptos, El cuello de la camisa, con el Sr. Suricalday amongst others, his most famous work is undoubtedly El barberillo de Lavapiés (1874), set to music by Francisco Asenjo Barbieri. Larra worked for the Ministry of Development and contributed to various publications of the time such as the Gaceta de Madrid, from which he resigned to devote himself exclusively to literature. He also wrote many comedies, among which is his much successful La oración de la tarde, drama in three acts and verse whose protagonist Don Diego de Mendoza, dragging his righteous indignation for reasons of honor, implements the evangelical precept of forgiving every injury. It premiered at Teatro del Circo on 25 November 1858 with great acclaim.

Luis Mariano de Larra (1809–1837) was a prominent Spanish journalist, playwright, and literary critic, known for his influential role in Spanish romanticism. Born in Madrid, he became a key figure in the Spanish literary scene and was recognized for his sharp wit and satirical style. Larra's writings often addressed social and political issues, reflecting the turbulence of his times, and he was an early advocate for freedom of expression. His most significant works include essays, articles, and plays, which contributed to the development of modern Spanish journalism and literature. Larra's life was marked by personal struggles, and he took his own life at the age of 27, leaving behind a legacy that inspired future generations of writers and intellectuals.

Beach too Sandy, Water too Wet is a comedy podcast hosted by siblings Alexander “Xandy” and Christine Schiefer. The podcast, featuring dramatic readings of one-star reviews of hyper local and national topics, launched in autumn 2018 and in January 2022 had more than 100,000 listeners. Updated weekly, the hosts challenge one another on specific topics and themes. It was named among the best new podcasts of 2019 by Spotify, the top ten comedy podcasts in 2021 by UrbanMatter, and the New York Times named it among the six to make the listeners feel good in 2022. Christine is also the host of And That's Why We Drink and Alex co-hosts Human Seeking Human with Liz Collins.
topic: Beach too Sandy, Water too Wet

text: "Beach Too Sandy, Water Too Wet" is a comedic podcast hosted by siblings Jack and Chloe Hurd. Launched in 2018, the show features the hosts reading and interpreting humorous one-star reviews of various locations, products, and experiences, often with a focus on travel-related content. The podcast is characterized by its witty commentary, playful banter, and creative responses, including original poems inspired by the reviews. With a growing fan base, the show has garnered attention for its unique format and has led to live performances and merchandise. The title reflects the absurdity of negative reviews in light of humorous and unexpected critiques.
topic: Beach too Sandy, Water too Wet

text: Coalfields Road, is a Western Australian highway linking Roelands (near Bunbury) on the South Western Highway with Arthur River on the Albany Highway. It is signed as State Route 107 and is about 125 kilometres (78 mi) long.

The Coalfields Highway is a major road in Western Australia that connects the town of Collie to the town of Baker's Hill, facilitating transportation between the Perth metropolitan area and the coal mining regions. The highway spans approximately 75 kilometers and serves as an essential route for the movement of goods, particularly coal and agricultural products. Established to support local industries and improve accessibility, the highway has undergone various upgrades to enhance safety and capacity. It plays a crucial role in the economic development of the surrounding areas, providing vital links for both commercial and recreational travel.

Maritza Salas Kelly (born January 3, 1975) is a retired female track and field athlete, who competed in the sprints events during her career. She represented her native country, Puerto Rico, at the 2000 Summer Olympics, where she was eliminated in the first round of the women's 4x400 metres relay competition, alongside Militza Castro, Sandra Moya and Beatriz Cruz. Salas ran the fourth and last leg in the heat 2 race. She is the only daughter of Raul Salas and Vinetta Kelly.

Maritza Salas is a prominent figure in the field of education and community activism, recognized for her contributions to promoting equity and access in learning environments. Born in the United States to immigrant parents, she has utilized her personal experiences to advocate for underrepresented communities. Salas has worked with various educational institutions and nonprofit organizations, focusing on curriculum development and outreach programs aimed at enhancing educational opportunities for marginalized students. Her efforts have earned her accolades and recognition within the educational sector, highlighting her as a role model and leader in advancing social justice in education.

The Cabinet of Shehu Shagari formed the government of Nigeria during the presidency of Shehu Shagari between 1979 and 1983, after the return to civilian rule with the Second Nigerian Republic. Among the cabinet Ministers are Adamu Ciroma, Bello Maitama Yusuf, Mamman Ali Makele etc. It was terminated by a military coup.

The Cabinet of Shehu Shagari was the executive governing body of Nigeria under President Shehu Shagari, who served from 1979 to 1983. This cabinet was formed following Nigeria's transition to civilian rule after several years of military governance, marking the Second Republic. Shagari's administration focused on economic development, education, and infrastructure, but faced challenges including widespread corruption, economic instability, and rising social unrest. The cabinet included key figures from various political factions and aimed to promote national unity and democratic processes. However, the government was ultimately overthrown in a military coup in December 1983, which ended Shagari's presidency and the Second Republic.

"I Can Tell You About Pain" is a single by American metalcore band Converge. It was released on July 25, 2017, as a 7-inch vinyl record via Epitaph and Deathwish, Inc., with the much longer track "Eve" as a B-side. The music video for the song "I Can Tell You About Pain" was directed by Tony Wolski.
topic: I Can Tell You About Pain

text: "I Can Tell You About Pain" is a narrative non-fiction work by the author and artist, K.D. McLellan. The book explores the complexities of chronic pain through personal anecdotes, scientific insights, and emotional reflections, aiming to provide a deeper understanding of how pain affects individuals' lives. McLellan combines storytelling with illustrations to convey the multifaceted nature of suffering, the stigma surrounding it, and the importance of empathy and support for those dealing with long-term pain conditions. The work has been acknowledged for its accessibility and ability to resonate with both sufferers and caregivers, contributing to ongoing conversations about pain management and mental health.
topic: I Can Tell You About Pain

text: Macrosoma lucivittata is a moth-like butterfly in the family Hedylidae. It was described by Francis Walker in 1863.

Macrosoma lucivittata is a species of moth belonging to the family Erebidae. It was first described by the entomologist Francis Walker in 1865. This species is primarily found in the tropical regions of Southeast Asia, particularly in Malaysia and Indonesia. M. lucivittata is characterized by its distinctive coloration and patterns, which play a role in camouflage among its natural habitats. Like many moths, it is nocturnal and exhibits behaviors typical of its family, including attraction to light sources. The ecology and life cycle of M. lucivittata, like that of many related species, involve larval stages that may feed on specific host plants. Further studies are needed to fully understand its role in the ecosystem and potential conservation needs.
Ouville-la-Rivière (French pronunciation: [uvil la ʁivjɛʁ]) is a commune in the Seine-Maritime department in the Normandy region in north-western France.

Ouville-la-Rivière is a commune in the Seine-Maritime department in the Normandy region of northern France. Located in the arrondissement of Rouen, Ouville-la-Rivière is characterized by its rural landscape and agricultural activities. The village is known for its historical architecture, including traditional Norman houses and its church. The surrounding area is marked by natural beauty, featuring the nearby Seine River which influences the local ecology and economy. The commune has a small population and is part of the local administrative structure that supports regional governance and community services.

Jerome William Van Gorkom (August 6, 1917 – March 17, 1998) was a United States businessman who was U.S. Under Secretary of State for Management 1982–83. He served as the CEO of TransUnion for eighteen years. Van Gorkom is probably best known as the named defendant in the landmark corporate law case of Smith v. Van Gorkom, 488 A.2d 858 (Supreme Court of Delaware 1985), which involved the merger of TransUnion with the Marmon Group in 1980.

Jerome W. Van Gorkom is an American astronomer renowned for his contributions to the study of galaxies, particularly in the field of extragalactic astronomy. He is best known for his research on the properties of spiral galaxies and the role of dark matter in the universe. Van Gorkom has also played a significant role in developing radio astronomy techniques and instruments, contributing to the understanding of cosmic phenomena. His work has been recognized with various awards and honors in the scientific community, and he has held academic positions at institutions such as Columbia University and the University of California, Berkeley.

Jundah is an outback town and locality in the Shire of Barcoo, Queensland, Australia. Jundah is the administrative centre of the Barcoo Shire local government area. In the 2021 census, the locality of Jundah had a population of 131 people.

Jundah is a rural town located in the Barcoo Shire, Queensland, Australia. Situated approximately 900 kilometers northwest of Brisbane, it serves as a service center for the surrounding agricultural areas, predominantly focused on cattle grazing and sheep farming. The town was established during the late 19th century and has a small population, which has fluctuated over the years due to economic factors and changes in the local industry. Jundah features essential amenities such as a school, a post office, and local stores, while also hosting festivals and community events that reflect its heritage and culture. The Barcoo River, which is nearby, is a significant geographical feature of the area.

Nonverbal communication is the transmission of messages or signals through a nonverbal platform such as eye contact (oculesics), body language (kinesics), social distance (proxemics), touch (haptics), voice (prosody and paralanguage), physical environments/appearance, and use of objects. When communicating, nonverbal channels are utilized as means to convey different messages or signals, whereas others interpret these messages. The study of nonverbal communication started in 1872 with the publication of The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals by Charles Darwin. Darwin began to study nonverbal communication as he noticed the interactions between animals such as lions, tigers, dogs etc. and realized they also communicated by gestures and expressions. For the first time, nonverbal communication was studied and its relevance noted. Today, scholars argue that nonverbal communication can convey more meaning than verbal communication. In the same way that speech incorporates nonverbal components, collectively referred to as paralanguage and encompassing voice quality, rate, pitch, loudness, and speaking style, nonverbal communication also encompasses facets of one's voice. Elements such as tone, inflection, emphasis, and other vocal characteristics contribute significantly to nonverbal communication, adding layers of meaning and nuance to the conveyed message. However, much of the study of nonverbal communication has focused on interaction between individuals, where it can be classified into three principal areas: environmental conditions where communication takes place, physical characteristics of the communicators, and behaviors of communicators during interaction. Nonverbal communication involves the conscious and unconscious processes of encoding and decoding. Encoding is defined as our ability to express emotions in a way that can be accurately interpreted by the receiver(s). Decoding is called "nonverbal sensitivity", defined as the ability to take this encoded emotion and interpret its meanings accurately to what the sender intended. Encoding is the act of generating information such as facial expressions, gestures, and postures. Encoding information utilizes signals which we may think to be universal. Decoding is the interpretation of information from received sensations given by the encoder. Culture plays an important role in nonverbal communication, and it is one aspect that helps to influence how we interact with each other. In many Indigenous American communities, nonverbal cues and silence hold immense importance in deciphering the meaning of messages. In such cultures, the context, relationship dynamics, and subtle nonverbal cues play a pivotal role in communication and interpretation, impacting how learning activities are organized and understood.

Nonverbal communication refers to the transmission of messages or information without the use of words. It encompasses a broad range of behaviors and signals, including facial expressions, body language, gestures, posture, eye contact, and proxemics (the use of space). Nonverbal cues can convey emotions, attitudes, and intentions, often complementing or conflicting with verbal communication. Cultural context significantly influences nonverbal communication, as certain gestures and expressions may hold different meanings across cultures. In interpersonal interactions, nonverbal communication plays a crucial role in establishing rapport and understanding, making it an essential aspect of human interaction and communication studies.

Bert Worsley (20 September 1911 – 21 June 1971) was a professional footballer who played as a right-winger for Leeds United and Fulham F.C. Earlier in his career when he was playing for Manchester North End, in October 1931 he was signed as a professional by Bolton Wanderers.

Bert Worsley (1928 – 2022) was a British artist renowned for his contributions to the field of sculpture and ceramics. Born in England, Worsley developed a distinctive style marked by organic forms and innovative glazing techniques that reflect a deep appreciation for nature. He pursued formal education at various art institutions and gained recognition for his ability to blend traditional craftsmanship with contemporary artistry. Over his career, Worsley exhibited his works in numerous galleries and exhibitions, contributing significantly to the British ceramics movement. His pieces are held in various public and private collections, underscoring his impact on the art community. In addition to his artistic endeavors, Worsley played a role in arts education, mentoring aspiring artists throughout his lifetime.

Kerala Kalamandalam, a deemed-to-be-University of Art and Culture by the Government of India, is a major centre for learning Indian performing arts, especially those that developed in the country's southern states, with special emphasis on those from Kerala. The institution, on the banks of the Bharathapuzha river, is situated in the small town of Cheruthuruthi in Thrissur district.

Kerala Kalamandalam is a renowned cultural institution located in Cheruthuruthy, Kerala, India, founded in 1930 by the legendary artist and scholar Vallathol Narayana Menon. It specializes in the traditional performing arts of Kerala, including Kathakali, Mohiniyattam, and Koodiyattam, offering formal training and fostering the preservation and promotion of these art forms. The institution has played a significant role in reviving and institutionalizing classical arts in Kerala and has produced many acclaimed performers. In recognition of its contributions, Kerala Kalamandalam was declared a "Deemed University" in 2001 and continues to be a pivotal center for cultural education and research in India.
Walter Bransen, also Walther Bransen (6 May 1886 – 21 October 1941), was a German physician, violinist and composer. His pseudonyms were Will Rollins, Sam Fox and Karl Wetter.

Walter Bransen (born March 12, 1978) is a fictional character featured in various literature and media, portraying themes of resilience and innovation. He is often depicted as an inventor and problem-solver, engaging in adventures that highlight his scientific knowledge and moral principles. Through his journeys, Bransen navigates complex challenges, offering insights into the human condition and the impact of technology on society. His character has inspired discussions on ethics in science and the importance of creativity in overcoming obstacles.

Samuel Richard Law (born January 1850, Handsworth) was an English footballer. He played in Aston Villa's first ever FA Cup tie. The 1879–80 English football season saw Aston Villa's entry into top flight competitive association football with their first tie in the FA Cup. The start of the official record was Saturday 13 December 1879 when Villa faced Stafford Road F.C., of the Stafford Road railway works, Wolverhampton. The match was played before a crowd of 2,000 at Stafford's Half-Way House Ground. In 2024, celebrating the club's 150th anniversary, Aston Villa Legacy Numbers were introduced indexing every first-team player who had represented the club in a competitive fixture. Although playing before shirt numbers were introduced in 1926, Goalkeeper John Ball received Legacy Number 1. Half-back Law received Legacy Number #7, Andy Hunter #4, officially became Villa's first goal-scorer in the 1–1 draw. The replay was held at Villa's Wellington Road ground on 24 January 1880 with inside-forward George Ramsay #12 replacing Charlie Johnstone. Villa defeated Stafford Road 3–1. Billy Mason scored a brace with Law getting the third goal. The 32-year-old Law played his final game on 21 January 1882 in Villa's 4th Round away defeat to Wednesbury Old Athletic. He subsequently retired.

Sammy Law is a former British professional football player known for his career primarily as a midfielder. Born on December 25, 1948, Law began his youth career at Newcastle United before making his professional debut in the 1960s. Throughout his career, he played for several clubs, including Hartlepool United, where he became known for his tenacity and leadership on the field. After retiring from professional football, Law transitioned into coaching and became involved in youth development programs, contributing to the sport at grassroots levels. He is remembered for his dedication to football and his influence on younger players.
Kingi Te Ahoaho Tahiwi (1 December 1883 – 20 December 1948) was a notable New Zealand teacher, interpreter, translator, rugby official and musician.

Kingi Te Ahoaho Tahiwi (c. 1840 – 1927) was a prominent Māori leader and politician known for his advocacy for Māori rights and his contributions to social and political life in New Zealand. He belonged to the Ngāti Ruanui iwi and played a significant role during the post-colonial period, particularly in matters relating to land disputes and Māori representation. Tahiwi was actively involved in the establishment of various Māori organizations aimed at promoting the interests of his people. His leadership and dedication to fostering Māori autonomy and cultural identity were notable aspects of his legacy, and he remains a respected figure in Māori history.

First Draft is the ninth extended play by American rapper Trippie Redd, released on 30 September 2022 through 10K Projects. The project was solely made available by streaming, and serves as a teaser for the rapper's sixth commercial mixtape A Love Letter to You 5 (2023). Production was primarily handled by Igor Mamet, alongside RRAREBEAR, Angel López and Jarom Su'a. In First Draft, Trippie Redd experiments with a more introspective, minimal musical approach. One critic wrote that the EP "see[s] him going in a more acoustic direction with pained vocals." The project was well-received critically.

First Draft is an extended play (EP) by American singer-songwriter Julia Michaels, released on January 24, 2020. The EP features a collection of six tracks that showcase Michaels' distinctive pop sound and poignant lyrical style, exploring themes of love, vulnerability, and self-discovery. It includes her single "Lie Like This," which received positive reviews for its candidness and production. First Draft has been well-received by critics and fans alike, further establishing Michaels as a prominent figure in contemporary pop music.

The 35th Producers Guild of America Awards (also known as 2024 Producers Guild Awards or 2024 PGA Awards), honoring the best film and television producers of 2023, were held at the Ray Dolby Ballroom at Ovation Hollywood in Los Angeles, California on February 25, 2024. The nominees in the documentary category were announced on December 12, 2023, the nominations in the sports, children's and short-form categories were announced on December 15, 2023, and the remaining nominations for film and television were announced on January 12, 2024. The nominations for the PGA Innovation Award were announced on February 9, 2024. The winners of the sports and children's categories were revealed on February 20, 2024, in Manhattan, while the winners of the PGA Innovation Award and short-film categories were announced on February 22, 2024, during the PGA nominees celebration in Los Angeles.

The 35th Producers Guild of America (PGA) Awards took place on March 24, 2024, honoring outstanding production achievements in film and television. The ceremony celebrated the work of producers across various categories, including Best Theatrical Motion Picture, Best Animated Feature, and Outstanding Producer of Live Entertainment and Variety. The event recognized notable films and television shows released in the previous year, with a focus on excellence in production and collaboration. Award winners were determined by the members of the Producers Guild of America, showcasing industry talent and contributions to storytelling in visual media.

Mechnikov (masculine, Russian: Мечников) or Mechnikova (feminine, Russian: Мечникова) is a Russian surname. Notable people with the surname include: Ilya Ilyich Mechnikov (1845–1916), Russian zoologist I. I. Mechnikov Odessa National University Mechnikov (crater), an impact crater on the far side of the Moon Lev Mechnikov (1838–1888), Russian anarchist and geographer

Élie Metchnikoff (1845–1916) was a Russian zoologist and immunologist known for his pioneering work in the field of immunology and cellular theory of immunity. He is best remembered for his discovery of phagocytosis, a process by which certain cells engulf and digest pathogens, which laid the foundation for modern immunological research. Metchnikoff was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1908, shared with Paul Ehrlich, for their contributions to the understanding of the immune system. Additionally, he studied aging and proposed that the consumption of lactic acid bacteria could enhance health and longevity, influencing future research in microbiology and probiotics. Metchnikoff's work has had a lasting impact on biology, medicine, and the understanding of disease resistance.

Ivan Korzhev (Russian: Иван Коржев, born 24 November 1973) is a Russian artist specialising in sculpture and modern architecture. He was an author of numerous monumental, indoor, memorial, park sculpture artworks. He was also known for his architectural creations and was awarded in 2008 the title of Merited Artist of the Russian Federation.

Ivan Korzhev (born March 28, 1988) is a Russian professional football player known for his versatility on the field, primarily playing as a midfielder. He began his career in youth football at FC Spartak Moscow before moving to senior teams in the lower divisions of Russian football. Korzhev's professional debut occurred in 2006, and over subsequent years, he played for several clubs, showcasing his skills in various positions and earning recognition for his tactical awareness and work ethic. His career includes stints in the Russian Premier League, where he contributed to his teams with notable performances. Korzhev is also known for his involvement in training younger athletes and promoting sportsmanship within the football community.

Samuel Fischer Scott (1907–1988) was an African-American educator. He was the first director of the Norfolk Unit of Virginia Union University established in 1935 and located in downtown Norfolk, Virginia. He led the Norfolk unit as director from September 1935 to summer of 1938.

Samuel Fischer Scott (1854–1934) was an influential American botanist and mycologist known for his contributions to the field of plant taxonomy and mycology. He served as a professor at the University of Illinois, where he conducted extensive research on fungi, particularly within the state. Scott published numerous papers, described various species, and contributed to significant botanical collections. His work advanced the understanding of plant ecology and the relationships between different fungal species. His legacy continues to be recognized in the field of botany, particularly through his taxonomic classifications and the ongoing relevance of his research.

Wings Above the Diamantina (1936) is a detective novel by Australian writer Arthur Upfield. It was the third to feature his recurring character Detective Inspector Napoleon 'Bony' Bonaparte. It was originally published in Australia by Angus & Robertson in 1936, and subsequently serialised in The Australian Journal between January and September 1936.

"Wings Above the Diamantina" is a 1990 Australian television miniseries directed by Michael McCunnie. The narrative is set in the early 20th century and follows the life of a young man who yearns to become a pilot in the outback of Queensland. The miniseries explores themes of ambition, adventure, and the challenging realities of rural life. It features a blend of drama and romance, highlighting the tensions between personal dreams and societal expectations. The show received positive reviews for its storytelling and character development, contributing to the repertoire of Australian television at the time.
Oppomorus is a genus of sea snails, marine gastropod mollusks in the family Muricidae, the murex snails or rock snails.

Oppomorus is a genus of moths in the family Erebidae, primarily found in the Americas. It is characterized by its distinctive wing patterns and nocturnal behavior. The genus was first described by the entomologist Francis Walker in the mid-19th century. Members of this genus are often studied for their ecological roles and are of interest in the field of taxonomy due to their varied morphological traits. The classification of species within Oppomorus has undergone revisions as a result of advancements in molecular techniques, leading to ongoing research and updates in the understanding of its biodiversity.

Houtskär (Finland Swedish: [ˈhuːtɕæːr] ; Finnish: Houtskari [ˈhou̯tskɑri]) is an island group and former municipality of Finland. On 1 January 2009, it was consolidated with Iniö, Korpo, Nagu and Pargas to form the municipality of Pargas (briefly named Väståboland). It is located in the Archipelago Sea in the province of Western Finland and is part of the Southwest Finland region. The municipality had a population of 621 (as of 31 December 2008) and covered a land area of 119.93 square kilometres (46.31 sq mi). The population density was 5.18 inhabitants per square kilometre (13.4/sq mi). The municipality was bilingual with the majority (86%) being Swedish and minority Finnish speakers. There are one store in Houtskär, a bank and post office, and the church of Santa Maria in Näsby center. At various times throughout the year dances, accompanied by live music, are held which are an old tradition kept alive by both older and younger participants who dance in a variety of styles. Houtskär consists of a group of bigger islands and many smaller islands in the surrounding sea area. Communications to the mainland is by the ”Archipelago road” through Pargas, Nagu and Korpo, with three ferries considered part of the road network, the trip with the last one from Korpo to Houtskär lasting half an hour. The main islands are connected by bridges or cable ferries, the outer inhabited islands usually have daily connections with ship-like ferries.

Houtskär is an archipelago and municipality located in the Turku Archipelago region of Finland. It consists of several islands, with the largest being Houtskär Island. The municipality has a population of approximately 800 residents, primarily Swedish-speaking. Houtskär is known for its natural beauty, maritime culture, and history, being popular among tourists for boating, fishing, and outdoor activities. The area is also characterized by its traditional wooden houses and cultural heritage, reflecting the long-standing Scandinavian influence in the region. Houtskär was established as a municipality in 1865 and has since maintained its unique identity within Finland's administrative framework.
The 2012 season was Geylang United FC's 18th consecutive season in the S.League. The club also competed in the Singapore Cup and the Singapore League Cup.

The 2012 Geylang United FC season was the club's 21st year in the Singapore S.League and their 12th consecutive season in the top tier of Singaporean football. Under the management of both V. Sundramoorthy, who took over mid-season, the team aimed to improve upon their previous season's performance. Geylang United finished the campaign in 9th place in the S.League, facing challenges with consistency and securing points. The club also competed in the Singapore League Cup and the Singapore FA Cup but had limited success in these tournaments. Player performances, coaching strategies, and key matches were pivotal in shaping the team's season outcome.

"Mixed-Up Confusion" is a song written and recorded by Bob Dylan and released as his first single. The song was recorded with an electric band on November 14, 1962, during the sessions for The Freewheelin' Bob Dylan but was not used on that album, which, aside from "Corrina, Corrina", was entirely acoustic. Instead the song, backed with "Corrina, Corrina" (a different take from the Freewheelin' one, still unreleased), a traditional blues song, appeared as Dylan's first single, released in the United States on December 14, 1962, as Columbia 4-42656. According to legend, Dylan wrote the song in a cab on the way to the Columbia studios for the recording session. A different version of the song, recorded on November 1, 1962, with later overdubbing, was released on the compilation album Masterpieces in 1978 and on the original 1985 issue of the Biograph box set; the 1997 reissue of Biograph includes a stereo version of the released single, recorded on November 14, 1962, in place of this alternative version. Olof Björner's website lists all the different takes of this song, recorded by Dylan in October and November 1962.

"Mixed-Up Confusion" is a song written by Bob Dylan and released in 1962 as part of his debut album, "Bob Dylan." The song is characterized by its folk style and features Dylan's signature poetic lyrics, exploring themes of love, confusion, and longing. It reflects the emotional turmoil of a relationship in disarray. The track has garnered attention for its vivid storytelling and has been covered by various artists over the years, contributing to Dylan's reputation as a pioneering figure in modern music.
