Martin G. Houle (born February 12, 1985) is a Canadian former professional ice hockey goaltender who played in one game in the National Hockey League (NHL) for the Philadelphia Flyers during the 2006–07 season.

Martin Houle is a Canadian professional ice hockey goaltender, known for his tenure in the American Hockey League (AHL) and East Coast Hockey League (ECHL). Born on January 23, 1984, in Longueuil, Quebec, he was selected 97th overall by the Ottawa Senators in the 2002 NHL Entry Draft. Houle played junior hockey in the Quebec Major Junior Hockey League (QMJHL) before embarking on a professional career that included stints with various teams across North America, including the Binghamton Senators and the Trenton Titans. Throughout his career, he was recognized for his athleticism and skill as a goaltender. After retirement, Houle transitioned into coaching and player development roles within the sport.
Nicolás Capaldo Taboas (born 14 September 1998) is an Argentine professional footballer who plays as a midfielder for Bundesliga club Hamburger SV.

Nicolás Capaldo is an Argentine professional footballer born on March 24, 1999, in Villa María, Argentina. He primarily plays as a midfielder and is known for his versatility on the field. Capaldo developed his career in the youth system of Boca Juniors and made his senior debut for the club in 2018. He gained attention for his performances in domestic leagues and international competitions. In 2021, he was loaned to the Spanish club, Real Betis, where he continued to develop his skills. Capaldo has also represented Argentina at various youth levels, showcasing his potential as a promising talent in Argentine football.
Perenethis sindica is a species of spider of the genus Perenethis. It is found in India, Sri Lanka, Nepal, China, and the Philippines.

Perenethis sindica is a species of moth belonging to the family Geometridae, commonly known as geometer moths. It is native to Sindh, a region in Pakistan. Like other members of its family, Perenethis sindica is characterized by its wings that exhibit varying patterns and colors, which assist in camouflage. The life cycle of this moth, including its larval stages and feeding habits, is typical of Geometridae, where larvae are commonly referred to as inchworms due to their distinctive looping movement. Further research may be required to fully understand its ecological role and conservation status.

Bendita seas is a 1956 Argentine film. A drama directed by Luis Mottura, script by Eliseo Montaine and María Luz Regás based on the theater play by Alberto Novión. The movie was released on March 8, 1956.

"Bendita seas" is a Spanish expression that translates to "Blessed be you" in English. It is often used to express gratitude or admiration towards someone, typically in a heartfelt or affectionate manner. The phrase is commonly found in various cultural contexts, particularly in Spanish-speaking countries, and may appear in literature, songs, and everyday conversation. The expression emphasizes positivity and appreciation, serving as a form of blessing or compliment.

Scotia was a barque that was built in 1872 as the Norwegian whaler Hekla. She was purchased in 1902 by William Speirs Bruce and refitted as a research vessel for use by the Scottish National Antarctic Expedition. After the expedition, she served as a sealer, patrol vessel and collier. She was destroyed by fire in January 1916.

Scotia was a barque launched in 1845 in Saint John, New Brunswick, Canada. Known for its robust construction and sailing capabilities, Scotia was primarily used for commercial trade routes, transporting goods across the Atlantic Ocean. Over the years, it became involved in various notable voyages, contributing to maritime trade in the 19th century. In the mid-1850s, Scotia encountered changing economic conditions and eventually fell into disrepair. The ship's history highlights the role of sailing vessels in the economic development of Canada during the period. Today, Scotia is remembered as a representative example of wooden shipbuilding and maritime commerce of the era.
Uğurtepe is a village in the Musabeyli District, Kilis Province, Turkey. The village had a population of 46 in 2022.

Uğurtepe is a village located in the Musabeyli District of Gaziantep Province, Turkey. Situated in the southeastern region of the country, Uğurtepe is characterized by its agricultural landscape and traditional rural lifestyle. The village is part of a region known for its rich cultural heritage and historical significance, with various archeological sites and landmarks nearby. The population primarily engages in farming and livestock raising, reflecting the area's agrarian economy. Uğurtepe plays a role in the local community dynamics within Musabeyli, contributing to the socio-economic fabric of the district.

Ash Kalra (born January 21, 1972) is a Canadian-American politician who has served as a member of the California State Assembly since 2016. A member of the Democratic Party, Kalra previously served on the San Jose City Council from 2008 to 2016. Kalra represents the 25th Assembly District, which encompasses parts of eastern San Jose, and chairs the California Legislative Progressive Caucus and the Assembly Labor and Employment Committee. A first-generation immigrant, he is the first Hindu and Indian American to serve in the California State Legislature.

Ash Kalra is an American politician and lawyer serving as a member of the California State Assembly, representing the 27th Assembly District since 2016. He is a member of the Democratic Party and has held several leadership positions within the Assembly, including serving as the Chair of the Assembly Judiciary Committee. Kalra's legislative focus includes criminal justice reform, environmental sustainability, and affordable housing. Prior to his election to the Assembly, he served on the San Jose City Council. Kalra is known for his advocacy for marginalized communities and his efforts to address issues such as immigration reform and healthcare access.

Wanås Castle (IPA: [ˈvânːoːs]; Swedish: Wanås slott) is an estate in Östra Göinge Municipality, Scania, in southern Sweden. It is situated to the west of Knislinge, approximately 20 kilometres (12 mi) north of Kristianstad.

Wanås Castle is a historic castle located in southern Sweden, near the village of Wanås in Skåne County. Originally constructed in the late 15th century as a defensive fortification, it has undergone significant renovations and expansions over the centuries, transforming into a stately residence. Wanås Castle is noted for its well-preserved architecture and beautiful surrounding landscapes, including a large park featuring contemporary art installations. The estate is now private property but is open to the public for tours and cultural events, making it a popular destination for both tourists and locals. The castle holds historical significance and is part of the broader cultural heritage of Sweden.
Louis Johnson (March 19, 1930 – March 31, 2020) was an American dancer, choreographer, teacher, and director whose work spanned ballet and modern dance.

Louis Johnson (dancer) is an American dancer and choreographer known for his innovative contributions to contemporary dance. He gained prominence in the 1980s as a member of several influential dance companies and has collaborated with renowned choreographers. Johnson is recognized for blending various dance styles, including hip-hop, jazz, and modern dance, resulting in a unique performance aesthetic. In addition to his stage work, he has taught dance internationally and served as a mentor for aspiring dancers. His contributions to the field have earned him accolades and recognition within the dance community.
Sansikhara is a census town in Dhanbad CD block in Dhanbad Sadar subdivision of Dhanbad district in the Indian state of Jharkhand.

Sansikhara is a term used in Buddhist philosophy, particularly in the context of the doctrine of dependent origination (pratītyasamutpāda). It refers to the mental formations or volitional actions that influence an individual's karma and contribute to the cycle of birth, death, and rebirth (samsara). In the Abhidharma tradition, sansikhara is categorized as one of the five aggregates (skandhas) that constitute a sentient being, specifically related to the activities of the mind and the intentions that shape one's experiences. The concept underscores the role of intention in moral actions and the formation of character, emphasizing that unwholesome sansikharas can lead to suffering, while wholesome ones can promote enlightenment and liberation from the cyclical nature of existence.

Luka Tunjić (born 19 November 2005) is a professional football player who plays as an attacking midfielder for Fortuna Sittard. Born in Germany and a former youth representative for Bosnia and Herzegovina, he is a youth international for Croatia.

Luka Tunjić is a Croatian professional footballer born on September 21, 1998, in Osijek, Croatia. Primarily playing as a forward, he began his career in the youth system of NK Osijek before making his senior debut with the club. Tunjić has since played for several teams in the Croatian leagues, showcasing strong performance that has attracted attention from domestic and international scouts. Known for his technical skills and goal-scoring ability, he continues to develop as a player in competitive leagues, contributing to both his team's performance and his individual growth in the sport.

Gucheng (simplified Chinese: 古城镇; traditional Chinese: 古城鎮; pinyin: Gǔchéng Zhèn) is a town in Fuchuan Yao Autonomous County, Guangxi, China. As of the 2018 census it had a population of 20,600 and an area of 42.65 square kilometres (16.47 sq mi).

Gucheng is a town located in Fuchuan County, Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region, China. It serves as the administrative center of the county and is known for its rich cultural heritage and scenic landscapes. Gucheng is predominantly inhabited by the Zhuang ethnic group, and the town plays a significant role in the local economy through agriculture and tourism, attracting visitors with its traditional festivals and natural beauty. The infrastructure of Gucheng includes various schools, health facilities, and local markets, supporting the community's needs and enhancing its development.

Mobile payment, also referred to as mobile money, mobile money transfer and mobile wallet, is any of various payment processing services operated under financial regulations and performed from or via a mobile device. Instead of paying with cash, cheque, or credit card, a consumer can use a payment app on a mobile device to pay for a wide range of services and digital or hard goods. Although the concept of using non-coin-based currency systems has a long history, it is only in the 21st century that the technology to support such systems has become widely available. Mobile payments were adopted in Japan in the 2000s and later all over the world in different ways. The first patent exclusively defined "Mobile Payment System" was filed in 2000. In a developing country, mobile payment solutions can be deployed as a means of extending services of financial institutions to the community known as the "unbanked" or "underbanked", which is estimated to be as much as 50 percent of the world's adult population, according to the Financial Access 2009 Report "Half the World is Unbanked". Such payment networks are often used for micropayments. A recent 2025 systematic review finds that digital payment technologies—including mobile payments and mobile money—significantly expand financial inclusion by enabling access to financial services among underserved and unbanked populations worldwide. A 2025 empirical study from Saudi Arabia further shows that the expansion of digital financial services through mobile payment platforms can enhance financial inclusion and support broader socioeconomic sustainability goals in the country. The use of mobile payments in developing countries has attracted public and private funding by organizations such as the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, the United States Agency for International Development, and Mercy Corps. Mobile payments are becoming a key instrument for payment service providers (PSPs) and other market participants, in order to achieve new growth opportunities, according to the European Payments Council (EPC). The EPC states that "new technology solutions provide a direct improvement to the operations efficiency, ultimately resulting in cost savings and in an increase in business volume".

Mobile payment refers to the process of making financial transactions through mobile devices such as smartphones and tablets. This payment method allows users to pay for goods and services, transfer money, or perform banking activities using applications or specialized software. Mobile payments may utilize various technologies, including Near Field Communication (NFC), QR codes, and mobile wallets, with popular platforms including Apple Pay, Google Pay, and Samsung Pay. The adoption of mobile payments has grown significantly, largely due to the increasing use of smartphones, enhanced security measures, and a global shift toward digital transactions. Mobile payment systems are supported by numerous industries, including retail, transportation, and e-commerce, promoting convenience and efficiency in financial exchanges.

Aragami (Japanese: 荒神, Hepburn: Kōjin; also known as Aragami: The Raging God of Battle) is a 2003 Japanese action film directed by Ryuhei Kitamura. It was Kitamura's contribution to the Duel Project, a challenge issued by producer Shinya Kawai to him and fellow director Yukihiko Tsutsumi to film a feature-length movie with only two actors, battling in one setting, in only the time frame of one week.

"Aragami" is a Japanese action horror film released in 2003, directed by Ryuhei Kitamura. The film centers around two warriors who engage in a fierce battle within a mysterious temple. As the story unfolds, the protagonist, a skilled swordsman, confronts an ancient, supernatural entity known as Aragami, which embodies the essence of wrath and revenge. The film explores themes of honor, destiny, and the duality of man, blending elements of martial arts and horror. "Aragami" is notable for its stylistic visual presentation and intense action sequences, showcasing Kitamura's signature direction. The film has garnered a cult following and is recognized for its unique take on traditional samurai narratives.

704Games Company (formerly known as Dusenberry Martin Racing) is an American video game developer and publisher based in Charlotte, North Carolina. The company acquired the license to be the exclusive developer of NASCAR video games in January 2015 and has since released six console games and a mobile game. The company has a long-standing relationship with the National Association for Stock Car Auto Racing (NASCAR) and has the exclusive rights to develop and publish video games featuring the NASCAR brand.

704Games is an American video game developer and publisher, known for producing the NASCAR Heat series of racing video games. Established in 2015, the company focuses on creating immersive racing experiences that cater to fans of the NASCAR franchise. 704Games operates as a subsidiary of Motorsport Games and has released several titles for various platforms, including PC, PlayStation, and Xbox. The studio's games feature realistic graphics, enhanced gameplay mechanics, and official licensing from NASCAR, which allows them to include real teams, cars, and tracks.
Rossino Mantovano (Latin: Rossinus Mantuanus "Rossino of Mantua") (fl. 1505–1511) was an Italian singer and composer.

Rossino Mantovano (born 1927) is an Italian singer and songwriter, known for his contributions to the traditional Italian music scene. He gained popularity in the mid-20th century with his distinctive voice and emotive performances, often focusing on themes of love, nostalgia, and Italian culture. Mantovano's work includes a variety of musical styles, but he is particularly recognized for his interpretations of folk and romantic songs. Throughout his career, he has released numerous albums and singles, earning a dedicated fan base and contributing to the preservation of Italian musical heritage.

Five men known as sons of Yagbe'u Seyon ruled as Emperor of Ethiopia in succession between 1294 and 1299. Their names were: Seyfa Ared (Amharic: ሰይፈ አርድ ፬ኛ) (1294–1295) (Throne name – Bahr Asgad) Hezba Asgad (Amharic: ሕዝበ አስግድ) (1295–1296) Qedma Asgad (Amharic: ቅድመ አስግድ) (1296–1297) Jin Asgad (Amharic: ጅን አስግድ) (1297–1298) Saba Asgad (Amharic: ሳባ አስግድ) (1298–1299) Though later tradition remembered them as sons of Yagbe'u Seyon, their actual relationship is not clear, though they did succeed him.

The Sons of Yagbe'u Seyon, also known as the Sons of Yagbe'u, were prominent figures in the history of the Ethiopian Empire during the late medieval period. They were the offspring of Yagbe'u Seyon, who served as a military leader and a regent during the reign of Emperor Menelik II. The sons played significant roles in the expansion and consolidation of the empire, participating in various military campaigns and the internal politics of Ethiopia. Their influence extended into the 19th century, contributing to the complex dynamics of Ethiopian nobility and imperial governance. The legacy of the Sons of Yagbe'u Seyon is reflective of the broader historical context of Ethiopian resistance to foreign invasions, particularly during the Scramble for Africa.

The 1946 Wichita Shockers football team, sometimes known as the Wheatshockers, was an American football team that represented the Wichita University (now known as Wichita State University) as a member of the Missouri Valley Conference during the 1946 college football season. In its second season under head coach Ralph Graham, the team compiled a 5–5 record (2–1 against conference opponents), finished second out of five teams in the MVC, and was outscored opponents by a total of 135 to 119. The team played its home games at Veterans Field, now known as Cessna Stadium. The 1946 season was the first for Wichita after being classified as a "major college" football program.

The 1946 Wichita Shockers football team represented Wichita University (now Wichita State University) during the 1946 college football season. They played as a member of the Missouri Valley Conference (MVC) and finished the season with a record of 4 wins and 4 losses. The team was led by head coach John B. McDill, marking his first season in that role. Home games were held at the old Cessna Stadium in Wichita, Kansas. The 1946 season was significant as it marked the return of college football after World War II, with the Shockers aiming to rebuild their program and attract fans to the newly resumed competition.

Sing is an American media franchise created by Garth Jennings and produced by Illumination. It features the voice talents of Matthew McConaughey, Reese Witherspoon, Scarlett Johansson, Nick Kroll, Taron Egerton, and Tori Kelly among others. The first film, Sing, was released on December 21, 2016, and received positive reviews from critics. The second film, Sing 2, was released on December 22, 2021. The series has grossed $1 billion worldwide. The franchise takes place in a world inhabited by talking, anthropomorphic animals. It follows a koala named Buster Moon who, with the help of other animals, puts on live theater shows that largely encompass singing.

The "Sing" franchise is an animated film series produced by Illumination Entertainment. It debuted with the release of the film "Sing" in 2016, directed by Garth Jennings, which centers on a koala named Buster Moon who organizes a singing competition to save his struggling theater. The film features a star-studded voice cast, including Matthew McConaughey, Reese Witherspoon, and Scarlett Johansson, and received positive reviews for its animation, humor, and musical performances. A sequel, "Sing 2," was released in December 2021, continuing the story of Buster Moon and his group of singing animals as they aim to impress a talent scout. The franchise has also expanded to include various merchandise, video games, and adaptations, capturing a wide audience with its themes of perseverance and the celebration of music.
Susann is a given name and surname, a variant of Susan. Notable persons with that name include:

Susann is a given name that can be a variation of the name Susan, which has roots in Hebrew, meaning "lily" or "rose." The name has been popular throughout different cultures and languages and can also appear as Susanne or Susannah. Several notable individuals bear the name Susann, including the American author Jacqueline Susann, known for her best-selling novels in the 1960s, particularly "Valley of the Dolls." The name is used across various countries, often reflecting cultural adaptations and spellings. In addition to personal names, Susann may also refer to various places or entities, especially in contexts where the name has cultural significance.
Barbara Stöckl (born 24 October 1956) is an Austrian cross-country skier. She competed in two events at the 1976 Winter Olympics.

Barbara Stöckl is a retired Austrian cross-country skier who competed in international events primarily in the 1980s. Born on February 2, 1961, Stöckl represented Austria at various World Championships and showcased her talent in numerous races, including distances ranging from sprints to long-distance events. She is known for her achievements in the FIS Cross-Country World Cup, where she earned notable results during her active years. Stöckl's contributions to the sport, along with her competitive spirit, have made her a recognized figure in the history of Austrian skiing.
Stanley Freeman (April 3, 1920 – January 13, 2001) was an American composer, pianist, lyricist, musical arranger, conductor, and studio musician.

Stan Freeman (born July 31, 1934) is a notable American computer scientist and educator, recognized for his contributions to the fields of artificial intelligence and software engineering. He served as a professor at the University of Silicon Valley, where he played a significant role in advancing computer science curricula. Freeman is also known for authoring several influential papers and books on algorithm design and machine learning. Throughout his career, he has been involved in various research projects and has collaborated with industry leaders, contributing to the development of innovative technologies. In addition to his academic work, Freeman has been an advocate for ethical standards in technology, emphasizing the importance of responsible AI development.

The 2006 State Twenty20 Cricket Knock-Out Tournament was a domestic Twenty20 tournament in New Zealand. The tournament was held in Invercargill on 10-12 November 2006. A prize of $30,000 was on offer for the winners of the tournament. Teams representing the six senior cricket associations of New Zealand (Auckland, Northern Districts, Wellington, Central Districts, Canterbury and Otago) took part, but owing to inclement weather only 4 of the 9 scheduled matches took place.

The 2006 State Twenty20 Cricket Knock-Out Tournament was the inaugural edition of a domestic Twenty20 cricket competition held in Australia, organized by the Cricket Australia. The tournament took place in January 2006 and featured teams representing various Australian states. It aimed to promote the shorter format of the game and enhance the popularity of cricket in Australia. The competition followed a knockout format, culminating in a final match to determine the champion. The tournament saw significant participation and contributed to the growth of Twenty20 cricket in Australia, setting the stage for future competitions in the format.
Deggenhausertal is a municipality in the district of Bodensee in Baden-Württemberg in Germany.

Deggenhausertal is a municipality in the Bodensee district of Baden-Württemberg, Germany. It is located in the scenic region of Lake Constance and is characterized by its picturesque landscapes and rural charm. The area is known for its various hiking and cycling trails, agricultural activities, and traditional Swabian architecture. Deggenhausertal is composed of several villages and hamlets, contributing to its small-community atmosphere. The local economy primarily relies on agriculture, tourism, and small businesses. The municipality also promotes cultural heritage through local festivals and events that highlight its history and community spirit.

Lhasa Converter Station (Chinese: 拉萨换流站, 拉萨±400千伏换流站) is the first ±400 kV converter station designed for high-altitude and cold-climate conditions. It is the largest of its kind, situated in Langtang Village, Lhünzhub County, Lhasa City, at an altitude of 3,825 meters. The station officially began operations on December 9, 2011, marking a significant milestone in addressing the power supply challenges faced by the Tibetan power grid at the time.

The Lhasa Converter Station is a key infrastructure facility located in Lhasa, Tibet, operated by the State Grid Corporation of China. It serves as a crucial component of the China–Tibet Power Transmission Project, which aims to enhance electricity distribution and improve energy stability in the region. The converter station is designed to convert high-voltage direct current (HVDC) from the Tibetan power grid into alternating current (AC), enabling efficient electricity transmission over long distances. The facility plays a significant role in harnessing and distributing the electrical resources of Tibet, contributing to the region's economic development and integration with the national grid.

Fernando Herrera Ávila (born 6 August 1968) is a Mexican politician affiliated with the PAN. He currently serves as Senator of the LXII Legislature of the Mexican Congress representing Aguascalientes. He also served as deputy of the Chamber of Deputies during the 2000-2003 legislature, and previously in the Congress of Aguascalientes.

Fernando Herrera Ávila (born September 13, 1973) is a Mexican politician affiliated with the Institutional Revolutionary Party (PRI). He has served in various political roles and is known for his contributions to regional development and local governance in Mexico. Herrera has garnered attention for his commitment to social issues and public policy reform, particularly in areas related to education and health. His political career includes positions as a local representative and involvement in several civic projects aimed at improving community welfare.

George is a city in Grant County, Washington, United States. The population was 809 at the 2020 census. The "humorous homage" to President George Washington has landed George on lists of unusual place names. The city is known for being near The Gorge Amphitheatre, sometimes called "The Gorge at George". The Gorge Amphitheatre was the location of the annual Sasquatch! Music Festival. The city also celebrates national holidays such as the Fourth of July, and Washington's Birthday, with cherry pies. The world's largest cherry pie is also baked every year on July 4, and served to a crowd.

George Washington (February 22, 1732 – December 14, 1799) was an American military leader, statesman, and Founding Father who served as the first President of the United States from 1789 to 1797. He played a pivotal role in the American Revolutionary War, leading the Continental Army to victory against British forces and earning a reputation for his leadership and military strategy. After the war, Washington presided over the Constitutional Convention of 1787, which resulted in the drafting of the United States Constitution. He is often referred to as the "Father of His Country" for his contributions to the founding of the nation and the establishment of its government. Washington declined to seek a third term, setting a precedent for future presidents, and is remembered for his commitment to republicanism and civic virtue. His legacy includes the establishment of the presidency and the promotion of national unity.

Russell Wilson (born 17 March 1959, in Blackpool) is a former English cricketer. He was a right-handed batsman and right-arm medium-fast bowler who played for Wiltshire. Having made his Minor Counties Championship debut for the team in 1979, he made his only List A appearance during the 1983 NatWest Trophy, against Northamptonshire. From the tailend, he scored 9 not out.

Russell Wilson was an English cricketer born on December 4, 1866, in Birmingham, England. He was a right-handed batsman and played first-class cricket primarily for Worcestershire during the late 19th century. Wilson made his debut in 1888 and was active in the sport until 1899, participating in 79 first-class matches. He was known for his batting skills, with a highest score of 142 runs in a single innings. Wilson also had brief stints in minor counties cricket. He passed away on January 17, 1929.

Emanuel Hirsch (14 June 1888 in Bentwisch, Province of Brandenburg – 17 July 1972 in Göttingen) was a German Protestant theologian and also a member of the Nazi Party and a number of Nazi-supporting organizations. He escaped denazification at the end of the war by quitting his professorship, allegedly for health reasons, losing the pension from his University. Hirsch studied at the Humboldt University of Berlin where his teachers were Karl Holl and Adolf von Harnack. He earned his PhD with the thesis Fichte's Philosophy of Religion in the context of his overall philosophical development (in German: Fichtes Religionsphilosophie im Rahmen der philosophischen Gesamtentwicklung Fichtes). Hirsch was a member of the Wingolf student fraternity. He was a professor at Göttingen University (1921–1945). Shortly after the Nazi seizure of power he wrote: No other people of the world has a statesman who is so serious about Christendom; when Adolf Hitler concluded his great speech on May 1st with a prayer everybody could feel the wonderful candor therein. In 1933 Hirsch signed the Vow of allegiance of the Professors of the German Universities and High-Schools to Adolf Hitler and the National Socialistic State. Emanuel Hirsch joined the NSDAP in 1937 and also became a Patron Member of the SS and joined the National Socialist People's Welfare. Hirsch was a leader of the German Christians and an advisor of Reich Bishop Ludwig Müller.

Emanuel Hirsch (1888–1972) was a prominent German philosopher and theologian known for his contributions to philosophy of religion and ethics. He was a significant figure in the development of neo-Kantian philosophy and engaged deeply with existentialist thought. Hirsch's work often explored the intersections of faith, reason, and moral responsibility, emphasizing the importance of individual autonomy in ethical decision-making. Throughout his academic career, he held various teaching positions at several universities, influencing a generation of scholars and students. His writings remain a subject of study for those interested in religious philosophy and the dialogue between faith and reason.
MSC Pocking is a German motorcycle speedway team based in Pocking, in the district of Passau, in Lower Bavaria, Germany.

MSC Pocking is a German container ship operated by Mediterranean Shipping Company (MSC). Launched in 2003, it is primarily used for transporting cargo across various international shipping routes. The vessel is named after the town of Pocking in Bavaria, Germany, and is part of MSC's fleet that includes numerous container ships of varying sizes and specifications. With a deadweight tonnage of approximately 8,700 tons and a length of 134 meters, MSC Pocking is designed for efficiency in cargo handling and fuel consumption. The ship embodies modern maritime technology, contributing to MSC’s position as one of the world's largest shipping lines.

Globovisión is a 24-hour television news network. It broadcasts over-the-air in Caracas, Aragua, Carabobo and Zulia on UHF channel 33. Globovisión is seen in the rest of Venezuela on cable or satellite (Globovisión has an alliance with DirecTV, where it can be seen on channel 110) and worldwide from their website. Some of Globovisión's programs can be seen in the United States on cable network Canal Sur and TV Venezuela, a channel offered in DirecTV's Para Todos package. In Latin America, Globovision can be seen in Argentina, Colombia, Chile, Ecuador, Peru, Uruguay and other territories as Aruba, Trinidad and Tobago, Barbados and Curaçao in DirecTV's package (channels 293 and 724).

Globovisión is a Venezuelan news television channel that was founded in 1994. It is known for its focus on news, political commentary, and economic issues, often providing a critical perspective on the Venezuelan government. The channel has played a significant role in the media landscape of Venezuela, particularly during periods of political unrest. Over the years, Globovisión has faced various challenges, including government pressure, legal issues, and changes in ownership. Despite these challenges, it continues to be a prominent source of news and information in the country.
Clavus biancae is a species of sea snail, a marine gastropod mollusk in the family Drilliidae.

Clavus biancae is a species of sea snail in the family Drilliidae, a group of marine gastropod mollusks. This species is characterized by a slender, elongated shell with distinctive spiral ridges and a pointed apex. Clavus biancae is found in marine environments, notably in the waters off the coast of Australia and in the Indo-Pacific region. The species was first described in 1999 by researchers B. A. Marshall and has since been recognized for its unique morphological features. Clavus biancae contributes to the biodiversity of marine ecosystems where it resides.
Joel Cox (born April 2, 1942) is an American film editor. He is best known for collaborating with Clint Eastwood in 33 films.

Joel Cox is an American film editor and director known for his extensive collaboration with filmmaker Clint Eastwood. He has edited numerous films, including "Unforgiven," "Mystic River," and "Gran Torino," contributing to the critical and commercial success of these projects. Cox's work has garnered him several awards and nominations, including Academy Awards and BAFTA Awards, solidifying his reputation in the film industry. In addition to editing, he made his directorial debut with the 2010 film "Hereafter," showcasing his versatility in the realm of filmmaking.

Pavel Stepanovich Molchanov (Belarusian: Павел Сцяпанавіч Малчанаў; 14 March 1902 – 24 February 1977) was a Soviet and Belarusian stage and film actor and theater director. People's Artist of the USSR (1948).

Pavel Molchanov is a Russian actor known for his versatile performances in film and television. He was born on August 21, 1987, in Moscow, Russia, and gained recognition for his work in various genres, ranging from drama to comedy. Molchanov graduated from the Russian Academy of Theatre Arts and began his career in the early 2000s, quickly establishing himself in the Russian entertainment industry. He has appeared in numerous television series and films, earning accolades for his acting skills and contributions to contemporary Russian cinema. In addition to his acting career, Molchanov is also involved in theater and has participated in various stage productions.

Cynthia Mirella Ore (born 1970s) is a Maryland woman who gained brief notoriety in the second half of 2005 and during the 2006 midterm election, as a result of her widely publicized extramarital affair with Pennsylvania's 10th congressional district Representative Don Sherwood. She subsequently accused Sherwood of abusing her during their relationship. In court documents and press interviews, Ore said she met Congressman Sherwood at a Young Republicans meeting in September 1999. Ore was 23 years old and Sherwood was 58 when they began a "close romantic and intimate personal relationship", according to a 2005 filing by Ore's attorney. Their five-year extramarital affair ended after Ore described several instances of physical abuse by the congressman. On the afternoon of September 15, 2004, Ore locked herself inside the bathroom of Sherwood's Washington, D.C. apartment. She used her cell phone to make an emergency call to police. When authorities arrived, Ore reported that she had been choked by Sherwood. The congressman told police he was only giving her a backrub. Initially, no charges were filed. Their relationship soon ended—though Ore has stated that Sherwood remained in contact with her. Sherwood initially denied the affair, stating that Ore was merely a "casual acquaintance". After a police report was leaked to the press in 2005, Sherwood publicly admitted his lengthy affair with Ore, but denied her reports of abuse. Ore filed a $5.5 million lawsuit against Sherwood in June 2005. The suit was settled out of court on November 8, 2005, but the terms of the monetary settlement have never been made public. In an interview with a Wilkes-Barre newspaper, Ore said she was born to a wealthy family and grew up in Peru. Various news stories in 2005 and 2006 gave her age as 29 and mentioned in passing that, in 2001, she had been an intern for U.S. Representative Bill Shuster, another Pennsylvania Republican, but left after two days. Newspapers also reported that she was said to be taking business classes at Johns Hopkins University and, in 2006, had changed her name.

Cynthia Ore is an American artist and educator known for her work in painting and mixed media. Her art often explores themes of identity, memory, and perception, utilizing vibrant colors and intricate compositions. Ore has exhibited her work in various galleries across the United States and has received several awards for her contributions to contemporary art. In addition to her artistic practice, she is involved in art education, teaching courses and workshops aimed at promoting creativity and artistic skills among students of all ages. Ore's work has been featured in notable publications and she is recognized for her engagement with the art community.

Myosotis cheesemanii is a species of flowering plant in the family Boraginaceae, endemic to the South Island of New Zealand. Donald Petrie described the species in 1886. Plants of this species of forget-me-not are perennial rosettes with bracteate inflorescences and white corollas.

Myosotis cheesemanii, commonly known as Cheeseman's forget-me-not, is a species of flowering plant in the family Boraginaceae. Native to New Zealand, it is characterized by its small, bright blue flowers and hairy leaves. The plant typically grows in alpine and subalpine habitats, often found in moist, well-drained soils. M. cheesemanii is known for its significance in local ecosystems as well as its ornamental value, contributing to biodiversity in its natural range. It is occasionally cultivated in gardens for its attractive flowers. Conservation efforts may be necessary due to habitat loss and environmental changes affecting its distribution.

RSPB The Lodge is a nature reserve run by the Royal Society for the Protection of Birds (RSPB), named after the building there, The Lodge, which is their headquarters. It is located south-east of the town of Sandy, Bedfordshire, in England.

The Lodge RSPB reserve is a nature reserve located in Sandy, Bedfordshire, England, managed by the Royal Society for the Protection of Birds (RSPB). Spanning approximately 200 hectares, it includes a diverse range of habitats such as heathland, woodland, grassland, and wetlands, providing a sanctuary for various bird species and other wildlife. Established in 1965, The Lodge serves both as a conservation area and a visitor center, offering facilities for educational programs, walking trails, and wildlife observation. It is particularly known for its populations of Nightjar, Woodlark, and various migratory birds, making it a prominent site for birdwatching and nature enthusiasts. The reserve plays a crucial role in habitat conservation and biodiversity within the region.
Hylephila is a genus of skippers in the family Hesperiidae.

Hylephila is a genus of moths in the family Erebidae, which is part of the order Lepidoptera. This genus includes several species, primarily distributed in tropical and subtropical regions. Hylephila moths are characterized by their distinctive wing patterns and coloration, which often play a role in camouflage and signaling. The classification of Hylephila has been subject to revision, and it is closely related to other genera within the Erebidae family. As with many moths, the life cycle of Hylephila includes a larval stage (caterpillar) that can have significant interactions with their environments, including host plants. Research on Hylephila contributes to the broader understanding of moth diversity and ecology.

Türst (German: [tʏʁst]) is a legendary folkloric figure from the agricultural communities of Lucerne, dating to the pre-Christian era. He is described as a "dreadful huntsman", of whom people should be wary in stormy weather. Türst blows his hunting horn through villages in the tempestuous months preceding Epiphany, accompanied by a baying pack of three-legged hunting dogs. Specific beliefs about him vary from region to region.

Türst is a term that may refer to different contexts depending on cultural or linguistic usage. In some contexts, it is associated with a term used in various languages for "door" or "gate." It can also appear in historical or regional texts, signifying an architectural element or symbolizing a threshold in various ceremonial practices. In certain folklore or mythology, Türst may represent a transition point or a boundary between different realms. The interpretation of the term can vary significantly depending on the historical, cultural, or geographical context in which it is used. Further detailed exploration and references may be necessary to fully understand its implications and meanings in specific traditions.
Mats Johan Edward Bångsbo (born 10 February 2003) is a Swedish professional footballer who plays for UAE Pro League club Al Jazira as a centre-back.

Johan Bångsbo is a Swedish artist known for his innovative contributions to contemporary art, particularly in mixed media and installation works. His pieces often explore themes of identity, memory, and the human experience, combining traditional techniques with modern technology. Bångsbo has exhibited in various galleries and art festivals across Europe, earning recognition for his thought-provoking narratives and visual aesthetics. He is associated with several artist collectives and engages in collaborative projects that challenge conventional art forms. Bångsbo continues to influence the Swedish art scene through his exhibitions, workshops, and community involvement.
A Glagwa is a bell-shaped shield of the Wandala peoples of northern Cameroon.

Glagwa is a traditional ceremonial drink originating from the cultural practices of Slavic peoples, particularly among communities in Eastern Europe. Typically made from fermented grains or fruits, Glagwa is often served during festive occasions and significant rituals, symbolizing hospitality and community bonding. Its preparation methods and specific ingredients can vary widely between regions and families, reflecting local traditions and agricultural practices. The drink has gained interest in recent years as part of a broader revival of ancient customs and folk traditions within Slavic cultural heritage.

The city of Mangaluru is proclaimed as the gateway to Karnataka and lies nestled between the blue waters of the Arabian Sea and the green, towering hills of the Western Ghats. The 184 square km city is spread out over the backwaters of the two rivers, Netravati and Gurpura.

Mangaluru, a coastal city in the Indian state of Karnataka, is known for its diverse tourist attractions that include historical, religious, and natural sites. Key attractions include the Kadri Manjunath Temple, an ancient Hindu shrine featuring stunning architecture, and the St. Aloysius Chapel, famous for its exquisite frescoes. The city is also home to Mangaladevi Temple, believed to be the origin of the city's name, and the serene Panambur Beach, popular for water sports and events. Other significant sites include the Sultan Battery fort, built in the 18th century, and the scenic surrounds of the Western Ghats, offering opportunities for trekking and eco-tourism. The city's rich cultural heritage, combined with its beautiful landscapes and vibrant local cuisine, attracts both domestic and international visitors.

El Polín Spring is a natural spring in San Francisco, California located in the Presidio. It is the source of the central tributary of El Polín Creek (also called Tennessee Hollow Creek). The spring was used by the Ohlone people, the Spanish military, and the U.S. Army as a freshwater source. Much of the stream was channelized or placed in underground culverts, and many riparian areas were used for landfill. Beginning in the 2000s, the Presidio Trust has worked to rehabilitate El Polín spring and daylight sections of the creek downstream. In 2005, 77,000 tons of landfill were removed from Thompson Reach, a downstream section, and native riparian species were planted. In 2011, El Polín was restored, with new trails, native plants, and a picnic area installed.

El Polín Spring is a natural spring located in the San Francisco Bay area of California, noted for its historical and ecological significance. Situated within the Presidio of San Francisco, the spring was a vital water source for indigenous Ohlone people and later for Spanish settlers in the 18th century. It is part of the larger Presidio Trust's efforts to restore and preserve the natural landscape and historical structures in the area. The spring has been recognized for its ecological value, serving as a habitat for various wildlife species. In recent years, El Polín Spring has undergone restoration projects aimed at reviving its natural flow and enhancing surrounding habitats.

Crooked River State Park is a 500-acre (2.0 km2) Georgia state park located near St. Mary's on the south bank of the Crooked River, providing an excellent coastal setting. The park is dedicated to the preservation of its natural resources and public education. The park is near the ruins of the McIntosh Sugarworks, built around 1825 and used as a starch factory during the American Civil War. It is the closest state park to Cumberland Island National Seashore and it is adjacent to the Naval Submarine Base Kings Bay.

Crooked River State Park is a state park located in southeastern Georgia, near the city of St. Marys. Established in 1989, the park encompasses approximately 500 acres and features a diverse ecosystem of salt marshes, tidal creeks, and forested areas. It offers various recreational activities, including hiking, fishing, camping, and birdwatching. The park is situated along the Crooked River, providing opportunities for boating and kayaking. It is also home to a variety of wildlife, including migratory birds and other native species. The park's facilities include picnic areas, hiking trails, and camping sites, making it a popular destination for both locals and visitors.

The Mexico City Mexico Temple (formerly the Mexico City Temple) is the 28th constructed and 26th operating temple of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (LDS Church). The intent to build the temple was announced on April 3, 1967, by church president Spencer W. Kimball. It is located in the north-eastern part of the Mexican capital, Mexico City. The architecture is influenced by the Mayan Revival style, and includes both Aztec and Mayan elements. The temple was designed by Emil B. Fetzer. A groundbreaking ceremony, to signify the beginning of construction, was held on November 25, 1979, conducted by Boyd K. Packer. It is the largest temple outside the United States.

The Mexico City Mexico Temple is a temple of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (LDS Church) located in Mexico City, Mexico. Announced in 1997, the temple was dedicated on December 17, 2000, by church president Gordon B. Hinckley. It serves as a hub for Latter-day Saints in Mexico and surrounding areas, providing sacred ordinances and worship services. The temple is notable for its modern architectural design and beautifully landscaped grounds. It was the first temple built in Mexico and is part of a broader expansion of Latter-day Saint temples worldwide. In 2020, the temple was closed to the public due to the COVID-19 pandemic and later reopened with safety measures in place.

Topobea is a genus of flowering plants in the family Melastomataceae. A 2013 study indicated that it is actually part of the genus Blakea. Species include: Topobea asplundii Wurdack Topobea brevibractea Gleason Topobea cutucuensis Wurdack Topobea eplingii Wurdack Topobea induta Markgr. Topobea macbrydei Wurdack Topobea maguirei Wurdack Topobea parvifolia (Gleason) Almeda Topobea pascoensis Wurdack Topobea toachiensis Wurdack Topobea verrucosa Wurdack

Topobea is a genus of flowering plants belonging to the family Cyclanthaceae, primarily found in the tropical regions of Central and South America. Comprising approximately ten species, Topobea is notable for its unique morphological characteristics, such as large, distinctive leaves and inflorescences composed of tubular flowers. These plants typically thrive in wet, shaded environments, often in forest understories. The genus has drawn interest for its ecological significance and potential applications in horticulture and conservation.
Stary Cykarzew [ˈstarɨ t͡sɨˈkaʐɛf] is a village in the administrative district of Gmina Mykanów, within Częstochowa County, Silesian Voivodeship, in southern Poland.

Stary Cykarzew is a village located in south-central Poland, within the administrative district of Gmina Świebodzin, in the Świętokrzyskie Voivodeship. The village is situated near the town of Świebodzin and is characterized by a rural landscape and agricultural activities. Stary Cykarzew, like many villages in Poland, reflects the cultural and historical influences of the region, which has undergone various changes throughout its history. The area is also known for its picturesque environment and proximity to natural attractions and traditional Polish countryside.
Gregory Pepper, born Gregory Andre Perets, is a musician based in Guelph, Ontario and signed to Fake Four Inc.

Gregory Pepper is an American artist and writer known for his contributions to contemporary art and literature. He is recognized for his experimental and often multidisciplinary approach, integrating visual art with narrative elements. Pepper's works often explore themes of identity, perception, and the intersection of reality and fiction. He has exhibited in various galleries and art venues and has published several works, both in artistic and literary formats. In addition to his creative practice, Pepper is engaged in collaborations and projects that promote artistic discourse and community engagement.

Samuel Andrews (1836–1904) was a chemist and inventor. Born in England, he immigrated to the United States before the American Civil War and settled in Cleveland, Ohio. He is best known as a partner in the oil refining firm of Rockefeller, Andrews & Flagler, the major predecessor company of the Standard Oil corporate empire. When the first unit was formed in 1870, Andrews owned 16.67% of Standard Oil stock. He sold his stock early on in 1874 and while he was wealthy, he did not participate in the level of wealth generation that the other founders did.

Samuel Andrews (1821–1887) was an American chemist and inventor, known for his pioneering work in the field of petroleum chemistry. He is notably recognized for his contributions to the understanding of the distillation of crude oil and the development of processes for refining petroleum products. Andrews authored "The Refining of Petroleum," published in 1875, which provided insights into the chemical composition of various petroleum fractions. His research laid the groundwork for subsequent advancements in the oil industry. Andrews also held several patents related to petroleum refining techniques and was involved in establishing the American Chemical Society. His work had a lasting impact on the methods used in oil production and refining, significantly influencing the industrial landscape of the late 19th century.
Tolga Özbahar (born 24 April 1984) is a Turkish handballer who plays for Nilüfer Bld. and the Turkey national team.

Tolga Özbahar is a Turkish entrepreneur and business executive known for his contributions to the technology and startup ecosystem in Turkey. He has co-founded several innovative companies in fields such as software development and artificial intelligence, particularly focusing on enhancing digital solutions for various industries. Özbahar is recognized for his advocacy of entrepreneurship and has been actively involved in mentoring young startups and fostering collaboration within the tech community. Throughout his career, he has participated in numerous conferences and panels, sharing insights on technology trends and the role of innovation in economic growth.

The University of Oxford introduced Titles of Distinction for senior academics in the 1990s. These are not established chairs, which are posts funded by endowment for academics with a distinguished career in British and European universities. However, since there was a limited number of established chairs in these universities and an abundance of distinguished academics it was decided to introduce these Titles of Distinction. 'Reader' and the senior 'Professor' were conferred annually. In the 1994–95 academic year, Oxford's Congregation (the university's supreme governing body) decided to confer the titles of Professor and Reader on distinguished academics without changes to their salaries or duties; the title of professor would be conferred on those whose research was "of outstanding quality", leading "to a significant international reputation". Reader would be conferred on those with "a research record of a high order, the quality of which has gained external recognition". This article provides a list of people upon whom the University of Oxford has conferred the title of professor. In July 1996, the University announced it had appointed 162 new Professors and 99 Readers as part of this move. In January 2001, Congregation's Personnel Committee recommended that the process for awarding titles of distinction should continue biennially, and in October 2001, details of the application process for the 2001–02 academic year were published to that effect, meaning the next awards would be made in October 2002. Awards were then made in 2004, 2006 and 2008. In 2005, a special task force was set up to report back to the University Council about career progression for academics. It made its recommendations in April 2010, when it was decided that the title of Reader should be discontinued and that the title of Professor should continue to be awarded biennially. These measures were given effect by the Vice-Chancellor in May 2010. The next round of awards would be made after Trinity term 2011, but were awarded retrospectively (from October 2010); the names of that cohort were announced in January 2012. The next set of awards were made in 2014, and further sets have been made annually since.
topic: Titles of distinction awarded by the University of Oxford

text: The University of Oxford awards various titles of distinction to recognize exceptional contributions to academia, public service, and leadership. These titles include honorary degrees, which are conferred to individuals without the requirement of academic qualifications, acknowledging their significant achievements in various fields. Other titles include Fellowships, awarded to scholars and researchers who have demonstrated excellence in their work, and distinctions such as the title of Emeritus Professor, granted to retired faculty members who have made enduring contributions to their discipline. The selection process for these honors typically involves nomination and approval by university officials or specific committees, reflecting the institution's commitment to excellence and recognition of notable figures in various domains.
topic: Titles of distinction awarded by the University of Oxford

text: Trevor Alfred Painter (2 July 1949 – 9 April 2012) was an English footballer who played in the Football League as a defender for Norwich City and Colchester United.

Trevor Painter is a former English professional footballer born on 15 December 1970 in Southampton, England. Primarily a midfielder, Painter began his career at Southampton, where he made a few appearances in the first team before moving to Brighton & Hove Albion in 1990. Over the years, he has played for several clubs, including Woking, Hereford United, and Stafford Rangers. After retiring from professional football, Painter remained involved in the sport, transitioning to coaching roles within youth systems and lower-league teams. Throughout his career, he was known for his tenacity on the pitch and his commitment to the teams he represented.

Hatsudai (初台) is a district of Shibuya, Tokyo, Japan. As of October 2020, the population of this district is 8,629. The postal code for Hatsudai is 151-0061. An Awa Odori festival is held here annually.

Hatsudai (初台) is a district located in the Shibuya ward of Tokyo, Japan. It is primarily known for its residential and commercial areas, as well as its proximity to cultural landmarks such as the New National Theatre Tokyo. The neighborhood has a diverse population and features a mix of contemporary developments and traditional Japanese architecture. Hatsudai benefits from convenient transportation options, including the Tokyo Metro and various bus services, making it easily accessible from other parts of the city. The area is characterized by its vibrant atmosphere, with a range of shops, restaurants, and parks contributing to its appeal as a lively urban center.

Greater Manchester Passenger Transport Executive (GMPTE) was the public body responsible for public transport in Greater Manchester between 1974 and 2011, when it became part of Transport for Greater Manchester.

The Greater Manchester Passenger Transport Executive (GMPTE) was the executive arm of the Greater Manchester Integrated Transport Authority, responsible for planning, developing, and promoting public transport in the Greater Manchester area of England. Established in 1969, GMPTE oversaw various modes of transport, including buses, trams, and trains, aiming to improve accessibility, connectivity, and sustainability within the regional transport network. In 2011, the executive was rebranded as Transport for Greater Manchester (TfGM), reflecting a broader focus on integrated transport solutions. TfGM continues to facilitate public transport operations, manage transport infrastructure, and invest in new projects to enhance the region's transport system.
Ludo Frijns (born 22 March 1957) is a Belgian former professional racing cyclist. He rode in the 1982 Tour de France.

Ludo Frijns (born 17 September 1995) is a Dutch professional racing driver known for his participation in various motorsport categories. He began his career in karting before progressing to single-seaters, where he competed in series such as the Eurocircuit and the European Formula 3 Championship. Frijns gained recognition for his competitive performances and skill behind the wheel, leading to further opportunities in the racing world, including stints in the World Endurance Championship and as a test driver for notable racing teams. His career reflects a diverse experience in both open-wheel and endurance racing formats.
City of Washington was an American merchant steamship that aided in rescuing the crew of USS Maine when it exploded in the harbor of Havana, Cuba, in 1898.

The City of Washington was a steamship launched in 1850, primarily used for passenger and cargo transport along the United States' eastern seaboard. Constructed in Philadelphia, she was notable for her size and advanced engineering for the time. The vessel operated on various routes, significantly contributing to commerce and travel before the advent of railroads. In 1856, City of Washington was involved in a significant maritime accident that led to her eventual sinking. Despite her loss, the ship is remembered as a symbol of mid-19th century maritime innovation and the growth of steam-powered vessels.
Birstein is a municipality on the northeastern edge of the Main-Kinzig-Kreis in Hesse, Germany with approximately 6,600 inhabitants. It was the home of the former principality of Isenburg-Birstein.

Birstein is a small town in the Ilfov county of Romania, located near the capital city of Bucharest. It is part of the larger metropolitan area and has experienced growth and development in recent years due to its proximity to urban centers. The town features residential neighborhoods, local businesses, and community facilities. Birstein is characterized by its rural charm, with nearby agricultural lands and green spaces. It is a part of the broader historical and cultural landscape of Romania, but it remains lesser-known compared to more prominent cities and towns in the region.

The Terre Haute, Brazil and Eastern Railroad was a short-line railroad that was incorporated May 1, 1987 and began operations on October 15, 1987. The TBER operated over 30 miles of the Pennsylvania Railroad's St. Louis-Indianapolis-Pittsburgh Line, which had been abandoned by Conrail in 1984 in favor of the former New York Central-Big Four St. Louis Line subdivision. The line extended from Terre Haute to Limedale and included a seven-mile branch line to the Amax Coal Company's Chinook Mine south of Brazil. The TBER interchanged with the Soo Line Railroad's Latta Subdivision, Conrail's St. Louis Line and CSX's CE&D Subdivision in Terre Haute. Traffic was an even mix of lumber, fertilizer, and plastic pellets inbound and grain, cement, and plastic pipe outbound. A tourist excursion called the Beaver Hawk Express was operated under contract between Brazil, Indiana and Limedale. Coal traffic was also anticipated but failed to materialize, as a result the line entered bankruptcy. The TBER ceased operation on December 31, 1993, and the line was dismantled.
topic: Terre Haute, Brazil and Eastern Railroad

text: The Terre Haute, Brazil and Eastern Railroad (THB&E) was a short-line railway established in the late 19th century in the United States, primarily connecting Terre Haute, Indiana, to the Brazilian region of Indiana. Formed in 1891, the railroad aimed to facilitate transportation and commerce in the area, linking agricultural regions with larger markets. The THB&E operated over a relatively short network with minimal extensions, and its role diminished in the early 20th century due to the expansion of larger railroads and changes in transportation needs. The railway was absorbed into larger rail systems, contributing to the eventual consolidation of the American railway industry. Although the THB&E saw limited success, it played a role in the early development of regional railroad infrastructure in Indiana.
topic: Terre Haute, Brazil and Eastern Railroad

text: Mohammad Khalil Naik (Urdu: محمد خليل نائیک) is a Kashmiri politician. He is a veteran leader of the Communist Party of India (Marxist) (CPI(M)) in Jammu & Kashmir. He served as Member of the Jammu & Kashmir Legislative Assembly representing the Wachi constituency 2002-2008. He is also a member of the Jammu & Kashmir State Committee of CPI(M). Mohammad Khalil Naik won the Wachi seat in 2002, defeating the Jammu & Kashmir People's Democratic Party candidate Ghulam Hassan Bhat by a margin of merely 80 votes. Mohammad Khalil Naik got 4,133 votes (29.19%). In April–May 2004, during the election campaign ahead of the Lok Sabha parliamentary election (in which fellow CPI(M) leader Mohammed Yousuf Tarigami contested the Anantnag constituency), militants conducted two attempts to kill Mohammad Khalil Naik. On 28 April 2004 militants hurled a grenade at his vehicle near Bungam as he was travelling to an election campaign meeting in Bonapora. The grenade missed the car but injured various bystanders, wounding two policemen and twelve civilians. And on 17 May 2004 Mohammad Khalil Naik's motorcade was attacked at Zainapora in the Shopian area of the Pulwama district. Mohammad Khalil Naik managed to escape unhurt from the attacks. On 8 August 2007 militants opened fire on Mohammad Khalil Naik's vehicle near Bahibag as the legislator was touring his constituency. Mohammad Khalil Naik and his security team escaped unhurt from the attack. Mohammad Khalil Naik lost the Wachi seat to J&KPDP leader Mehbooba Mufti in the 2008 Jammu & Kashmir Legislative Assembly election, finishing in fourth place with 2,366 votes (7.01%).

Mohammad Khalil Naik is an Indian Islamist preacher and the founder of the Islamic Research Foundation (IRF), known for promoting Islamic teachings and interfaith dialogue. Naik gained prominence through his lectures and debates on various religious topics, especially through his televised speeches and online platforms. He is an advocate for a puritanical interpretation of Islam and has been a controversial figure due to his views on other religions, which critics have labeled as intolerant. In 2016, he was banned from entering several countries, including the United Kingdom and Canada, due to concerns over his influence on extremism. Naik's organization, IRF, was also designated as unlawful in India in 2020 for its alleged links to terrorism and money laundering activities.

The Cibaeño Revolution (Spanish: La Revolución Cibaeño) was an armed uprising that occurred in the Dominican Republic between July 1857 and June 1858, centered in the Cibao region. It was led by liberal sectors and merchants opposed to the dictatorial regime of President Buenaventura Báez, who had centralized political and economic power in the capital, Santo Domingo. The revolution culminated in the establishment of a provisional government in Santiago and later with the fall of Báez. The insurgent movement was notable for its defense of democratic and federal principles, while promoting a redistribution of political power among the country's different regions. It had the support of important civilian and military figures from the Cibao, including José Desiderio Valverde and Benigno Filomeno de Rojas, who played fundamental roles in the organization and leadership of the insurrection. The Cibaeño Revolution represented one of the first significant demonstrations of opposition to centralism in Dominican political history. Although short-lived, its impact was decisive in the evolution of the national political system, as it led to the fall of an authoritarian regime and set a precedent for future regional conflicts and debates over the structure of the Dominican Republic.

The Cibaeño Revolution, also known as the Cibao Revolution, refers to a series of uprisings that took place in the Cibao region of the Dominican Republic during the late 19th century, primarily between 1891 and 1896. The movement emerged as a response to political instability and social inequities, fueled by dissatisfaction with the ruling government, particularly under President Ulises Heureaux. The revolution was characterized by a desire for greater autonomy and reforms in land ownership and governance. Multiple factions, including liberal and conservative groups, participated in the conflict, which was marked by violent confrontations and shifting allegiances. Ultimately, the revolution failed to achieve its goals and was suppressed, leading to further political repression and instability in the Dominican Republic. However, it laid the groundwork for future political movements and is considered a significant episode in the country's struggle for democratic governance.
In mathematics, the Gelfond–Schneider theorem establishes the transcendence of a large class of numbers.

The Gelfond–Schneider theorem is a fundamental result in transcendental number theory, established by Aleksandr Gelfond and Wolfgang Schneider in the 1930s. It states that if \( a \) is an algebraic number (not equal to 0 or 1), and \( b \) is a different algebraic number that is not rational, then the number \( a^b \) is transcendental. This theorem implies that certain exponentials involving algebraic numbers yield transcendental results and provides a basis for establishing the transcendental nature of numbers such as \( e \) and \( \pi \). Its proof relies on methods from both algebra and analysis, reinforcing the interconnections between these areas in mathematics.
Seymour station is a commuter rail stop on the Waterbury Branch of the Metro-North Railroad's New Haven Line, located in Seymour, Connecticut.

Seymour station is a railway station located in Seymour, Victoria, Australia. It serves as a crucial stop on the North East railway line, providing passenger services to the regional area. The station was originally opened in 1872 and underwent significant upgrades over the years, including the establishment of a modern passenger facilities. Seymour station features several platforms and services operated by V/Line, connecting passengers to Melbourne and other regional destinations. The station also plays an important role in freight transport, supporting the local economy and industries. It is recognized for its heritage significance and has been included in the Victorian Heritage Register.

Caldoche (French pronunciation: [kaldɔʃ] ) is the name given to inhabitants of the French overseas collectivity of New Caledonia of European ethnic origin who have settled in New Caledonia since the 19th century. The formal name to refer to this particular population is Calédoniens, short for the very formal Néo-Calédoniens, but this self-appellation technically includes all inhabitants of the New Caledonian archipelago, not just the Caldoche. Another European demographic within the population of New Caledonia are expatriates from metropolitan France who have arrived recently or live there temporarily as French government civil servants and contract workers. Caldoche emphasise their own distinct identity and position as permanent locals who have lived in New Caledonia for several generations by referring to the temporary French expatriates as métros (short for métropolitains) or as Zoreilles (informally zozos) in local slang. A majority of the Caldoche are of French descent and have their origins as free colonial or penal settlers, with smaller but significant numbers of Caldoche being of Italian, German, British, Polish, Belgian and Irish heritage. However, the separate slang term Poken is also used to refer to Anglophones, especially Australians and New Zealanders, but also British people. French is the main language spoken by the Caldoche. New Caledonia was used as a penal colony from 1854 to 1922 by France. From this period and on, many Europeans (particularly of French and, to some extent, German origin) settled in the territory and they intermingled with Asian and Polynesian settlers. Code de l'indigénat, introduced in 1887, provided the free settler population with an advantageous status over the indigenous Melanesian peoples, known collectively as Kanak. Caldoches settled and gained property on the dry west coast of the main island Grande Terre where the capital Nouméa is also located, pushing the Kanaks onto small reservations in the north and east. With the superior position, they constituted the ruling class of the colony and they were the ones who widened the usage of the word Canaque as a pejorative.

Caldoche refers to a term predominantly used in New Caledonia to describe the descendants of European settlers, particularly those of French origin, who have lived in the region for several generations. This ethnocultural group is distinct from the indigenous Kanak people and has contributed significantly to the social, economic, and political landscape of New Caledonia. Caldoche society is characterized by a blend of French cultural influences and local adaptations, which manifest in language, cuisine, and social practices. The term can sometimes carry connotations related to identity, cultural heritage, and the complex dynamics of post-colonial relationships in the Pacific.

The Saint Petersburg Institute of History (Russian: Санкт-Петербургский Институт истории) is a research institute of the Russian Academy of Sciences in the field of Russian and foreign history. It is part of the Department of Historical and Philological Sciences of the Russian Academy of Sciences. The organization is located in the estate of N. P. Likhachyov in Saint Petersburg, where he lived from 1902 to 1936, so the institute is also known as the "Likhachyov Mansion". As a research center, the institute continues the traditions of the St. Petersburg historical school, established by K. Bestuzhev-Ryumin, S. Platonov, A. Lappo-Danilevsky, A. Presnyakov and others. Since 1936, the organization operated as the Leningrad branch of the Institute of History, Academy of Sciences of the Soviet Union. After the proclamation of independence of the Russian Federation, the organization began to exist as a branch of the Russian Academy of Sciences, and since 2000 has had its current name. At different times, such historians as B. Grekov, S. Zhebelev, V. Struve, Y. Tarle, I. Shaskol'skii, S. Kovalev, A. Lublinskaya, I. Petrushevsky, M. Sergeyenko worked at the institute. At present, the institute's staff conducts research in the following areas: classical studies, Byzantine studies, medieval history of Italian cities, American studies, history of the socio-political movements in Russia in the 19th century, history of feudalism in Russia, history of St. Petersburg, history of the Russian Revolution, history of the Siege of Leningrad. The Institute has an archive that stores documents of the Archaeographic Commission, as well as documents from the collection of N. P. Likhachyov and other private collectors. In total, the archive contains over 188 thousand storage units. The documents concern the history of Russia (13th — 20th centuries) and Europe (7th — 20th centuries). Among the documents are: acts of Italian and German cities, papal bulls, documents of European monarchs; monastery funds (archives of Solovetsky, Alexander-Svirsky, Siya, Valday, Tikhvin and other monasteries of North-West Russia); family funds (archives of Vorontsov, Demidov, Tatishchev, Stroganov, Shuvalov and others Russian noble families).

The Saint Petersburg Institute of History is a prominent academic institution located in Saint Petersburg, Russia, dedicated to the study and research of historical scholarship. Established in 1934 as part of the Russian Academy of Sciences, the institute focuses on a wide range of historical topics, including Russian history, the history of Eastern Europe, and global historical developments. The institute conducts extensive research, organizes scholarly conferences, and publishes academic works, contributing significantly to historical discourse. It serves as a center for historical education and collaboration, attracting historians, researchers, and students from various countries. The institute is noted for its rigorous academic standards and its role in advancing historical research in Russia and beyond.
Heliophanoides spermathecalis is a jumping spider species in the genus Heliophanoides that lives in India. The female was first described in 1992.

Heliophanoides spermathecalis is a species of moth in the family Erebidae, first described by entomologist Walter Rothschild in 1910. It is primarily found in regions of South America, where it inhabits various ecosystems. Like other moths in its family, H. spermathecalis is nocturnal and plays a role in local ecosystems, such as pollination and serving as a food source for predators. The species is characterized by distinctive wing patterns and coloration, which aid in camouflage against natural predators. Further research is necessary to understand its life cycle, habitat preferences, and ecological impact.
The Rural Municipality of Perdue No. 346 (2016 population: 445) is a rural municipality (RM) in the Canadian province of Saskatchewan within Census Division No. 12 and SARM Division No. 5.

The Rural Municipality of Perdue No. 346 is a rural municipality located in Saskatchewan, Canada. Established on December 1, 1910, it is situated in the west-central part of the province, within the Census Division No. 11. The municipality encompasses a total area of approximately 715.25 square kilometers and includes the villages of Perdue and the hamlet of Aline. As of the 2016 census, the population of the municipality was recorded at 528 residents. The RM is governed by an elected council, led by a reeve, and it focuses on local governance, community development, and the provision of municipal services to its residents. The area is characterized by agriculture, with farming being a predominant economic activity.

Saitama 16th district (埼玉県第16区, Saitama-ken dai-ju-rokku or simply 埼玉16区, Saitama-ju-rokku) is a single-member constituency of the House of Representatives in the national Diet of Japan located in Saitama Prefecture.

Saitama 16th district is a legislative electoral district in Saitama Prefecture, Japan. It is part of the National Diet of Japan and is represented in the House of Representatives. The district encompasses several cities and towns, and its electorate primarily consists of residents from urban and suburban areas. Established after the 1994 electoral reform, the district has experienced shifting demographic and political trends over the years. Typically, it has showcased competitive elections, reflecting the broader political landscape of Saitama Prefecture. As of the most recent elections, the district’s representative is a member of the Liberal Democratic Party.
Coxcatlán Municipality is a municipality in the central Mexican state of San Luis Potosí.

Coxcatlán is a municipality and town located in the San Luis Potosí state of Mexico. It is situated in the southeastern part of the state, characterized by its rural landscape and agricultural activities. The region is primarily inhabited by Indigenous communities, with a culture deeply rooted in traditional practices. Coxcatlán's economy is mainly based on agriculture, with maize and beans being staple crops. The area is also notable for its natural beauty, including rivers and mountainous regions that attract visitors. The town celebrates various local festivals, reflecting its cultural heritage and community spirit.

Marion L. Longstaff (March 11, 1904 – February 29, 1984) was an American politician from Maine. A Republican, Longstaff represented Crystal, Aroostook County, Maine in the Maine House of Representatives from 1943 to 1950 (91st-94th Legislatures). During the 94th Legislature, Longstaff was one of three women elected to the House of Representatives. Marguerite R. Fay and Lucia M. Cormier were the others.

Marion L. Longstaff (born 1935) is an American scientist and esteemed professor known for her contributions to the field of mathematics, particularly in the areas of topology and mathematical education.
