She has held academic positions at several universities, where she has promoted innovative teaching methods and engaged in research that enhances the understanding of mathematical concepts. Longstaff has authored numerous scholarly articles and textbooks, emphasizing the importance of mathematics in various applications. Additionally, she has been active in initiatives aimed at increasing diversity in STEM fields. Her work has garnered recognition and awards within the academic community.
Neuenkirchen (German pronunciation: [ˈnɔʏənˌkɪʁçn̩] ) is a municipality in the district of Dithmarschen, in Schleswig-Holstein, Germany.

Neuenkirchen is a municipality located in the district of Stormarn in Schleswig-Holstein, Germany. It is situated in the northern part of the country, near the border with Hamburg. The municipality is characterized by its rural landscape and small population. Neuenkirchen includes several localities and agricultural lands, reflecting its historical roots in farming. The region offers various recreational activities, poised between the proximity to urban centers and the tranquility of the countryside. The administrative structure of Neuenkirchen is governed by local councils, and it participates in regional development initiatives to enhance community living and environmental sustainability.

Maguy Maccario Doyle is Ambassador Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary of the Principality of Monaco to the United States and Canada, as of 2013 and 2014 respectively, and Monaco's Permanent Observer to the Organization of American States. Maccario Doyle was appointed Ambassador of Monaco to the United States by Prince Albert II of Monaco on November 12, 2013, and presented her credentials to President Barack Obama on December 3 that same year. In December 2014, she presented her credentials to Canada's Governor General, H.E. David Johnston, assuming the positions both as Ambassador of Monaco to Canada, and as the Permanent Observer of Monaco to the OAS. Prior to her current positions in Washington, Maccario Doyle had worked at the Monaco Government Tourist Office in New York since 1976, where she was appointed Director for North America in 1994. Maccario Doyle became the first female Monégasque career consul ever when, in 1995, Prince Rainier III appointed her as consul of Monaco in New York, then consul general in 1997. Distinctions received by Maccario Doyle at this time include the "Chevalier de l'Ordre de Saint-Charles" distinction, presented by Prince Rainier in 1996, and a gold medal from ALSAC/ St. Jude Children's Research Hospital for her support of its international programs in 1998. Maccario Doyle continues to work with St. Jude's, having served on their Professional Advisory Board since 2004. In 2011, she was appointed to the International Advisory Council for ALSAC. Maccario Doyle was appointed by Prince Albert II as vice president of the US chapter of the Prince Albert II of Monaco Foundation-USA in 2008; during her tenure she launched local fundraising and awareness events and partnerships. She also created Monaco's Environmental Stewardship Gallery, an installation demonstrating the history and prospects of Monaco's environmental conservation efforts, which was displayed at various institutions in Chicago, New York, and Boston. In May, 2016, Maccario Doyle was appointed President of the US chapter of the Princess Charlene of Monaco Foundation.

Maguy Maccario Doyle is a prominent political figure from Monaco, known for her role in the National Council of Monaco, where she serves as a member. Born on April 18, 1966, she has been an influential advocate for various social and political issues within the Monegasque government. Maccario Doyle is recognized for her efforts in promoting education, environment, and women's rights in Monaco. She has also held leadership roles in various committees and is considered a significant voice in the legislative process of the principality. Her work reflects a commitment to progressive policies and civic engagement.

The siege of Nicaea of 727 was an unsuccessful attempt by the Umayyad Caliphate to capture the Byzantine city of Nicaea, the capital of the Opsician Theme. Ever since its failure to capture the Byzantine Empire's capital, Constantinople, in 717–718, the Caliphate had launched a series of raids into Byzantine Asia Minor. In 727, the Arab army, led by one of the Caliph's sons, penetrated deep into Asia Minor, sacked two Byzantine fortresses and in late July arrived before Nicaea. Despite constant attacks for 40 days, the city held firm and the Arabs withdrew and returned to the Caliphate. The successful repulsion of the attack was a major boost for Byzantine emperor Leo III the Isaurian's recently initiated campaign to abolish the veneration of icons in the Empire; Leo claimed it as evidence of divine favour for his policy. The siege of Nicaea marks also the high point of the Umayyad raids, as new threats and defeats on their far-flung frontiers decreased Umayyad strength elsewhere, while Byzantine power strengthened afterwards.

The Siege of Nicaea in 727 was a military confrontation during the Byzantine-Arab Wars, occurring between the Byzantine Empire and the Umayyad Caliphate. The siege was initiated by the Caliphate under the command of General Maslama ibn Abd al-Malik, aiming to capture the strategically significant city of Nicaea, located in Bithynia. The Byzantines, led by Emperor Leo III, mounted a determined defense, utilizing fortifications and local resources to resist the attackers. After several months of protracted conflict, the siege was ultimately unsuccessful for the Umayyad forces, who withdrew in 728 due to logistical issues and the onset of winter. The unsuccessful siege was significant for bolstering Byzantine morale and solidifying Leo III’s position as a defender of the Empire against external threats.
Lucas Esteban Ceballos (born 3 January 1987) is an Argentine professional footballer who plays as a defender for Mitre.

Lucas Ceballos is an Argentine professional footballer born on April 6, 1987, in Buenos Aires, Argentina. Primarily a midfielder, Ceballos began his career in youth football before making his professional debut with Club Atlético Vélez Sarsfield. Over the years, he has played for various clubs, including stints in Argentina's top divisions and several international teams. Known for his versatility on the field, Ceballos has developed a reputation for his passing skills and tactical awareness. Throughout his career, he has contributed to his teams' successes in league competitions and domestic cups.
Tit Štante (born 11 October 1998) is a Slovenian snowboarder. He competed in the 2018 Winter Olympics.

Tit Štante was a prominent Yugoslav and Slovenian painter and graphic artist known for his contributions to modern art throughout the 20th century. Born in 1941 in Slovenia, he gained recognition for his distinctive use of color and dynamic abstract forms. Štante's work often explores themes of identity, nature, and the human experience, reflecting the cultural transitions of his time. He was involved in various exhibitions both nationally and internationally, earning accolades for his artistic innovation. His style evolved over the decades, encompassing elements of expressionism and conceptual art, and he remains an influential figure in the contemporary Slovenian art scene.

Giuseppina Aliverti (4 December 1894 – 10 June 1982) was an Italian geophysicist specializing in several fields of terrestrial physics. She is remembered for developing the Aliverti-Lovera method of measuring the radioactivity of water.

Giuseppina Aliverti (born September 21, 1960) is an Italian politician and member of the Italian Senate. She is associated with the center-right political party, Brothers of Italy. Aliverti has served in various capacities within local government and has held roles that focus on social policies and public administration. Her legislative work emphasizes issues such as economic development, women's rights, and regional autonomy. Throughout her political career, she has been involved in initiatives aimed at promoting local communities and enhancing citizen engagement in governance.

The German–Soviet Boundary and Friendship Treaty was a second supplementary protocol of the Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact of 23 August 1939. It was a secret clause as amended on 28 September 1939 by Nazi Germany and the Soviet Union after their joint invasion and occupation of sovereign Poland. It was signed by Joachim von Ribbentrop and Vyacheslav Molotov, the foreign ministers of Germany and the Soviet Union respectively, in the presence of Joseph Stalin. Only a small portion of the protocol, which superseded the first treaty, was publicly announced, while the spheres of influence of Nazi Germany and the Soviet Union remained secret. The third secret protocol of the Pact was signed on 10 January 1941 by Friedrich Werner von Schulenburg and Molotov, in which Germany renounced its claims on a part of Lithuania, west of the Šešupė river. Only a few months after this, Germany started its invasion of the Soviet Union.

The German–Soviet Boundary and Friendship Treaty, signed on September 28, 1939, formalized the territorial changes resulting from the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact, a non-aggression agreement between Nazi Germany and the Soviet Union. The treaty established boundaries between the two powers in Eastern Europe, particularly regarding Poland, which was partitioned following the invasion by both nations. It recognized Soviet influence in the Baltic States and parts of Eastern Poland while delineating the border in the East. This treaty underpinned the collaboration between the two states until Germany's invasion of the Soviet Union in 1941, ultimately contributing to the geopolitical landscape of World War II.

Francesc Arín i Simó (1891–1936) was a Valencian trade unionist and journalist. A metalworker by trade, Arín became a union leader in the Confederación Nacional del Trabajo (CNT), within which he formed part of the moderate faction. He led the metalworkers' union through the early 1920s, which saw him arrested, exiled and blacklisted. He then switched professions to work in the fishing industry, within which he likewise became a trade union leader. During the dictatorship of Primo de Rivera, he joined the Solidaridad group led by Ángel Pestaña and continued to organise the CNT throughout the period. When he was elected as the General Secretary of the CNT in 1930, he began to pursue a policy of legalisation, opposing the use of conspiratorial methods against the government. After the proclamation of the Second Spanish Republic, he argued for the temporary defence of the republic until the CNT had consolidated its strength and the threat of a reactionary coup d'état had been averted. Together with Pestaña, he published the Manifesto of the Thirty, which criticised premature calls for insurrection by individual cadres. The faction gradually lost influence within the CNT and eventually split from it entirely, forming the Federación Sindicalista Libertaria (FSL). Arín pushed for the FSL's reintegration into the CNT and rejoined the organisation by 1936. He was killed by the Nationalists in the first days of the Spanish Civil War.

Francesc Arín is a Spanish athlete known for his accomplishments in the sport of athletics, particularly in long-distance running. Born on June 12, 1990, in Spain, Arín has competed in various national and international competitions, representing his region and country. He has gained recognition for his performances in events such as the marathon and half-marathon, showcasing his endurance and speed. Over the years, he has contributed to the growing popularity of athletics in Spain, inspiring emerging athletes in the field.

The Women's 400 metre individual medley event at the 2010 Commonwealth Games took place on 9 October 2010, at the SPM Swimming Pool Complex. Two heats were held. The heat in which a swimmer competed did not formally matter for advancement, as the swimmers with the top eight times from the entire field qualified for the finals.
topic: Swimming at the 2010 Commonwealth Games – Women's 400 metre individual medley

text: The women's 400 metre individual medley at the 2010 Commonwealth Games took place on October 6, 2010, at the Jawaharlal Nehru Stadium in New Delhi, India. This event featured female swimmers competing in a race that included all four swimming strokes: butterfly, backstroke, breaststroke, and freestyle. The competition consisted of heats in the morning session, with the top eight contestants advancing to the finals held later that day. The event showcased top international talent, and the medalists included representatives from Australia, Scotland, and Canada. The race was notable for its display of technique and endurance across multiple strokes.
topic: Swimming at the 2010 Commonwealth Games – Women's 400 metre individual medley

text: The Chinese ambassador to Zimbabwe is the official representative of the People's Republic of China to the Republic of Zimbabwe.
topic: List of ambassadors of China to Zimbabwe

text: The List of ambassadors of China to Zimbabwe provides a record of the diplomatic representatives appointed by the People's Republic of China to Zimbabwe since the establishment of formal diplomatic relations between the two countries. The ambassadors play a crucial role in facilitating bilateral relations, promoting trade, and fostering cultural exchanges. The list includes the names of the ambassadors, their terms of service, and significant contributions to China-Zimbabwe relations. This office has evolved with the changing political and economic landscape in Zimbabwe, reflecting the broader strategic interests of China in Southern Africa.
topic: List of ambassadors of China to Zimbabwe

text: Koro Nulu, also known as Koro Ija, is a Plateau language of Nigeria, one of several languages which go by the ethnic name Koro. It is not closely related to other languages. It has very low (~ 7%) lexical similarity with Koro Zuba, which speakers consider to be a variant of the same language (along with the Jilic languages) due to ethnic identity. However, the Jilic languages are Plateau and Koro Zuba is apparently Nupoid, and Koro Nulu has yet to be classified.

Koro Nulu is an Austronesian language spoken by the Koro people in the province of Central Maluku in Indonesia. It is primarily used in the small island community of Koro, which is located near the larger island of Seram. The language is classified within the Malayo-Polynesian branch of Austronesian languages and is notable for its unique phonetic and grammatical features, distinguishing it from neighboring languages. Koro Nulu has relatively few speakers, leading to concerns about language endangerment as younger generations increasingly adopt dominant languages in the region. Efforts to document and preserve Koro Nulu are ongoing, reflecting a broader interest in linguistic diversity in Indonesia.
Macromphalina robertsoni is a species of very small sea snail, a marine gastropod mollusk in the family Vanikoridae.

Macromphalina robertsoni is a species of small sea snail belonging to the family Calyptraeidae, commonly known as slipper snails or American slipper shells. This marine gastropod mollusk is characterized by its distinctively shaped shell, which is typically rounded or flattened and can vary in color. M. robertsoni is found in shallow waters along the coasts of the eastern United States and parts of the Caribbean. The species plays a role in the ecosystem as a grazer on algae and is also of interest to marine collectors and researchers studying coastal biodiversity. Its habitat includes rocky substrates and sandy bottoms, where it can be observed at low tide.
The Uruguayan Championship 1919 was the 19th season of Uruguay's top-flight football league.

The 1919 Campeonato Uruguayo Primera División was the 17th edition of Uruguay's top football league, organized by the Uruguayan Football Association. The tournament featured several clubs competing in a league format. Club Nacional de Football emerged as champions, securing their 12th title in the history of the competition. The league was characterized by its competitive matches and notable player performances, contributing to the growth and popularity of football in Uruguay during this period.

1383 Limburgia, provisional designation 1934 RV, is a carbonaceous asteroid from the outer region of the asteroid belt, approximately 23 kilometers in diameter. It was discovered on 9 September 1934, by Dutch astronomer Hendrik van Gent at the Leiden Southern Station, annex to the Johannesburg Observatory in South Africa. It is named for the Dutch province Limburg.

The 1383 Limburgia refers to a conflict between the Duchy of Brabant and the County of Hainaut, currently part of modern-day Belgium, during the late medieval period. The conflict arose over territorial disputes and succession issues following the death of the Brabantian Duke. The term "Limburgia" specifically highlights the region of Limburg, which was a significant area of contention due to its strategic importance and economic wealth. The dispute culminated in a series of battles and diplomatic negotiations that underscored the complexities of feudal allegiances and territorial claims in the Low Countries during that era. Ultimately, the conflict was part of the broader political dynamics of the region, influencing future relations among the surrounding principalities.

Chegongmiao station is a station on Line 1, Line 7, Line 9 and Line 11 of the Shenzhen Metro. It is located underneath Shennan Road, at the west of Xiangmihu Road, in Futian District, Shenzhen, China. It is near China Merchants Bank Tower and Donghai Pacific Mall. It is the first four-line interchange station in the Shenzhen Metro, and the second in mainland China after the Century Avenue station of the Shanghai Metro. The Line 1 platforms opened on 28 December 2004, the Line 11 platforms opened on 28 June 2016 and the Line 7 and Line 9 platforms opened on 28 October 2016. This station provides cross-platform interchange between Line 7 and Line 9, but not between Line 1 and Line 11.

Chegongmiao station is a rapid transit station located in the Futian District of Shenzhen, China. It serves as an interchange between Line 1 and Line 7 of the Shenzhen Metro system. Opened in 2004, Chegongmiao station is situated near the bustling business area of Shenzhen and is known for its proximity to various commercial establishments and transportation hubs. The station features modern amenities, including escalators and elevators, to facilitate access for all passengers. Its strategic location makes it a key point in Shenzhen's urban transit network.

The musket Modèle 1777, and later Modèle 1777 corrigé en l'an IX (Model 1777 corrected in the year IX, or 1800 in the French Revolutionary Calendar) was one of the most widespread weapons on the European continent. It was part of a weapon family with numerous variants, e.g. for the light infantry, artillery and a musketoon for the cavalry.

The Musket Model 1777, also known as the French Model 1777, is a flintlock musket that was introduced by the French Army during the late 18th century. Designed primarily for infantry use, it featured a .69 caliber barrel and was capable of firing both round balls and paper cartridges. The Model 1777 was notable for its improved accuracy and reliability compared to earlier models and became standard issue for French soldiers during the American Revolutionary War and the Napoleonic Wars. Its design influenced later musket developments, and the Model 1777 remained in service until the advent of rifled firearms in the 19th century. Various iterations and modifications were produced, and the musket is recognized for its historical significance in European and military history.
Eugenia mabaeoides is a species of plant in the Myrtaceae. It is found only in Sri Lanka.

Eugenia mabaeoides is a species of flowering plant in the family Myrtaceae, native to tropical regions of Southeast Asia, particularly found in the Malay Peninsula and surrounding islands. It is characterized by its evergreen foliage, aromatic leaves, and small, edible fruits which resemble those of the mabea tree, giving rise to its specific epithet 'mabaeoides'. The plant can reach heights of up to 20 meters and is often cultivated for its fruit and ornamental value. Eugenia mabaeoides thrives in well-drained soil and a humid climate, making it a significant species in local agriculture and ecology. Conservation efforts are important for this species due to habitat loss and environmental changes impacting its native range.

Arctostaphylos osoensis is a species of manzanita known by the common name Oso manzanita. It is endemic to San Luis Obispo County, California, where it is known from only two occurrences on the northern edge of the Los Osos Valley. It grows exclusively on volcanic dacite from the Morro Rock-Islay Hill Complex associated with the Nine Sisters.

Arctostaphylos osoensis, commonly known as Oso Manzanita, is a species of flowering plant in the Ericaceae family. It is native to California, specifically found in the coastal regions and mountainous areas. This evergreen shrub typically reaches heights of 1 to 4 meters and is characterized by its reddish-brown, smooth bark and small, dark green leaves. The plant produces clusters of small, urn-shaped white to pale pink flowers, which bloom in the late winter to early spring, attracting various pollinators. Oso Manzanita is often found in chaparral and mixed evergreen forests and is valued for its ornamental qualities and as a wildlife habitat. Conservation efforts are important for its survival due to habitat loss and other environmental pressures.

San Sebastiano al Vesuvio (Neapolitan: San Bastiano) is a comune (municipality) in the Metropolitan City of Naples, located on the western slopes of Mount Vesuvius. Its elevation means that it is often a few degrees cooler than the neighbouring metropolis of Naples. In 1944 it was destroyed and two children were killed by the eruption of Mount Vesuvius during the Allies' occupation of southern Italy, but has since been rebuilt. The main attraction is the church of San Sebastiano Martire from the 16th century. Gaetano Filangieri, a Neapolitan jurist, was born in San Sebastiano al Vesuvius. He had an extensive correspondence with Benjamin Franklin.

San Sebastiano al Vesuvio is a comune (municipality) located in the Province of Naples, Campania, Italy. Situated at the foot of Mount Vesuvius, it is known for its scenic views and proximity to the famous volcano. The municipality covers an area of approximately 6.7 square kilometers and has a population of around 5,000 residents. San Sebastiano al Vesuvio features a mix of residential neighborhoods and agricultural land, with local industries including agriculture and small-scale manufacturing. The town is rich in history, with several historical sites and a vibrant local culture. It is well-connected to the surrounding areas by road and public transportation.

New Forms is the debut studio album by British drum and bass group Roni Size & Reprazent. It was released on 23 June 1997 through Talkin' Loud, and later re-released by Mercury Records and Universal Music Group. The album was released to critical and commercial success, winning the 1997 Mercury Prize, certifying platinum in the UK, and often being cited as their magnum opus. New Forms was promoted by the release of its four singles, "Share the Fall", "Heroes", "Brown Paper Bag" and "Watching Windows", all of which entered the UK singles chart and featured vocals from Onallee. The album features production credits from Roni Size, Krust, DJ Die and Suv.

New Forms refers to innovative artistic and cultural expressions that emerge in response to contemporary societal changes, technological advancements, and shifts in aesthetic values. This concept encompasses a variety of disciplines, including visual arts, music, literature, and performance, where traditional boundaries and genres are often blurred. Prominent in the late 20th and early 21st centuries, New Forms challenge established norms and encourage interdisciplinary collaboration, often embracing digital media and interactive experiences. Artists associated with New Forms frequently explore themes of identity, globalization, and the impact of technology on human experience, contributing to dynamic, evolving cultural landscapes.

Dastjerd (Persian: دستجرد; also known as Dastgerd and Dastgird) is a village in Karkas Rural District, in the Central District of Natanz County, Isfahan Province, Iran. As of the 2006 census, its population was 71, composed of 43 families.

Dastjerd is a historic village located in the Natanz County of Isfahan Province, Iran. Renowned for its picturesque landscapes and traditional Persian architecture, Dastjerd has been an important cultural and agricultural hub in the region. The village is notable for its ancient qanat irrigation system, which has been pivotal in supporting local agriculture in the arid climate of the area. Dastjerd has also attracted attention for its historical sites, including mosques and structures that reflect the rich heritage of Persian civilization. The village’s strategic location and historical significance make it a point of interest for both tourism and academic study.
Kidsgrove Town Hall is a municipal building in Liverpool Road in Kidsgrove, Staffordshire, England. The building, which is the meeting place of Kidsgrove Town Council, is locally listed.

Kidsgrove Town Hall is a municipal building located in Kidsgrove, Staffordshire, England. Constructed in the early 20th century, it serves as the formal headquarters for the local government and community activities. The town hall features a distinctive architectural style typical of the Edwardian period, characterized by its prominent façade and clock tower. It has been a central hub for civic events and local government meetings since its opening. The building is also notable for its historical significance within the community, representing local governance and civic pride in Kidsgrove. Over the years, it has undergone various renovations to preserve its structure and adapt to modern needs.

The 1968 All-Ireland Intermediate Hurling Championship was the eighth staging of the All-Ireland Intermediate Hurling Championship since its establishment by the Gaelic Athletic Association in 1961. London entered the championship as the defending champions. The All-Ireland final was played at Croke Park in Dublin on 29 September 1968 between London and Dublin, in what was their first ever meeting in the final. London won the match by 4–15 to 0–03 to claim their second All-Ireland title overall and a second title in succession.

The 1968 All-Ireland Intermediate Hurling Championship was the inaugural edition of the annual hurling competition for intermediate teams in Ireland, organized by the GAA. It featured teams from various provinces, with the championship culminating in a final held on September 8, 1968. The final saw Kerry emerge victorious against Antrim, winning their first title in this newly established competition. This championship aimed to provide a platform for teams not competing at the senior level to showcase emerging talent in the sport. The event was significant in promoting hurling at the intermediate level and set the foundation for future championships in subsequent years.

Dennis Haueisen (born 13 September 1978 in Gera) is a retired German professional road bicycle racer who was active between 2001 and 2008. He is a son of Lutz Haueisen, amateur cyclist who won two world titles in track events.

Dennis Haueisen is a German professional footballer, born on March 6, 1991, in Halle, Germany. He primarily plays as a defender and has played for various clubs in Germany's lower leagues. Haueisen began his career in youth football before advancing to senior teams, showcasing his versatility and defensive skills. He is known for his tactical awareness and ability to read the game, contributing both defensively and offensively during matches. His career has seen him participate in several regional leagues, highlighting his commitment to the sport despite not reaching top-tier professional status.
Meiothrips is a genus of thrips in the family Phlaeothripidae.

Meiothrips is a genus of thrips, small, slender insects belonging to the family Thripidae. This genus is characterized by its elongated body and distinctive wing structure, which features fringed edges. Meiothrips species are typically found in various habitats, including agricultural and garden settings, where they can affect plant health by feeding on leaves and flowers. They are known for their role in pollination and as potential pests of crops, leading to economic impact in horticulture. Research on Meiothrips contributes to the understanding of thrips biology, ecology, and their interactions with plants.

Axonopus is a genus of plants in the grass family, known generally as carpet grass. They are native primarily to the tropical and subtropical regions of the Americas with one species in tropical Africa and another on Easter Island. They are sometimes rhizomatous and many are tolerant of periodic submersion.

Axonopus is a genus of grass belonging to the family Poaceae, commonly found in tropical and subtropical regions. This genus includes several species, many of which are characterized by their adaptability to a variety of soil types and moisture levels. Axonopus species are often used in pasture management and soil conservation due to their ability to withstand grazing and improve soil structure. Notable species include Axonopus affinis and Axonopus compressus, which are frequently utilized in forage production. The genus is important in ecological restoration and landscape management, contributing to biodiversity and habitat stability.
The 48th Brigade was a brigade of the British Army.

The 48th Brigade was an infantry brigade of the British Army, part of the Territorial Force (TF) established in the early 20th century. It was formed in 1908 and operated as a unit within the 16th (Irish) Division during World War I. The brigade's units were primarily composed of volunteer soldiers from Ireland, and it participated in key battles on the Western Front, including the Battle of the Somme and the Battle of Messines. After the war, the brigade was restructured and eventually disbanded during the interwar period. It was reactivated during World War II but served in different configurations, primarily supporting various operations. The brigade symbolizes the contributions of local forces during major conflicts, representing the integration of civilian soldiers into the British military framework.
The 1st Tennessee and Alabama Vidette Cavalry served in the Union Army between September 10, 1863, and June 16, 1864, during the American Civil War.

The 1st Tennessee and Alabama Independent Vidette Cavalry was a cavalry regiment that served in the Confederate Army during the American Civil War. It was formed in 1863 from volunteers in Tennessee and Alabama, primarily composed of men who had experience in previous cavalry units or local militia. The regiment was tasked with reconnaissance, scouting, and light cavalry duties, operating primarily in the Western Theater of the war. The unit was known for its involvement in various engagements and skirmishes, providing essential intelligence and support to larger Confederate forces. Following the war, the regiment was disbanded, and its members returned to civilian life.

Mount Mather is a 12,096-foot (3,687 m) mountain in the Alaska Range, in Denali National Park and Preserve. Mount Mather lies to the northeast of Denali, overlooking Brooks Glacier. The mountain itself is covered by glaciers. Mount Mather was named in 1947 in honor of National Park Service director Stephen Mather.

Mount Mather is a prominent peak located in the Alaska Range of Alaska, United States. Standing at an elevation of 6,250 feet (1,905 meters), it is part of the Denali National Park and Preserve. The mountain is named after William A. Mather, the chief geologist of the U.S. Geological Survey’s Alaska Division. Its remote location and challenging terrain make it a less frequented destination for climbers compared to nearby peaks such as Denali. The surrounding area features stunning glacial landscapes and diverse wildlife, attracting outdoor enthusiasts and nature lovers. Mount Mather was first climbed in the 1950s, and it continues to be of interest for mountaineering and alpine exploration.
Henderson is a suburb of Perth, Western Australia, located within the City of Cockburn.

Henderson is a suburb located in the City of Kwinana, Western Australia, situated approximately 30 kilometers south of Perth's central business district. It is primarily an industrial area, home to the Kwinana Industrial Area, which hosts various manufacturing, petrochemical, and shipping facilities. The suburb is also notable for its proximity to the Australian Marine Complex, which supports shipbuilding and maintenance activities. Henderson was established in the late 20th century and features a mix of residential and commercial developments. It has access to the coast, providing various recreational opportunities, including water sports and fishing. Henderson is well-connected to the surrounding regions via major roads and public transportation services.

The 2005 North Carolina A&T Aggies football team represented North Carolina A&T State University as a member of the Mid-Eastern Athletic Conference (MEAC) during the 2005 NCAA Division I-AA football season. Led by third-year head coach George Small, the Aggies compiled an overall record of 3–8, with a mark of 2–6 in conference play, and finished tied for sixth in the MEAC.
topic: 2005 North Carolina A&T Aggies football team

text: The 2005 North Carolina A&T Aggies football team represented North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University in the Southern Intercollegiate Athletic Conference (SIAC) during the 2005 NCAA Division I FCS football season. Under the leadership of head coach Ken Morris, in his second year, the Aggies finished the season with a record of 5 wins and 6 losses. The team played its home games at Aggie Stadium in Greensboro, North Carolina. The 2005 season was notable for the Aggies as they aimed to improve upon previous performances and demonstrate the development of their football program.
topic: 2005 North Carolina A&T Aggies football team

text: The 1973 Anglo-Italian Cup was the fourth Anglo-Italian Cup competition. The European football competition was played between eight clubs from England and eight clubs from Italy and was the last professional Anglo-Italian Cup until it was re-incarnated in 1992.

The 1973 Anglo-Italian Cup was a football competition held between English and Italian clubs, featuring a series of matches across both countries. This edition was notable for its format, which included group stages followed by knockout rounds. The tournament consisted of teams from the English Football League and the Italian Serie A and aimed to enhance the footballing relationship between the two nations. Ultimately, it was won by Leeds United, who secured the title after defeating the Italian club Fiorentina in the final. The competition highlighted the growing interest in international club football during the 1970s, although it would not be continued in subsequent years.

Bensenville is a station on Metra's Milwaukee District West Line in Bensenville, Illinois. The station is 17.2 miles (27.7 km) away from Chicago Union Station, the eastern terminus of the line. In Metra's zone-based fare system, Bensenville is in zone 3. As of 2018, Bensenville is the 115th busiest of Metra's 236 non-downtown stations, with an average of 414 weekday boardings. As of February 15, 2024, Bensenville is served by 42 trains (20 inbound, 22 outbound) on weekdays, by all 24 trains (12 in each direction) on Saturdays, and by all 18 trains (nine in each direction) on Sundays and holidays. Bensenville station is located on the south side of Main Street across from the railroad tracks. A wide at-grade pedestrian crosswalk provides access between West Main Street and the station. Parking is available along the south side of West Main Street west of Addison Street, but also behind the station house at the intersection of Center & Railroad Streets. CPKC's Bensenville Yard is east of the station.

Bensenville station is a commuter rail station located in Bensenville, Illinois, primarily serving the Metra's Milwaukee District West Line. It is situated at 100 E. Main Street and features two side platforms and three tracks, facilitating passenger access to the region. The station was originally established in 1889 and has undergone various renovations to enhance service and accessibility. It provides connections for local commuters and supports transit-oriented development in the surrounding area. Bensenville station is equipped with parking facilities and is a key stop for daily travelers between Bensenville and downtown Chicago.
Dalida internationale is the eighth album by Dalida, released in 1961. Several of the singles were hits.

Dalida internationale refers to the global recognition and influence of the French-Italian singer and actress Dalida, who gained widespread fame in the 1950s and 1960s. Known for her powerful voice and emotive performances, she recorded songs in multiple languages including French, Italian, Arabic, Spanish, and English, appealing to diverse audiences. Dalida's career spanned over three decades, during which she released numerous albums and singles that became hits across Europe, the Middle East, and beyond. Her personal life, marked by tragedy, has further contributed to her enduring legacy, making her an iconic figure in the entertainment industry. Dalida's impact is celebrated through tributes, music festivals, and biographical works, solidifying her status as a cultural icon both in France and internationally.

"The Changingman" is a song by British singer-songwriter Paul Weller, released in April 1995 by Go! Discs as the lead single from his third solo album, Stanley Road (1995). The song charted at number seven on the UK Singles Chart.

"The Changingman" is a song written and performed by the British rock band The Jam, featured on their fifth studio album, "The Gift," released in 1982. The song was primarily composed by band frontman Paul Weller, who explored themes of personal transformation and social change, reflecting the band's evolving musical style and a move towards more introspective and abstract lyrics. "The Changingman" stands out for its blend of punk rock energy with new wave influences, characterized by its catchy melody and Weller's emotive vocal delivery. The track received critical acclaim and is regarded as one of The Jam's signature songs, reinforcing their status in the British rock scene of the late 20th century.

John A. Dietz (November 24, 1870 – October 11, 1939) was an American sport shooter who competed at the 1908 Summer Olympics and the 1912 Summer Olympics. He won two gold medals as part of the American pistol team. In 1908, he also competed in the individual pistol event and finished ninth. In 1912 he finished fourth in the 30 metre military pistol team event. He also competed in the 30 metre rapid fire pistol event finishing fourth and in the 50 metre pistol finishing again ninth.

John Dietz (born January 16, 1970) is an American former professional baseball player who played as a pitcher in Major League Baseball (MLB) for the Minnesota Twins during the 1991 season. Dietz was selected by the Twins in the second round of the 1988 MLB Draft. He made his MLB debut on September 8, 1991, and appeared in eight games that season. After retiring from professional play, Dietz transitioned to coaching and has been involved in youth baseball programs. His career in MLB, though brief, is noted for contributing to the Twins' efforts in a period leading up to their successful World Series championship in 1991.

Enišasi, was a city, or city-state located in the Beqaa Valley-(called Amqu, or Amka) of Lebanon, during the 1350-1335 BC Amarna letters correspondence. Of the 382–Amarna letters, Enišasi is only referenced in two letters. Enišasi was located near Hašabu, (Tell Hašbe), and Hasi, (Tell Hizzin?), southwest of Baalbek. Two 'mayors', or rulers of Enišasi were Šatiya and Abdi-Riša, who each authored a letter to the Egyptian pharaoh, EA 187- (title: A daughter sent to the Pharaoh), and EA 363- (title: A joint report on Amqu–(4)). (EA is for 'el Amarna'.)

Enišasi, also known as Enišasi of Uruk, is a figure from Sumerian mythology and literature, regarded as a goddess of the underworld and associated with death and the afterlife. Often depicted in ancient texts as a ruler of the netherworld, she plays a significant role in various myths, including the Epic of Gilgamesh. Enišasi is frequently linked with themes of judgment, fate, and the cyclical nature of life and death. Her character reflects the ancient Sumerians’ beliefs about mortality and the spiritual realm, emphasizing the importance of burial practices and the afterlife in their culture. As a divinity, she highlights the complexities of Sumerian spirituality and the cultural understanding of the afterlife during that era.
Pojo Municipality is the second municipal section of the Carrasco Province in the Cochabamba Department, Bolivia. Its seat is Pojo.

Pojo Municipality is a municipality located in the La Paz Department of Bolivia, known for its picturesque landscapes and agricultural activities. It encompasses the town of Pojo, which serves as the municipal seat. The region is predominantly rural, with a focus on farming and livestock production, benefiting from its fertile terrain and favorable climate. Pojo Municipality is characterized by its vibrant cultural heritage, which includes traditional festivals and local craftsmanship. The local government is responsible for administrative and developmental initiatives, aimed at improving the quality of life for its residents. The population is primarily composed of indigenous groups, contributing to the area's rich cultural diversity.

Dartmouth House is a Georgian house in Mayfair, central London, England. It now serves as the headquarters of the English-Speaking Union (ESU), an educational charity. It is located at 37 Charles Street, southwest of Berkeley Square. Over 40,000 people use the building each year. The original building was constructed in the mid 18th-century; what today comprises Dartmouth House was two separate residences, numbers 37 and 38 Charles Street. The first owner of number 37 was Henry Herbert, 2nd Earl of Carnarvon, from 1757 to 1776. In 1870, the banker Edward Baring bought both properties and on his creation as Baron Revelstoke in 1885, he converted the two houses into one to house his collection of French antique furniture and art. He remodelled and refurbished the house in a French Rococo style. There is a painted ceiling by Pierre-Victor Galland above the grand staircase. However, a crisis at Baring Brothers and Co Bank meant that spending on the buildings was curtailed and all building and design work ceased. Many of Lord Revelstoke's furnishings and objets d’art had to be sold at auction, although he continued to live here until his death in 1897. The next owner, William Legge, 6th Earl of Dartmouth, made the most significant changes to the interior of the house in 1900, with the creation of the Long and Small Drawing Rooms. The house was used as the Dartmouth family home until the outbreak of war in 1914, when it was used by the British Red Cross as a military hospital. It was sold again in 1918 to the Hon. Mrs Robert Lindsay. Dartmouth House was purchased by the English-Speaking Union in 1926 for the sum of £45,000 from the Hon. Mrs Robert Lindsay. It was formally opened as the London Headquarters of the ESU by the Prime Minister, Sir Stanley Baldwin, on the 22 February 1927. Dartmouth House has been listed Grade II* on the National Heritage List for England since December 1987.

Dartmouth House is a historic building located in London, England, which serves as the headquarters of the Royal Society of St. George. Designed by the architect Edward Glenny, it was completed in 1910 and is situated in the Mayfair area. The house features a mix of architectural styles, integrating elements of Edwardian and neo-Georgian design. It hosts various events, including meetings and social gatherings for members of the society, and is notable for its representation of Anglo-American relations, celebrating British heritage. The building is recognized for its cultural and historical significance, contributing to the social fabric of the community in the surrounding area.

NBI is short for the Nanomaterial-Biological Interactions Knowledgebase at Oregon State University, a repository for annotated data on nanomaterials characterization (purity, size, shape, charge, composition, functionalization, agglomeration state), synthesis methods, and nanomaterial-biological interactions (beneficial, benign or deleterious) defined at multiple levels of biological organization (molecular, cellular, organismal). Computational and data mining tools are being developed and incorporated into the NBI to provide a logical framework for species, route, dose, and scenario extrapolations and to identify key data required to predict the biological interactions of nanomaterials. Information currently being gained in the emerging field of nanotechnology is extremely diverse, including a multitude of widely varying nanomaterials that are being or will be tested in a broad array of animal systems and in vitro assays. Knowledge of nanomaterial-biological interactions will likely require inclusion and consideration of the entire body of data produced from global research efforts, which will allow the definition of nanomaterial structure-activity relationships. Such mathematical representations can be used to predict nanomaterial properties in the absence of empirical data.

The NBI Knowledgebase refers to a centralized repository of information managed by the National Bureau of Investigation (NBI) in the Philippines. It serves as a comprehensive source of data related to criminal cases, investigations, and law enforcement processes. The knowledgebase is utilized by NBI personnel to enhance investigative efficiency and improve the sharing of intelligence across various units within the agency. It also aims to aid in crime prevention and resolution by providing critical insights and information analytics. The system is part of the NBI's modernization efforts to leverage technology in its operations and enhance public safety.

Real is the eighth album by L'Arc-en-Ciel, released on August 30, 2000. It was the band's last original studio album before a prolonged hiatus. It reached number one on the Oricon chart and sold over a million copies, being certified by the RIAJ. The album cover features a photo taken by Mote Sinabel of a gargoyle found on the Notre Dame Cathedral in Paris, France. One of the singles from the album, "Stay Away", is a playable song in the computer game DrumMania 4th Mix.

"Real" is the seventh studio album by the Japanese rock band L'Arc-en-Ciel, released on March 16, 1998. The album features a mix of rock genres and includes hit singles such as "Tiger Cry," "Shout at the Devil," and "Fate." "Real" showcases the band's signature sound, characterized by emotionally charged lyrics and intricate instrumentation. The album received positive reviews from both fans and critics, further solidifying L'Arc-en-Ciel's status in the Japanese music scene. It achieved significant commercial success, contributing to the band's growing popularity.

The Power baronetcy, of Edermine in the County of Wexford, was created in the Baronetage of the United Kingdom on 18 October 1841 for John Power, a member of the Powers distilling dynasty. The 2nd and 3rd Baronets represented County Wexford in the House of Commons. The title became extinct on the death of the 6th Baronet in 1930.

The Power baronets of Edermine is a title in the Baronetage of the United Kingdom, created on 10 September 1841 for the Irish landowner and politician Sir John Power. The baronetcy was associated with Edermine House in County Wexford, Ireland. The Power family has a long history in the region, with Sir John being instrumental in local politics during his lifetime. The title has passed through several generations, maintaining a notable presence in Irish society. The family is recognized for its contributions to local governance and agriculture throughout the 19th and 20th centuries.

Rose Garden is an oil painting of a wild rosebush completed in 1882. It was created by American artist Maria Oakey Dewing, and is currently on display in the permanent collection of the Memorial Art Gallery in Rochester, New York.

Rose Garden (Dewing) is a notable historic landscape located in the East Bay region of California, renowned for its elegant design and diverse collection of rose species. Originating from the vision of landscape architect Frederick Law Olmsted Jr., the garden reflects early 20th-century horticultural practices and aesthetic principles. Established in the 1920s, it features meticulously arranged flower beds, walking paths, and ornamental features that enhance its serene beauty. The garden serves as a public space for enjoyment and education, promoting horticultural appreciation and environmental stewardship. It has been recognized as a significant cultural landmark and attracts visitors for its seasonal blooms and tranquil atmosphere.

TNT Sports (formerly Turner Sports) has occasionally televised college football games on its networks since 1982; that year, under an agreement with the NCAA, TBS became the first broadcaster to nationally televise college football on cable. After the NCAA broadcasting package was dismantled in 1984 following a Supreme Court ruling, TBS would broadcast SEC football from 1984 to 1992, along with selected bowl games through 2000. Regular-season college football returned to TBS in 2002 as part of a sub-licensing agreement with Fox Sports Net, broadcasting a package of Pac-10 and Big 12 games through 2006. In 2024, ESPN announced that it had reached an agreement with TNT Sports to televise College Football Playoff games on TNT beginning that season, with these games being a presentation of ESPN College Football rather than produced by TNT Sports. This was followed by its acquisition of a Mountain West Conference package on TruTV for the 2024 season, and a Big 12 package that premiered in 2025 as part of a sub-licensing agreement with ESPN.

College Football on TNT Sports refers to the coverage of college football games broadcast by the TNT Sports network, a service that emerged from a rebranding of the former BT Sport in the UK. Launched as part of a strategy to enhance sports programming, TNT Sports has included college football in its lineup, showcasing NCAA games and events, notably the American college football season. The network's coverage aims to provide fans with access to key matchups, highlight reels, and expert analysis of the games. TNT Sports has positioned itself as a significant player in the sports broadcasting landscape, expanding its offerings to include popular collegiate competitions.

A Luta Continua (Portuguese for The Struggle Continues) is a studio album by the Jamaican musician Big Youth, released in 1985. The title track is about the fight to end apartheid. Jonathan Demme was inspired to use the phrase in the credits of his movies Something Wild and Married to the Mob. In addition, the track "Feel It" is featured in Something Wild.

"A Luta Continua" is an album by the Angolan musical group Semba do Leste, released in 1974. The title translates to "The Struggle Continues" and reflects the socio-political context of Angola during its liberation movements against Portuguese colonial rule. The album blends traditional Angolan music styles, particularly semba, with revolutionary lyrics that resonate with the country’s fight for independence. It has been recognized for its cultural significance and contribution to the Angolan identity, showcasing the resilience and spirit of the Angolan people during a tumultuous period in their history. The album remains influential in the realms of both music and political activism.

The Sierra del Tontal is a mountain range in the southern part of San Juan Province, Argentina. The range is in the precordillera area of La Rioja, San Juan and Mendoza, in the Cuyo region. It extends south of the Sierra del Tigre, from which it is separated by the valley of the San Juan River. Its southern foothills are located in Mendoza Province. The city of San Juan is located east of the mountains and the Calingasta valley to the west. The range extends for about 100 kilometres (62 mi) in a northwest–southeast direction. The highest point is the Ojo de Agua mountain at 4,140 metres (13,580 ft). Most of the range extends to the south of this peak, and is over 4,000 metres (13,000 ft) in elevation. The rocks include greywackes and shales. The fossil remains of plants that probably date to the middle or upper Devonian period are found in the shales.

The Sierra del Tontal is a mountain range located in the northwest of Argentina, primarily within the province of Mendoza. Part of the Andes, this range is known for its rugged terrain and stunning landscapes, making it a notable feature of the region's geography. The Sierra del Tontal is characterized by a combination of high peaks, deep valleys, and diverse ecosystems, which provide habitat for various flora and fauna. The area is also popular for outdoor activities such as hiking, climbing, and wildlife observation. Due to its ecological significance and natural beauty, the Sierra del Tontal attracts both tourists and researchers interested in studying its unique environment.

Tuvshinbayar Naidan (Mongolian: Найдангийн Түвшинбаяр born 1 June 1984) is a Mongolian former professional judoka. He is the 2008 Olympic Champion, 2012 Olympic silver medalist, 2014 Asian games champion, 2017 World Championships bronze medalist, 2016 Asian Championships gold medalist, 2007 silver medalist and two-time (2008, 2011) bronze medalist in ‍–‍100 kg division. Naidan is serving a sixteen-year jail term since 2021 for killing fellow judoka and childhood friend Erdenebileg Enkhbat.

Naidangiin Tüvshinbayar is a Mongolian judoka who achieved international recognition for his accomplishments in the sport. Born on March 12, 1985, he is noted for winning the gold medal in the men's 100 kg division at the 2008 Beijing Olympics, making him the first Mongolian athlete to win an Olympic gold medal in judo. Tüvshinbayar has also secured multiple medals at the Asian Judo Championships and has represented Mongolia in various international competitions. His impact on judo in Mongolia and his Olympic success have made him a national sports hero.

19521 Chaos (provisional designation 1998 WH24) is a cubewano, a Kuiper belt object not in resonance with any planet. Chaos was discovered in 1998 by the Deep Ecliptic Survey with Kitt Peak's 4 m telescope. Occultations suggest it is a compact or contact binary equivalent to a sphere 400 to 500 km in diameter. On 20 November 2020, Chaos occulted a magnitude 16.8 star. Three observers detected the occultation, finding that the object is likely smaller than 600 km in diameter. Another occultation was recorded on 14 January 2022; full results on size, shape, geometric albedo, and the spin-axis orientation have not been released. A further occultation occurred on 28 September 2023, with a shadow crossing most of North America. This occultation was observed by over 30 observers; preliminary analysis suggests that Chaos is a binary (possibly a contact binary).

19521 Chaos is a main-belt asteroid that was discovered on October 9, 1998, by astronomers at the Near-Earth Asteroid Tracking (NEAT) program. It is classified as a member of the Hecuba family of asteroids and has a rather irregular shape. The asteroid orbits the Sun with an average distance of approximately 2.77 astronomical units and takes about 4.62 years to complete one orbit. Its size is estimated to be around 2.5 kilometers in diameter. The name "Chaos" reflects the object's unpredictable nature, in line with the mythological concept of chaos in creation.
Mary, Countess Cowper (née Clavering) (1685 – February 5, 1724) was an English courtier and diarist, and the wife of William Cowper, 1st Earl Cowper.

Mary Cowper (née Moore; 1733–1797) was an English poet and the wife of the prominent poet William Cowper. Born in Hertfordshire, she was originally from a well-connected family and had a significant impact on her husband's literary life. Mary supported William through his struggles with mental health and was a source of inspiration for many of his works. She herself published several poems, which reflect her experiences and sentiments. Mary Cowper is noted for her role in the literary circle of the time and her contributions to the promotion of her husband's writing. She is often recognized for her resilience and the profound influence she had on William Cowper's career.

Since April 2024, several Southeast Asian countries have experienced record-breaking temperatures which have left several people dead. Heat indices peaked at 53 °C (127 °F) in Iba in the Philippines on 28 April 2024. The heat wave has been attributed to a combination of causes, including climate change and El Niño. In some countries, the high heat has caused excessive energy demand. Drought conditions have worsened across the region. In Indonesia, dengue infections increased. In Myanmar, the heat wave exacerbated the humanitarian crisis caused by the ongoing civil war. Many government agencies have declared local emergencies and advised safety measures to protect residents from the heat, and some countries have closed schools or shortened their hours.

The 2024 Southeast Asia heat wave refers to an extreme weather phenomenon that impacted multiple countries in Southeast Asia during the summer of 2024. Characterized by unprecedented high temperatures and prolonged periods of heat, the heat wave led to increased health risks, including heat-related illnesses and fatalities among vulnerable populations. The event caused significant disruptions in agriculture, exacerbating food insecurity in the region, and strained water resources as demand for irrigation and potable water soared. The heat wave was attributed to a combination of climate change effects, El Niño conditions, and local meteorological patterns. Governments and organizations responded with emergency measures, including public health advisories, cooling centers, and agricultural assistance programs to mitigate the impacts. The event highlighted the need for improved climate resilience and adaptation strategies in the face of escalating weather extremes in Southeast Asia.

Vasia Tzanakari (born 1980) is a Greek writer. Born in Serres, she studied English at the Aristotelian University of Thessaloniki and completed postgraduate studies in Athens. She has published two books: Eleven little murders: Stories inspired by Nick Cave songs (2008) and a novel Johnny & Lulu (2011). She has also edited a music magazine, worked as a journalist, and translated literary works from English to Greek, for example, works by Ian Rankin and Gillian Flynn. Tzanakari lives in Athens. Her father, Vasilis Tzanakaris (born in Pentapoli, Serres) is also a writer.

Vasia Tzanakari is a Greek artist and designer known for her innovative work in contemporary art and design. Born in Greece, she has gained recognition for her unique approach that blends traditional Greek elements with modern techniques. Tzanakari's work often explores themes of identity, culture, and the interplay between the past and present. Her pieces have been exhibited in various galleries and art festivals, contributing to the discourse on contemporary art in Greece and beyond. In addition to her artistic endeavors, she is involved in community-based projects aimed at promoting art education and cultural exchange.
Glyphipterix ataracta is a species of sedge moth in the genus Glyphipterix. It was described by Edward Meyrick in 1888. It is found in New Zealand.

Glyphipterix ataracta is a species of moth belonging to the family Glyphipterigidae, commonly known for their intricate wing patterns and small size. This species was first described by the entomologist Edward Meyrick in 1931. Glyphipterix ataracta is primarily found in Australia, where it inhabits various environments. The adults are characterized by their slender bodies and distinctive wing structures, contributing to the ecological diversity of their habitats. As with other members of its family, G. ataracta plays a role in the ecosystem, although specific details about its behavior and life cycle remain limited.

Reñihúe Airport (Spanish: Aeropuerto de Reñihúe, (ICAO: SCRH)) is an airport serving the hamlet of Reñihúe, 41 kilometres (25 mi) north-northeast of Chaitén, a city in the Los Lagos Region of Chile. Reñihué is at the eastern end of Reñihué Fjord, which opens into the Gulf of Ancud. There is mountainous terrain in all quadrants except approaches to the airport along the fjord or along the Reñihué River valley. The Chaiten VOR-DME (Ident: TEN) is 19.5 nautical miles (36.1 km) southwest of the airport.

Reñihúe Airport (IATA: RHC, ICAO: SCRN) is a small regional airport located near the town of Reñihúe in the Los Ríos Region of southern Chile. Primarily serving domestic air traffic, the airport features a single runway suitable for small aircraft. Reñihúe Airport is known for its scenic surroundings, located near the picturesque Reñihué Lake and surrounded by mountainous terrain. It is mainly used for general aviation and facilitates access to remote areas, contributing to the local economy and tourism. As of the latest information, the airport does not operate on a regular commercial schedule, with flights being arranged on an ad-hoc basis.

Hermiston School District 8R is a public school district in Hermiston, Oregon, United States. For the beginning of the 2019–2020 academic year, enrollment totaled 5,809 students in Pre-K through 12th grades with one high school, Hermiston High School, two middle schools, and five elementary schools. Founded in the early 1900s, the school district covers the Hermiston proper bordering with the Umatilla School District to the West and the Stanfield District to the East.

The Hermiston School District is a public school district located in Hermiston, Oregon, serving the educational needs of students in grades K-12. Established in the late 19th century, the district operates several elementary, middle, and high schools, providing a comprehensive curriculum that includes academic, extracurricular, and vocational programs. The district is known for its commitment to improving student achievement and enhancing educational opportunities. It has implemented various initiatives to support diverse learners, including special education services and English language learner programs. The Hermiston School District is governed by a school board, and community involvement plays a significant role in its operations and decision-making processes.

The Izu-Jūkan Expressway (伊豆縦貫自動車道, Izu-Jūkan Jidōsha-dō) is an incomplete two-lane national expressway in Shizuoka Prefecture. It is owned and operated primarily by the Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism (MLIT), but has a short section maintained and tolled by the Central Nippon Expressway Company at its northern terminus with the Tōmei Expressway. The route is signed E70 under MLIT's "2016 Proposal for Realization of Expressway Numbering."

The Izu-Jūkan Expressway is a toll road in Japan that spans the Izu Peninsula in Shizuoka Prefecture. It connects the towns of Nishiizu and Ito, facilitating transportation and tourism in the region. The expressway is approximately 50 kilometers long and features scenic views of the coastline and mountainous terrain. Opened in stages starting in the late 1970s, it serves as a critical infrastructure to enhance accessibility and support local economies. The road is particularly popular among tourists visiting hot springs, beaches, and national parks in the Izu area.

U.S. Route 87 (US 87) is a part of the U.S. Highway System that travels Port Lavaca, Texas, north to Havre, Montana. In the state of Montana, It extends approximately 444 miles (715 km) from the Wyoming state line near Wyola north to its terminus at US 2 near Havre.

U.S. Route 87 (US 87) is a major north-south highway in the state of Montana, serving as an important transportation corridor. Stretching approximately 150 miles, it begins at the Wyoming state line near Cooke City and extends northward to the Canadian border at the Sweet Grass–Carway Port of Entry. The route passes through several key towns, including Billings and Great Falls, and intersects with other significant highways, enhancing regional connectivity. US 87 is notable for traversing diverse landscapes, including plains and foothills, and is utilized for both freight and recreational travel. The highway is part of the larger U.S. Highway System and has a rich history, having been established in the early 20th century.

Sir Talbot Sydney Duckmanton (25 October 1921 – 12 June 1995) was an Australian broadcaster and radio and television administrator. As general manager of the Australian Broadcasting Commission he oversaw the advent of colour television, ABC Classic FM and Triple J.

Talbot Duckmanton (1870–1945) was an English painter known for his vivid landscapes and depictions of rural life. Born in Derbyshire, Duckmanton developed a passion for art at a young age and later studied at the Royal Academy Schools in London. His work is characterized by a rich use of color and attention to natural detail, often capturing the essence of the British countryside. Duckmanton exhibited widely, including at the Royal Academy, and gained recognition for his contributions to regional art. In addition to painting, he was involved in art education, mentoring young artists. Duckmanton’s legacy endures through his works held in various public and private collections.
Pohwa Sunim (born July 15, 1955) is the head monk of the Young Pyung Sa International Zen Center in South Korea.

Pohwa is a traditional Korean dish usually prepared with thinly sliced pork or beef, marinated and grilled to enhance its flavor. It is characterized by its distinctive flavor, stemming from a marinade made of soy sauce, garlic, sesame oil, and other seasonings. Often served with a variety of side dishes (banchan), Pohwa is commonly enjoyed with rice and can be accompanied by dipping sauces. The dish is popular in Korean cuisine for its savory taste and is often served at family gatherings and celebrations. Variations may include different types of meats or additional ingredients such as vegetables.
Obaid Khalifa Mubarak Mesari (born April 13, 1985) is an Emirati football player who plays for Al Sharjah SC.

Obaid Khalifa is a prominent Emirati businessman and entrepreneur known for his significant contributions to the business landscape in the United Arab Emirates. He is notably recognized for his leadership role in several key industries, including real estate and finance. Khalifa has been instrumental in developing large-scale projects that have shaped urban development in the UAE. His business acumen and strategic investments have earned him a reputation as a forward-thinking leader in the Middle Eastern market. Additionally, he is involved in various philanthropic activities aimed at improving education and community welfare in the region.

In the United Kingdom, foundation schools are groups of medical education organisations in a region which work together to deliver the UK Foundation Programme, a form of postgraduate education for medical practitioners. These organisations include medical schools, local education and training boards, integrated care systems, and healthcare providers such as NHS trusts, primary care networks, local authority public health departments, and private or voluntary organisations such as hospices and care homes. As of 2025, there are 18 foundation schools covering the country:

A foundation school in the medical context refers to a training program for newly graduated medical doctors in the United Kingdom, typically lasting two years. It is part of the Foundation Programme, which serves as a transitional phase between medical school and specialty training. During this period, foundation doctors rotate through various specialties, gaining practical experience and enhancing their clinical skills. The program aims to prepare them for independent practice and further specialization, ensuring they meet required competencies and are equipped to deliver safe patient care. Foundation schools are linked to local health trusts and educational institutions, facilitating structured educational experiences and assessments.
Giorgos Pontikou (Greek: Γιώργος Ποντικού; born 8 January 2003) is a Cypriot footballer who plays as a left midfielder for Apollon Limassol in the Cypriot First Division.

Giorgos Pontikou is a notable figure in the field of Greek literature and culture, recognized for his contributions as an author, poet, and translator. His work often explores themes of identity, history, and the human experience, drawing from both contemporary and traditional influences. Pontikou has gained acclaim for his ability to weave personal narratives with broader societal issues, reflecting the complexities of modern Greek life. His literary style is marked by its lyrical quality and depth, earning him a dedicated readership. Throughout his career, he has been involved in various cultural projects and literary festivals, furthering the appreciation of Greek literature both nationally and internationally.

The weightlifting competition at the 2014 Central American and Caribbean Games was held in Veracruz, Mexico. The tournament was scheduled to be held from 20–23 November at the Coatzacoalcos Convention Center.
topic: Weightlifting at the 2014 Central American and Caribbean Games

text: Weightlifting at the 2014 Central American and Caribbean Games took place from November 17 to November 22 in Veracruz, Mexico. This edition of the Games featured competitions in various weight categories for both men and women. Athletes from multiple countries in the Central American and Caribbean region participated, competing for medals in snatch, clean and jerk, and total lifts. The event was significant for showcasing emerging talent in weightlifting and contributed to the development of the sport within the region. The medal tally was dominated by several countries, highlighting the competitive nature of weightlifting in Central America and the Caribbean.
topic: Weightlifting at the 2014 Central American and Caribbean Games

text: Jackson Township is one of the nine townships of Montgomery County, Ohio, United States. As of the 2020 census the population was 6,076.

Jackson Township is a township located in Montgomery County, Ohio, United States. As of the 2020 census, it had a population of 2,737 residents. The township is part of the Dayton metropolitan area and encompasses a mix of rural and suburban communities. Established in the early 19th century, Jackson Township is governed by a three-member board of trustees and has its own fiscal officer. It features various amenities, including parks and recreational facilities, and is characterized by a blend of residential developments and agricultural land. The township is named after President Andrew Jackson and is served by local schools within the Northmont City School District.

Oʻtgan kunlar is a 1969 Soviet-era Uzbek film directed by Yoʻldosh Aʼzamov, based on the 1925 novel Oʻtgan kunlar. The film stars Oʻlmas Alixoʻjayev and Gulchehra Jamilova in the main roles as the lovers Otabek and Kumush, with Abbos Bakirov, Rahim Pirmuhamedov, Nabi Rahimov, Habib Narimanov, Hamza Umarov, Razzoq Hamroyev, Razzoq Hamroyev, and Maryam Yakubova in supporting roles. Aʼzamov, Muhamedov, Bakirov, Hamroyev, Umrarov and the cameraman Mikhail Krasnyansky received the 1970 State Hamza Prize for their work in the film.

Oʻtgan kunlar is a 1978 Uzbek drama film directed by Yodgor Sa'dullayev. The film tells the story of a young man named Akmal, who grapples with the tensions of youth and the complexities of life in a rapidly changing society. Set against the backdrop of Uzbekistan's cultural landscape, Oʻtgan kunlar explores themes of love, loss, and the quest for identity. The film features a notable cast, including actors who portray the evolving dynamics between traditional values and modern influences in Uzbek life. It is recognized for its poignant storytelling and visual artistry, contributing to the canon of Soviet-era cinematic works.

Mount Eissinger (70°2′S 67°44′W) is a large ridge-like mountain at the north side of Riley Glacier on the west side of Palmer Land. The feature has a snow-topped upper surface, bare rock cliffs along the north side, and an impressive rectangular rock buttress rising in an unbroken, near-vertical sweep from the glacier to 500 metres (1,600 ft) at the west end. It was mapped by the United States Geological Survey (USGS) in 1974, and was named by the Advisory Committee on Antarctic Names for Karlheinz Eissinger, a USGS topographic engineer with the Ellsworth Land Survey party, 1968–69.

Mount Eissinger is a prominent mountain located in the Ellsworth Mountains of Antarctica, within the larger Sentinel Range. It rises to an elevation of 4,350 meters (14,272 feet) and is characterized by its rugged terrain and glaciated slopes. The mountain was named after Paul Eissinger, an early 20th-century American geologist. Mount Eissinger is primarily notable for its challenging climbing routes and significant geological features, making it a focal point for both scientific study and mountaineering expeditions in the harsh Antarctic environment. Its isolated position contributes to its unique ecological and climatic conditions.
Luis German Valdés Larralde, MD (March 27, 1923 – August 15, 2012) was a Cuban-American surgeon.

Luis Valdés is a prominent Chicano playwright, director, and producer, recognized for his significant contributions to Latino theater in the United States. Born on June 29, 1940, in La Loma, New Mexico, he became famous for his works that explore themes of identity, culture, and social justice within the Chicano community. Valdés is best known for founding El Teatro Campesino, a theater company founded in 1965 to support the United Farm Workers’ movement and empower farmworkers through art. His influential plays, including "Zoot Suit" and "The Sheik," have garnered both critical and popular acclaim, and he has received numerous awards for his work in the arts. Valdés continues to be an important figure in the promotion of Latino voices in American theater.

DeJon "D.J." Gomes (born November 17, 1989) is an American former professional football safety. He was selected in the fifth round of the 2011 NFL draft by the Washington Redskins. Gomes played college football for the University of Nebraska–Lincoln. He was also a member of the Detroit Lions.

DeJon Gomes is a former American football safety who played in the National Football League (NFL). Born on March 15, 1989, in Los Angeles, California, Gomes played college football at the University of Nevada, Las Vegas (UNLV). He was selected by the Washington Redskins in the fifth round of the 2011 NFL Draft. During his NFL career, Gomes also played for the Detroit Lions and had a reputation for being a versatile defensive back. Known for his physical playing style, Gomes contributed primarily on special teams and as a backup safety before retiring from professional football.

The Royal National College for the Blind (RNC) is a co-educational specialist residential college of further education based in the English city of Hereford. Students who attend the college are aged 16 to 25 and blind or partially sighted. They can study a wide range of qualifications at RNC, from academic subjects such as English and Mathematics to more vocational topics such as Massage and Complementary Therapies. Alongside regular further education subjects and vocational training, the college offers training in mobility, assistive technology, Braille, independent living skills and personal development. Founded in 1872 in London as the Royal Normal College and Academy for the Blind, the college had a number of homes before moving to its campus in Hereford; it was renamed The Royal National College for the Blind in the late 1970s. It has been a pioneer in the education of visually impaired people in Britain since the Victorian era, and, as of 2010, is the only college for visually impaired students in the United Kingdom to have been awarded Beacon Status in recognition of its outstanding teaching and learning. RNC hosts the UK's first VI Sports Academy, having begun as the home of the first football academy for visually impaired players and the England blind football team. It hosted the 2010 World Blind Football Championship and also served as a training facility for participants in the 2012 Paralympic Games. The college is actively involved in the development of assistive technology, including student participation in the Tech Novice Cafe, run for members of the public who are not confident in computer use. Two notable devices were developed at RNC; the Mountbatten Brailler, an electronic braille writer, and the T3, a talking tactile device that helped with the reading of maps and diagrams. Early in the 21st century, there was dramatic departmental restructuring at the college, and a significant redevelopment and modernisation of the Hereford campus. The campus, located on Venns Lane, Hereford, is home to RNC's teaching, residential and leisure facilities. Students live in halls of residence, which enable them to gain a level of independence within the college environment. RNC operates a leisure facility, thePoint4, which is open to the public. The college is a registered charity (number 1000388), and its patron is King Charles III. There are several high-profile supporters, including Dave Clarke, former captain of the England and Great Britain blind football teams. RNC has a number of notable people among its alumni, including former Home Secretary David Blunkett. The college was the subject of a 2007 film for the Channel 4 Cutting Edge documentary strand, which followed three students through their first term of study. The film won a 2008 Royal Television Society Award.
topic: Royal National College for the Blind

text: The Royal National College for the Blind (RNCB) is a leading educational institution in the United Kingdom dedicated to serving the needs of students with visual impairments. Founded in 1872 and located in Hereford, England, the college provides specialized education, vocational training, and support services to individuals aged 16 and over. RNCB aims to empower students through personalized learning programs that focus on independence, employability, and life skills.
