The college is known for its innovative approaches, including the use of assistive technology, and partnerships with various organizations to enhance educational outcomes and promote inclusivity for visually impaired individuals within the broader community.
topic: Royal National College for the Blind

text: HDEP-28 or ethylnaphthidate is a stimulant drug of the piperidine and naphthylaminopropane groups, closely related to ethylphenidate, but with the benzene ring replaced by naphthalene. It is even more closely related to HDMP-28, which acts as a potent serotonin–norepinephrine–dopamine reuptake inhibitor with several times the potency of methylphenidate and a short duration of action. It has been sold as a designer drug since around 2015.

HDEP-28 is a novel synthetic compound within the class of small molecules known for its potential applications in pharmacology and therapeutic development. It has been studied for its effects on various biological pathways, particularly in relation to cellular signaling and metabolic processes. Preliminary research indicates that HDEP-28 may exhibit anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective properties, prompting investigations into its use for treating conditions like neurodegenerative diseases and chronic inflammation. Current studies focus on optimizing its pharmacokinetic profiles and evaluating its safety and efficacy in clinical trials. Further research is necessary to fully understand its mechanisms of action and potential therapeutic benefits.

The Two Rivals (Spanish: Los dos rivales, Italian: Meravigliosa) is a 1960 Italian-Spanish film directed by Carlos Arévalo and Siro Marcellini and starring Luciano Tajoli, Rita Giannuzzi and Nunzio Gallo.

The Two Rivals is a 1960 French-Italian comedy film directed by André Hunebelle. The film stars Jacques Lussin, Jean-Claude Pascal, and Martine Carol, and revolves around a comedic rivalry between two characters vying for the affection of the same woman. Notable for its light-hearted tone and engaging performances, the film explores themes of love, competition, and friendship, set against a backdrop of humor and romance. Although not a major hit upon its release, it has garnered attention for its performances and production design typical of early 1960s European cinema.

The Conglomerate are a jazz quartet formed in 2004 as side project by Harry Angus on trumpet and vocals; and Ollie McGill on piano. At the time, both were also members of ska, jazz band, the Cat Empire. The pair were joined in the Conglomerate by Jules Pascoe on double bass and Harry Shaw-Reynolds on drums. The group released an independent album, Go to the Beach (5 September 2005), distributed by MGM. It was recorded at Sing Sing Studios, Melbourne. John Shand of The Sydney Morning Herald described their work as providing, "loose, rambunctious, celebratory excitement." The Conglomerate's second album, Hold Your Breath, was released on 28 March 2008.

The Conglomerate is an Australian music group formed in Sydney in 2020, known for its fusion of various musical genres, including rock, pop, and electronic music. The group consists of a diverse lineup of musicians hailing from different backgrounds, which contributes to their eclectic sound. The Conglomerate gained recognition for their innovative approach to songwriting and production, as well as their dynamic live performances. Their debut album, released in 2021, showcased a blend of catchy melodies and introspective lyrics, receiving critical acclaim and establishing them as a prominent act in the Australian music scene. The band continues to evolve, exploring new musical territories and collaborating with various artists.

The Home Government Association was a pressure group launched by Isaac Butt in support of home rule for Ireland at a meeting in Bilton's Hotel, Dublin, on 19 May 1870. The meeting was attended or supported by sixty-one people of different political and religious persuasions, including six Fenians, Butt seemingly having consulted with the Irish Republican Brotherhood before launching his initiative. Its inaugural public meeting was held on 1 September 1870. Active in campaigning in several elections for the association was P. F. Johnson. It became the Home Rule League in 1873.

The Home Government Association (HGA) was a political organization in the early 20th century, primarily active in the United Kingdom, advocating for the establishment of self-governing dominions within the British Empire. Founded in 1910, the HGA aimed to promote a decentralized form of governance, allowing colonies and territories to exercise greater autonomy while remaining part of the Empire. The organization played a significant role in influencing public opinion and political discourse regarding home rule, especially in regions like Ireland and India. Its activities contributed to the broader discussions surrounding colonial governance, leading to varying degrees of self-governance in several territories throughout the 20th century. The HGA's influence waned with the rise of nationalist movements and the eventual decolonization process after World War II.

"Mr. Tung Twista" is the first single released by Twista, who was then known as Tung Twista, from his debut album, Runnin' Off at da Mouth. It was released on November 22, 1991, shortly before Twista became the Guinness Book of World Records holder as the fastest emcee in 1992. A promotional music video was released and got played on shows such as Yo! MTV Raps and Rap City, but the single itself failed to make it to the Billboard charts.

Mr. Tung Twista, known simply as Tung Twista, is a fictional character often associated with the concept of the fast-talking, quick-witted storyteller in various forms of media. The character combines aspects of humor and charisma, typically engaging audiences through elaborate tales delivered at a rapid pace. He is often depicted as a performer or entertainer, captivating listeners with his unique style. The archetype reflects cultural themes around communication, performance art, and the social dynamics of storytelling. Tung Twista's influence can be seen in various entertainment platforms, emphasizing the importance of expression and oratory skills in contemporary culture.
Tourism in Alderney is promoted by Alderney Tourism.

Tourism in Alderney, one of the Channel Islands, is an essential part of its economy, attracting visitors with its natural beauty, historical sites, and outdoor activities. The island offers a range of attractions, including unspoiled beaches, coastal walks, and diverse wildlife, particularly birdwatching opportunities. Key historical sites include Victorian forts, the Alderney Trail, and the island’s unique maritime heritage. The tourism season tends to peak during the summer months, with visitors arriving mainly from the UK and France. Accommodations range from hotels to guesthouses, catering to different budgets. While tourism is not as developed as in other Channel Islands, Alderney promotes sustainability and conservation to preserve its natural and cultural resources.
Gredine (Serbian: Гредине) is a village in the municipality of Bugojno, Bosnia and Herzegovina.

Gredine is a municipality located in the autonomous province of South Tyrol, Italy. It is situated in the eastern Alps and is characterized by its mountainous terrain and scenic landscapes. The area is known for its agricultural practices, especially in the cultivation of grapes and various fruits, benefiting from the region's favorable climate. Gredine's cultural heritage reflects the influences of both German and Italian traditions, evident in its architecture, festivals, and local cuisine. The municipality is also a destination for outdoor activities such as hiking and skiing, attracting visitors to its natural beauty and recreational opportunities.

Rhythm, Salt and Pepper (Spanish:Ritmo, sal y pimienta) is a 1951 Argentine musical comedy film of the classical era of Argentine cinema, directed by Carlos Torres Ríos and starring Lolita Torres, Ricardo Passano and María Esther Gamas.

"Rhythm, Salt and Pepper" is a 1996 American comedy film directed by Charles Stone III. The film follows the escapades of two best friends, played by David Alan Grier and J. J. Abrams, who inadvertently become involved in a high-stakes drug operation while trying to create a profitable rhythm and blues act. It combines elements of comedy, music, and adventure, offering a unique take on friendship and ambition in the backdrop of the entertainment industry. The film is noted for its humorous dialogue and engaging performances, contributing to its status as a cult classic among fans of '90s cinema.
Rahmatullah Khairkhah (born 21 February 1996) is an Afghan footballer who plays as a midfielder for Afghan club Toofan Harirod and the Afghanistan national team.

Rahmatullah Khairkhah is an Afghan politician known for his roles in various governmental positions in Afghanistan, particularly during the transitional period following the fall of the Taliban in the early 2000s. He has been associated with initiatives aimed at reconstruction and development in the country, focusing on areas such as governance, education, and human rights. Khairkhah has also been involved in peace negotiations and efforts to stabilize Afghanistan, navigating complex political dynamics and the ongoing challenges faced by the nation. His contributions have been recognized within the context of Afghanistan's post-conflict recovery and democratic process.

Niko and the Sword of Light is an American flash animated television series co-produced by Amazon Studios and Titmouse, Inc. The series is based on an animated comic book of the same name and debuted on Amazon Prime Video as a pilot that premiered January 15, 2015. It was later picked up as a full series. On August 4, 2017, it was announced that the series was renewed for a second and final season. The first half of season 2 premiered on December 27, 2018. The series concluded on September 6, 2019.
topic: Niko and the Sword of Light

text: "Niko and the Sword of Light" is a 2018 animated adventure series created by the studio Studio Pupil. It follows the story of a young fox named Niko who embarks on a quest to retrieve the legendary Sword of Light and restore peace to his homeland, the Land of Light, which has been plunged into darkness. Accompanied by his friends, including a brave dog named Nika and a wise mentor figure, Niko faces various challenges and adversaries, including the sinister forces led by the evil overlord, the Darkness. The series is notable for its vibrant animation style and its themes of friendship, bravery, and self-discovery. It premiered on platforms such as YouTube, garnering a following for its engaging narrative and well-developed characters.
topic: Niko and the Sword of Light

text: Aynalem Eshetu Shefrawe (born 5 February 1992) is an Ethiopian race walker. She has won multiple medals on a continental level. She currently holds several national records at various distances.

Aynalem Eshetu is an Ethiopian long-distance runner who specializes in road running and cross-country events. Born on 13 November 1991, she gained international recognition for her performances in various marathons and half marathons, representing Ethiopia in numerous competitions. Eshetu has achieved notable placements in major races, contributing to Ethiopia's reputation as a powerhouse in long-distance running. Throughout her career, she has been celebrated for her athletic prowess and has earned several podium finishes in international events.

Susanna Gossweiler (1740–1793) was a Swiss educator. She was appointed the first principal of the girls' school Töchterschule in Zürich in 1774, and is regarded as a pioneer of women's education in Switzerland.

Susanna Gossweiler (born September 3, 1930) is a prominent Swiss politician and member of the Swiss Federal Council, representing the Social Democratic Party. She served as a Councilor from 1981 to 1993, becoming the first woman to head the Federal Department of Transport, Communications and Energy. Gossweiler was known for her advocacy of social justice, environmental issues, and women's rights. After retiring from political office, she remained active in various social initiatives and organizations, contributing to public discourse in Switzerland. Her legacy includes significant advancements in infrastructure and communication policies during her tenure.

The California Central Valley grasslands is a temperate grasslands, savannas, and shrublands ecoregion in California's Central Valley. It a diverse ecoregion containing areas of desert grassland (at the southern end), prairie, savanna, riparian forest, marsh, several types of seasonal vernal pools, and large lakes such as now-dry Tulare Lake (which was the largest freshwater lake in the United States west of the Mississippi), Buena Vista Lake, and Kern Lake. The Central Valley has been greatly altered by human activity. Most of the land has been converted to farms, pastureland, or cities. Most of the valley's wetlands have been drained. The introduction of exotic plants, especially grasses, has altered the region's ecology.

The California Central Valley grasslands are a significant ecological region located within the Central Valley of California, characterized by expanses of grass and herbaceous plants. Historically, these grasslands supported diverse flora and fauna, including numerous endemic species. The region's climate is Mediterranean, featuring hot, dry summers and mild, wet winters, which supports the growth of native grasses such as purple needlegrass and wild oats. Due to extensive agricultural development, urbanization, and land conversion, much of the original grassland habitat has been diminished, leading to the decline of several species and changes in soil and ecosystem health. Conservation efforts are underway to restore and protect the remaining grasslands and their biodiversity. The region plays a crucial role in carbon sequestration and serves as a vital habitat for wildlife, including migratory birds and small mammals.

The Coma Star Cluster (also known as Melotte 111 or Collinder 256) is a nearby open cluster located in the constellation Coma Berenices. The cluster contains about 40 brighter stars (between magnitudes 5 and 10) with a common proper motion. The brighter stars of the cluster make out a distinctive "V" shape as seen when Coma Berenices is rising. The cluster used to represent the tail of Leo. However, in around 240 BC, Ptolemy III renamed it for the Egyptian queen Berenice's legendary sacrifice of her hair. The Hipparcos satellite and infrared color-magnitude diagram fitting have been used to establish a distance to the cluster's center of approximately 86 parsecs (280 ly). The distance established via the independent analyses agree, thereby making the cluster an important rung on the cosmic distance ladder. The open cluster is roughly twice as distant as the Hyades and covers an area of more than 7.5 degrees on the sky. The cluster is approximately 450 million years old.

The Coma Star Cluster, also known as Coma Berencis, is a prominent open cluster located in the constellation Coma Berenices. It consists of approximately 40 stars that are relatively young and unassociated with the Milky Way's main population of stars. The cluster is situated about 300 light-years away from Earth and spans an area of roughly 6 degrees in the night sky, making it a notable component of the Coma Berenices constellation. The prominent stars within the cluster include several bright blue-white stars, which are indicative of its youthful age. The Coma Star Cluster is best viewed in the spring months and is often studied by astronomers for insights into stellar formation and evolution.

The Air Mail Act of 1925, also known as the Kelly Act, was a key piece of legislation that intended to free the airmail from total control by the Post Office Department. In short, it allowed the Postmaster General to contract private companies to carry mail. The Act was sponsored by Pennsylvania representative Clyde Kelly, and became legislation in February that year. The act created a bidding period for small airmail routes, setting rates and subsidies contractors would receive for flying mail. The first contracts were awarded to Colonial Air Transport, National Air Transport, Robertson Aircraft Corporation, Western Air Express and Varney Air Lines. Contractors were paid $3.00 per pound of mail for the first 1,000 miles traveled. Due to the surplus aircraft available after the First World War, particularly de Havilland DH-4s, the act bolstered a nascent aviation industry in the United States. By 1927, over 2.5 million miles were traveled by US Airmail Service planes, carrying over 22 million letters. Further regulation ensued quite rapidly, such as those issued by second assistant postmaster general Col. Paul Henderson, which required pilots and their aircraft to receive a certificate of airworthiness from the Post Office, and that each company needed to post at least ten thousand dollars in good faith bonds.

The Air Mail Act of 1925 was a significant piece of legislation in the United States that aimed to promote and expand the use of air transportation for mail delivery. Signed into law by President Calvin Coolidge on February 2, 1925, the act authorized the U.S. Post Office to enter into contracts with private airlines for the transportation of mail, thereby facilitating the establishment of a commercial air mail service. This legislation marked a turning point in the development of aviation, contributing to the growth of the airline industry and setting the foundation for airmail services that would enhance postal efficiency and connectivity across the country. The act also included provisions for setting rates and regulations governing air mail operations. It provided a framework that ultimately paved the way for the later expansion of air travel and commerce in the United States.
Traveller Supplement 1: 1001 Characters is a 1978 role-playing game supplement for Traveller published by Game Designers' Workshop.

Traveller Supplement 1: 1001 Characters is a supplemental book for the science fiction role-playing game Traveller, published by Game Designers' Workshop (GDW) in 1979. This supplement provides a comprehensive list of characters that players can use to enhance their gameplay experience. The character entries include detailed descriptions, backgrounds, and statistics, allowing for rich NPC interactions and world-building within the Traveller universe. The book is notable for offering a wide variety of character types, ranging from military personnel to merchants and scholars, catering to diverse storytelling possibilities. As a resource, it has been used by both players and game masters to inspire creativity and expand the narrative dimensions of their Traveller campaigns.
Weldon Watson (born December 8, 1947) is an American politician who served in the Oklahoma House of Representatives from the 79th district from 2006 to 2018.

Weldon Watson (born November 29, 1944) is a prominent American artist known for his contributions to contemporary visual arts, particularly in painting and mixed media. Based in New York City, Watson's work often explores themes of identity, culture, and the human experience, utilizing vibrant colors and innovative techniques. He has exhibited in numerous galleries and museums throughout the United States and internationally, receiving critical acclaim for his unique style and thought-provoking subjects. Watson is also recognized for his role in mentoring emerging artists and his involvement in art advocacy.
The following is a list of endemic bird areas of the world, as defined by Birdlife International – see main article (Endemic Bird Area).
topic: List of endemic bird areas of the world

text: The List of endemic bird areas of the world identifies regions where unique bird species are found, highlighting areas with high biodiversity and significant conservation value. These areas, often characterized by unique habitats and geographical isolation, are critical for the survival of many bird species that do not occur elsewhere. The list aids in prioritizing conservation efforts and understanding avian biodiversity, as it includes various ecological regions, such as islands and mountainous terrains, where specific environmental conditions foster distinct avian populations. The identification of these endemic bird areas is essential for policymakers and conservationists in designing effective strategies to protect threatened species and their habitats.
topic: List of endemic bird areas of the world

text: Robert Barrington "Barry" Scott (9 October 1916 – 6 April 1984) was an Australian cricketer. He played first-class cricket for Victoria between 1935 and 1940 and for New South Wales in 1940–41.

Barry Scott is a former English cricketer born on August 27, 1963, in Colchester, Essex. He was a right-handed batsman and a right-arm fast-medium bowler. Scott played for Essex County Cricket Club from 1981 to 1986 and made his first-class debut in 1982. Throughout his career, he appeared in a total of 60 first-class matches, in which he scored over 1,400 runs and took 99 wickets. He also represented England in Under-19 matches. After retiring from professional cricket, Scott pursued a career in coaching and has been involved in developing young cricketers.
Liberty is a town in Randolph County, North Carolina, United States. As of the 2020 census, Liberty had a population of 2,655.

Liberty is a town located in Randolph County, North Carolina, United States. As of the 2020 census, it had a population of 2,074 residents. Founded in the early 1800s, Liberty has a rich history tied to various agricultural developments and the establishment of local businesses. The town is known for its small-town charm, community events, and proximity to natural attractions, including parks and recreational areas. Liberty is also part of the Greensboro-High Point metropolitan area, contributing to its growth and development in recent years.

The 2021 UAB Blazers football team represented the University of Alabama at Birmingham (UAB) as a member of the West Division in Conference USA (C-USA) during the 2021 NCAA Division I FBS football season. Led by Bill Clark in his sixth and final season as head coach, the Blazers compiled an overall record of 9–4 with a mark of 6–2 in conference play, placing second in C-USA's West Division. UAB was invited to the Independence Bowl, where the Blazers defeated BYU. The team played home games at the newly-opened Protective Stadium in Birmingham, Alabama.

The 2021 UAB Blazers football team represented the University of Alabama at Birmingham (UAB) during the 2021 NCAA Division I FBS football season. Under the leadership of head coach Bill Clark, in his seventh season, the Blazers played their home games at Protective Stadium in Birmingham, Alabama. UAB was a member of Conference USA (C-USA) and finished the season with a record of 8 wins and 4 losses. The team had a strong showing in the conference, and they secured a spot in the Bahamas Bowl, where they faced off against the Malvern Minutemen. The 2021 season continued to build on UAB's resurgence in football after the program’s reinstatement in 2017, solidifying their position as a competitive force within the conference.

Liberty Grove is a town in Door County, Wisconsin, United States. The population was 2,096 at the 2020 census. The unincorporated communities of Ellison Bay, Gills Rock, North Bay, Northport, and Rowleys Bay are in the town.

Liberty Grove is a town in Door County, Wisconsin, United States. Established in the late 19th century, it was officially organized in 1880. The town is known for its scenic beauty, including views of Green Bay and access to various recreational activities, such as boating and fishing. Liberty Grove includes the communities of Ellison Bay, Gills Rock, and a portion of Sister Bay, and it is part of the Door County peninsula, a popular tourist destination. The population of Liberty Grove is approximately 1,000 residents, with an emphasis on preserving its natural environment and community heritage. The town hosts various events and festivals that promote local culture and tourism.
Kava (Persian: كوا) is a village in Ashrestaq Rural District of Yaneh Sar District in Behshahr County, Mazandaran province, Iran.

Kava is a village located in the Fars Province of Iran, specifically within the Kavar County. It is part of the Central District and the Kava Rural District. According to the 2006 census, Kava had a population of 1,130 residents, comprised of 264 families. The village is characterized by its traditional Persian architecture and agricultural practices, with the surrounding area known for its diverse landscapes including mountains and valleys. Kava serves as a representation of rural life in Iran, contributing to the cultural and historical tapestry of the Fars region.
Marie Dorin Habert (French pronunciation: [maʁi dɔʁɛ̃ abɛʁ]; born 19 June 1986) is a French retired biathlete.

Marie Dorin Habert is a retired French biathlete, born on March 22, 1986, in Chambéry, France. She has achieved notable success in her sport, including participating in multiple World Championships and the Winter Olympics. Dorin Habert is recognized for her achievements in the Women's Relay and Individual events, contributing to France's prominence in biathlon. Beyond her athletic career, she has also transitioned into roles in sports commentary and advocacy. Known for her strong performances and competitive spirit, she remains a respected figure in the world of biathlon.

The Kali Panchwin, now formally called the 2nd Battalion, Maratha Light Infantry Regiment, is one of the oldest battalions of the Indian Army. It consists of troops known as Ganpats from Maharashtra in Western India.

The 2nd Battalion, Maratha Light Infantry Regiment, is a prestigious infantry battalion of the Indian Army, part of the Maratha Light Infantry, which is one of the oldest regiments in the Indian Armed Forces. Formed on 1st September 1942 during World War II, the battalion has participated in various significant operations, including the Indo-Pakistani wars and peacekeeping missions. Known for its exceptional performance and valor, the battalion has earned numerous commendations and accolades, reflecting its commitment to duty and excellence. The unit is recognized for its distinct traditions and is headquartered in the state of Maharashtra, contributing to India's defense and security efforts through its skilled and dedicated personnel.
Neoptychodes trilineatus is a species of flat-faced longhorn beetles in the subfamily Lamiinae.

Neoptychodes trilineatus is a species of beetle belonging to the family Cerambycidae, which is characterized by long antennae and often vibrant coloration. This species is endemic to various regions of Southeast Asia, where it typically inhabits forested areas. N. trilineatus exhibits a distinctive three-striped pattern on its elytra, which can serve as a form of camouflage against predators. Like other members of its family, the larvae of N. trilineatus are wood-borers, developing primarily in dead or decaying wood. The species plays a role in the ecosystem by contributing to the decomposition process and nutrient cycling. Little is known about its population trends or specific conservation status.

The Southbridge-Sargent Manufacturing District encompasses a collection of three 19th-century factory buildings near a historically important railroad junction in southern Worcester, Massachusetts. They were built near the intersection of Sargent and Gold Streets, just south of Southbridge Street. The location is close to a junction of three major railroads: the Boston and Albany, the Norwich and Worcester, and the New York, New Haven, and Hartford. The area was once a major industrial part of the city, but has lost many of its historic factory buildings in the 20th century. The most significant building in the district is the former Sargent Card-Clothing Factory, 300 Southbridge Street. This Second Empire three story brick structure, built in 1866, is one of the oldest surviving factory buildings in the city. It was built for the Sargent brothers, leading manufacturers of card making machinery, and is built to a plan that was used, with some modifications, for later purpose-built factory buildings in the city. The Rice and Griffin Factory at 5 Sargent Street was built c. 1890. It was originally three stories with a pitched gable roof, but the roof was removed and a fourth floor added. Rice and Griffin was founded in 1866, and was listed at that address from 1872 until 1905. The company's principal business was the production of architectural building parts, and it is probable that their products decorated a significant number of Worcester's Victorian houses. The third factory, at 125 Gold Street, was built c. 1892 for the Whitcomb Manufacturing Company. It is a utilitarian two story brick building. The company began operations in space rented at 5 Sargent Street before building these premises. The company produced metalworking machinery, including planers, shearing and punching tools. The district was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1980.

The Southbridge-Sargent Manufacturing District is a designated historic district located in Southbridge, Massachusetts. Recognized for its significance in the industrial development of the town, the district encompasses a collection of manufacturing buildings primarily constructed between the late 19th and early 20th centuries. The area reflects the historical influence of the textile and shoe industries that were prevalent in Southbridge during this period. Characterized by its diverse architectural styles, the district showcases structures that exemplify the industrial aesthetic of the era, including brick factories and warehouses. The district is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, highlighting its importance to the cultural and economic history of Southbridge and the broader region. Efforts have been made to preserve the district's historical character amid modern development.

The 1954–55 County Antrim Shield was the 66th edition of the County Antrim Shield, a cup competition in Northern Irish football. Linfield won the tournament for the 22nd time, defeating Crusaders 3–0 in the final at Solitude.

The 1954–55 County Antrim Shield was the 79th edition of the annual knockout football competition held in County Antrim, Northern Ireland. The tournament featured several clubs from the Irish Football Association, competing for the trophy. The final took place on 22 May 1955, where Glentoran emerged victorious against their rivals, securing the championship title. This victory added to Glentoran's historical successes in the competition, further cementing their status in Northern Irish football. The tournament showcased the competitive spirit of local clubs, highlighting the significance of the County Antrim Shield in the regional football calendar.
János Kelen (11 February 1911 – 13 October 1991) was a Hungarian long-distance runner who competed at the 1936 Summer Olympics.

János Kelen (born 1949) is a Hungarian physicist and educator known for his contributions to the field of nuclear physics and his work in educational reform. He received his PhD in physics from Eötvös Loránd University and later became a prominent figure in Hungarian scientific circles. Kelen has been involved in various research projects related to particle physics and has published numerous articles in peer-reviewed journals. In addition to his research, he has played a significant role in promoting science education in Hungary, advocating for modern teaching methods and curriculum development in physics. Kelen has also participated in international scientific collaborations and conferences, furthering his influence in the global scientific community.

This is a list of civic sheriffs and high sheriffs of the County of the City of Bristol, England. The office of high sheriff is over 1,000 years old, with its establishment before the Norman Conquest. The high sheriff remained first in precedence in the counties until the reign of Edward VII when an Order in Council in 1908 gave the lord-lieutenant the prime office under the Crown as the sovereign's personal representative. The high sheriff remains the sovereign's representative in the county for all matters relating to the judiciary and the maintenance of law and order. Bristol is unusual in having had county status since medieval times (1373). The Lord Mayor and one or sometimes two sheriffs served as part of its civic governance. The county was expanded to include suburbs such as Clifton in 1835, and it was named a county borough in 1889, when the term was first introduced. However, on 1 April 1974 it became a part of the local government county of Avon. On 1 April 1996 Avon was abolished, allowing the city of Bristol to become a unitary authority and gain its ceremonial independence and county status as the County of City of Bristol, with the royal appointments of Lord-Lieutenant and High Sheriff.

The High Sheriff of Bristol is a ceremonial position recognized by the monarchy, representing the sovereign in the county of Bristol, England. The role dates back to medieval times and was originally created to uphold law and order in the area. The High Sheriff is responsible for various judicial and ceremonial duties, including attending court sessions and supporting local justice initiatives. The appointment is made annually, and the officeholder is typically a notable local figure. Although the role has diminished in terms of administrative power, it remains an important symbol of civic pride and community engagement within the city. The High Sheriff of Bristol also plays a role in charities and community events, reflecting the evolving nature of the position.

The Silver Pit is a long valley in the bed of the North Sea, 45 km (28 mi) east of Spurn Head in England. At some point in time the Silver Pit was part of the valley of the Wash River. Also notable is the Silverpit crater, a suspected impact crater located north east of the Silver Pit and named after it, discovered in 2002. The Outer Silver Pit also lies to the north east.

The Silver Pit is a geological depression located in the North Sea, situated approximately 150 kilometers east of the Yorkshire coast. It is a significant feature of the North Sea Basin, formed during the Late Cretaceous period and characterized by its distinct geological formations, including sedimentary rocks and potential hydrocarbon deposits. The area has been the subject of exploration for oil and gas resources, contributing to the understanding of the region's geological history. Additionally, the Silver Pit is known for its rich marine biodiversity, serving as a habitat for various species of fish and other marine life. Its ecological significance, alongside its geological and economic potential, makes it a point of interest for scientific research and environmental conservation efforts.
The Battle of Gedaref was fought on 22 October 1898 to relieve the besieged British garrison at Gedaref.

The Battle of Gedaref was a military engagement that occurred during the Second Sudanese Civil War near the town of Gedaref in eastern Sudan. Fought on 18 November 1989, it involved Sudanese government forces against the Sudan People's Liberation Army (SPLA). The battle was part of the broader context of the conflict, which pitted the northern government against southern rebel forces. The SPLA sought to gain control over strategic locations to enhance their military position. Despite initial gains by the SPLA, the government forces, bolstered by better resources and support, ultimately managed to stabilize the area. The battle underscored the ongoing tensions and complexities of the Sudanese civil conflict, contributing to the protracted violence in the region.
World Tour Golf is a sports video game by Evan Robinson, Nicky Robinson, and Paul Reiche III which was published by Electronic Arts in 1986 for Commodore 64, Amiga, Apple IIGS, and MS-DOS.

World Tour Golf is a series of golf video games developed by the multimedia company, is a notable franchise in the sports game market that provides players with a realistic simulation of professional golf. Launched initially in the late 1990s, the franchise is known for its detailed graphics, various course recreations, and endorsements from professional golfers. The series has evolved to include gameplay modes such as tournaments, career progression, and multiplayer options, appealing to both casual players and golf enthusiasts. Over the years, World Tour Golf has been released on multiple gaming platforms and has contributed to the popularity of golf games in the digital entertainment industry.
Pauanui Aerodrome (ICAO: NZUN) is a small aerodrome located in the middle of the Pauanui Beach township on the Coromandel Peninsula of the North Island of New Zealand.

Pauanui Aerodrome is a small public-use airport located in Pauanui, a coastal town in the Thames-Coromandel District of New Zealand. It serves as a base for general aviation and recreational flying, offering a grass runway and basic facilities for pilots and passengers. The airport is situated near the Pacific Ocean, making it a popular destination for both local aviators and tourists. While primarily used for light aircraft, Pauanui Aerodrome is also known for its scenic flights and is often utilized for flight training and private aviation services. Its proximity to beach resorts contributes to its appeal among visitors to the region.
Gaetano Thiene (born July 1, 1947) is an Italian Emeritus Professor of Cardiovascular Pathology at the University of Padua. His professional interests include cardiology and pathology.

Gaetano Thiene, also known as Saint Cajetan, was an Italian priest and co-founder of the Theatines, a religious order dedicated to the reform of the clergy and the promotion of spiritual renewal in the Catholic Church during the Counter-Reformation. Born on December 1480 in Vicenza, Italy, he was a prominent figure in the religious revival of the 16th century. Thiene emphasized the importance of personal piety, prayer, and charitable works. He played a key role in establishing institutions for the education of clergy and laity, and he also worked to address the issues of poverty and social injustice in his community. He is venerated as a saint in the Catholic Church, with his feast day celebrated on August 7. Thiene's legacy continues through the ongoing work of the Theatines and their influence on Catholic education and reform.

Original righteousness is a concept variously appraised in Roman Catholic and Reformed theology relating to the Edenic state of sinlessness. According to this doctrine, Adam and Eve were created without sin (a point that all Christian churches agree upon), and this original righteousness meant that a number of conditions that are now "natural" did not apply. Humans were, in such a state, united with their essence, and therefore their forms were, as their spirits, untainted. The Adamic man was immortal, experienced no excess of desire (in either lust or gluttony or greed), and had neither obesity nor starvation. Where Roman Catholicism believes the original moral state of humanity to have been the result of a donum superadditum, humanity having been created in a morally-neutral state with an extra grace that was rescinded at the fall, Reformed theology holds to original righteousness as facultative and an inherent personal quality of goodness obtained at creation, a donum concreatum. However, even within scholastic and Roman Catholic theology, the precise relationship between a donum superadditum and original righteousness is debated. The concept of original righteousness shows up throughout discussions of the lost Eden in Western literature. The twentieth-century poet Gerard Manley Hopkins's poetry consistently addresses the implications of "natural" and "nature" falling with man's sin. The fall of man is, in this theological scheme, a fall of nature as well (for nature, too, was perfect and essential only in the Garden of Eden), and man's own sin causes it to contain evil. Additionally, the loss of original righteousness is important to both concepts of what the essence of man is (e.g. whether man is essentially perverse or some righteousness remains in a mitigated form), the nature of the resurrection of the dead into perfected flesh at the last judgment (e.g. whether it is a figurative or literal recasting of perfected bodies for the risen), and the nature of the forcefulness of grace (e.g. if man's soul is entirely depraved, then grace must carry more force than if man has an echo of original righteousness). Few churches disagree with the fundamentals of the concept of original righteousness, but the exact nature of the human in a state of original righteousness is rejected or amplified by some denominations. Some of those who deny the Augustinian doctrine of the transmission of original sin hold that infants are in pure righteousness; although this view is not widespread, it has been used in discussions of infant baptism.

Original righteousness refers to the state of purity and moral integrity that humanity possessed before the Fall, according to various religious traditions, especially in Christianity. It denotes the innate goodness and perfect relationship with God that Adam and Eve had in the Garden of Eden. This concept emphasizes that human beings were created in the image of God, reflecting His righteousness and holiness. The loss of original righteousness is associated with the original sin, which resulted in a fallen state of humanity characterized by moral corruption and separation from God. Interpretations of original righteousness vary among different denominations and theological frameworks, influencing doctrines of sin, redemption, and human nature.
Katsuya Yokoyama (横山 勝也, Yokoyama Katsuya; 2 December 1934 – 21 April 2010) was a Japanese musician who played the shakuhachi, a traditional vertical bamboo flute.

Katsuya Yokoyama is a Japanese professional baseball player known for his versatility as an infielder and his contributions to Japanese baseball leagues. Born on January 16, 1991, in Japan, he began his career with the Yomiuri Giants in Nippon Professional Baseball (NPB), where he quickly established himself as a reliable player. Yokoyama has been recognized for his defensive skills and ability to perform under pressure, earning accolades throughout his career. In addition to his success in domestic leagues, he has also represented Japan in international competitions, showcasing his talent on a global stage. His playing style combines technical proficiency with athleticism, making him a valued member of his teams.

Imre Bíró (born 1959) is a Hungarian handball player. He participated at the 1988 Summer Olympics, where the Hungarian national team placed fourth, and at the 1992 Summer Olympics, where the team placed seventh. He has later coached the Hungarian women's club DVSC.

Imre Bíró (born August 25, 1974) is a Hungarian-born Canadian table tennis player and coach known for his achievements in the sport during the late 20th and early 21st centuries. He represented Hungary in various international competitions before relocating to Canada, where he continued to compete and contribute to the development of table tennis. Bíró has participated in multiple national championships and has earned recognition for his coaching efforts, fostering the next generation of players. His impact extends beyond his competitive success, as he emphasizes skill development and sportsmanship in his coaching philosophy.
The 1980–81 Australian region cyclone season was an above average tropical cyclone season. It officially started on 1 November 1980 and officially ended on 30 April 1981.

The 1980–81 Australian region cyclone season was a significant period for tropical cyclones, characterized by a total of 12 named storms, including 6 that intensified into tropical cyclones. The season commenced on November 1, 1980, and ended on April 30, 1981. The most notable cyclone was Cyclone Kathy, which caused considerable damage in Queensland. The Australian Bureau of Meteorology monitored the season's activity, which included several systems that formed in the Coral Sea and the Indian Ocean. The season was marked by varying impacts on coastal communities, highlighting the necessity for improved forecasting and disaster preparedness in cyclone-prone areas.

The Gild Fryske Mounders (English: Frisian Millers Guild) is an association of volunteer millers operating windmills in the Dutch province Friesland. Its goal is to preserve and spread miller knowledge and skills to ensure the availability of enough millers who can responsibly operate all functional windmills in Frisia. For this purpose it offers a volunteer miller training program. Furthermore, it organises the Frisian mills day, excursions and together with De Fryske Mole (English: Frisian Mills Foundation) publishes a quarterly magazine De Utskoat .

Gild Fryske Mounders is a traditional Frisian cultural organization based in Friesland, Netherlands, dedicated to the preservation and promotion of Frisian customs, crafts, and heritage. Established in the early 20th century, the guild focuses on fostering regional pride through various activities, including events, workshops, and performances that highlight Frisian language, music, dance, and folklore. Members, often dressed in traditional Frisian costumes, participate in local festivals and celebrations, playing a significant role in maintaining the cultural identity of Friesland. The Gild also engages in educational initiatives to promote awareness and appreciation of Frisian traditions among younger generations.

The Battle of Dermbach was the first clash between Prussian and Bavarian troops in the Austro-Prussian War near Dermbach, Saxe-Weimar-Eisenach in modern-day Thuringia on 4 July 1866. On the Prussian side, the 13th Division under Lieutenant General von Goeben was deployed which faced parts of the 3rd and 4th Bavarian infantry divisions with a total strength of approx. 15,000 men. The Commander-in-Chief on the Bavarian side was Prince Karl.

The Battle of Dermbach was a military engagement that took place in April 1945 during World War II, as Allied forces advanced into Germany. It occurred near the village of Dermbach in Thuringia, where German troops attempted to hold back advancing American forces. The battle was characterized by intense fighting and strategic maneuvers on both sides. Ultimately, the American troops were able to secure a decisive victory, contributing to the overall collapse of German defenses in the region. The victory at Dermbach was part of the larger Allied offensive that led to the eventual defeat of Nazi Germany in May 1945. The battle is noted for its significance in the context of late-war operations in the European theater.

Parliamentary elections were held in Croatia on 3 January 2000 to elect members of the Chamber of Representatives. The ruling Croatian Democratic Union entered the elections weakened by a series of corruption scandals that came to light in the previous parliamentary term and fractures between its hardline nationalists and more moderate members. However, the most important factor was the deteriorating health of the party leader and Croatian president Franjo Tuđman, which left no successor within the party. On the other side, two major Croatian opposition parties – the Social Democratic Party of Croatia and Croatian Social Liberal Party – had their coalition formally agreed in 1998 and spent more than a year preparing for the elections. At first, they were to run together with the Croatian Peasant Party, Croatian People's Party, Istrian Democratic Assembly and Liberal Party, but as Tuđman's condition worsened, leaders of the SDP and HSLS concluded that they could win elections even without the four other parties, which later formed a separate bloc. On 25 May, the governing HDZ and the six mainstream opposition parties signed an agreement to develop a consensus-based legislative framework for the upcoming parliamentary elections. This agreement also included a commitment to restructure Croatian Radiotelevision (HRT) as a public broadcaster. As before all previous elections since the breakup of Yugoslavia, the electoral laws were altered in an attempt to improve the chances for the ruling party; this included a new voting system and redistricting. The first-past-the-post constituencies introduced in the previous election were scrapped and proportional representation was implemented (with the exception of ethnic minority seats). The country was divided into ten electoral districts, all drawn in order to maximise the support for HDZ. Each district had to elect 14 members with an electoral threshold of 5%. Due to Tuđman's illness and death in December 1999, the elections were repeatedly postponed for constitutional reasons. There was speculation about elections being held during the Christmas holidays in order to have as many Croatian expatriates (traditionally HDZ supporters) in the country, but the date of 3 January was chosen as the most suitable. As election day, the outcome became more certain. The campaign was brief and relatively uneventful with the HDZ being visibly weakened and demoralised by the death of its long-term leader. On election day, voter turnout – the highest since 1990 – indicated the desire to see a change of government. Following the elections, Social Democratic Party leader Ivica Račan was appointed prime minister on 27 January 2000, by a decree of Acting President and Speaker of the outgoing assembly of Parliament, Vlatko Pavletić. The appointment was confirmed by a parliamentary vote on 2 February in which 122 of 151 MPs voted in favor and one against the cabinet, while one MP abstained. Račan led a coalition of SDP and HSLS, which together with a bloc of four other parties held a supermajority in parliament, allowing them to amend the Constitution and transform Croatia from a semi-presidential system into an incomplete parliamentary system in November 2000, and abolish the upper chamber, the Chamber of Counties, in March 2001. The constitutional changes of 2000 greatly limited the power of the president, but retained the direct election of the office.

The 2000 Croatian parliamentary election was held on January 3, 2000, marking the first election since the end of the long-standing leadership of Franjo Tuđman and the Croatian Democratic Union (HDZ). This election resulted in a significant shift in the political landscape of Croatia, with the opposition coalition led by the Social Democratic Party (SDP) gaining a majority in the Sabor. The SDP, led by Ivica Račan, won 58 out of 128 seats, enabling them to form a government with the support of smaller parties. The election was widely viewed as a turning point in Croatian politics, reflecting public dissatisfaction with the HDZ’s rule and a desire for democratic reform following the Croatian War of Independence. Voter turnout was approximately 77.7%. The outcome of the election ultimately contributed to Croatia's efforts to integrate into European structures and improve its international standing.

George William Coleman (February 1, 1939 – May 24, 2024) was an American prelate of the Roman Catholic Church. Coleman served as bishop of the Diocese of Fall River in Massachusetts from 2003 to 2014.

George William Coleman (1824–1898) was an American artist and educator known for his contributions to the field of landscape painting. Born in New York, he studied at the National Academy of Design and later became an influential figure in the Hudson River School movement, which emphasized the beauty of the American wilderness. Coleman was recognized for his detailed and atmospheric depictions of natural scenes, often featuring the landscapes of the northeastern United States. In addition to painting, he was dedicated to art education, teaching and mentoring numerous students throughout his career. His works are represented in various museums and collections across the United States, highlighting his enduring impact on American art.

The CZ 455 is a series of magazine-fed bolt-action rimfire repeating rifle manufactured by the Czech firearms manufacturer Česká Zbrojovka Uherský Brod (abbreviated "CZ-UB", English: Czech Weapons Factory — Uherský Brod) and imported into the United States by CZ-USA. It is a successor of the highly popular CZ 452 rifles, and is chambered for the .22 LR (1:16" twist rate), .22 WMR (1:16" twist) and .17 HMR (1:9" twist) calibers. Most versions of the 455 rifle series were discontinued in 2018 and replaced by the CZ 457. The CZ 455 is produced with new manufacturing technology capable of closer tolerances for improved accuracy and smoother operation. All CZ 455s have hammer-forged, hand-lapped steel barrels, a trigger that is adjustable for weight of pull, and the capability to interchange barrels in different contours and calibers by simply removing two set screws from the receiver.

The CZ 455 is a bolt-action rifle manufactured by Česká zbrojovka (CZ) in the Czech Republic. Introduced in 2010 as a successor to the CZ 452, it is designed for various shooting applications, including target shooting, varmint hunting, and recreational shooting. The CZ 455 is available in multiple calibers, including .22 Long Rifle, .22 Winchester Magnum Rimfire (WMR), and .17 HMR. It features a detachable magazine, adjustable trigger, and an interchangeable barrel system, allowing users to easily switch barrels for different calibers. The rifle is known for its accuracy, reliability, and ergonomic design, making it popular among both novice and experienced shooters. It has received positive reviews for its performance and build quality.

The United States Undetectable Firearms Act of 1988 (18 U.S.C. § 922(p)) makes it illegal to manufacture, import, sell, ship, deliver, possess, transfer, or receive any firearm that is not as detectable by walk-through metal detection as a security exemplar containing 3.7 oz (105 g) of steel, or any firearm with major components that do not generate an accurate image before standard airport imaging technology. It was signed into law by President Ronald Reagan on November 10, 1988.

The Undetectable Firearms Act is a United States federal law enacted in 1988 that prohibits the manufacture, importation, sale, or possession of firearms that are not detectable by metal detectors or that do not have the capability of being identified through X-ray machines. The law aims to prevent individuals from circumventing airport security and law enforcement by using firearms made of plastic or other non-metallic materials. The Act requires firearms to contain at least a specified amount of metal to ensure they are detectable. It has been amended multiple times to include provisions related to technological advancements in firearms manufacturing.

Lincolnshire is one of the few counties within the UK that still uses the eleven-plus to decide who may attend grammar school, in common with Buckinghamshire and Kent. As a result, many towns in Lincolnshire have both a grammar school and a secondary modern school. Lincoln, however, is primarily non-selective, as is the area within a radius of about seven miles. In this area, most children attend comprehensive schools, though it is still possible to opt into the eleven-plus system. This gives rise to the unusual result that those who pass the eleven-plus can attend a grammar school outside the Lincoln comprehensive area, but those who do not pass still attend a (partly non-selective) comprehensive school.

Education in Lincolnshire encompasses a range of primary and secondary schools, including state schools, academies, and independent institutions. The county has a diverse educational landscape, with a significant number of rural schools serving smaller communities alongside larger urban institutions in cities such as Lincoln and Grantham. Lincolnshire County Council oversees local education services, focusing on improving educational outcomes and addressing challenges such as the recruitment of teachers and funding disparities. The region is also home to several colleges offering further education and vocational training, as well as higher education institutions like the University of Lincoln. Educational initiatives in the county aim to enhance access, quality, and inclusivity for students of all backgrounds.

In the Middle Ages, the Volga trade route connected Northern Europe and Northwestern Russia with the Caspian Sea and the Sasanian Empire, via the Volga River. The Rus used this route to trade with Muslim countries on the southern shores of the Caspian Sea, sometimes penetrating as far as Baghdad. The powerful Volga Bulgars (cousins of today's Balkan Bulgarians) formed a seminomadic confederation and traded through the Volga river with Viking people of Rus' and Scandinavia (Swedes, Danes, Norwegians) and with the southern Byzantine Empire (Eastern Roman Empire) Furthermore, Volga Bulgaria, with its two cities Bulgar and Suvar east of what is today Moscow, traded with Russians and the fur-selling Ugrians. Chess was introduced to Medieval Rus via the Caspian-Volga trade routes from Persia and Arabia. There was a second route from the Baltic Sea to the Dnieper, which ran along the Western Dvina (Daugava) between Lovat and Dnieper in the Smolensk region and along the Kasplya River to Gnezdovo. The Volga trade route functioned concurrently with the Dnieper trade route, better known as the trade route from the Varangians to the Greeks, and lost its importance in the 11th century.

The Volga trade route, also known as the Volga-Varangian trade route, was a key commercial and cultural artery in Eastern Europe during the Middle Ages, facilitating trade between the Byzantine Empire, the Norse countries, and the Silk Road. It connected the rivers of the Volga and Dnieper, allowing merchants to transport goods such as furs, wax, honey, and metalwork. The route played a crucial role in the economic development of the Kievan Rus and contributed to the exchange of ideas and cultures between the East and West. Its significance declined with the rise of new trade patterns and political changes in the region, but its historical impact on trade and cultural exchange remains notable.

The Billboard Hot 100 is a chart that ranks the best-performing songs in the United States. Its data, published by Billboard magazine and compiled by Nielsen SoundScan, is based collectively on each song's weekly physical and digital sales, as well as the amount of airplay received on American radio stations and streaming on online digital music outlets. During 2019, fifteen singles reached number one on the Hot 100; a sixteenth single, "Thank U, Next" by Ariana Grande, began its run at number one in November 2018. Of those fifteen number-one singles, four were collaborations. In total, eighteen acts topped the chart as either lead or featured artists, with ten—Swae Lee (as a solo artist), Bradley Cooper, Jonas Brothers, Lil Nas X, Billy Ray Cyrus, Billie Eilish, Shawn Mendes, Lizzo, Lewis Capaldi, and Selena Gomez—achieving their first Hot 100 number-one single. Lil Nas X's "Old Town Road" was the longest-running number-one of the year, leading the chart for nineteen weeks (one for the song's original version, credited solely to Lil Nas X, and eighteen for a remix featuring Billy Ray Cyrus); in doing so, it broke the record as the longest-running number one single in Billboard history - a record previously held by the sixteen-week runs of both "One Sweet Day" by Mariah Carey and Boyz II Men (1995–96), and "Despacito" by Luis Fonsi and Daddy Yankee featuring Justin Bieber (2017). It also topped the Billboard Year-End Hot 100 ranking as the best-performing single of 2019. In December, Mariah Carey's "All I Want for Christmas Is You" hit number one for the first time 25 years after its debut in 1994, breaking the record for longest climb to the top spot of the Hot 100 since release. It became her 19th number-one single, extending her own record for most number ones among soloists on the Hot 100. It was also the first Christmas song to top the Hot 100 since "The Chipmunk Song" by David Seville and the Chipmunks in 1958–59. Post Malone and Ariana Grande were the only acts to have multiple number-one songs in 2019, with two apiece.
topic: List of Billboard Hot 100 number ones of 2019

text: The list of Billboard Hot 100 number ones of 2019 details the songs that reached the top position on the Billboard Hot 100 chart throughout the year. This chart ranks the most popular songs in the United States based on sales, radio airplay, and online streaming data. In 2019, a diverse range of artists topped the chart, with notable hits including "Old Town Road" by Lil Nas X, which became the longest-running number one of the year, and "Bad Guy" by Billie Eilish. A total of 30 different songs reached number one during the year, reflecting the eclectic music trends and genres popular at the time.
topic: List of Billboard Hot 100 number ones of 2019

text: Charles Lucien Jules Laurent Bonaparte, 2nd Prince of Canino and Musignano (French pronunciation: [ʃaʁl lysjɛ̃ ʒyl lɔʁɑ̃ bɔnapaʁt]; 24 May 1803 – 29 July 1857) was a French naturalist and ornithologist, and a nephew of Napoleon. Lucien and his wife had twelve children, including Cardinal Lucien Bonaparte.

Charles Lucien Bonaparte (1803–1857) was a French ornithologist and nobleman, known for his contributions to the study of birds and his classification systems. Born into the Bonaparte family, he was the nephew of Napoleon Bonaparte and held the title of Prince. Bonaparte's influential works include the "Connaissance des Oiseaux," which detailed numerous species and improved the understanding of avian biology. He played a key role in the establishment of the scientific nomenclature in ornithology and served as the first president of the American Ornithologists' Union. His legacy lies in his extensive research and collections, which significantly advanced the field of ornithology during the 19th century.
USS Satellite (SP-1012) was a yacht acquired by the U.S. Navy for use as a training ship during World War I. She operated out of Key West, Florida, and was scrapped at war's end.

USS Satellite (SP-1012) was a United States Navy patrol vessel that was commissioned during World War I. Originally a civilian yacht, she was built in 1907 and was later acquired by the Navy in 1917 for naval service. USS Satellite served primarily in the New York Harbor area and along the Atlantic coast, conducting patrol and escort duties. After the war, she was decommissioned and returned to her civilian owner in 1919. The vessel is notable for its contribution to maritime security efforts during the conflict, reflecting the Navy's expansion and adaptation of civilian vessels for military purposes during wartime.

The East Ohio Gas Company is an American natural gas public utility that has existed since 1898. The company was formed by Standard Oil and provided natural gas to cities in Ohio. East Ohio remained under the ownership of the Standard Oil Company (New Jersey) until 1942, when it was transferred to a new holding company called Consolidated Natural Gas. Consolidated was acquired in 2000 by Dominion Energy. In March 2024, Enbridge acquired East Ohio and two other natural gas companies from Dominion. Since the acquisition, the company has conducted business under the trade name Enbridge East Ohio.

East Ohio Gas was a natural gas utility company that served a significant portion of northeastern Ohio, particularly the city of Cleveland and surrounding areas. Founded in the late 19th century, the company was involved in the production, distribution, and sale of natural gas until it was acquired by the conglomerate, Dominion Resources, in 2000. Following the acquisition, East Ohio Gas continued to operate under its name but became part of Dominion East Ohio, a subsidiary focused on providing natural gas services in the region. The utility has played a crucial role in the development of natural gas infrastructure in Ohio and has been involved in various initiatives to promote energy efficiency and customer service.

The International Federation of Chemical, Energy, Mine and General Workers' Unions (ICEM) was a global union federation of trade unions. In November 2007, ICEM represented 467 industrial trade unions in 132 countries, claiming a membership of over 20 million workers.
topic: International Federation of Chemical, Energy, Mine and General Workers' Unions

text: The International Federation of Chemical, Energy, Mine and General Workers' Unions (ICEM) is a global trade union federation representing unions in the chemical, energy, mining, and general sectors. Founded in 2001 through the merger of several unions, including the International Federation of Chemical, Energy, and General Workers' Unions (ICEM) and the International Federation of Miners' Unions, it aims to promote workers' rights and interests on behalf of its member organizations across various countries. The federation advocates for issues such as safe working conditions, fair wages, and social justice, while also addressing global challenges such as climate change and industrial sustainability. The ICEM provides resources, training, and support to its affiliated unions, enhancing their capacity to negotiate and protect the rights of workers in their respective industries.
topic: International Federation of Chemical, Energy, Mine and General Workers' Unions

text: The Glasgow Necropolis is a Victorian cemetery in Glasgow, Scotland. It is on a low but very prominent hill to the east of Glasgow Cathedral (St Mungo's Cathedral) in an area bordered by the Townhead and Dennistoun districts to the north east of the modern city centre. Fifty thousand individuals have been buried here. Typical for the period, only a small percentage are named on monuments and not every grave has a stone. Approximately 3,500 monuments exist here.

The Glasgow Necropolis is a Victorian cemetery located on a hill to the east of Glasgow Cathedral in Scotland. Established in 1833, it is renowned for its elaborate tombs, monuments, and sculptures, which reflect the wealth and status of the deceased. The necropolis covers around 37 acres and is the final resting place of many prominent figures from Glasgow's history, including merchants, shipbuilders, and politicians. Designed in the style of a garden cemetery, it features a diverse range of architectural styles, predominantly Neoclassical and Gothic Revival. The necropolis is also notable for its panoramic views of the city and is a popular site for visitors and historians, as well as a significant cultural and heritage landmark.
Bunzac (French pronunciation: [bœ̃zak]) is a commune in the Charente department in southwestern France.

Bunzac is an innovative software platform designed for enhancing the management and analysis of large-scale datasets, primarily in the fields of finance, healthcare, and research. The platform combines advanced data visualization tools with machine learning algorithms, enabling users to extract insights and make data-driven decisions more efficiently. Launched in 2020, Bunzac supports collaboration across teams by providing cloud-based services and secure data-sharing capabilities. The platform is recognized for its user-friendly interface and flexibility, allowing organizations of various sizes to integrate it into their existing workflows seamlessly. As of 2023, Bunzac continues to evolve, adding new features and expanding its user base globally.

The 728th Military Police Battalion performs Military Police operations to allow for United States forces to move freely through an assigned area. The Battalion also provides law and order to all installations within United States Army Garrison Hawaii. The 728th Military Police comprises the 58th Military Police Company, the 552d Military Police Company, the Headquarters and Headquarters Detachment, and the 520th Military Working Dog Detachment. The 728th Headquarters is located at Schofield Barracks, Hawaii. The Battalion works in support of Headquarters and Headquarters Detachment (HHD) and the Soldiers assigned to it.

The 728th Military Police Battalion is a unit of the United States Army that specializes in military law enforcement and security operations. Activated on April 14, 1944, during World War II, the battalion has a storied history, having participated in various military campaigns and peacekeeping missions. Over the years, it has been involved in both domestic and overseas deployments, contributing to the enforcement of military law, maintaining order within military facilities, and providing security support in combat zones. The battalion continues to play a critical role in ensuring good order and discipline within the Army and supporting broader mission objectives. Based at Fort Campbell, Kentucky, it operates as part of the larger structure of the U.S. Army's military police forces.

Michael C. O'Donovan is a Scottish psychiatric geneticist who researches the genetics of schizophrenia. He is a clinical professor in the Division of Psychological Medicine and Clinical Neurosciences and the deputy director of the MRC Centre for Neuropsychiatric Genetics and Genomics at the Cardiff University School of Medicine in Cardiff, Wales. He also leads the Schizophrenia Group of the Psychiatric Genomics Consortium. Educated at Glasgow University, he also serves as Academic Psychiatry Lead for the Royal College of Psychiatrists in Wales. He was lead author of a 2014 study in Nature which identified over 100 genetic loci associated with an increased risk of schizophrenia. The study, the largest of its kind undertaken at the time, was covered extensively in the media. It was also praised by Thomas Insel, the then-director of the National Institute of Mental Health, who described the study as "a big step forward".

Michael C. O'Donovan was an Irish-American lawyer and politician known for his contributions to the legal field and community service. Born in Ireland, he immigrated to the United States, where he established a successful legal practice. O'Donovan served in various political roles, including a tenure as a local government official, advocating for legislation that supported immigrant rights and community development. His legacy includes efforts to improve access to legal resources for underserved populations and his engagement in civic activities. His work has been recognized within both the legal community and the broader society, reflecting his commitment to justice and public service.

"Kiss You Back" is a song written by Shock G, Money-B, George Clinton Jr. and Philippé Wynne, and performed by American hip hop group Digital Underground. It was released in 1991 through Tommy Boy Records as the lead single from the group's second full-length studio album Sons of the P. Produced and mixed by D.U. in-house production team credited as The Underground Production Squad, it contains a sample from Funkadelic's song "(Not Just) Knee Deep". The song made it to number 40 on the Billboard Hot 100, number 50 on the Radio Songs, number 13 on the Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Songs, number 5 on the Hot Rap Songs and number 7 on the Dance Singles Sales charts in the United States. It also peaked at number 31 in New Zealand. On March 5, 1992, the song was certified gold by the Recording Industry Association of America for selling 500,000 copies in the US alone. Released a year after the group's breakthrough single "The Humpty Dance", "Kiss You Back" became Digital Underground's second single to reached the US top-40.

"Kiss You Back" is a song by American singer-songwriter T-Pain, released in 2011 as part of his album "rEVOLVEr." The track features a blend of R&B and pop elements, showcasing T-Pain's signature Auto-Tune vocal style. Lyrically, the song revolves around themes of romance and mutual affection, with T-Pain expressing his desire to reciprocate love and affection. The song received moderate commercial success, charting on various music platforms and contributing to T-Pain's ongoing reputation as an influential figure in contemporary hip-hop and R&B. Its catchy melody and relatable lyrics resonated with audiences, further solidifying T-Pain's role in the genre during the early 2010s.
The women's floor competition at the 2018 Asian Games took place on 21 and 24 August 2018 at the Jakarta International Expo Hall D2.
topic: Gymnastics at the 2018 Asian Games – Women's floor

text: The Women's floor exercise competition at the 2018 Asian Games took place on August 24, 2018, at the GRAHA BUDI RAHARDJO in Palembang, Indonesia. The event featured gymnasts from various Asian nations competing for medals in one of the most dynamic and expressive disciplines of artistic gymnastics. The competition format included preliminary rounds leading to the finals, with athletes showcasing their routines based on difficulty, execution, and artistry. The Chinese team dominated the event, with Liu Tingting winning the gold medal, followed by teammates Tang Xijing and Chen Yile, who secured silver and bronze, respectively. The event highlighted the growing prominence of gymnastics in Asia and the rising talents in women's artistic gymnastics.
topic: Gymnastics at the 2018 Asian Games – Women's floor

text: Thomas J. Turner (born August 8, 1952) is an American politician who served as a Republican member of the Kentucky House of Representatives for the 85th district from 1997 to 2021. He did not seek reelection in 2020.

Tommy Turner is an American politician known for his service as a member of the Kentucky House of Representatives, representing the 8th district. A member of the Republican Party, he was first elected to the legislature in 2016 and has since been involved in various legislative initiatives focusing on economic development, education, and healthcare issues in Kentucky. Turner has also held positions in local government and community organizations prior to his state legislative career. Throughout his tenure, he has been recognized for his commitment to serving his constituents and promoting conservative values.

P. Suresh Unnithan (born 30 July 1956) is an Indian film and television director who works in Malayalam films. His popular works are Mukha Chithram (1991), Utsavamelam (1992), Jaathakam (1989), Bhaagyavaan (1993) and Aardram (1992).

Suresh Unnithan is an Indian politician and a prominent member of the Indian National Congress party, known for his involvement in local governance and community development in Kerala. He has held various leadership positions within the party and has been an active participant in state-level politics. Unnithan is recognized for his work in advocating for social justice, education, and economic development in his constituency. His political career has been marked by efforts to engage with grassroots issues and promote inclusivity within the political framework of the region.
Arthur Bruce Williams (January 27, 1872 – May 1, 1925) was a politician from the U.S. state of Michigan.

Arthur B. Williams (born 1952) is an American author, educator, and advocate known for his contributions in the field of education reform and literacy. Williams has written several books focusing on educational methodologies and the importance of reading in early childhood development. He has served as a professor at various universities, where he has developed programs aimed at improving literacy among underserved communities. Additionally, Williams is recognized for his community outreach efforts and has collaborated with numerous organizations to promote educational access and equity. His work has been influential in shaping contemporary discussions on educational practices and policies in the United States.

George I, Prince of Anhalt-Dessau (c. 1390 – 21 September 1474), was a German prince of the House of Ascania and ruler of the principality of Anhalt-Dessau. He was the second son of Sigismund I, Prince of Anhalt-Dessau, by his wife Judith, daughter of Gebhard XI, Count of Querfurt.

George I, Prince of Anhalt-Dessau (1515–1561), was a German prince of the House of Ascania and ruler of the principality of Anhalt-Dessau. He ascended to the throne in 1544 after the death of his father, Joachim I. Known for his administrative reforms, he is credited with modernizing the principality's governance and strengthening its economy. George I was a patron of the arts and education, contributing to the establishment of schools and promoting the Protestant Reformation in his territory. His reign was marked by efforts to maintain peace and stability in the region during a time of religious conflict in Europe. He was succeeded by his son, George II.
Simon Nielsen (born 27 October 1986) is a Danish ice hockey professional goaltender who currently plays for Herning Blue Fox in the Metal Ligaen.

Simon Nielsen is a Danish professional ice hockey player, born on July 14, 1993, in Aalborg, Denmark. He plays as a goaltender and has competed in various leagues, including the Danish Metal Ligaen and the various international circuits. Nielsen has represented Denmark at multiple junior and senior international tournaments, showcasing his skills on the global stage. Known for his agility and reflexes, he has garnered attention for his performances both in club play and international competition. Throughout his career, Nielsen has been recognized for his contributions to the growth and development of ice hockey in Denmark.

Dorotheus (Greek: Δωρόθεος) was Arian Archbishop of Constantinople from c. 388 until his death in 407. Preceding his elevation to the see of Constantinople, Dorotheus had served as Arian bishop of Antioch, having succeeded Euzoius of Antioch in 376. Dorotheus' accession to the episcopal throne in Constantinople marked a period of turmoil within the Arian community of the metropolis. Dorotheus displaced the previous Archbishop, Marinus of Thrace, leading to a division of the community.

Dorotheus of Antioch was a Christian theologian and bishop in the early 4th century, known for his influential role in the development of early Christianity in the eastern Roman Empire. He is recognized for his contributions to the early church's doctrinal formulations and ecclesiastical governance. Dorotheus is often associated with the deliberations of the First Council of Nicaea in 325 AD, which sought to address the Arian controversy and establish a unified Christian doctrine. His writings, though largely lost, are noted for their emphasis on the importance of apostolic tradition and the authority of the church. He is celebrated in various Christian traditions, particularly for his leadership and dedication to the faith during a time of significant theological contention and persecution.

Crab Louie salad, also known as Crab Louis salad or the King of Salads, is a type of salad featuring crab meat. The recipe dates back to the early 1900s and originates on the West Coast of the United States.
