Crab Louie is a classic American seafood salad that typically consists of lump crab meat, hard-boiled eggs, tomatoes, and a dressing, often based on mayonnaise and flavored with ingredients such as lemon juice, Worcestershire sauce, or chili sauce. It is traditionally served on a bed of lettuce and may also include additional ingredients like avocado or celery. The dish has its origins in the United States, particularly in the early 20th century, and is often associated with the Pacific Northwest and California cuisine. Variations of the Crab Louie salad can be found in various restaurants and menus across the country, highlighting its enduring popularity.
The Asylum Tour was a concert tour by American rock band Kiss, in support of their thirteenth studio album, Asylum.

The Asylum Tour was a concert tour by the American rock band Kiss, promoting their album "Asylum," released in 1985. The tour commenced in 1985 and included performances across North America, Europe, and Japan. Characterized by elaborate stage shows featuring pyrotechnics and the band's iconic costumes, the tour showcased a mix of songs from their then-recent albums and classic hits. The Asylum Tour was notable for its efforts to revitalize the band's popularity during a period of fluctuating commercial success. It marked the last tour to feature guitarist Bruce Kulick, who became a key member of the band during this era.
Daniel Cazés Menache (13 September 1939, México City – 20 December 2012) was a Mexican anthropologist and gender studies scholar.

Daniel Cazés (born 1967) is a French mathematician recognized for his contributions to functional analysis and operator theory. He has published extensively on topics such as Banach spaces, operator ideals, and noncommutative geometry. Cazés is also known for his work in education, mentoring students and collaborating with researchers in the field. He holds a position at a prominent university, where he continues to advance mathematical research and pedagogy.

Moghanlu (Persian: مغانلو, also Romanized as Moghānlū; also known as Maganlu and Maqanlu) is a village in Gorgin Rural District, Korani District, Bijar County, Kurdistan Province, Iran. At the 2006 census, its population was 96, in 23 families. The village is populated by Azerbaijanis.

Moghanlu is a village located in the Kurdistan Province of Iran, within the Sanandaj County. It is part of the Central District and the Saral Rural District. The village is predominantly inhabited by Kurds and reflects the cultural and linguistic characteristics of the Kurdish people. As of the 2006 census, Moghanlu had a population of 122 residents living in 29 households. The village is known for its picturesque landscapes and traditional Kurdish architecture, contributing to the cultural diversity of the region.

Abū Jaʿfar Muḥammad ibn Jarīr ibn Yazīd al-Ṭabarī (Arabic: أَبُو جَعْفَر مُحَمَّد بْن جَرِير بْن يَزِيد ٱلطَّبَرِيّ; 839–923 CE / 224–310 AH), commonly known as al-Ṭabarī (Arabic: ٱلطَّبَرِيّ), was a Sunni Muslim scholar, polymath, historian, exegete, jurist, and theologian from Amol, Tabaristan, present-day Iran. Among the most prominent figures of the Islamic Golden Age, al-Tabari is widely known for his historical works and expertise in Quranic exegesis, and has been described as "an impressively prolific polymath". He authored works on a diverse range of subjects, including world history, poetry, lexicography, grammar, ethics, mathematics, and medicine. Among his most famous and influential works are his Quranic commentary, Tafsir al-Tabari, and historical chronicle, Tarikh al-Tabari. Al-Tabari followed the Shafi'i school for nearly a decade before he developed his own interpretation of Islamic jurisprudence. His understanding of it was both sophisticated and remarkably fluid, and, as such, he continued to develop his ideas and thoughts on juristic matters right until the end of his life. Al-Tabari's school of jurisprudence "flourished among Sunni ulama for two centuries after his death", before it eventually became extinct. It was commonly designated as the Jariri school.

Al-Tabari (839–923 CE) was a Persian scholar, historian, and exegete known for his comprehensive historical chronicle "History of the Prophets and Kings" (Tarikh al-Umam wa al-Muluk), which provides a detailed account of Islamic history from creation to his own time. He also authored a significant Quranic exegesis, "Tafsir al-Tabari," which is among the earliest and most influential works of Islamic interpretation. Al-Tabari's methodological approaches combined traditional narratives with critical analysis, helping to shape Islamic historiography and theology. His works remain pivotal in the study of early Islamic history and the development of Islamic thought.
Arild Olsen (born 14 January 1952) is a Norwegian footballer. He played in two matches for the Norway national football team from 1976 to 1979.

Arild Olsen (born October 11, 1946) is a retired Norwegian sprinter specializing in the 100 meters and 200 meters events. He gained prominence in the 1970s, representing Norway in various international competitions, including the European Athletics Championships. Olsen achieved national success, holding several records in his specialty events. He is recognized for his contributions to Norwegian athletics and his status as one of the country's renowned sprinters during his active years. After retiring from competitive athletics, Olsen remained involved in sports and athletics promotion in Norway.
Zgoda [ˈzɡɔda] is a village in the administrative district of Gmina Jarosław, within Jarosław County, Subcarpathian Voivodeship, in south-eastern Poland.

Zgoda is a village located in the administrative district of Gmina Komańcza, within Sanok County, in the Podkarpackie Voivodeship of southeastern Poland. It lies in the picturesque landscape of the Bieszczady Mountains, known for its natural beauty and outdoor recreational opportunities. The village is part of the historic region of Lesser Poland and is characterized by its traditional wooden architecture and rural charm. Zgoda is situated approximately 10 kilometers southwest of Komańcza and around 30 kilometers from the larger town of Sanok. The area is primarily agricultural, with a strong emphasis on local traditions and crafts.
Australozethus is an Australasian genus of potter wasps.

Australozethus is a genus of small to medium-sized beetles belonging to the family Zethididae, primarily found in Australia and surrounding regions. Characterized by their distinctive elongated bodies and often brightly colored exoskeletons, these beetles are known for their unique ecological adaptations and behaviors. The genus includes several species, which exhibit variations in size, coloration, and habitat preferences. Australozethus beetles are often associated with specific plant species, where they play a role in pollination and contribute to the ecosystem's biodiversity. Descriptions and classifications of species within this genus have evolved as taxonomic studies continue to refine their relationships within the beetle family.

In the United Kingdom there is a maximum fine of £2,500 for persistent littering. Different local authorities also have the powers to impose on-the-spot fines to those caught littering. These are generally under £100. Cigarette butts constitute the majority of litter in terms of the number of items, but contribute to a small proportion of the overall litter volume. Small plastic bottles and non-alcoholic drink cans account for a significant portion of the litter volume, but are a smaller proportion of the item count.

Litter in the United Kingdom refers to the unwanted waste materials that are improperly discarded in public spaces, contributing to environmental degradation and urban blight. The issue of litter has become a significant concern for local communities, government agencies, and environmental organizations since it affects pollution levels, wildlife, and public health. Efforts to combat littering include national campaigns, such as the Great British Spring Clean, and strict laws against littering that impose fines on offenders. The UK government and various NGOs have also promoted awareness and education programs to encourage responsible waste disposal and recycling. Despite these initiatives, littering remains a persistent challenge, exacerbated by factors such as increased consumerism and inadequate waste management infrastructure.
Elaphidion cubae is a species of beetle in the family Cerambycidae. It was described by Warren Samuel Fisher in 1932.

Elaphidion cubae is a species of longhorn beetle belonging to the family Cerambycidae. Described by the entomologist Carl August Adrian Gerstaecker in 1855, this beetle is native to Cuba. E. cubae is characterized by its elongated body and distinctive antennae, which can be longer than the body itself. Like other longhorn beetles, it plays a role in the ecosystem as a decomposer, aiding in the breakdown of wood and other plant materials. Research on its behavior, habitat, and population dynamics is limited, reflecting the need for further studies on its ecological role and conservation status.
Arruitz is a town and a local council area in the municipality of Larraun within the Autonomous Community of Navarra, northern Spain.

Arruiz is a surname of Spanish origin, primarily found in Spain and Latin America. It is associated with various individuals and families, and may also refer to geographic locations or historical figures. The name has been noted in cultural and genealogical records, indicating its significance in lineage and heritage. The meaning and variations of the surname can reflect regional dialects and historical contexts.
Elton Muçollari (born 14 September 1980) is an Albanian retired footballer who played several seasons in the Albanian Superliga.

Elton Muçollari is an Albanian professional footballer born on August 25, 1996, in Vlorë, Albania. He primarily plays as a midfielder and is known for his technical skills and playmaking abilities. Muçollari began his youth career at Vllaznia Shkodër before moving to various clubs in the Albanian Superliga, where he gained recognition for his performances. He also had stints in lower-tier leagues, contributing to his development as a player. Muçollari continues to pursue his career in football, aiming to make an impact both in domestic and international competitions.

Haslington is a civil parish in Cheshire East, England. It contains 12 buildings that are recorded in the National Heritage List for England as designated listed buildings. Of these, one is listed at Grade I, the highest grade, and the others are at Grade II. The parish contains the villages of Haslington, Oakhanger and Winterley, but is otherwise rural. The listed buildings consist of two churches, two former manor houses, a former vicarage with its lodge, other houses and cottages, and a public house.

Haslington, a village in Cheshire, England, is home to several listed buildings, which are structures deemed of special architectural or historic interest by the statutory authorities. The most notable of these is St. John's Church, recognized for its distinctive Gothic Revival architecture. Other listed buildings in Haslington include various residential properties and structures that reflect the village's historical development and architectural styles from different periods. The designation of these buildings helps preserve their character and provides guidelines for their conservation, ensuring that Haslington's cultural heritage is maintained for future generations.

Caldwell is a borough located in northwestern Essex County, in the U.S. state of New Jersey, about 16 miles (26 km) west of New York City and 6 miles (9.7 km) north-west of Newark, the state's most populous city. As of the 2020 United States census, the borough's population was 9,027, an increase of 1,205 (+15.4%) from the 2010 census count of 7,822, which in turn reflected an increase of 238 (+3.1%) from the 7,584 counted in the 2000 census. Caldwell was incorporated as a borough by an act of the New Jersey Legislature on February 10, 1892, from portions of Caldwell Township (now Fairfield Township), based on the results of a referendum held on the previous day. In 1981, the borough's name was changed to the "Township of the Borough of Caldwell", as one of seven Essex County municipalities to pass a referendum to become a township, joining four municipalities that had already made the change, of what would ultimately be more than a dozen Essex County municipalities to reclassify themselves as townships in order take advantage of federal revenue sharing policies that allocated townships a greater share of government aid to municipalities on a per capita basis. Effective January 26, 1995, it again became a borough. Grover Cleveland, the 22nd and 24th President of the United States and the first president to serve two nonconsecutive terms, was born in Caldwell on March 18, 1837. His father, the Rev. Richard Falley Cleveland, was pastor of the First Presbyterian Church. The Grover Cleveland birthplace—the church's former manse—is now a museum and is open to the public. Though today the Caldwell area is considered to be a suburb of both Newark and New York City, the area originally developed as its own individual, self-contained community and economy rather than as urban sprawl from a larger city. When it was formed, miles of woods separated downtown Caldwell from Newark or any of its developing suburbs. New Jersey Monthly magazine ranked Caldwell as its third-best place to live in its 2010 rankings of the "Best Places To Live" in New Jersey.

Caldwell is a borough in Essex County, New Jersey, United States. It was incorporated as a borough on March 4, 1892, from portions of West Essex Township and named after its founder, Caldwell, a community established in the mid-19th century. Caldwell covers an area of approximately 1.2 square miles and has a population of around 7,500 residents, according to the 2020 United States Census. The borough is known for its suburban character, historic sites, and vibrant downtown area, which features various shops, restaurants, and cultural institutions. Caldwell is served by public transportation, including NJ Transit train and bus services, making it accessible to New York City. The local government operates under the borough form, and Caldwell is part of the Caldwell-West Caldwell School District, which serves students from both Caldwell and the neighboring borough of West Caldwell.
John Franklin Bash III (born 1980/1981) is an American attorney who served as the United States Attorney for the Western District of Texas from 2017 to 2020.

John Bash is an American lawyer and political appointee, known for his role as the United States Attorney for the Western District of Texas from 2018 to 2021. Appointed by President Donald Trump, he gained attention for his involvement in various high-profile cases and his work on immigration enforcement issues. Prior to his appointment, Bash served as a special assistant to the Attorney General and held positions within the Department of Justice. He is a graduate of Harvard College and the University of Chicago Law School. In 2021, Bash transitioned to private practice, focusing on litigation and advising clients on regulatory matters.
Juhani Laakso (27 April 1942 – 1 June 2014) was a Finnish sports shooter. He competed in the 300 metre rifle event at the 1968 Summer Olympics.

Juhani Laakso (born 5 May 1935) is a Finnish mathematician noted for his contributions to topology, particularly in the fields of homotopy theory and categorical algebra. He has served as a professor at the University of Helsinki, where he has been involved in both teaching and research. Laakso is recognized for introducing concepts related to the "Laakso space," which has implications in geometric group theory. His work has influenced various areas of mathematics, and he is respected for his rigorous approach and insights into complex mathematical structures.
Cunningham is an unincorporated community in Obion County, Tennessee, United States.

Cunningham is an unincorporated community in Obion County, Tennessee, United States. Located in the northwestern part of the state, Cunningham is part of the Union City micropolitan area. The community is predominantly rural, characterized by agricultural land and small residential populations. Transportation access is primarily through local roads. While it lacks formal municipal status, Cunningham is recognized for its small-town atmosphere and community-focused living. The area is historically linked to the development of agriculture in Tennessee and offers a glimpse into the region's rural lifestyle.

Chilperic (sometimes Childeric in the chronicles of the time) was the infant son of Charibert II, and briefly king of Aquitaine in 632. He was killed shortly after his father’s death in 632, under orders by Dagobert I, Charibert's half-brother.

Chilperic of Aquitaine (died 585) was a Merovingian king and the son of King Charibert I. He ruled over Aquitaine and is noted for his efforts to strengthen the authority of the Merovingian dynasty in the region. Chilperic's reign was marked by conflicts with neighboring rulers and internal strife among the Frankish aristocracy. He is best remembered for his patronage of monasticism and his interactions with the church, notably in supporting the spread of Christianity. Additional historical references to Chilperic are sparse, but some accounts highlight his complex relationship with his siblings and the shifting political dynamics of 6th-century Gaul. His death led to a realignment of power in the Merovingian kingdom, contributing to the eventual rise of his successor, Alboin.

Trail of Kit Carson is a 1945 American Western film directed by Lesley Selander, written by Jack Natteford and Albert DeMond, and starring Allan Lane, Helen Talbot, Tom London, Twinkle Watts, Roy Barcroft and Kenne Duncan. It was released on July 11, 1945, by Republic Pictures.

The Trail of Kit Carson refers to the historic pathways traveled by American frontiersman and explorer Kit Carson during the mid-19th century, particularly in relation to westward expansion and the California Gold Rush. Carson, known for his role as a fur trapper, guide, and an Indian agent, played a significant role in the exploration of the American West. His expeditions often crossed through present-day states such as Colorado, New Mexico, and California, contributing to the mapping and settlement of these regions. The trail is not only significant for its association with Carson's adventures but also for its impact on Native American populations and the environmental changes brought by settlers. Today, the Trail of Kit Carson is recognized for its historical importance and is sometimes marked for educational and tourism purposes.

The James H. Wilkinson Prize in Numerical Analysis and Scientific Computing is awarded every four years by the Society for Industrial and Applied Mathematics (SIAM). The award, named in honor of James H. Wilkinson, is made for research in, or other contributions to, numerical analysis and scientific computing during the 6 years preceding the year of the award. The prizewinner receives the prize, with $2000 (US), at the autumn conference of SIAM and gives a lecture there. It is intended to stimulate younger scientists in the early years of their careers.
topic: James H. Wilkinson Prize in Numerical Analysis and Scientific Computing

text: The James H. Wilkinson Prize in Numerical Analysis and Scientific Computing is an award presented by the Society for Industrial and Applied Mathematics (SIAM) to recognize outstanding contributions to numerical analysis and scientific computing. Established in 2006, the prize is named after James H. Wilkinson, a prominent figure in the field known for his work on numerical methods and floating-point arithmetic. The award is intended to honor individuals or teams who have made significant advancements or achievements in the theory, practice, or applications of numerical analysis and scientific computing. Recipients are selected based on their exceptional contributions and the broader impact of their work on the field.
topic: James H. Wilkinson Prize in Numerical Analysis and Scientific Computing

text: Curcy-sur-Orne (French pronunciation: [kyʁsi syʁ ɔʁn] , literally Curcy on Orne) is a former commune in the Calvados department in the Normandy region in northwestern France. On 1 January 2016, it was merged into the new commune of Thury-Harcourt-le-Hom. The former commune is part of the area known as Suisse Normande.

Curcy-sur-Orne is a commune in the Calvados department located in the Normandy region of northwestern France. It is situated along the Orne River and is part of the Lisieux arrondissement and the Mézidon-Canon canton. The commune covers an area of approximately 11.04 square kilometers and has a small population, reflecting a typical rural community in the region. Curcy-sur-Orne features traditional Norman architecture and is characterized by its scenic countryside and agricultural land. The commune is accessible via local roads and is in proximity to larger towns, making it a quaint destination for visitors interested in the Normandy landscape and culture.
Miguel de Miguel (born Miguel de Miguel Carrasco, 17 April 1975 in Nerja, Spain), is a Spanish actor.

Miguel de Miguel is a Spanish actor born on February 13, 1973, in Madrid, Spain. He gained popularity for his starring roles in various television series, including "Los hombres de Paco," where he played the character Lucas Fernández, and "La casa de los líos." In addition to his television work, de Miguel has appeared in several films and theatrical productions, showcasing his versatility as an actor. He is known for his charisma and ability to portray a range of characters, contributing to his reputation as a prominent figure in Spanish entertainment. Over his career, he has received recognition for his performances and has established a loyal fan base.

Pipers Vale is a 19.7 hectare Local Nature Reserve on the southern outskirts of Ipswich in Suffolk. It is owned and managed by Ipswich Borough Council. This site on the bank of the River Orwell is part of Orwell Country Park. Its diverse habitats include heath, reedbeds, scrub and alder carr. Over 100 bird species have been recorded here, including redwings, whimbrels and bullfinches. There is access from Gainsborough Lane.

Pipers Vale is a suburb located in Ipswich, Suffolk, England. It is situated to the northeast of the town center and is primarily residential in nature. The area is characterized by a mix of housing, local amenities, and green spaces. Pipers Vale is served by schools and parks, making it a suitable location for families. The suburb is also notable for its proximity to major roadways and public transport, providing access to Ipswich and surrounding areas. Historical developments in Pipers Vale reflect the broader trends of urban expansion in the region throughout the 20th century.

L'incantesimo (“The Spell”) is a short opera in one act by Italian composer Italo Montemezzi. Its libretto was written by playwright Sem Benelli who had previously collaborated with the composer on his most famous opera, L'amore dei tre re. Benelli finished the text in 1933, and Montemezzi started work on the score, but the composer's unpleasant relationship with Mussolini's government made Italy inconducive to creative work. Montemezzi resumed work on L'incantesimo during the summer of 1943 in Beverly Hills, his home during a 10-year sojourn in the United States. Upon completion of the score, he offered it to the NBC Symphony, professing admiration for their orchestra.

L'incantesimo is a 1999 Italian romantic fantasy film directed by Alessandro Ratti. The story follows a young woman who discovers an ancient spell that allows her to transform her mundane life into a magical existence filled with adventure and romance. As she navigates the complexities of her newfound powers, she faces various challenges that test her relationships and understanding of love. The film blends elements of fantasy and romance, and it explores themes of self-discovery and the consequences of wielding magical abilities. It received a moderate reception in Italy and has been noted for its enchanting visuals and engaging performances.

The term Know-Nothing Riot refers to a number of political actions by members of the Know Nothing Party in the United States of the mid-19th century. These anti-immigrant and anti-Catholic protests culminated into riots in Philadelphia in 1844; St. Louis in 1854, Cincinnati and Louisville in 1855; Baltimore in 1856; Washington, D.C., and New York City in 1857; and New Orleans in 1858.
topic: Know-Nothing Riots in United States politics

text: The Know-Nothing Riots were a series of violent events in the United States during the mid-1850s, primarily fueled by tensions related to immigration and nativism. The riots were associated with the Know-Nothing Party, a political movement that emerged in response to increasing numbers of Irish and German Catholic immigrants, whom its members viewed as a threat to American Protestant values. Conflicts erupted in various cities, including New York, Philadelphia, and Chicago, often resulting in physical confrontations, widespread property damage, and casualties. These riots reflected the growing social and political divisions in the U.S. leading up to the Civil War, highlighting the clash between nativist sentiments and the realities of a diversifying nation. The decline of the Know-Nothing Party by the late 1850s marked a shift in American politics towards other forms of identity-based polarization.
topic: Know-Nothing Riots in United States politics

text: Fannie Nampeyo (1900–1987) (also known as Fannie Lesou Polacca and Fannie Nampeyo Polacca) was a modern and contemporary fine arts potter, who carried on the traditions of her famous mother, Nampeyo of Hano, the grand matriarch of modern Hopi pottery.

Fannie Nampeyo (c. 1890–1987) was a prominent Native American potter of the Hopi tribe, renowned for her innovative contributions to traditional Hopi pottery techniques. She was born in the village of Hotevilla on the Hopi reservation in Arizona and was a descendant of the famous potter Nampeyo (her grandmother). Fannie played a crucial role in revitalizing and preserving ancestral pottery traditions, incorporating natural materials and ancient designs into her works. Her pieces are celebrated for their intricate decoration and craftsmanship, often featuring traditional Hopi motifs. Fannie Nampeyo's work has been exhibited nationally, and she is recognized as a significant figure in the cultural heritage of Native American art.

The Carlsson's III Cabinet (Swedish: Regeringen Carlsson III) was the cabinet and Government of Sweden from 7 October 1994 to 22 March 1996. The cabinet was a single-party minority government consisting the Social Democrats. The cabinet was led by Prime Minister Ingvar Carlsson who had led his party to victory in the 1994 general election. Ingvar Carlsson had previously been Prime Minister from 1986 until defeat in the 1991 general election. The cabinet resigned on 22 March 1996 as Ingvar Carlsson retired from political life. The cabinet was succeeded by the cabinet of Göran Persson.

The Carlsson III cabinet was the 53rd government of Sweden, formed by the Social Democratic Party under Prime Minister Ingvar Carlsson. It governed from 1994 to 1996, succeeding the Carlsson II cabinet and preceding the Liedgren cabinet. The cabinet was notable for its efforts to stabilize the Swedish economy in the aftermath of a severe financial crisis in the early 1990s, implementing policies aimed at reducing public debt and fostering economic growth. The cabinet also focused on social welfare reforms and labor market issues. It was characterized by a coalition between the Social Democrats and the Left Party, while also receiving support from the Green Party in certain matters. The cabinet concluded its term after the 1994 general elections, which saw the Social Democrats retain power but with a reduced majority.
Emma Ruth Hedeman (1910 – 2006) was an American solar astronomer at McMath-Hulbert Solar Observatory in Michigan.

E. Ruth Hedeman (1936 – March 22, 2022) was an American biochemist and researcher known for her contributions to the field of oxidative stress and its effects on cell biology. She earned her Ph.D. in biochemistry from the University of Wisconsin-Madison and held various academic positions throughout her career, including a faculty role at the University of Nevada, Reno. Hedeman's research focused on the mechanisms of cellular damage caused by reactive oxygen species and the cellular responses to oxidative stress. She published numerous articles in scientific journals and was regarded for her mentorship of students and involvement in educational outreach in the sciences.

Latvian Air Force (Latvian: Latvijas Gaisa spēki) is the aviation branch of the National Armed Forces. The first air force units were established in 1919 and re-established in 1992. It has no air combat capability, thus the defense of Latvian air space is maintained by NATO, with rotating detachments of four aircraft to Lithuania at four-monthly intervals (see Baltic Air Policing).

The Latvian Air Force (LAF) is the aerial warfare service branch of the National Armed Forces of Latvia. Established on February 6, 1992, following the country's independence from the Soviet Union, the LAF is responsible for the air defense of Latvian territory and the protection of its airspace. It operates a small fleet of aircraft, including transport and training planes, and focuses on air policing, reconnaissance, and support for ground operations. The Air Force collaborates with NATO and engages in joint exercises to enhance its capabilities. The LAF emphasizes modernization and integration with allied forces to ensure effective defense and security in the Baltic region.

Mobolaji Ebenezer Aluko (born 2 April 1955) is a former Professor of Chemical Engineering at Howard University, and is now "Commissioner for Infrastructure & Public Utilities," in Ekiti state, Nigeria. He was appointed as the inaugural vice-chancellor of Federal University, Otuoke by the Federal Government of Nigeria from 2011 till the expiration of his tenure in 2016.

Bolaji Aluko is a Nigerian-American engineer, academic, and entrepreneur known for his contributions to the fields of petroleum engineering and energy. He holds a bachelor's degree in Chemical Engineering from the University of Nigeria, Nsukka, and advanced degrees in Petroleum Engineering from the University of Tulsa and the University of Texas at Austin. Aluko has held various academic positions, including professorships at universities in the United States and Nigeria, and has published numerous research papers and articles on energy-related topics. He is also recognized for his involvement in oil and gas consultancy and has served in leadership roles within professional organizations. In addition to his engineering career, Aluko is noted for his advocacy for renewable energy solutions in Nigeria and Africa.
Nicolae Onică (born 24 June 1993) is a Romanian weightlifter. He participated at the 2018 World Weightlifting Championships, winning a medal. He is also European champion in 2018 in 94 kg category.

Nicolae Onică is a Romanian politician and member of the Social Democratic Party (PSD). He has served in various political roles, notably as a local councilor and later as a member of the Romanian Parliament. Onică is known for his engagement in local governance, focusing on social issues and development policies. His parliamentary work has involved participation in committees related to education and health, advocating for reforms and improvements in these sectors. Additionally, he has been active in promoting initiatives aimed at enhancing community welfare and public services within his constituency.
The Caatinga cacholote (Pseudoseisura cristata) is a species of bird in the Furnariinae subfamily of the ovenbird family Furnariidae. It is endemic to Brazil.

The Caatinga cacholote (Pseudoseisura gutturalis) is a medium-sized bird belonging to the Furnariidae family, primarily found in the caatinga ecosystem of northeastern Brazil. Characterized by its distinctive plumage that blends shades of brown and gray, it is known for its melodious calls and secretive behavior. The species inhabits scrublands, dry forests, and areas with dense vegetation where it forages for insects and seeds. The Caatinga cacholote plays an important role in its ecosystem by aiding seed dispersal. While the species has a stable population, habitat destruction poses a threat to its natural habitat. Conservation efforts are essential to ensure the survival of this unique bird in its native environment.

KLMJ (104.9 FM) is a radio station broadcasting a gold-based adult contemporary format. Located near Hampton, Iowa, United States, the station serves the Mason City area. The station is currently licensed to On The Go Media, Inc.

KLMJ is the ICAO code for the James M. “Buddy” McCarty Field, a public-use airport located in the city of McCarty, Mississippi. The airport primarily serves general aviation and is named after Buddy McCarty, a local figure known for his contributions to aviation and the community. KLMJ features a single asphalt runway and accommodates various small aircraft operations, including private, recreational, and flight training activities. The airport plays a vital role in enhancing local accessibility and promoting aviation in the region.

Dewa Mountains (Japanese: 出羽山地) is a mountain range that runs north and south on the west side of the Tohoku region of Japan. The mountain range spans Aomori, Akita, and Yamagata prefectures. The Dewa Mountains are split into several sub-ranges by the Yoneshiro River, the Omono River and the Mogami River. These sub-ranges include the Shirakami Mountains, the Tahei Mountains, The Choki Mountains, and the Chotake Mountains.The highest peak of the mountain range is Mount Chokai (2,236m).

The Dewa Mountains are a mountain range located in the northern part of Honshu, Japan, predominantly within Yamagata and Niigata Prefectures. The range is known for its rugged terrain, steep peaks, and rich biodiversity, including various endemic species. Mount Chōkai is the highest peak in the Dewa Mountains, reaching an elevation of 2,236 meters (7,336 feet). The area is culturally significant, featuring many historic sites and traditional pilgrimage routes, as it is considered sacred in Shinto beliefs. The Dewa Mountains are also popular for outdoor activities such as hiking, skiing, and hot springs, attracting both locals and tourists.

Varbla (German: Werpel) was an Estonian municipality located in Pärnu County. It had a population of 1013 on 1 January 2006 and an area of 314 km2 (121 sq mi). In 2017, Varbla Parish, Lihula Parish, Hanila Parish, and Koonga Parish were merged to form Lääneranna Parish.

Varbla Parish is a rural municipality located in Pärnu County, Estonia. Covering an area of approximately 187.4 square kilometers, it features a mix of coastal and inland landscapes. The parish includes several small villages, with Varbla being its administrative center. Varbla Parish is characterized by its agricultural activities and natural beauty, including proximity to the Baltic Sea. Since the administrative reform in 2017, the parish has engaged in efforts to enhance local governance and improve community services. The population is relatively small, reflecting the trend of depopulation seen in many rural areas of Estonia.
Jamesburg is an unincorporated community in Blount Township, Vermilion County, Illinois, United States.

Jamesburg is an unincorporated community located in Ogle County, Illinois, United States. Situated in the northwest part of the state, it lies approximately 3 miles southwest of the city of Byron and is part of the larger Rockford metropolitan area. Established in the 19th century, Jamesburg features a rural setting with a mix of residential properties and agricultural land. The community's history is tied to the development of the region's transportation infrastructure and agricultural economy. Despite its small size, Jamesburg is characterized by its close-knit community and local events that contribute to its cultural identity.

"Just Give Me a Reason" is a song recorded by American singer Pink featuring Nate Ruess of the band Fun. The song was chosen as the third single from Pink's sixth studio album, The Truth About Love (2012). Written By Pink and Ruess alongside producer Jeff Bhasker, the song is a pop ballad about the desire to hold on to a relationship even when it appears to be breaking down. The song received critical acclaim, with many critics deeming the song as the best track on the album. Prior to its release, the song charted in many regions due to strong digital sales, which was the reason for its release. The song attained worldwide success, topping the charts in twenty-one countries including the United States, Austria, Australia, Canada, the Czech Republic, Iceland, Ireland, Lebanon, Italy, Luxembourg, Mexico, Netherlands, New Zealand, Poland, Portugal, Scotland, Slovakia, Sweden, as well as peaking within the top five in more than ten countries as United Kingdom, Belgium, Brazil, Denmark, Finland, France, Israel, Norway, Switzerland and Spain. In the US, it became Pink's fourth number-one single on the Billboard Hot 100. It also became her third number-one on the Billboard Digital Songs chart after "So What" (2008) and "Raise Your Glass" (2010). "Just Give Me a Reason" has sold more than 4 million digital downloads in the United States. "Just Give Me a Reason" won the Billboard Mid-Year Award for Favorite Hot 100 No. Single, and garnered two Grammy Award nominations for Best Pop Duo/Group Performance and Song of the Year at the 2014 Grammy Awards, where Pink and Nate Ruess performed the song after a trapeze-accompanied rendition of "Try". The music video for "Just Give Me a Reason" featured Nate Ruess as well as Pink's husband, off-road truck and former motocross racer Carey Hart, in a romantic setting which resembles an artificial marsh. The video won the MTV Video Music Award for Best Collaboration in 2013.

"Just Give Me a Reason" is a song by American recording artist P!nk featuring fun. lead singer Nate Ruess. Released in 2012 as the second single from P!nk's sixth studio album, "The Truth About Love," the song is a pop ballad that explores themes of love and communication within a troubled relationship. The collaboration combines P!nk's powerful vocal delivery with Ruess's distinctive falsetto, creating a duet that emphasizes vulnerability and the desire for reassurance. The track achieved commercial success, reaching the top ten in multiple countries, including the United States, where it peaked at number one on the Billboard Hot 100. The song received positive reviews from critics and was nominated for several awards, further solidifying P!nk's status in contemporary pop music.
The Algerian ambassador in Tehran is the official representative of the Government in Algiers to the Government of the Iran.
topic: List of ambassadors of Algeria to Iran

text: The List of ambassadors of Algeria to Iran includes the official representatives of Algeria in Iran, reflecting the bilateral relations between the two countries. The ambassadors serve to promote diplomatic, economic, and cultural relations and to represent Algerian interests in Iran. The position has seen various diplomats since the establishment of formal ties, with a history of cooperation in several areas. Each ambassador’s tenure is marked by efforts to enhance political dialogue and foster mutual understanding between Algeria and Iran. Detailed records of the ambassadors, their terms, and notable achievements can provide insights into the evolving relationship between the two nations.
topic: List of ambassadors of Algeria to Iran

text: Mario Maraschi (28 August 1939 – 3 December 2020) was an Italian professional football player and manager who played as a striker. In 2018, he was inducted into ACF Fiorentina Hall of Fame.

Mario Maraschi is an Italian astrophysicist known for his contributions to the study of active galactic nuclei and gamma-ray astronomy. He has extensively researched the physical properties and emissions of quasars and blazars, focusing on the mechanisms of high-energy radiation. Maraschi has been involved in numerous international collaborations, contributing to flagship projects such as the Fermi Gamma-ray Space Telescope and the Cherenkov Telescope Array. He has published over 200 scientific papers and has held various academic positions, including director of the Astronomical Observatory of Brera in Italy. His work has had a significant impact on the understanding of cosmic phenomena and the underlying physics of black holes and neutron stars.

The family of non-collagenous proteins known as SIBLING proteins, standing for small integrin-binding ligand, N-linked glycoprotein, are components of the extracellular matrix of bone and dentin. Evidence shows that these proteins play key roles in the mineralization of these tissues. The following are categorized as SIBLING proteins: osteopontin (OPN) bone sialoprotein (BSP) dentin matrix protein 1 (DMP1) dentin sialophosphoprotein (DSPP) matrix extracellular phosphoglycoprotein (MEPE) The genes coding for members of the SIBLING protein family are similarly organized and are all located on human chromosome 4q21-23.

SIBLING proteins, or Small Integrin-Binding LIgand N-linked Glycoproteins, are a family of extracellular matrix proteins characterized by their ability to bind to integrins and their involvement in various biological processes, including cell adhesion, migration, and signaling. Key members of this protein family include bone sialoprotein, osteopontin,matrix extracellular phosphoglycoprotein, and dentin matrix protein 1. SIBLING proteins are predominantly expressed in mineralized tissues and play essential roles in bone and tooth development, as well as in pathological processes such as cancer metastasis and tissue repair. They are distinguished by their unique structural features, including the presence of an RGD (arginine-glycine-aspartate) sequence that facilitates their interaction with integrin receptors. Research into SIBLING proteins continues to uncover their significance in both health and disease.

Ricau de Tarascon (also spelled Ricautz or Ricavi) was a Provençal knight and troubadour from Tarascon, active between 1200 and 1240. He served Count Raymond Berengar V of Provence as an administrator. His vida portrays him as a good "servant" of ladies. He wrote both sirventes and cansos, but only two songs survive. "Ab tan de sen cum Deus m'a dat", a canso, is accepted by scholars to be his and is generally ascribed to him in the chansonniers, although it is also ascribed to Gui de Cavalhon and Aimeric de Peguillan. Ricau knew Gui personally, since together they composed a tenso (debate poem), "Cabrit, al mieu vejaire", in which Gui is called "Cabrit". The tenso is jocular in tone. Its poetic structure is borrowed from a song of Perdigon's that was also reused by Joan Esteve.

Ricau de Tarascon is a legendary figure from Occitan folklore, primarily associated with the region of Tarascon in southern France. He is often depicted as a hero and a model of chivalry who undertakes various adventures and battles against adversaries, including dragons and other mythical creatures. The tales of Ricau are characterized by themes of bravery, romance, and moral virtue, reflecting the cultural values of the time. He is sometimes considered a symbol of local identity and pride in the Tarascon area, and his stories have been passed down through oral tradition and written literature. As a cultural icon, Ricau de Tarascon contributes to the rich tapestry of Occitan heritage and legendary narratives in medieval Europe.

"A Boy Is a Gun" (stylized as "A BOY IS A GUN*") is a song by American rapper and producer Tyler, the Creator from his sixth album Igor. The song was written by Tyler, the Creator with multiple producers. Released on May 17, 2019, beside the other songs in the album, it charted in the Billboard Hot 100, the US Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Songs, and the Canadian Hot 100. The song also gained a platinum certification from the Recording Industry Association of America and a gold certification from Music Canada. An accompanying music video directed by Tyler himself is centered around Igor–Tyler's alter ego. Igor is interested in a love interest, while the latter is disinterested.

"A Boy Is a Gun" is a song by American rapper and singer-songwriter T Peters, released in 2019. The track, characterized by its blend of hip-hop and alternative influences, addresses themes of love, vulnerability, and the complexities of masculinity. It features a melodic hook and introspective lyrics, exploring the metaphor of a boy as a gun, symbolizing both charm and potential danger in relationships. The song received critical acclaim for its production quality and lyrical depth, contributing to Peters' growing recognition in the contemporary music scene.

Falcon 9 booster B1058 was a reusable Falcon 9 Block 5 first-stage booster manufactured by SpaceX. B1058 was the first Falcon 9 booster to fly fourteen, fifteen, sixteen, seventeen, eighteen and nineteen times and broke a turnaround record on its later flights. Its first flight was for Crew Dragon Demo-2, on 30 May 2020, the first crewed orbital spaceflight by a private company. The booster was the first and only Falcon 9 booster to feature NASA's worm logo and meatball insignia, the former of which was reintroduced after last being used in 1992. The booster was destroyed several days after successfully landing on the autonomous spaceport drone ship Just Read the Instructions on 23 December 2023. B1058 toppled over as the drone ship sailed toward Port Canaveral in rough seas. While in service, B1058 had an average turnaround time of 72 days with a record turnaround of 27 days and 8 hours. For its 19 flights, it held the Guinness World Record of most missions flown by a rocket first stage, until B1062 surpassed it by completing its 20th flight on 13 April 2024.

Falcon 9 B1058 is a reusable first stage booster of SpaceX's Falcon 9 rocket. It was the first Falcon 9 booster to complete five flights, achieving this milestone on May 30, 2021, during the Transporter-4 mission. B1058 was originally launched for the Crew Dragon Demo-2 mission on May 30, 2020, marking the first crewed flight of a commercial spacecraft to the International Space Station. The booster subsequently participated in multiple missions, including satellite deployments and resupply missions to the ISS. B1058 exemplifies SpaceX's advancements in reusable rocket technology, contributing to the reduction of launch costs and increasing the frequency of spaceflight missions.

The Light the Fuse Tour is the eighth headlining concert tour by New Zealand born-Australian country music singer Keith Urban, in support of his eighth studio album Fuse (2013). This is the first headlining amphitheater tour for Urban, playing in over seventy cities across the United States and Canada. The tour also traveled to Australia. It began on 18 July 2013 and finished on 29 June 2014.

The Light the Fuse Tour was a concert tour by American rock band No Doubt, promoting their album "Push and Shove." The tour took place in 2012 and included performances across North America and parts of Europe. It featured an eclectic mix of the band's hits alongside tracks from their latest album. The tour was notable for its elaborate stage design and energetic performances, showcasing the band's signature ska-punk sound and Gwen Stefani's dynamic stage presence. It garnered positive reviews from both fans and critics, highlighting the group's strong live performances and their ability to engage audiences. The tour marked No Doubt’s return to the stage after a lengthy hiatus, reaffirming their status in the music industry.
Preben Rasmussen (born 14 November 1941) is a Danish boxer. He competed in the men's light welterweight event at the 1964 Summer Olympics.

Preben Rasmussen is a Danish professional footballer, born on May 8, 1981, in Denmark. Primarily playing as a midfielder, he began his career in the Danish lower leagues before moving to more prominent clubs in the Superliga. Known for his versatile playing style and strong tactical awareness, Rasmussen has contributed to various teams throughout his career, including notable stints at clubs like FC Copenhagen and Aalborg BK. In addition to his club career, he has represented Denmark at various youth levels, showcasing his skills on the international stage. After retiring from professional play, Rasmussen remained involved in football through coaching and mentorship roles.

Alexander Panteleimonovich Grammatin (Russian: Александр Пантелеймонович Грамматин; January 6, 1931, in Leningrad – August 28, 2014, in St. Petersburg) was a Soviet and Russian scientist in the field of computational optics, the developer of the theory and author of the first national program for the automated calculation of computer parameters of optical systems by criteria of image quality. He was the Doctor of Technical Sciences, professor, the USSR State Prize laureate (1977) and holder of the Prize of the Council of Ministers of the USSR (1983).

Aleksandr Grammatin (born 15 July 1980) is a Russian professional football player known for his versatility on the field as a midfielder and defender. Throughout his career, he has played for various clubs in the Russian football leagues, including teams in the top divisions such as FC Spartak Moscow and FC Torpedo Moscow. Grammatin has also represented his country at various youth levels, showcasing his skills and contributing to his teams' performances. His career has spanned over two decades, featuring numerous matches and helping his teams achieve notable successes in domestic competitions.

The Shadow Cabinet appointed by Conservative Party leader William Hague was the Official Opposition Shadow Cabinet from 1997 to 2001. Following his initial appointments in June 1997, Hague reshuffled the Shadow Cabinet five times before his resignation as leader following defeat in the 2001 general election.

The Hague shadow cabinet refers to the group of senior members of the UK Conservative Party who served alongside then-opposition leader William Hague in the late 1990s and early 2000s. Formed following the party's defeat in the 1997 general election, the shadow cabinet aimed to provide an alternative government and hold the ruling Labour Party accountable. Hague's leadership, which lasted from 1997 to 2001, was marked by efforts to modernize the party and appeal to a broader electorate, though it faced challenges in the wake of internal divisions and electoral setbacks. The shadow cabinet included prominent figures such as Michael Portillo and David Lidington, and served as a critical platform for developing Conservative policies and strategies leading into the 2001 general election.

Sam Hose (born Samuel Thomas Wilkes; c. 1875 – April 23, 1899) was an African American man who was tortured and murdered by a white lynch mob in Coweta County, Georgia, after being accused of rape and murder.

The lynching of Sam Hose occurred in 1899 in Georgia, USA, and became one of the most infamous examples of racial violence in American history. Sam Hose, an African American man, was accused of murdering a white plantation owner and allegedly assaulting the owner's wife. Following his arrest, a mob of white residents seized Hose from jail, subjecting him to brutal torture and ultimately lynching him. The lynching was meticulously documented by the media, garnering national attention and sparking outrage among civil rights advocates and organizations. The event highlighted the widespread racism and vigilantism present in the South during this period and is frequently cited as a significant example of the violent racial oppression faced by African Americans in the post-Reconstruction era.

Alsophila ferruginea, synonym Cyathea ferruginea, is a species of tree fern endemic to the Philippines. It is native to the islands of Negros, Palawan and Balabac. It grows in mossy forest up to an altitude of about 1,200 metres (3,900 ft).

Alsophila ferruginea, commonly known as the rusty tree fern, is a species of tree fern belonging to the family Cyatheaceae. Native to the tropical regions of New Guinea and surrounding islands, this plant typically grows in moist, shaded environments, often in mountainous areas. The fern features a stout trunk and can reach heights of up to 3 meters. Its fronds are large, finely divided, and have a characteristic rusty-brown coloration on the undersides due to the presence of fine, rusty hairs. Alsophila ferruginea is of interest for its ecological role in forest ecosystems and its potential use in horticulture. Conservation statuses may vary based on habitat loss and environmental changes affecting its native regions.
Armando Jesús “Chiri” Méndez Alcorta (born 31 March 1996) is a Uruguayan professional footballer who plays as a right-back for Newell's Old Boys.

Armando Méndez (born August 16, 1996) is a professional footballer from Uruguay, known for his role as a defender. He began his career with the youth academy of Club Atlético River Plate Montevideo before making his professional debut in 2015. Over the years, Méndez has played for various clubs in the Uruguayan Primera División, as well as had stints in international leagues. He is recognized for his tactical versatility and strong defensive skills. In addition, Méndez has represented Uruguay at youth levels, contributing to the national team's successes in regional competitions.

Aqualad is the alias of several superheroes in American comic books published by DC Comics and appearing in media published by DC Entertainment. The character was originally created by writer Robert Bernstein and artist Ramona Fradon, serving as the sidekick and junior counterpart to superhero Aquaman alongside contemporary, Aquagirl. The character's first incarnation, Garth, debuted in Adventure Comics #269. A native Atlantean unlike his mentor, Garth in both continuities has purple eyes signifying a heritage considered evil and was an outcast taken in by Aquaman. He eventually abandons the "Aqualad" role and adopts the alias, "Tempest". The second incarnation of the character, Kaldur'ahm, originally was set to debut in 2010, created for the Young Justice animated television series by Brandon Vietti, Greg Weisman, and Phil Bourassa. Several months before the series' premiere, an altered version of the character, Jackson Hyde, was brought into comic books by writer Geoff Johns and artist Ivan Reis. Both versions of the character, sharing the common quality of Aquaman's half-human heritage, are protege of Aquaman whose human heritage originates from their father, Aquaman villain Black Manta. While sharing similar designs, both versions of the character possess different backgrounds and inherent abilities. The Garth version of Aqualad made his live-action debut in the television series Titans, played by Drew Van Acker.

Aqualad is a fictional superhero appearing in American comic books published by DC Comics. Created by writer Bob Haney and artist Nick Cardy, he first appeared in "Aquaman" #35 in 1967. Aqualad is notably associated with Aquaman and serves as his sidekick, typically depicted as a young Atlantean with the ability to communicate with marine life and swim at superhuman speeds. Over the years, multiple characters have assumed the Aqualad mantle, with notable iterations including Garth, who is the original Aqualad, and Kaldur'ahm, introduced in the "Young Justice" animated series and later in comic adaptations. Kaldur'ahm, who is of Atlantean descent, has gained prominence for his role in various story arcs and adaptations, including the "Young Justice" animated series where he is portrayed as a leader and key member of the team. Aqualad's character has been recognized for promoting themes of aquatic awareness and environmentalism while exploring identity and heritage in the context of superhero narratives.

Lin Yu-ting (Chinese: 林郁婷; pinyin: Lín Yùtíng; born 13 December 1995) is a Taiwanese amateur boxer. She has won two gold medals at the IBA World Boxing Championships, in addition to two gold medals at the Asian Games and one at the Asian Amateur Boxing Championships. She competed for Taiwan at the 2024 Summer Olympics where she defeated Julia Szeremeta of Poland in the final of the women's 57 kg category (featherweight). Lin is the first Taiwanese boxer to win an Olympic gold medal.

Lin Yu-ting is a Taiwanese professional footballer who plays as a midfielder. Born on February 19, 1998, he began his career in Taiwan before moving to international leagues. Lin is known for his technical skills and playmaking abilities. He has represented Taiwan at various youth levels and made his senior national team debut in 2021. In club football, he has played for several teams in the Taiwanese league and has gained recognition for his performances both domestically and internationally. His contributions to football in Taiwan have made him a notable figure in the sport.
Tullio Pizzorno (10 February 1921 in Greco Milanese – 28 July 1988 in Thiene) was an Italian sailor who competed in the 1960 Summer Olympics.

Tullio Pizzorno (born August 14, 1937) is an Italian sociologist and political scientist known for his work in the fields of social theory and political analysis. He is recognized for contributions to understanding the dynamics of social structures, power relations, and the role of elite groups within society. Pizzorno's research emphasizes the interaction between societal institutions and individual agency, advancing theories on civil society and its implications for democracy. He has held academic positions in various institutions and has published numerous articles and books, influencing both sociological and political discourse.

The Source Presents: Hip Hop Hits is the first annual music compilation album to be contributed by The Source magazine. Released on December 16, 1997, and distributed through Polygram Records, Hip Hop Hits: Volume 1 features eighteen hip hop and rap hits. Five tracks on the album had reached number-one on the Billboard Hot Rap Tracks chart: "Bow Down," "Can't Nobody Hold Me Down", "Crush on You," "Elevators" and "Hypnotize". Two of the songs, "Hypnotize" and "Can't Nobody Hold Me Down" reached number-one on the Billboard R&B/Hip-Hop and pop charts.
topic: The Source Presents: Hip Hop Hits

text: The Source Presents: Hip Hop Hits is a compilation album featuring various artists from the hip hop genre, released by The Source magazine in 1999. The album showcases popular tracks from prominent hip hop artists of the time, serving as a representation of the era's musical landscape. It includes hits that reflect the cultural and social themes prevalent in hip hop, making it a notable collection for fans of the genre. The compilation is part of a broader effort by The Source to highlight influential works and artists in hip hop music.
topic: The Source Presents: Hip Hop Hits

text: oneM2M is a global partnership project founded in 2012 and constituted by 8 of the world's leading ICT standards development organizations, notably: ARIB (Japan), ATIS (United States), CCSA (China), ETSI (Europe), TIA (USA), TSDSI (India), TTA (Korea) and TTC (Japan). The goal of the organization is to create a global technical standard for interoperability concerning the architecture, API specifications, security and enrolment solutions for Machine-to-Machine and IoT technologies based on requirements contributed by its members. The standardised specifications produced by oneM2M enable an Eco-System to support a wide range of applications and services such as smart cities, smart grids, connected car, home automation, public safety, and health.

OneM2M is a global standard for Machine-to-Machine (M2M) and Internet of Things (IoT) communications, initiated to enhance interoperability among various IoT applications and services. Established in 2012 by a partnership of global standards organizations, including the European Telecommunications Standards Institute (ETSI) and the International Telecommunication Union (ITU), OneM2M defines a common framework that supports a range of industries, enabling devices to communicate across different networks and systems. The architecture focuses on scalability, efficiency, and security, facilitating the development of smart cities, connected vehicles, and industrial automation solutions. OneM2M promotes a modular approach, allowing for the integration of existing and new technologies, thereby fostering innovation in the IoT ecosystem.

The Wendy's Championship for Children was an annual golf tournament for professional female golfers on the LPGA Tour from 1999 through 2006 in the Columbus, Ohio area. The tournament was played at the New Albany Country Club in New Albany, Ohio from 1999 to 2001. In 2002, it moved to Tartan Fields Golf Club in Dublin, Ohio. Proceeds from the event benefited pediatric cancer research and treatment at the Columbus Children's Hospital. From 2000 to 2005, over $2.7 million was raised. On August 31, 2006, corporate sponsor Wendy's announced that its relationship with the tournament had come to an end as a result of a disagreement between the corporation and the LPGA over future dates of the event. The last tournament was held August 22–27, 2006. Tournament names through the years: 1999-2000: New Albany Golf Classic 2001-2006: Wendy's Championship for Children

Wendy's Championship for Children is a charitable golf event organized by Wendy's, intended to raise funds for various children's causes, particularly those supported by the Dave Thomas Foundation for Adoption. Established in 2001, the tournament takes place annually and features participation from professional golfers, corporate sponsors, and influencers in the culinary and entertainment industries. Proceeds from the event are utilized to support programs that help improve the lives of children in foster care and promote adoption. The Championship helps raise awareness about the challenges faced by children in the system and fosters community engagement to support their well-being.
Vinicka (Serbian Cyrillic: Виницка) is a village in the municipality of Berane, Montenegro.

Vinicka is a village located within the Berane Municipality in northern Montenegro. It is situated in the mountainous region characterized by its rural landscape and traditional Montenegrin architecture. The village contributes to the cultural and social fabric of the municipality and is primarily inhabited by a small population engaged in agriculture and local crafts. Vinicka serves as an example of the rural lifestyle prevalent in this part of Montenegro, where community ties and local heritage play a significant role in daily life. The surrounding area offers opportunities for outdoor activities such as hiking and exploring the natural beauty of the region.

Sabine Elisabeth Winton (née Fenn; born 24 April 1965) is an Australian politician and former teacher. She has been a Labor member of the Western Australian Legislative Assembly since the 2017 state election, representing Wanneroo. She currently serves as the Western Australian Minister for Education; Early Childhood; Preventative Health; Wheatbelt. Winton previously served in Cabinet as Minister for Early Childhood Education; Child Protection; Prevention of Family and Domestic Violence and Community Services from December 2022 to March 2025. Prior to her election, Winton was a teacher for 27 years and also served on the City of Wanneroo Council from 2013 to 2017.

Sabine Winton is a British actress, known for her work in film, television, and theatre. She gained recognition for her performances in various television series and stage productions, showcasing her versatility as an actress. Winton has appeared in both dramatic and comedic roles, earning critical acclaim and a dedicated fan base. Her career spans several decades, during which she has collaborated with prominent directors and actors in the industry. Winton is also recognized for her advocacy for arts education and her involvement in various charitable initiatives.
Tomohiro Yamamoto (山本智大, Yamamoto Tomohiro; born 5 November 1994) is a Japanese national volleyball player from Ebetsu, Hokkaido. He plays in V.League division 1 for Panasonic Panthers.

Tomohiro Yamamoto is a retired Japanese volleyball player known for his skills as a setter. He was born on March 3, 1983, in Nagoya, Japan. Yamamoto played for the Japan national volleyball team and participated in various international competitions, including the FIVB World Championships and Asian Games. His club career included stints with prominent teams in Japan's V.League, where he earned recognition for his playmaking abilities and tactical acumen on the court. Following his retirement, Yamamoto has been involved in promoting volleyball and mentoring young players.

Dennis Chamberland (born 1951) is an American bioengineer, explorer, author, award-winning nuclear engineer, space life scientist, and aquanaut. He was Mission Commander for seven NASA underwater missions, and designed and built the NASA Scott Carpenter Space Analog Station underwater habitat.

Dennis Chamberland is an American engineer, entrepreneur, and space advocate known for his work in the fields of aerospace and oceanography. He is notable for his contributions to the development of advanced technologies for human exploration of space, including concepts related to space habitats and sustainable living in extraterrestrial environments. Chamberland has been involved in various initiatives promoting space tourism and colonization, and has collaborated with organizations focused on the commercialization of space. He has also conducted research on underwater living conditions and their applications for space exploration. Chamberland is a proponent of utilizing innovative engineering solutions to enhance human adaptability in extreme environments.

Coolaroo station is a railway station operated by Metro Trains Melbourne on the Craigieburn line, part of the Melbourne rail network. It serves the northern suburb of Coolaroo in Melbourne, Victoria, Australia. Coolaroo station is a ground-level unstaffed station, featuring two side platforms. It opened on 6 June 2010.

Coolaroo railway station is a railway station located in the suburb of Coolaroo in Melbourne, Australia. Part of the Metropolitan Rail network, it serves the Craigieburn line and is situated approximately 19 kilometers from the central business district of Melbourne. The station was opened in 2009, providing access to the growing residential areas in the north of Melbourne. Facilities at the station include a ticketing office, waiting areas, and accessibility features for disabled passengers. Coolaroo serves as a vital transport hub for local commuters, linking them to the broader rail network.

Spanish Ranch Creek is a small river in San Mateo County, California. It flows about 1 mile (2 km) west from its source to its confluence with Weeks Creek, which in turn joins La Honda Creek in the La Honda Creek Regional Open Space, about 2 miles north of the town of La Honda.

Spanish Ranch Creek is a stream located in the Sierra Nevada region of California, United States. It is a tributary of the Middle Fork of the American River, flowing through Placer and El Dorado Counties. The creek originates near Spanish Ranch, a historic site that dates back to the Gold Rush era. The area surrounding Spanish Ranch Creek is characterized by mountainous terrain and diverse ecosystem, making it a popular destination for outdoor activities such as hiking and fishing. The creek plays a significant role in the local watershed, contributing to the ecological health and water supply of the region.

Woodford v. Ngo, 548 U.S. 81 (2006), is a United States Supreme Court case about the procedures determining when prison litigation may be commenced in federal court. Justice Samuel Alito, writing for the majority, ruled that prisoners must exhaust all state-court remedies in accordance with the rules thereof before filing claims in federal court. Justice Stephen Breyer filed a concurrence. Justice John Paul Stevens filed a dissent.

Woodford v. Ngo is a notable case decided by the United States Supreme Court in 2006, addressing the procedural requirements for inmates to exhaust administrative remedies before filing a lawsuit under the Prison Litigation Reform Act (PLRA). The case arose from a dispute involving a California state prison inmate, who claimed inadequate medical care. The Court ruled that inmates must fully comply with prison grievance procedures before seeking judicial intervention, emphasizing the importance of the exhaustion requirement in promoting administrative efficiency and reducing litigation in the prison system. The decision underscored the need for inmates to adhere strictly to established prison rules regarding grievances.

Ocean's 11 is a 1960 American heist film directed and produced by Lewis Milestone from a screenplay by Harry Brown and Charles Lederer, based on a story by George Clayton Johnson and Jack Golden Russell. The film stars an ensemble cast and five members of the Rat Pack: Frank Sinatra, Dean Martin, Sammy Davis Jr., Peter Lawford, and Joey Bishop. Centered on a series of Las Vegas casino robberies, the film also stars Angie Dickinson, Richard Conte, Cesar Romero, Patrice Wymore, Akim Tamiroff, and Henry Silva. It includes cameo appearances by Shirley MacLaine, Red Skelton, and George Raft. Ocean's 11 premiered in Las Vegas on August 3, 1960, and was theatrically released in the United States on August 10 by Warner Bros. Pictures. The film received mixed reviews from critics. It originated the Ocean's film series.

"Ocean's 11" is a 2001 American heist film directed by Steven Soderbergh, featuring an ensemble cast including George Clooney, Brad Pitt, and Julia Roberts. It is a remake of the 1960 film of the same name, which starred the Rat Pack. The plot follows Danny Ocean, a con artist who plans an elaborate casino robbery in Las Vegas, targeting three prestigious casinos owned by the ruthless Terry Benedict. The film received critical acclaim for its stylish direction, engaging plot, and strong performances, leading to the successful formation of the "Ocean's" franchise, which includes several sequels and a spin-off. It grossed over $450 million worldwide and has been praised for its witty dialogue and slick cinematography.
Elisabeth Wintzer (16 June 1863 – 12 June 1933) was a German composer and writer.

Elisabeth Wintzer is a notable figure recognized for her contributions in the field of art and design. She gained prominence through her innovative works that often explore the intersection of contemporary aesthetics and traditional techniques. Wintzer has exhibited her art in various galleries and has participated in several international art fairs. In addition to her artistic practice, she is also involved in educational initiatives, aiming to inspire and mentor emerging artists. Her work often reflects themes of identity, culture, and the human experience, making her a significant voice in the contemporary art scene.
Peperomia disjunctiflora is a species of plant in the family Piperaceae. It is endemic to Ecuador.

Peperomia disjunctiflora is a species of flowering plant in the family Piperaceae, native to the tropical regions of South America. Characterized by its succulent leaves and compact growth habit, this ornamental plant is commonly grown indoors as a houseplant. The species features distinctive thick, fleshy leaves that are arranged alternately along its stems. P. disjunctiflora is appreciated for its unique appearance and ease of care, making it a popular choice among plant enthusiasts. It thrives in well-draining soil and prefers indirect sunlight, contributing to its suitability for indoor environments. As with many plants in the Peperomia genus, it is relatively resistant to pests and requires minimal maintenance.

Sommers (Russian: Соммерс; Finnish: Someri; Swedish: Sommarö) is an islet and a lighthouse in the eastern part of the Gulf of Finland, an arm of the Baltic Sea, just outside the Gulf of Vyborg, about 19 kilometres south of Virolahti, Finland, but it is now possessed by Russia. The lighthouse is situated on a rocky skerry, which is elevated a maximum of 16 metres above the Baltic Sea.

Sommers Light is a style of American light lager known for its crisp, refreshing taste and low alcohol content. Originating from the United States, it is often characterized by a pale golden color, light body, and subtle hop bitterness. The beer is named after the Sommers family, who played a significant role in its creation and development. Sommers Light is typically enjoyed as a session beer, making it popular at social gatherings, outdoor events, and casual drinking occasions. Its light flavor profile appeals to a wide range of consumers, contributing to its presence in bars and retail outlets across the country.

Mohan Makijany (24 April 1938 – 10 May 2010), popularly known as Mac Mohan, was an Indian actor, who worked in Hindi cinema. He was known for his villainous roles in films throughout the 1970s and 1980s. He appeared in over 200 films, including Don, Karz, Satte Pe Satta, Zanjeer, Rafoo Chakkar, Shaan, Khoon Pasina, and Sholay.

Mac Mohan (1938–2021) was an Indian actor known for his work in Hindi cinema, particularly during the 1970s and 1980s. Born in a Sikh family, he gained fame for his distinctive roles as a villain in many popular films, including "Sholay" (1975), where he played the character of Sambha. Mohan's career spanned over four decades, during which he appeared in numerous films and television serials, becoming a recognizable figure in the Indian film industry. His contributions to cinema were characterized by his versatility in portraying various character roles. Mohan also had a successful stint in television and was celebrated for his comedic performances. He passed away on April 10, 2021, leaving behind a legacy of memorable performances.

The enzyme (+)-δ-cadinene synthase (EC 4.2.3.13) catalyzes the chemical reaction (2E,6E)-farnesyl diphosphate ⇌ {\displaystyle \rightleftharpoons } (+)-δ-cadinene + diphosphate This enzyme belongs to the family of lyases, specifically those carbon-oxygen lyases acting on phosphates. The systematic name of this enzyme class is (2E,6E)-farnesyl-diphosphate diphosphate-lyase (cyclizing, (+)-δ-cadinene-forming). This enzyme participates in terpenoid biosynthesis. It employs one cofactor, magnesium. δ-Cadinene synthase, a sesquiterpene cyclase, is an enzyme expressed in plants that catalyzes a cyclization reaction in terpenoid biosynthesis. The enzyme cyclizes farnesyl diphosphate to δ-cadinene and releases pyrophosphate. δ-Cadinene synthase is one of the key steps in the synthesis of gossypol, a toxic terpenoid produced in cotton seeds. Recently, cotton plants that stably underexpress the enzyme in seeds have been developed using RNA interference techniques, producing a plant that had been proposed as a rich source of dietary protein for developing countries.

Delta-cadinene synthase is an enzyme that catalyzes the biosynthesis of delta-cadinene, a type of terpenoid found in various plant species. It is classified as a sesquiterpene synthase, which plays a crucial role in the production of natural products involved in plant defense mechanisms and aroma. This enzyme utilizes geranylgeranyl diphosphate as a substrate and is essential for the formation of sesquiterpene compounds that contribute to the plant's chemical ecology. Delta-cadinene has applications in the fragrance industry and in traditional medicine. Genetic studies have provided insights into the enzyme's structure, function, and its regulation within plant metabolic pathways.

Alpheus P. Hodges (1821 – July 29, 1858) was the first mayor of Los Angeles, California after the city was incorporated on April 4, 1850. However, he was preceded by Stephen C. Foster, who was the first mayor under American rule.

Alpheus P. Hodges (1820–1889) was an American politician and lawyer who served as a U.S. Congressman from Maryland. Born in Baltimore, he pursued a legal career and became involved in politics during the mid-19th century. Hodges was known for his work in the Democratic Party and was elected to the Forty-first Congress, serving from 1869 to 1871. After his term in Congress, he continued his legal practice and was active in local affairs. His contributions to Maryland's legislative history and his role in post-Civil War politics are noted, although he remained a relatively obscure figure in the broader history of the era.
David Angeli from the Imperial College London, UK was named Fellow of the Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE) in 2015 for contributions to nonlinear control theory.

David Angeli is an American mathematician known for his contributions to the fields of differential geometry and mathematical physics. He has published significant research on topics such as symplectic geometry, topological methods in mathematics, and their applications to quantum mechanics. Angeli earned his Ph.D. from the University of California, Berkeley, and has held faculty positions at various academic institutions. He is recognized for his interdisciplinary approach, bridging theoretical mathematics with practical applications in physics.
The 1981 Individual Speedway Junior European Championship was the fifth edition of the European Under-21 Championships. The Championship was won by Shawn Moran.

The 1981 Individual Speedway Junior European Championship was the inaugural edition of the annual speedway competition for junior riders in Europe, held under the auspices of the European Motorcycle Union (UEM). The championship took place in Plzeň, Czechoslovakia, on September 5, 1981. The event featured riders from various European countries competing in a series of heats to determine the overall champion. The title was claimed by the British rider, who emerged victorious over other competitors, establishing the championship as a key event for developing young talent in the sport of speedway racing.
Gopal Mishra (1933 – November 1, 2009) was an Indian journalist and columnist.

Gopal Mishra is a prominent figure in Indian politics and a member of the Indian National Congress party. He is known for his advocacy on social issues and contributions to community development in his constituency. Mishra has held various positions within the party and has been involved in numerous initiatives aimed at addressing the needs of marginalized communities. His political career has been marked by a focus on education, healthcare, and economic development. Additionally, he is recognized for his efforts in promoting transparency and accountability in governance.
