The conference which has been held since 1964 had as focus, the topic of the First Intifada, an uprising by the Palestinians against Israeli occupation and the consequent upswing in Israeli-Palestinian violence. The Arab states resolved to support the intifada financially. Other things decided in the conference were their concerns over Iran-Iraq war and displeasure with the United States over its bias in policy over the Arab-Israeli conflict.

The 1988 Arab League summit, held in Amman, Jordan, on November 28-29, marked a significant moment in the Arab world's political landscape, gathering leaders from the 21 member states. The summit addressed various regional issues, notably the Israeli-Palestinian conflict and the ongoing Iran-Iraq War. Key outcomes included the endorsement of the Palestinian Declaration of Independence, proclaimed by the Palestine Liberation Organization (PLO) in Algiers earlier that year, and the affirmation of Palestinian rights. The summit also focused on regional stability and cooperation among Arab nations, although differing national interests and political divisions limited the effectiveness of the resolutions adopted.

St. Marys or Saint Marys is a city in Warren County, Iowa, United States. The population was 108 at the time of the 2020 census. St. Marys is part of the Des Moines–West Des Moines Metropolitan Statistical Area.

St. Marys is a small, unincorporated community located in Marion County, Iowa, United States. It is situated southwest of the city of Knoxville and is part of the Knoxville Micropolitan Statistical Area. St. Marys is primarily residential and is known for its rural character and agricultural surroundings. The community is served by local schools and has access to various recreational activities due to its proximity to natural landscapes. Its population and infrastructure reflect the typical features of small Midwestern towns. St. Marys is also noted for its historical significance in the region and its role in the development of Marion County.

Pacific Southwest Airlines Flight 182 was a scheduled flight on September 25, 1978 by Pacific Southwest Airlines (PSA), from Sacramento (SMF) to San Diego (SAN), with a stopover at Los Angeles (LAX). The aircraft involved were a Boeing 727-214 (registration: N533PS) and a private Cessna 172 (light aircraft; N7711G) which collided mid-air over San Diego. It was Pacific Southwest Airlines' first fatal accident and it remains the deadliest air disaster in California history. At the time, it was the deadliest air crash to occur in the United States and remained so until the crash of American Airlines Flight 191 in May 1979. Excluding intentional crashes, it currently stands as the seventh-deadliest to occur on American soil. Following their collision, both the Boeing and the Cessna crashed into North Park, a residential but urban uptown neighborhood located roughly three miles northeast of downtown San Diego. PSA 182 crashed just north of the intersection of Dwight and Nile Streets, killing all 135 people aboard the aircraft along with seven bystanders on the ground or residents in their homes, including two children. The Cessna struck Polk Avenue, between 32nd and Iowa Streets, killing the two pilots on board. Nine others on the ground were injured and a total of twenty-two residences were destroyed or damaged by the impact and debris.

Pacific Southwest Airlines Flight 182 was a scheduled domestic passenger flight from Los Angeles to San Diego that tragically collided with a private Cessna 172 aircraft over San Diego, California, on September 25, 1978. The crash resulted in the death of all 135 people aboard the PSA flight, as well as the pilot and passenger of the Cessna, marking it as one of the deadliest aviation accidents in U.S. history. The collision occurred in visual flight conditions, and subsequent investigations identified a combination of air traffic control errors and pilot miscommunication as contributing factors. The accident prompted significant changes in air traffic control procedures and increased awareness regarding mid-air collision avoidance.

AntWeb is the leading online database on ants: storing specimens images and records, and natural history information, and documenting over 490,000 specimens across over 35,000 taxa of ants in its open source and community driven repository as of November 2014. It was set up by Brian L. Fisher in 2002, and cost US$30,000 to build.

AntWeb is a comprehensive online database and resource for the study of ants, managed by the California Academy of Sciences. Launched in 1999, it aims to provide researchers and the public with access to detailed information about ant species, including taxonomy, distribution, images, and ecological information. AntWeb features a vast collection of high-resolution photographs, specimen data, and interactive tools that facilitate the identification and classification of ants. Users can search by various criteria, including genus and species, and find information on ant behaviors and habitats. The platform is continually updated with contributions from researchers worldwide, making it an essential tool for myrmecologists and biodiversity studies.

An explosimeter is a gas detector which is used to measure the amount of combustible gases present in a sample. When a percentage of the lower explosive limit (LEL) of an atmosphere is exceeded, an alarm signal on the instrument is activated. The device, also called a combustible gas detector, operates on the principle of resistance proportional to heat—a wire is heated, and a sample of the gas is introduced to the hot wire. Combustible gases burn in the presence of the hot wire, thus increasing the resistance and disturbing a Wheatstone bridge, which gives the reading. A flashback arrestor is installed in the device to avoid the explosimeter igniting the sample external to the device. Note, that the detection readings of an explosimeter are only accurate if the gas being sampled has the same characteristics and response as the calibration gas. Most explosimeters are calibrated to methane, hydrogen, and carbon monoxide. Explosimeters are important because workspaces may contain a flammable or explosive atmosphere due to the accumulation of flammable gases or vapors. Sparks from ordinary battery-powered portable equipment, including cameras, cell phones, laptop computers, or anything else located on the job site may serve as an ignition source. The explosimeter warns the user of dangerous atmospheric conditions before a possible explosion can occur.

An explosimeter is an analytical instrument used for detecting and measuring the concentration of combustible gases and vapors in the atmosphere, primarily for safety purposes. It functions by utilizing various sensing technologies, such as catalytic beads, infrared sensors, or electrochemical cells, to identify the presence of flammable substances. Explosimeters are commonly used in industries such as oil and gas, chemical manufacturing, and construction, where there is a risk of explosive atmospheres. The readings provided by an explosimeter help ensure compliance with safety regulations and protect personnel from hazardous conditions. Regular calibration and maintenance are essential for accurate readings and effective safety monitoring.
George Villiers, 1st Duke of Buckingham commissioned works of art (paintings and plays) to enhance his personal image, as a means to aid his political career and advancement at court.
topic: Art patronage of George Villiers, 1st Duke of Buckingham

text: George Villiers, 1st Duke of Buckingham (1592–1628), was a prominent English courtier and art patron during the early 17th century. Close to King James I and later to King Charles I, Buckingham was instrumental in the promotion of arts and culture in England. He supported various artists, including the painter Anthony van Dyck, and was known for commissioning significant works that reflected his status and influence. Buckingham's patronage extended to architecture, exemplified by the construction of the grand Buckingham House (now part of Buckingham Palace) and the expansion of his estate at Burley-on-the-Hill. His dedication to the arts contributed to the flourishing of English Baroque art and architecture during this period. Despite his assassination in 1628, his legacy as a patron endures in the artistic heritage of England.
topic: Art patronage of George Villiers, 1st Duke of Buckingham

text: Charles Jaggard (born 22 May 1973) is an English former cricketer. He was a left-handed batsman who played for Buckinghamshire. He was born in Chalfont St. Peter. Jaggard appeared in the Minor Counties Championship for the first time in 1995, and made a single List A appearance, against Essex in the NatWest Trophy in 1997. He scored 28 runs in the match. Jaggard continued to play for Buckinghamshire until 1998.

Charles Jaggard (born 21 September 1860; date of death unknown) was an English professional footballer who played as a forward. He began his career with local clubs before joining the Football League in the early 1880s. Jaggard is most notably associated with his time at clubs such as Woolwich Arsenal and later with teams in the lower divisions of English football. Despite a relatively short career in the top tier, he contributed to the development of early professional football in England. His playing style and contributions are remembered in football history, although detailed statistics and records from the era are sparse.

No. 6 Squadron RNZAF is a maritime squadron of the Royal New Zealand Air Force. It has a history going back to World War II, when it operated flying boats, and has been disbanded and re-formed several times through changes in the country's military structure. The squadron motto, originally "Fortitudine et Diligentia", is now “Vigilance with Patience”.

No. 6 Squadron RNZAF is a unit of the Royal New Zealand Air Force (RNZAF) that specializes in air transport and logistics support. Established in 1947, it has played a significant role in numerous operational deployments and humanitarian missions both domestically and internationally. The squadron operates a variety of aircraft, including the Lockheed C-130 Hercules and Boeing 757, and has been involved in peacekeeping missions, disaster relief efforts, and support during military operations. The squadron is headquartered at RNZAF Base Auckland and is known for its adaptability and commitment to serving New Zealand's defense and civil obligations.

David Norman Verry (September 18, 1922 – October 12, 1961) was an American professional football player and coach. He played college football at the University of California (USC), earning third-team All-America and first-team All-Pacific Coast honors in 1942 as Tackle. Verry played professionally with Chicago Rockets of the All-America Football Conference (AAFC) from 1946 to 1947. He served as the head football coach at El Camino College in Alondra Park, California from 1952 to 1960, compiling a record of 54–27–2. Verry coached the line at his alma mater, USC, in 1947 and 1948. In 1949, he assisted Marty Arnaga in coaching the football team at Inglewood High School in Inglewood, California. He joined the staff at El Camino as line coach in 1950 and succeeded Ambrose Schindler as head football coach two years later. Verry died on October 12, 1961, at St. Vincent's Hospital in Los Angeles, after suffering a brain hemorrhage three weeks prior.

Norm Verry (born October 15, 1975) is a Canadian former professional ice hockey player who played primarily as a defenseman. Verry was drafted by the New Jersey Devils in the 7th round of the 1993 NHL Entry Draft. He played parts of three seasons in the National Hockey League (NHL) with the Devils and later had a successful career in various international leagues and minor leagues, including the American Hockey League (AHL) and the East Coast Hockey League (ECHL). Known for his physical play and defensive skills, Verry retired from professional hockey in 2006 and transitioned into coaching and youth development programs.

Victor Huot (August 26, 1867 – April 28, 1915) was a French cartographer, who drew maps of various areas including South America and Africa. Huot worked for publisher Hachette et Cie. He was a member of the Paris Geographical Society and the American Geographical Society. He made a map of the Andes. Hout was killed in battle on April 28, 1915.

Victor Huot (1836–1888) was a renowned French cartographer noted for his contributions to the field of map-making during the 19th century. He specialized in the creation of detailed topographic maps and was influential in the mapping of colonial territories, particularly in North Africa. Huot worked extensively with the French military, which relied on his expertise for military expeditions and territorial claims. His maps were characterized by their precision and aesthetic detail, earning recognition among contemporary geographers and cartographers. Huot's legacy is marked by his innovative approach to cartography and the role his work played in enhancing navigation and geographical understanding during a pivotal era of exploration and colonial expansion.
Ulvaria obscura is an intertidal and subtidal benthic marine algae found in temperate and Arctic ocean waters around the world.

Ulvaria obscura is a species of small moth belonging to the family Crambidae, commonly known as grass moths. It is found primarily in North America, where it inhabits a variety of regions, often in environments that support its preferred host plants. The adult moths are characterized by their narrow wings and distinctive coloration, which can vary among individuals. The larvae of U. obscura typically feed on grasses, contributing to the ecological dynamics of their habitats. Like many moths, U. obscura plays a role in the ecosystem as both a pollinator and a food source for various predators. Researchers continue to study its behavior, distribution, and interaction with its environment to better understand its ecological impact.

Zangiabad (Persian: زنگي اباد, also Romanized as Zangīābād) is a village in Mokriyan-e Shomali Rural District, in the Central District of Miandoab County, West Azerbaijan Province, Iran. At the 2006 census, its population was 56, in 10 families.

Zangiabad is a village located in the West Azerbaijan Province of Iran, situated within the Central District of Urmia County. It is part of the Zangan Rural District. As of the 2006 census, Zangiabad had a population of 331 residents, living in a total of 80 families. The village is characterized by its rural setting and is primarily inhabited by ethnic Azerbaijani communities. Zangiabad serves as a local hub for surrounding agricultural activities, reflecting the region's economic reliance on farming and related industries.

In mathematics, an elliptic partial differential equation is a type of partial differential equation (PDE). In mathematical modeling, elliptic PDEs are frequently used to model steady states, unlike parabolic and hyperbolic PDEs, which generally model phenomena that change in time. The canonical examples of elliptic PDEs are Laplace's equation and Poisson's equation. Elliptic PDEs are also important in pure mathematics, where they are fundamental to various fields of research such as differential geometry and optimal transport.

Elliptic partial differential equations (PDEs) are a class of second-order PDEs characterized by their resemblance to elliptic geometric entities and their properties in relation to boundary value problems. They typically arise in various fields such as physics, engineering, and mathematics, particularly in contexts involving steady-state heat conduction, fluid flow, and potential theory. The standard form of a linear elliptic PDE involves a non-negative definite coefficient matrix associated with the second derivatives of the unknown function. A prominent example is the Laplace equation, which is essential in electrostatics and fluid dynamics. Elliptic equations exhibit unique solutions under appropriate boundary conditions, reflecting stable and well-behaved phenomena. Key methods for studying elliptic PDEs include variational techniques, maximum principles, and conformal mappings.

The 2009 Bank of Beijing China Open was a professional ranking snooker tournament that took place between 30 March and 5 April 2009 at the Beijing University Students' Gymnasium in Beijing, China. The event was sponsored by Bank of Beijing and Hyundai. The defending champion was Stephen Maguire, but he lost in the first round 0–5 against Dave Harold. Peter Ebdon won in the final 10–8 against John Higgins.

The 2009 China Open was a professional snooker tournament held from March 30 to April 5, 2009, at the Capital Indoor Stadium in Beijing, China. It was part of the 2008/2009 snooker season and featured a ranking event format. The tournament was won by England's Ronnie O'Sullivan, who defeated Scotland's John Higgins 10–8 in the final, securing his second China Open title. The event saw a participation of many top-ranking players and was notable for its high level of competition. The total prize fund for the tournament was £500,000, with O'Sullivan receiving £75,000 as the champion.

Michael Francis Maroney (1 November 1910 – 5 March 1972) was an Australian rules footballer who played with North Melbourne in the Victorian Football League (VFL). Maroney was a leading sprinter in his youth, notably winning the Wangaratta Gift as an 18 year old in 1930. He later served in the Australian Army during World War II.

Mick Maroney is an Australian rugby union player known for his position as a lock. He played for various clubs in Australia and has represented national teams at different age levels. Maroney gained recognition for his physical playing style and strong lineout skills. His career includes participation in competitive league matches and a focus on developing rugby talent in Australia. In addition to his performance on the field, Maroney has been involved in coaching and mentoring emerging players in the sport.

Roberto Mario Gómez y Bolaños (21 February 1929 – 28 November 2014), more commonly known by his stage name Chespirito, or "Little Shakespeare", was a Mexican actor, comedian, screenwriter, humorist, director, producer, and author. He is widely regarded as one of the icons of Spanish-speaking humor and entertainment and one of the greatest comedians of all time. He is also one of the most loved and respected comedians in Latin America. He is mostly known by his acting role Chavo from the sitcom El Chavo del Ocho.

Chespirito was the television and comedy persona of Mexican writer, actor, comedian, film director, television director, and producer Roberto Gómez Bolaños (1929–2014). He is best known for creating and starring in iconic television series such as "El Chavo del Ocho" and "El Chapulín Colorado," which became immensely popular in Latin America and among Spanish-speaking audiences worldwide. Chespirito's shows were characterized by their humor, memorable characters, and social commentary, often appealing to both children and adults. His influence on Latin American entertainment is significant, with his work continuing to be celebrated long after his passing. Chespirito received various awards for his contributions to television and comedy throughout his career.

Impact! Miniatures is a company started in April 2006 by members of the Blood Bowl online community with the aim of producing figures suitable for use with Fantasy Football games such as Elfball, Blood Bowl, Street Brawl, Street Bowl and Deathball.

Impact! Miniatures is a company specializing in the design and manufacture of tabletop miniatures for gaming and hobbyists. Founded in the early 2000s, the company is known for producing a wide variety of figures, primarily for sports games like football (American) and for fantasy and science fiction themes. Impact! Miniatures emphasizes quality and detail in their sculpts, offering products in different sizes and materials, including resin and metal. They provide a mix of original designs as well as licensed character figures, catering to both casual gamers and serious collectors. The company also engages with the gaming community through crowdfunding campaigns, online retail, and participation in conventions.
Sasha Palatnikov (born March 15, 1989) is an American mixed martial artist who competed in the Welterweight division of the Ultimate Fighting Championship. He currently competes in Karate Combat.

Sasha Palatnikov is a Russian-born professional mixed martial artist competing in the welterweight division. He began his career in 2014 and quickly gained recognition for his striking skills and resilience in the cage. Palatnikov has fought in various promotions, including the Ultimate Fighting Championship (UFC), where he made his debut in 2020. Known for his dynamic fighting style, he has a background in kickboxing and has achieved multiple victories by knockout. Throughout his career, Palatnikov has been noted for his work ethic and determination to advance in the sport.
The Aristaeus-class repair ship was a class of repair ships of the United States Navy during the Second World War.

The Aristaeus-class repair ship is a class of naval auxiliary vessels designed primarily for the repair and maintenance of naval ships and submarines. Developed by the United States Navy, these ships are equipped with advanced facilities that allow for both on-station maintenance and emergency repairs at sea. The Aristaeus-class ships typically feature workshops, storage for parts and equipment, and capabilities for conducting electrical, mechanical, and structural repairs. Their design enhances fleet operational capabilities by extending the range and effectiveness of naval forces, allowing them to remain deployed for longer periods without returning to port for service. The class was named after Aristaeus, a figure from Greek mythology associated with various beneficial arts.

Freddie and Frieda Falcon are the mascots of Bowling Green State University in Bowling Green, Ohio. The pair are anthropomorphized peregrine falcons. They are somewhat of a rarity among collegiate mascots, being one of the few male-female mascot pairs in existence. In 2006 they were both named "Best Collegiate Mascot" at the 2006 NCA Cheer Camp in Nashville, Tennessee at Vanderbilt University.

Freddie and Frieda Falcon are fictional characters and the official mascots of the National Football League's Atlanta Falcons. Introduced in the early 1980s, Freddie, a male falcon, and Frieda, a female falcon, embody the spirit of the team and the city of Atlanta. They appear at home games and various community events, engaging with fans and promoting team spirit. Their design features a vibrant color palette of red and black, reflecting the team's branding. Over the years, they have become recognizable symbols of the franchise, contributing to the overall fan experience and community outreach efforts.

Mary Pope Osborne (born May 20, 1949) is an American author of children's books and audiobook narrator. She is best known as the author of the Magic Tree House series, which as of 2017 sold more than 134 million copies worldwide. Both the series and Osborne have won awards, including for Osborne's charitable efforts at promoting children's literacy. One of four children, Osborne moved around in her childhood before attending the University of North Carolina. Following college, Osborne traveled before moving to New York City. She somewhat spontaneously began to write, and her first book was published in 1982. She went on to write a variety of other children's and young adult books before starting the Magic Tree House series in 1992. Osborne's sister Natalie Pope Boyce has written several compendium books to the Magic Tree House series, sometimes with Osborne's husband Will.

Mary Pope Osborne is an American author best known for her children's book series "Magic Tree House," which features the adventures of siblings Jack and Annie as they travel through time and space. Born on May 20, 1949, in Fort Scott, Kansas, Osborne developed a passion for storytelling at an early age. She has written over 50 novels, including the "Magic Tree House" books, which have captivated young readers since their debut in 1992. Osborne's works often blend elements of history, science, and fantasy, encouraging curiosity and learning among children. In addition to her writing, she has taken on various initiatives to promote literacy and has received numerous awards for her contributions to children's literature.
Aepylopha is a genus of moths in the family Geometridae. It contains only one species, Aepylopha thalassia, which is found in Australia.

Aepylopha is a genus of moths belonging to the family Erebidae, specifically within the subfamily Arctiinae, which is commonly known as tiger moths. The genus was first described by the entomologist Paul D. H. Heinemann in 1950. Aepylopha species are primarily found in the Neotropical region, with some species documented in Central and South America. Like other members of the Arctiinae, Aepylopha moths are characterized by their vivid coloration and unique patterns, which serve as a defense mechanism against predators. Research on the specific behaviors, ecological roles, and conservation status of Aepylopha moths remains limited, highlighting the need for further entomological studies in their habitats.
Selaserica vagans is a species of beetle of the family Scarabaeidae. It is found in India (Nilgiri Hills).

Selaserica vagans is a species of beetle belonging to the family Scarabaeidae, commonly known as scarab beetles. This species is part of the subfamily Scarabaeinae and is primarily found in various habitats across parts of Africa. Like other members of its family, Selaserica vagans plays a role in the ecosystem as a dung beetle, helping to decompose organic matter and recycle nutrients. The species exhibits sexual dimorphism, with males typically being more robust than females. Its identification features include distinctive body coloration and morphology, which are characteristic of the genus Selaserica. Conservation status and population dynamics are subject to local environmental conditions and habitat preservation efforts.
Scarus dubius, also known as regal parrotfish, is a parrotfish endemic to the Hawaiian Islands.

Scarus dubius, commonly known as the doubtful parrotfish, is a species of parrotfish belonging to the family Scaridae. It is primarily found in shallow coastal waters and coral reefs of the Indo-Pacific region, including areas around the Red Sea, East Africa, and the Pacific Islands. This species is recognized for its vibrant coloration, which can vary between individuals. Like other parrotfish, Scarus dubius plays a crucial ecological role in coral reef ecosystems by grazing on algae and contributing to the maintenance of coral health. Their feeding habits help prevent algal overgrowth, promoting biodiversity within their habitat. Conservation status assessments are ongoing, but habitat destruction and climate change pose threats to its populations.
2016 Giravanz Kitakyushu season.

The 2016 Giravanz Kitakyushu season was the club's fifth season in Japan's J2 League. The team, based in Kitakyushu, Fukuoka Prefecture, aimed to improve upon their previous year's performance. Under the management of Kiyoshi Okuma, Giravanz Kitakyushu finished the season in 18th place, resulting in relegation to the J3 League for the following season. The season was marked by struggles in both offensive and defensive aspects, with the team facing challenges maintaining consistency in their performances. Despite the difficulties, several players had notable individual showings throughout the year. The club's activities during this season included participation in the Emperor's Cup and the J.League Cup, where their efforts were commendable but ultimately did not lead to significant advances in those tournaments.

The New Synagogue (German: Neuen Synagoge) is a Jewish congregation, community center, and synagogue, located on Synagogenplatz, Mainz in the state of Rhineland-Palatinate, in Germany. The Modernist-styled building was erected in 2010, on the site of the former main synagogue, destroyed in 1938, on the Hindenburgstraße of Mainz Neustadt.

The New Synagogue in Mainz, Germany, is a historic Jewish house of worship that was inaugurated in 2010, replacing a previous synagogue that was destroyed during the Kristallnacht pogrom in 1938. Designed by architect Manuel Herz, the structure is notable for its modern architectural style that incorporates traditional elements. The synagogue serves as a center for the Jewish community in Mainz and includes facilities for cultural and educational activities, alongside its primary function as a place of worship. The New Synagogue also emphasizes themes of memory and reconciliation, reflecting the Jewish community's long history in Mainz, which dates back to the Middle Ages.
Falling Up (known in Australia as The Golden Door) is a 2009 American romantic comedy film that was released direct-to-video in late 2009 or early 2010 depending on region.

"Falling Up" is a 2009 American romantic comedy-drama film directed by Tony W. T. Hsu, featuring a screenplay by Vance DeGeneres and Jennifer Crittenden. The film stars Joe Anderson, Sarah Roemer, and Senta Moses, and follows the story of a young man who becomes embroiled in the lives of a group of eccentric characters while navigating the ups and downs of life and love in a vibrant urban setting. With a blend of humor and heartfelt moments, "Falling Up" explores themes of personal growth, relationships, and the pursuit of dreams amidst challenges. The film received mixed reviews from critics but has garnered attention for its unique storytelling and character development.
Notosetia is a genus of minute sea snails or micromolluscs, marine gastropod molluscs.

Notosetia is a genus of flowering plants in the family Euphorbiaceae, native to regions in the southeastern and eastern parts of Asia. It is characterized by its herbaceous or shrubby growth forms, with variations in leaf shape and flower structure. The genus includes several species, some of which are significant in traditional medicine and local agriculture. Research on Notosetia has focused on its ecological roles and potential uses in horticulture and pharmacology. The taxonomy of the genus is complex, and it has undergone various classifications within the broader Euphorbiaceae family.

The team eventing event at the 2020 Summer Olympics is scheduled to take place from 30 July to 2 August 2021 at the Baji Koen and Sea Forest Cross-Country Course. Like all other equestrian events, the eventing competition is open-gender, with both male and female athletes competing in the same division. 45 riders (15 teams of 3) from 15 nations are expected to compete.
topic: Equestrian at the 2020 Summer Olympics – Team eventing

text: The team eventing competition in equestrian at the 2020 Summer Olympics took place in Tokyo, Japan, from July 30 to August 2, 2021, due to the COVID-19 pandemic. The event featured teams from various nations competing in three phases: dressage, cross-country, and show jumping. Each team consisted of three horse-rider pairs, with the final score determined by the cumulative penalties incurred across all phases. The German team won the gold medal, followed by the Australian team taking silver, and the Great Britain team securing the bronze. This event showcased the skills and teamwork of equestrian athletes at an elite level.
topic: Equestrian at the 2020 Summer Olympics – Team eventing

text: Haustein is a surname. Notable people with the surname include: Dennis Haustein (born 1990), German politician Gene Haustein (1907–1984), American racing driver Hans Haustein (1894–1933), German scientist Knut-Olaf Haustein (1934–2006), German physician

Haustein is a surname of German origin, often associated with individuals in various fields such as arts, sciences, and politics. The name may also refer to specific locations in Germany, characterized by cultural or historical significance. In addition to its genealogical associations, Haustein may appear in literature and scholarly works, reflecting its use in naming conventions and local heritage. The significance of the name can vary based on regional contexts and the prominence of individuals bearing it.
The 2018 season was the 109th season in the history of Sport Club Corinthians Paulista.

The 2018 season for Sport Club Corinthians Paulista was notable for the team's performance in multiple competitions, including the Campeonato Brasileiro Série A, Copa do Brasil, and Copa Libertadores. Under manager Fábio Carille, the club aimed to build on their success from previous seasons but faced challenges throughout the year. In the Série A, Corinthians finished 13th, struggling with consistency. In the Copa do Brasil, they reached the quarter-finals but were eliminated by the eventual champions. The team's performance in the Copa Libertadores was also disappointing, as they were knocked out in the group stage. Additionally, the season saw several key player transfers and a focus on youth development as part of the club’s long-term strategy. Overall, the 2018 season was a transitional year for Corinthians, marked by a mix of challenges and efforts to revitalize the squad.

Rożniatów [rɔʐˈɲatuf] is a village in the administrative district of Gmina Uniejów, within Poddębice County, Łódź Voivodeship, in central Poland. It lies approximately 9 km (6 mi) north of Uniejów, 19 km (12 mi) north-west of Poddębice, and 53 km (33 mi) north-west of the regional capital Łódź.

Rożniatów is a village located in the administrative district of Gmina Warta, within Sieradz County, Łódź Voivodeship, in central Poland. It lies approximately 3 kilometers southeast of Warta, 17 kilometers northeast of Sieradz, and 66 kilometers west of the regional capital, Łódź. The area is characterized by its rural landscape and is part of the historical region of Greater Poland. The village is predominantly agricultural, with local traditions and community life reflective of the surrounding rural culture.
Rakitny (Russian: Ракитный; masculine), Rakitnaya (Ракитная; feminine), or Rakitnoye (Ракитное; neuter) is the name of several inhabited localities in Russia.

Rakitny is a village located in the Chuvash Republic of Russia. It is part of the Kanashsky District and is known for its rural landscape and agricultural activities. The village is characterized by traditional Russian architecture and a close-knit community. Rakitny's economy primarily revolves around farming and local craftsmanship. The region is also notable for its cultural heritage, including local festivals and customs that reflect Chuvash traditions. As of the latest census, the population of Rakitny is relatively small, reflecting the demographic trends seen in many rural areas of Russia.

Max Hürlimann (born 30 July 1951) is a Swiss former professional tennis player. Active in the 1970s, Hürlimann was based in Zürich and didn't turn professional until the age of 26, due to his tertiary studies in accounting. He reached a best singles ranking inside the world's top 200 during his time on tour, with career wins over Rick Fagel and Butch Seewagen. Between 1975 and 1978 he made appearances for the Switzerland Davis Cup team, winning two of his four singles rubbers.

Max Hürlimann (born 3 December 1956) is a Swiss politician and a member of the Swiss Federal Council. He represents the Swiss People's Party and has held various positions within the government, focusing on issues related to agriculture, rural affairs, and environmental policy. Hürlimann previously served as the head of the Federal Department of Finance and has been actively involved in shaping Switzerland's economic strategies. Known for his pragmatic approach and commitment to Swiss sovereignty, he has also advocated for sustainable development and innovation in the agricultural sector.
Robert Tenorio (born December 29, 1950) is a Kewa (Santo Domingo) potter.

Robert Tenorio is a notable figure in the field of contemporary art, recognized for his work in painting and sculpture. His art often explores themes of cultural identity, heritage, and the intersection of tradition and modernity. Tenorio's works frequently reflect his experiences and background, drawing on influences from his Native American heritage. Throughout his career, he has participated in various exhibitions and has received recognition for his contributions to the art community. Tenorio is also involved in educational initiatives, promoting art awareness and appreciation among diverse audiences. His works are held in private and public collections, affirming his impact on contemporary artistic discourse.
Mussy-la-Fosse (French pronunciation: [mysi la fos]) is a commune in the Côte-d'Or department in eastern France.

Mussy-la-Fosse is a commune in the Aube department in the Grand Est region of north-central France. It is located approximately 20 kilometers southeast of Troyes and is part of the administrative arrondissement of Troyes and the canton of Bar-sur-Aube. The commune covers an area of 9.09 square kilometers and has a population that fluctuates around a small number, reflecting the rural nature of the area. Mussy-la-Fosse is characterized by its agricultural landscape and historical architecture, including traditional houses and churches. The commune is served by local road networks and is known for its peaceful environment and proximity to natural settings.

Pontibacillus halophilus is a Gram-positive, moderately halophilic, strictly aerobic and motile bacterium from the genus of Pontibacillus which has been isolated from a sea urchin from the Laizhou Bay.

Pontibacillus halophilus is a species of halophilic, Gram-positive bacteria belonging to the genus Pontibacillus. It was first isolated from saline environments and is characterized by its ability to thrive in high-salt concentrations. The organism exhibits a rod-shaped morphology and is known for its potential applications in biotechnology, particularly in the production of enzymes and biopolymers in saline conditions. P. halophilus is significant for understanding extremophiles and their adaptations to harsh environments, as well as for its ecological roles in saltwater ecosystems.
Walter Symes (1852 – 14 October 1914) was a Liberal Party Member of Parliament in New Zealand.

Walter Symes (1883–1919) was a British naval officer and writer noted for his contributions to maritime literature and naval strategy. Born in England, Symes served in the Royal Navy during World War I, where he gained recognition for his tactical insights and leadership. Following the war, he published several influential works highlighting naval operations and the significance of maritime power. His writings reflect the evolving nature of naval warfare during the early 20th century and provide historical perspectives on naval tactics. Symes's legacy continues to resonate in discussions of naval history and strategy. He passed away in 1919 at the age of 36.

Causal inference is the process of determining the independent, actual effect of a particular phenomenon that is a component of a larger system. The main difference between causal inference and inference of association is that causal inference analyzes the response of an effect variable when a cause of the effect variable is changed. The study of why things occur is called etiology, and can be described using the language of scientific causal notation. Causal inference is said to provide the evidence of causality theorized by causal reasoning. Causal inference is widely studied across all sciences. Several innovations in the development and implementation of methodology designed to determine causality have proliferated in recent decades. Causal inference remains especially difficult where experimentation is difficult or impossible, which is common throughout most sciences. The approaches to causal inference are broadly applicable across all types of scientific disciplines, and many methods of causal inference that were designed for certain disciplines have found use in other disciplines. This article outlines the basic process behind causal inference and details some of the more conventional tests used across different disciplines; however, this should not be mistaken as a suggestion that these methods apply only to those disciplines, merely that they are the most commonly used in that discipline. Causal inference is difficult to perform and there is significant debate amongst scientists about the proper way to determine causality. Despite other innovations, there remain concerns of misattribution by scientists of correlative results as causal, of the usage of incorrect methodologies by scientists, and of deliberate manipulation by scientists of analytical results in order to obtain statistically significant estimates. Particular concern is raised in the use of regression models, especially linear regression models.

Causal inference is a statistical method used to determine whether a relationship between two variables is causal rather than merely correlational. It aims to identify the effect of one variable on another, often utilizing frameworks such as randomized controlled trials, observational studies, and statistical modeling techniques. Key concepts in causal inference include confounding variables, treatment effects, and counterfactual reasoning. Researchers employ tools like propensity score matching, regression discontinuity, and instrumental variables to draw causal conclusions while addressing potential biases. Causal inference is widely applied across disciplines such as epidemiology, economics, social sciences, and machine learning, contributing to informed decision-making and policy development.

Robert E. Wilson is an astrophysicist, academic, and author. He is a professor emeritus at the University of Florida (UF). Wilson's research covers various aspects of astrophysics including stellar models, stellar structure and evolution, and close binary stars. He has co-edited and co-authored books on astrophysical topics, and his research has received awards such as the Humboldt Prize from Germany's Alexander von Humboldt Foundation. Wilson is a member of the American Astronomical Society, the Royal Astronomical Society, the Astronomical Society of the Pacific, the Norristown (PA) Eisenhower High School Hall of Fame, and the International Astronomical Union.

Robert E. Wilson (born June 10, 1935) is an American biologist known for his contributions to biodiversity, ecology, and evolutionary biology. He is a professor at Harvard University and has authored or co-authored several influential works, including the widely regarded book "The Diversity of Life." Wilson is also known for the development of the theory of island biogeography, which he co-created with fellow biologist Edward O. Wilson, leading to insights into species diversity and conservation strategies. His research has significantly influenced conservation policy and ecological studies. In addition to his academic career, Wilson is an advocate for science communication and environmental preservation.
Luis de Bolaños (1549? – 11 October 1629) was a Spanish Franciscan friar and missionary evangelist, initiator of the system of reductions (indigenous towns) in Paraguay and northeastern Argentina.

Luis de Bolaños (1564 – 1634) was a Spanish Jesuit missionary and linguist known for his extensive work in the Philippines, particularly in the introduction and promotion of Christianity among the indigenous populations. He is recognized for his contributions to the study and documentation of local languages, particularly the Tagalog language, and for his efforts in compiling catechisms and religious texts to aid in missionary work. Bolaños also played a significant role in the establishment of Jesuit missions and educational institutions in the region, influencing the cultural and religious landscape of the Philippines during the Spanish colonial period. His legacy includes both the promotion of Christianity and the preservation of indigenous languages and cultures.

The eastern worm snake (Carphophis amoenus amoenus) is a subspecies of the worm snake, Carphophis amoenus, a nonvenomous colubrid endemic to the Eastern Woodlands region of North America. The species' range extends from southwest Massachusetts, south to southern Alabama, west to Louisiana and north to Illinois. This species is common in the ecotone between woodlands and wetlands. It may also be found in grasslands adjacent to woodlands. Though this snake can be abundant in parts of its range, it is rarely seen because of its fossorial lifestyle. When not underground, C. a. amoenus resides mostly under rocks, logs and leaf litter, or burrowed within rotting woody debris. This snake is perfectly safe to pick up, as it cannot bite, but may produce a foul-smelling excretion.

The Eastern worm snake (Carphophis amoeenus) is a species of snake belonging to the family Colubridae, primarily found in the eastern United States. This small, burrowing snake is characterized by its smooth, shiny scales and a slender body, typically measuring between 10 to 15 inches in length. The dorsal coloration is usually a uniform brown or gray, while the underside is lighter in color, often white or pinkish. Eastern worm snakes are often mistaken for earthworms due to their shape and movement. They primarily feed on soft-bodied invertebrates such as earthworms and slugs. Preferring moist environments like forests, fields, and gardens, these snakes are non-venomous and pose no threat to humans. Their populations are considered stable, although habitat loss may impact local densities.

Calystegia occidentalis is a species of morning glory known by the common names Modoc morning glory or chaparral false bindweed. It is native to California and Oregon, where it grows in hilly and mountain habitat, such as woodland and chaparral slopes and the high Sierra Nevada.

Calystegia occidentalis, commonly known as the western bindweed or California bindweed, is a perennial flowering plant in the morning glory family (Convolvulaceae). Native to western North America, it typically grows in a variety of habitats, including meadows, grasslands, and scrub areas. The plant is characterized by its twining stems, heart-shaped leaves, and large, trumpet-shaped white to pale pink flowers that bloom from late spring to early fall. C. occidentalis is often utilized in ornamental gardening for its attractive flowers and vigorous growth, but it can also exhibit invasive tendencies in some regions. Its ecological role includes providing nectar for pollinators, although caution is advised due to its potential to dominate and disrupt native plant communities.

Sons of Abraham were a five-piece Jewish straight edge metalcore band from Long Island, New York. They released one demo, a split EP with Indecision, and a CD/LP Termites In His Smile. They disbanded in 1998, as guitarists Justin Beck and Todd Weinstock decided to concentrate on their other band, Glassjaw with friend Daryl Palumbo.

The Sons of Abraham is a term often used to refer to the descendants of the biblical patriarch Abraham in the context of the Abrahamic religions—Judaism, Christianity, and Islam. In Judaism, the term primarily identifies the Jewish people as descendants of Abraham through his son Isaac. In Christianity, believers are often referred to as spiritual descendants of Abraham, emphasizing faith over ethnic lineage. In Islam, Abraham (known as Ibrahim) is an important prophet, and his sons Isaac and Ishmael are considered ancestors of the Jewish and Arab peoples, respectively. The concept exemplifies the shared heritage and interconnectedness of these three major world religions, highlighting themes of faith, covenant, and divine promise. The Sons of Abraham also serves as a metaphor for interfaith dialogue and the pursuit of unity among the three faiths.

The Stroh violin or Stroviol is a type of stringed musical instrument that is mechanically amplified by a metal resonator and horn attached to its body. The name Stroviol refers to a violin, but other instruments have been modified with the amplification device, including the viola, cello, double bass, ukulele, mandolin, and guitar. Johannes Matthias Augustus Stroh, an electrical engineer from Frankfurt, invented the instrument in London in 1899.

The Stroh violin is a type of acoustic violin that incorporates a brass or metal resonator, enhancing its sound projection. Invented in the late 19th century by the German instrument maker Joseph Stroh, it was designed to be more audible in settings such as orchestras and recording studios, where traditional violins might struggle to project their sound. The instrument features a distinctive design, with a long neck, a curved body, and a large, often cone-shaped resonator that amplifies sound through vibration. Popular in the early 20th century, particularly during the rise of radio and recording technologies, the Stroh violin has since become less common but is still used by some musicians for its unique tonal qualities and aesthetic appeal.

Retinyl palmitate, or vitamin A palmitate, is the ester of retinol (vitamin A) and palmitic acid, with formula C36H60O2. It is the most abundant form of vitamin A storage in animals. An alternate spelling, retinol palmitate, which violates the -yl organic chemical naming convention for esters, is also frequently seen. In 2021, vitamin A was the 298th most commonly prescribed medication in the United States, with more than 500,000 prescriptions.

Retinyl palmitate is a synthetic derivative of vitamin A (retinol) formed by the esterification of retinol with palmitic acid. It is commonly used in cosmetics and skincare products for its antioxidant properties and ability to promote skin cell turnover, which can improve the appearance of fine lines, wrinkles, and overall skin texture. Additionally, retinyl palmitate is utilized in dietary supplements and fortification of food products to enhance vitamin A intake. While generally considered safe for topical use, excessive exposure can lead to skin irritation. Its efficacy and safety have been subjects of research and regulatory scrutiny, particularly regarding potential links to skin sensitivity and other adverse effects.

James Carey (1845–1883) was a Fenian, most notable for his involvement in the Phoenix Park Murders. He has been called "the most militant-minded republican you could possibly meet" by historian Dr Shane Kenna.

James Carey (1838–1883) was an Irish Fenian and prominent figure in the Irish nationalist movement. Born in County Tipperary, he became actively involved in the Fenian Brotherhood, an organization aimed at establishing an independent Irish Republic. Carey gained notoriety as a key participant in the Fenian Rising of 1867, an unsuccessful insurrection against British rule in Ireland. Following the Rising, he was arrested and sentenced to death, though his sentence was later commuted to life imprisonment. After serving a decade in prison, Carey was released during the 1870s and subsequently emigrated to the United States. His later years were marked by controversy as he cooperated with British authorities, leading to tensions within the Fenian community. Carey was ultimately killed in 1883 in Cape Town, South Africa, believed to be by assassins sent by his former associates. His life illustrates the complexities of loyalty and betrayal within revolutionary movements.

Dogdakedar (Nepali: दोगडाकेदार) is a Gaupalika (Nepali: गाउपालिका, romanized: gaupalika) in Baitadi District in the Sudurpashchim Province of far-western Nepal. Dogdakedar has a population of 24632.The land area is 126.38 km2.

Dogdakedar Rural Municipality is a rural municipality located in the Baitadi District of Sudurpashchim Province in Nepal. It was established in 2017 as part of the government's federal restructuring of local governance. The municipality encompasses several wards and is part of the larger administrative framework for local development and governance. Dogdakedar is characterized by its rural landscape, agricultural activities, and various cultural practices. It aims to promote sustainable development while catering to the needs of its residents. The local government is responsible for providing essential services, infrastructure development, and enhancing the overall quality of life for the community.
Selin Kuralay (born 25 January 1985 in Melbourne, Australia) is an Australian soccer player of Turkish ancestry. She plays at national league level for Australian W-League team Melbourne Victory.

Selin Kuralay is a Turkish actress, born on June 29, 1982, in Istanbul, Turkey. She studied acting at the Istanbul University State Conservatory and gained recognition for her roles in various Turkish television series and films. Kuralay made her television debut in the early 2000s and has since appeared in prominent series such as "Yalancı Yarim" and "İkimizin Yerine." In addition to her work in television, she has also participated in theatrical productions and films, showcasing her versatility as an actress. Kuralay is known for her dramatic performances and has garnered critical acclaim throughout her career.

The Great Brain Robbery is the second and final studio album by British indie rock band The Crocketts. Recorded at Chapel Studios, Monnow Valley Studio and Ridge Farm Studio with producers Charlie Francis and Bird & Bush, the album was released by Blue Dog Records in conjunction with V2 Records on 17 April 2000. "Host", "On Something" and "1939 Returning/Chicken vs. Macho" were released as singles in 2000, all of which reached the top 100 on the UK Singles Chart.

The Great Brain Robbery is the second studio album by the British hip hop duo Bad Meets Evil, consisting of rapper Royce da 5'9" and fellow rapper Eminem. Released on June 14, 2011, the album serves as a sequel to their 1999 EP, Hell: The Sequel. Featuring a mix of hip hop and rap, it showcases the duo's lyrical prowess and chemistry, with production from a variety of producers including DJ Khalil and Eminem himself. The album received generally positive reviews from critics and debuted at number 1 on the US Billboard 200 chart, featuring hit singles such as "Fast Lane" and "Lighters." The Great Brain Robbery is noted for its clever wordplay, intricate rhyme schemes, and the artists' exploration of themes like ambition, collaboration, and personal growth.

The 1984 South African presidential election was the first to be held under the new South African Constitution of 1983, which abolished the office of Prime Minister and merged its powers into the position of State President, who was now both head of state and government. According to the new basic law, the State President was to be elected by an electoral college composed of members of the majority party in each of the chambers of the new tricameral parliament. As such, the electoral college was composed of 50 National Party members elected by the white House of Assembly, 25 Labor Party members from the Coloured House of Representatives, and 13 members of the National People's Party elected by the Indian House of Delegates. The election came after the South African elections of 1984 to the two new legislative chambers in the South African Parliament: the House of Representatives, filled with representatives of the colored population in South Africa, and the House of Delegates, filled with representatives of the Indian population. The House of Assembly, the assembly representative of the white population last elected in 1981, was not reelected until 1987. By unanimous vote of the members of the electoral college (88 votes), Pieter Willem Botha, outgoing Prime Minister and leader of the ruling National Party, was elected State President of South Africa, the first South African head of state to simultaneously be Head of Government. The inauguration of Botha's five-year term, marked by a ceremony in Parliament, took place in Cape Town on September 14, 1984.

The 1984 South African presidential election was held on September 6, 1984, as part of the controversial tricameral parliamentary system instituted by the apartheid government. This system allowed for separate representation for white, Coloured, and Indian populations while excluding the black majority. The election saw P.W. Botha, the leader of the National Party and the incumbent prime minister, secure a resounding victory, reaffirming his position as head of state. Voter turnout was reported to be low, particularly among the non-white populace, reflecting widespread discontent with the apartheid system. The election resulted in Botha's continued grip on power during a period marked by domestic unrest and increasing international isolation due to apartheid policies.

USS Keokuk (AN-5/CM-8/CMc-6/AKN-4) was a mine and net laying ship of the United States Navy during World War II. Laid down in 1914 as the SS Henry M. Flagler by William Cramp & Sons, Philadelphia, and renamed SS Columbia Heights in 1940, she was acquired by the U.S. Navy on 28 July 1941 for conversion to a coastal minelayer, CMc-6. Reclassified as a net layer, AN-5, and named USS Keokuk on 15 August 1941, she was commissioned on 28 February 1942. Reclassified as a minelayer, CM-8, on 18 May 1942, and again as a net cargo ship, AKN-4, in November 1943.

USS Keokuk (CMc-6) was a coastal minelayer of the United States Navy built during World War II. Launched on 20 December 1944 and commissioned on 7 February 1945, she served primarily in the Atlantic and was involved in mine-laying operations. Keokuk was designed to assist in clearing enemy sea lanes and protect naval vessels by deploying mines. After the war, she was decommissioned on 9 November 1945 and subsequently struck from the Naval Register. The ship was sold for scrapping in 1947. Keokuk's service highlights the importance of mine warfare in naval operations during the wartime period.

Polish Party of Animal Protection (Polish: Polska Partia Ochrony Zwierząt, PPOZ) is an animal rights party in Poland founded in 2011 and registered in 2018 by animal rights activists. The party brings together animal protection groups from all over Poland, initially coming from an animal foundation Pod Psią Gwiazdą in Kłodzko. The party cites the Dutch Party for the Animals as one of its inspirations and wishes to improve the animal protection in Poland by political activism. Apart from Polanica-Zdrój, the Polish Party of Animal Protection also has party structures in Gdynia, Wrocław, Katowice and Poznań. The party is made up of animal lovers and activists associated with animal protection and no-kill pet shelters. Main objectives of the party include improving the living conditions of pets and farm animals, as well as phasing out meat production in favour of vegetarianism. The party fields activists of animal advocacy organisations, arguing that the politicians of the Sejm had failed to bring about any change in terms of animal rights. The PPOZ wants animal owners to be more accountable and introduce harsher penalties for animal torture and abuse. The PPOZ mainly addresses and campaigns against chained dog abuse, mass cattle and poultry farming, fur farming, animal slaughter, animal use in circuses and laboratories, climate change and hunting.

The Polish Party of Animal Protection (Polska Partia Ochrony Zwierząt, PPOZ) is a political party in Poland dedicated to advocating for animal rights and welfare. Founded in 2016, the party aims to address issues related to animal protection legislation, promote ethical treatment of animals, and raise public awareness about animal welfare concerns. The party seeks to influence national policies and legislation to enhance the status and protection of animals within Polish society. It participates in elections and engages in various campaigns to draw attention to animal rights issues, striving to represent the interests of animals in political discourse.

Yunzhong Commandery was a historical commandery of China. Its territories were located between the Great Wall and Yin Mountains, and correspond to part of modern-day Hohhot, Baotou and Ulanqab prefectures in Inner Mongolia. The commandery was created during King Wuling of Zhao's reign after a successful campaign against the Linhu (林胡) and Loufan (樓煩) peoples. After the establishment of Han dynasty, the commandery became the frontier between Han and the Xiongnu. In early Han dynasty, the region saw frequent Xiongnu raids. However, from Emperor Wu's reign onwards, it became an important base of military operations in the wars against the Xiongnu. In 127 BC, it was from Yunzhong that General Wei Qing led a 40,000-men strong cavalry force and conquered the modern Hetao and Ordos regions. In 2 AD, the commandery administered 11 counties, namely Yunzhong (雲中), Xianyang (咸陽), Taolin (陶林), Zhenling (楨陵), Duhe (犢和), Shaling (沙陵), Yuanyang (原陽), Shanan (沙南), Beiyu (北輿), Wuquan (武泉) and Yangshou (陽壽). The population totaled 38,303 households, or 173,270 people. During Eastern Han, 3 counties were abolished, while 3 new counties were added from Dingxiang Commandery. In 140 AD, the population was 5,351 households, or 26,430 households. Toward the end Han dynasty, the area's population decreased sharply as residents fled from invading northern nomadic peoples, and the commandery was dissolved.

Yunzhong Commandery was an administrative region of ancient China during the Han dynasty, established around the 2nd century BCE. It was located in what is now part of modern-day Inner Mongolia and northern Shanxi Province. The commandery played a significant role in the Han Empire's military and economic expansion into the northern territories, serving as a strategic point for both governance and trade along the Silk Road. Yunzhong was known for its diverse population, which included various nomadic tribes and settled agrarian communities. Over time, the commandery underwent administrative changes and eventually declined in importance, particularly during the Three Kingdoms period when territorial realignments altered the region's control and significance. The legacy of Yunzhong continues to be of interest to historians studying the dynamics of imperial administration and cultural exchange in ancient China.

Spring Park is a small area in London, England. It is within the London Borough of Bromley and the London Borough of Croydon, straddling the traditional Kent-Surrey border along The Beck. Spring Park is located north of Addington, west of West Wickham and south of Monks Orchard and Shirley.

Spring Park is a suburban area located in the London Borough of Croydon, England. It is notable for its residential character and is bordered by the areas of Shirley, Addiscombe, and the Croydon town centre. The area primarily consists of family homes, parks, and green spaces, with Spring Park itself serving as a local recreational area that features pathways and playgrounds. The neighborhood is well-served by public transport, with nearby access to train and bus services facilitating connections to central London and surrounding areas. Spring Park is also in proximity to several schools, shops, and community amenities, making it a desirable location for families and commuters alike.

The monocyte monolayer assay (MMA) is used to determine the clinical significance of alloantibodies produced by blood transfusion recipients. The assay is used to assess the potential for intravascular hemolysis when incompatible cellular blood products are transfused to the anemic patient. When donor cells possess substances that are not produced by the recipient, the recipient's immune system produces antibodies against the substance; these are called alloantibodies. Specific white blood cells, called monocytes, are tasked with ingesting foreign material and become activated during certain inflammatory events. These activated monocytes come in contact with antibody-sensitized red blood cells (RBC) and may or may not exhibit phagocytosis (ingestion) and destroy the donor red blood cells. If monocytes destroy the RBC, the antibody attached to those RBC is considered clinically significant.

The Monocyte Monolayer Assay (MMA) is a laboratory technique used to assess the functional state of monocytes, a type of white blood cell involved in the immune response. In this assay, monocytes are isolated from blood and cultured to form a monolayer on a culture dish. Their responses to various stimuli, such as pathogens or cytokines, can be evaluated by measuring parameters such as adhesion, migration, and cytokine production. The MMA is valuable in immunological research, drug development, and clinical diagnostics, providing insights into monocyte behavior in health and disease. It is particularly relevant in studying inflammation, infection, and autoimmunity.

The band model is a conformal model of the hyperbolic plane. The band model employs a portion of the Euclidean plane between two parallel lines. Distance is preserved along one line through the middle of the band. Assuming the band is given by { z ∈ C : | Im ⁡ z | < π / 2 } {\displaystyle \{z\in \mathbb {C} :\left|\operatorname {Im} z\right|<\pi /2\}} , the metric is given by | d z | sec ⁡ ( Im ⁡ z ) {\displaystyle |dz|\sec(\operatorname {Im} z)} . Geodesics include the line along the middle of the band, and any open line segment perpendicular to boundaries of the band connecting the sides of the band. Every end of a geodesic either meets a boundary of the band at a right angle or is asymptotic to the midline; the midline itself is the only geodesic that does not meet a boundary. Lines parallel to the boundaries of the band within the band are hypercycles whose common axis is the line through the middle of the band.

The band model is a theoretical framework used in solid-state physics to describe the electronic properties of materials, particularly semiconductors and insulators. It characterizes the energy levels of electrons in a solid as bands, which are ranges of energy that electrons can occupy. The band model distinguishes between the valence band, which contains the energy levels of electrons participating in chemical bonding, and the conduction band, which consists of higher energy levels where electrons can move freely and conduct electricity. The band gap is the energy difference between these two bands; its size determines a material's electrical conductivity, categorizing it as a conductor, semiconductor, or insulator. The band model is fundamental for understanding phenomena such as electrical conduction, optical absorption, and the behavior of materials in response to external fields.

Mikio Ikemoto (Japanese: 池本 幹雄, Hepburn: Ikemoto Mikio; born January 13, 1977) is a Japanese manga artist. He originally worked as the chief assistant for Masashi Kishimoto's manga series Naruto from 1999 to 2014. After its conclusion, Ikemoto was chosen to illustrate the 2016 sequel series, Boruto: Naruto Next Generations, working alongside writer Ukyō Kodachi.

Mikio Ikemoto (池本幹雄) is a renowned Japanese artist and sculptor, born in Osaka in 1960. He is known for his work in contemporary sculpture, often incorporating elements of traditional Japanese aesthetics and modern design. Ikemoto's sculptures frequently explore themes of nature, identity, and the human experience, utilizing materials such as wood, metal, and stone. His artistry has been exhibited widely in Japan and internationally, earning him critical acclaim and various awards. In addition to his sculptural works, Ikemoto is also involved in art education, mentoring emerging artists and contributing to the cultural discourse in Japan.
Events in the year 2024 in Kosovo.

The year 2024 in Kosovo is marked by ongoing political developments, international relations, and social issues. As a partially recognized country, Kosovo continues to navigate its relationship with Serbia, which remains tense amid ongoing dialogue facilitated by the European Union. The domestic political landscape is characterized by challenges related to governance, economic growth, and public services. Additionally, Kosovo must address pressing societal issues such as ethnic integration and the return of displaced persons. The year may also witness various cultural events, sporting activities, and efforts to strengthen ties with Western countries amid aspirations for further integration into European structures.
Frances Jones Bonner (1919 - December 27, 2000) was an American psychoanalyst.

Frances Jones Bonner (1906–1992) was an influential American educator and civil rights advocate known for her significant contributions to the promotion of African American education and community empowerment. Born in Alabama, she pursued a career in teaching and served as a principal and administrator within the public school system. Bonner was a prominent figure in the civil rights movement, actively participating in initiatives aimed at improving educational access and quality for African Americans. Her work included advocating for equitable funding for schools and mentoring young black educators. Bonner's dedication to social justice and educational reform left a lasting impact on her community, making her a notable figure in the history of African American education.

Thomas Krüger (born 20 June 1959) is a German politician. He is the President of the Federal Agency for Civil Education (since 2000) and the President of the German Children's Fund (since 1995). He served as the final Governing Mayor of East Berlin in 1991.

Thomas Krüger is a German politician and member of the Social Democratic Party (SPD). Born on June 27, 1949, he served as a member of the German Bundestag from 1990 to 1994, representing the district of Cottbus. Krüger is notably recognized for his tenure as the President of the Federal Agency for Civic Education (Bundeszentrale für politische Bildung) from 2000 to 2012, where he focused on promoting political education and civic engagement. He has also held various public offices and participated in numerous initiatives aimed at enhancing democratic understanding and participation within Germany.

Martinice u Onšova is a municipality and village in Pelhřimov District in the Vysočina Region of the Czech Republic. It has about 50 inhabitants. Martinice u Onšova lies approximately 19 kilometres (12 mi) north-west of Pelhřimov, 40 km (25 mi) north-west of Jihlava, and 76 km (47 mi) south-east of Prague.

Martinice u Onšova is a small village located in the Vysočina Region of the Czech Republic, within the administrative district of Pelhřimov. The village is situated near the town of Onšov, from which it derives its name. The area is characterized by its rural landscape and traditional Czech architecture. As of the latest census, Martinice u Onšova has a population of approximately 100 residents. The village is known for its peaceful environment and local agricultural activities, contributing to the cultural fabric of the region.

An insertion reaction is a chemical reaction where one chemical entity (a molecule or molecular fragment) interposes itself into an existing bond of typically a second chemical entity e.g.: A + B − C ⟶ B − A − C {\displaystyle {\color {red}{\ce {A}}}+{\color {blue}{\ce {B-C}}}\longrightarrow {\color {blue}{\ce {B}}-}{\color {red}{\ce {A}}}{\color {blue}{\ce {-C}}}} The term only refers to the result of the reaction and does not suggest a mechanism. Insertion reactions are observed in organic, inorganic, and organometallic chemistry. In cases where a metal-ligand bond in a coordination complex is involved, these reactions are typically organometallic in nature and involve a bond between a transition metal and a carbon or hydrogen. It is usually reserved for the case where the coordination number and oxidation state of the metal remain unchanged. When these reactions are reversible, the removal of the small molecule from the metal-ligand bond is called extrusion or elimination. There are two common insertion geometries— 1,1 and 1,2 (pictured above). Additionally, the inserting molecule can act either as a nucleophile or as an electrophile to the metal complex. These behaviors will be discussed in more detail for CO, nucleophilic behavior, and SO2, electrophilic behavior.

Insertion reactions are chemical reactions in which a compound is formed by the insertion of one reactant into a pre-existing bond of another molecule. Commonly encountered in organic and inorganic chemistry, these reactions often involve the addition of a small molecule or atom into a complex structure, leading to the formation of new bonds and the modification of molecular frameworks. A notable example includes the insertion of alkenes into metal-carbon bonds in transition metal catalysis. Insertion reactions play a crucial role in various synthetic pathways, including polymerization and catalysis, facilitating the creation of diverse chemical compounds and materials.

The Trials for Felony Act 1836 (6 & 7 Will. 4. c. 114) was an Act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom. This act was extended to the Turks and Caicos Islands by the act of the Legislative Council 4 Vic. c. 30 (TC). This act was repealed in part by the Summary Jurisdiction Act 1848. The words "and be it further enacted, that" wherever they occurred were repealed by section 1 of, and the Schedule to, the Statute Law Revision (No. 2) Act 1888 (51 & 52 Vict. c. 57). In a report dated 27 September 1985, the Law Commission and the Scottish Law Commission said that section 4 was the only provision that had not been repealed. They said that it was redundant. They recommended that the act be repealed. This Act was repealed by section 1(1) of, and Group 1 of Part I of Schedule 1 to, the Statute Law (Repeals) Act 1986. This act was repealed for Northern Ireland by section 15(2) of, and schedule 2 to, the Criminal Law Act (Northern Ireland) 1967. This act was repealed for the Republic of Ireland by section 16 of, and the Third Schedule to, the Criminal Law Act 1997.

The Trials for Felony Act 1836 was an Act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom aimed at reforming the trial procedures for felony offenses. It addressed concerns regarding the legal rights of defendants and the jury's role in the trial process. The Act made significant provisions for the representation of defendants in felony trials, including the allowance for legal counsel and the establishment of clearer evidentiary standards. It sought to streamline court procedures and reduce instances of wrongful conviction. The legislation marked an important step in the evolution of criminal justice in England, emphasizing fairness and the protection of legal rights in felony trials.
Amjad Bobby (Punjabi, Urdu: امجد بوبی; 1942 – 15 April 2005) was an acclaimed Pakistani music composer and director.

Amjad Bobby was a renowned Pakistani music composer and producer, known for his significant contributions to the Pakistani film and television industry. He gained recognition for his innovative blending of traditional and contemporary musical styles, resulting in popular soundtracks for numerous films and television dramas. His work is characterized by its emotional depth and cultural resonance, making him a pivotal figure in the evolution of modern Pakistani music. Throughout his career, Bobby collaborated with various artists and earned accolades for his creativity and influence in the entertainment sector. He remains a celebrated name in the history of South Asian music.

Free range denotes a method of farming husbandry where the animals can roam freely outdoors for at least part of the day, rather than being confined in an enclosure for 24 hours each day. On many farms, the outdoors ranging area is fenced, thereby technically making this an enclosure, however, free range systems usually offer the opportunity for the extensive locomotion and sunlight that is otherwise prevented by indoor housing systems. Free range may apply to meat, eggs or dairy farming. The term is used in two senses that do not overlap completely: as a farmer-centric description of husbandry methods, and as a consumer-centric description of them. There is a diet where the practitioner only eats meat from free-range sources called ethical omnivorism. In ranching, free-range livestock are permitted to roam without being fenced in, as opposed to intensive animal farming practices such as the concentrated animal feeding operation. In many agriculture-based economies, free-range livestock are quite common.

Free range refers to a farming system in which animals are allowed to roam freely outdoors for at least part of the day, as opposed to being confined in cages or enclosures. This method is commonly associated with poultry, especially chickens, which are given access to pasture or fenced areas where they can engage in natural behaviors such as foraging, scratching, and socializing. The free-range approach is often promoted for producing meat and eggs with higher welfare standards, better nutritional profiles, and enhanced flavor. However, the definition of "free range" can vary significantly by country, with differing regulations regarding the minimum required outdoor space and conditions for the animals. Critics of free-range farming raise concerns about land use, environmental impact, and the effectiveness of welfare claims.

Alyona Andreyevna Khomich (Russian: Алёна Андреевна Хомич; born 26 February 1981), also known as Alena Khomich (from the mis-romanization of her given name), is a Russian retired ice hockey defenseman and former member of the Russian national ice hockey team. A four-time Olympian, she represented Russia at the Winter Olympic Games in 2002, 2006, 2010, and 2014.

Alyona Khomich is a Russian professional esports player known for her significant contributions to the competitive gaming scene, particularly in the multiplayer online battle arena (MOBA) genre. Gaining recognition through her skillful gameplay, Khomich has participated in various high-profile tournaments and competitions. She has been influential in promoting women in gaming and showcasing female talent within esports. In addition to her playing career, she is involved in community initiatives aimed at supporting aspiring gamers and fostering inclusivity in the esports industry.
Sir Harry Trelawney Eve (13 October 1856 – 10 December 1940) was an English barrister, judge and Liberal Party politician.

Harry Eve was an influential English composer, conductor, and educator, known for his contributions to contemporary classical music and his dedication to music education. Born in the early 20th century, Eve studied at renowned musical institutions and later became a prominent figure in various orchestras and music academies. His works often incorporated elements from diverse musical traditions, reflecting a broad range of influences. In addition to his compositional activities, Eve played a significant role in mentoring young musicians and advocating for innovative teaching methodologies. He has been recognized for his efforts in promoting accessibility to music and fostering a vibrant artistic community.

Jacob Thomas Schell (27 December 1850 – 29 July 1916) was a merchant and political figure. He represented Glengarry in the House of Commons of Canada from 1900 to 1908 as a Liberal member.
