He was born in East Oxford Township, Canada West in 1850, the son of Jacob Schell, and educated in Woodstock. He is greatgrandson of Johann Christian Schell, a Palatine German from German Flatts, New York, who died fighting for the Americans in 1782 on his farm. The family moved to Canada after the American Revolution to take advantage of the land available in Ontario. In 1882, he moved to Alexandria and became a partner with David Murdoch Macpherson in a cheese box factory there. The company also manufactured other wooden items. Schell was also involved in railway construction. He ran unsuccessfully for the same seat in the House of Commons in 1891. Schell served on the town council of Alexandria. After his death in 1916, he was buried in Woodstock. His brother Malcolm Smith Schell also served in the Canadian House of Commons, and at the same time.

Jacob Thomas Schell (b. 1995) is an American entrepreneur and technology executive known for his contributions to the development of innovative software solutions in the fintech industry. He co-founded several startups focused on financial technology, including payment processing and digital banking services. Schell has been recognized for his leadership and visionary approach in leveraging blockchain technology to enhance transaction security and efficiency. He serves on multiple advisory boards and is actively involved in mentoring emerging entrepreneurs in the tech ecosystem.

Veydelevsky District (Russian: Ве́йделевский райо́н) is an administrative district (raion), one of the twenty-one in Belgorod Oblast, Russia. Municipally, it is incorporated as Veydelevsky Municipal District. It is located in the southeast of the oblast. The area of the district is 1,356 square kilometers (524 sq mi). Its administrative center is the urban locality (a settlement) of Veydelevka. Population: 21,332 (2021 census); 21,670 (2010 census); 24,555 (2002 Census); 24,108 (1989 Soviet census). The population of Veydelevka accounts for 34.4% of the district's total population.

Veydelevsky District is an administrative and municipal district located in Belgorod Oblast, Russia. It is situated in the southeastern part of the region and covers an area of approximately 1,120 square kilometers. The administrative center of the district is the urban-type settlement of Veydelevka. As of the latest census, the district has a population of around 18,000 residents, with a predominantly rural demographic. The local economy is primarily based on agriculture, with significant contributions from crop production and livestock farming. The district is characterized by its scenic landscapes and is part of the Belgorod region's historical and cultural heritage.

The mottled wood owl (Strix ocellata) is a species of large owl found in India and Nepal. They are found in gardens and thin deciduous forests adjacent to dry thorn forests or farmland. They are easily detected by their distinctive tremulous, eerie calls at dawn and dusk. The characteristic call is a duet of the male and female, while other notes include a low hoot and a screech. Their large size, lack of "ear" tufts and the concentric barring on the face make them easy to identify.

The mottled wood owl (Strix ocellata) is a species of owl native to the forests of Southeast Asia, particularly found in regions such as Malaysia, Indonesia, and the Philippines. Recognizable by its mottled brown and white plumage, this nocturnal bird is known for its distinctive round head and yellow eyes. The mottled wood owl primarily inhabits lowland forests where it preys on small mammals, birds, and insects. Its call is a series of deep hoots, which can be heard during the night, aiding in communication and territory establishment. Conservation efforts are in place to protect its habitat due to deforestation and human encroachment, which threaten its populations. Despite these challenges, the mottled wood owl is not currently classified as endangered.

Lesiba Precious Mashele (born 13 October 1990) is a South African long-distance runner. In 2018, he competed in the men's half marathon at the 2018 IAAF World Half Marathon Championships held in Valencia, Spain. He finished in 44th place. In 2020, he competed in the men's race at the 2020 World Athletics Half Marathon Championships held in Gdynia, Poland. He competed in the men's 5000 metres event at the 2020 Summer Olympics held in Tokyo, Japan.

Lesiba Precious Mashele is a South African entrepreneur and public figure known for his contributions to the fields of technology and digital innovation. He gained prominence through his work in promoting youth entrepreneurship and digital literacy across various communities in South Africa. Mashele has been involved in several initiatives aimed at empowering young people with skills necessary for success in the digital economy. His efforts have earned him recognition as a leader in fostering economic development and social change within the region. Additionally, he has participated in numerous forums and discussions on entrepreneurship, technology, and sustainable development.

The Coffee Club is an Australian multinational coffeehouse-style café chain. Originally created in 1989 as a place to get "an excellent coffee", the concept includes cafébar/restaurant stores with expanded menus and full table service. The Coffee Club has over 400 stores in 15 countries including Australia, New Zealand, Thailand, the UAE, China, Vietnam, Indonesia, Seychelles, Maldives, Cambodia and Singapore.

The Coffee Club is a renowned Australian café franchise founded in 1989 by Emmanuel and Nadia Roubicek in Brisbane, Queensland. Known for its relaxed atmosphere, The Coffee Club offers a diverse menu featuring breakfast items, light meals, and a variety of coffee beverages. The brand has expanded significantly, operating over 400 locations across Australia and internationally, including countries such as New Zealand, Thailand, and the UAE. The Coffee Club focuses on providing quality food and service, and it has become a popular meeting place for social gatherings and casual dining. In addition to its food and beverage offerings, the franchise is recognized for its community involvement and sponsorship initiatives.

Rufus Blodgett (October 9, 1834 – October 3, 1910) was a United States senator from New Jersey and Superintendent of the New York & Long Branch Railroad for 25 years. He served as the Mayor of Long Branch, New Jersey on five occasions. He was the only person in either house of Congress to vote against the Sherman Antitrust Act.

Rufus Blodgett (1794–1864) was an American politician and businessman notable for his contributions to the early development of Michigan. Born in New York, Blodgett settled in what would become Michigan Territory, where he engaged in various enterprises, including lumber and agriculture. He served in multiple political roles, including as a member of the Michigan State Senate and as a delegate to the Michigan constitutional convention. Blodgett was also involved in the establishment of important infrastructure in the region. His work significantly impacted the economic growth of Michigan during its formative years.

Blackhouse Records is an American independent record label founded by Scott Rozell (drummer for Moral Crux and Scatterbox), and Tony Shields. The label is based in Coeur d'Alene, Idaho and Spokane, Washington, and was originally a logo created in 2000 for the purpose of selling Scatterbox records and promoting local Inland Northwest punk rock concerts. Over the years, it has evolved into a moderate-sized independent record label. Throughout the 2000s, most of the bands on Blackhouse were punk and pop punk groups, while there are many heavy metal and rap acts signed to the label as well. Blackhouse started a short-lived imprint, "TF Media", which signed noise and grindcore bands, however they all now roll up under the Blackhouse imprint.

Blackhouse Records is an independent record label based in the United Kingdom, known for producing and promoting a diverse range of music genres, including electronic, hip hop, and alternative rock. Founded in 2015, the label aims to support emerging artists and foster creative partnerships. Blackhouse Records has gained recognition for its innovative marketing strategies and commitment to artistic freedom, distinguishing itself in the competitive music industry. The label has released several successful albums and singles, contributing to the careers of various underground and mainstream artists.

The Fundamental Statute for the Secular Government of the States of the Church was the constitution of the Papal States conceded by Pope Pius IX as a result of the 1848 revolutions. It was published on 14 March 1848. The statute provided for two legislative chambers. The first was to consist of members nominated for life by the Pope and the second, of one hundred elected deputies. The laws adopted by these two chambers had first to undergo the scrutiny of the College of Cardinals, before being submitted to the Pope for his assent or rejection. Ecclesiastical, or ecclesiastico-political, affairs were exempted from parliamentary interference. Also the parliament was required to abstain from the enactment of laws conflicting with criticism of the diplomatic and religious relations of the Holy See with foreign powers.
topic: Fundamental Statute for the Secular Government of the States of the Church

text: The Fundamental Statute for the Secular Government of the States of the Church, enacted in 1848, was a constitution that established a secular administration for the Papal States during a period of significant political upheaval in Italy. It introduced a constitutional framework that limited the powers of the Pope and provided for civil liberties and the separation of church and state. The statute was part of broader movements advocating for liberal reforms and national unification in Italy. Although short-lived, it represented a critical moment in the transition from papal governance to modern secular government and influenced subsequent constitutional developments in the region. The statute was eventually abrogated in 1850 when the papacy reasserted its control over the territories.
topic: Fundamental Statute for the Secular Government of the States of the Church

text: A Debian Pure Blend is a project completely inside of Debian targeting a server or a desktop installation in very broad and general terms. A Debian Pure Blend aims to cover interests of specialised users, who might be children, lawyers, medical staff, visually impaired people, certain academic fields, etc. The common goal of those is to make installation and administration of computers for their target users as easy as possible, and to serve in the role as the missing link between software developers and users well.

Debian Pure Blends are specialized distributions of the Debian operating system that cater to specific user groups or use cases. Each Blend aims to provide a comprehensive set of packages, documentation, and configuration tailored to the needs of its target audience, such as educators, scientists, or medical professionals. They are built on top of the core Debian system, ensuring adherence to Debian's principles of stability, freedom, and community-driven development. Each Pure Blend is developed collaboratively by volunteers who package, maintain, and promote software relevant to their specific area. Notable examples include Debian Med for healthcare, Debian Edu for education, and Debian Games for gaming.

The Mississippi Synodical College is a historic building in Holly Springs, Mississippi, USA. Formerly a religious college, it is home to the Marshall County Historical Society and Museum. It was listed on the National Register of Historic Places since 1983.

Mississippi Synodical College was a private Christian college located in Clinton, Mississippi, affiliated with the Presbyterian Church. Established in 1850, the institution aimed to provide a liberal arts education with a focus on the principles of the church. Throughout its history, Mississippi Synodical College offered undergraduate degrees and played a role in the education of women, being one of the early institutions to do so in the region. The college faced various challenges, including financial difficulties and changes in enrollment trends, which ultimately led to its closure in 1937. Following its shutdown, the campus and facilities were repurposed for other educational uses, reflecting the shifting landscape of higher education in Mississippi.

Eastern Orthodoxy in the Democratic People's Republic of Korea is a Christian denomination in North Korea, which has developed in the country since 2006. As of 2011, the number of Orthodox Christians in North Korea is estimated at 50-60 people, which is about 0.0002% of the country's population. There were no Eastern Orthodox priests in the country, so the Korean Orthodox Committee, established in North Korea in 2002, contacted the Russian Orthodox Church. The committee sent four students to the Moscow Ecclesiastical Seminary in April 2003. All four were freshly baptized Christians who had formerly worked for the North Korean intelligence service. One of them, Feodor Kim (Kim Hoe-il), said it was difficult for them to adopt the Orthodox faith. After the seminary, they were dispatched to Vladivostok to gain practical experience. Of the Orthodox churches, only the Russian Orthodox Church is represented, which, with the assistance of Kim Jong Il, built the Church of the Life-Giving Trinity in the capital Pyongyang, located in the Jongbaek-dong, Rangrang District. When the church was consecrated in 2006 by Metropolitan Kirill of Smolensk, the first North Korean priests were ordained - Feodor Kim and John Ra. On December 28, 2018, the Holy Synod of the Russian Orthodox Church established the Patriarchal Exarchate in Southeast Asia, which included North Korea, and on February 26, 2019, the Korean Diocese was established within the exarchate, which included North and South Korea. In March 2019, Metropolitan Sergius Chashin noted that For many years, there has been the Trinity Cathedral there, built by order of the now deceased leader of North Korea, Kim Jong Il. Students from the DPRK have studied at the Moscow Theological Academy and have been ordained to the priesthood. Another group of five people is currently studying at the Khabarovsk Theological Seminary. Patriarch Kirill ordained two of them in Moscow in August 2018... The authorities are very favourable to the Russian Orthodox Church. Our diplomats, as well as employees of the Bulgarian and Romanian embassies, come to the temple and pray. We have no difficulties with North Korea in terms of carrying out our activities.

Eastern Orthodoxy in North Korea is a small religious presence within a predominantly atheistic state that is influenced by Juche ideology, which emphasizes self-reliance and the state over external religious affiliations. The Orthodox Church has a limited history in North Korea, primarily tied to the presence of Russian expatriates in the early 20th century. The most notable institution is the Korean Orthodox Church, which operates under the jurisdiction of the Moscow Patriarchate. Despite the harsh religious restrictions imposed by the North Korean government, Eastern Orthodox Christians, along with other religious groups, continue to practice their faith discreetly. The Church faces challenges including government surveillance and repression, yet maintains an identity that connects it with the larger global Orthodox community.

True North is a 2006 British drama film directed by Steve Hudson and starring Martin Compston, Peter Mullan, Gary Lewis and Steven Robertson as sailors aboard a Scottish fishing boat smuggling illegal immigrants from China into the United Kingdom. The film premiered at the Toronto International Film Festival in Canada on 11 September 2006 and was also shown at the Max Ophüls Festival in Germany, the Karlovy Vary Film Festival in the Czech Republic, the Copenhagen International Film Festival in Denmark and the Ourense International Film Festival in Spain. There is a 2014 Korean film, Haemoo, with the same story which is attributed to a 2007 stage play by the same name. An animated film of the same name concerning North Korean prisoners was released on 1 October 2020.

True North is a 2006 Canadian drama film directed by David Barlow, focusing on the impact of a fictional incident involving the Canadian Coast Guard and a group of asylum seekers. The narrative follows a young fisherman who becomes embroiled in the rescue of a group of refugees attempting to reach Canada via boat. The film explores themes of compassion, morality, and the complexities surrounding immigration and human rights. It received critical acclaim for its poignant storytelling and was praised for its performances and cinematography. True North highlights the struggles faced by those seeking refuge and the ethical dilemmas faced by individuals caught between duty and humanity.

Cerconota atricassis is a moth of the family Depressariidae. It is found in French Guiana. The wingspan is 19–20 mm. The forewings are rather light fuscous with a short blackish mark on the base of the dorsum. The stigmata are faintly darker, the plical obliquely beyond the first discal and there are three faintly darker hardly perceptible rather oblique curved slender lines, the third from three-fourths of the costa to the tornus. The hindwings are rather dark grey, lighter anteriorly.

Cerconota atricassis is a species of moth in the family Depressariidae, primarily found in Bolivia. This species was described by the entomologist Edward Meyrick in 1925. The moth has a wingspan of approximately 22 to 23 millimeters and features a distinct coloration with a dark brown forewing adorned with varying shades and patterns. The hindwings are typically a lighter brown. Like other members of its family, Cerconota atricassis is presumed to have a nocturnal adult phase, though specific behaviors and ecology remain largely undocumented. This species contributes to the biodiversity of the region and reflects the unique lepidopteran fauna of Bolivia.

Molva is a genus of lotid fishes, the lings, with these currently recognized species: Molva dypterygia (Pennant, 1784) (blue ling) Molva macrophthalma (Rafinesque, 1810) (Spanish ling) Molva molva (Linnaeus, 1758) (common ling)

Molva is a village located in the Mersin Province of Turkey, situated in the Mediterranean region. It is part of the Tarsus district and features typical characteristics of Turkish rural settlements, including traditional architecture and agricultural practices. The population primarily engages in farming and small-scale commerce, reflecting the economic activities common in the area. Molva is also known for its scenic landscapes, with proximity to natural attractions that reflect the region's Mediterranean climate. The village contributes to the cultural and social fabric of the province, showcasing local traditions and community life.
Catascopellus is a genus in the ground beetle family Carabidae. This genus has a single species, Catascopellus crassiceps. It is found in Chile.

Catascopellus is a genus of flowering plants belonging to the family Asteraceae, commonly known as the daisy family. This genus is primarily found in tropical regions, including parts of Africa and the Indian subcontinent. Catascopellus species are characterized by their distinctive inflorescences and varied leaf forms, which often adapt to their environment. The plants typically thrive in diverse habitats, such as grasslands and woodlands. As a part of ongoing taxonomic studies, Catascopellus contributes to the understanding of plant biodiversity within the Asteraceae family, with some species still under research for their ecological and medicinal properties.

Paul Baines (born 9 May 1973) is a British marketing academic, specialising in the topic of marketing for political parties and candidates. He is the professor in Political Marketing at Cranfield University. He was the former Director of Business Development at the Middlesex University Business School and the former the Director for the Baines Associates Limited, since 2008. In 2019 he joined the University of Leicester as Professor of Political Marketing and Associate Dean. Paul’s research has particularly focused on the application of market research and strategic marketing by political and corporate organisations in a political context. This includes marketing for political parties, government and special interest groups, war PR and propaganda, and lobbying, with market positioning, segmentation and persuasion as the key underlying dimensions.

Paul Baines is a British academic known for his expertise in marketing and business strategy. He serves as a Professor of Marketing at the University of Bedfordshire and has held various academic roles throughout his career. Baines has published extensively in peer-reviewed journals and has contributed to the development of marketing theory and practice, particularly in areas such as digital marketing, consumer behavior, and brand management. He is also recognized for his work on the intersection of marketing and public policy. In addition to his scholarly contributions, Baines is involved in consultancy and has delivered training programs for businesses and organizations.

Germaine Koh (born 1967) is a Malaysian-born and Canadian conceptual artist based in Vancouver. Her works incorporate the artistic styles of neo-conceptual art, minimalism, and environmental art, and is concerned with the significance of everyday actions, familiar objects and common places. Koh is an independent curator and partner in the independent record label Weewerk. She also used to be an assistant curator of contemporary art at the National Gallery of Canada. Her exhibition history includes the Baltic Centre (Newcastle), De Appel (Amsterdam), Musée d'art contemporain de Montréal, Para/Site Art Space (Hong Kong), Frankfurter Kunstverein, Bloomberg Space (London), The Power Plant (Toronto), Seoul Museum of Art, Artspace (Sydney), The British Museum (London), the Contemporary Art Gallery (Vancouver), Plug In ICA (Winnipeg), Art Gallery of Ontario (Toronto), and the Liverpool, Sydney and Montréal biennials.

Germaine Koh is a Canadian artist known for her interdisciplinary work that often explores themes of technology, environment, and the everyday experience. Born in 1967 in Vancouver, British Columbia, Koh's practice encompasses installation, video, and public art, utilizing diverse materials and processes to engage audiences and provoke thought about her subjects. She gained recognition for her projects that reflect on human interactions with nature and the socio-political implications of technology. Koh has exhibited widely in Canada and internationally, and her work is included in various public and private collections. Additionally, she is involved in community initiatives and has served as an educator, contributing to discussions around contemporary art and practice.

Robert Lima Fonseca (18 June 1972 – 17 June 2021) was an Uruguayan football player and manager. He played as a defender. He played for Peñarol, Chacarita and Sporting Cristal, among others. With Peñarol he became five times Primera División champion between 1993 and 1997. At the last club he played for, Cerro Largo, he became assistant coach. Being an assistant coach for several clubs between 2008 and 2017 he became manager of Juticalpa in 2019. Lima died on 17 June 2021, aged 48.

Robert Lima, born on January 15, 1990, is a Brazilian professional footballer known for his position as a midfielder. He began his career in the youth ranks of several clubs in Brazil before making his professional debut. Over the years, Lima has played for multiple teams in various leagues, showcasing his skills in both domestic and international competitions. His playing style is characterized by his technical ability, vision on the field, and versatility, allowing him to adapt to different roles within the midfield. Throughout his career, Lima has been acknowledged for his contributions to his teams, and he continues to be an influential player in the football community.

"Stuck in the Middle with You" (sometimes known as "Stuck in the Middle") is a song written by Scottish musicians Gerry Rafferty and Joe Egan and performed by their band Stealers Wheel. The band performed the song on the BBC's Top of the Pops in May 1973, and the song charted at No. 8 on the UK Singles Chart. It also became an international hit, reaching No. 6 on the US Billboard Hot 100.
topic: Stuck in the Middle with You

text: "Stuck in the Middle with You" is a song by the Scottish folk rock group Stealers Wheel, released in 1972. Written by band members Gerry Rafferty and Joe Egan, the track features a catchy melody and distinctive harmonica riff. The song became a commercial success, reaching the top ten in several countries, including the United States, where it peaked at number six on the Billboard Hot 100 chart. Its blend of folk rock and pop elements, coupled with lyrics that convey feelings of confusion and disillusionment, has contributed to its enduring popularity. The song gained further recognition after being featured in iconic films and television shows, notably Quentin Tarantino's "Reservoir Dogs," where it is famously associated with a violent scene. "Stuck in the Middle with You" remains a classic and is often covered by various artists.

text: USS Alliance was a 36-gun frigate of the Continental Navy. Originally named Hancock, she was laid down in 1777 on the Merrimack River at Amesbury, Massachusetts, by the partners and cousins, William and James K. Hackett, launched on 28 April 1778, and renamed Alliance on 29 May 1778 by resolution of the Continental Congress. Her first commanding officer was Capt. Pierre Landais, a former officer of the French Navy who had come to the New World hoping to become a naval counterpart of Gilbert du Motier, Marquis de Lafayette. The frigate's first captain was widely accepted as such in America. Massachusetts made him an honorary citizen and the Continental Congress gave him command of Alliance, thought to be the finest warship built to that date on the western side of the Atlantic.

USS Alliance was a wooden-hulled frigate of the Continental Navy during the American Revolutionary War. Launched in 1778, she was one of the last ships constructed for the Continental Navy and was designed by the naval architect Joshua Humphreys. Alliance served primarily as a warship and a convoy escort in operations against British forces. Under the command of Captain John Barry, she became known for her effectiveness in battle, particularly during engagements against British merchant ships. The ship was instrumental in various naval confrontations, contributing to American efforts for maritime dominance. After the war, USS Alliance was ultimately sold in 1785, concluding her brief but significant service in the early history of the United States Navy.
Mohamed Rifai El-Sayed (Arabic: مُحَمَّد رِفَاعِيّ السَّيِّد; born 7 August 1939) is an Egyptian former footballer. He competed in the men's tournament at the 1960 Summer Olympics.

Mohamed Rifai (born July 12, 1985) is a prominent Egyptian actor, director, and playwright, known for his contributions to contemporary Egyptian theater and film. He gained recognition for his dynamic performances and innovative storytelling, often addressing social and political issues within Egyptian society. Rifai has collaborated with various influential figures in the arts and has been involved in multiple acclaimed productions. His work has earned him several awards and nominations, solidifying his status as a significant figure in the Arab performing arts scene.

Remigijus Pocius (born 21 March 1968) is a retired Lithuanian football forward, who last played for KFK Siauliai. He obtained a total number of seven caps for the Lithuania national football team, scoring one goal.

Remigijus Pocius (born March 21, 1983) is a Lithuanian professional basketball coach and former player, known for his contributions to the sport in various capacities. As a player, he primarily occupied the shooting guard and small forward positions and had a career in both Lithuanian and international leagues. Pocius played college basketball at Duke University, where he was part of the 2001 NCAA championship team. Following his playing career, he transitioned into coaching, taking on roles in youth development and professional teams. He is recognized for his basketball acumen and commitment to fostering new talent in Lithuania.

Venice, the Bridge of Sighs is an 1840 cityscape painting by the English artist J.M.W. Turner. It depicts a view of Venice, then part of the Austrian Empire, looking towards the famous Bridge of Sighs. The Doge's Palace and the City Prison are either side of the bridge. He displayed the painting at the Royal Academy's Summer Exhibition in 1840. It appeared with lines from Lord Byron's Childe Harold's Pilgrimage referencing the view. All seven works Turner exhibited that year met with widespread critical derision. Nonetheless he continued to be increasingly experimental in style during his later career. The work was part of the Turner Bequest of 1856 and is now in the collection of the Tate Britain.

The Bridge of Sighs (Italian: Ponte dei Sospiri) is a renowned enclosed bridge in Venice, Italy, constructed in 1600. It spans the Rio di Palazzo and connects the Palazzo Ducale (Doge's Palace) to the prison, serving as a passageway for prisoners. The bridge is named for the sighs of inmates who, upon crossing it, would catch their last glimpse of the beautiful city before imprisonment. Architect Antonio Contino designed it in the Venetian Baroque style, featuring a white limestone façade and intricate details. The Bridge of Sighs is a popular tourist attraction and a symbol of Venice, often romanticized in literature and art.
Events in the year 1978 in Turkey.

In 1978, Turkey experienced significant political, social, and economic turmoil. The year was marked by escalating violence between leftist and rightist groups, contributing to a climate of instability that foreshadowed the military coup of 1980. Key events included the 1 May labor protests and clashes in various cities, most notably in Kahramanmaraş, where a violent pogrom against the Alevi population resulted in numerous deaths. The political landscape was characterized by the fragmentation of parties, with major players such as the Republican People's Party (CHP) and the Nationalist Action Party (MHP) facing growing extremism among their supporters. Economically, Turkey confronted rising inflation and unemployment, leading to strikes and unrest among workers. The combination of political polarization and social strife in 1978 set the stage for the forthcoming military intervention.
Alexander Houstoun Jr. (4 October 1752 – 22 March 1822) was a Scottish politician who served as the interim governor of Grenada from 1796 to 30 September 1797. He was succeeded by Sir Charles Green.

Alexander Houstoun (c. 1740 – 1812) was an American politician and military leader known for his role as the second governor of the state of Georgia, serving from 1798 to 1801. Born in Scotland, he immigrated to the American colonies where he became involved in colonial military efforts during the American Revolutionary War. After the war, Houstoun was elected to various governmental positions, including a member of the Georgia House of Representatives and the Continental Congress. His governorship was marked by efforts to stabilize the state's economy and promote agricultural development. Houstoun's tenure saw the establishment of several policies aimed at enhancing infrastructure and public welfare. After leaving office, he continued to be active in local politics and public service until his death in 1812.

Caucher Birkar (Kurdish: کۆچەر بیرکار, romanized: Koçer Bîrkar, lit. 'migrant mathematician'; born Fereydoun Derakhshani (Kurdish: فەرەیدوون درەخشانی، Persian: فریدون درخشانی); July 1978) is a UK-based Iranian Kurdish and British mathematician (born in Iran) and a professor at Tsinghua University. He is also an Honorary Professor at the University of Nottingham. Birkar is an important contributor to modern birational geometry. In 2010 he received the Leverhulme Prize in mathematics and statistics for his contributions to algebraic geometry, and in 2016, shared the AMS Moore Prize for the article "Existence of minimal models for varieties of log general type". He was awarded the Fields Medal in 2018, "for his proof of boundedness of Fano varieties and contributions to the minimal model program". In his office at the university, Birkar has two photographs of Alexander Grothendieck, his favorite mathematician, who like Birkar, was a refugee and Fields medalist. Birkar maintains strong ties to his Kurdish heritage and actively encourages Kurdish identity while also separating it from nationalism and politics. According to Birkar, his strong Kurdish identity is not a part of nationalism nor politics and he is not striving for such achievements. This can be a reflection of his name change to Caucher Birkar which roughly translates into ”the Migrating Mathematician”.

Caucher Birkar is an Iranian-born British mathematician known for his contributions to algebraic geometry and number theory. He is a professor at the University of Cambridge and has made significant advances in the areas of Fano varieties and birational geometry. In 2018, he was awarded the prestigious Fields Medal for his work on the Minimal Model Program, which deals with classifying higher-dimensional algebraic varieties. Birkar, who emigrated from Iran to the UK in the early 2000s, has become a prominent figure in the mathematical community, recognized for both his research and his role in mentoring young mathematicians.
The Man from Red Gulch is a 1925 American silent Western film directed by Edmund Mortimer and featuring Harry Carey.

"The Man from Red Gulch" is a 1944 American Western film directed by William Berke. The film stars Charles Starrett as the protagonist, a cowboy who confronts outlaws and seeks justice in the fictional town of Red Gulch. Notably, it features a blend of traditional Western elements, including gunfights, horse chases, and themes of honor and retribution. The film also marks one of the early appearances of the character Durango Kid, a persona often portrayed by Starrett in various Westerns throughout the 1940s and 1950s. "The Man from Red Gulch" was part of the era's growing fascination with the Western genre, contributing to its popularity in American cinema during that time.
Mewali (Village ID 124909) is an Indian village in Jagner block in Agra district. According to the 2011 census it has a population of 2333 living in 390 households.

Meoli refers to a family of fish primarily recognized for their unique characteristics and adaptations, often studied within the fields of marine biology and ecology. The term may encompass various species and genera that display specific traits, such as size, habitat preference, and behavior that distinguish them from other fish families. Additionally, the study of Meoli may contribute to understanding biodiversity, ecosystem dynamics, and conservation efforts in aquatic environments. Notably, discussions surrounding Meoli might involve their ecological roles, evolutionary adaptations, and responses to environmental changes.

Wyken Pippin is an old cultivar of domesticated apple originating in the Netherlands, or have originated in the garden of the Wyken Manor house in England from a seedling that was sourcing back to the Netherlands or Belgium, possibly in the early 1700s. It has several other names including 'Alford Prize' and 'Pheasant's Eye'. It is a small greenish-yellow fruit with lenticels, flattened shape and intense and delicious flavor. Flesh texture is dense. Uses are mainly for fresh eating. It has a lower content of vitamin C relative to other apple cultivars. It was one of the top cultivars to be used for home gardening in the 19th century. It is an ancestor of Laxton's Superb.

The Wyken Pippin is a variety of apple known for its distinctive flavor and historical significance, originating from the United Kingdom. It is characterized by its large, typically green-yellow fruit with a pronounced sweet and fruity taste, often described as having a hint of spice. The apple is considered a dessert type and is especially noted for its excellent storage qualities, making it suitable for late-season consumption. The Wyken Pippin was first recorded in the 19th century, and while it has fallen out of favor in commercial orchards, it remains valued among heritage fruit enthusiasts and is grown in a number of traditional orchards across Britain.

Ei Niš (full legal name: Holding-Korporacija Elektronska industrija a.d. Niš) or Electronics Industry Niš, is a holding company with headquarters in Niš, Serbia. The company has operated from 1948 until it declared bankruptcy in 2016.

Ei Niš is a Serbian private company based in Niš, specializing in the production and distribution of electrical equipment and components. Established in the early 2000s, the company has expanded its operations to include various sectors, such as telecommunications, construction, and manufacturing industries. Ei Niš is known for its innovative solutions and commitment to quality, having achieved several industry certifications. The company aims to enhance energy efficiency and sustainability through its products, contributing to the development of the electrical engineering sector in Serbia and the broader Balkan region.

Badri Teymourtash (Persian: بدری تیمورتاش; 1908–1995) (1287-1374 S.H) was the first female dentist in Iran, and is called "Mother of Dentistry" in Iran. she was born in Kalpoosh village in Semnan region in Iran

Badri Teymourtash was an Iranian politician and military officer known for his role in the political landscape of Iran during the early 20th century. Born in 1884, Teymourtash served as a close associate of Reza Shah Pahlavi and played a significant role in the modernization and centralization of the Iranian state. He held various positions, including Minister of War, and was instrumental in the establishment of the Imperial State of Iran. Teymourtash's political career was marked by his efforts to promote secularism and suppress political dissent. He was arrested and executed in 1940 during a period of political purges, making him a controversial figure in Iranian history. His legacy is often intertwined with the narrative of the Pahlavi dynasty and the modernization initiatives of early 20th century Iran.

Waterville is an unincorporated community located in Bradley County, Tennessee. It is located about 3 miles southeast of the business district of Cleveland along State Route 60 (Dalton Pike), and is part of the Cleveland metropolitan area. It is located just west of Wildwood Lake. Waterville Golf Course is a golf course located within the community. Waterville Elementary School, part of Bradley County Schools, serves students in the area.

Waterville is an unincorporated community located in the southeastern region of Tennessee, within the boundaries of the state’s McMinn County. Positioned near the intersection of U.S. Route 411 and State Route 30, Waterville is characterized by its rural setting and small population. The community is part of the Athens, Tennessee micropolitan area and features a blend of residential homes and local businesses. Its proximity to natural attractions and nearby towns contributes to its charm as a quiet, close-knit community. Historically, Waterville grew as part of the expansion of the region and continues to retain elements of its heritage through its local traditions and community events.
The Port of Melilla is a cargo, fishing, and passenger port and marina located in Melilla, a Spanish autonomous city off the coast of North Africa.

The Port of Melilla is a crucial maritime facility located in the autonomous city of Melilla, a Spanish exclave on the northern coast of Africa. The port serves as a significant logistical hub for ferry services, connecting Melilla to the Spanish mainland, primarily to Málaga and Almería. Additionally, it plays an important role in trade and commerce, facilitating the import and export of goods between Spain and Morocco. The port has modern infrastructure, including passenger terminals and cargo handling facilities, catering to both local and international maritime traffic. Its strategic location enhances its importance in regional shipping routes and contributes to the economy of Melilla.
Bad Dinosaurs is a children's animated television series about a family of dinosaurs, which was released on Netflix on March 28, 2024.

"Bad Dinosaurs" is a term that may refer to fictional depictions or portrayals of dinosaurs in literature, film, and popular culture that emphasize aggressive or malevolent behaviors. Often anthropomorphized, these "bad" dinosaurs are characterized by their roles as antagonists in stories aimed at children and families, where they may represent themes of fear or danger. Popular examples include the Tyrannosaurus rex in various animated films and television shows where it is depicted as a villain. The concept of "bad dinosaurs" contrasts with more benign or heroic portrayals, reflecting a cultural fascination with prehistoric creatures as symbols of power and ferocity. Additionally, certain scientific discussions on dinosaur behavior may delve into the predatory instincts of certain species, contributing to the narrative of "bad" dinosaurs in the context of both entertainment and actual paleontological studies.
Joris Teepe is a Dutch jazz bassist, composer, arranger, and big-band leader. He plays contemporary jazz, bebop, and free jazz.

Joris Teepe is a Dutch jazz double bassist and composer, known for his contributions to the international jazz scene and collaborations with various renowned musicians. Born on May 27, 1969, in the Netherlands, Teepe developed a deep interest in music at a young age and pursued formal studies at the conservatories of Amsterdam and Groningen. His career spans performances across Europe, the United States, and Asia, where he blends traditional jazz elements with contemporary styles. Teepe has also been involved in education, sharing his expertise through teaching and masterclasses. His discography includes several albums as a bandleader and contributions to numerous ensemble projects.

Vincent Sapienza is an American civil servant who is serving as the Chief Operating Officer of the New York City Department of Environmental Protection. From 2017 to 2022, he was the agency's Commissioner.

Vincent Sapienza is an American politician and member of the Democratic Party, known for his work in local government and public service. He has served in various capacities, including as the Commissioner of the New York City Department of Environmental Protection. In this role, he was responsible for overseeing the city’s water supply, wastewater management, and environmental protection initiatives. Sapienza is recognized for his efforts to enhance infrastructure and promote sustainability within the city. Throughout his career, he has been involved in numerous initiatives aimed at improving the quality of life for New Yorkers and addressing environmental challenges.

Daniel Glimmenvall (born September 10, 1974) is a Swedish professional ice hockey player. He was formerly known as Daniel Johansson until 2009. Currently, he is the captain of Rögle BK in the Swedish Allsvenskan.

Daniel Glimmenvall is a Swedish musician, composer, and producer known for his work in various genres, including rock, pop, and electronic music. He gained recognition for his innovative approach to music production and his ability to blend traditional and modern musical elements. Glimmenvall has collaborated with numerous artists and bands, contributing to both songwriting and performances. He has also released solo work, showcasing his versatility and distinct musical style. In addition to his music career, Glimmenvall is involved in community arts initiatives, promoting music education and engagement in Sweden.
Jonathan Chace (July 22, 1829 – June 30, 1917) was a United States representative and Senator from Rhode Island.

Jonathan Chace (1835–1915) was an American politician and businessman from Rhode Island. He served as a Republican member of the United States Senate from 1887 to 1889. Prior to his Senate term, Chace was involved in various enterprises, notably in the textile industry, where he held several managerial positions. His political career included serving as a member of the Rhode Island General Assembly. After leaving the Senate, Chace continued to engage in business and civic affairs until his death in 1915.
Rovon (French pronunciation: [ʁɔvɔ̃]) is a commune in the Isère department in southeastern France.
