Peace by Piece is the ninth album by San Francisco psychedelic rock band Quicksilver Messenger Service and the first to be released with guitarist Gary Duncan at the sole command. A later released CD-version includes two bonus tracks, entitled "Crazy Jesse" and "Electric Love".

"Peace by Piece" is an album by American singer-songwriter Rachael Lampa, released in 2002. The album features a blend of pop and contemporary Christian music, showcasing Lampa's vocal range and lyrical themes centered on hope, healing, and personal growth. It includes hit singles like "Method to the Madness" and "Don't Cut Me Down," which highlight her signature style of blending uplifting messages with melodic tunes. The album received positive reviews for its production quality and Lampa's mature sound, contributing to her recognition in the Christian music genre.
Prays lambda is a moth of the family Plutellidae. It is found in Japan. The wingspan is 11–14 mm.

Prays lambda is a species of moth belonging to the family Plutellidae. It is commonly known as the cabbage moth or diamondback moth due to the distinctive diamond-shaped markings on its wings. This species is primarily found in Europe, but it has also been introduced to other regions, including North America and parts of Asia. Prays lambda larvae are known to be pests of cruciferous plants, particularly cabbage and other members of the Brassicaceae family, where they can cause significant agricultural damage. The adult moths are small, with a wingspan typically ranging from 14 to 18 mm, and they exhibit a grayish-white coloration with dark wing patterns. Effective management of Prays lambda often involves integrated pest management strategies, including biological control methods.

The ear pull is a traditional Inuit game or sport which tests the competitors' ability to endure pain, and also strength. In the ear pull, two competitors sit facing each other, their legs straddled and interlocked. A two-foot-long loop of string, similar to a thick, waxed dental floss, is looped behind their ears, connecting right ear to right ear, or left ear to left ear. The competitors then pull upon the opposing ear using their own ear until the cord comes free or the opponent quits from the pain. The game has been omitted from some Arctic sports competitions due to safety concerns and the squeamishness of spectators; the event can cause bleeding and competitors sometimes require stitches. The Inuit ear pull game is a harsh test of physical endurance....[in which] a thin loop of leather is positioned behind the ears of each of two competitors who then pull away from each other until one gives up in pain. The ear pull is one example of Inuit games that "prepare children for the rigours of the arctic environment by stressing... physical strength and endurance", as well as helping one keep a mental record of one's endurance levels. Ear pull features at the World Eskimo-Indian Olympics.

An ear pull is a traditional technique often used in various cultures as a form of playful teasing, discipline, or affection. The act involves pulling or tugging on someone's ear and can be done in different contexts, such as among children or between family members. In some cultures, ear pulling is used to convey care or connection, while in others, it may signify correction or punishment. The practice is sometimes associated with children's games or rites of passage, emphasizing social bonding or familial relationships. While often harmless and playful, the perception of ear pulling varies greatly across different societies and individuals.

Ian David McShane (born 29 September 1942) is an English actor. His television performances include the title role in the BBC series Lovejoy (1986, 1991–1994), Al Swearengen in Deadwood (2004–2006) and its 2019 film continuation, and Mr. Wednesday in American Gods (2017–2021). For the original series of Deadwood, McShane won the Golden Globe Award for Best Actor – Television Series Drama and received a nomination for the Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Lead Actor in a Drama Series. As a producer of the film, he was nominated for the Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Television Movie. His film roles include Harry Brown in The Wild and the Willing (1962), Charlie Cartwright in If It's Tuesday, This Must Be Belgium (1969), Wolfe Lissner in Villain (1971), Teddy Bass in Sexy Beast (2000), Frank Powell in Hot Rod (2007), Tai Lung in Kung Fu Panda (2008), reprised the role in Kung Fu Panda 4 (2024), Blackbeard in Pirates of the Caribbean: On Stranger Tides (2011), and Winston Scott in the John Wick franchise (2014–present).

Ian McShane is an English actor, producer, and director, born on September 29, 1942, in Blackburn, Lancashire, England. He is best known for his roles in television series and films, particularly for portraying Al Swearengen in the critically acclaimed HBO series "Deadwood" and its film adaptation. McShane has received numerous accolades throughout his career, including a Golden Globe Award for Best Actor. His work spans several decades, with notable performances in films such as "The Last Tycoon," "Pirates of the Caribbean: On Stranger Tides," and "John Wick." In addition to his film and television work, McShane has lent his voice to various animated projects, including the character of Mr. Wednesday in the TV adaptation of Neil Gaiman's "American Gods." McShane's distinctive voice and commanding presence have made him a prominent figure in the entertainment industry.
Gurusamy Govandar a/l Kandasamy (born 11 January 1989) is a Malaysian professional footballer who plays as a midfielder.

Gurusamy Kandasamy (born January 1, 1975) is an Indian mathematician and educator known for his contributions to the fields of applied mathematics and numerical analysis. He has published extensively on computational methods and their applications in engineering and physical sciences. Kandasamy is a faculty member at a prominent university in India, where he teaches advanced mathematics and supervises postgraduate research. He is also recognized for his involvement in various mathematical organizations and conferences, promoting research and collaboration in the mathematical community.
Nodocion floridanus is a species of ground spider in the family Gnaphosidae. It is found in the United States and Mexico.

Nodocion floridanus is a species of freshwater snail belonging to the family Planorbidae, commonly known as ramshorn snails. It is native to North America, specifically found in the freshwater habitats of Florida. This species is characterized by its coiled shell and plays a role in the aquatic ecosystem, contributing to the detritus food web. Like other members of its family, Nodocion floridanus is an herbivore, primarily feeding on algae and decaying plant material. Conservation efforts may be important for its habitat due to environmental changes affecting freshwater ecosystems.
Tripadi (Kannada, lit. tri: three, pad or "adi": feet) is a native metre in the Kannada language dating back to c. 700 CE.

Tripadi is a traditional genre of Sanskrit poetry characterized by its concise structure and rhythmic pattern. It consists of three lines, typically with eight syllables per line, and is often used in devotional and philosophical literature. The Tripadi format is appreciated for its ability to convey profound meanings in a brief yet impactful manner. This poetic form has been employed by various poets throughout history to express spiritual themes and moral teachings. It is particularly notable in the works of poets from the Bhakti movement, where it served to enhance the emotional articulation of devotion.

Gold River is a village municipality located close to the geographic centre of Vancouver Island in British Columbia, Canada. In terms of the Island's human geography it is considered to be part of the "North Island", even though it technically is on the Island's west coast.

Gold River is a small village located in the Strathcona Regional District on Vancouver Island, British Columbia, Canada. Established in the 1960s, it was initially created as a company town to support a nearby pulp mill. The community is situated at the mouth of the Gold River and is surrounded by dense forests and mountains, making it popular for outdoor activities such as fishing, hiking, and wildlife viewing. Gold River serves as a gateway to Strathcona Provincial Park and has a population of approximately 1,200 residents. The village is known for its scenic beauty and a strong sense of community, with various local events and services supporting its residents.
Babe's & Ricky's Inn is a documentary film directed by Ramin Niami about the famed blues club, Babe's & Rickey's Inn. The film premiered April 5, 2013 at Laemmle Monica in Santa Monica, California.

Babe's & Rickey's Inn is a historic bar and restaurant located in the Adams Morgan neighborhood of Washington, D.C. Established in 1933, it has a reputation for its vibrant atmosphere, serving as a popular gathering spot for locals and visitors alike. The establishment offers a diverse menu featuring American comfort food and a wide selection of beverages. Known for its eclectic decor and lively entertainment, including live music and dancing, Babe's & Rickey's Inn has become a cultural landmark in the area, reflecting the rich social history of the neighborhood and the city's nightlife scene.

The Gregory Dipping Vat is a historic cattle dipping facility at 122 Rogers Street on the outskirts of Lake Village, Arkansas. The vat is a concrete structure 27 feet (8.2 m) long, 4 feet (1.2 m) wide, and 7 feet (2.1 m) deep. It is located on a grassy lane off Rogers Road, in a wooded area not far from Bayou Macon, whose waters were used to fill it. The vat was built c. 1930 as part of a statewide program to eradicate Texas tick fever, which was at the time a serious problem affecting the area's cattle farmers. After the program was ended in 1943, the vat became a play area for local youth. It remains in good condition as a reminder of the economically important tick eradication program. The vat was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2006.

The Gregory Dipping Vat is a historical apparatus used in the textile and dyeing industry, particularly prominent in the late 18th to early 19th centuries. Named after its inventor, English chemist and dye manufacturer George Gregory, the vat is designed for submerging fabric in dye solutions, allowing for even and efficient dye absorption. It operates on the principle of controlling temperature and chemical reactions, facilitating better colorfastness and consistency in dyeing. The invention contributed significantly to advances in textile processing, leading to improved production techniques and broader application of synthetic dyes in subsequent years. The Gregory Dipping Vat represents an important innovation in industrial chemistry and textile manufacturing.

Acton is an unincorporated community in Shelby County, in the U.S. state of Alabama. It is partially located within the city of Hoover. It is not to be confused with the village of Acton, approximately 6+1⁄2 miles to the northeast in Jefferson County.

Acton is an unincorporated community in Elmore County, Alabama, United States. It is situated along State Route 14, northwest of the city of Wetumpka. The community is characterized by its rural setting and agricultural surroundings. Acton is part of the Montgomery metropolitan area and is primarily residential, with a close-knit community atmosphere. The area contributes to the diverse cultural and historical fabric of Elmore County.

Persico Dosimo (Cremunés: Pérsec Dóseem) is a comune (municipality) in the Province of Cremona in the Italian region Lombardy, located about 80 kilometres (50 mi) southeast of Milan and about 8 kilometres (5 mi) northeast of Cremona. Persico Dosimo borders the following municipalities: Castelverde, Corte de' Frati, Cremona, Gadesco-Pieve Delmona, Grontardo, Pozzaglio ed Uniti. Among the local churches are: San Lorenzo, Quistro Santi Cosma e Damiano, Persico San Giovanni Battista Decollato, Dosimo

Persico Dosimo is a municipality in the province of Cremona, Lombardy, Italy. It is situated approximately 15 kilometers southeast of the city of Cremona and is part of the Cremona metropolitan area. The area is characterized by its rural landscape and agricultural activities. The town is known for its historical architecture, including the local parish church, and cultural heritage. As of the latest census, Persico Dosimo has a small population and maintains a close-knit community atmosphere. The economy of the municipality is primarily based on agriculture, although some residents also engage in activities related to commerce and services.

Loprazolam (triazulenone) marketed under many brand names is a benzodiazepine medication. It possesses anxiolytic, anticonvulsant, hypnotic, sedative and skeletal muscle relaxant properties. It is licensed and marketed for the short-term treatment of moderately-severe insomnia. It was patented in 1975 and came into medical use in 1983.

Loprazolam is a medication belonging to the benzodiazepine class, primarily used for the short-term treatment of insomnia and anxiety. Its sedative-hypnotic properties make it effective in promoting sleep and alleviating symptoms of anxiety disorders. Loprazolam functions by enhancing the effects of the neurotransmitter gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) at the GABA-A receptor, leading to increased neuronal inhibition and sedation. Due to its potential for dependence and withdrawal symptoms, it is typically prescribed for short durations. Common side effects may include drowsiness, dizziness, and impaired coordination. Loprazolam is not recommended for long-term use, especially in elderly patients or those with a history of substance use disorder.

This is a list of players who have appeared in at least one regular season or postseason game in the National Football League (NFL) for the Washington Commanders franchise and whose last names fall between "A" and "Ke". For the rest of the players, see Washington Commanders all-time roster (Kh–Z). This list is accurate through the end of the 2024 NFL season. This list also includes its predecessors, the Boston Braves (1932) Boston Redskins (1933–1936), Washington Redskins (1937–2019), and Washington Football Team (2020–2021). The Washington Redskins franchise was founded in Boston, Massachusetts as the Boston Braves, named after the local baseball franchise. The name was changed the next year to the Redskins. In 1937, the franchise moved to Washington, D.C. They played as the Washington Football Team from 2020 to 2021. The Redskins played over 1,000 games. In those games, the club won five professional American football championships including two NFL Championships and three Super Bowls. The franchise captured ten NFL divisional titles and six NFL conference championships. Overall, the Redskins had a total of 23 players and coaches (17 primary, six minor)[b] inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame. Many Redskins players have also had successful college football careers, including six who were Heisman Trophy winners: Gary Beban, Desmond Howard, Vic Janowicz, George Rogers, Danny Wuerffel, and Robert Griffin III. In addition, the Heisman Trophy sculpture was modeled after Ed Smith in 1934, who became a Redskins player in 1936. Several former players became head coach of the Redskins, including Turk Edwards, Dick Todd, and Jack Pardee. In addition, former players have become assistant coaches, such as Earnest Byner, Russ Grimm, Greg Manusky, and Keenan McCardell. Other players have also become successful in non-sport activities, like acting (Terry Crews and Jamal Duff) and politics (Tom Osborne and Heath Shuler). Players on the Commanders have also been related from time to time. In 1957, Redskins end Joe Walton became the first son of an NFL player to play in the league. His father, Frank Walton also played on the Redskins. Joe Krakoski and his son, also named Joe Krakoski, also both played for the Redskins. In addition, four sets of brothers have played with each other while on the Redskins: Chris and Nic Clemons, Cecil and Ray Hare, Ed and Robert Khayat, and Dan and Matt Turk.

The Washington Commanders all-time roster (A–Ke) encompasses players who have made significant contributions to the team throughout its history. This roster includes athletes whose last names begin with the letters A through Ke, showcasing a variety of positions and eras in the franchise's extensive timeline. The Commanders, established in 1932, have seen numerous Hall of Famers, Pro Bowl selections, and pivotal figures that have shaped the team’s identity and success. The roster highlights the diversity of talent and the impact these players have had on the team's achievements, including multiple championships and playoff appearances.

The Ford Rhein (type G398TS) is a truck model that Ford Germany manufactured together with its smaller sister model Ford Ruhr between 1948 and 1951. The new truck came as the successor to the Ford V8-51 and offered only a few changes to it such as larger headlights. It had a V8 gasoline engine with 3924 cc displacement, which initially produced 90 PS (66 kW) and was increased by 5 PS to 95 PS (70 kW) from 1949. The engine power was passed on to the rear wheels via a four or five-speed gearbox. An all-wheel drive system by Marmon-Herrington was also available on request. After a thorough modernization in 1951, the Rhein/Ruhr range was renamed Ford FK, followed by a four-digit code indicating its weight rating.

The Ford Rhein is a compact car produced by the Ford Motor Company in Europe from 1961 to 1967. Designed as a family sedan, the Ford Rhein was based on the popular Ford Anglia and shared many components with it, including engines and transmissions. The vehicle was well-regarded for its reliability and practicality, appealing to a broad market segment. The Rhein was available in a range of models, including two- and four-door variants, and was noted for its simple yet functional design. Production of the Ford Rhein ended in 1967 as the company shifted focus to other models, notably the more modern Ford Escort. While it is less well-known compared to other Ford models, the Rhein remains a notable part of automotive history in the 1960s.

Fussballclub Bern (FC Bern) is a football team from Bern, the capital city of Switzerland, who currently play in the Gruppe 1, in the Bern/Jura canton. In 1921, the club won the Och Cup (that was considered as the former Swiss Cup). The Och Cup ran only for two years. In 1925, in a single game, FC Bern won the cup against the other winner of the Och Cup.

FC Bern, commonly known as FC Bern, is a Swiss football club based in Bern, the capital city of Switzerland. Founded in 1894, the club has a rich history and is recognized for its contributions to Swiss football. FC Bern plays its home matches at the Stadion Wankdorf, which has a capacity of around 31,000 spectators. The team's colors are red and white. Over the years, FC Bern has won multiple national championships and domestic cups, securing its place in the top tier of Swiss football. The club is known for its passionate fanbase and has a rivalry with other Swiss teams, notably BSC Young Boys.

Diadenosine hexaphosphate hydrolase (ATP-forming) (EC 3.6.1.61, Ndx1) is an enzyme with systematic name P1,P6-bis(5'-adenosyl)hexaphosphate nucleotidohydrolase (ATP-forming). This enzyme catalyses the following chemical reaction (1) P1,P6-bis(5'-adenosyl)hexaphosphate + H2O ⇌ {\displaystyle \rightleftharpoons } 2 ATP (2) P1,P5-bis(5'-adenosyl)pentaphosphate + H2O ⇌ {\displaystyle \rightleftharpoons } ATP + ADP (3) P1,P4-bis(5'-adenosyl)tetraphosphate + H2O ⇌ {\displaystyle \rightleftharpoons } ATP + AMP The enzyme requires the presence of the divalent cations (Mn2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, and Co2+).

Diadenosine hexaphosphate hydrolase (ATP-forming) is an enzyme that catalyzes the hydrolysis of diadenosine hexaphosphate (Ap6A) to produce adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and adenosine monophosphate (AMP). This enzyme plays a critical role in nucleotide metabolism and energy transfer within cells. It is primarily found in eukaryotic organisms and is associated with various physiological processes, including cellular signaling and the regulation of ATP levels. The enzyme's activity contributes to the maintenance of cellular energy homeostasis and is of interest in studies related to cellular metabolism and pathophysiological conditions.

Teriyaki (Japanese: 照り焼き) is a cooking technique in which foods are broiled or grilled with a glaze of soy sauce, mirin, and sugar. Although commonly associated with Japanese cuisine, the technique was adapted for use in fast food meals in Seattle. Fish – yellowtail, marlin, skipjack tuna, salmon, trout, and mackerel – and chicken are mainly used in Japan, while other white and red meats – pork, lamb, and beef – are more often used in the West. Other ingredients sometimes used in Japan include squid, hamburger steak, and meatballs. The word teriyaki derives from the noun teri (照り), which refers to a shine or luster given by the sugar content in the tare (タレ), and yaki (焼き), which refers to the cooking method of grilling or broiling. Traditionally the meat is dipped in or brushed with sauce during cooking. It is believed that teriyaki in Japan evolved during the 1600s. The tare (タレ) is traditionally made by blending soy sauce, sake (or mirin), and sugar over heat. The sauce is boiled and reduced to the desired thickness, then used to marinate meat, which is then grilled or broiled. Sometimes ginger is added and the final dish may be garnished with spring onions.

Teriyaki is a Japanese cooking technique characterized by the use of a glaze made from soy sauce, sake, mirin, and sugar, which is applied to meat, fish, or vegetables. The term "teriyaki" derives from the Japanese words "teri," referring to the shine or luster of the sauce, and "yaki," meaning grilled or broiled. Commonly grilled dishes include chicken, beef, and salmon. Teriyaki is renowned for its sweet and savory flavor profile and has been adapted in various forms around the world, often incorporating different ingredients and cooking methods. In addition to its traditional uses, teriyaki sauce is widely available commercially and is utilized in a variety of international cuisines.

Thiruneelakantar is a 1939 Indian Tamil-language film directed by Raja Sandow and starring M. K. Thyagaraja Bhagavathar. The film, based on the life of Tirunilakanta Nayanar, was a big hit and was known for Thyagaraja Bhagavathar's acting apart from his songs. The noted nadaswaram player T. N. Rajarathinam Pillai makes a guest appearance.

Thiruneelakantar, also known as Thiruneelakanta, is a revered figure in Tamil Shaivism, celebrated as a saint and poet. He is traditionally regarded as one of the Nayanars, a group of 63 saints dedicated to Lord Shiva. His life and teachings are primarily documented in the "Nayanar Vaibhavam," which highlights his devotion and exemplary service to the deity. Thiruneelakantar is often depicted engaged in acts of piety and devotion, emphasizing themes of spiritual surrender and humility. His poems and hymns, characterized by deep devotion, form an integral part of Tamil devotional literature and contribute to the rich tradition of Bhakti in South India. He is especially worshipped in various temples dedicated to Shiva, where his legacy continues to inspire devotees.

Rebagliati pronounced [rebaʎˈʎaːti]) is an Italian surname. Notable people with the surname include: Chiara Rebagliati (born 1997), Italian archer Edgardo Rebagliati (1895–1958), Peruvian lawyer and journalist, Minister of Public Health Raúl Ferrero Rebagliati (1911–1977), Peruvian politician and Prime Minister Ross Rebagliati (born 1971), Canadian snowboarder

Rebagliati is a surname of Italian origin. Notably associated with the Canadian snowboarder Ross Rebagliati, who gained international fame after winning the gold medal in men's snowboarding at the 1998 Winter Olympics, becoming the first Olympic champion in the sport. His victory was later marred by a scandal involving a positive test for marijuana, leading to a temporary disqualification. The name has since been linked to discussions around drug policies in sports and the cultural perceptions of marijuana use among athletes. Rebagliati's legacy continues to influence the snowboarding community and sports policy debates.

Love's Awakening (German: Liebeserwachen) is a 1936 German drama film directed by Herbert Maisch and starring Eugen Klöpfer, Karin Hardt and Hans Schlenck. It was shot at the Marienfelde Studios of Terra Film in Berlin.

Love's Awakening is a 1936 American drama film directed by Benjamin Stoloff. The story revolves around a young woman who struggles with her feelings of love and emotional turmoil after a series of romantic entanglements. Starring Margaret Lindsay, John Litel, and David Mannes, the film explores themes of love, sacrifice, and personal growth. It was produced by Columbia Pictures and is notable for its melodramatic portrayal of romance and societal expectations of the era. The film received mixed reviews upon release and has since garnered attention for its performances and narrative style characteristic of 1930s cinema.

Hajia Maryam Inna Ciroma (born 11 September 1954) was appointed Nigerian minister of Women Affairs in July 2005 by President Olusegun Obasanjo. She was replaced by Saudatu Bungudu when President Umaru Yar'Adua swore in his cabinet in July 2007.

Maryam Ciroma is a Nigerian politician and public servant known for her work in women's empowerment and advocacy in Nigeria. She has served in various capacities, including as the Commissioner for Women Affairs and Social Development in Niger State. Ciroma is recognized for her efforts to promote gender equality, education, and health initiatives for women and children. She has also participated in national discussions aimed at increasing women's representation in politics and decision-making processes. Through her initiatives, she has garnered a reputation as a significant advocate for women's rights and social development in Nigeria.

Kahramanmaraş Castle is a castle in Turkey. Kahramanmaraş (popularly Maraş) is an old city. The castle is in the urban fabric of Kahramanmaraş city. It is at 37°35′11″N 36°55′33″E. It is on a tumulus so it isn't particularly high with respect to surrounding. The exact construction date of the castle is not known. It was built between the 1st century B.C. and the 2nd century A.D. Its plan is rectangular. Its outer dimensions are 150 metres (490 ft) north to south and 75 metres (246 ft) east to west. Three of its square plan bastions have been restored by the Ministry of Culture and Tourism. The outer walls of the bastions are of asthar stone and the inner walls are of rubble stone. The main gate of the castle is on the south wall. The arrow slits are on the eastern wall.

Kahramanmaraş Castle, known in Turkish as Kahramanmaraş Kalesi, is a historic fortress located in the city of Kahramanmaraş in Turkey. The castle is believed to have been originally built during the Roman period and has undergone various renovations and expansions throughout its history, particularly during the Byzantine and Seljuk eras. Characterized by its strategic hilltop position, the castle served as a defensive stronghold and played a significant role in the region's military history. The site features well-preserved walls, towers, and remains of structures that reflect its long-standing significance. Today, Kahramanmaraş Castle is a prominent tourist attraction, offering panoramic views of the surrounding area and insights into the historical architecture of the region.

Claudia Olsson (born 27 December 1983) is a Swedish technology expert, business leader, and speaker with a background in engineering and economics. She is CEO and founder of Exponential AB, founder of Stellar Capacity AB, an Associate Faculty member at Singularity University, a David Rockefeller Fellow at the Trilateral Commission, as well as a Young Global Leader with the World Economic Forum.

Claudia Olsson is a Swedish entrepreneur and technology leader known for her work in the fields of artificial intelligence, blockchain, and digital transformation. She is the founder and CEO of the company "Futurist" and has been involved in various initiatives aimed at promoting the ethical use of technology. Olsson is also recognized for her contributions to international organizations and has been a speaker at prominent conferences, addressing the impacts of technological advancements on society. Her work emphasizes the importance of guiding innovation towards sustainable and inclusive practices.

Andrew Peck (October 15, 1836 – March 21, 1918) was an American businessman who founded Peck & Snyder, a purveyor of sporting goods, in 1866. The company is considered to have been one of the earliest baseball card distributors in America.

Andrew Peck is a businessman known for his leadership in various financial and investment sectors. He has held prominent positions in several companies and is recognized for his strategic vision and entrepreneurial spirit. Peck has a background in finance, having studied at prestigious institutions, and has contributed to numerous successful ventures, often focusing on innovation and growth in the markets. His influence extends to mentoring emerging leaders in the business community and participating in philanthropic efforts aimed at supporting education and entrepreneurship.

The South Bonnie Brae Tract Historic District is a historic district of Victorian houses in Los Angeles, California, along the 1000 block of South Bonnie Brae Street and the 1800 block of West 11th Street in the Pico Union section of the city. The homes in the district date to the 1890s and reflect Queen Anne and Colonial Revival architecture. Based on its well-preserved period architecture, the district was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1988. The listing included 15 contributing buildings on 3.2 acres (1.3 ha). The Athletic Model Guild was located in the district at the intersection of 11th and Bonnie Brae Streets.
topic: South Bonnie Brae Tract Historic District

text: The South Bonnie Brae Tract Historic District is a designated historic area located in the city of Denver, Colorado. Established in the early 20th century, this district is notable for its residential architecture that reflects the development trends of the period, showcasing a variety of styles including Bungalow, Craftsman, and Colonial Revival. The district was listed on the National Register of Historic Places for its historical significance and concentration of well-preserved homes, contributing to the understanding of Denver's urban development and suburban growth. The area is characterized by tree-lined streets and features a community-oriented atmosphere, making it a valuable example of early suburban planning in the region.
topic: South Bonnie Brae Tract Historic District

text: The 2022–23 Liga 2 was the sixth season of the Liga 2 under its current name and the 13th season under its current league structure. 28 teams, an increase of four teams from the previous season's campaign, participated for the season. The league along with 2022–23 Liga 3 were abandoned due to a combination of factors, the most notable among them was the Kanjuruhan Stadium disaster.

The 2022–23 Liga 2 was the second season of Indonesia's Liga 2, the second tier of professional football in Indonesia. The season commenced on 26 August 2022 and concluded on 22 January 2023. The league featured various clubs competing for promotion to Liga 1, with the bottom teams facing relegation to Liga 3. The season was marked by competitive matches and displayed a mixture of experienced teams and rising clubs in Indonesian football. PSIS Semarang emerged as the season's champions, securing promotion to the top tier. The league operated under football regulations established by the Indonesian Football Association and followed a format that included a regular season followed by playoffs.

Mons Gruithuisen Delta is a mountain of the surface of the Moon. It is located north of Gruithuisen crater, east of Mons Gruithuisen Gamma. It has an altitude of about 1800 meters and a diameter of about 27 kilometers. The name, given in 1935, was named after Franz von Paula Gruithuisen, a German astronomer. The mountain has an extremely low bulk density of 2150±150 kg/m3.

Mons Gruithuisen Delta is a prominent volcanic dome located on the Moon, situated within the larger Gruithuisen crater near the lunar nearside. It is characterized by its unique, dome-like structure and is believed to be a product of volcanic activity, potentially formed from the eruption of low-viscosity lava. The formation is part of a group that includes Mons Gruithuisen Gamma, and together they are recognized for their resemblance to terrestrial volcanic features. The age of Mons Gruithuisen Delta is estimated to be relatively young in lunar terms, making it an area of interest for studies on lunar volcanism and geological history. The features of Mons Gruithuisen Delta provide valuable insights into the Moon's thermal evolution and tectonic processes.

William Mitchell (1670–1727) was a 17th/18th century Scottish minister who served as Moderator of the General Assembly of the Church of Scotland five times. He was Chaplain in Ordinary to the King (George I).

William Mitchell is a prominent figure in the field of online discourse and community moderation, best known for his work as an administrator and moderator across various internet platforms. He has contributed to the development of community guidelines and policies aimed at fostering respectful communication and reducing toxicity in online environments. Mitchell is recognized for his efforts in promoting civil discourse and addressing issues of harassment and misinformation in digital forums. His influence extends to various online communities, where he has been involved in discussions regarding the balance between free expression and community standards.

Susan Dietrich, known as The Space Lady (TSL) (formerly Suzy Soundz), born 1948 in Pueblo, Colorado and raised in Las Animas, Colorado, US, is a singer and musician in the genres of space music, synth pop, and psychedelic pop. She is also a paragon of the genre known as outsider music.

The Space Lady is the stage name of Susan M. K. H. “The Space Lady,” an American musician known for her unique blend of synth-pop and lo-fi music. She gained recognition in the 1980s for her distinctive performances, which featured her playing a Casio keyboard and singing original songs as well as covers, often in a dreamy, ethereal style. The Space Lady's music is characterized by its otherworldly themes and melodic simplicity, earning her a cult following over the years. In 2017, she released her first full-length album, "The Space Lady’s Greatest Hits," which compiled tracks from her extensive repertoire. Her influence can be seen in contemporary indie and alternative music, and she continues to perform and create music, engaging both longtime fans and new listeners.

The Indonesian ocellated flounder, Psammodiscus ocellatus, is an edible flatfish of the family Pleuronectidae. It is a demersal fish that lives on sandy bottoms in the eastern Indian Ocean, particularly Indonesia and northwestern Australia. It can reach 15 centimetres (5.9 in) in length.

The Indonesian ocellated flounder (Bothus mancus) is a species of flatfish belonging to the family Bothidae, commonly found in tropical marine environments across the Indo-Pacific region, particularly around coral reefs and sandy substrates. Characterized by its bilateral symmetry and distinct ocellated markings, it exhibits a flattened body enabling it to camouflage effectively against the ocean floor. The species is primarily carnivorous, feeding on small fish and invertebrates. As a relatively resilient fish, it plays a role in the local ecosystem and is of interest to fisheries and aquarium enthusiasts. Conservation status assessments for the Indonesian ocellated flounder indicate that it is currently not endangered, although habitat degradation poses potential risks.

"(Straight to Your Heart) Like a Cannonball" is a song written by Van Morrison that was first released on his 1971 album Tupelo Honey. It was also released as the third single from the album but did not chart.
topic: (Straight to Your Heart) Like a Cannonball

text: "(Straight to Your Heart) Like a Cannonball" is a song by the American rock band **Jellyfish**, released in 1993 as part of their debut album, "Bellybutton." The track showcases the band's signature power pop style, characterized by melodic harmonies and intricate instrumentation. It received positive reviews for its catchy chorus and lyrical themes of love and longing. The song contributed to Jellyfish's recognition in the early 1990s alternative music scene, although the band disbanded shortly after the album's release. text: Phloeotribus is a genus of crenulate bark beetles in the family Curculionidae. There are at least 150 described species in Phloeotribus.

Phloeotribus is a genus of bark beetles belonging to the family Curculionidae, subfamily Scolytinae. This genus includes various species that are typically associated with the wood and bark of trees, where they play a significant role in forest ecology by contributing to the decomposition of dead wood and influencing tree health. Phloeotribus species are found in diverse habitats and are particularly noted for their impact on coniferous forests. Commonly, these beetles exhibit a range of morphological adaptations suited for tunneling through bark and wood, and their life cycles involve complex interactions with their environments, including symbiotic relationships with fungi. Identification of Phloeotribus species is often based on specific morphological traits, which vary among species within the genus.

Sevan Kirder (born 9 February 1980) is the flutist/bagpiper for the Swiss band Red Shamrock (Celtic Folk). He hosts a Boardgame Podcast since April 2019 "Der Brettspiele Podcast, den die Welt nicht braucht".

The film explores themes of power, corruption, and societal struggles, framed within a backdrop of folk music and traditional values. Featuring an ensemble cast that includes Samantha Ruth Prabhu, Jagapati Babu, and Prakash Raj, Rangasthalam received critical acclaim for its storytelling, performances, and technical aspects, particularly its cinematography and music. Arnaud's is a restaurant in the French Quarter of New Orleans, Louisiana, specializing in Louisiana Creole cuisine. Established in 1918, it is one of the older and more famous restaurants in the city. The restaurant also houses a museum of the New Orleans Mardi Gras.

Arnaud's is a historic Creole restaurant located in the French Quarter of New Orleans, Louisiana, known for its classic Cajun and Creole cuisine. Established in 1918 by Arnaud Cazenave, the establishment features a mix of fine dining and a lively atmosphere, complete with traditional jazz music. Waffles + Mochi (pronounced Waffles and Mochi) is an American children's cooking musical comedy puppet television series. Three companion books were also released in 2021: Follow That Food! by Christy Webster and Sarah Rebar, Pickle Party by Frank Berrio and Sarah Rebar, and Waffles + Mochi: Get Cooking! by Yewande Komolafe. On November 23, 2021, Netflix released a special holiday episode titled "Waffles + Mochi's Holiday Feast". On October 17, 2022, Netflix released a spinoff series titled Waffles + Mochi's Restaurant.

"Waffles + Mochi" is a children's television series that premiered on Netflix on March 16, 2021. Created by Eric Appel and produced by Higher Ground Productions, the show features Waffles, a young yeti, and Mochi, a rice cake, who embark on culinary adventures to explore different cultures and learn about food. The series emphasizes themes of friendship, curiosity, and cultural diversity while incorporating engaging storytelling and musical elements. Victor Edward Milne M.B., CH.B, D.P.H (22 June 1897 – 6 September 1971), was a professional footballer who is best known for his time with Aston Villa. Before playing for Villa, Milne played for Aberdeen. Milne was the son of the first chairman of Aberdeen and a qualified doctor by the time he joined Villa. Milne retired from football while still at Villa and went on to be club doctor and a local GP.

Vic Milne was a Scottish professional footballer, born on 10 March 1945 in Glasgow, Scotland. Primarily a winger known for his speed and agility, Milne began his career in youth football before signing with a professional club. He played for several teams during his career, notably making significant contributions to his teams in the lower divisions of Scottish football. After retiring from professional football, he remained involved in the sport through coaching and community initiatives. His legacy is marked by his impact on the game at the grassroots level and his contribution to the development of young footballers.

"Dance with Me" is a 1975 hit single by American soft rock band Orleans from their second studio album, Orleans II (1974). Featuring a melodica solo by Larry Hoppen, "Dance with Me" was introduced on the band's second album, Orleans II, and later included on their third album Let There Be Music (1975). The song was issued as a single on July 19, 1975, to become Orleans' first Top 40 hit, peaking at No. 6 on the Billboard Hot 100 the week of October 18, 1975.

"Dance with Me" is a song by the American rock band Orleans, released in 1979 as part of their album "Let There Be Music." The track was written by group members John Hall and Johanna Hall and features a blend of soft rock and pop elements. It gained significant popularity, reaching the top 10 on the Billboard Hot 100 chart. The song is characterized by its catchy melody and romantic lyrics, which emphasize the theme of dancing and connection between partners. "Dance with Me" remains one of Orleans' most recognizable hits and is often associated with the band's signature sound from the late 1970s.

Geisfeld is a small village located in Bavaria, Germany. It is in Upper Franconia, in the Bamberg district. Geisfeld is a constituent community of Strullendorf. In 2009, Geisfeld had a total population of 911.

Geisfeld is a municipality located in the district of Kulmbach in Bavaria, Germany. It is situated in the picturesque region of Upper Franconia, characterized by rolling hills and scenic landscapes. The village showcases traditional Bavarian architecture and is known for its agricultural activities. Geisfeld serves as a small rural community, providing a tranquil lifestyle with various local amenities. Alfeus Island (Bulgarian: остров Алфеус, romanized: ostrov Alfeus, IPA: [ˈɔstrof ɐɫˈfɛos]) is the 310 m long in west-southwest to east-northeast direction and 120 m wide rocky island lying off the north coast of Smith Island in the South Shetland Islands, Antarctica. The island is “named after the ocean fishing trawler Alfeus of the Bulgarian company Ocean Fisheries – Burgas whose ships operated in the waters of South Georgia, Kerguelen, the South Orkney Islands, South Shetland Islands and Antarctic Peninsula from 1970 to the early 1990s. The Bulgarian fishermen, along with those of the Soviet Union, Poland and East Germany are the pioneers of modern Antarctic fishing industry.”

Alfeus Island is a small, uninhabited island located in the southern region of the Indian Ocean, specifically within the territorial waters of the French Southern and Antarctic Lands. The island is part of the sub-Antarctic islands and is characterized by its rugged terrain and diverse marine life. Its remote location and challenging climate make it a less explored area, though it is ecologically significant for various seabird species and seals that inhabit the surrounding waters. Alfeus Island is primarily of interest for scientific research and conservation efforts, particularly in the context of climate change and its impacts on sub-Antarctic ecosystems.

The 2007 South American U-17 Championship squads comprised national under-17 teams from ten South American countries competing in the tournament held in Ecuador. Longchaeus conicus is a species of sea snail, a marine gastropod mollusk in the family Pyramidellidae, the pyrams and their allies.

Longchaeus conicus is a species of beetle belonging to the family Cerambycidae, commonly known as longhorn beetles. Longchaeus conicus is characterized by its elongated body and long antennae, typical features of longhorn beetles. The species was first described by the entomologist H. D. B. Hinton in 1940. Eurypepla is a genus of tortoise beetles and hispines in the family Chrysomelidae. There are at least four described species in Eurypepla.

Eurypepla is a genus of moths belonging to the family Carposinidae, primarily found in Australia. The genus was first described by entomologist Edward Meyrick in 1885. Species within this genus are characterized by their distinctive wing patterns and are typically small to medium-sized moths. Eurypepla moths are nocturnal and are often attracted to artificial lights. The ecology and biology of Eurypepla remain relatively under-researched, but they are believed to have specific host plants. Alexander Mathis is an Austrian mathematician, computational neuroscientist and software developer. He is currently an assistant professor at the École polytechnique fédérale de Lausanne (EPFL) in Switzerland. He is working at the intersection of computational neuroscience, and machine learning, focusing on trying to understand the statistics of behavior and how the brain creates behavior.

Alexander Mathis is a prominent scientist known for his contributions to the field of biochemistry and molecular biology. The Karditsa electoral constituency (Greek: περιφέρεια Καρδίτσας) is a parliamentary constituency of Greece.

Karditsa is a parliamentary constituency in Central Greece, part of the regional unit of Karditsa. Headspace is a digital health company that specializes in mindfulness and meditation. Mengu-Timur ( MEN-goo TIM-ər) or Möngke Temür (Mongolian: ᠮᠥᠩᠬᠡᠲᠡᠮᠦᠷ, Мөнхтөмөр; died 1280) was Khan of the Golden Horde, a division of the Mongol Empire, from 1266 to 1280. He was a son of Toqoqan Khan, himself the son of Batu, and Köchu Khatun of Oirat, the daughter of Toralchi Küregen and granddaughter of Qutuqa Beki. His name literally means "Eternal Iron" in the Mongolian language.

Mengu-Timur is a prominent historical figure of the Mongol Empire, known for his role as a military leader and member of the Borjigin clan, which was the ruling dynasty of the empire. He was the grandson of Genghis Khan and played a significant part in the expansion of Mongol territories during the 13th century. Mengu-Timur is particularly noted for his leadership during various military campaigns and his administrative contributions in consolidating Mongol rule across conquered regions. His legacy is intertwined with the larger narrative of the Mongol Empire's rise, illustrating the complexities of intra-clan dynamics and the broader socio-political landscape of the time.

Alfred Traber Goshorn (July 15, 1833 – February 19, 1902) was a Cincinnati, Ohio, businessman and booster who served as director-general of the 1876 Centennial Exposition in Philadelphia. That was the first world's fair in the United States and so resounding a success that Queen Victoria knighted Goshorn and the leaders of Europe presented him with numerous accolades.

Alfred T. Goshorn (1829–1892) was an American businessman, civil engineer, and politician notable for his role in the development of the Cincinnati, Ohio, region. Firearms is a first-person shooter mod for Half-Life which originated from a Quake modification. Initially developed in 1998, Firearms was created as a quasi-realistic team-based FPS. The mod's main feature is the large amount of usable weapons in the game.

Firearms is a tactical first-person shooter modification for the video game Half-Life, originally released in 1999. text: The Pays de Bitche (French pronunciation: [pe.i də bitʃ], literally Land of Bitche, German: Bitscherland or Bitcherland) is a natural region in the Moselle department of the Grand Est region of France. It corresponds to the present French part of the former principality of Zweibrücken-Bitsch and to the part of the Northern Vosges that lies within Lorraine. The Pays de Bitche has a total of 47 municipalities. 46 of them are gathered into the Bitche canton and the remaining one, Kalhausen, is a part of the Sarreguemines canton.

The Pays de Bitche is a geographical region located in northeastern France, primarily within the Moselle department of the Grand Est region. It encompasses the area around the town of Bitche and is known for its historical significance, diverse landscapes, and cultural heritage. The region features a mix of rural and urban areas, with a focus on agriculture and tourism. Notable attractions include the citadel of Bitche, a well-preserved fortress that reflects the area's military history, as well as natural parks and hiking trails that draw visitors to its scenic environment. The Pays de Bitche also has a rich cultural identity, influenced by both French and German traditions, owing to its proximity to the border with Germany.

Richard James Allen (born 1960) is a contemporary Australian poet, dancer, actor and filmmaker. The former artistic director of the Poets Union Inc, and founding director of the Australian Poetry Festival, Allen was co-artistic director with Karen Pearlman of That Was Fast (New York City) and Tasdance (Launceston), and now at The Physical TV Company (Sydney). Allen has published thirteen books of poetry, fiction or performance texts, most recently Text Messages from the Universe (2023), More Lies (2021), The short story of you and I (2019), Fixing the Broken Nightingale (2014), The Kamikaze Mind (2006), and Performing the Unnameable: An Anthology of Australian Performance Texts (1999), co-edited with Karen Pearlman. He received the 2005 University of Technology, Sydney, Chancellor's Award for Best Doctoral Thesis. A multi-award-winning film adaptation of his Kenneth Slessor Prize for Poetry-nominated book, Thursday's Fictions (1999), was first broadcast by the Australian Broadcasting Corporation in 2007. This surreal dance fantasy also has a Second Life presence, Thursday's Fictions in Second Life. He is the grandson of World War II Major General Arthur Samuel "Tubby" Allen, son of novelist and short story writer Robert Allen, and the brother of art critic Christopher Allen.

Richard James Allen (born 1949) is an Australian poet, writer, and educator known for his contributions to contemporary Australian literature. He has published numerous poetry collections and is recognized for his distinctive style that often explores themes of identity, nature, and the complexities of human experience. Allen has also served in various educational roles, promoting literature and creative writing within academic settings. Kathaleen Saint Jude McCormick (born 1979) is an American lawyer and judge on the Delaware Court of Chancery, first as a vice chancellor from 2018 to 2021 and then as the current chancellor since 2021. She is the first female chancellor in Delaware history.

Kathaleen McCormick is an American attorney and jurist who serves as the Chancellor of the Delaware Court of Chancery, having been appointed to the position in 2021. McCormick is the first woman to hold this role, which is significant in the context of corporate law and governance. She has a background in corporate litigation and has previously worked as a vice chancellor on the court. McCormick's legal career has included a focus on cases involving mergers and acquisitions, director fiduciary duties, and complex commercial disputes, positioning her as a prominent figure in Delaware's legal landscape.

Stewart is a Scottish and English surname, also used as a given name. It is possibly derived from the old English word "stigweard", a compound of "stig" meaning household, and "weard", a guardian (ward), or from the Gaelic Stiùbhart meaning steward. Alternative spellings are Stuart, Steward and Steuart. The surname Stewart has large concentrations in the United States (mainly in the Deep South, and the other southern states), United Kingdom (mainly in Scotland, Northern Ireland, North East England, South West England, Cumbria, Lancashire, and Yorkshire), Canada, Jamaica, New Zealand, Australia and elsewhere that has large Scottish or Ulster Scots diaspora. The progenitor of the Stewart family was Alan fitz Flaad, a Breton knight who settled in England after the Norman Conquest. His son, Walter fitz Alan, relocated to Scotland during the Anarchy and became the High Steward of Scotland, hence the origin of the surname. In 2014, Stewart was the 66th-most common surname in the United Kingdom.

Stewart is a surname of Scottish origin, derived from the Middle English word "steward," meaning steward or keeper of a household. Historically, the name has been associated with the Scottish royal family, particularly through the House of Stuart (or Stewart), which produced monarchs like James VI and I. The name is prevalent in various forms, including Stuart and Steuart, and is also used as a given name. Notable individuals with the surname include the American actor Jon Stewart, the Scottish artist Sir Richard Stewart, and the Canadian politician Doug Stewart. The name has also been widely adopted in the English-speaking world, leading to various place names and organizations bearing the Stewart name.

Operation Royal Amethyst was a military operation carried out by the Portuguese Guinean Battalion of Commandos, which was held on 8 May 1973 in the course of the Colonial War in Guinea-Bissau, which had the purpose of "annihilating or at least dismantling the organisation of the guerrillas in the region of Guidaje-Bigene".

Operation Royal Amethyst was a military operation conducted by the British Army during the Korean War in 1951. The operation aimed to secure the withdrawal of British Commonwealth forces from the Chinese-North Korean front lines following heavy casualties and strategic reassessment. It involved a series of planned maneuvers to safely extract troops while maintaining defensive positions. The operation was characterized by its emphasis on minimizing further losses while ensuring the safe retreat of personnel. It ultimately contributed to the broader efforts of allied forces in the conflict and highlighted the challenges of military logistics in a complex wartime environment.
Afraciura zernyi is a species of tephritid or fruit flies in the genus Afraciura of the family Tephritidae.

Afraciura zernyi is a species of moth belonging to the family Pyralidae, which encompasses a diverse group of snout moths. First described by the entomologist Hans Georg Amsel in 1961, this species is primarily found in the regions of Africa. Like other moths in its family, Afraciura zernyi undergoes a complete metamorphosis, including larval, pupal, and adult stages. Its ecological role and behaviors, as well as its conservation status, remain subjects of ongoing research within the field of lepidopterology.
Schweikert Factory is a former factory building belonging to the Schweikert family, located at 251 Wolczanska Street in Łódź.

The Schweikert Factory, established in the early 20th century, was a prominent manufacturing facility located in the United States, known for producing a variety of consumer goods, particularly textiles and garments. The factory gained a reputation for its innovative production techniques and played a significant role in the local economy by providing employment to hundreds of workers. Over the decades, the Schweikert Factory underwent several ownership changes and expansions, adapting to shifts in market demand and technology. However, like many manufacturing plants, it faced challenges due to globalization and the decline of domestic production, leading to its eventual closure in the late 20th century. Today, the site is often referenced in discussions about the evolution of American industry and the socio-economic impacts of manufacturing decline.

Cocoa necrosis virus (CoNV) is a plant pathogenic virus of the genus Nepovirus that infects Theobroma cacao en natura causing cacao necrosis disease. CoNV is considered synonymous with Strain S of cacao swollen shoot virus. Unlike Cacao swollen shoot virus, it is not transmitted by mealybugs nor vectored by aphids, beetles, or leafhoppers that also commonly infest cacao. Serologically, it is distantly related to Tomato black ring virus and very distantly related to Grapevine chrome mosaic virus.

Cocoa necrosis virus (CNV) is a viral pathogen belonging to the genus Badnavirus and is known to infect cacao plants (Theobroma cacao). It primarily causes cocoa necrosis disease, which leads to significant yield losses in cocoa production. Symptoms of the disease include leaf necrosis, stunted growth, and premature fruit drop. CNV is transmitted primarily through mealybug vectors and can also spread through vegetative propagation. The management of cocoa necrosis virus involves integrated disease management strategies, including the use of resistant cultivars, insect control measures, and good agricultural practices. The impact of CNV on cocoa agriculture poses economic challenges, particularly in regions where cocoa is a vital cash crop.

Selce (Hungarian: Telep) is a village and municipality in the Poltár District in the Banská Bystrica Region of Slovakia. In the village there is a food store and a neoclassical evangelical church from the 19th century.

Selce is a village situated in the Poltár District of Slovakia, within the Banská Bystrica Region. The village is primarily inhabited by Slovak citizens and has a small, close-knit community. Selce has a history that reflects the broader socio-economic changes in Slovakia over the years, although specific historical details may vary.

Torre Paradox is a residential skyscraper in the Chapultepec district of Mexico City, Mexico. Built between 2014 and 2018, the tower stands at 196.2 m (644 ft) tall with 60 floors, and is the current 15th tallest building in Mexico and the 8th tallest in Mexico City.

The Torre Paradox is a philosophical and mathematical concept that arises in the context of game theory and decision-making under uncertainty. It highlights situations in which an agent's preferences can seem contradictory when considering the outcome of their choices in a sequential manner. Specifically, the paradox illustrates how a rational decision-making process can lead to unexpected and seemingly illogical conclusions, particularly when the outcomes are contingent on prior actions or beliefs. The Torre Paradox has implications in various fields, including economics, behavioral psychology, and cognitive science, where it challenges traditional notions of rationality and decision-making consistency.

Varad Point (Bulgarian: нос Варад, ‘Nos Varad’ \'nos va-'rad\) is the rocky point on Foyn Coast, Antarctic Peninsula situated 12.8 km west-southwest of Spur Point and 33.6 km north-northwest of Cape Robinson. The feature is named after the settlement of Varad in Southern Bulgaria.

Varad Point is a geographical feature situated in the South Shetland Islands of Antarctica. It is located on Livingston Island, one of the most prominent islands in the archipelago. The point is notable for its position at the northern tip of a small peninsula, which extends into the surrounding waters. Varad Point is named after the Bulgarian folkloric figure Varad, reflecting the cultural contributions of Bulgaria to Antarctic research and exploration. The area surrounding the point is characterized by its rugged terrain and glacial features, making it a significant landmark for navigational purposes in the region.

On May 16, 2025, ten inmates of the Orleans Parish Prison, located in New Orleans, Louisiana, escaped through a hole in the wall behind a toilet. Six of the ten inmates were charged with murder or attempted murder, and all inmates have since been recaptured as of October 8. Nine of the inmates are pre-trial.

The 2025 New Orleans jailbreak refers to a significant incident involving the escape of several inmates from a correctional facility in New Orleans, Louisiana, occurring in January 2025. The jailbreak was marked by a coordinated effort that utilized various methods, including breaches in security protocols and potential inside assistance. The event drew widespread media attention due to its implications for local law enforcement and prison security. Following the escape, authorities launched a large-scale manhunt, deploying specialized units and seeking public assistance. The incident raised discussions on prison reform, security measures, and the reintegration of former inmates into society. Investigations into the jailbreak are ongoing, focusing on the circumstances that allowed the escape and accountability within the correctional system.

Grace Reksten Skaugen (born November 1953) is the chairperson of the Norwegian Institute of Directors and deputy chairperson of Statoil. She is a member of the council of the International Institute for Strategic Studies.

Grace Reksten Skaugen (born 16 December 1946) is a Norwegian author and screenwriter renowned for her contributions to the literary and film industries. She is best known for her works that often explore themes of identity, belonging, and the complexities of human relationships. Skaugen's writing is characterized by its lyrical style and deep psychological insights. In addition to her literary career, she has also written screenplays for Norwegian cinema, further establishing her influence in the cultural landscape of Norway. Over the years, Skaugen has received several awards for her work, highlighting her significance as a contemporary voice in Norwegian literature.
The Apprentices Act 1536 (28 Hen. 8. c. 5) was an act of the Parliament of England.

The Apprentices Act 1536 was a significant piece of legislation enacted under the reign of King Henry VIII in England. It established a legal framework for the apprenticeship system, regulating the employment and training of apprentices in various trades. The Act aimed to ensure the proper oversight of apprenticeship contracts, outlining the rights and responsibilities of both masters and apprentices. It also set minimum age requirements for apprentices and addressed issues related to the employment conditions and duration of apprenticeships. The legislation was part of broader efforts to improve economic standards and promote skilled labor in Tudor England, contributing to the growth of various crafts and industries during the period.

Mazi Smith (born June 16, 2001) is an American professional football defensive tackle for the New York Jets of the National Football League (NFL). He played college football for the Michigan Wolverines and was selected by the Dallas Cowboys in the first round of the 2023 NFL draft.

Mazi Smith is an American football player who plays as a defensive tackle. Born on July 4, 2001, in Detroit, Michigan, he gained recognition for his athleticism and skill on the field during his collegiate career at the University of Michigan. Smith was a standout player in high school and contributed to the Wolverines' defensive line, earning accolades for his performance. Following his college career, he entered the NFL Draft and was selected by the Dallas Cowboys in 2023. Smith is known for his strength, agility, and potential to become a significant player in professional football.

Where Were You Last Night is a 1989 album released by Swedish pop artist Ankie Bagger. It was released in 1989 through Sonet Records "People Say It's in the Air", "Where Were You Last Night", the Kiss cover "I Was Made for Lovin' You" and "Love Really Hurts Without You" were released as singles. "Where Were You Last Night" has been covered several times, most notably by the Finnish metal band Nightwish.

"Where Were You Last Night" is a country music song recorded by the American singer Alan Jackson, released as a single in 1990. The song is featured on his album "Here in the Real World." Its lyrics recount a man's feelings of betrayal and confusion as he confronts a partner about their unexplained absence the previous night. The song showcases Jackson's signature blend of honky-tonk sound and emotional storytelling, and it contributed to his rising fame in the country music genre. "Where Were You Last Night" was well-received, reaching the Top 10 on the Billboard Hot Country Songs chart.

Neptis trigonophora, the barred sailer, is a butterfly of the family Nymphalidae. It is found in southern Africa. Wingspan is 45–50 mm in males and 48–55 mm in females. Adults are on the wing possibly year round with peak from March to April. The larvae feed on Paullinia pinnata.

Neptis trigonophora, commonly known as the three-spotted navigator, is a species of butterfly in the family Nymphalidae. This species is primarily found in the tropical and subtropical regions of Asia, including parts of India, Myanmar, and Thailand. The three-spotted navigator is characterized by distinctive wing patterns featuring dark brown and orange hues, with three prominent spots on its wings. It typically inhabits wooded areas and is often seen near forest edges. The larvae of Neptis trigonophora feed on various host plants, contributing to its role in the ecosystem. Conservation efforts are important to maintain its populations, as habitat loss poses a threat to its survival.

János Oscar Plesch (18 November 1878 – 28 May 1957) was a Hungarian academic pathologist, physiologist, and physician. Plesch was well known internationally for his clinical research on blood circulation. He also invented the first convenient device for taking blood pressure. Plesch was a close friend and doctor to Albert Einstein for 25 years. Plesch's autobiography contains two chapters on Einstein. Plesch lived in Berlin for much of his early working life. As a Jew, he left Germany after the Nazi Party took control and moved to England where he was required to re-qualify as a medical doctor. In the UK, Plesch became the physician to the economist John Maynard Keynes and was credited with helping Keynes to survive heart attacks and declining health through his skilled medical treatment. Plesch's son, Peter, was an art collector.

János Plesch (1889-1974) was a Hungarian-born physician and biochemist recognized for his contributions to medical science, particularly in the field of nutrition and metabolism. He emigrated to the United States in the early 20th century, where he conducted research on vitamin deficiencies and their physiological impacts. Plesch's work in nutritional science played a significant role in the understanding of essential nutrients and their effects on human health. Throughout his career, he published numerous articles and was involved in various scientific organizations, contributing to advancements in medical education and research methodologies. His legacy continues to influence the fields of biochemistry and nutrition.

Dawa Khan Menapal (died 6 August 2021; sometimes written Meenapal, Minapal or Mina Pal) was the director of the Afghanistan government's media and information centre, and worked as a spokesman for Afghan President Ashraf Ghani. He had previously been a journalist for Radio Azadi (formerly Radio Free Afghanistan). Menapal obtained a degree in Law and Political Science at Kabul University. In 2008 he was kidnapped by the Taliban on the Kabul-Kandahar highway, but released three days later after protests by tribal leaders who respected his journalism as balanced and fair. In 2009, he was assaulted by Afghan police. He won the David Burke Award for Distinguished Journalism in 2010, "for his professionalism in reporting from Kandahar, Afghanistan, despite harassment by government authorities and militants." On 6 August 2021 he was assassinated by the Taliban, during their retaking of the country. A Taliban spokesman said he had been "punished for his deeds". He was attacked by gunmen on Darul Aman Road in Kabul while going to Friday prayers.

Dawa Khan Menapal (1976 - 2021) was an Afghan journalist and civil rights activist known for his contributions to media and advocacy for freedom of expression in Afghanistan. Born in the Panjshir Province, he graduated with a degree in journalism and began his career working with various media outlets, including Afghanistan's national television and local radio stations. Menapal was a vocal critic of government corruption and frequently addressed issues concerning democracy and human rights in Afghanistan. His work earned him both respect and threats, leading to a turbulent career marked by political challenges. Tragically, Menapal was killed in a targeted attack in September 2021, highlighting the dangers faced by journalists in conflict zones. His legacy continues to inspire media professionals and activists advocating for press freedom and human rights in Afghanistan and beyond.
Arima Naozumi (有馬 直純; 1586 – June 3, 1641) was a Japanese samurai lord who was daimyo of Shimabara Domain and head of the Hizen-Arima clan.

Arima Naozumi (有馬 直澄, 1862 – 1940) was a prominent Japanese politician and a member of the House of Peers during the Meiji and Taishō periods. Born into a samurai family in Kumamoto Prefecture, he became involved in the political landscape of Japan during a time of significant social and political change. Arima played a key role in advocating for modernization and the establishment of a constitutional government in Japan. He was known for his efforts in promoting education and infrastructure development. After the Meiji Restoration, he served in various political capacities, contributing to the formulation of policies that shaped modern Japan. In addition to his political career, Arima was also recognized for his contributions to public service and his involvement in various civic organizations. His legacy is noted for reflecting the transitional nature of Japan during the early 20th century.

West Baptist Church (Bible Church of God; Old School; Hardshell Baptist Church) is a historic church building at 500 W. Mulberry Street in Lebanon, Ohio. The Greek Revival building was constructed in 1860 and added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1984.

West Baptist Church is a Christian congregation located in various regions, commonly associated with the Baptist denomination. Known for its emphasis on biblical teachings, community service, and worship, West Baptist Churches often engage in various outreach programs, including educational initiatives and social assistance. The architecture and size of the church can vary widely, reflecting local cultural influences. Many West Baptist Churches also participate in regional and national Baptist associations, fostering collaboration among congregations. The congregations typically uphold core Baptist beliefs, including the autonomy of the local church, believer's baptism, and the authority of scripture.

Shinji Nakano (中野慎詞, Nakano Shinji; born 8 June 1999) is a Japanese track cyclist. He is also active in Japan as a keirin cyclist. He competed at the 2024 Summer Olympics in the Men's keirin in Paris on 11 August 2024. He is not to be confused with the ex-Formula 1 driver of the same name or the similar named ex-MotoGP rider Shinya Nakano

Shinji Nakano is a former Japanese professional road racing cyclist known for his participation in the Tour de France and other prestigious cycling events. Born on December 25, 1967, in Kumamoto, Japan, Nakano gained prominence in the cycling community during the 1990s, competing for various teams and representing Japan in international competitions. He was noted for his climbing abilities and resilience in stage races. Throughout his career, Nakano achieved several national titles and earned recognition for his contributions to Japanese cycling. After retiring from competitive cycling, he has been involved in promoting the sport within Japan.
Port-Louis (French pronunciation: [pɔʁ lwi] ; Guadeloupean Creole: Pòlwi) is a commune in the department of Guadeloupe, on the northwest of Grande-Terre.

Port-Louis is a commune located on the northern coast of the island of Grande-Terre in Guadeloupe, an overseas region of France in the Caribbean. It serves as a notable fishing and agricultural center, known particularly for its production of sugar and rum. The town features a rich history influenced by its colonial past, with remnants of its early days visible in its architecture and cultural practices. Port-Louis is also recognized for its scenic coastline, including beaches and mangroves, which attract tourists. The local economy is primarily driven by agriculture, tourism, and fishing. The population is approximately 5,000 residents, reflecting a vibrant community that blends French and Caribbean cultures. The area is characterized by its warm tropical climate, contributing to its agricultural productivity and appeal as a tourist destination.

Ó Ciardha, modern spelling Ó Ciara: Anglicised Carey, Keary, Kearey, Carry , O Carry . Mainly a midland (Leinster) surname. One major sept of Ó Ciardha was that of Carbury in modern County Kildare, which receives very early mention in the Irish Annals, such as the Annals of Ulster in 954 AD, 'ua Ciardai, ri Cairpri', i.e. O Carey, king of Carbury, and the Annals of Tigernach in 993 AD. Other names are also Anglicised Carey, including Ó Ciaráin (Munster), Ó Céirín (Connaught, Munster). All three of these patronymics have Old Irish root 'ciar' or 'céir'- 'dark, black'.

Ó Ciardha is an Irish surname that is derived from the Gaelic "Ó Ciardha," meaning "descendant of Ciardha," with Ciardha being a personal name of uncertain origin. This surname is associated with Gaelic Ireland and is prevalent in counties such as Kerry and Limerick. Like many Irish surnames, Ó Ciardha reflects a lineage tied to familial clans and often signifies territorial or ancestral connections. Notable individuals with this surname have made contributions in various fields, including academia, literature, and public service. The surname may be anglicized in different forms, including O'Keary or O'Ciardha, depending on regional variations. The historical significance of the Ó Ciardha clan contributes to the broader tapestry of Irish heritage and identity.
Paseuthsack Souliyavong (born 26 October 1990) is a former Laotian professional footballer who played as a midfielder.

Paseuthsack Souliyavong is a prominent Laotian politician known for his roles within the Lao People's Revolutionary Party and the government of Laos. He has held various significant positions, including serving as a member of the Central Committee of the Lao People's Revolutionary Party. Souliyavong has been influential in the development of economic policies and governance in Laos, contributing to the country's political landscape. His career reflects the ongoing efforts of the Lao government to modernize and engage with regional and international partners. As of October 2023, specific details regarding the most recent developments in his political career may be limited.

Zeydi (Persian: زيدي, also Romanized as Zeydī) is a village in Siyarastaq Yeylaq Rural District, Rahimabad District, Rudsar County, Gilan Province, Iran. At the 2006 census, its population was 26, in 6 families.

Zeydi, also known as Zaidi or Zaydi, is a branch of Shia Islam primarily found in Yemen and parts of Saudi Arabia. It is named after Zayd ibn Ali, the great-grandson of the Prophet Muhammad. Zeydis believe in the leadership of the Imams from the lineage of Ali and Fatimah, and emphasize the importance of social justice and the struggle against tyranny. Differing from the Twelver and Ismaili branches of Shia Islam, Zeydis do not believe in a divinely appointed infallible Imam but accept the Imamate as a position that can be filled by qualified individuals from the Prophet's family. Historically, Zeydi states flourished in Yemen, particularly during the Zaydi imamate, which lasted until the 20th century. The Zaydi community plays a significant role in Yemeni politics and society, particularly in the northern regions of the country.
Alan Zelick Trachtenberg (March 22, 1932 – August 18, 2020) was an American historian and the Neil Gray Jr. Professor of English and professor emeritus of American Studies at Yale University.

Alan Trachtenberg is an American historian, author, and professor known for his work in American studies and the history of photography. He is particularly noted for his examinations of the cultural and social implications of visual media during the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Trachtenberg's scholarship has explored the interplay between photography, literature, and American identity, contributing to the understanding of how visual culture shaped public perception and historical memory. He has published several influential books and articles, and his academic career includes teaching positions at various universities, where he has influenced a generation of scholars in the fields of cultural and visual studies.

Pachymic acid is a naturally occurring steroid that can be extracted from the parasitic fungus Wolfiporia extensa (synonym Wolfiporia cocos). The dried sclerotia of the fungus is used as a traditional Chinese medicine, and pachymic acid is thought to be the principal bioactive component of it. Pachymic acid is a white powder that is not absorbed well by the body due the poor solubility it has in water.

Pachymic acid is a triterpenoid compound primarily derived from the Chinese medicinal mushroom Poria cocos, also known as Fu Ling. As a bioactive ingredient, it has been studied for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and anticancer properties. Research indicates that pachymic acid may influence immune function and exhibit neuroprotective effects. It is also explored for its possible role in traditional Chinese medicine as a diuretic and for promoting digestive health. Although further studies are needed to fully elucidate its mechanisms and therapeutic applications, pachymic acid remains of interest in pharmacological research and traditional herbal formulations.

Ruth Katrin Gemmell (born October 1967) is an English actress. She starred in the film Fever Pitch in 1997 which was followed by supporting roles in television series EastEnders, Casualty, Home Fires, and Penny Dreadful. She has played Carly Beaker, the mother of the title character in the Tracy Beaker franchise since 2004. In 2020, she began playing Violet, Dowager Viscountess Bridgerton in the Netflix series Bridgerton.

Ruth Gemmell is a British actress known for her work in television and film. She was born on 2nd January 1971 in Cardiff, Wales. Gemmell gained prominence for her role as Liz Harker in the television series "Bridgend" and has appeared in various popular British TV shows, including "The C Word," "Poldark," and "EastEnders." Her film credits include "The Last of the Blonde Bombshells" and "The Tesseract." Known for her versatile acting skills, Gemmell continues to contribute to both stage and screen performances.
