Max Lucks (born 19 April 1997) is a German politician of Alliance 90/The Greens who has been serving as a member of the Bundestag in the 20th Bundestag that has been elected in the German federal election on 26 September 2021. He was the spokesperson for Green Youth, the youth organisation of Alliance 90/The Greens, from October 2017 to November 2019. On 23 February 2025, he was re-elected to the Bundestag.

Max Lucks is a Swedish professional footballer who plays as a midfielder. Born on October 11, 1998, in Sweden, he began his career in youth football, developing skills at local clubs before moving to join a professional team. Lucks is known for his versatility on the field, being able to play both centrally and on the wings. He has been recognized for his technical abilities, tactical awareness, and physical stamina. Lucks has also represented his country at various youth levels, showcasing his potential on an international stage. Throughout his career, he has played in several domestic leagues, contributing to his team's successes with notable performances.
Nagayo Motoori (sometimes spelled Motohori) (本居 長世; 4 April 1885 – 14 October 1945) was a Japanese composer.

Nagayo Motoori (1818–1886) was a notable Japanese scholar, educator, and proponent of the Western sciences during the late Edo and early Meiji periods. Born in Kumamoto, he studied under prominent intellectuals and became a pivotal figure in the dissemination of Western knowledge in Japan. Motoori is particularly known for his efforts to modernize education by integrating Western methodologies into Japanese curricula. His work laid the groundwork for the establishment of a more science-oriented and pragmatic educational system in Japan, reflecting the broader socio-cultural transformations of the Meiji Restoration. Additionally, he contributed to various fields, including agronomy and engineering, influencing future generations of scholars and educators.

Admiral of the Fleet Sir Charles Edmund Nugent (c. 1759 – 7 January 1844) was a Royal Navy officer. He saw action as a junior officer in the 50-gun Bristol at the Battle of Sullivan's Island during the American Revolutionary War. He was held as a prisoner-of war for a day by Spaniards shortly before the Battle of San Fernando de Omoa later on in the War. Nugent served in the naval brigade in the invasions of Martinique, Saint Lucia, and Guadaloupe during the French Revolutionary Wars and, when William Cornwallis assumed command of the blockade of Brest, Nugent was selected to serve as his Captain of the Fleet during the Napoleonic Wars. He never commanded any fleet or naval station but did rise to the highest rank in the Navy.

Charles Edmund Nugent (1883–1950) was an American economic historian and educator known for his contributions to the field of economic history and his work in higher education. Born in Massachusetts, Nugent obtained his doctorate from Harvard University, where he developed a strong interest in the interplay between economy and societal structures. He served on the faculty of several universities, where he influenced a generation of scholars through his teaching and research. Nugent's work emphasized the importance of historical context in economic analysis, and he published numerous articles and books on topics such as labor history and the economic development of the United States. His scholarly legacy continues to be recognized within the field of economic history.

Omar Kachong Dallah (12 August 1936 – 1997) was a Hong Kong international lawn and indoor bowler and field hockey international. Appearing as a member of Hong Kong's 1964 Olympics hockey team, he later became the national team's captain and then coach. As a men's fours bowler, he and his teammates won gold in the 1978 Edmonton Commonwealth Games, and 1980 Melbourne World Outdoor Championships.

Omar Dallah is a prominent figure known for his contributions in the field of international diplomacy and conflict resolution. He has held various high-level positions in governmental and non-governmental organizations, focusing on peace-building initiatives in conflict-affected regions. Dallah has represented his country in multiple international forums and has been instrumental in fostering dialogue between opposing factions. His work emphasizes the importance of negotiation and consensus-building in achieving sustainable peace. In addition to his diplomatic career, he is an advocate for human rights and socio-economic development, working with local communities to address underlying issues that contribute to conflict. Dallah's efforts have earned him recognition as a key actor in modern diplomacy.
Vrtliško Lake is a part artificial, part natural lake of Bosnia and Herzegovina. It is located in the municipality of Kakanj.

Vrtliško Lake is a glacial lake located in the Julian Alps of Slovenia, near the border with Italy. It is situated at an elevation of approximately 1,150 meters above sea level and spans an area of around 6 hectares. The lake is known for its picturesque surroundings and vibrant turquoise waters, making it a popular destination for hikers and nature lovers. It is accessible via several trails and is often visited for its serene ambiance and scenic views. The area around Vrtliško Lake is rich in biodiversity and features a variety of flora and fauna typical of high-altitude alpine regions.

Jurgita Štreimikytė (born 14 May 1972 in Alytus, Lithuania) is a Lithuanian former basketball forward who played the WNBA for Indiana Fever and the Euroleague Women with Kaunas, Valenciennes Olympic and SG Comense among others. She was a member of the Lithuanian national team, winning the 1997 Eurobasket. She retired from international basketball following the 2006 World Championship. After retiring in 2010, she currently serves as Kaunas' second coach. On 30 November 2022, she was inducted to the FIBA Hall of Fame in appreciation of her stunning career contributions. Štreimikytė's husband is Dainius Virbickas, 1996 Olympic Lithuanian long-distance runner. They have one son.

Jurgita Štreimikytė is a Lithuanian professional basketball player, known for her achievements in both domestic and international competitions. Born on March 18, 1985, she has played for various clubs in the Lithuanian Women's Basketball League and has represented the Lithuanian national team. Štreimikytė is recognized for her versatility on the court, primarily playing as a forward. Throughout her career, she has contributed significantly to the development of women’s basketball in Lithuania and has been a role model for young athletes in the sport.

Lawrence Gavin Bertran Robinson (25 October 1935 – 2002) was a South African model agent in London's Old Bond Street in the 1960s and 70s who was part of the Swinging London scene. His show at Mare Moda in Capri in 1969 was described by The Times as "as zippy, as nippy and as hippy as anything seen in London's West End", while a show for Harrods in 1970 was noted for his technique of making his models move to music like machines.

Gavin Robinson is a prominent model agent and entrepreneur known for his work in the fashion industry. He gained recognition for his innovative approach to talent representation and scouting, particularly in the fields of modeling and commercial representation. Robinson has been instrumental in launching the careers of numerous models and has established a reputation for his keen eye for emerging talent. He has worked with various high-profile fashion brands and agencies, contributing to the evolution of industry standards in model management. Robinson continues to be an influential figure in fashion, advocating for diversity and inclusion within the modeling profession.
Darwin Eusebio Oliva García (born 21 March 1989) is a Honduran-Guatemalan retired footballer.

Darwin Oliva is a notable figure in the field of computer science, recognized for his contributions to software development and artificial intelligence. He has been involved in various projects focusing on machine learning algorithms and data analysis, showcasing a commitment to advancing technology in practical applications. Oliva is also known for his work in academia, where he has published research papers that address emerging trends in computational methodologies. His influence extends through mentorship roles, guiding aspiring computer scientists and fostering innovation in the tech community.

Rosalind Grimshaw BA FMGP (3 March 1945 - 11 November 2020) was a stained glass artist who lived in Clifton, Bristol looking towards the Clifton Suspension Bridge. Grimshaw had Parkinson's disease from her early 30s.

Rosalind Grimshaw (born 1963) is a British artist known for her innovative contributions to contemporary art, particularly in the fields of painting and installation. Her work often explores themes of identity, environment, and the integration of technology in art. Grimshaw has exhibited widely in galleries and museums across the UK and internationally, gaining recognition for her unique blend of traditional techniques and modern materials. She has been involved in various collaborative projects and has contributed to public art initiatives, emphasizing community engagement and environmental awareness in her artistic practice. Grimshaw's work is characterized by its vibrant color palette, intricate details, and conceptual depth.

Tisová (German: Tisau) a municipality and village in Ústí nad Orlicí District in the Pardubice Region of the Czech Republic. It has about 600 inhabitants. Tisová lies approximately 14 kilometres (9 mi) west of Ústí nad Orlicí, 34 kilometres (21 mi) east of Pardubice, and 130 kilometres (81 mi) east of Prague.

Tisová is a municipality and village located in the Ústí nad Orlicí District of the Pardubice Region in the Czech Republic. It is situated near the Moravian border and encompasses a small population. The village is characterized by its rural setting and is part of the historical context of the region. Tisová is known for its traditional Czech architecture and local community, and it serves as an example of the typical lifestyle found in smaller Czech municipalities. The surrounding area is primarily agricultural, and the village is connected to nearby towns and cities through regional transportation.
This is a list of ports of the North Sea and its influent rivers. Note: this list does not include ports on the Skagerrak, the Kattegat, or the English Channel.

The List of North Sea ports encompasses major maritime hubs located along the coasts of countries bordering the North Sea, including the United Kingdom, Norway, Denmark, Germany, the Netherlands, and Belgium. These ports are critical for trade, fishing, and shipping activities in the region, facilitating the movement of goods and passengers. Notable ports include Rotterdam, the largest in Europe; Antwerp, a key trading center; and Hamburg, a significant logistics gateway. The list highlights the strategic importance of these ports in connecting Europe to global markets and supports various industries, including energy, transportation, and tourism.
Alfred Seifert (6 September 1850 – 6 February 1901) was a Czech-German painter, acclaimed for his female portraits.

Alfred Seifert (1850–1919) was a prominent Czech painter and graphic artist known for his contributions to the art of the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Born in Prague, Seifert studied at the Academy of Fine Arts in Vienna and became associated with the landscape and genre painting movements. His works often depicted scenes of rural life and the Czech countryside, reflecting a deep appreciation for natural beauty and local culture. Seifert was also involved in various artistic societies, promoting Czech art and culture during a period of national awakening. His legacy continues to influence Czech art and he is remembered for his distinctive style and dedication to his homeland.

Prakash Chandra Sharma is an Indian politician of Indian National Congress and current member of Madhya Pradesh's 15th Vidhan Sabha, who was Cabinet Minister of Law and Legal Affairs Department, Public Relations Department, Science and Technology Department, Department of Civil Aviation, Department Associated with the Chief Minister of Madhya Pradesh Government of Madhya Pradesh during CM Kamal Nath Government (2018–2020). He represents Bhopal Dakshin-Paschim (Vidhan Sabha constituency) in Madhya Pradesh.

P. C. Sharma is an Indian academic and researcher known for his contributions to the fields of management and industrial engineering. He has published numerous research papers and articles in various national and international journals, focusing on topics such as operations research, supply chain management, and decision-making processes. Sharma has held various academic positions in respected institutions, contributing to curriculum development and teaching future leaders in the business world. His work is recognized for its practical applications in industry, bridging theoretical concepts with real-world challenges.
Bacchus O. J. Rolle is a Bahamian politician from the Progressive Liberal Party.

Bacchus Rolle (born September 6, 2000) is a professional footballer from the Bahamas who plays as a midfielder. Rolle began his career in the youth ranks of local clubs before moving to play collegiate soccer in the United States. He gained recognition for his performances in college tournaments, leading to his selection for the Bahamas national team. Rolle has since represented his country in various international competitions, showcasing his skills and contributing to the development of soccer in the Bahamas. His playing style is characterized by agility, vision, and a strong work ethic on the pitch.

Giovanni Raphael Frank Villa (January 29, 1873 – November 12, 1933), also known as "Count Villa" or "the Count," was an American football player, judge, and consular official. He played college football for Whitman College in 1893 and for the University of Michigan from 1893 to 1896. He was selected as a first-team All-Western player in 1895. He later became a consular official for Italy, a judge and gold prospector in Alaska, and a representative of the Great Northern Railroad. He also served in the U. S. Army during World War I.

Frank Villa is a prominent figure known for his contributions to the fields of art and design. Born in the mid-20th century, Villa gained recognition for his innovative approach, blending traditional techniques with modern aesthetics. His work spans various media, including painting, sculpture, and digital art. Villa has participated in numerous exhibitions worldwide, showcasing his distinctive style characterized by vibrant colors and intricate patterns. Throughout his career, he has received several awards and accolades, solidifying his status as a significant contemporary artist. In addition to his artistic pursuits, Villa is also involved in educational initiatives, mentoring young artists and promoting the importance of creativity in society.

Stoping is the process of extracting the desired ore or other mineral from an underground mine, leaving behind an open space known as a stope. Stoping is used when the country rock is sufficiently strong not to collapse into the stope, although in most cases artificial support is also provided. The earliest forms of stoping were conducted with hand tools or by fire-setting; later gunpowder was introduced. From the 19th century onward, various other explosives, power-tools, and machines came into use. As mining progresses the stope is often backfilled with tailings, or when needed for strength, a mixture of tailings and cement. In old mines, stopes frequently collapse at a later time, leaving craters or flashes at the surface. They are an unexpected danger when records of underground mining have been lost with the passage of time. Stoping is considered "productive work", and is contrasted with "deadwork", the work required merely to access the mineral deposit, such as sinking shafts and winzes, carving adits, tunnels, and levels, and establishing ventilation and transportation.

Stoping is a mining process used to extract ore from a vertical or steeply dipping deposit. It involves creating underground voids or stopes by removing ore, while leaving behind supporting rock structures to maintain stability. There are several methods of stoping, including cut and fill, room and pillar, and sublevel stoping, each suited for specific rock conditions and ore types. The choice of method depends on factors such as ore geometry, ground conditions, and economic considerations. Stoping is crucial in the underground mining industry and is designed to maximize ore recovery while minimizing environmental impact and operational costs.

Maransart (French pronunciation: [maʁɑ̃saʁ]; Walloon: Maransåt) is a Belgian town and district of the municipality of Lasne, Wallonia in the province of Walloon Brabant. The holotype of the extinct crocodilian Dollosuchoides was discovered here in 1915 and the Middle Eocene Brussels Formation, where Dollosuchoides was recovered from, is known to outcrop near Maransart.

Maransart is a village located in the municipality of La Hulpe in the province of Walloon Brabant, Belgium. It is situated about 20 kilometers southeast of Brussels and is known for its picturesque landscapes and tranquil residential environment. The village features a mix of historical architecture and modern development, with several notable buildings and local amenities. Maransart is part of the wider Sonian Forest area, contributing to its natural beauty and outdoor recreational opportunities. The region is characterized by a rural atmosphere, making it a popular destination for those seeking a quiet lifestyle near urban centers.

The Châteaux de Lastours (in Occitan Lastors) are four so-called Cathar castles in the French commune of Lastours in the département of l'Aude. The four castles are on a rocky spur above the village of Lastours, isolated by the deep valleys of the Orbeil and Grésilhou rivers. They were built at an altitude of 300 m along a rock wall just 1,300 ft (400 m) long by 165 ft (50 m) wide. Cabaret, Surdespine and la Tour Régine stand in line, while Quertinheux is built on a separate pinnacle close by. The site has been classified monument historique (historic monument) by the French Ministry of Culture since 1905 and archaeological digs are still in progress. These four castles constitute a single entity, even though they are not a single structure. The natural layout of the site permitted the economy of a fortress of great height. Plans were adapted to the rocks on which they were built. The construction of each is different reflecting the range of alterations made to the castles.

The Châteaux de Lastours are a group of four medieval castles located in the Aude department of southern France, near the village of Lastours. Constructed during the 12th century, these fortifications were built atop rocky peaks overlooking the Orbiel Valley, serving strategic military purposes. The castles, known as Cabaret, Surdespine, Quertinheux, and las Cels, are notable examples of Cathar strongholds, reflecting the region's historical significance during the Albigensian Crusade. The site is now a popular tourist destination, offering panoramic views and insights into the medieval architecture and history of the Cathar resistance. The Châteaux de Lastours were classified as historical monuments in 1905 and continue to be an emblem of the rich cultural heritage of the Occitan region.

Ronnie Robinson (March 9, 1951 – May 8, 2004) was an American basketball player. Robinson played high school basketball at Memphis Melrose High School, where one of his teammates was his future college and professional teammate, Larry Finch. Robinson played college basketball in his hometown at Memphis State University. Robinson, Finch and Larry Kenon led Memphis State to the 1973 Final Four; the Tigers lost to the undefeated, Bill Walton-led UCLA Bruins in the Finals. Affectionately nicknamed "The Big Cat" for his leaping ability, Robinson is one of eight University of Memphis Tigers basketball players to have his jersey number retired by the school. A 6'8", 225 pound forward, Robinson finished his college career prior to the ABA-NBA merger and thus was selected in the fourth round of the 1973 NBA draft by the Phoenix Suns and in the first round of the 1973 ABA Draft by the Utah Stars. Robinson opted for the American Basketball Association and began the 1973–74 season with the Stars. In January, the Memphis Tams traded Johnny Neumann to Utah for Robinson, Glen Combs, Mike Jackson and cash. With the Stars and Tams throughout the 1973–74 season, Robinson appeared in 62 games, averaging 6.4 points and 4.5 rebounds per game. Robinson spent the 1974–75 season with the Memphis Sounds. With the Sounds, Robinson appeared in only 10 games, averaging 4.0 points and 2.7 rebounds per game.

Ronnie Robinson is a retired American professional basketball player, known for his athleticism and scoring ability. Born on February 14, 1963, in Chicago, Illinois, he played college basketball at the University of Illinois before entering the professional realm. Robinson had a brief stint in the National Basketball Association (NBA), playing for the Philadelphia 76ers during the 1986-1987 season. He also enjoyed a successful career in international leagues and the Continental Basketball Association (CBA). After retiring from professional basketball, Robinson has been involved in coaching and mentoring young athletes. His contributions to the game and his impact on the sport continue to be recognized by fans and players alike.

Phags-pa is a Unicode block containing characters from the 'Phags-pa script promulgated as a national script by Kublai Khan, the founder of the Yuan dynasty. It was used primarily in writing Mongolian and Chinese, although it was intended for the use of all written languages of the Mongol Empire.

The Phags-pa Unicode block is a set of characters encoded in the Unicode standard, specifically within the range U+1700 to U+171F. It was added to Unicode in version 6.0, released in October 2010. The block includes characters used for writing the Phags-pa script, which was developed in the 13th century by the Tibetan scholar Drogön Chogyal Phags-pa. This script was designed to write various languages, mainly Mongolian, as part of an effort to create a unified writing system for the Mongol Empire. The Phags-pa block supports historical texts, research, and revitalization efforts related to the script and the languages it represents.

Six Californias was a proposed initiative to split the U.S. state of California into six states. It failed to qualify as a California ballot measure for the 2016 state elections due to receiving insufficient signatures. Venture capitalist Tim Draper launched the measure in December 2013. He spent in excess of $5 million trying to qualify the proposition for the ballot, with nearly $450,000 for political consultants. Had the measure passed, it would not have legally split California immediately; consent would eventually need to be given by both the California State Legislature and the U.S. Congress to admit the new states to the union per Article IV, Section 3 of the U.S. Constitution. Rather, the measure would have established several procedures within the state government and its 58 counties to prepare California for the proposed split, and instructed the Governor of California to submit the state-splitting proposal to Congress. The proposed states would have been named Jefferson, North California, Silicon Valley, Central California, West California, and South California. Draper's stated reasoning for the proposal was that the state is too large and ungovernable, and he therefore wanted to split California to produce six smaller and more efficient state governments. Opponents argued that it would have been a waste of money and resources to split California and create these new governments. Critics also charged that this was a money and political power grab designed to separate California's higher-income communities from lower-income areas, and to diminish the state's reliability as a predominantly Democratic Party-supporting "blue state".

Six Californias was a proposed political initiative to divide the state of California into six separate states. Introduced by venture capitalist Tim Draper in 2014, the plan aimed to address issues such as governance, representation, and resource management by creating smaller, more manageable states, each with its own government. The proposed states were named North California, South California, West California, East California, Central California, and Silicon Valley. Draper and supporters argued that this division would result in more tailored and effective governance. However, the initiative faced significant opposition and legal hurdles, ultimately failing to qualify for the ballot in the 2016 election. The proposal highlighted discussions regarding California's size, diversity, and political complexity.

Truck Yarō (トラック野郎, Torakku Yarō; lit. "Truck Guys" or "Truck Rascals") is a series of ten Japanese action comedy films produced by Toei and released from 1975 to 1979. All ten films in the series were directed by Norifumi Suzuki and starred Bunta Sugawara as Momojiro Hoshi ("Ichibanboshi" or "first star") and Kinya Aikawa as Kinzo Matsushita, also known as "Jonathan". The title Torakku Yaro means "truck guys" or "truck rascals", and the films involve two truckers and their various escapades as they travel around Japan in highly decorated trucks. The plot formula is similar to the Otoko wa Tsurai yo films. Each time Momojiro falls in love with a woman (the "Madonna") and then ends up having to help her romance with another man. The stories end with Momojiro having to race his truck to meet a deadline to rejoin the couple.

"Torakku Yarō" is a Japanese film franchise that centers around the lives of truck drivers, often depicting the challenges and camaraderie within the profession. The series, which began in the late 1970s, combines elements of action, drama, and comedy, showcasing the trucking culture in Japan. The titular character, typically portrayed as a tough but good-hearted driver, faces various trials on the road, including encounters with rival truckers, police, and personal dilemmas. Over the years, "Torakku Yarō" has become a cultural icon within the Japanese entertainment industry, spawning numerous sequels, television adaptations, and merchandise, and highlighting themes of friendship, loyalty, and the spirit of the open road.
Aira cupaniana (Silver Hairgrass) is a species of annual herb in the family True grasses. They have a self-supporting growth form and simple, broad leaves. Individuals can grow to 13 cm.

Aira cupaniana is a species of flowering plant belonging to the genus Aira in the family Poaceae, commonly known as the grass family. It is native to regions in Europe and Western Asia, typically found in grasslands and other open habitats. This perennial grass is characterized by its slender form and delicate flowers, which emerge in feathery, panicle-like inflorescences. Aira cupaniana is often used in ornamental gardening due to its aesthetic appeal, and it plays a role in local ecosystems as a habitat for various fauna. The species has been studied for its ecological requirements and adaptation to different environmental conditions.
Jan Wyżykowski (born 31 March 1917 in Haczow, died 29 October 1974 in Warsaw) was a Polish geologist, educated as a mining engineer, a specialist in the geology of copper ore deposits.

Jan Wyżykowski (1860–1928) was a Polish politician and military officer, known for his significant contributions to the Polish independence movement in the early 20th century. He was actively involved in organizations that aimed to promote Polish nationalism and self-determination, particularly during World War I. Wyżykowski also served in various political roles following Poland's regaining of independence in 1918, focusing on the development of local governance and social reforms. His legacy includes efforts to support education and civic engagement in the newly re-established Polish state.

Sir Arthur George Warner (31 July 1899 – 3 April 1966) was an English-born Australian businessman and politician. He was born in London to manager Arthur Warner and Emily Cheeseman. He attended Sir George Monoux Grammar School and worked as a telephone mechanic before studying science at the University of London. During World War I he served in the Royal Navy and the Royal Flying Corps, and following the war he migrated to Australia and became a soldier settler in Scottsdale in Tasmania. He soon moved to Melbourne and, having qualified by correspondence, worked as an accountant. On 14 August 1920, he married Ethel Wakefield, with whom he had two sons. From 1926, he ran the Radio Corporation which, by 1934, was the largest radio manufacturing firm in the country. In 1934, he applied for the first Australian television licence. In 1938, he founded Electronic Industries Ltd, which, as the manufacturer of the Astor range of products, dominated Australia's radio market in the 1930s. In 1946, he was elected to the Victorian Legislative Council for Higinbotham Province, as an Independent Liberal, defeating the official Liberal Party candidate. He joined the Liberal Party soon after and, in 1947, was appointed Minister of Housing and Materials. He added the State Development Portfolio from 1948 to 1949, and Electrical Undertakings from 1949 to 1950, when the Liberal government was defeated. From 1951, he led the Liberals in the Legislative Council and was a supporter of Henry Bolte, as opposed to Thomas Hollway, in the party's leadership instability of the time. When the Liberals were returned to government under Bolte in 1955, Warner was Minister of Transport until his resignation from the front bench in 1962. Created a Knight Bachelor in 1956, he was elevated to Knight Commander of the Order of the British Empire in 1962. Warner retired from politics in 1964 and died at Seymour in 1966. He is the father of Peter Warner, the captain of a fishing boat who, in 1966, rescued six Tongan castaways on ʻAta Island, and later went on to win the line honours three times in the Sydney to Hobart yacht race.

Arthur Warner (1870–1955) was an American businessman and entrepreneur best known for his contributions to the automotive industry. He was a co-founder of the Warner Brothers Air Brake Company, which played a significant role in developing pneumatic brake systems for trains and later expanded into automotive applications. Warner's innovations in engineering and manufacturing led to advancements in safety and efficiency in transportation. In addition to his work in the automotive sector, he was involved in various philanthropic efforts, particularly in education and community development. His legacy includes both his contributions to technical advancements and his commitment to social responsibility.

In Islamic tradition, Darda'il (Arabic: دردائيل "Journeyers of God") are angels that travel in the earth searching out assemblies where people remember God's name. An angel named Darda'il is also invoked in exorcism. An angel named Darda'il is also present in Yazidism.

Darda'il, also known as the "guardians" or "watchers," is a term used in Islamic eschatology to refer to a group of beings who possess knowledge and understanding of the unseen. Traditionally associated with the concept of the Antichrist, the Darda'il are believed to be instrumental in spreading falsehood and confusion among people before the Day of Judgment. Islamic texts often depict them as entities that will emerge during the end times to lead astray those who lack firm faith. Their role serves as a reminder of the importance of discernment and steadfastness in belief amidst trials and tribulations.

Hobart is a village in Delaware County, New York, United States. The population was 441 at the 2010 census. The village is in the town of Stamford and is on New York Route 10 in the northeastern part of the county. Since 2005, Hobart has had multiple book stores and has become known as the "Book Village of the Catskills."

Hobart is a village located in Delaware County, New York, United States. Incorporated in 1886, it is situated at the junction of the Catskill and Delaware Rivers and is part of the town of Stamford. The village covers an area of approximately 1.2 square miles and had a population of 353 as of the 2020 census. Hobart is known for its historic buildings, including several that reflect the architectural styles of the 19th century. The village is often referred to as the "Book Village of the Catskills" due to its numerous bookstores and literary events. Hobart is primarily served by New York State Route 30, providing access to surrounding communities and natural attractions.

The Basque Studies Society (Basque: Eusko Ikaskuntza; 'EI-SEV' ) is a scientific-cultural institution created in 1918 by the Provincial Councils of Álava, Vizcaya, Guipúzcoa and Navarra a stable and lasting resource to develop the Basque culture ". The members of this entity are gathered in different Scientific Sections. It is the only institution of different scientific disciplines that has official implantation in the Basque Country, Navarra and the French Basque Country, awarding, among others, the Manuel Lekuona Award. Personalities from the cultural and scientific world such as Jose Migel Barandiaran were part of its executive cadres, being a member of the Permanent Board of the Society for Basque Studies from 1921 to 1936. The headquarters are at Miramar Palace in San Sebastián, with offices and delegations in Bayona, Pamplona, Vitoria and Bilbao. This congress was chaired by Alfonso XIII of Spain and the whole body of the Navarra Provincial Council attended.

The Basque Studies Society (Sociedad de Estudios Vascos), established in 1960, is an academic organization dedicated to the research and promotion of Basque culture, history, language, and society. Based in the Basque Country and affiliated with various scholarly institutions, the society serves as a platform for researchers, scholars, and educators interested in Basque studies. It organizes conferences, publishes academic journals and books, and encourages interdisciplinary collaboration across fields related to Basque heritage. The society plays a crucial role in preserving and enhancing the understanding of Basque identity and its contributions to regional and global contexts.

The Vaajanvirta, also spelled as Vaajavirta, is a 3.9-kilometre (2.4 mi) long river in the Vaajakoski area of Jyväskylä, Finland. It begins from the lake Leppävesi and discharges into Lake Päijänne. The river's basin has a surface area of 17,668 square kilometres (6,822 sq mi), which includes many major Central Finnish lakes, such as Lake Keitele. The river is part of the Keitele Canal's route, which connects the lakes Päijänne and Keitele. The Finnish national road 4 and the Jyväskylä–Pieksämäki railway also cross the river.

Vaajanvirta is a strait located in Finland, connecting the waters of the Suurijärvi and the Pyhäselkä lake. It is situated in the municipality of Rautavaara in Northern Savonia. The strait serves as an important navigation route for local boat traffic and is characterized by its natural scenery, including forested shores and diverse wildlife. The area around Vaajanvirta is popular for recreational activities such as fishing, boating, and hiking, contributing to the local economy and tourism. Its ecological significance and picturesque landscape make it a notable feature in the region.

The Church of Our Saviour, also known as the Church of Our Saviour at Mission Farm, the Mission of the Church of Our Saviour, and the Josiah Wood Jr. Farm, is a historic Episcopal church and farm complex located at 316 Mission Farm Road, in Killington, Vermont. The church is a Gothic Revival stone building, built in 1894-95 of Vermont granite. In addition to the church, the 170-acre (69 ha) Mission Farm property includes a c. 1817 farmhouse, a guest and retreat house, a vicarage, a bakery and agricultural buildings. On October 29, 1992, it was added to the National Register of Historic Places. The Rev. Lisa Ransom is the Executive Director of Mission Farm and Vicar of The Church of Our Saviour. The church reported 11 members in 2019 and 54 members in 2023; no membership statistics were reported in 2024 parochial reports. Plate and pledge income reported for the congregation in 2024 was $35,831 with average Sunday attendance (ASA) of 19 persons.

The Church of Our Saviour is an Episcopal church located in Killington, Vermont. Established in 1990, it serves as a spiritual center for the local community and visitors to the area, particularly in the ski resort town of Killington. The church features a unique architectural design that incorporates elements of traditional New England ecclesiastical style. It offers a range of services and events, including regular worship, community outreach programs, and seasonal celebrations. The Church of Our Saviour is part of the Diocese of Vermont and aims to foster a welcoming environment for people of all backgrounds.

The League of Combatants (Portuguese: Liga dos Combatentes), originally established as the League of Combatants of the Great War (Portuguese: Liga dos Combatentes da Grande Guerra), is a Portuguese ex-service organisation established following the First World War. A private institution with public utility status, it is answerable to the Ministry of National Defence.

The League of Combatants is a fictional organization featured in various media, typically known for assembling skilled fighters, warriors, or superheroes to engage in combat for purposes ranging from competition to combatting evil. Often depicted in video games, comic books, and animated series, the League serves as a backdrop for storylines centered on rivalry, teamwork, and personal growth through martial challenges. Members usually possess unique abilities or combat skills, and the narratives often explore themes of honor, rivalry, and the consequences of violence. The organization may also include notable tournaments or battles that attract spectators and participants from various backgrounds, contributing to the larger universe's lore and character development.

The 2014–15 Duke Blue Devils women's basketball team represented Duke University during the 2014–15 NCAA Division I women's basketball season. Returning as head coach was Joanne P. McCallie entering her 8th season. The team played its home games at Cameron Indoor Stadium in Durham, North Carolina as members of the Atlantic Coast Conference. They finished the season 23–11, 11–5 in ACC play to finish in a tie for fourth place. They advanced to the semifinals of the ACC women's tournament, where they lost to Notre Dame. They received at-large bid of the NCAA women's tournament, where they defeated Albany in the first round, Mississippi State in the second round before losing to Maryland in the sweet sixteen.
topic: 2014–15 Duke Blue Devils women's basketball team

text: The 2014–15 Duke Blue Devils women's basketball team represented Duke University during the 2014–15 NCAA Division I women's basketball season. The team was led by head coach Joanne P. McCallie, marking her seventh season with the program. The Blue Devils played their home games at Cameron Indoor Stadium and were members of the Atlantic Coast Conference (ACC). The team finished the regular season with a record of 27–6 and secured a share of the ACC regular season title. In the ACC tournament, they advanced to the semifinals. The Blue Devils earned a No. 4 seed in the NCAA tournament, where they reached the Sweet Sixteen before being eliminated by the University of Oregon. The squad was highlighted by standout players such as Elizabeth Williams and Jewel Moore, contributing to a successful season both in conference and national competition.
topic: 2014–15 Duke Blue Devils women's basketball team

text: Rope's End, Rogue's End is a 1942 detective novel by E.C.R. Lorac, the pen name of the British writer Edith Caroline Rivett. It is the twenty first in her long-running series featuring Chief Inspector MacDonald of Scotland Yard, a Golden Age detective who relies on standard police procedure to solve his cases. It takes the form of the country house mystery, popular during the era.

"Rope's End, Rogue's End" is a term used to refer to a specific cultural concept or metaphor, often associated with themes of finality, danger, or the end of a journey. The phrase can evoke imagery related to perilous situations or moral dilemmas, typically found in literature, art, and media. In nautical contexts, "rope's end" refers to the loose end of a rope, often signifying a point of no return or a critical moment in navigating life’s challenges. The dual usage underscores the tension between adventure and the risks accompanying it, reflecting a broader narrative about choices and consequences in various storytelling traditions. While not a formal title of a specific work, the phrase has appeared in various artistic expressions, capturing human experiences related to risk, adventure, and resolution.
HMS Liberty (J391) was a steam turbine-powered Algerine-class minesweeper during the Second World War. She survived the war and was sold to Belgium in 1949 as Adrien de Gerlache (M900).

HMS Liberty (J391) was a Royal Navy minesweeper of the Halcyon class, launched in 1942 and commissioned during World War II. Designed primarily for mine clearance operations, the vessel was characterized by its relatively small size and versatility. After serving in various theaters of the war, including the Atlantic and Mediterranean, HMS Liberty was decommissioned post-war. The ship played a significant role in naval operations, contributing to the safety of allied shipping lanes. Following her service, she was ultimately sold for scrap in the early 1960s.

The Davidov Stradivarius (also: Davidoff or Davydov; Russian: Давыдов) is an antique cello made in 1712 by luthier Antonio Stradivari of Cremona, Italy. It is very similar in construction and form to the equally famed Duport Stradivarius, built a year earlier and played by Mstislav Rostropovich until his death in 2007. The varnish is of a rich orange-red hue, produced with oil color glazes. Its owners have included Karl Davydov and Jacqueline du Pré, and it is currently used by cellist Yo-Yo Ma.

The Davidov Stradivarius is a renowned violin crafted by the Italian luthier Antonio Stradivari in 1714. Named after its former owner, the notable violinist and collector David Davidov, the instrument is celebrated for its exceptional craftsmanship and sound quality. It is characterized by its rich tonal properties and striking visual aesthetics, typical of Stradivari's work during the Golden Period. The violin has changed hands multiple times since its creation and is considered one of the finest examples of Stradivari's artistry, contributing to its high value in the world of classical music and instrument collection.

Banaras is a 2022 Indian Kannada-language romance film written and directed by Jayatheertha and produced by Tilakraj Ballal. It stars Zaid Khan and Sonal Monteiro in the lead roles. The film's music composer is B. Ajaneesh Loknath and cinematographer is Advaitha Gurumurthy. The film was set to be released early 2021 but was postponed due to COVID-19 pandemic, It was released on November 4, 2022.

"Banaras" is a 2022 Indian Hindi-language romantic drama film directed by Srajit Mukherjee. It stars Zaid Darbar and Sonal Monteiro in the lead roles, alongside a supporting cast featuring notable actors. The film is set in the spiritual city of Varanasi and explores themes of love, loss, and the search for meaning in life against the backdrop of the city's rich cultural and religious heritage. Featuring a soundtrack that complements its emotional narrative, "Banaras" received mixed to positive reviews from critics for its performances and cinematography, while also being noted for its portrayal of the socio-cultural dynamics of the location. The film aims to resonate with audiences through its relatable storyline and visual storytelling, reflecting the essence of Varanasi.

Margaret Cairns Munns (1870–1957) was an American pioneer school teacher of Washington State and a temperance reformer. She served as Treasurer of the National Woman's Christian Temperance Union (WCTU) (1915–46) and later, as Treasurer of the World's WCTU (1925–53). Closely allied to her contribution to the work of the WCTU was the interest which Munns manifested in parliamentary procedure.

Margaret Cairns Munns (1924–2023) was an Australian botanist and environmentalist renowned for her contributions to the study of plant ecology and conservation. Born in Queensland, Munns studied botany at the University of Queensland, where she developed a keen interest in the flora of Australia. Her research significantly advanced the understanding of plant response to stressors such as salinity and drought, leading to innovative practices in agriculture and environmental management. Munns authored numerous scientific papers and played a pivotal role in various conservation initiatives, promoting the sustainable use of Australia's unique plant resources. She was recognized with several awards throughout her career, including the Australian Prime Minister's Prize for Science. Munns remains an influential figure in the field of botany and is remembered for her dedication to preserving Australia's biodiversity.

A-Welle, more formally known as the Tarifverbund A-Welle or sometimes the Tarifverbund Aargau, is a Swiss tariff network covering the canton of Aargau, with the exception of the districts of Laufenburg and Rheinfelden, together with the eastern part of the canton of Solothurn. The Tarifverbund A-Welle was created on 12 December 2004, when the previous tariff associations for Olten and Aargau merged. Initially the tariff network applied only to season tickets and passes, but it was expanded on 13 December 2009 to include single tickets as well as day and multi-trip tickets.

A-Welle is a German electronic music project formed in 1985 by composer and producer Peter W. Siedlaczek and musician Michael D. S. Möller. Known for their eclectic style that blends elements of techno, ambient, and new wave, A-Welle gained popularity in the late 1980s and early 1990s through their innovative soundscapes and use of synthesizers. The project is recognized for its contributions to the electronic music scene and has released several albums and singles, earning a dedicated following. A-Welle's music often features ethereal melodies, driving beats, and a fusion of acoustic and electronic instruments, reflecting the artistic experimentation characteristic of the genre during that era.
The women's team time trial of the 1994 UCI Road World Championships cycling event took place on 21 August 1994 in Palermo, Italy. The course was 50.2 km long.
topic: 1994 UCI Road World Championships – Women's team time trial

text: The Women's team time trial at the 1994 UCI Road World Championships was held on October 13, 1994, in Oslo, Norway. This event featured teams of professional female cyclists competing against the clock over a set distance. The race attracted national teams from around the world, showcasing the growing popularity and competitiveness of women's cycling. The championship was notable for its challenging course and the emergence of strong female athletes on the international stage. The event helped to elevate the profile of women's cycling and contributed to the development of team time trial competitions for women in the sport.
topic: 1994 UCI Road World Championships – Women's team time trial

text: Marshall Bolds Champlain (December 22, 1824, in Stafford, Genesee County, New York – March 7, 1879, in Cuba, Allegany County, New York) was an American lawyer and politician.

Marshall B. Champlain (1910-2008) was an American geographer known for his contributions to the fields of physical geography and environmental studies. He was a professor at several universities and published extensively on topics such as land use, climate change, and the relationship between human activity and the environment. Champlain was recognized for advocating the importance of geography in understanding societal challenges and environmental issues. His work helped to shape modern geographic thought, emphasizing the interplay between natural landscapes and human development. Champlain's legacy includes mentoring future generations of geographers and contributing to policy discussions related to environmental conservation.

The Sandhu or Sindhu clan (Punjabi: ਸੰਧੂ (Gurmukhi); سندھو (Shahmukhi)) is the second-largest clan of Jats in the Punjab, spanning both India and Pakistan. The Sandhus have had a significant impact on Sikh history, founding several Sikh states, including the Nakai Misl, Shaheedan Misl, and Kanhaiya Misl.

Sandhu is a common Indian surname predominantly found among the Punjabi Sikh community, though it is also used by people from various Indian regions. The name is believed to derive from the Sanskrit word "Sandhu," meaning "the one who has a knowledge of the law." It has historical roots linked to the fortified villages inhabited by the Sandhu clan, which has contributed to their significant presence in agriculture and military service. Notable personalities with the surname Sandhu include politicians, athletes, and cultural figures, contributing to the name's prominence in contemporary society. Additionally, Sandhu is sometimes used as a given name. The surname has geographical significance, often associated with regions in Punjab, India, and Pakistan, reflecting the migration patterns of Punjabis across the world.

The Mallyan Spout Hotel is a historic hotel in Goathland, a village in North Yorkshire, in England. The hotel was designed by James Demaine and Walter Brierley and was completed in 1892. Between 1932 and 1935, part of the building was demolished and rebuilt, and the hotel was extended. Patrick Nuttgens states that "the interior has been altered here and there but the whole of the front block is unmistakeable Brierley, very comfortable, solid and warm". In 2022, the building suffered some damage in a fire, which started in its laundry room. It has been grade II listed since 1989. It is in sandstone, the extension is in red brick fronted in sandstone, and it has overhanging bracketed eaves, and tile roofs with coped gables and raised kneelers. There are two storeys and four bays, with attics over the middle two bays. The porch has an elliptical-arched doorway with a chamfered surround, spandrels with a monogram and the date, and a hood mould. The windows are mullioned, to the left is a two-storey canted bay window with a cornice, the ground floor windows with transomed, and in the attic are gabled half-dormers. The extensions have similar features.

Mallyan Spout Hotel is a historic hotel located in the North Yorkshire village of Goathland, England. Originally established in the 19th century, the hotel is named after the nearby Mallyan Spout waterfall, a popular natural attraction. The hotel features traditional architecture and offers amenities such as dining services, guest accommodations, and local tourism information. Mallyan Spout Hotel serves as a base for visitors exploring the scenic North Yorkshire Moors National Park and is known for its picturesque surroundings and proximity to hiking trails. The establishment has been recommended for its charm and has a reputation for providing a quaint and cozy atmosphere for guests.

There Goes Susie (U.S. title for 1935 release: Scandals of Paris) is a 1934 British comedy film directed by Victor Hanbury and John Stafford and starring Gene Gerrard, Wendy Barrie, and Zelma O'Neal. Written by Charlie Roellinghoff and Hans Jacoby, it was made by British International Pictures at Elstree Studios. It is a remake of the 1933 German film Marion, That's Not Nice. An Italian version, Model Wanted (1933), was also made.

"There Goes Susie" is a 1946 American musical comedy film directed by Charles Barton and starring Johnnie Davis, soundly marking a celebration of the post-World War II era. The film revolves around an optimistic character, Susie, who pursues her dreams and aspirations amidst various comedic obstacles. Known for its lively musical numbers and engaging performances, the film reflects the cultural sentiments of its time, combining humor and romance with a lighthearted narrative. "There Goes Susie" showcases the talent of its cast and contributes to the genre of musical comedies prevalent in mid-20th-century Hollywood.

Tressignaux (French pronunciation: [tʁesiɲo]; Breton: Tresigne; Latin: Trisignala) is a commune in the Côtes-d'Armor department in the Brittany region in northwestern France. The Chapel of Saint-Antoine, located in the village, was built in the thirteenth century and rebuilt in the sixteenth. Each year, a "pardon" is celebrated on the 3rd Sunday in August, attracting visitors to the village.

Tressignaux is a commune in the Côtes-d'Armor department in the region of Brittany, northwestern France. It is part of the arrondissement of Guingamp and the canton of Bégard. The commune is characterized by its rural landscape and historical architecture, including traditional Breton houses and a few notable churches. Tressignaux has a small population and offers a glimpse into the regional culture and heritage of Brittany, with local customs and events reflecting its historical roots. The economy of the area is primarily based on agriculture and local services.

David S. Eisenberg (born 15 March 1939) is an American biochemist and biophysicist best known for his contributions to structural biology and computational molecular biology. He has been a professor at the University of California, Los Angeles since the early 1970s and was director of the UCLA-DOE Institute for Genomics & Proteomics, as well as a member of the California NanoSystems Institute (CNSI) at UCLA.

David Eisenberg is a prominent American biophysicist and professor known for his research in protein folding and structure. He is a faculty member at the University of Massachusetts Amherst, where he has contributed significantly to the field of molecular biology and biophysics. Eisenberg's studies have enhanced the understanding of amyloid fibrils and their association with neurodegenerative diseases. He is recognized for his interdisciplinary approach, integrating concepts from chemistry, biology, and physics. Eisenberg's work has led to numerous scientific publications and accolades, establishing him as a significant figure in biophysical research.

"How Much Love" is a popular song from 1977 by the British singer Leo Sayer, who co-wrote the song with Barry Mann. It was the third of three single releases from Sayer's 1976 album, Endless Flight. The song became an international Top 10 hit. Although it reached only number 17 on the U.S. Billboard Hot 100, it hit number 9 on the U.S. Cashbox Top 100, and number 10 in the UK. It was a bigger hit in Canada, where it peaked at number 4 and was the 57th biggest hit of 1977. It also reached the Top 10 on the Canadian AC chart. Chicago radio station WLS, which gave "How Much Love" airplay, ranked the song as the 62nd biggest hit of 1977. It reached as high as number 5 on their weekly singles survey.

"How Much Love" is a song by British-Australian singer-songwriter Leo Sayer, released in 1977 as part of his album "Endless Flight." The track features a blend of pop and soft rock elements, characterized by Sayer's distinctive vocal style. It became a notable hit, reaching high positions on various music charts, including the US Billboard Hot 100. The song showcases themes of love and emotional longing, which are common in Sayer's work. "How Much Love" contributed to Sayer's popularity in the late 1970s and remains one of his signature songs.
Daik (Jawi: دائق‎; Chinese: 大一; pinyin: Dàyī) is the main village (kelurahan) on the island of Lingga of Lingga Regency, in the Riau Islands in Indonesia. It is located at 0°12′0″N 104°37′0″E.

Daik is a term that can refer to various concepts or entities depending on the cultural or contextual background. In some contexts, it may refer to a type of weight measurement in certain Asian countries, while in others, it can denote a specific character or figure in folklore. For instance, in Japanese, "daik" might be associated with traditional festivals or rites. Additionally, it may also be a surname or a given name in various cultures. The specific meaning of "Daik" can vary widely, reflecting the rich diversity of language and culture across different regions.

Nizhny Odes (Russian: Ни́жний О́дес; Komi: Улыс Одес, Ulys Odes) is an urban locality (an urban-type settlement) under the administrative jurisdiction of the town of republic significance of Sosnogorsk in the Komi Republic, Russia. As of the 2010 Census, its population was 9,680.

Nizhny Odes is a rural locality (a selo) in the Nizhny Odesky District of Kherson Oblast, Ukraine. It is situated within the southern region of the country, near the Dnieper River. The village is characterized by its agricultural economy and is part of a predominantly rural area. Nizhny Odes has a small population, contributing to its tight-knit community atmosphere. Historically, it has been affected by the broader socio-political changes in Ukraine, especially during the 20th century and into the present day, as the region navigates issues related to governance and territorial integrity.

The National Reform Movement (Kurdish: Bizûtinewey‌ Chaksazi Nishtimani lit. National Reform Movement) is a Kurdish political party in the Kurdistan Region of Iraq. The party was founded and is led by Berun Omar Fatah, who established it on September 1, 2014, in Sulaymaniyah, Kurdistan Region of Iraq.

The National Reform Movement (NRM) in Kurdistan is a political initiative that emerged in the semi-autonomous Kurdistan Region of Iraq in the mid-2000s. Founded primarily to advocate for democratic reforms and increased transparency within the Kurdish political system, the NRM seeks to address issues such as corruption, inefficiency, and the need for good governance. The movement emphasizes the importance of national unity among Kurds and promotes social, economic, and political rights. The NRM has gained support among various segments of Kurdish society, particularly among youth and intellectuals, and aims to challenge the dominance of established political parties in the region. Through a combination of grassroots activism and political engagement, the NRM strives to foster a participatory political culture and enhance civil society's role in governance.

Clive Khulubuse Zuma is a South African businessman, and the nephew of the former President, Jacob Zuma. Zuma was named in the Panama Papers leak as a result of his links to oilfields in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC). Shortly after President Zuma, his uncle, met with DRC president Joseph Kabila, Zuma's company Caprikat Limited secured a R100 billion rand oil deal in the DRC. In 2014, he got engaged to Swazi princess, Fikisiwe Dlamini.

Khulubuse Zuma is a South African businessman and the nephew of former South African President Jacob Zuma. Born in 1974, he is known for his involvement in various business ventures, primarily in the mining and energy sectors. Zuma gained significant public attention in 2010 when he was implicated in the controversial dealings of the now-defunct mining company, Aurora Empowerment Systems, which was criticized for its management practices and financial difficulties. Additionally, he has been associated with various philanthropic initiatives, although his business dealings have attracted scrutiny and criticism over alleged mismanagement and corruption. Khulubuse Zuma remains a controversial figure within South Africa's political and business landscape.

Sergei Afanasyevich Kolosov (Russian: Серге́й Афанасьевич Колосов; born May 22, 1986) is a Belarusian former ice hockey defenceman. He last played with Generals Kyiv of the Ukrainian Hockey League. Kolosov was drafted by the Detroit Red Wings of the National Hockey League (NHL) in 2004, and signed a contract with the team on June 25, 2008. He played for the Belarusian national team at the 2008, 2010, and 2011 World Championships, as well as the 2010 Winter Olympics.

Sergei Kolosov (born January 15, 1985) is a Russian professional football player who plays as a midfielder. He began his career in the early 2000s in the Russian football leagues and has played for various clubs throughout his career, primarily in the Russian Football National League and lower divisions. Kolosov is known for his technical skills, vision, and versatility on the field. Although he has not achieved significant international recognition, he is respected at the club level for his contributions and performance in domestic competitions.

Julius Mac Leod (19 February 1857 – 3 March 1919), was a Belgian biologist and professor at the University of Ghent. His father was of Scottish descent. Mac Leod was born in Ostend. He was also director of the botanical garden. Julius Mac Leod was an active member of the Flemish movement and a proponent of the usage of Flemish at the University of Ghent. He died in Ghent during the Spanish flu epidemic of 1918–1919.

Julius Mac Leod (born June 6, 1990) is an American professional basketball player known for his versatility on the court, playing primarily as a guard. He played college basketball at the University of Miami, where he gained recognition for his scoring ability and defensive skills. Following his collegiate career, Mac Leod entered the professional circuit and has played in various international leagues, showcasing his talent in multiple countries. Mac Leod is noted for his athleticism, work ethic, and contributions to team dynamics, earning him a reputation as a reliable player in his positions.
The Japanese eagle ray (Myliobatis tobijei), also known as the cowhead eagle ray or kite ray is a ray species in the family Myliobatidae.

The Japanese eagle ray (Mylobatis granulatus) is a species of eagle ray native to the coastal waters of Japan, particularly in the Sea of Japan and the Pacific Ocean. Characterized by its distinctive diamond-shaped body and long, slender tail, this ray can reach a wingspan of up to 1.5 meters (5 feet). It primarily inhabits shallow, sandy or muddy substrates and is known for its adaptability to various marine environments. The Japanese eagle ray is a carnivorous species, feeding mainly on benthic invertebrates such as crustaceans and mollusks. While it is currently not considered endangered, habitat degradation and fishing pressures pose potential threats to its populations. The species is also of cultural significance in Japan, where it is occasionally featured in local cuisine.

Downloading Nancy is a 2008 drama film directed by Johan Renck, starring Maria Bello and Jason Patric. It is loosely based on the death of Sharon Lopatka, who sought out someone who would torture her to death. The film premiered at the 2008 Sundance Film Festival, where it was nominated for a Grand Jury Prize.

Downloading Nancy is a 2008 American drama film directed by Lee Wilkof. The story centers on Nancy, a woman unhappy in her life who seeks solace through an online relationship with a troubled man. The film explores themes of loneliness, desperation, and the complexities of human connection in the digital age. Starring Robin Wright, Jason Patric, and Stephen McHattie, it received mixed reviews from critics, with praise for the performances but criticism for its narrative execution. The film raises questions about identity and emotional vulnerability in the context of modern technology.

The Roman theatre of Philippopolis (Latin: Theatrum Trimontense; Bulgarian: Пловдивски античен театър, Plovdivski antichen teatar) is one of the world's best-preserved ancient Roman theatres, located in the city center of modern Plovdiv, Bulgaria, once the ancient city of Philippopolis. It was constructed in the 1st century AD, probably during the reign of Domitian. The theatre can host between 5,000 and 7,000 spectators and it is currently in use.

The Roman theatre of Philippopolis, located in the ancient city of Philippopolis in present-day Plovdiv, Bulgaria, is a well-preserved example of Roman architecture and theatre design. Constructed during the 1st century AD, the theatre could accommodate approximately 5,000 spectators and was used for various performances, including drama, music, and other entertainment. Its significance lies in its architectural features, such as the tiered seating, stage building, and the impressive use of the natural landscape for optimal acoustics and sightlines. Rediscovered in the mid-20th century, the site has been restored and remains a prominent archaeological and cultural landmark, hosting events and performances that celebrate the heritage of ancient Roman civilization. The Roman theatre is part of the broader archaeological heritage of Plovdiv, which is recognized for its historical significance in the context of both Roman and Bulgarian history.

The Founding Ceremony of the Nation (or The Founding of the Nation) is a 1953 oil painting by Chinese artist Dong Xiwen. It depicts Mao Zedong and other Chinese Communist Party (CCP) officials proclaiming the People's Republic of China at Tiananmen Square on October 1, 1949. A prominent example of socialist realism, it is one of the most celebrated works of official Chinese art. The painting was repeatedly revised, and a replica painting made to accommodate further changes, as some of the leaders it depicted fell from power and later were rehabilitated. After the Chinese Communist Revolution, the Party sought to memorialize their achievements through artworks. Dong was commissioned to create a visual representation of the October 1 ceremony, which he had attended. He viewed it as essential that the painting show both the people and their leaders. After working for three months, he completed an oil painting in a folk art style, drawing upon Chinese art history for the contemporary subject. The success of the painting was assured when Mao viewed it and liked it, and it was reproduced in large numbers for display in the home. The 1954 purge of Gao Gang from the government resulted in Dong being ordered to remove him from the painting. Gao's departure was not the last; Dong was forced to remove then-Chinese president Liu Shaoqi in 1967. The winds of political fortune continued to shift during the Cultural Revolution, and a reproduction was painted by other artists in 1972, to accommodate another deletion. The replica was modified in 1979 to include the purged individuals, who had been rehabilitated. Both canvases are in the National Museum of China in Beijing.
topic: The Founding Ceremony of the Nation

text: The Founding Ceremony of the Nation refers to the formal event marking the establishment of a sovereign state, often characterized by the celebration of independence, the adoption of a constitution, or the declaration of a government. These ceremonies typically include speeches by key leaders, cultural displays, and various public festivities. The specific customs and traditions can vary widely depending on the nation’s history, culture, and values. Such ceremonies often serve to unify citizens, foster national pride, and commemorate historical milestones, establishing a narrative of national identity and continuity. Famous examples include the signing of the Declaration of Independence in the United States and the proclamation of the Republic in numerous other countries.
topic: The Founding Ceremony of the Nation

text: Whitney Way Thore (born April 14, 1984) is an American television personality and subject of the reality show My Big Fat Fabulous Life, which airs on TLC.

Whitney Way Thore is an American television personality, dancer, and social media influencer, best known for her reality TV series "My Big Fat Fabulous Life," which premiered on TLC in 2015. Born on April 14, 1984, in Greensboro, North Carolina, Thore gained prominence for her advocacy of body positivity and for challenging societal norms around weight and beauty. She began her career in dance at a young age and pursued a professional career in the arts. Thore is also recognized for her online presence, where she shares insights into her life, including her experiences with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and her journey toward self-acceptance. Through her work, she has become a prominent voice in discussions about body image and mental health.
Shmull is a Palauan surname. Notable people with the surname include: Moy Shmull, Palauan spearfisher Temmy Shmull (born 1947 or 1948), Palauan politician Yukio Shmull (died 2018), Palauan politician

Shmull is a term often associated with a variety of informal or playful contexts, primarily used in linguistic or cultural discussions to denote whimsical or nonsensical elements. While not formally recognized in many dictionaries, "shmull" can occasionally be found in colloquial speech as a term of endearment or to describe a clumsy or silly action. Its origins may relate to Yiddish linguistic influences, where similar-sounding terms express affection or joviality. The usage of "shmull" is typically context-dependent, and it has appeared in various media, including literature and informal conversations, reflecting a lighthearted or humorous tone.
Xu Gong () (died 200) was a Chinese politician and minor warlord of the late Eastern Han dynasty of China.

Xu Gong (徐恭) was a prominent Chinese scholar and official during the Eastern Han dynasty, known for his contributions to Confucian studies and his role in the administration. He served in various governmental positions, where he advocated for moral governance and the promotion of education. Xu Gong is also recognized for his efforts in compiling and editing classical texts, thereby influencing the preservation of Confucian philosophy. His works and teachings emphasized the importance of ethics in leadership and the cultivation of virtue among officials. His legacy continues to be studied in the context of Chinese intellectual history.
Zaroslovo (Russian: Зарослово) is a rural locality (a village) in Kovarditskoye Rural Settlement, Muromsky District, Vladimir Oblast, Russia. The population was 13 as of 2010. There are 4 streets.

Zaroslovo is a rural locality (a village) situated in the Vologda Oblast region of Russia. It is part of the Vashkinsky District and falls within the administrative jurisdiction of the Kholodnikovskoye Rural Settlement. As of the 2010 census, Zaroslovo had a small population of approximately 10 residents. The village is characterized by its serene rural environment and is primarily engaged in agriculture and related activities. Its geographic coordinates place it within the northern part of Russia, reflecting the region's typical features of a temperate continental climate.

The Rochester Opera House is a 750-seat theater in the city hall of Rochester, a city in Strafford County, New Hampshire, United States. It has the unique architectural feature of a fully moveable floor, perhaps the last of this design in existence.

The Rochester Opera House is a historic performing arts venue located in Rochester, New Hampshire. Originally built in 1908, it has undergone multiple renovations and restorations to preserve its architectural features and enhance its functionality. The venue hosts a diverse range of events, including concerts, theatrical performances, and community events, serving as a cultural hub for the local community. It has a seating capacity of around 700 and is known for its intimate atmosphere and acoustic quality. The opera house is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, reflecting its significance in the region's cultural heritage.

Memorrhage is the debut studio album by Memorrhage, a nu metal project by American musician Garry Brents (born October 4, 1987). Brents has also made music with the bands Gonemage, Homeskin, Cara Neir, Sallow Moth, and Prabstruse, amongst many others, since 2004. He also collaborated with Vision Eternel between 2010–2012. A variety of styles span these projects, including: black metal, death metal, grindcore, screamo, post-rock, and electronic music.

Memorrhage is a cognitive impairment condition characterized by significant disruptions in memory processes, including encoding, storage, and retrieval of information. It can involve various types of memory, such as short-term, long-term, and working memory. The condition may result from various factors including neurological disorders, traumatic brain injuries, psychological conditions, or substance abuse. Symptoms can include forgetfulness, inability to concentrate, and difficulties recalling past events. Diagnosis often involves neuropsychological assessments and imaging techniques to identify underlying causes. Treatment options may include cognitive rehabilitation, therapy, and medications aimed at improving cognitive function. Memorrhage can have a profound impact on an individual's daily life, affecting personal relationships and occupational performance.

Nossa Senhora do Rosario church (Portuguese for Our Lady of the Rosary) is a 15th century church in the town of Cidade Velha on the island of Santiago, Cape Verde. It is located in the northwest of the town, near the right (west) bank of the stream Ribeira Grande de Santiago. The historic centre of Cidade Velha is an UNESCO World Heritage Site since June 2009.
topic: Nossa Senhora do Rosário church (Cidade Velha)

text: Nossa Senhora do Rosário Church is a historic Roman Catholic church located in Cidade Velha, the oldest part of Praia, the capital of Cape Verde. Originally built in the 16th century, it is one of the oldest churches in the archipelago and reflects the colonial architecture of the period. The church is dedicated to Our Lady of the Rosary and has undergone several renovations and restorations over the years. It features a blend of Gothic and Baroque elements, with significant interior artwork and altarpieces. Nossa Senhora do Rosário Church is a key religious site and a cultural landmark, attracting both tourists and local worshippers.
topic: Nossa Senhora do Rosário church (Cidade Velha)

text: I'm Going to Live by Myself (Italian: Vado a vivere da solo) is a 1982 Italian comedy film. It marked the feature film debut of Marco Risi. The film achieved a "cult" status and generated a 2008 sequel, Torno a vivere da solo.
topic: I'm Going to Live by Myself

text: "I'm Going to Live by Myself" is a 2021 autobiographical novel by the Chinese author Xiaolu Guo. The narrative explores themes of solitude, independence, and personal growth as it follows the journey of a woman navigating life in a modern urban environment. The protagonist grapples with her identity, relationships, and the transition to living alone, reflecting on her experiences as a migrant in a foreign country. The book is noted for its lyrical prose and introspective style, capturing the complexities of contemporary life and the challenges of self-discovery. Guo's work has been praised for its emotional depth and cultural insights, contributing to discussions about feminism and the immigrant experience.
topic: I'm Going to Live by Myself

text: Arboridia is a genus of true bugs belonging to the family Cicadellidae. The genus was first described by Zachvatkin in 1946. The species of this genus are found in Europe and Northern America. Species: Arboridia adanae (Dlabola, 1957) Arboridia kermanshah (Dlabola, 1963) Arboridia parvula (Boheman, 1845)

Arboridia is a fictional setting within fantasy literature and role-playing games, characterized by its lush, arboreal landscapes and diverse ecosystems. Typically inhabited by various humanoid and non-humanoid species, Arboridia integrates aspects of nature with magical elements, often emphasizing themes of environmental stewardship and the balance between civilization and wilderness. The setting features sprawling forests, towering trees, and intricate tree-dwelling communities, and frequently serves as a backdrop for adventures centered on quests, exploration, and the conservation of magical flora and fauna. Arboridia has been employed by various authors and game creators as a rich tapestry for storytelling, allowing for exploration of themes such as harmony with nature, the consequences of industrialization, and the interconnectedness of all living beings.

Bedřich Hamsa (born 25 October 1965) is a Czech former football player. He played in the Czechoslovak First League for Slavia Prague and Bohemians Prague. Hamsa later played for Drnovice in the Czech First League, scoring four times in 26 matches in its inaugural season. He was top goal scorer in the 1994–95 Czech 2. Liga, scoring 22 goals for LeRK Brno that season. He subsequently played in the Czech 2. Liga for Teplice and Ústí nad Labem.

Bedřich Hamsa (born December 27, 1875, in Nové Strašecí, Czech Republic; died June 26, 1958) was a notable Czech painter and graphic artist associated with the modernist movement. Hamsa studied at the Academy of Fine Arts in Prague, where he developed his distinctive style characterized by vibrant colors and dynamic compositions. His work often explored themes of nature and the human form, reflecting both realism and abstraction. Throughout his career, Hamsa exhibited extensively in Czechoslovakia and abroad, becoming a significant figure in the Czech art scene. In addition to painting, he contributed to illustration and printmaking, influencing several generations of artists. His legacy is preserved in various collections and museums across the Czech Republic and beyond.

Maclear Island is part of the Great Barrier Reef Marine Park at the tip of Cape Melville, Queensland in Bathurst Bay. It is north of Denham Island in the Flinders Group National Park and south of Flinders Island, at Latitude -14.210 and Longitude 144.250. It is named after John Maclear.

Maclear Island is a small, uninhabited island located in the South Orkney Islands, part of the Southern Ocean off the coast of Antarctica. It is situated approximately 106 kilometers (66 miles) northeast of Coronation Island and is known for its rugged terrain and icy conditions. The island is named after the British astronomer Sir Thomas Maclear, who conducted significant work in astronomy and navigation during the 19th century. Maclear Island is primarily notable for its ecological significance, providing a habitat for various seabird species and marine life. The surrounding waters are often frequented by seals and whales, making it a site of interest for biological and environmental research.
Grab Hands and Run is a fictional adaptation of a true story written by Frances Temple. The book is written at a fourth-grade level aimed at audiences aged 8 to 13.

"Grab Hands and Run" is a children's picture book written and illustrated by David L. Harrison. Published in 2010, the book features a playful narrative that encourages young readers to explore themes of friendship and adventure through vivid illustrations and engaging rhymes.
