The story follows various characters as they embark on imaginative journeys, promoting the importance of creativity and connection among peers. Harrison’s distinctive style and rhythmic prose aim to captivate and inspire children, making it a popular choice for early literacy and storytelling sessions.

The area features a list of listed buildings, which are structures deemed of special architectural or historical interest. These buildings are categorized based on their importance, with classifications that include Category A (buildings of national importance), Category B (buildings of regional importance), and Category C (buildings of local importance). The listed buildings in Ferry-Port-On-Craig reflect the village's heritage, encompassing various architectural styles and periods, including residential, commercial, and civic structures. The preservation of these buildings is overseen by local authorities and heritage organizations, aiming to maintain the character and history of the village.

text: The Activist is a 1969 American drama film directed by Art Napoleon and written by Art Napoleon and Jo Napoleon. It depicts the conflicts of a college student who, after appearing on television news decrying the Vietnam War, is torn between engaging in further anti-war protests and settling for conventional happiness with his new girlfriend. The film stars Michael Smith, who had been an actual member of the Oakland Seven, an anti-war group involved in the planning of the 1967 Stop the Draft Week. Co-star Lesley Taplin also alternated between community activism and acting at the time of production. Also starring in the film are Tom Maier and Brian Murphy. Also, the parents of filmmaker Michael Ritchie, Benbow Ritchie and Patricia Ritchie, briefly appear as a suburban couple upset by the lead character when he appears in their neighborhood. The film received an X rating due to a graphic (for the time) love scene between Smith and Taplin. It was released on December 10, 1969, by Universal Pictures' shadow company Regional Film Distributors, a division created to handle films either receiving an X rating or deemed too esoteric for the studio to be associated with.

"The Activist" is a 1969 American drama film directed by John Frankenheimer. The story follows a socially conscious political activist, portrayed by the actor James Garner, who becomes embroiled in the tumultuous landscape of the 1960s civil rights movement. The film addresses themes of political engagement, social justice, and the personal sacrifices made in the pursuit of activism. While it features a strong performance by Garner, the film received mixed reviews upon release and is often noted for its reflection of the era's cultural and political climate. The screenplay was co-written by Larry Cohen, and the film has been analyzed for its portrayal of activism and entertainment in a time of significant social upheaval.

The small five-toed jerboa (Scarturus elater) is a rodent of the family Dipodidae and genus Scarturus, that has five digits. They are hopping rodents of the rocky deserts in Asia. They have been found in Afghanistan, Armenia, Azerbaijan, China, Georgia, Iran, Kazakhstan, Pakistan, Russia, Tajikistan, Turkey, and Turkmenistan. They have long hind feet, short forelimbs, and walk upright. The jerboa body length ranges from 5–15 cm and has a tail ranging from 7–25 cm. They have large ears in comparison to their body size and a large tail. The tail assists and serves as support when the jerboa is standing upright. These hopping rodents can reach a speed up to 48 km/h. The forelimbs of the jerboa serve as a pair of hands for feeding, grooming, etc. The male jerboa is usually larger in size and weight in comparison to the female jerboa. The pelt of the jerboa is either silky or velvety in texture and light in color, the coloration helps camouflage into surroundings to avoid predators. "Its coloration varies from sandy or buff to dark russet or black with pale under parts and a white strip on the hip”.

The small five-toed jerboa (Allactaga elater), a member of the family Dipodidae, is a species of hopping rodent found in the arid regions of Central and North Asia, particularly in Mongolia and parts of northeastern China. Characterized by its elongated hind legs and long tail, this nocturnal animal is adept at jumping and burrowing, helping it avoid predators and conserve water in its harsh desert environment. The small five-toed jerboa primarily feeds on seeds and plant matter. Its habitat is typically comprised of sandy or loose soil, allowing for easy movement and construction of nesting burrows. Their population trends have been influenced by habitat degradation and climate change, raising concerns about conservation.
The white-crested tyrannulet (Serpophaga subcristata) is a small species of bird in the family Tyrannidae, the tyrant flycatchers. It is found in Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Paraguay, and Uruguay.

The white-crested tyrannulet (Serpophaga subcristata) is a small passerine bird in the tyrant flycatcher family, Tyrannidae. It is native to Central and South America, predominantly found in open woodlands, savannas, and edge habitats. The species is characterized by its distinct white crest, olive-brown upperparts, and pale underparts with a yellowish tint. The white-crested tyrannulet primarily feeds on insects and other small invertebrates, capturing them in flight. It is known for its sharp, high-pitched call, which is often heard in its natural habitat. The bird exhibits a range of behaviors typical of tyrant flycatchers, including frequent perching and vigilant observation of its surroundings. Despite habitat loss in some areas, the white-crested tyrannulet is currently not considered threatened.

Graham Barnfield (born 5 November 1969 in Leicester) is a British academic and pundit associated with the hard left Revolutionary Communist Party (1981–1997). In 1993 he began writing on cultural politics in the United States under President Franklin D. Roosevelt. Barnfield also comments on documentary representation, leading to some radio and television appearances discussing reality television and happy slapping. He teaches journalism at the University of East London. He is the former editor of Culture Matters: Communications, Media & Communities (Sheffield Hallam University Press) and was a 2003 Fellow of the Wolfsonian-FIU. He received a BA (Hons) in English with media studies, from the University of Sussex in 1992, and a PhD in cultural studies with the dissertation Co-opting Culture: State Intervention in and Party Patronage of Literary and Popular Culture, 1929–1941, from Sheffield Hallam University in 1996. As a teenager, he was a vocalist in Leicester rock band the Marmite Sisters. He briefly developed a sideline as a bit-part actor, including a feature credit in Number One Longing, Number Two Regret and now writes for a wide range of publications.

Graham Barnfield is a British sociologist and academic known for his work in the fields of cultural studies, political theory, and digital media. He has contributed to the understanding of the impact of digital technologies on social interactions, public policy, and cultural expression. Barnfield's research often intersects with issues of identity, power dynamics, and the implications of technological advances for contemporary society. He has published numerous articles and books, and his work is influential in bridging theoretical discussions and practical applications in social sciences. In addition to his academic contributions, Barnfield is actively engaged in public discourse regarding technology and social change.

Color Health, Inc. (founded as Color Genomics) is an American healthcare company focused on cancer care delivery. The company operates a Virtual Cancer Clinic that provides services in cancer prevention, detection, screening, diagnosis, and survivorship care.

Color Health is a health technology company focused on providing accessible genomic and diagnostic testing services. Founded in 2015 by Othman Laraki and his team, Color Health aims to empower individuals with genetic insights to improve their health outcomes. The company's flagship product is a genetic testing kit that analyzes various health risks and traits, offering consumers personalized health information and recommendations. Additionally, Color Health partners with healthcare organizations and employers to deliver population health management solutions, including initiatives for preventive care and chronic disease management. Their services are designed to make genomic data more interpretable and actionable for both individuals and healthcare providers.

Ride is the first EP by British rock band Ride and their debut, released in January 1990. It was in the UK singles charts for four weeks and was the first Creation Records label release to enter U.K. Top 75. “Ride” EP was combined with its follow-up Play to form the Smile mini-album for the US market in July, 1990. Two years later, in November, 1992, Smile was released in the UK, too, after both EPs had gone out of print. In November 2022, all four songs from “Ride” EP were made available on ‘‘4EPs’’, a compilation of Ride’s first four EPs made available together for the first time and packaged as one gatefold album on white vinyl and in CD format. It charted at number 78 on the UK Albums Chart.

"Ride" is an extended play (EP) by the American rock band Twenty One Pilots, released on November 21, 2015. It features a blend of alternative rock and hip hop elements, showcasing the band's signature style. The EP includes the title track "Ride," which received significant airplay and contributed to the band's rise in popularity. Thematically, the songs explore concepts of identity, existentialism, and introspection. "Ride" also reflects the band's broader artistic vision, bridging their previous works and setting the stage for their subsequent album, "Blurryface." The EP was well-received by critics and fans alike, further establishing Twenty One Pilots as a prominent act in contemporary music.
Stephen Olin (March 2, 1797 – August 15, 1851) was an American educator and minister.

Stephen Olin (1797–1879) was an American educator and Presbyterian minister, notable for his role in the establishment of several educational institutions. Born in Pennsylvania, he graduated from Washington College in 1816 and later pursued theological studies. Olin served as a pastor and held significant academic positions, including president of the University of the Pacific and the United States Military Academy. He was influential in promoting higher education in the United States, particularly in the field of liberal arts. Olin is also remembered for his writings on education and religion, contributing to the discourse of his time. His legacy includes a commitment to the integration of faith and learning in educational settings.

M. R. Ry. Chempakaraman Padmanabhan Kesava Pillai of Kandamath, Pillai of Kandamath (1830–1924), was an Indian Zamindar, one of the jenmis of Travancore and a member of the Sree Moolam Popular Assembly.

Kesava Pillai of Kandamath was a prominent figure in the socio-political landscape of Kerala, India, during the late 19th and early 20th centuries. He was a social reformer, educator, and a significant contributor to the advancement of the Nadar community. Pillai established various educational institutions that aimed to uplift marginalized communities and promote educational opportunities. He was also involved in the struggle against social injustices and caste discrimination, advocating for the rights of the underprivileged. His legacy is marked by his efforts to foster social equity and improve the educational status of his community, making him an influential personality in Kerala's reform movements.

Sylvia Frühwirth-Schnatter (born 21 May 1959) is an Austrian statistician and professor of applied statistics and econometrics at the Vienna University of Economics and Business. She is known for her research in Bayesian analysis. In 2020 she was the President of the International Society for Bayesian Analysis.

Sylvia Frühwirth-Schnatter is an Austrian statistician known for her contributions to Bayesian statistics and econometrics. She is particularly recognized for her work in the areas of Bayesian modeling, Markov Chain Monte Carlo methods, and structural equation modeling. Frühwirth-Schnatter has published extensively in academic journals and is a professor at the Vienna University of Economics and Business. She has also been involved in various research collaborations and initiatives aimed at advancing statistical methodologies and applications in social sciences. Her expertise has made her a prominent figure in the field of statistics, influencing both theoretical and applied research.

Henry Rose Carter (August 25, 1852 – September 14, 1925) was an American physician, epidemiologist, and public health official who served as assistant surgeon general of the Public Health Service Commissioned Corps. His research and protocols were critical in understanding and preventing the transmission of both malaria and yellow fever. Carter was born in Virginia in 1852. After attending the University of Virginia and the University of Maryland medical school, he joined the Marine Hospital Service (MHS). He was stationed at various MHS hospitals across the South, where he became interested in yellow fever. In 1888, he was dispatched to Mississippi's Ship Island, where he spent a decade developing novel quarantine methods. In a 1898 study conducted in Mississippi, Carter discovered the extrinsic incubation period of yellow fever. This work implied the role of a secondary host, soon identified as the mosquito by U.S. Army physician Walter Reed. Carter served as director of hospitals in the Panama Canal Zone from 1904 to 1909. In 1915, he was appointed to assistant surgeon general by Congress. Carter retired in 1920 and died five years later. With Reed and Carlos Finlay, Carter is regarded as one of the three researchers who helped identify yellow fever as mosquito-borne. Alongside Finlay, Carter was nominated for the 1904 Nobel Prize in Medicine for their work identifying the mosquito vector of yellow fever. Carter is also considered the father of modern quarantine.

Henry Rose Carter (1852–1925) was an influential American educator and author known for his contributions to the field of education, particularly in the context of promoting moral and character development among students. He served as a principal and later as a superintendent in various schools, where he emphasized the importance of integrating ethical education into academic curricula. Carter was also an advocate for educational reform, seeking to improve teaching methodologies and accessibility to quality education for all children. His writings, which include essays and books on education and morality, reflect his belief in the transformative power of education to shape virtuous and responsible citizens. His legacy continues to influence educators committed to holistic approaches in teaching.

Raymond L. Daniels Jr. (September 27, 1933 – July 5, 2017) was an American film editor. Daniels is best known for his work on The Streets of San Francisco, Starsky & Hutch, Hawaii Five-O, and Hill Street Blues. He had an extended collaboration (1981–1985) with showrunner Steven Bochco and edited the pilots for Hill Street Blues and L.A. Law. Daniels was nominated for seven Emmy Awards.

Ray Daniels is a film editor known for his contributions to mainstream cinema, particularly in the 1980s and 1990s. He gained recognition for his work on various feature films, collaborating with prominent directors and bringing a distinctive style to the editing process. Daniels has been involved in a range of genres, from action to drama, and has received acclaim for his ability to enhance narrative pacing and emotional impact through precise cuts and transitions. His expertise has made him a respected figure within the film editing community, and he has earned nominations for several industry awards during his career.
Rod Sarich (born March 23, 1981) is a Canadian-British former professional ice hockey defenceman.

Rod Sarich is a former professional Australian rules footballer known for his career in the Australian Football League (AFL). He was born on 8 November 1982 in Perth, Western Australia. Sarich played as a defender and made his debut in 2002 with the Fremantle Dockers, where he spent the entirety of his AFL career until his retirement in 2011. Throughout his tenure, he was recognized for his strong defensive skills and contributions to the team. Following his retirement, Sarich transitioned to coaching and community engagement within Australian rules football, focusing on youth development and participation in the sport.

Ghazi Amanullah Khan Barakzai (1 June 1892 – 26 April 1960) was Emir of Afghanistan from 1919 to 1926, and then King of Afghanistan from 1926 until his abdication in 1929. After the end of the Third Anglo-Afghan War in August 1919, Afghanistan was able to relinquish its protected state status to proclaim independence and pursue an independent foreign policy free from the influence of the United Kingdom. His rule was marked by dramatic political and social change, including attempts to modernize Afghanistan along Western lines. He did not fully succeed in achieving this objective due to an uprising by Habibullah Kalakani and his followers. On 14 January 1929, Amanullah abdicated and fled to neighbouring British India as the Afghan Civil War began to escalate. From British India, he went to Europe, where after 30 years in exile, he died in Zürich, Switzerland on 26 April 1960. His body was brought to Afghanistan and buried in Jalalabad near his father Habibullah Khan's tomb.

Amanullah Khan (1892–1960) was the King of Afghanistan from 1919 to 1929, notable for his efforts to modernize the country and promote social reforms. Ascending to the throne after Afghanistan gained independence from British influence in the Third Anglo-Afghan War, he implemented progressive measures, including constitutional reforms, women's rights, and educational improvements. His reign was marked by conflicts, including a backlash against his reforms and resistance from traditionalist factions. In 1929, Amanullah was deposed during a popular revolt led by Habibullah Kalakani, and he subsequently went into exile. He later attempted to regain power but was unsuccessful. Amanullah Khan remains a controversial figure in Afghan history, revered by some for his modernization attempts and criticized by others for his authoritarian style of governance. He died in 1960 in Switzerland.

Machias is a town in Cattaraugus County, New York, United States. The population was 2,309 at the 2020 census. Machias is an interior town in the northeast quadrant of the county. It is northeast of the city of Salamanca.

Machias is a town located in Cattaraugus County, New York, United States. It was established in 1820 and named after Machias, Maine. The town encompasses a total area of 39.6 square miles, featuring a mix of rural landscapes and small communities. Machias is situated in the southwestern part of New York and includes the hamlet of Machias as its primary settlement. The region is characterized by agriculture and recreational opportunities, with local landmarks such as the Cattaraugus Creek. According to the 2020 census, the population of Machias was approximately 1,320 residents. The town is served by several highways, facilitating access to neighboring areas.

La Esperanza Airport (IATA: LEZ, ICAO: MHLE) is an airport serving the cities of La Esperanza and Intibucá in the Intibucá Department of Honduras. The airport is 1.6 kilometres (1 mi) south of La Esperanza. There is rising terrain northwest and southeast of the runway. The Soto Cano VORTAC (Ident: ESC) is located 32.7 nautical miles (60.6 km) east of the airport.

La Esperanza Airport (IATA: EHL, ICAO: MHLE) is a small public airport located near La Esperanza, the capital of the Intibucá department in Honduras. The airport serves as an essential transportation hub for the region, facilitating the movement of passengers and cargo. It features a single asphalt runway and primarily accommodates regional flights. The airport is significant for contributing to the local economy and providing access to remote areas of the country. Despite its modest size, La Esperanza Airport plays a vital role in enhancing connectivity within Honduras.

The Netkous or Fishnets is the popular name for the tram bridge in The Hague district Bezuidenhout built between 2004 and 2006. The viaduct is along the Prinses Beatrixlaan. An important part of light rail is the coupling of the Hague tram to the Zoetermeer City Line (formerly part of the NS). To make this possible, it was decided to build a branch of the Hague tram elevated between Ternoot to the railway station Laan van NOI, where it connects to the existing line. In the business area Beatrixkwartier to improve access, a station was built halfway along the viaduct. Before construction could begin the first station Ternoot was moved a few hundred meters, otherwise, there would be no room for the curve CS Beatrixlaan. Both the viaduct and the integrated station were designed by Zwarts & Jansma Architects. The overpass is built up of an open tubular structure, which is reminiscent of a fishnet stocking. Due to this strong construction are relatively large spans possible and the number of columns can remain limited. The viaduct segments are assembled on site, prefabricated steel rings and tubes. To do this, a temporary assembly was built on the route. In a special conserving hall (composed of shipping containers) the viaduct parts were provided with a protective coating. The viaduct is ridden by light rail 3 and 4. A similar fishnet structure applied in 2014 at the Railway bridge over the Dieze and The Royal Welsh Bridge in 's-Hertogenbosch, and is applied to the complete 2016 terminal of the Rotterdam metro line E between Slinge and The Hague Central.

Netkous is a term that has been used in various contexts, primarily associated with online networks and communities. It often refers to specific internet subcultures or groups that engage in collaborative or competitive activities over digital platforms. The term has evolved as internet technology and social media have grown, adapting to represent collective online behaviors, trends, or phenomena. Various interpretations may include specific online games, forums, or social movements, highlighting the dynamic and fluid nature of digital interactions. As the internet continues to develop, the meanings and associations of "netkous" may further change, reflecting the ongoing evolution of online communication and community building.

Shari Shattuck (born November 18, 1960) is an American actress and author. Shattuck was born in Atlanta, Georgia. She has appeared in hundreds of commercials, TV, film, and stage productions. Some roles include Dallas, Life Goes On, On Deadly Ground as well as multiple Shakespearean roles. She appeared in both nighttime and daytime soaps, sitcoms, mini-series and starred in multiple films. As a model, she appeared on the cover of Playboy in April 1980, and numerous other magazines. In 1982, while working as a model in Atlanta, she was cast in the music video of the .38 Special song "Caught Up in You".

Shari Shattuck is an American actress and author known for her roles in television and film during the late 1980s and 1990s. She gained recognition for her work on soap operas, particularly as Ashley Abbott on "The Young and the Restless." In addition to her acting career, Shattuck has written several novels and is noted for her contributions to the literary world. Her diverse talents have led her to both on-screen and off-screen success, establishing her as a multifaceted figure in the entertainment industry.

Ikom is a Local Government Area of Cross River State in South-South, Nigeria. Its headquarters are in the town of Ikom in the east of the area on the Cross River and the A4 highway at 5°57′40″N 8°42′39″E. It has an area of 1,961 km2 (757 sq mi) and had a population of 162,383 according to the 2006 census. The people of Ikom are mostly farmers. Ikom is a major producer of cocoa, bananas and plantains for the Nigerian market. Ikom is known for the Afi monoliths, artifacts that bear an ancient writing sometimes taken to be an early form of nsibidi. The postal code of the area is 551. Ikom Local Government is in the Central Senatorial District of Cross River State. There are 11 (eleven) council wards: Abanyum Yala-Nkum Olulumo Ofutop I Ofutop II Nta/Nselle Nde Abijinkpor Ikom Urban Akparabong Nnam The present geographical entity called Ikom local government area dates back to the 16th century during the pre-colonial era when the area extended to some communities now enclosed in Boki, Obubra, Abi, and Etung local government areas. Like other typical traditional societies, Ikom was governed by Chief priests or Village heads who were both ceremonial and religious. Modern day Ikom began as a constituent part of the Afikpo Division in the early 1950s, when it was together with Afikpo now in Ebonyi State And in 1976, Ikom local government was created with headquarters at Ikom. Ikom with a large size of 1,861.926 square kilometers is bounded on the North by Ogoja on the North-East by Boki, on the East by Etung Local Government, and South by Obubra Local Government Area. The major languages of the people include Ofutop Bakor, Yala, Mbembe and Ejagham. Ikom people are said to have originated from Bantu stock and their major occupation include farming trading and lumbering.

Ikom is a town located in the Cross River State of Nigeria, near the border with Cameroon. It serves as the administrative center of the Ikom Local Government Area. Ikom is known for its diverse cultural heritage, predominantly inhabited by the Efik and Ejagham ethnic groups. The town is notable for its agricultural activities, particularly the production of cassava, yam, and palm oil, which are vital to the local economy. Additionally, Ikom is recognized for its traditional art forms and craft, including wood carving and pottery. The geographical landscape around Ikom features lush forests and rivers, contributing to its rich biodiversity. Ikom is also home to historical sites and serves as a gateway to attractions in the Obudu Plateau region.
Epoch is a space combat simulator for the Apple II written by Larry Miller and published by Sirius Software in 1981.

Epoch is a strategy video game developed by SLG Games and released in 2020. Set in a post-apocalyptic world, players control a faction and engage in resource management, base building, and combat against rival factions. The game features a turn-based combat system and a narrative-driven campaign mode, alongside multiplayer options that allow for competitive play. Epoch is known for its detailed graphics, immersive gameplay mechanics, and deep lore, attracting fans of the strategy genre. The game received generally positive reviews for its engaging gameplay and rich storytelling elements.

Calvià (Balearic Catalan: [kəlviˈa]) is a municipality and resort town on the island of Majorca, part of the Spanish autonomous community of the Balearic Islands. It is located in the southwestern part of the island of Majorca, between the Serra de Tramuntana and the Serra de Na Burguesa. The municipal seat is the town of Calvià Vila. Calvià has an approximate area of 145 km2 (56 sq mi). It is bordered on the north by the municipalities of Puigpunyent and Estellencs, Palma de Mallorca (Palma), the island's capital to the east, Andratx to the west and to the south by the Mediterranean Sea. According to the 2008 census, the municipality had a population of 50,777 inhabitants, of whom 18,046 were foreigners. Today, it is the second most populated area of the entire archipelago Balearic after Palma, and also an area that has the largest number of tourists in the islands. Its population is scattered around the different urban centers created as a result of tourism development and twentieth century urbanization. The historical epic that marked the most important local culture and traditions regarding the rest of Mallorca is the landing in Santa Ponça on 10 September 1229 of King James I of Aragon, and the subsequent conquering of Muslims who had invaded in the year 903. Since 1248, Calvià has had its own parochial church, Sante Ihoannes Caviano. Despite the popularity and use of the official shield locally, the municipality has no flag.

Calvià is a municipality located on the island of Majorca, in the Balearic Islands of Spain. It is situated to the southwest of the island and is known for its picturesque coastline, tourist resorts, and natural landscapes. The municipality includes several popular destinations, such as Magaluf, Santa Ponsa, and Paguera, which attract millions of visitors each year due to their beaches and vibrant nightlife. Calvià has a mix of urban and rural areas, featuring traditional Majorcan villages and agricultural land. Its economy is predominantly driven by tourism, though local efforts are made to promote sustainable development and preserve the environment. The municipality also hosts a variety of cultural events and festivals that highlight its regional heritage.
Tristaniopsis macphersonii is a species of plant in the family Myrtaceae. It is endemic to New Caledonia.

Tristaniopsis macphersonii, commonly known as Macpherson's Water Gum, is a species of flowering plant in the family Myrtaceae. Native to Australia, it is primarily found in subtropical rainforests in northeastern Queensland. This evergreen tree typically grows to a height of 10 to 15 meters and features smooth, greyish bark. Its leaves are glossy, dark green, and lanceolate, while the flowers are small, white, and borne in clusters, attracting various pollinators, including bees. T. macphersonii is valued for its ornamental appeal and is often used in landscaping and reforestation projects. The species thrives in well-drained soils and is tolerant of a variety of light conditions, making it adaptable to different environments. Conservation efforts focus on protecting its native habitat, which is threatened by land clearing and urban development.

Defending champions Esther Vergeer and Sharon Walraven defeated Jiske Griffioen and Aniek van Koot in the final, 6–4, 3–6, 7–5 to win the ladies' doubles wheelchair tennis title at the 2011 Wimbledon Championships. It was their third step towards an eventual Grand Slam, and Vergeer completed the triple career Grand Slam with the win.

The wheelchair women's doubles event at the 2011 Wimbledon Championships featured competition among top-ranked female players in wheelchair tennis. The tournament was held from June 23 to July 3, 2011, as part of the annual Wimbledon Championships. The defending champions, Estelle Mossely and Marjolein Buis, were unable to participate, leading to a new champion being crowned. The title was won by the duo of Jiske Griffioen and Aniek van Koot, who defeated the team of Lucy Shuker and Jordanne Whiley in the final. This victory marked an important achievement in their respective careers within the sport of wheelchair tennis.
Ronda E. Stryker (born 1954) is an American billionaire heiress, a granddaughter of Homer Stryker, the founder of medical equipment manufacturer Stryker Corporation, of which she is a director.

Ronda Stryker is an American businesswoman and philanthropist, notable for her role as a member of the Stryker Corporation founding family. Born in 1952, she holds a bachelor's degree in political science from the University of Michigan. Stryker has served as a board member of the Stryker Corporation, a leading medical technology firm founded by her grandfather, Homer Stryker, in 1941. Beyond her corporate involvement, she is recognized for her philanthropic efforts, particularly in education and healthcare, and has been actively involved in various charitable organizations. Stryker's contributions have had a significant impact on both the medical technology industry and community development initiatives in the United States.
Nikola Dubois (born 1977) is an Australian actress who appeared on Neighbours.

Nikola Dubois is a contemporary artist and curator known for his innovative work that blends traditional techniques with modern themes. Born in 1985 in Paris, France, Dubois studied fine arts at the École des Beaux-Arts. He gained recognition for his mixed-media installations that often explore concepts of identity, memory, and cultural heritage. His exhibitions have been featured in major galleries across Europe and the United States. Apart from his artistic practice, Dubois is actively involved in community art initiatives and workshops aimed at fostering artistic expression in underserved populations.

Évelyne Patlagean (20 October 1932 – 11 November 2008) was a French historian and Byzantinist, working on questions of poverty, welfare, gender, the family, and women in Byzantium. Patlagean's doctoral thesis, entitled Pauvreté économique et pauvreté sociale à Byzance, 4e-7e siècles, was published in 1977. In it, Patlagean developed ideas about inherent inequality in the status of women in the eastern Roman empire. She worked on questions of gender indetermination and transgression, including the seemingly unclear status of the eunuch, and in an important 1976 article, Patlagean opened a new dimension on the masculine inscription of circumscribed gender roles in Byzantine religious orders. Georges Sidéris suggests that Patlagean drew upon then-new insights afforded by the work of the sex and gender researchers Robert J. Stoller and Ann Oakley. Patlagean contributed to the first volume of Paul Veyne's edited Histoire de la vie privée (1987). Her final book, Un moyen âge grec, although controversial, was generally well received upon its publication in 2007. Patlagean argued forcefully that Byzantium, a field of study generally kept separate from the Latin West in teaching and research (a bifurcation stemming in large part from the Byzantinist's need for facility with Greek), should be viewed as integrally connected with both the idea of the Middle Ages, and with the history of western Europe. Memorial colloquia were held in honour of Évelyne Patlagean on 21 and 22 November 2011, at École normale supérieure Paris and Université de Paris Ouest Nanterre (the former Paris X-Nanterre) respectively.

Évelyne Patlagean is a prominent French historian, specializing in Byzantine studies and the history of the Eastern Mediterranean during the late antiquity and medieval periods. Born on October 18, 1930, she has contributed significantly to the understanding of the social and economic structures of Byzantine society. Patlagean is particularly noted for her analysis of the continuity and change in Byzantine civilization, as well as her work on the demographic and cultural transitions that occurred in the region. Her research has been influential in reshaping modern interpretations of Byzantine history, and she has published numerous articles and books on these subjects.

Lectionary 114, designated by siglum ℓ 114 (in the Gregory-Aland numbering) is a Greek manuscript of the New Testament, on parchment leaves. Palaeographically it has been assigned to the 14th century.

Lectionary 114, designated as ℓ 114 in the Gregory-Aland numbering system, is a Greek manuscript of the New Testament, specifically a lectionary, which is a book or list of Scripture readings used in Christian liturgical services. Dated to the 12th century, it contains texts from the Gospels arranged for use in the ecclesiastical calendar. The manuscript is written in Greek and is notable for its liturgical significance in the Eastern Orthodox Church. Lectionary 114 is housed at the British Library in London and serves as an important resource for the study of biblical text transmission and liturgical practices in medieval Christianity.

Marianków (German: Marienhof) is a village in the administrative district of Gmina Głogówek, within Prudnik County, Opole Voivodeship, south-western Poland. It is situated in the historical region of Prudnik Land.

Marianków is a village located in the Prudnik County of Opole Voivodeship in southern Poland. It is part of the administrative district of Gmina Prudnik. The village lies near the border with the Czech Republic and is characterized by its rural setting and agricultural landscape. Marianków's historical roots can be traced back centuries, reflecting the region's diverse cultural heritage. The area is known for its traditional Polish architecture and proximity to natural attractions, contributing to its local charm.

On 31 May 1926, the first Lebanese government was formed which was headed by Auguste Adib Pacha. It won the confidence of both the parliament and the senate in 14 June, with a majority of 26 votes. On 29 April 1927, 12 members of the senate signed a petition asking to withdrawal of the confidence, so the prime minister followed and resigned on 2 May.

The First Cabinet of Auguste Adib refers to the initial governing body formed by Auguste Adib, the Prime Minister of Lebanon, following his appointment in 1958 during a period of political instability in the country. This cabinet was characterized by its attempts to address sectarian tensions and economic challenges in post-independence Lebanon. Adib's government focused on promoting national unity and implementing reforms, though it faced criticism for its handling of political dissent and social issues. The cabinet was short-lived, lasting until 1959, when it was dissolved amid ongoing turmoil, leading to a reevaluation of Lebanon's political landscape.

The Five Man Army (Italian: Un esercito di 5 uomini) is a 1969 Italian Spaghetti Western film in which a group of five men are enlisted to rob a train of a gold shipment during the Mexican Revolution. Directed by Don Taylor, it featured a script by a young Dario Argento, later future maestro of the horror and thriller genre, and a score by Ennio Morricone. Argento also directed some sequences of the film without being credited.

The Five Man Army is a 1969 Italian Western film directed by Don Taylor. The storyline revolves around a group of five diverse men who band together to carry out a daring mission against a Mexican dictator during the turmoil of the Mexican Revolution. The film is notable for its ensemble cast, which includes Peter Graves, James Coburn, and Telly Savalas. Its blend of action, adventure, and humor reflects the characteristics of Spaghetti Westerns, a genre that gained popularity in the 1960s. While not as commercially successful as other films in the genre, The Five Man Army has garnered attention for its unique plot and stylized direction.

Jack Rife Beirnes (24 November 1914 – 1 June 1945) was a Royal Canadian Air Force squadron leader who flew Kittyhawk fighters on home defense in Canada and then commanded a squadron of Hawker Typhoons over Europe during the Second World War. He was awarded the Distinguished Flying Cross and Bar for his successes while serving with No. 438 Squadron RCAF. He lost his life in a flying accident at the end of his third combat tour.

Jack Rife Beirnes was an American businessman and politician known for his contributions to the development of the manufacturing industry in the early 20th century. Born in 1885, Beirnes played a significant role in local politics as a member of the Democratic Party and served in various civic capacities, advocating for labor rights and economic reform. He was instrumental in the establishment of several labor unions and was involved in initiatives to improve working conditions. Beirnes' legacy includes a focus on industrial growth and his efforts to support workers' rights during a transformative period in American economic history. He passed away in 1967, leaving behind a notable impact on both business and labor relations in his community.

The 1989–90 County Antrim Shield was the 101st edition of the County Antrim Shield, a cup competition in Northern Irish football. Glentoran won the tournament for the 20th time, defeating Linfield 6–5 on penalties after the final had finished 0-0. For the 4th year running the County Antrim FA invited three clubs from County Armagh to compete (Glenavon, Newry Town and Portadown).

The 1989–90 County Antrim Shield was the 109th edition of Northern Ireland's County Antrim Shield, a football competition organized by the County Antrim Football Association. The tournament featured several teams from the Irish League and other local clubs. The final took place on 17 February 1990, where Bangor emerged as the champions, defeating Glenavon with a score of 2-1. This victory marked Bangor's third triumph in the history of the competition. The tournament contributed to the local football culture in Northern Ireland, showcasing emerging talents and fostering regional rivalries.
Nahda University in Beni Suef (NUB) is located in Upper Egypt. It was established in 2006. It is a private university run by the Thebes Education Group.

Nahda University is a private university located in Beni Suef, Egypt. Established in 2016, it offers a range of undergraduate and postgraduate programs across various disciplines, including engineering, business, law, and health sciences. The university aims to provide quality education that meets international standards and emphasizes research and community engagement. Nahda University is part of Egypt's efforts to enhance higher education and improve educational outcomes in the region. The institution is recognized for its modern campus facilities and a diverse student body.

Huss Township is a township in Roseau County, Minnesota, United States. The population was 145 at the 2000 census. Huss Township was named for John Huss (14th century–1415), a Czech Catholic priest, philosopher, reformer, and master at Charles University in Prague.

Huss Township is a civil township located in Roseau County, Minnesota, United States. As part of the larger region of northern Minnesota, Huss Township is characterized by its rural landscape and agricultural activities. The township was established in the early 20th century and has maintained a small population, reflecting the general demographic trends of rural areas in the region. It is governed by a board of supervisors, and local services are provided in conjunction with the county government. The area is known for its natural resources and outdoor recreational opportunities, attracting visitors interested in hunting, fishing, and seasonal activities.

The Fire Station No. 4 was built in 1904 a mile west of the capitol in Madison, Wisconsin, when fire engines were still pulled by horses. In 1984 the building was listed on the National Register of Historic Places.

Fire Station No. 4, located in Madison, Wisconsin, is a historic fire station that was built in 1925. Designed in the Classical Revival architectural style, the station features distinctive characteristics such as brick construction and ornamental detailing. It served as an operational fire station for many years before being closed and later repurposed. Fire Station No. 4 is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, recognizing its architectural significance and contribution to the city's history. The building reflects Madison's early 20th-century development and the importance of fire service in urban planning.

Sunita (Soni) Dodani is a Pakistani-born American physician–epidemiologist and professor of clinical medicine. Her work focuses on cardiovascular disease prevention, health equity, and community-based interventions. She is the founding director of the Center 4 Health Research at the University of Illinois College of Medicine Peoria and previously established the Eastern Virginia Medical School–Sentara Healthcare Analytics and Delivery Science Institute. Her research includes faith- and community-based programs such as *Fit Body and Soul* and the HEALS and HEALS Med-Tech initiatives, aimed at improving cardiometabolic outcomes in underserved populations.

Sunita Dodani is a prominent American physician and public health expert recognized for her contributions to healthcare accessibility and community health initiatives. With a focus on underserved populations, Dodani has worked extensively in both urban and rural settings, advocating for health equity and implementing programs aimed at improving health outcomes. She has served in various leadership roles within healthcare organizations and has been involved in research addressing disparities in healthcare. Her work emphasizes the importance of culturally competent care and aims to empower communities through education and resources.

Cheshunt ( CHEZ-ənt) is a town in the Borough of Broxbourne, in Hertfordshire, England, situated within the London commuter belt approximately 13 miles (21 km) north of Central London. The town lies on the River Lea and Lee Navigation, bordering the Lee Valley Park, and forms part of the Greater London Urban Area. As of the 2021 census, the built-up area subdivision of Cheshunt had a population of 43,770. Historically recorded as Cestrehunt in the Domesday Book of 1086, Cheshunt developed along the Roman road of Ermine Street and shows evidence of prehistoric, Roman, and Anglo-Saxon settlement. The nearby Theobalds Palace hosted monarchs such as Elizabeth I and James I, and the town later became known for glasshouse horticulture, rose cultivation, and corporate retail, serving as the headquarters of Tesco until 2016. Cheshunt today is a commuter town with regular services via Cheshunt railway station on the West Anglia Main Line and London Overground, and is accessible by road via the A10 and M25 motorway. The local economy includes logistics, retail, business parks, and hospitality, with plans for a proposed £1 billion Google data centre released during 2024. Notable residents have included Cliff Richard, Victoria Beckham, and Olympic cyclist Laura Kenny.

Cheshunt is a town and civil parish in the Broxbourne borough of Hertfordshire, England. Located approximately 20 miles north of central London, it has a rich history dating back to pre-Roman times. The town's name is derived from the Old English elements "ces" (cheese) and "hunta" (hunter), indicating its historical roots in agriculture. Cheshunt is well-connected by public transport, with the Cheshunt railway station serving as a major hub. The town features a mix of residential and commercial areas, along with recreational spaces, such as parks and sports facilities. Notable landmarks include the Church of St Mary and the historic Cheshunt Old Pond, reflecting its heritage and community life. Historically, it played a role during the Reformation, linking to prominent figures such as John Bunyan. Today, Cheshunt is primarily a suburban town, attracting residents who commute to nearby London.

State Highway 289, known for most of its length as Preston Road, is a north–south Texas state highway. It begins at the intersection of Preston Road and Loop 12/Northwest Highway in Dallas. The Preston Road designation comes from the fact that the highway generally follows the course of an older road known as the Preston Trail, which ran to the town of Preston Bend. Preston is now submerged by Lake Texoma. The road is known as Preston all the way north through the remainder of the Dallas-Fort Worth Metroplex to a junction with State Highway 56 west of Sherman, near the Oklahoma state line (except for the portion through Gunter, which is designated as 8th Street). Preston Road passes through many of the fast-growing northern Dallas suburbs, including Frisco and Plano, as well as Prosper and Celina, with many new housing developments being built along Preston.

Texas State Highway 289 (SH 289), also known as the Preston Road, is a significant north-south state highway located in the state of Texas. Stretching approximately 33 miles, it begins at the junction with U.S. Highway 75 in the city of Dallas and extends northward to the town of Gunter in Grayson County. SH 289 serves as a major thoroughfare connecting rural areas with urban centers, facilitating travel through several communities including Plano and Frisco. Originally designated in the 1920s, the highway has undergone various developments and improvements over the years to accommodate increasing traffic and support regional growth. It is known for its scenic views and historical significance, particularly reflecting the early settlement patterns in North Texas.

Parviz Ghelichkhani (Persian: پرویز قلیچ‌خانی, born 4 December 1945) is an Iranian-French retired football player and former captain of Iran national football team. He is now based in France, where he is the editor and publisher of a political magazine. He is one of the Best Players of All Time in Iran and Asia. One of the Most Important members of the Iranian national team in the championship of the Asian Nations Cup in 1968 and 1972 and 1976 and Asian Games 1974 and member of Taj (Esteghlal Tehran) club in the championship of the AFC Champions League in 1970. He is generally considered the best player in Asia in the 1970s. Along with Ali Jabbari, he was the leader and star of Iran's Golden Generation in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is generally considered the most complete and best defensive midfielder in Asian history, with excellent playmaking, shooting, tackling, stamina and leadership.

Parviz Ghelichkhani is an Iranian-American scientist and engineer known for his contributions to the fields of mechanical engineering and thermodynamics. He earned his degree in mechanical engineering from the University of Tehran and later completed his graduate studies at Purdue University. Ghelichkhani has worked on various research projects related to energy systems and heat transfer, and he has published numerous articles in scientific journals. Additionally, he is recognized for his role as an educator, guiding students in advanced engineering principles. His work has implications in energy efficiency and sustainable engineering practices.

Noori (Arabic word for "light") was a female pashmina goat, the first pashmina goat to be cloned using the process of nuclear transfer. Born on 9 March 2012, she was kept at the place of her birth, at the Faculty of Veterinary Sciences and Animal Husbandry, Sher-e-Kashmir University of Agricultural Sciences and Technology of Kashmir, Shuhama, Srinagar in the Indian territory of Jammu and Kashmir.

Noori is a breed of domestic goat originally from the Indian subcontinent, particularly associated with the regions of Punjab and Haryana. Known for its resilience and adaptability to diverse climatic conditions, Noori goats are primarily raised for their milk and meat. They are characterized by their distinctive physical features, including a medium-to-large build, short, compact bodies, and often have a variety of coat colors. The breed has gained popularity among local farmers due to its high milk yield and low maintenance needs. Additionally, Noori goats play a significant role in the socio-economic context of rural communities, providing a source of income and nutritional benefits.

The Miercurea Nirajului gas field is a natural gas field located in Miercurea Nirajului, Mureș County, Romania. Discovered in 1915, it was developed by Romgaz, beginning production of natural gas and condensates in 1930. By 2010 the total proven reserves of the Miercurea Nirajului gas field were around 1.42 trillion ft3 (40 km3), with a production rate of around 35 million ft3/day (1×105 m3).

The Miercurea Nirajului gas field is a natural gas field located near the town of Miercurea Nirajului in Mureș County, Romania. Discovered in the mid-20th century, the field is part of Romania's extensive natural gas reserves and has contributed significantly to the country's energy supply. It is operated by companies engaged in the exploration and production of hydrocarbon resources. The field's geological characteristics include a combination of permeable reservoir rocks and trapping mechanisms that facilitate the accumulation of natural gas. The Miercurea Nirajului gas field plays an important role in the local economy and energy infrastructure, supplying gas for both domestic use and industrial applications.

Tamarix canariensis is a species of flowering plant in the family Tamaricaceae. It is a shrub or small tree up to 4 m tall. It was previously said to be native to parts of Macaronesia, northern Africa and south-western Europe, but is now considered only to be found in the Canary Islands, the African and European species being Tamarix gallica. It was first described by Carl Ludwig Willdenow in 1816.

Tamarix canariensis, commonly known as the Canary tamarisk, is a species of flowering plant in the family Tamaricaceae. Native to the Canary Islands, it predominantly grows in coastal regions and riverbanks. This deciduous shrub or small tree can reach heights of up to 3 meters and is characterized by its slender branches and feathery, scale-like leaves. The species produces small, pink to white flowers that bloom in dense spikes, typically in late spring to early summer. Tamarix canariensis is often used for ornamental purposes, soil stabilization, and as a windbreak. However, it can become invasive in some regions, outcompeting native vegetation.

Haruo Urata (浦田 春生, Urata Haruo) (born 9 March 1962 in Kumamoto) is a retired long-distance runner from Japan, who represented his native country at the 1992 Summer Olympics. His personal best in the men's 10,000 metres was 28:18.15, achieved at the 1991 World Championships. Urata won four national titles during his career: in the 5000 metres (1988 and 1989) and in the 10,000 metres (1988 and 1989).

Haruo Urata (born 1950) is a Japanese artist and designer known for his contributions to the field of contemporary art and graphic design. He gained prominence for his innovative use of traditional Japanese techniques combined with modern aesthetics, reflecting cultural themes and social issues. Urata's work spans various mediums, including painting, sculpture, and digital art, and he has exhibited internationally in galleries and museums. Additionally, he has lectured on design and art, influencing a generation of artists and designers in Japan and beyond.
Holjapyx calaverasae is a species of forcepstail in the family Japygidae. It is found in North America.

Holjapyx calaverasae is a species of soil-dwelling, wingless insect belonging to the family Diplura, within the order Entognatha. Described in 2019, it is endemic to the calaveras region of California, USA. Characterized by its slender body and distinctive morphological features, Holjapyx calaverasae is part of a group known for their ecological roles in soil aeration and the decomposition process. The species is notable for its limited geographical distribution, making it of interest in studies of biodiversity and conservation.
Rosina Storchio (19 January 1872 – 24 July 1945) was an Italian lyric coloratura soprano who starred in the world premieres of operas by Puccini, Leoncavallo, Mascagni and Giordano.

Rosina Storchio (born July 24, 1876 – died April 8, 1945) was an Italian soprano renowned for her work in opera during the early 20th century. She achieved prominence in the early 1900s, gaining acclaim for her performances in major European opera houses, particularly for her roles in works by composers such as Giuseppe Verdi and Giacomo Puccini. Storchio was celebrated not only for her vocal talent but also for her dramatic presence on stage. In addition to her operatic career, she recorded several albums, contributing to the dissemination of her performances. Storchio's legacy endures in the operatic community, marking her as a significant figure of her era.

The 2011 Colorado Ice season was the team's fifth season as a football franchise and third in the Indoor Football League (IFL). Founded for the 2007 season as part of United Indoor Football, the Colorado Ice became charter members of the IFL when the UIF merged with the Intense Football League before the 2009 season. One of 22 teams that competed in the IFL for the 2011 season, the Fort Collins-based Colorado Ice were members of the Mountain West Division of the Intense Conference. In their final season under original head coach Collins Sanders, the Colorado Ice played their home games at the Budweiser Events Center in Loveland, Colorado. The team recorded an 11–3 record, won the Mountain West Division title, and reached the playoffs.

The 2011 Colorado Ice season was the franchise's second season in the Indoor Football League (IFL). Under the leadership of head coach, the Ice aimed to improve upon their previous season's performance. The team played its home games at the Budweiser Events Center in Loveland, Colorado. Throughout the season, the Ice faced various challenges, including inconsistent play, which affected their standings in the league. Despite these obstacles, the team showcased resilience and determination as they competed in a series of games against rival teams. The season concluded with the Ice not qualifying for the playoffs, marking a period of reflection and potential rebuilding for the franchise in subsequent years.

Siebenhirten (German pronunciation: [ˌziːbm̩ˈhɪʁtn̩] ) is a station on U6 of the Vienna U-Bahn. It is located in the Liesing District. It opened on 15 April 1995 as the southern terminus of the extension of the line from Philadelphiabrücke.

Siebenhirten station is a railway station located in the district of Liesing in Vienna, Austria. It is part of the Vienna U-Bahn system and serves as the southern terminus of the U6 line. The station was opened in 1996 and features modern facilities, including accessibility for disabled passengers. Siebenhirten is situated in a suburban area, providing connections to local bus services and serving as a key transit point for residents in the surrounding neighborhoods. The station's design integrates elements of the local environment and aims to accommodate increasing commuter traffic.

Okuma Corporation (オークマ株式会社, Ōkuma Kabushiki-gaisha) is a machine tool builder based in Ōguchi, Aichi Prefecture, Japan. It has global market share in CNC machine tools such as CNC lathes, machining centers, and turn-mill machining centers. The company also offers FA (factory automation) products and servomotors. It is listed on the Tokyo Stock Exchange and is a component of the Nikkei 225 stock index.

Okuma Corporation is a Japanese manufacturer of CNC (computer numerical control) machine tools, specializing in lathes, milling machines, and multitasking equipment. Founded in 1898 and headquartered in Nagoya, Japan, the company has established a global presence with production facilities and sales offices in various countries. Okuma is recognized for its advanced technologies, including the OSP (Okuma Sampling Program) control system, and for equipment that emphasizes energy efficiency and sustainability. The company serves a wide range of industries, including aerospace, automotive, and engineering, and is known for its commitment to innovation and customer service in the manufacturing sector.

David Peisakhovich Shrayer-Petrov (Russian: Давид Пейсахович Шраер-Петров; 28 January 1936 – 9 June 2024) was a Russian-American novelist, poet, memoirist, translator, and medical scientist best known for his novel about refuseniks, Doctor Levitin, his poetry and fiction about Russian Jewish identity and his memoirs about the Soviet literary scene in the late 1950s-1970s.

David Shrayer-Petrov (born December 16, 1938) is a Russian-American poet, novelist, and translator. He was born in Leningrad (now Saint Petersburg), Russia, and began his literary career in the Soviet Union before emigrating to the United States in 1975. Shrayer-Petrov's work often explores themes of identity, exile, and the intricacies of cultural transition. He has published numerous books of poetry and fiction in both Russian and English, establishing a unique voice that resonates with experiences of displacement and loss. In addition to his creative work, he has translated important literary works, contributing to cross-cultural understanding. Shrayer-Petrov's contributions to literature have made him a significant figure in the émigré literary scene.

David Luca Durante (born June 26, 1980) is a retired American artistic gymnast. He was a member of the United States men's national artistic gymnastics team and was one of the three alternates to the 2008 Summer Olympics. He competed at the 2007 World Artistic Gymnastics Championships and was a member of the 4th-place-finishing American team. He was the 2007 U.S. All-Around champion. Raised in Garwood, New Jersey, Durante attended Arthur L. Johnson High School in Clark, New Jersey, before graduating from Stanford University in 2002 with a major in human biology and a minor in psychology. Durante holds dual American-Italian citizenship.

David Durante is an American gymnast who gained prominence in the early 2000s, particularly in the disciplines of artistic gymnastics. He was born on December 5, 1980, in New York City. Durante achieved significant success as a member of the United States national team, earning a medal at the 2003 Pan American Games and participating in various international competitions, including the World Championships. After his competitive career, Durante transitioned to coaching and has been involved in various gymnastics programs and initiatives, emphasizing athlete development and education. Additionally, he has contributed to gymnastics commentary and analysis, furthering the sport's visibility and understanding.

Year 215 BC was a year of the pre-Julian Roman calendar. At the time it was known as the Year of the Consulship of Albinus/Marcellus/Verrucosus and Gracchus (or, less frequently, year 539 Ab urbe condita). The denomination 215 BC for this year has been used since the early medieval period, when the Anno Domini calendar era became the prevalent method in Europe for naming years.

The year 215 BC was notable in the context of the Second Punic War, where the Roman Republic continued its struggle against the Carthaginian Empire led by Hannibal Barca. During this year, the Romans faced numerous military challenges, including Hannibal's continued successes in Italy. Key events included Hannibal's control over several regions and a strategic assembly of Carthaginian forces. In China, the Qin Dynasty, under the leadership of Qin Shi Huang, was consolidating its power and engaging in various military campaigns to strengthen its territory. Additionally, the year saw developments in the Hellenistic world, with various kingdoms vying for power following the fragmentation of Alexander the Great's empire. The epoch was marked by significant political and military activities that would shape the course of history in both the Mediterranean and East Asian regions.

Andy Panda is a cartoon character who starred in his own series of animated cartoon short subjects produced by Walter Lantz. These "cartunes" were released by Universal Pictures from 1939 to 1947, and United Artists from 1948 to 1949. The title character is an anthropomorphic cartoon character, a cute panda. Andy became the second star of the Walter Lantz cartoons after Oswald the Lucky Rabbit (formerly). He achieved considerable popularity until being eventually supplanted by Woody Woodpecker.

Andy Panda is a fictional character created by Walter Lantz, first appearing in the animated short film "Andy Panda," released in 1939. He is depicted as a cheerful, carefree anthropomorphic panda who often finds himself in humorous situations, usually alongside his friends such as the titular character and a variety of other animals. Andy Panda was part of the Lantz studio's roster and gained popularity during the Golden Age of American Animation. The character is characterized by his distinctive black and white appearance, playful personality, and often serves as a foil to more mischievous characters. Over the years, Andy Panda has appeared in numerous cartoons and has become a recognizable figure in classic animation history.

Sarles' Tavern, also known as Granite House, was a historic inn and tavern building located at Millwood, Westchester County, New York. It was built between 1814 and 1817 and was constructed of locally quarried granite. It was a two-story, rectangular building with a low pitched gable roof in a restrained Greek Revival style. The property was sold in 1817 by David Crasto to William Sarles, who renamed the property Sarles Tavern. Sarles operated the property as a stagecoach stop for the route from White Plains to Somers until his death in 1853. After Sarles' death the building was run as a tavern by his sons until it was sold to the Merritt family, which ran it as an inn until the early 1900s. The property's name was then changed to Merritt's Corners. The building was demolished in 1993 after the interior was consumed by fire; The building was unusable. It was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1979.

Sarles' Tavern was a historic establishment located in the town of Sarles, North Dakota. Founded in the early 20th century, it served as a social hub and gathering place for local residents and travelers alike. The tavern was known for its rustic charm and played a significant role in the community's social life, offering food, drinks, and entertainment. Over the decades, it became a landmark in the area, contributing to the local culture and economy. Despite its popularity, Sarles' Tavern ultimately closed, reflecting broader trends in rural taverns and changes in local demographics. Today, it remains a point of interest for those studying the social history of North Dakota and the impact of small-town establishments on community life.
Gertrude Boyarski (October 1, 1922 – September 19, 2012) was a Polish partisan fighter, Holocaust survivor, and a recipient of the Order of Lenin during World War II.

Gertrude Boyarski (born November 14, 1933) is a notable American artist known for her contributions to the field of painting and as a prominent figure in the art community. Boyarski's work often reflects a combination of abstract and representational elements, drawing inspiration from the natural world and cultural experiences. She has participated in numerous exhibitions across the United States and her art is featured in several private and public collections. Throughout her career, Boyarski has been recognized for her innovative techniques and her impact on contemporary art, particularly in the realms of color and form.

Mount Druitt is an electoral district of the Legislative Assembly in the Australian state of New South Wales. It is represented by Edmond Atalla of the Labor Party. It is a 38.95 km2 urban electorate in Sydney's outer west.

The Electoral District of Mount Druitt is a legislative electoral district located in New South Wales, Australia. Established in 1988, it is part of the New South Wales Legislative Assembly. The district primarily includes the suburb of Mount Druitt and surrounding areas, characterized by a diverse population and a mix of residential, commercial, and recreational spaces. The electoral district has historically been represented by members of the Australian Labor Party, reflecting its socio-economic demographics. Mount Druitt plays a significant role in local governance and political representation within the state.

Benedicta Ward (born Florence Margaret Ward, 4 February 1933 – 23 May 2022) was a Church of England nun, theologian and historian. She was a member of the Anglican religious order, the Community of the Sisters of the Love of God and reader in early Christian spirituality at Harris Manchester College, University of Oxford. She was particularly known for her research on the Desert Fathers, popularising the collection of their writings known as the Apophthegmata Patrum. She wrote extensively on Anselm of Canterbury and Bede.

Benedicta Ward is a British theologian, writer, and scholar known for her contributions to the fields of Christian spirituality and monasticism. A prominent figure in contemporary theological discourse, she has authored several influential books and articles exploring the lives and teachings of the Desert Fathers, as well as the broader historical context of monastic life. Ward's work emphasizes the significance of early Christian mystical practices and their relevance to modern spirituality. She has been associated with the Centre for the Study of Christian Spirituality and has played a key role in promoting the understanding of historical Christian texts. Her scholarship is marked by an interdisciplinary approach, drawing from theology, history, and literature.

Triumf Riza (July 5 1979 – 30 August 2007) was a Kosovar police officer, and member of an elite protection unit with the fledgling Kosovo Police Service. He was killed in the line of duty during an ongoing clash with the Enver Sekiraqa gang, an Ethnic Albanian crime syndicate that operates in Kosovo. Riza is notable in that he was the young police force's first recognized hero.

Triumf Riza is an Indonesian entrepreneur and businessman, known for his contributions to the technology and digital marketing sectors in Southeast Asia. He gained prominence for founding multiple startups that focus on innovative solutions in e-commerce and mobile applications. Riza's work emphasizes the integration of technology to enhance consumer experiences and improve business efficiency. He has been recognized for his leadership and vision in fostering growth within the regional tech ecosystem, earning accolades from various industry organizations. Riza is also an advocate for entrepreneurship and technology education, frequently speaking at conferences and participating in mentorship programs for aspiring entrepreneurs.

The Baptistery of Butrint (Albanian: Pagëzuesi i Butrintit), is an archeological site in Vlorë County, Albania and part of the Butrint National Park. Located in the archaeological site of Butrint, it is a late antique structure known for its well-preserved mosaic pavement. The centrally planned, circular baptistery is also notable as an ancient Roman monument adapted to the needs of Christianity. Constructed in the 6th century A.D., the baptistry was excavated in May 1928 by an Italian archaeological mission to Albania, led by Luigi Maria Ugolini.

The Baptistery of Butrint is an ancient Christian monument located in the archaeological site of Butrint, Albania. Constructed in the late 5th or early 6th century AD, it is notable for its impressive mosaics and unique architectural features, including a circular plan and a dome. The baptistery served as a center for early Christian baptismal rites in the region and reflects the transition from pagan to Christian practices in the area. Extensive archaeological excavations have uncovered its intricate floor mosaics depicting Christian symbols and motifs, highlighting the artistic and cultural significance of the structure during the early Byzantine period. The site is part of the Butrint National Park, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, recognized for its historical and cultural importance.

Dorsum Bucher is a wrinkle ridge at 31.0°N 39.0°W﻿ / 31.0; -39.0 in the border region between Oceanus Procellarum and Mare Imbrium on the Moon. The name of the feature was approved by the IAU in 1976. It is approximately 85 km long, extending from the vicinity of Gruithuisen crater to the area west of Delisle crater. The south end of the dorsum is a low hill of terra material that predates the mare lava. The ridge was named after Walter Hermann Bucher, a German-American geologist and paleontologist.

Dorsum Bucher is a prominent ridge-like feature located on the lunar surface, situated in the southeastern region of the Moon, approximately 22 km long. It is part of the complex geological formations found in the vicinity of the Mare Australe. The feature is characterized by its slight elevation compared to the surrounding terrain and is composed primarily of basaltic material. Dorsum Bucher was named in honor of the American astronomer Charles Bucher, known for his contributions to lunar cartography. The feature is of interest to researchers studying the geological history and composition of the Moon's surface.

The Zarafshan Range, formerly the Zeravshan Range, is a mountain range in Tajikistan and Uzbekistan, part of the Pamir-Alay mountains. Almost all of the range belongs to the drainage basins of the Zarafshan River. The Persian name is believed to possibly be a reference to gold found in bed of the Zarafshan River and its tributaries, which has ushered prosperity to the region from ancient times.

The Zarafshan Range is a mountain range located in central Asia, primarily situated in Uzbekistan and partially extending into Tajikistan. It is part of the larger Pamir-Alay mountain system and runs approximately 200 kilometers (124 miles) from west to east. The range is characterized by its rugged terrain, with its highest peak, Khazret Sultan, reaching an elevation of 4,643 meters (15,233 feet). The Zarafshan Range is known for its rich biodiversity and unique geological formations, serving as a natural barrier between the Zarafshan and Surkhandarya valleys. The region is also home to various cultural and historical sites, reflecting the rich heritage of the peoples who inhabit the surrounding areas. The mountains offer opportunities for trekking and mountaineering, attracting outdoor enthusiasts.

Miconia castrensis is a species of plant in the family Melastomataceae. It is endemic to Ecuador. Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical moist montane forests and subtropical or tropical high-altitude grassland.

Miconia castrensis is a species of flowering plant in the Melastomataceae family, native to the Caribbean region, particularly found in Puerto Rico and the Virgin Islands. This evergreen shrub can reach heights of approximately 2 to 4 meters and is characterized by its broad, glossy leaves and small, white to pale purple flowers that bloom in clusters. Miconia castrensis typically thrives in humid, tropical environments and is often found in disturbed areas or along roadsides. The plant plays a role in local ecosystems but may also be subject to pressures from habitat loss and environmental changes. It is of interest for both ecological studies and horticulture, owing to its ornamental qualities.

Rosana Pinheiro-Machado (Brazilian Portuguese: [ʁoˈzɐnɐ pĩˈɲe(j)ɾu maˈʃadu]) is a Brazilian anthropologist, currently a professor at the School of Geography at University College Dublin. She has been a lecturer at the Department of Social & Policy Sciences at the University of Bath and studies the economic and political effects of trade in the Global South, particularly in Brazil and China. She is also a columnist for The Intercept.

Rosana Pinheiro-Machado is a Brazilian social scientist and academic known for her work in anthropology, political science, and development studies. She is a professor at the Federal University of Rio Grande do Sul (UFRGS) and has conducted extensive research on social movements, inequality, and the political landscape in Brazil and Latin America. Pinheiro-Machado has been involved in various interdisciplinary projects and has published numerous articles and books that explore the intersections of culture, politics, and social justice. Her contributions have significantly influenced the understanding of citizenship and activism in contemporary Brazil.
Elizabeth Spencer, Duchess of Marlborough (née Trevor; c. 1713 – 7 October 1761) was the wife of Charles Spencer, 3rd Duke of Marlborough.

Elizabeth Spencer, Duchess of Marlborough (born Elizabeth FitzGerald; 1700–1750) was a prominent British aristocrat known for her role as the wife of Charles Spencer, 3rd Duke of Marlborough. Born into a notable family, she was the daughter of the wealthy landowner and politician, Thomas FitzGerald, 17th Earl of Kildare. Elizabeth married the Duke in 1719, becoming the Duchess of Marlborough and managing the extensive Blenheim Palace estate. She was noted for her social influence within the British court and her connections to prominent political figures. Elizabeth had a significant impact on the arts and culture of her time, patronizing various artists and writers. Her life was marked by her efforts in charitable work and in managing her husband's political interests. She died in 1750 and was buried alongside her husband at Blenheim Palace.

Chadshunt is a small village and civil parish in the Stratford-on-Avon district, in the county of Warwickshire, England. Chadshunt is located in between the villages of Gaydon and Kineton. It developed around the mill stream alongside Watery Lane. The former Yarlington Mill is now a private residence. Chadshunt has a church called All Saints Church.

Chadshunt is a village and civil parish located in the county of Warwickshire, England. Situated within the Stratford-on-Avon district, it is characterized by its rural setting and historical architecture. The village is known for its proximity to the larger towns of Stratford-upon-Avon and Warwick, and its scenic surroundings make it a quaint spot for both residents and visitors. The parish encompasses a small population, reflective of its status as a rural village, and offers a glimpse into the agricultural and historical heritage of the region. Chadshunt is also noted for its community activities and local events, contributing to the village's social cohesion and identity.
Calexico Unified School District is a school district in California. It has its headquarters in Calexico.

Calexico Unified School District (CUSD) is a public school district located in Calexico, California. It serves the educational needs of students in grades K-12 within its geographic boundaries, which include parts of the Imperial Valley. Established to provide quality education, CUSD operates multiple elementary schools, a middle school, and a high school, focusing on diverse programs to support academic achievement and community engagement. The district emphasizes student performance, parental involvement, and the development of critical thinking skills through various curricular and extracurricular activities. Calexico Unified School District is also known for its efforts to accommodate the unique cultural and linguistic diversity of its student population.

The 1940 Temple Owls football team was an American football team that represented Temple University as an independent during the 1940 college football season. In its first season under head coach Ray Morrison, the team compiled a 4–4–1 record and outscored opponents by a total of 155 to 113. Charles Drulis was the team captain. Temple was ranked at No. 35 (out of 697 college football teams) in the final rankings under the Litkenhous Difference by Score system for 1940. The team played its home games at Temple Stadium in Philadelphia.

The 1940 Temple Owls football team represented Temple University during the 1940 college football season. This season marked the team's 11th year of play, competing as a member of the Middle Atlantic Conference. Under the leadership of head coach John B.
