"Buddy" Parker, who was in his second year, the Owls achieved a record of 6 wins and 2 losses. The team demonstrated a strong performance, finishing third in the conference standings. The season was significant for the Owls as they built a solid foundation for future success in college football.

Wyndham Hills is a community in York County, Pennsylvania, United States. One of the suburbs of York, Wyndham Hills, is located in Spring Garden Township on the hills of the same name. Among the other suburbs of York. Wyndham Hills is on the highest elevation. The subdivision plan from 1931 included a restrictive covenant that prohibited home ownership or occupancy by any "negro" or "Mongolian" (a term that then meant anyone of Oriental descent). The racist occupancy covenant is no longer in force, but other covenants remain in force.

Wyndham Hills is a census-designated place located in York County, Pennsylvania, United States. It is situated within the larger borough of West York and is known for its residential community and suburban character. The area features a mix of single-family homes and local amenities. Wyndham Hills is part of the York-Hanover metropolitan area and is characterized by its family-friendly environment, parks, and proximity to various services and recreational activities. The population and demographics are reflective of typical suburban communities, with a mix of families and professionals residing in the area.
Gallienia is a monotypic genus of flowering plants in the family Rubiaceae. The genus contains only one species, viz. Gallienia sclerophylla, which is endemic to Madagascar.

Gallienia, also known as Gaston Gallieni, was a French military officer and colonial administrator known for his significant role in the early 20th century, particularly during World War I. Born on April 24, 1849, in the province of Orléanais, Gallieni served in various capacities within the French Army, distinguishing himself through innovative military strategies and effective leadership. He gained notoriety for his defense of Paris during the crucial early months of the war in 1914, where his coordinated efforts helped repel German forces. Gallieni's work extended beyond military engagements, as he also contributed to colonial governance in Madagascar, where he implemented essential reforms. He died on May 27, 1916, leaving a legacy as a prominent figure in French military history.

Wolf Mountains Battlefield/Where Big Crow Walked Back and Forth was the site of the Battle of Wolf Mountain, the last major combat of the Great Sioux War of 1876–77. It was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 2001, and was designated a National Historic Landmark in 2008.

Wolf Mountains Battlefield, located in Montana, is a site of significant historical importance due to its association with the Nez Perce War of 1877. The battlefield marks the location of a series of confrontations between the United States Army and the Nez Perce tribe, who were attempting to escape to Canada after being pursued by American forces. The most notable engagement at Wolf Mountains occurred in late September 1877, where the Nez Perce, under Chief Joseph, demonstrated strategic military tactics. Today, the site is preserved as a testament to the conflict between Native American tribes and the U.S. government during westward expansion, and it serves as a location for education and remembrance of the indigenous struggle for rights and territory. The battlefield is part of Montana’s rich historical landscape and is recognized for its cultural significance.

Henry Boehm (June 8, 1775 – December 28, 1875) was an American clergyman and pastor. The son of noted clergyman Martin Boehm, Henry preferred to be a traveling preacher, going to different churches and lecturing about various religious topics. Boehm established many Methodist churches and ministerial services throughout the United States, but the first Methodist church was located in Lancaster, Pennsylvania. This is where Henry Boehm preached for over seventy years. His centennial was held in high regard throughout the small villages and cities where Boehm had visited. Boehm preached his last sermon a few days before his death at age 100 in 1875.

Henry Boehm (1814–1886) was an American Methodist bishop and a prominent leader in the Methodist Episcopal Church. Born in Pennsylvania, he exhibited a strong commitment to education and evangelism throughout his ministry. Boehm was known for his role in organizing and expanding church missions, particularly in the Midwest and the West during the mid-19th century. He played a significant part in addressing social issues of his time, advocating for the abolition of slavery and supporting temperance movements. In 1852, he was elected as a bishop, where he continued to influence the church's direction and growth until his death. His legacy includes efforts to foster church unity and improve outreach to diverse communities.

Florence Jepkemoi Barsosio (born 11 August 1976 in Keiyo District) is a Kenyan athlete who specialises in long-distance running. Her sister, Sally Barsosio, is also a professional runner. She has competed at world championship finals in long-distance track events, cross country running and road running. The majority of her success has come in marathon races: she won the 2000 Turin Marathon in her debut over the distance and has gone on to win marathons in Paris, Madrid, Florence and Vienna. Barsosio has taken part in two New York Marathons: finishing fifth in 2000 (with her 2:27:00 personal best performance), and eleventh at the 2001 race. Her only Marathon World Championship race came in 2001 and she finished in sixth position. She has also competed over the half marathon distance and has won the Paris Half Marathon and Prague Half Marathon.

Florence Barsosio is a Kenyan long-distance runner born on October 19, 1987, known for her achievements in marathons and road races. She gained international recognition for her performances in various competitive events, particularly her success in the marathon category. Barsosio's career highlights include multiple podium finishes in prestigious marathons and a reputation for her strong competitive spirit and endurance. Apart from her athletic accomplishments, she represents a notable presence in the Kenyan running community, contributing to the country's legacy in long-distance running.

Jukebox the Ghost is an American three-piece power pop band formed in the Washington, D.C. metro area. The band consists of Ben Thornewill (vocals & piano), Tommy Siegel (vocals & guitar) and Jesse Kristin (drums). The band has been active since 2003.

Jukebox the Ghost is an American pop band formed in 2006 in Washington, D.C. The band consists of Ben Thornewill (vocals and piano), Tommy Siegel (vocals and guitar), and Jesse Kristin (drums). Known for their catchy melodies and energetic performances, Jukebox the Ghost's music blends elements of pop, rock, and indie styles. They gained popularity with their albums, including "Let Live & Let Ghosts" (2008), "Everything Escape" (2010), and "Off to the Races" (2018). The band has garnered a dedicated fan base and has toured extensively across the United States and internationally. Their upbeat and infectious sound is characterized by vibrant instrumentation and intricate vocal harmonies.

Russ Jones (born July 16, 1942 in Ontario) is a Canadian novelist, illustrator, and magazine editor, active in the publishing and entertainment industries over a half-century, best known as the creator of the magazine Creepy for Warren Publishing. As the founding editor of Creepy in 1963, he is notable for a significant milestone in comics history by proving there was a readership eager to read graphic stories in a black-and-white magazine format rather than in a color comic book. During the mid-1960s, Jones also pioneered the presentation of original comics formatted directly for paperback books, such as Christopher Lee's Treasury of Terror (Pyramid, 1966).

Russ Jones is an American football coach known for his expertise in defensive strategies. He has held various coaching positions at the college level, contributing to the success of multiple teams. Jones is recognized for his ability to develop players and enhance team performance, particularly in defensive roles. His coaching career includes stints at notable universities, where he has implemented innovative defensive schemes and mentored athletes who have progressed to professional careers.

Shree Shyam Mandir, Guwahati is a temple located in Chatribari, one of the main trade hubs in the Indian city of Guwahati. It has His Supremacy, Shri Khatushyamji or Shyam baba as the presiding deity and many sub-temples dedicated to other deities viz. Shri Ganeshji, Biyala's sati dadi or Bajawa ki sati dadi, Shri Bajrang Bali, Shri Shiva Parivar, Shri Lakshmi Mata, Shri Rani Sati Dadiji, and Shri Santoshi Maa. The temple is visited by hundreds of Shyam Bhakts daily, especially during Aartis and a larger number on Ekadashis and a still larger number on the Shukla paksha Ekadashis of the Kartik, Falgun and Margshirsh months of the Hindu lunar calendar.

Shree Shyam Mandir is a prominent Hindu temple located in Guwahati, Assam, India. Dedicated to Lord Shyam, a manifestation of Lord Krishna, the temple attracts devotees from across the region, particularly during festivals such as Janmashtami and Holi. The temple features intricate carvings and a serene environment, making it a significant cultural and religious site in Assam. Established in the early 20th century, it has become a vital center for spiritual activities and community gatherings, showcasing the region's rich religious heritage.

Richard Tryon (31 August 1837 — 12 December 1905) was an English first-class cricketer and British Army officer. The son of Thomas Tryon and Anne Trollope, he was born in August 1837 at Bulwick Park in the Northamptonshire village of Bulwick. He was commissioned into the British Army as an ensign in the Rifle Brigade in November 1854. Shortly after he was promoted to lieutenant in February 1855. Tryon purchased the rank of captain in July 1858, later retiring from active service nearly a decade later in May 1867. Tryon made a single appearance in first-class cricket for the Marylebone Cricket Club (MCC), captained by W. G. Grace, against Kent at Lord's in 1871. Batting once in the match, he was dismissed by Bob Lipscomb for 7 runs in the MCC first innings. A resident of The Lodge, Oakham in the County of Rutland, Tryon was nominated to be Sheriff of Rutland in November 1880. He was unsuccessful, with Francis Pierremont Cecil being made Sheriff; however, Cecil went on active naval service and was replaced by Tryon in April 1881. He was made a deputy lieutenant of Rutland in December 1901. He additionally served as a justice of the peace for Rutland. Tryon died at Marylebone in December 1905, following a short illness. He married Jane Anna Lucy Johnson, daughter of General William Augustus Johnson, in 1867. Two sons, Henry and Richard, were killed in the First World War. A brother was the Royal Navy Vice-Admiral Sir George Tryon.

Richard Tryon (born January 29, 1975) is an American professional wrestler and promoter known for his work in various independent wrestling promotions. He gained notoriety in the late 1990s and early 2000s for his high-energy performances and charismatic persona. Tryon has competed in numerous wrestling events across the United States and has held multiple championship titles during his career. In addition to his in-ring work, he has also made contributions to the wrestling industry as a promoter, helping to develop new talent and organize matches. His influence extends beyond the ring, as he often engages with fans and is involved in charitable activities connected to the wrestling community.

The Rein da Tuma is a river in Switzerland, in the Canton of Graubünden. It rises east of the Rossbodenstock with two or three mountain streams, that flow across the Tuma Alp into the Lake Toma (German: Tomasee, Romansh: Lai da Tuma). Before reaching the lake, it flows through a plain and forms small meanders. The Rein da Tuma flows out of Lake Toma on the north east and flows as Anterior Rhine past the village of Tschamut in the municipality of Tujetsch.

Rein da Tuma is a traditional folk music genre originating from the Tuma region in Portugal, characterized by its use of local dialects, improvisational lyrics, and instrumentation that typically includes stringed instruments like the guitarra and traditional percussion. Often performed at social gatherings and community celebrations, Rein da Tuma embodies the cultural heritage of the region, incorporating themes of daily life, love, and nature. The genre plays a significant role in preserving local traditions and has been influential in shaping contemporary Portuguese folk music. Efforts to promote and revive Rein da Tuma have increased in recent years, fostering interest among younger generations and contributing to the broader folk music scene in Portugal.
Seguenzia wareni is a species of extremely small deep water sea snail, a marine gastropod mollusk in the family Seguenziidae.

Seguenzia wareni is a species of marine gastropod mollusk belonging to the family Seguenzidae, commonly known as deep-sea snails. This species is primarily found in deep ocean environments, where it plays a role in the marine ecosystem as part of the benthic community. Characterized by its distinctively shaped shell, Seguenzia wareni was first described in 1994 by marine biologists who studied its morphology and distribution. Like other members of its family, it is adapted to life at great depths, and its diet likely consists of detritus or microscopic organisms found in the sediment. Due to its habitat, little is known about its biology and ecology, highlighting the need for further research in deep-sea gastropod studies.

Grande Fratello (also known by the acronym GF) is the Italian version of the reality television franchise Big Brother, created by producer John de Mol Jr. in 1997, which began airing on 14 September 2000 on Canale 5, it has gone on to become a cultural phenomenon in Italy. The show follows the format of other national editions, in which a group of contestants, known as "housemates", live together in a specially constructed house that is isolated from the outside world. Live television cameras and personal audio microphones continuously monitor them. Throughout the competition, housemates are "evicted" from the house by public televoting. The last remaining housemate wins the competition and a cash prize. The series is named after the fictional totalitarian dictator from George Orwell's 1949 novel Nineteen Eighty-Four. Over the course of its nineteen seasons, the program has been hosted by Daria Bignardi (2000–2001), Barbara D'Urso (2003–2004, 2018–2019), Alessia Marcuzzi (2006–2012, 2014–2015), Alfonso Signorini (2023–2025) and Simona Ventura (from 2025). The reality show also had seven editions of the spin-off Grande Fratello VIP, reserved for famous people and one of the spin-off The Couple - Una vittoria per due, reserved for couples.

Grande Fratello is the Italian version of the reality television franchise Big Brother, created by John de Mol. It premiered on September 14, 2000, and is produced by Endemol Shine Italy. The show features a group of contestants, known as housemates, who are isolated in a house under constant surveillance and are recorded for 24 hours a day. Contestants must compete in various challenges and are eliminated through public voting. The winner receives a cash prize and is crowned the "winner" of the season. Grande Fratello has spawned several spin-offs, including celebrity editions and VIP versions, and has significantly influenced reality TV culture in Italy. The series has varied in format and length, airing multiple seasons with some featuring themes or twists that differentiate them from traditional reality formats.

Gerda Laski (4 June 1893, Vienna – 24 November 1928, Berlin) was an Austrian/German physicist known for her research in infrared radiation. She went to a private girls secondary grammar school in Vienna and graduated in 1913. She earned her doctorate in physics from the University of Vienna in 1917 on "Size Determination of Submicroscopic Particles Based on Optical and Mechanical Effects". From 1918 to 1919, she worked as an assistant at the University of Göttingen and, in 1920, as an assistant at the Physical Institute of the Technische Hochschule in Charlottenburg (now Technische Universität Berlin), where she was introduced to the experimental technique that became her major interest. Her early research concerned extensions of the Bohr model of hydrogen atoms to hydrogen molecules. Laski was a student of Peter Debye, who was awarded the Nobel Prize in chemistry in 1936. Debye studied the dispersion of light using Bohr's hydrogen model and found that the theoretical curve corresponded satisfactorily to the curve observed. Laski later showed agreement between theory and experiment, however based on an erroneous interpretation of data. Laski's main research focus later on was infrared research. This included the examination of selected chemical substances by means of infrared radiation—a field of application. Beginning in 1924, Laski was the director of the Infrared Department at the Institute for Fibre Chemistry of the Kaiser Wilhelm Society. This department was closed due to lack of financing. She then became a voluntary assistant at the Physikalisch-Technische Bundesanstalt (Imperial Physical-Technical Institute) in 1927, in order to establish an infrared laboratory. After serious illness, the Kaiser Wilhelm Institute for Physics provided Laski with a monthly stipend until her death in 1928. Her final work was on special methods for infrared measurement and thermoelectricity. Her research also included studying natural infrared frequencies of diatomic Bohr gas molecules and their specific heat at high temperatures.

Gerda Laski (1914–2018) was a British novelist and short story writer known for her poignant exploration of human relationships and the complexities of personal identity. Born in Germany to a Jewish family, she fled the Nazis in the late 1930s, settling in the United Kingdom. Laski's literary career spanned several decades, during which she published numerous works, including novels such as "The Victorian Chaise-longue" (1953) and "The Village" (1974). Her writing often drew from her experiences of displacement and her Jewish heritage. Laski was also involved in the literary community, contributing to various anthologies and participating in writing mentorship programs. She received acclaim for her contributions to contemporary literature and was awarded multiple honors throughout her lifetime.

Minky Worden is an American human rights advocate and author. She serves as Director of Global Initiatives at Human Rights Watch. She has been an adjunct associate professor at Columbia University's School of International and Public Affairs since 2013.

Minky Worden is an American human rights advocate known for her work with Human Rights Watch (HRW), where she serves as the director of global initiatives. She has been influential in raising awareness about human rights issues across various regions, particularly in China, where she has focused on issues related to Tibet and the treatment of ethnic minorities. Worden has also been involved in promoting human rights in connection to the Olympic Games, notably advocating for accountability in host countries. In addition to her advocacy work, she has contributed to publications and reports on human rights topics and has participated in numerous panels and discussions to advance the dialogue around global human rights.
The 26th CARIFTA Games was held at the National Stadium in Bridgetown, Barbados, on April 4–6, 1997.

The 1997 CARIFTA Games was the 26th edition of the annual track and field competition held for athletes from the Caribbean region. The event took place in Bermuda from April 19 to April 21, 1997, and featured a variety of junior and youth events for both boys and girls. Countries participating included Jamaica, the Bahamas, and Trinidad and Tobago, among others. Jamaica emerged as the dominant nation, showcasing its strength in sprinting and field events. The games served as an important platform for young athletes to gain international experience and compete against their peers from across the Caribbean.

Emma Parker (pseud. "Emma De Lisle"; fl 1809–1817) was an Anglo-Welsh novelist of whom very little is known, although her work was generally well-reviewed during her lifetime. Her epistolary novel Self-Deception explores the cultural and religious differences between the English and the French.

Emma Parker is a fictional character from the popular television series "Beyond the Horizon." Portrayed by actress Sarah Johnson, Emma is an adventurous young woman who embarks on a journey of self-discovery while navigating complex relationships and challenges in a futuristic setting. Introduced in the first season, her character evolves significantly throughout the series, becoming a symbol of resilience and empowerment. The show has received critical acclaim for its engaging storytelling and character development, and Emma Parker has become a fan favorite, inspiring various merchandise and spin-off content.
The Salmon Run Bell Tower is a bell tower and glockenspiel in Vancouver, Washington's Esther Short Park, in the United States.

The Salmon Run Bell Tower is a prominent landmark located in the Salmon Run Mall in Watertown, New York. The tower features a unique architectural design that has become a recognizable symbol of the shopping center. It houses a functioning bell and is often used for seasonal events and celebrations, enhancing the community atmosphere of the mall. The Bell Tower not only serves as a visual focal point for shoppers but also plays a role in local heritage and community gatherings. Its design and purpose reflect a blend of commercial and civic importance within the region.

The Timpanogos Storytelling Festival is an annual storytelling event held in Lehi, Utah, on the weekend after Labor Day. The event typically lasts three days and features professional storytellers from across the country. In addition to daytime performances on Friday and Saturday, there are usually themed evening performances such as Look Who's Talking, Bedtime Stories, My Favorite Stories, and Laughin' Night.

The Timpanogos Storytelling Festival is an annual event held in Orem, Utah, celebrating the art of storytelling. Founded in 1995, the festival showcases a diverse range of storytelling styles and genres, featuring acclaimed storytellers from around the United States and beyond. It includes performances, workshops, and activities for all ages, promoting literacy, creativity, and community engagement. The festival typically takes place in early September and attracts thousands of attendees, making it one of the prominent storytelling festivals in the region. Its mission emphasizes preserving and promoting the oral tradition of storytelling as an important cultural heritage.

Verticordia sect. Corynatoca is one of six sections in the subgenus Eperephes. It includes the single species Verticordia ovalifolia. Plants in this section are sometimes bushy shrubs, sometimes tall and open. The flower cup has very short appendages and the staminodes have raised glands on their surface. When Alex George reviewed the genus in 1991 he described the section and gave it the name Corynatoca. The name "Corynatoca" is from the Greek koryne (a club) and atakos (barren), referring to the unusual staminodes. The type and only species in this section is Verticordia ovalifolia.

Verticordia sect. Corynatoca is a section within the genus Verticordia, belonging to the myrtle family, Myrtaceae. This section is characterized by its distinct floral morphology, including unique corolla shapes and arrangements. It is predominantly found in southwestern Australia, where it inhabits a variety of ecological niches, primarily in sandy soils. Members of sect. Corynatoca are notable for their ornamental qualities, often being cultivated for horticultural purposes. The classification and systematics of this section have undergone revisions, with ongoing studies focusing on phylogenetic relationships and conservation status due to habitat loss.

The 1903 Boston Americans season was the third season for the professional baseball franchise that later became known as the Boston Red Sox. The Americans finished first in the American League (AL) with a record of 91 wins and 47 losses, 14+1⁄2 games ahead of the Philadelphia Athletics. Boston went on to participate in the first World Series held between the AL and National League (NL) champions. The Americans won the 1903 World Series in eight games over the Pittsburgh Pirates. The team was managed by Jimmy Collins and played its home games at Huntington Avenue Grounds.

The 1903 Boston Americans season was the inaugural season of the Boston Americans in Major League Baseball, playing as a member of the American League. Managed by Jimmy Collins, the team finished the regular season with a record of 91 wins and 47 losses, securing the American League pennant. The Americans were notable for their strong performance, which led them to the first-ever World Series, where they faced the Pittsburgh Pirates. The series was a closely contested battle, culminating in a decisive Game 8, where the Americans emerged victorious, clinching the championship. Highlights of the season included standout performances from players such as pitcher Cy Young and outfielder Tris Speaker, contributing to the team's early success in Major League history.

Hungarian National Party (Hungarian: Magyar Nemzeti Párt, MNP, Czech: Maďarská národní strana, Slovak: Maďarská národná strana) was one of political parties of ethnic Hungarians in the First Republic of Czechoslovakia. The party was founded in February 1920 in Komárno under the name National Hungarian Smallholder and Farmer Party (Országos Magyar Kisgazda és Földműves Párt). From May 1925, it used the name National Hungarian Smallholders, Farmers and Small-business Party (Országos Magyar Kisgazda, Földműves és Kisiparos Párt), often abbreviated as Hungarian Smallholders Party (Magyar Kisgazda Párt). In 1925, the name was changed to Hungarian National Party (Magyar Nemzeti Párt). On June 21, 1936, the party merged with Provincial Christian-Socialist Party (OKszP), another large Hungarian party, into the United Hungarian Party (Egyesült Magyar Párt, EMP) led by János Esterházy as national executive chairman (until then leader of OKszP) and Andor Jaross as national chairman. The main objective of the party was initially an autonomy for ethnically Hungarian parts in Slovakia. This stance was later revised, and the party advocated a revision of the Trianon Treaty. In the economic sphere, the party advocated free market and called for government support for smallholders and peasants. After establishment of Slovak State in 1939, which had about 65,000 ethnic Hungarians, the party, under name Hungarian Party in Slovakia (Szlovenskói Magyar Párt), remained as one of few allowed political parties alongside the ruling Hlinka's Slovak People's Party and German minority German Party. During Slovak National Uprising (1944) the party was banned on the area controlled by insurgents. The ban was reconfirmed after the end of World War II.

The Hungarian National Party (Czechoslovakia) was a political party established in 1918 that represented the interests of the Hungarian minority in Czechoslovakia, particularly in the areas of Slovakia and Ruthenia. It was founded after the dissolution of Austria-Hungary and operated during a period of significant political upheaval and national identity formation in Central Europe. The party aimed to promote Hungarian cultural and educational rights, as well as political representation for Hungarians within the newly formed Czechoslovak state. Despite its efforts to advocate for minority rights, the party faced challenges due to Czechoslovak nationalism and the central government's policies. The Hungarian National Party was part of a broader struggle of ethnic minorities in Czechoslovakia and eventually lost influence after World War II, aligning with the geopolitical changes in the region.

Yanshan County (simplified Chinese: 盐山县; traditional Chinese: 鹽山縣; pinyin: Yánshān Xiàn; lit. 'Salt Mountain') is a county in the east of Hebei province, China, bordering Shandong to the south and east. It is under the administration of Cangzhou City, and it has a population of 411,356 residing in an area of 795 km2 (307 sq mi). China National Highway 205 runs northwest–southeast through the county.

Yanshan County is a county located in the Hebei province of China, under the jurisdiction of Qinhuangdao city. It is situated in the southeastern part of Hebei, bordering the Bohai Sea to the east. Yanshan County is known for its mountainous terrain and picturesque landscapes, which include part of the Yanshan mountain range. The county's economy is primarily based on agriculture, with local crops including grains and fruits, as well as aquaculture owing to its proximity to coastal waters. Yanshan also has historical significance, featuring various cultural and historical sites that reflect the region's rich heritage. The population is predominantly Han Chinese, with a mix of other ethnic groups. The county is well-connected by road and rail, contributing to its development and accessibility.
Marcelo Amado Djaló Taritolay (born 8 October 1993) is a professional footballer who plays as a centre-back for Uthai Thani. Born in Spain, he represents Guinea-Bissau at international level.

Marcelo Djaló is a professional footballer of Guinea-Bissauan descent, born on January 7, 1992, in Bissau, Guinea-Bissau. Primarily playing as a central defender, Djaló began his career in Portugal, where he developed through the youth ranks of several clubs, including Sporting CP. He made his professional debut with the Portuguese club Olhanense in 2011, later playing for other teams such as Olhanense, and more prominently, C.D. Aves and Marítimo. Djaló is known for his strong defensive skills and has represented the Guinea-Bissau national team, contributing to their qualification for major international tournaments. His career continues to evolve in various European leagues, where he is recognized for his versatility and experience on the field.

The European Institute of Oncology (Italian: Istituto Europeo di Oncologia, IEO) is a non-profit private-law comprehensive cancer centre located in Milan, Italy. It serves as a clinic, a research centre, and a training institution. IEO is a member of EU-LIFE, an alliance of leading life science research centres in Europe. The European Institute of Oncology works on the prevention, diagnosis and treatment of cancer by developing clinical and scientific research coupled with organisation and management. It provides a professional network for its members.

The European Institute of Oncology (Istituto Europeo di Oncologia, IEO) is a leading comprehensive cancer center located in Milan, Italy. Founded in 1994 by oncologist Umberto Veronesi, IEO focuses on cancer research, treatment, prevention, and education. The institute integrates clinical and scientific activities, promoting innovative approaches to cancer care. It features state-of-the-art facilities, including advanced imaging and surgical technology, and collaborates extensively with national and international research networks. IEO is recognized for its multidisciplinary approach, combining different specialties to provide personalized treatment plans for patients, as well as its commitment to clinical trials and translational research.
Anna Kim (born 10 September 1977, in Daejeon) is an Austrian writer.

Anna Kim is a contemporary American writer known for her contributions to fiction, poetry, and essays. Born in South Korea and raised in the United States, her multicultural background influences her work, often exploring themes of identity, belonging, and the immigrant experience. She has published several acclaimed books, including novels and poetry collections, which have garnered various literary awards and nominations. In addition to her literary work, Kim is involved in teaching and mentoring emerging writers. Her writing is characterized by its lyrical prose and nuanced characters, earning her a place among notable modern authors.

Hooding is the placing of a hood over the entire head of a prisoner. Hooding is widely considered to be a form of torture; one legal scholar considers the hooding of prisoners to be a violation of international law, specifically the Third and Fourth Geneva Conventions, which demand that persons under custody or physical control of enemy forces be treated humanely. Hooding can be dangerous to a prisoner's health and safety. It is considered to be an act of torture when its primary purpose is sensory deprivation during interrogation; it causes "disorientation, isolation, and dread." According to the International Committee of the Red Cross, hooding is used to prevent a person from seeing, to disorient them, to make them anxious, to preserve their torturer's anonymity, and to prevent the person from breathing freely. In 1997, the United Nations Committee Against Torture had concluded that hooding constituted torture, a position it reiterated in 2004 after the committee's special rapporteur had "received information on certain methods that have been condoned and used to secure information from suspected terrorists." Hooding is a common prelude to execution.

Hooding refers to the ceremonial act of placing a hood over the head of a graduate during a commencement ceremony, symbolizing the attainment of a degree or advanced academic achievement. Typically associated with master's and doctoral degrees, the hood is often made of velvet and lined with colors representing the institution and academic field. The practice has roots in medieval European universities, where hoods were originally functional garments used by scholars. Today, hooding ceremonies often include specific rituals and are an important tradition in many universities, celebrating the achievements of students and the significance of their academic journeys.

Eothyrsites ("dawn Thyrsites") is an extinct genus of prehistoric snake mackerel from the Eocene. It contains a single species, E. holosquamatus from the mid-late Eocene (late Bartonian or early Priabonian)-aged Burside Mudstone Formation of the South Island, New Zealand. It is one of the few Eocene-aged marine fishes from New Zealand known from a relatively complete skeleton. It was described by Frederick Chapman on the basis of a partial skeleton, but other fragments have also been recovered from the same site, all of which likely belong to the same individual as the holotype. Analysis of the skeleton suggests that it is most closely related to the "gemfish"-type gempylids such as Rexea and Promethichthys. Eothyrsites was likely an epipelagic predatory genus, similar to modern members of the group. Its presence in New Zealand during the Late Eocene suggests that the gempylids must have colonized the southern Pacific from their region of origin in the northern Tethys Ocean by this point.

Eothyrsites is an extinct genus of synapsid that lived during the late Carboniferous to early Permian periods, approximately 300 million years ago. It is classified within the group known as pelycosaurs, which are early relatives of mammals. Eothyrsites is notable for its unique skull structure, including large temporal fenestrae, which suggest adaptations that may have been linked to a more active lifestyle. Fossils of Eothyrsites have been found in North America, and it is considered to be one of the early representatives of the lineage leading to later therapsids, the ancestors of mammals. Its discovery has contributed to the understanding of the evolutionary transition from reptilian to mammalian characteristics during the Paleozoic era.

Shota Ishimine (伊志嶺 翔大, born May 12, 1988) is a professional baseball outfielder for the Chiba Lotte Marines of the Pacific League in Nippon Professional Baseball. Ishimine attended Tokai University. He competed for the Japanese national baseball team in the 2010 World University Baseball Championship, being named an All-Tournament outfielder, along with Mikie Mahtook and Yoenis Céspedes.

Shota Ishimine is a Japanese actor known for his work in film and television. Born on March 19, 1992, in Okinawa Prefecture, Japan, he gained popularity for his versatility in portraying various characters across different genres. Ishimine made his acting debut in the early 2010s and has since appeared in several notable Japanese dramas and movies, showcasing his talent and earning acclaim from audiences and critics alike. His performances often highlight his ability to convey deep emotions and his commitment to his roles. In addition to acting, Ishimine has engaged in stage performances and has participated in various cultural events in Japan.

Michałki-Kolonia (Polish pronunciation: [miˈxawkʲi kɔˈlɔɲa]) is a village in the administrative district of Gmina Rokitno, within Biała Podlaska County, Lublin Voivodeship, in eastern Poland, close to the border with Belarus.

Michałki-Kolonia is a village located in eastern Poland, within the administrative district of Gmina Włodawa, in Włodawa County, Lublin Voivodeship. It is situated near the border with Belarus and is part of a region characterized by its rural landscape and agricultural activities. The village is home to a small population and features traditional Polish architecture. Michałki-Kolonia is primarily known for its serene environment and local cultural heritage, contributing to the overall charm of the area. The vicinity includes various natural attractions and recreational opportunities, appealing to both residents and visitors.
The England women's cricket team toured South Africa in 2004–05, playing two women's One Day Internationals prior to the 2005 Women's Cricket World Cup.
topic: England women's cricket team in South Africa in 2004–05

text: The England women's cricket team toured South Africa from October 2004 to January 2005, participating in a series of matches that included One Day Internationals (ODIs) and Test matches. The tour featured three ODIs and a single Test match. England's performances were characterized by strong batting and effective bowling, with key contributions from players such as Charlotte Edwards and Karen Smithies. The series showcased the growing competitiveness of women's cricket and helped to strengthen the teams' profiles internationally. England emerged victorious in the ODI series, winning all three matches, while the Test match ended in a draw. This tour was significant in promoting women's cricket in both countries and served as a foundation for future international competitions.
topic: England women's cricket team in South Africa in 2004–05

text: Kanjavec is a 2,569-metre (8,428 ft) mountain in the center of Triglav National Park. It lies between the Dolič Pass at 2,164 m, the Hribarice Pass at 2,306 m and the Prehodavci Pass at 2071 m. Kanjavec is also a popular ski touring destination. From the summit, there is a panoramic view of the surrounding mountains. It is relatively easy to ascend, with a few exposed areas.

Kanjavec is a mountain located in the Julian Alps of Slovenia, standing at an elevation of 2,568 meters (8,425 feet). It is situated within the Triglav National Park and is known for its distinctive rugged terrain and scenic views. The mountain is a popular destination for hikers and climbers, offering various trails and routes that cater to different skill levels. Kanjavec is part of a group of peaks that includes the prominent Mount Triglav, which is the highest mountain in Slovenia. The region surrounding Kanjavec features rich biodiversity and is home to various wildlife species, making it a significant area for both ecological study and recreational activities.

Old Believers Church in Foļvarka (Latvian: Foļvarkas vecticībnieku kopienas lūgšanu nams) is an Old Believers place of worship in Foļvarka, in the Kastuļina Parish of Krāslava Municipality, in the Latgale region of Latvia. Its building began in 1894 and the church was incepted in 1910. During the World War II, Germans wanted to take down the church bell, but locals prevented it by giving away their money and jewelry. In the 1970s, Foļvarka church was robbed several times and from the 162 icons there is left only one. Since 2013, the church is one of the national architectural monuments of Latvia.

The Old Believers Church in Foļvarka is a historic religious structure associated with the Old Believers, a group of Eastern Orthodox Christians who separated from the main church in the 17th century due to reforms they deemed heretical. Located in Foļvarka, a village in Latvia, the church serves as a cultural and spiritual center for Old Believer communities, preserving traditional liturgy, rituals, and customs. The architectural style reflects the influences of Russian Orthodox design, characterized by ornate wooden elements and vibrant frescoes. The church is significant for its role in maintaining the identity and heritage of Old Believers in the region, and it attracts visitors interested in cultural history and religious practices.
Dactylia varia is a species of sponge in the family Callyspongiidae. They are omnivores. They also reproduce sexually. It has been found near New Zealand and Australia.

Dactylia varia is a species of flowering plant in the family Poaceae, commonly known as the grass family. This perennial grass is native to various regions and is characterized by its tufted growth habit and flexible stems. Dactylia varia can be found in diverse habitats, often occurring in disturbed areas, grasslands, and open woods. The species is notable for its adaptability and ability to thrive in different soil types. Dactylia varia plays a role in local ecosystems, providing forage for herbivores and contributing to soil stabilization. Its uses include ornamental landscaping and potential applications in erosion control.

The 1967 Vancouver Royal Canadians season was the first season in the history of the Vancouver Royal Canadians soccer club. The club played in the United Soccer Association (USA), a league that used clubs from Europe and South America to represent American and Canadian cities. First division Football League side Sunderland A.F.C, who finished 17th in the 1966–67 Football League, was the club that represented Vancouver in the USA. The club played its home games at Empire Stadium in Vancouver, British Columbia. Following the 1967 season, the USA and the National Professional Soccer League merged to form the North American Soccer League. The San Francisco Golden Gate Gales of the USA merged with Vancouver, and the club was renamed the Vancouver Royals for the inaugural season of the NASL.

The 1967 Vancouver Royal Canadians season was the inaugural season for the Canadian professional football team, the Vancouver Royal Canadians, competing in the Canadian Football League (CFL). The team was established in response to the increasing popularity of football in the region and aimed to bring professional play to Vancouver. In their debut season, the Royal Canadians finished with a record of 5 wins and 11 losses, placing them in the Western Division. Despite their struggles on the field, the team garnered significant local support and laid the foundation for future professional football in the city. The season was marked by key player performances and community engagement, setting the stage for the development of football culture in Vancouver.
Ines Pellegrini (born 7 November 1954) is an Italian retired actress of Eritrean origin.

Ines Pellegrini is a prominent Italian-American architect and educator known for her innovative designs and contributions to sustainable architecture. Born in Italy, she moved to the United States to further her studies and began her career in urban design. Pellegrini has worked on various high-profile projects that emphasize ecological sensitivity and community engagement. As a professor at several universities, she has influenced a generation of architects through her teachings on environmental design and architectural theory. Additionally, her work has been recognized with numerous awards for excellence in design and sustainability, making her a significant figure in contemporary architecture.

The caladrius (or charadrius), according to Christian mythology in the Physiologus, is a snow-white bird that lives in the king's house. It is said to be able to take the sickness into itself and then fly away, dispersing the sickness and healing both itself and the sick person. The caladrius legend formed part of medieval bestiary materials, which typically provided a Christian moralization for the animals they discussed.

Caladrius is a mythical bird from ancient Roman and medieval English folklore, often depicted as a pure white creature capable of healing the sick. According to legend, the Caladrius has the ability to absorb the illness of a person who gazes upon it and then flies away to dispose of the sickness in the sun. This bird symbolizes hope and the divine intervention of healing. It has been referenced in various literary works and is sometimes associated with the concept of resurrection and the importance of faith in recovery. The Caladrius is often depicted in medieval manuscripts and art, where it is portrayed as a noble and benevolent creature.
Cogîlniceni is a village in Rezina District, Moldova.

Cogîlniceni is a commune in the Straseni District of Moldova, comprising the villages of Cogîlniceni and Bălășești. The area is characterized by its rural setting and agricultural economy. Historically, Cogîlniceni has been influenced by Romanian culture and traditions. As of the latest census, the population is predominantly Moldovan, with a mixture of ethnic groups. The commune has local governance and serves as an administrative unit within the broader structure of Moldova's local government. Key features include its local schools, community centers, and agricultural activities.
Baron of Howorth de Sacavém, sometimes simplified to Baron of Sacavém, was a noble title created by King Luís I of Portugal, by decree of 16 July 1885, in favour of John Stott Howorth.

The title Baron of Howorth de Sacavém is a noble title in Portugal, attributed to significant figures within the Portuguese aristocracy. The barony is primarily associated with the historic region of Sacavém and is often connected to families that have played pivotal roles in the political and cultural landscape of the country. The title exemplifies the blend of English and Portuguese influences in nobility, reflecting the interconnectedness of European aristocratic traditions. As with many noble titles, the Baron of Howorth de Sacavém descends from a lineage of landowners and has been passed down through generations, playing a role in regional governance and social structure.

The IAMG Distinguished Lectureship is a special lecture series established in the year 2002 by the International Association for Mathematical Geosciences (IAMG). Each year IAMG selects IAMG Distinguished Lecturer, who is an outstanding individual with (i) demonstrated ability to communicate mathematical concepts to general geological audience, (ii) a clear enthusiasm for mathematical geology, (iii) recognition fork in their field, and (iv) established skill in working with individuals and in group discussions on geological problems. The selected IAMG Distinguished Lecturer must be ready to travel and to (i) Prepare and present a lecture suitable for a general geological audience, (ii) Prepare and present one or two lectures on a more specialized topic, and Interact and hold discussions with individuals, both professionals and students, on applications of mathematical geology to local problems of interest.

The IAMG Distinguished Lectureship is an initiative by the International Association for Mathematical Geosciences (IAMG) that aims to recognize and promote excellence in the field of mathematical geosciences. Established to highlight significant contributions and advancements in the discipline, the lectureship invites esteemed researchers to deliver talks at conferences, universities, and other educational venues. The program not only serves to disseminate knowledge but also fosters collaboration and engagement among scientists, students, and professionals in geosciences. Nominees for the lectureship are typically distinguished by their research impact, innovation, and dedication to the advancement of mathematical methods applied to geosciences.

Ann Cinnika Beiming (born 1972) is a Swedish politician and former member of the Riksdag, the national legislature. A member of the Social Democratic Party, she represented Stockholm County between October 1998 and October 2006. She was also a substitute member of the Riksdag for Pär Nuder between February 1997 and October 1998.

Cinnika Beiming is a notable figure in the field of art and cultural studies, recognized for her contributions to contemporary art theory and criticism. She is particularly known for her interdisciplinary approach, merging traditional art forms with modern technology and social commentary. Born in [birth year], Beiming has held several exhibitions in prestigious galleries worldwide and has authored numerous publications addressing the intersection of art, identity, and society. Her work often explores themes of cultural representation and challenges conventional narratives in visual arts. In addition to her artistic practice, Beiming is an active lecturer and mentor, aiming to inspire new generations of artists and scholars.
Frickley & South Elmsall Colliery was opened by the Carlton Main Colliery Company Ltd in 1903 in South Elmsall, in Yorkshire, England.

Frickley Colliery was a coal mine located near the village of South Elmsall in West Yorkshire, England. Established in the late 19th century, it became one of the significant coal producers in the region, operating under the ownership of the Frickley Colliery Company. The mine played an important role in the local economy and employed a large workforce throughout its operation. It was known for its deep mining activities and contributed to the industrial development of South Yorkshire. Frickley Colliery closed in 1993 due to declining profitability and changes in the coal industry. The site has since been redeveloped, with parts of the former colliery now serving different commercial and recreational purposes.

This is a list of FIPS 10-4 region codes from D-F, using a standardized name format, and cross-linking to articles. On September 2, 2008, FIPS 10-4 was one of ten standards withdrawn by NIST as a Federal Information Processing Standard. The list here is the last version of codes. For earlier versions, see link below.

The List of FIPS region codes (D–F) includes the Federal Information Processing Standards (FIPS) codes associated with specific geographical regions beginning with the letters D, E, and F. FIPS codes are numeric codes that were developed by the National Institute of Standards and Technology to classify regions for data processing and data sharing. This particular list covers designated regions such as states, counties, and incorporated areas primarily in the United States. Each entry includes the region's name along with its corresponding FIPS code, enabling uniform data categorization and utilization across federal and state governmental agencies. The codes serve various analytical purposes and facilitate geographic information system (GIS) applications.

Beati Paoli is the name of a secretive sect thought to have existed in medieval Sicily and possibly also in Malta. The sect, as described by the author Luigi Natoli in his historic novel I Beati Paoli (written as a series under the pseudonym William Galt in 1909, then re-published as books in 1921 and 1949), resembles an order of chivalry fighting for the poor and the commoners. Whereas the novel is fictitious, Sicily's history bears some evidence that the Beati Paoli existed. In 1071 feudalism was introduced in Sicily by its conqueror, the Norman lord Roger I de Hauteville. As the nobles started to exploit their feudal rights in the centuries to come, the Inquisition also got a foothold in Sicily. Any action by the commoners that could be interpreted by the state or the church as acts of treason or heresy was punishable by death. Such actions could be unauthorized assemblies or the formation of societies with goals other than supporting the current state/church regime. In this environment, several orders and sects rose to existence – albeit a secret one. The Beati Paoli was allegedly formed to oppose both the church and the state, defending the commoners from infringements posed by the regime. They wore black hooded coats and operated at night from their refuge in the remains of the catacombs and underground channels of Palermo. It is not known when the Beati Paoli was established, but the novel by Luigi Natoli sets the scene in the first two decades of the 18th century, mainly in the town of Palermo. The origin of the name is also unknown, although some tie it to Francis of Paola, or "Beato Paola". The Beati Paoli have the same connotation to many Sicilians as Robin Hood has to Northwestern Europeans. Today, traces of the Beati Paoli can be found in the Capo district of Palermo, where a square, a street and a restaurant bear their name. The Beati Paoli have also tradition meanings to the Maltese. A Maltese old saying -"qala' xebgħa tal-beati pawli", meaning "he was beaten up badly (the Beati Paoli way)", may refer to this old sect . Some historians suggested that the Beati Paoli were real and that may have had roots also in Malta (circa the 15th till at least to the early 19th century) and that Malta may have been the sect's last stronghold. Today there is even a small restaurant in Valletta named Beati Paoli and a statue of Saint Francis of Paola is in Saint Elmo Street corner, also in Valletta. There are even rumors that the Beati Paoli still exist today, but whether the sect is still active or not remains a mystery . If so, that would make the Beati Paoli one of the most ancient and most elusive sects in Malta.

The Beati Paoli were a secret society that emerged in 18th-century Palermo, Sicily. Originally formed in the late 1670s as a form of vigilante justice, the group became notable for its mysterious practices and aims, which included fighting against corruption and abuse of power among local nobility and officials. Members were rumored to wear black cloaks and masks, leading to an enigmatic reputation. The group's influence waned by the mid-19th century, but the Beati Paoli have since become a cultural symbol, inspiring various works of literature, film, and folklore in Sicily and beyond. Their legacy reflects themes of resistance against oppression and social justice within the context of Sicilian history.

KNBT (92.1 FM) is a radio station broadcasting an Americana music format. Licensed to New Braunfels, Texas, United States, the station serves the San Antonio area. The station is the longtime home of the New Braunfels Unicorns high school football team. The station is currently owned by New Braunfels Communications, Inc. The station is a pioneer in the genre of Americana music since adopting the format in the mid-1990s. The station also hosts frequent live concerts in studio, as well as a weekly show "Roots & Branches of Americana" hosted by Ray Wylie Hubbard.

KNBT is a radio station licensed to serve the New Braunfels, Texas area, broadcasting on the 1420 AM frequency. The station, which features a variety of programming including country music and local news, is owned by the Texas-based media company, Texas State Network. KNBT also has an FM translator that broadcasts on 94.1 FM, expanding its reach to a broader audience. Established in the mid-1990s, KNBT has become a prominent source for community-centered content and local events, contributing to the cultural landscape of New Braunfels and surrounding regions. The station's format and community engagement efforts make it a vital part of the local media ecosystem.

Safarzadeh is a surname. Notable people with the surname include: Abouzar Safarzadeh (born 1995), Iranian footballer Hajar Safarzadeh (born 2000), Iranian para-athlete Jamshid Safarzadeh (born 1982), Iranian santur player and composer Mehrdad Safarzadeh (born 1962), Iranian former cyclist Saeid Safarzadeh (born 1985), Iranian cyclist Siamak Safarzadeh (born 1964), Iranian former cyclist

Safarzadeh is a Persian surname commonly found in Iran and among Persian-speaking communities. The name is derived from the Persian word "safar," meaning "journey" or "travel," and "zadeh," which means "born of" or "descendant." Historically, individuals with this surname have contributed to various fields such as literature, music, and politics. Notable figures bearing the surname include individuals involved in Iran's cultural and artistic movements, reflecting the rich heritage associated with the name.
Erik A. Eriksson (born 1969) is a Swedish politician. He is a member of the Centre Party. He has been a member of the Parliament of Sweden since 2006.

Erik A. Eriksson (1924–2002) was a prominent Swedish-American biochemist known for his contributions to the field of molecular biology, particularly in the study of enzymes and metabolic pathways. He earned his Ph.D. in biochemistry from the University of Uppsala and later joined various research institutions in the United States. Eriksson's research helped to elucidate the biochemical mechanisms underlying cellular processes, and he published extensively in academic journals. In addition to his research, Eriksson was also involved in teaching and mentoring a generation of scientists, contributing to the advancement of biochemistry as a discipline. His work has had a lasting impact on the understanding of metabolic regulation and enzyme function.
Stenocercus scapularis is a species of lizard of the family Tropiduridae. It is found in Peru.

Stenocercus scapularis is a species of lizard in the family Tropiduridae, native to South America. It is primarily found in the tropical forests and savannas of Bolivia and Brazil. This species is characterized by its elongated body, distinctive coloration, and the presence of prominent scales along its dorsal surface. Stenocercus scapularis is typically a diurnal and terrestrial lizard, feeding on a diet of insects and other small invertebrates. The species is of interest to herpetologists due to its unique adaptations to its habitat and its role in the local ecosystem. Conservation status assessments suggest that habitat destruction poses a potential threat to its populations.
Pierre-Ignace-Liévin van Alstein (1733 – 27 December 1793) was a French slave trader.

Pierre Ignace Liévin van Alstein (1802–1872) was a Belgian painter known for his historical and genre scenes. Born in Antwerp, he trained at the Royal Academy of Fine Arts in the city and later became influenced by the Romantic movement. Van Alstein's works often depict vivid narratives, capturing moments of everyday life and significant historical events. His style is characterized by a strong use of color and attention to detail. Throughout his career, he exhibited in various salons and attracted considerable attention for his contributions to Belgian art. He is remembered as a notable figure in the 19th-century Belgian artistic landscape.

Patriota (Portuguese pronunciation: [patɾiotɐ], lit. 'Patriot'), abbreviated PATRI and formerly known as the National Ecological Party (Portuguese: Partido Ecológico Nacional, abbreviated PEN), was a right-wing to far-right political party in Brazil. It was registered in the Superior Electoral Court in the summer of 2012. The last president of the party was the former State Deputy of São Paulo Adilson Barroso, who before creating PEN was a member of the Social Christian Party. The party's Superior Electoral Court identification number was 51. Its platform involved support for conservative policies, a strong national military policy, zero tolerance politics regarding violence and crime, support for the Brazilian agrarian sector, and rejection of social and indigenous movements like the Landless Workers' Movement as well as an anti-communist stance. It stated it is against corruption in Brazil, and seeks to uphold traditional values based on Christian ethics. The party had links with the Assemblies of God, the largest Christian evangelical denomination in Brazil. After the 2022 Brazilian general elections, Patriota failed to break through the electoral threshold, thus cutting access to party subsidies and free political advertisement. Thus, in November 2023 it merged with the Brazilian Labour Party to form the Democratic Renewal Party.

Patriota is a term derived from the Spanish and Portuguese words for "patriot," denoting an individual who loves and supports their country, often demonstrated through national pride and civic engagement. The term is also used in various contexts, including political movements and organizations that emphasize nationalism or support for national interests. In contemporary contexts, "Patriota" may refer to specific political parties or groups, such as the Brazilian political party "Patriota" (formerly known as PRTB), which promotes conservative values. Additionally, the term may appear in cultural references, literature, and media, often highlighting themes of loyalty and patriotism.

The 1920 New York state election was held on November 2, 1920, to elect the governor, the lieutenant governor, the secretary state, the state comptroller, the attorney general, the state treasurer, the state engineer, two judges of the New York Court of Appeals and a U.S. Senator, as well as all members of the New York State Assembly and the New York State Senate.

The 1920 New York state election was held on November 2, 1920, to elect various state offices, including the Governor, Lieutenant Governor, and members of the New York State Legislature. The election was notable for the context of post-World War I America and the onset of the Roaring Twenties. Incumbent Republican Governor Nathan L. Strong was defeated in his bid for re-election by Democrat Al Smith, who became the first Catholic to win the governorship of New York. The election also reflected changing political sentiments, with strong support for Prohibition among voters, setting the stage for future political dynamics in the state. Overall, the 1920 election marked a significant shift in New York politics and laid the groundwork for Smith's later national prominence.

The J.H. Cerilles State College (JHCSC; formerly known as Josefina H. Cerilles Polytechnic College) is a public college in the Philippines. Its main campus is located in Mati, San Miguel, Zamboanga del Sur. On February 20, 1995, Republic Act No. 7895 was signed into law converting the then Josefine H. Cerilles National High School into a college to become Josefina H. Cerilles Polytechnic College. By virtue of R.A. 9159 authored by Cong. Aurora Cerilles, the college was converted into J.H. Cerilles State College. This state college is the smallest yet fastest growing state college in its first three years of operation. The local political climate has a constant impact on its operation and development.

J.H. Cerilles State College (JHCSC) is a public college located in the province of Zamboanga del Sur, Philippines. Established in 2002 through Republic Act No. 9258, it was named after a prominent local politician and educator, Jose H. Cerilles. The college aims to provide quality education and vocational training to enhance the skills and competencies of its students. JHCSC offers a variety of undergraduate programs in fields such as agriculture, business administration, education, and engineering, as well as graduate programs. The institution is committed to community development and has initiatives that engage students in local outreach and service programs.
St Ters of Antwerp is one of the names given to the statue of a phallic saint, supposedly worshiped for promoting fertility.

Sullivan Amory Meredith (1816–1874) was an American businessman and volunteer army officer. He became a Colonel in the Union Army at the outset of the American Civil War, serving throughout the war, raising and leading two regiments of volunteers, and rising to the rank of brigadier general. In 1868 he penned a series of letters to the Buffalo Commercial Advertiser, in which he attacked Robert Ould, the former Confederate Commissioner for the exchange of prisoners of war.

Sullivan Amory Meredith (1821–1864) was a notable American soldier and politician from Virginia. He served as a Confederate Army officer during the American Civil War, achieving the rank of brigadier general. Meredith participated in various significant battles, including the First Battle of Bull Run and the Battle of Gettysburg. After the war, he returned to Virginia, where he was involved in local politics and community affairs. His contributions to both military service and post-war governance reflect the complexities of the Southern experience during and after the Civil War era. Meredith's legacy is remembered in the context of his leadership and service during a pivotal time in American history.

Abdallah ibn Azzuz or Abu Mohammed Abdallah ibn Azzuz al-Kurashi al-Shadhili al-Marrakushi also known as Sidi Balla (died 1789) was a writer from Morocco. He mainly wrote on mysticism, but also on medicine. Ibn Azzuz's Mahab al-kuzuf wa-nafy al-zulumai fi il al-tibb wa l-tabai wa l-hikma, a collection of therapeutic formulae, was especially well-known. His work about medicinal plants, Kashf al-rumuz was equally well-known.

Abdallah ibn Azzuz was a prominent figure in the 12th century, known for his contributions to Islamic scholarship and literature. Hailing from the region of Al-Andalus, he gained recognition for his extensive knowledge of theology, philosophy, and science. Ibn Azzuz became a notable teacher and scholar, influencing a generation of students with his innovative ideas and writings. His works, although lesser-known in the broader spectrum of Islamic literature, reflect the intellectual vibrancy of medieval Islamic culture and contribute to the understanding of the historical context in which he lived. His legacy endures through the appreciation of his contributions to education and scholarly thought in the Islamic world.

Anthony Harry Leviero (November 23, 1905 – September 3, 1956) was an American journalist who spent over two decades as a reporter for The New York Times. He won the Pulitzer Prize for National Reporting in 1952.

Anthony Leviero (1910–1990) was an American journalist and columnist known for his work with the New York Journal-American and later the New York Post. He gained prominence in the mid-20th century for his sports reporting, particularly covering baseball, and became recognized for his colorful writing style and insightful analysis. Leviero was part of the inaugural group of sportswriters to travel with Major League Baseball teams, significantly influencing how sports journalism evolved. He received various accolades throughout his career and left a lasting impact on sports media before his retirement in the 1980s.

The 2020 Shimadzu All Japan Indoor Tennis Championships was a professional tennis tournament played on indoor hardcourts. It was the second edition of the tournament which was part of the 2020 ITF Women's World Tennis Tour. It took place in Kyoto, Japan between 17 and 23 February 2020.

text: The 2020 Shimadzu All Japan Indoor Tennis Championships was a professional tennis tournament held in Kyoto, Japan, from February 10 to February 16, 2020. It featured both men's and women's events and was played on indoor hard courts. The tournament is part of the Japan Tennis Association’s annual calendar and attracts top domestic talent. In the men's singles final, Yuichi Sugita claimed the title, while the women's singles title was won by Nao Hibino. The event serves as a key competition for Japanese players to showcase their skills and gain ranking points.

text: Frederick David Bryan Wrench (27 November 1936–18 June 2018) was a British rugby union player and politician.

David Wrench is a former rugby union player from New Zealand, known for his position as a flanker. He played professionally for various clubs during his career, including notable stints in both New Zealand and overseas leagues. Wrench gained recognition for his athleticism, work rate, and tactical understanding of the game. In addition to his club career, he represented regional teams, contributing to their successes in domestic competitions. After retiring from professional play, he has remained involved in rugby through coaching and development initiatives, fostering new talent in the sport.

Channel 16 VHF (156.800 MHz) is a marine VHF radio frequency designated as an international distress frequency. Primarily intended for distress, urgency and safety priority calls, the frequency may also carry routine calls used to establish communication before switching to another working channel.

Channel 16 VHF is a maritime radio channel that operates in the Very High Frequency (VHF) band at a frequency of 156.800 MHz. It is designated as an international distress, safety, and calling frequency for maritime communications. Primarily used by ships and coast stations, Channel 16 is monitored by the relevant authorities for emergency situations, allowing vessels in distress to communicate their needs. In addition to emergency broadcasts, it is also used for routine communications to establish contact before switching to another channel for conversation. Channel 16 is regulated by the International Telecommunication Union (ITU) and is recognized globally, making it a crucial component of maritime safety protocols.

Wasawasa is a popular dish, eaten in Dagbon and other parts of Northern Ghana. It is also eaten in some West African countries, such as Burkina Faso, Benin, Nigeria etc. It is made from dried yam peelings called jaling, which have been grounded into flour and steamed. Wasawasa is mostly eaten with spicy sauces and sometimes garnished with vegetables, accompanied with shea butter or raw groundnut oil and fried fish. Wasawasa is sometimes served with beans, pasta, and salad.

Wasawasa, often referred to in psychological contexts, describes intrusive and persistent thoughts or mental imagery that can lead to feelings of anxiety or discomfort. It is associated with conditions such as obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) and can manifest in various forms, including fears, doubts, or distressing memories. The term is derived from the concept of "wasa," which means a mental disturbance in some cultures. Treatment for wasawasa typically involves cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT), mindfulness practices, and in some cases, medication to help manage symptoms.
Philipotabanus is a genus of horse fly belonging to the family Tabanidae subfamily Tabaninae.

Philibotabanus is a genus of horse flies within the family Tabanidae, characterized by their large size and distinctive wing veining patterns. They are typically found in temperate and tropical regions around the world, often inhabiting areas near water bodies where their larvae develop. Adult Philobotabanus females are known to be blood-feeders, primarily targeting large mammals, including livestock and wildlife, which can pose challenges for animal husbandry due to potential disease transmission. Research into their behavior, ecology, and control measures is ongoing, as these flies are of significant interest for both veterinary and environmental studies.

Renzo Renzi (born c. 1946) is a Sammarinese politician. Renzi became a communist activist at the age of seventeen. In 1983 he was a member of the secretariat of the Sammarinese Communist Party. In October 1983 he was sworn in as one of the two Captains Regent (i.e. head of state of San Marino) for a six-month period. He was 37 years old at the time.

Renzo Renzi (born 26 November 1946) is an Italian politician known for his tenure as the mayor of the city of Faenza, Italy. A member of the Democratic Party, Renzi has played a significant role in local governance and development projects within the region of Emilia-Romagna. He is recognized for his efforts in promoting civic engagement and addressing urban challenges. Additionally, Renzi has been involved in various cultural and social initiatives aimed at enhancing the quality of life in his community. His leadership style emphasizes collaboration, transparency, and sustainability.
Ascension Guerra Gonzalez (born 28 February 1955 in Madrid) is a Spanish archer.

Ascension Guerra is a contemporary artist known for her vibrant, large-scale abstract paintings that explore themes of identity, culture, and emotional expression. Born in Puerto Rico in the mid-1980s, Guerra moved to the United States to pursue her artistic career, where she gained recognition for her innovative use of color and texture. Her work often incorporates elements from her Caribbean heritage, blending traditional motifs with modern artistic techniques. Guerra has exhibited her work in various prestigious galleries and institutions, contributing to the discourse on the role of cultural narratives in contemporary art. She is also involved in community outreach programs, promoting art education and cultural awareness.
Maximiliano R. Ramírez (born October 11, 1984) is a Venezuelan former professional baseball catcher. He played in Major League Baseball (MLB) for numerous teams.

Max Ramírez is a former professional baseball catcher from the United States, born on July 15, 1984, in San Pedro de Macorís, Dominican Republic. He played in Major League Baseball (MLB) for the Texas Rangers and the Florida Marlins between 2009 and 2012. Ramírez was originally drafted by the Tampa Bay Devil Rays in the 2004 MLB Draft and later traded to the Rangers, where he made his MLB debut. Known for his strong offensive skills as a switch hitter, Ramírez had a notable minor league career, earning several accolades before transitioning to professional baseball in various international leagues following his MLB career.

The St. Nicholas Ukrainian Catholic Church was built in 1909 to serve the growing immigrant community. Preceding World War I, Wilmington experienced an exponential influx of new residents from Ukraine. The neighborhood that subsenquently became known as “Little Ukraine” was home to Saints Peter and Paul Ukrainian Orthodox Church, and also to St. Nicholas Ukrainian Catholic Church. The church became the pulse of the Ukrainian community, serving as a gathering place for debates and resolutions on current events both local and national. Eventually, the church membership outgrew the capacity of the structure to accommodate all, and in 1968 erected a new building on Miller Road and Lea Boulevard. The South Heald St. structure was eventually sold to the New Calvary Baptist Church.
topic: St. Nicholas Ukrainian Catholic Church (Wilmington, Delaware)

text: St. Nicholas Ukrainian Catholic Church is a parish of the Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church located in Wilmington, Delaware. Founded in the early 20th century to serve the local Ukrainian immigrant community, the church has played a vital role in preserving Ukrainian culture and traditions in the area. The church's architecture reflects Eastern European influences, and it serves as a center for religious services, cultural events, and community gatherings. Over the years, St. Nicholas has been involved in various outreach and charitable efforts, aiming to support both the parish community and the wider society in Wilmington.
topic: St. Nicholas Ukrainian Catholic Church (Wilmington, Delaware)

text: A Nait The or Massage Practitioner (Burmese: အနှိပ်သည်) is a 1922 Burmese black & white silent comedy short film.

A massage practitioner is a trained professional who provides massage therapy to manipulate soft tissues of the body, primarily for therapeutic, rehabilitation, and relaxation purposes. Practitioners utilize various techniques, including Swedish, deep tissue, sports, and trigger point massage, to address physical ailments, reduce stress, and enhance overall well-being. The profession is often regulated, requiring practitioners to complete formal education and obtain certification or licensure, depending on the region. Massage therapy is recognized for its potential benefits, including pain relief, increased circulation, and improved flexibility; however, specific conditions may contraindicate its use, warranting a careful and informed approach from practitioners.

Several vessels have borne the name Enchantress: Enchantress, launched in 1802 at Ringmore, became HMS Enchantress (1804) Enchantress (1818 ship) was launched at Plymouth in 1818. Between 1821 and 1823 she made one voyage as a sealer to the South Shetland Islands. There the Enchantress Rocks are named for her. After her return to England she traded widely. In 1826 pirates plundered her in the Mediterranean. She was last listed in 1864. Enchantress (1825 ship) was launched at Bristol. She was wrecked on 16 July 1835 at Van Diemen's Land. Enchantress (1828 ship) was launched at Bristol as a West Indiaman. She then traded with India as an East Indiaman, sailing under a license issued by the British East India Company. She next transported convicts to Van Diemen's Land. She was wrecked in February 1837 at Bermuda. Enchantress (1852) was launched from the shipyard of J. C. Smith, Hoboken, New Jersey. She was 450 tons burthen and commanded by Captain William J. Jyler. Enchantress (yacht) 19th-century racing yacht, winner of several national and international Cups. Enchantress (pilot boat) 19th-century Sandy Hook pilot boat built in 1851 by John Maginn.

Enchantress was a vessel originally launched in the early 19th century, recognized for its significance in maritime history and various roles throughout its operational life. It served multiple purposes, including merchant and passenger transport. The ship operated under various flags and ownerships, reflecting the shipping practices of the time. Enchantress gained a measure of historical interest due to its involvement in significant voyages and trade routes, contributing to the development of maritime commerce. The ship eventually fell into obscurity and was lost to time, with detailed records of its final fate being scarce. Its legacy remains as a representation of early 19th-century seafaring endeavors.

The Community of Democratic Choice Youth Forum (CDC Youth Forum) is an international organization registered in Riga, Latvia. It aims to promote partnership and dialogue among youth from the region of the Community of Democratic Choice. Founded under the name Youth Forum of Europe's New Democracies, it arose from a youth forum that was part of the Vilnius Conference 2006 of the Community of Democratic Choice.
