The youth forum consists of members from Armenia, Azerbaijan, Belarus, Slovenia, Bulgaria, Estonia, Germany, Georgia, Latvia, Lithuania, Moldova, Poland, Romania, Russia, Ukraine and United Kingdom. The main task of the forum is the promotion of democracy, human rights and the rule of law. In March 2007, delegates of the forum met in Tallinn, Estonia to institutionalize the forum and it was renamed into Community of Democratic Choice Youth Forum.
topic: Community of Democratic Choice Youth Forum

text: The Community of Democratic Choice Youth Forum (CDC Youth Forum) is an initiative established to engage young leaders from member countries in promoting democratic values, civic engagement, and regional cooperation. Formed in the early 2000s as a platform for youth interested in democracy and development, the forum focuses on fostering dialogue, sharing best practices, and encouraging active participation in political processes. It aims to empower young people by providing them a voice on issues affecting their communities and by facilitating collaboration between youth organizations across participating nations. The CDC Youth Forum is affiliated with the Community of Democratic Choice, which seeks to strengthen democratic institutions and civil societies in Eastern Europe and adjacent regions.
topic: Community of Democratic Choice Youth Forum

text: Sadie Carrie Owney Horton (October 25, 1899 – December 8, 1997) was an American female mariner who worked aboard an East Coast U.S. Merchant Marine barge. In February 2017, Horton posthumously received official U.S. military veteran's status for her wartime service, becoming the first recorded female Merchant Marine veteran of World War II.

Sadie O. Horton (1891–1982) was an American civil rights activist and educator known for her significant contributions to the civil rights movement in the United States, particularly in Ohio. Born in a segregated community, Horton dedicated her life to advocating for racial equality and education for African Americans. She served as a teacher and later became involved in various local and national organizations aimed at combatting racial discrimination. Horton’s efforts included organizing community events, participating in protests, and promoting educational programs for black youth. Her legacy is marked by her commitment to fostering social change and empowering marginalized communities through education and activism.

Hannover Bridge is a container ship registered in Panama but has a Japanese ship-owner and German name. The ship carries containers, which is the fastest-developing sector in cargo shipping. Hannover Bridge was built in 2006 in the ship-yard of IHI Marine United in Kure, Hiroshima, Japan. The ship can carry 8,212 TEUs, according to company requirements, but IMO claims that the vessel's capacity is larger than ten thousand. The operator of Hannover Bridge is the container shipping sector of K Line Group, which maintains a fleet of 55 container ships. The gross tonnage of Hannover Bridge is 98,747, the ship's length is 336 metres, and its beam is 46 metres. The vessel's main diesel engine is a Daewoo and allows to the ship to reach a maximum speed of 24 knots. The cruising speed of Hannover Bridge is 21 knots and the economy speed is 20.5 knots. All these speeds are reached with more than 20% lower consumption of heavy fuel. The emissions of CO2 are decreased in the new innovated diesel engine, which is less harmful to the environment.

Hannover Bridge is a notable bridge located in Hannover, Germany, spanning the River Leine. The bridge serves as a vital connection for road traffic and pedestrians, linking different parts of the city and enhancing urban mobility. Originally opened in the early 20th century, its design reflects the architectural styles of that period, combining functionality with aesthetic appeal. The bridge has undergone several renovations over the years to accommodate increasing traffic demands and to ensure structural integrity. It is an important landmark within Hannover, contributing to the city’s infrastructure and serving as a symbol of the area's historical and cultural significance.
The Bivings-Converse House is a historic house located at 1 Douglas Street near Glendale, Spartanburg County, South Carolina.

The Bivings-Converse House is a historic residence located in the West End neighborhood of Boston, Massachusetts. Constructed in 1835, the house showcases Greek Revival architectural style, characterized by its symmetrical façade and classic portico. The property was originally built for prominent Bostonian John Bivings and later acquired by merchant William Converse. It has been recognized for its cultural significance and historical value, being listed on the National Register of Historic Places. The Bivings-Converse House represents the affluent lifestyle of early 19th-century Boston and is an example of the transitional architecture from the Federal to the Greek Revival period in the United States.

Rhoden is a surname. Notable people with the surname include: Christian Rhoden (born 1974), German high jumper Dwight Rhoden, American choreographer George Rhoden (born 1926), Jamaican athlete Hermann Rhodén (born 1993), Norwegian footballer Jared Rhoden (born 1999), American basketball player John Rhoden (1918–2001), American sculptor Larry Rhoden (born 1959), American politician Phil Rhoden (born 1945), Australian rules footballer Philip Rhoden (1914–2003), Australian army officer Richanda Rhoden (1917-2016), Native-American painter Rick Rhoden (born 1953), American golfer and former baseball player Rudolf Klein-Rhoden (1871–1936), German actor Shawn Rhoden (1975–2021), Jamaican-born American bodybuilder Wayne Rhoden, Jamaican singer William C. Rhoden, American sports journalist

Rhoden is a surname of English origin, predominantly found in the United States. It is thought to derive from various geographical locations in England, such as the village of Rhoden in Lancashire. The name has various spellings and forms, including Rhoden, Rhoades, and Roaden, with variations appearing in historical records. Individuals with the surname have made notable contributions in various fields, including sports, academia, and the arts. The surname is also associated with a few prominent figures, which has further popularized its recognition.

In chemistry, a pnictogen bond (PnB) is a non-covalent interaction, occurring where there is a net attractive force between an electrophilic region on a 'donor' pnictogen atom (Pn) in a molecule, and a nucleophilic region on an 'acceptor' atom, which may be in the same or another molecule. Closely related to halogen and chalcogen bonding, pnictogen bonds are a form of non-covalent interaction which can be considered in terms of charge-transfer and electrostatic interactions.

A pnictogen bond is a type of non-covalent interaction that occurs between a pnictogen atom (elements from group 15 of the periodic table, including nitrogen, phosphorus, arsenic, antimony, and bismuth) and a Lewis base. It is characterized by the involvement of the pnictogen's lone pair of electrons, which allows it to act as an electron-deficient site that attracts electron-rich species. This bond is similar to hydrogen bonding but generally involves heavier pnictogens, leading to distinct physical and chemical properties. Pnictogen bonding has garnered interest in areas such as supramolecular chemistry, materials science, and drug design, contributing to the understanding of molecular interactions and the design of new compounds.
Kondapi is a village in Prakasam district of the Indian state of Andhra Pradesh. It is the mandal headquarters of Kondapi mandal in Kandukur revenue division.

Kondapi is a village located in the Prakasam district of Andhra Pradesh, India. It falls under the jurisdiction of the Kandukur revenue division and is part of the Srikakulam mandal. The village is primarily known for its agricultural practices, with local farmers engaging in the cultivation of various crops, including paddy and groundnut. Kondapi features a mix of traditional and contemporary lifestyles, with basic amenities such as schools, healthcare facilities, and local markets serving its population. The village is also characterized by its cultural heritage, with celebrations of local festivals and traditions playing a significant role in the community’s social life.

Bhujel are a Tibeto-Burman ethnic group now living in Nepal, India, and Bhutan. They are scattered in several districts, mostly in Tanahu and Syangja. The inhabitants living near the Bhuji Khola river called Bhujel. Bhujel are divided into four subcastes – Bhujyal, Gharti, Nisel and Khawas.

Bhujel is an ethnic group primarily found in the hilly regions of Nepal, particularly in the central and eastern parts of the country. They belong to the broader Magar ethnic community and are considered to be one of the indigenous people of Nepal. The Bhujel people mainly speak the Bhujel language, which is a part of the Indo-Aryan language family. Traditionally, they are engaged in agriculture and farming, with social structures often based on clan affiliations. The culture of the Bhujel is characterized by unique traditions, rituals, and folk songs, reflecting their rich historical heritage. In recent years, efforts have been made to preserve their cultural identity amid modernization and urbanization in Nepal.

Lewis Sprague Mills Sr. (; 5 September 1874 – 7 March 1965) was an American educator, photographer, writer, and local politician noted for his contributions to Connecticut history and to education in Northwestern Connecticut. In November 1960 a regional high school was built in Burlington, Connecticut which was chosen to be named Lewis S. Mills High School after Mills' contributions to education in the area.

Lewis S. Mills (born April 18, 1995) is an American professional basketball player known for his versatility and athleticism on the court. He played college basketball at Williams College, where he achieved All-American honors. After going undrafted in the 2017 NBA Draft, Mills pursued a professional career overseas, playing for various teams in Europe and Asia. He is recognized for his scoring ability, defensive skills, and leadership qualities, contributing significantly to the teams he has played for. Off the court, Mills is involved in community service, focusing on youth development programs.
Eduardo de Zulueta y Dato (4 December 1923 – 28 July 2020) was a Spanish nobleman and a diplomat.

Eduardo de Zulueta y Dato (1849–1936) was a Spanish politician and diplomat known for his role in the political landscape of early 20th-century Spain. Born in the province of Santa Cruz de Tenerife, Zulueta served in various governmental positions, including as a minister and later as a senator. He was associated with the Liberal party and played a significant part in the transitions between different Spanish governments during the critical years leading up to the Spanish Civil War. His legacy includes contributions to political reforms and diplomatic relations while navigating the complexities of Spanish politics during a tumultuous era. He passed away in Madrid, leaving behind a notable political legacy.

The 1991–92 Georgia Southern Eagles men's basketball team represented Georgia Southern University during the 1991–92 NCAA Division I men's basketball season. The Eagles, led by 10th-year head coach Frank Kerns, played their home games at Hanner Fieldhouse in Statesboro, Georgia as members of the Trans America Athletic Conference. The team won the regular season conference title and the TAAC tournament to earn an automatic bid to the NCAA tournament. As the No. 15 seed in the Southeast region, the Eagles lost in the opening round to Oklahoma State, 100–73, to finish with a 25–6 record (13–1 TAAC).
topic: 1991–92 Georgia Southern Eagles men's basketball team

text: The 1991–92 Georgia Southern Eagles men's basketball team represented Georgia Southern University during the 1991–92 NCAA Division I men's basketball season. Under the leadership of head coach Frank Kearse, the team played its home games at Hanner Fieldhouse in Statesboro, Georgia. The Eagles finished the season with a record of 19 wins and 12 losses, including a 12–6 mark in the Southern Conference, allowing them to finish in second place in the conference standings. The team made an appearance in the 1992 NCAA Tournament, where they competed in the East Regional but were eliminated in the first round. This season marked a significant period in the history of Georgia Southern men's basketball as they continued to build their program in Division I competition.
topic: 1991–92 Georgia Southern Eagles men's basketball team

text: Dame Anne Margaret Glover (born 6 February 1954) is CEO and co-founder of Amadeus Capital Partners, a venture capital firm that invests in European high-technology companies.

Anne Glover is a prominent Scottish businesswoman known for her roles in technology and venture capital. She served as the CEO of Amadeus Capital Partners, a leading UK venture capital firm, where she was instrumental in fostering innovation and investment in technology startups. Glover has been recognized for her efforts to promote diversity in the tech industry and has held various board positions in several high-profile companies and organizations. Her leadership and contributions have made her a significant figure in entrepreneurship and investment in the UK.
Livhuwani Ethel Ligaraba is a South African politician who has been a Member of Parliament (MP) for the African National Congress.

Livhuwani Ethel Ligaraba is a South African politician and member of the African National Congress (ANC). She has served as a Member of the National Assembly of South Africa, where she has been involved in various legislative initiatives and community outreach programs. Ligaraba is known for her focus on issues such as education, health, and socio-economic development in underserved communities. Her career reflects a commitment to public service and the advancement of social justice in South Africa.
Dudua tetanota is a moth of the family Tortricidae. It is found in Thailand and India.

Dudua tetanota is a species of moth belonging to the family Erebidae, commonly found in various regions of the Americas. First described by the entomologist Francis Walker in 1858, this moth exhibits a distinct appearance characterized by its striking coloration and patterns, which serve as camouflage against predators. The life cycle of Dudua tetanota includes various stages from egg to larva, pupa, and adult, with larvae primarily feeding on specific host plants. The species plays a role in the ecosystem as both a pollinator and as a prey species for various predators. Its conservation status remains relatively stable, although habitat destruction poses potential threats to its populations.

In enzymology, a lignostilbene alphabeta-dioxygenase (EC 1.13.11.43) is an enzyme that catalyzes the chemical reaction 1,2-bis(4-hydroxy-3-methoxyphenyl)ethylene + O2 ⇌ {\displaystyle \rightleftharpoons } 2 vanillin Thus, the two substrates of this enzyme are 1,2-bis(4-hydroxy-3-methoxyphenyl)ethylene and O2, whereas its product is vanillin. This enzyme belongs to the family of oxidoreductases, specifically those acting on single donors with O2 as oxidant and incorporation of two atoms of oxygen into the substrate (oxygenases). The oxygen incorporated need not be derived from O2. The systematic name of this enzyme class is 1,2-bis(4-hydroxy-3-methoxyphenyl)ethylene:oxygen oxidoreductase (alphabeta-bond-cleaving). It employs one cofactor, iron.

Lignostilbene alphabeta-dioxygenase is an enzyme that catalyzes the oxidative cleavage of lignostilbene, a type of stilbene compound, in the presence of molecular oxygen. This enzyme plays a critical role in the metabolic pathways of certain fungi and plants, facilitating the degradation of lignin and contributing to the overall carbon cycle. It introduces two oxygen atoms into the lignostilbene substrate, transforming it into various products that can be further metabolized or utilized in cellular processes. Studies on lignostilbene alphabeta-dioxygenase enhance the understanding of plant-microbe interactions and the biochemical transformations of aromatic compounds. The enzyme's unique mechanisms and potential applications in biotechnology and environmental science continue to be a subject of research.
Wendy Turnbull and Virginia Wade defeated Rosie Casals and Sharon Walsh-Pete in the final, 3–6, 6–3, 7–5.

The Ladies' invitation doubles at the 1992 Wimbledon Championships was a special doubles event held during the prestigious tennis tournament. It featured a select group of female players, including former champions and prominent figures in women's tennis. The competition was part of the Wimbledon tradition that celebrates the history of the sport and honors notable players. The event took place on the outdoor grass courts of the All England Club in Wimbledon, London, contributing to the rich legacy of the tournament. The 1992 edition highlighted the skills and sportsmanship of its participants, with matches drawing attention from fans and media alike.

Guy Learmonth (born 20 April 1992 in Berwick-upon-Tweed) is a Scottish and Great British athlete specialising in the 800 metres. His personal best currently stands as 1:44.73 and he is the third fastest Scottish 800m athlete of all time. Learmonth reached the final at the 2014 Commonwealth Games, the 2015 European Indoor Championships and the 2022 World Athletics Indoor Championships, finishing sixth overall in all three. He was named the GB&NI captain for the European Indoor Championships in 2019 and also competed outdoors at the 2017 World Championships. He broke the Scottish 800m Junior Record 9 times, and holds the English AAA U20 Indoor & BUCS Championship records.

Guy Learmonth is a Scottish middle-distance runner born on November 23, 1993, in Edinburgh. He specializes in the 800 meters event and has represented Great Britain at various international competitions, including the European Championships and the Commonwealth Games. Learmonth gained prominence for his strong performances on the track and has set personal bests that place him among Scotland's top middle-distance athletes. He is known for his competitive spirit and has contributed to promoting athletics in Scotland.
Koen is an unincorporated community in Prowers County, Colorado, United States.

Koen is an unincorporated community located in Otero County, Colorado, United States. It is part of the larger La Junta area. The community is situated along the Arkansas River and is characterized by its rural landscape and agricultural surroundings. Koen does not have a formal governance structure as it is unincorporated, which means it is administered as part of the county. The area is primarily served by the local school district and is noted for its small-town charm and proximity to larger urban centers.

Andrew David Davies (born 5 May 1952) is a Welsh politician who represented the constituency of Swansea West in the National Assembly for Wales from 1999 to 2011. He is a member of Welsh Labour. Davies was born in Hereford to Welsh parents; his mother is from Llandeilo and his father from Holywell, Flintshire. Davies attended Hereford Cathedral School and Swansea University, where he trained as a teacher.

Andrew Davies is a Welsh Labour politician who has served as a Member of the National Assembly for Wales (AM) since its establishment in 1999. He represents the South Wales Central constituency and has held various positions within the Welsh Government, including Minister for Finance and Minister for Transport. Davies has been influential in shaping policies related to economic development and public transport in Wales. Before entering politics, he worked in the public sector and for the Labour Party in various capacities. His legislative work focuses on issues such as infrastructure, social justice, and environmental sustainability.

This page lists Japan-related articles with romanized titles beginning with the letter M. For names of people, please list by surname (i.e., "Tarō Yamada" should be listed under "Y", not "T"). Please also ignore particles (e.g. "a", "an", "the") when listing articles (i.e., "A City with No People" should be listed under "City").

The Index of Japan-related articles (M) is a curated listing of topics, articles, and subcategories in Wikipedia that begin with the letter "M" and are related to Japan. This index serves as a navigational tool for users seeking information about various aspects of Japanese culture, history, geography, economy, and other relevant fields. It includes entries on notable figures, places, events, and concepts associated with Japan, allowing for easier access to relevant content and facilitating comprehensive understanding of Japan's contributions to global society.
Manar Khaled Fayyad or Al Fayyad (Arabic: منار خالد فياض) is a Jordanian chemist and former President of the German Jordanian University.

Manar Fayyad is a Palestinian artist and activist known for her contributions to contemporary art and her advocacy for social justice issues in the Palestinian community. Fayyad's work often addresses themes of identity, displacement, and the human experience through various mediums, including painting, photography, and installation. She has exhibited her art in multiple galleries and cultural institutions both locally and internationally, earning recognition for her powerful visual narratives that reflect the struggles and resilience of her people. Fayyad is also involved in community outreach, using art as a tool for education and empowerment.
Berkelium(III) nitride is a binary inorganic compound of berkelium and nitrogen with the chemical formula BkN.

Berkelium(III) nitride is an inorganic compound with the formula BkN, consisting of berkelium in its +3 oxidation state and nitrogen. It is part of the actinide series and is notable for its radioactivity and rarity, given that berkelium is a synthetic element typically produced in trace amounts in nuclear reactors. This compound is of interest in materials science and nuclear chemistry due to its potential applications in advanced nuclear materials and as a source of neutron production. Due to the challenges associated with working with radioactive materials, research on berkelium(III) nitride is limited, and its properties are less well-characterized compared to more stable nitrides.
TV Jangadeiro (channel 12) is a television station licensed to Fortaleza, Ceará, affiliated with SBT and it is part of the pool of enterprises named Sistema Jangadeiro de Comunicação.

TV Jangadeiro is a Brazilian television network based in Fortaleza, Ceará. It was founded in 1992 and is part of the Sistema Jangadeiro de Comunicação, which also includes radio stations and other media outlets. The network originally focused on local programming, including news, sports, and entertainment, and has gradually expanded its reach in the Northeastern region of Brazil. TV Jangadeiro is associated with several national television networks, allowing it to broadcast a variety of programs while maintaining a strong emphasis on regional content. The station is known for its commitment to community engagement and local culture.

The Air Rescue Wing Matsushima Detachment (松島救難隊 (matsushima-kyūnan-tai)) is a unit of the Air Rescue Wing of the Japan Air Self-Defense Force. It is also known as the Matsushima Air Rescue Squadron. Responsible for airborne search and rescue, it is based at Matsushima Air Base in Iwate Prefecture. It is equipped with UH-60J and U-125A aircraft.

The Air Rescue Wing Matsushima Detachment is a unit of the Japan Air Self-Defense Force (JASDF), primarily responsible for air rescue operations. Located in Matsushima, Miyagi Prefecture, the detachment operates search and rescue missions using helicopters and other aviation resources. Established to provide rapid response capabilities for disaster relief and emergency assistance, the unit plays a critical role in supporting civilian authorities, especially during natural disasters such as earthquakes and tsunamis. The Matsushima Detachment is equipped with advanced technology and highly trained personnel, ensuring effective support for both domestic and international humanitarian efforts.
Deniz Barış (born 2 July 1977) is a Turkish former professional footballer who played as a defensive midfielder or centre-back.

Deniz Barış is a retired Turkish footballer born on 19 July 1983 in Istanbul, Turkey. Primarily a defender, he began his professional career with Zeytinburnuspor and subsequently played for several clubs in Turkey, including MKE Ankaragücü, where he gained recognition. Barış is also known for his time at other clubs such as Konyaspor and Manisaspor. He made significant contributions to each team he played for, earning respect for his defensive skills and leadership on the pitch. After retiring from professional football, he transitioned into coaching and football management.

Sun (Italian: Sole) is a 1929 Italian silent drama film directed by Alessandro Blasetti and starring Marcello Spada, Vasco Creti and Dria Paola. The film was set around the planned draining of the Pontine Marshes by Benito Mussolini's Fascist government. It was shot partly on location, which added a sense of realism. Mussolini was impressed by the result and described it as "the dawn of the Fascist film". The film was destroyed during the Second World War, and survives only in still photographs.

"Sun" is a 2021 Indian drama film directed by A. L. Vijay. The story revolves around the themes of family, sacrifice, and resilience, highlighting the journey of a father who navigates life's challenges to support his children. The film stars notable actors including Dulquer Salmaan and Aditi Rao Hydari, and features a score composed by the acclaimed music director Ilaiyaraaja. "Sun" received mixed reviews from critics, with praise for its emotional depth and performances, while some criticism was directed towards its pacing and storyline execution. The film contributes to the ongoing conversation about familial bonds and the struggles of parenting in contemporary society.
The Waterloo-Cedar Falls Courier is an online newspaper that is published three times a week by Lee Enterprises for people living in Waterloo and Cedar Falls, Iowa as well as northeast Iowa.

The Waterloo-Cedar Falls Courier is a daily newspaper based in Waterloo, Iowa, serving the surrounding areas of Cedar Falls and northeastern Iowa. Founded in 1892, it provides local, state, and national news coverage, along with features on sports, entertainment, and community events. The newspaper has a significant online presence, offering digital subscriptions and a website that includes multimedia content. The Courier operates under the ownership of Lee Enterprises, which oversees several newspapers across the United States. It plays a vital role in the community by keeping residents informed and engaged with local issues and events.

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly is a quarterly academic journal of psychoanalysis established in 1932 and, since 2018, published by Taylor & Francis. The journal describes itself as "the oldest free-standing psychoanalytic journal in America". The current editor-in-chief is Neal Vorus.

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly is a peer-reviewed academic journal that focuses on the field of psychoanalysis. Established in 1932, the journal publishes scholarly articles, theoretical discussions, and clinical case studies that advance psychoanalytic theory and practice. It serves as a platform for psychoanalysts, psychologists, and other mental health professionals to share research and insights related to psychoanalytic concepts and treatments. The journal is known for its emphasis on the integration of psychoanalytic theory with contemporary psychological understanding and is recognized as a significant publication within the field.

Fred (Friedrich) Neufeld (17 February 1869 – 18 April 1945) was a physician and bacteriologist who discovered the pneumococcal types. This discovery led Fred Griffith to show that one pneumococcal type could be transformed into another (Griffith's experiment). Subsequently, Oswald Avery demonstrated that the transforming substance was DNA. All modern molecular biology has evolved from this work.

Fred Neufeld is a Canadian mathematician known for his contributions to number theory, particularly in the areas of Diophantine equations and algebraic geometry. He has published numerous research papers and has been involved in various mathematical conferences and collaborations. Neufeld is also recognized for his efforts in mathematics education and outreach, promoting the subject among high school and university students. His work has garnered attention within the mathematical community, contributing to a deeper understanding of complex mathematical concepts.

Hilliard P. Jenkins (February 29, 1922 - August 23, 1992) was an American farmer, philanthropist, and civic leader. His family homestead, located in Loxley, Alabama, was considered one of the state's richest farms, and he earned distinction for his agricultural techniques. Jenkins also maintained leadership positions in a number of statewide and county organizations, including the Alabama Democratic Conference.

Hilliard P. Jenkins (born October 18, 1921) was an American politician and member of the Democratic Party who served as a member of the Pennsylvania House of Representatives from 1955 to 1964. Jenkins represented the 27th District and was active in various legislative efforts during his tenure. Following his time in the House, he continued to engage in public service and local community activities. His contributions to Pennsylvania politics are noted for their focus on education and social issues. Jenkins passed away on January 15, 1994.

Han Young-suk (Korean: 한영숙; Hanja: 韓英淑; February 2, 1920 – October 8, 1990), was an Ingan-munhwage for the Seungmu and Hakmu, which is an Important Intangible Cultural Properties of Korea. She was designated on July 4, 1969. She was a master of Korean dance especially Seungmu, Hakmu, Taepyeongmu and Salpuri. She used Byeoksa (Korean: 벽사; Hanja: 碧史) as a pseudonym.

Han Young-suk is a South Korean politician and member of the Democratic Party of Korea. She was born on November 19, 1964, in Gangneung, South Korea. Han serves as a member of the National Assembly, where she has focused on issues such as social welfare, education, and women's rights. She has held multiple positions within the party and has been active in legislative activities since her election. Han is recognized for her advocacy work and contributions to various social initiatives in South Korea.
Arena Arda is a football stadium in Kardzhali, Bulgaria, and the home ground of Arda Kardzhali.

Arena Arda is a multi-purpose sports complex located in the city of Sofia, Bulgaria. Opened in 2019, it primarily serves as a venue for indoor sporting events, concerts, and cultural activities. With a seating capacity of approximately 12,000, Arena Arda is designed to host a range of events, including basketball, volleyball, and handball matches, as well as concerts and exhibitions. The arena features modern facilities, including practice areas, hospitality suites, and advanced audiovisual technology, making it a key venue for both local and international events. Its establishment has contributed to the development of sports and entertainment in Bulgaria, providing a platform for various activities and fostering community engagement.

Artur Manuel Ribeiro Soares Dias (born 14 July 1979) is a former Portuguese football referee who was a listed international referee for FIFA and UEFA from 2010 to 2024. After having accompanied his countryman Olegário Benquerença in several UEFA Champions League as additional assistant referee, Soares Dias refereed matches at 2012–13 UEFA Europa League. Furthermore, he was appointed by the European football governing body to take charge of the Under-21 Championship play-off match between Italy and Sweden. He was in the U20 World Cup in New Zealand in 2015 and refereed the quarter-final USA vs Serbia. He was also in Serbia in U17 Euro and refereeing the semi-final in 2011. On 5 January 2017, while Soares Dias was getting ready to do his training in Maia, two days before officiating the Primeira Liga match between Paços de Ferreira and FC Porto, he and his family received death threats by individuals whom he identified as being part of Super Dragões, an official supporters group of FC Porto. He reported the incident to the police against "unknown individuals". On 13 May 2024, he was selected by UEFA to referee the Europa Conference League final between Olympiacos and Fiorentina.

Artur Soares Dias is a Portuguese football referee, born on 22 November 1978 in Porto, Portugal. He is known for officiating matches in the Primeira Liga, the top tier of Portuguese football, as well as in various international competitions. Soares Dias became a FIFA-listed referee in 2013, allowing him to officiate international matches, including European club competitions and national team games. He has been recognized for his consistent performances and is regarded as one of the leading referees in Portugal. His career includes numerous domestic cup finals and significant matches across European tournaments.

Bilbrough () is a village and civil parish in the county of North Yorkshire, England, 6 miles (10 km) south-west of York, and just outside the York city boundary. According to the 2001 Census it had a population of 319 increasing to 348 at the 2011 census. The village was historically part of the West Riding of Yorkshire until 1974. From 1974 to 2023 it was part of the district of Selby. It is now administered by the unitary North Yorkshire Council.

Bilbrough is a small village located in North Yorkshire, England, situated approximately 7 miles south of York. Historically part of the East Riding of Yorkshire, it has a population that sits within the civil parish of Bilbrough, where it is known for its rural character and community-oriented atmosphere. The village primarily features residential properties, agricultural land, and several local amenities, contributing to its appeal as a quiet residential area. Bilbrough is also linked to transportation routes including the nearby A64 road, providing access to larger cities and towns in the region. The village falls under the jurisdiction of the City of York council and is served by local schools and services.

Derrynaflaw (likely from Irish Doire na Flatha, meaning 'the oakgrove of the chief'), also known as Dernaflaw, is a small village and townland in County Londonderry, Northern Ireland. It is about 2 kilometres west of Dungiven on the Foreglen Road. It is designated as a Hamlet and in the 2001 Census it had a population of 168 people. It lies within Causeway Coast and Glens district.

Derrynaflaw is a townland located in County Tyrone, Northern Ireland. It is situated within the civil parish of Arboe and lies in the historical barony of Dungannon Upper. The name Derrynaflaw is derived from the Irish "Daire na Flá," which translates to "oak grove of the flood." The area is characterized by its rural landscape and features a mix of agricultural land and residences. Derrynaflaw is notable for its proximity to various local landmarks and its contributions to the cultural heritage of the region. The population and demographic trends of the townland reflect the broader social patterns of Northern Ireland.

Contract with Death (Russian: Контракт со смертью, romanized: Kontrakt so smertyu) is a 1998 Russian/Belarusian crime drama film directed by Dmitry Astrakhan. The film was nominated for Grand Prize of the Festival at the 1998 Sochi Open Russian Film Festival.

"Contract with Death" is a 1992 horror film directed by John R. Hand and starring Michael McCoy and Lisa L. Black. The plot revolves around a man who is granted the opportunity to make a deal with Death to extend his life, but as the agreement unfolds, he faces moral dilemmas and unintended consequences. The film blends elements of supernatural drama and psychological horror, exploring themes of mortality and the human desire to evade death. Despite its modest budget and independent production, "Contract with Death" has garnered a following among fans of the genre and is noted for its unique premise and exploration of existential themes.

Yasmeen Mjalli (born 1996) is an American and Palestinian fashion designer, photographer and anti-street harassment activist. She is the founder of the fashion brand Nöl Collective and the Not Your Habibti campaign.

Yasmeen Mjalli is a Palestinian-American activist, entrepreneur, and advocate for women's rights and social justice in Palestine. She is best known for her work in promoting gender equality and empowerment through various initiatives, including co-founding the non-profit organization "The Women's Center for Legal Aid and Counseling" in Ramallah. Mjalli has been vocal about issues such as the impact of the Israeli occupation on Palestinian women and has participated in numerous international forums to raise awareness about these challenges. Additionally, she has engaged in art and dialogue projects to foster cross-cultural understanding and support for contemporary Palestinian culture.
Zaosechye (Russian: Заосечье) is a rural locality (a village) in Voskresenskoye Rural Settlement, Cherepovetsky District, Vologda Oblast, Russia. The population was 2 as of 2002.

Zaosechye is a rural locality (a village) in the Vladimir Oblast region of Russia. It falls under the jurisdiction of the Muromsky District and is part of the Nizhneuzenskoye Rural Settlement. The village is characterized by its small population and agricultural lifestyle, typical of rural areas in the region. Zaosechye is situated approximately 22 kilometers northwest of the district administrative center, Murom. The region is known for its historical significance and scenic landscapes.

The 1982 Waltham Forest Council election took place on 6 May 1982 to elect members of Waltham Forest London Borough Council in London, England. The whole council was up for election and the Labour party stayed in overall control of the council.
topic: 1982 Waltham Forest London Borough Council election

text: The 1982 Waltham Forest London Borough Council election was held on 6 May 1982 as part of the larger local government elections across England. This election determined the composition of the Waltham Forest Council, with all 60 council seats being contested. The election was marked by significant political shifts, reflecting national trends during the 1980s. The Labour Party retained control of the council, although the results indicated a decrease in their overall majority. The election highlighted local concerns surrounding housing, social services, and community development, playing a crucial role in shaping local governance in Waltham Forest during a period of economic challenges.
topic: 1982 Waltham Forest London Borough Council election

text: The Immortals are a band consisting of Belgian electronic musicians Maurice "Praga Khan" Engelen and Olivier Adams, best known for their work in the techno/industrial dance/New Beat band Lords of Acid. The side project was created for the first Mortal Kombat soundtrack in 1994; however, they debuted their track "Techno Syndrome (Mortal Kombat)" in 1993 when it was released as a single. The original music was mixed with sound effects from the Mortal Kombat arcade game. The song was used as part of the Mortal Kombat commercial for the home systems that announced its single release as well. It was also used in TV commercials for the Mortal Kombat movie and Mortal Kombat: Live Tour, and it was released the same year when the game was released for home consoles. The track has also subsequently become known as "the Mortal Kombat theme song".

The Immortals were an American symphonic rock band formed in 1994 in Los Angeles, California. Known for their theatrical style and incorporation of classical elements into rock music, the band gained attention for their debut album, "The Last Goodbye," released in 1996. The group's lineup featured vocalist Jimmy McGill, guitarist Mike Mage, bassist Tom Blades, and drummer Steve Grin. The Immortals combined progressive rock influences with storytelling and elaborate live performances, often drawing comparisons to bands like Yes and Genesis. Despite their initial success, the band disbanded in the early 2000s after releasing two studio albums and a live recording. Their music continues to have a cult following among fans of the genre.

Mickey Coll (12 February 1951, in San Juan, Puerto Rico – 23 December 1972, on a motorcycle accident in Barceloneta, Puerto Rico) was a Puerto Rican basketball player who competed in the 1972 Summer Olympics.

Mickey Coll (born October 14, 1941) is a former professional American football player who played as a linebacker in the National Football League (NFL). He was drafted in the sixth round of the 1964 NFL Draft by the Philadelphia Eagles. Coll played college football at the University of Alabama, where he was recognized for his athleticism and skill on the field. Over his career, he played for several teams, including the Eagles and the Chicago Bears. After retiring from professional football, Coll contributed to the sport through coaching and various community engagements, leaving a lasting impact on youth sports programs.

Olle Alsing (born May 1, 1996) is a Swedish professional ice hockey defenceman for HV71 of the Swedish Hockey League (SHL). Alsing has previously played for the Ottawa Senators in the National Hockey League and Djurgårdens IF and Leksands IF of the Swedish Hockey League.

Olle Alsing is a Swedish professional ice hockey defenseman, born on December 7, 1997, in Sundsvall, Sweden. He began his career with Frölunda HC in the Swedish Hockey League (SHL) and has since played for various teams in European leagues. Alsing is known for his strong skating ability and offensive skills from the blue line, making him a valuable asset on the ice. In 2020, he signed a contract with the Ottawa Senators of the National Hockey League (NHL), furthering his career in North America. He has represented Sweden in international tournaments, contributing to the national team's efforts in junior competitions.
Bulbophyllum acropogon is a species of orchid in the genus Bulbophyllum.

Bulbophyllum acropogon is a species of orchid belonging to the genus Bulbophyllum, which is characterized by a diverse array of plants with unique floral structures. This species is native to specific regions in Southeast Asia and is typically found in tropical climates, often growing in epiphytic conditions on trees in rainforests. Bulbophyllum acropogon features distinctive bulbous pseudobulbs and is known for its unique flower morphology, which typically exhibits intricate and varied colors. Like other members of the Bulbophyllum genus, it requires specific cultural conditions to thrive, including humidity and warm temperatures, and is often cultivated for its ornamental appeal. The plant also plays a role in the ecology of its native habitat, contributing to the diversity of the orchid family and the ecosystems in which it resides.

The New York City Subway is a heavy-rail public transit system serving four of the five boroughs of New York City. The present New York City Subway system inherited the systems of the Interborough Rapid Transit Company (IRT), Brooklyn–Manhattan Transit Corporation (BMT), and the Independent Subway System (IND). New York City has owned the IND since its inception; the BMT and IRT were taken over by the city in 1940. The former IRT system is now known as the A Division, while the B Division is the combined former BMT and IND systems. In the New York City Subway nomenclature, a "line" refers to the physical trackage that is used by numbered or lettered "services", which change periodically. Today, the division or company names are not used publicly, while the line names may occasionally be used.
topic: List of New York City Subway lines

text: The New York City Subway is a rapid transit system that serves New York City and is one of the largest subway systems in the world by number of stations and length of routes. The system comprises multiple lines, each designated by a letter or number and identified by a color on the transit map. The subway lines collectively cover four boroughs: Manhattan, Brooklyn, Queens, and parts of the Bronx, with a total of 472 stations. These lines are categorized based on their service types, including local, express, and shuttle services. The network operates 24 hours a day, seven days a week, providing critical transportation for millions of residents and visitors. The lines are organized into various routes and services, including the numbered lines (1 through 7) and the lettered lines (A through Z), along with specialty services like the Staten Island Railway and the AirTrain. The history and expansion of the subway since its inception in 1904 have significantly shaped New York City's urban landscape.
topic: List of New York City Subway lines

text: Dol pri Ljubljani (pronounced [ˈdoːl pɾi ljuˈbljaːni]; German: Lusttal) is a settlement in central Slovenia. It is the seat of the Municipality of Dol pri Ljubljani. It is part of the traditional region of Upper Carniola and is now included in the Central Slovenia Statistical Region.

Dol pri Ljubljani is a small settlement located in the Municipality of Dol pri Ljubljani, Slovenia. Situated to the southeast of the capital city, Ljubljana, Dol pri Ljubljani is characterized by its picturesque rural landscape and traditional Slovenian architecture. The settlement has a population that primarily engages in agriculture and local industries. It is well connected to nearby urban centers, making it a desirable location for those seeking a suburban lifestyle while maintaining proximity to the amenities of the capital. The area is also known for its cultural heritage and community events, which reflect the traditions of the region.

Behala Bazar is an elevated metro station on the North-South corridor of the Purple Line of Kolkata Metro which is located in Behala Bazar, Kolkata, West Bengal, India. This station was commissioned on 30 December 2022.

Behala Bazar metro station is a planned underground station on the Kolkata Metro Line 2, part of the Kolkata Metro network in India. Located in the Behala area of Kolkata, the station aims to enhance connectivity in the southern suburbs of the city. It is expected to facilitate easier access to various local markets and commercial hubs. The station will provide essential public transport options to residents and visitors, promoting the use of metro services in an area traditionally reliant on road transportation. The construction of the station is part of the broader Kolkata Metro expansion project, which seeks to alleviate traffic congestion and improve urban mobility. As of October 2023, the station is under construction and is anticipated to open to the public in the coming years.

Isolated hyperchlorhidrosis, also known as Carbonic anhydrase XII deficiency, is a rare autosomal recessive genetic condition characterized by a lifelong tendency to lose massive amounts of sodium and chloride through sweat which leads to various symptoms.

Isolated hyperchlorhidrosis is a medical condition characterized by excessive sweating due to increased production of chloride-rich sweat without the presence of other systemic conditions. It primarily affects specific areas of the body, such as the palms, soles, and axillae, leading to significant discomfort and social impairment. The condition is thought to be caused by hyperactivity of the sweat glands, although the exact mechanisms remain unclear. Diagnosis typically involves clinical evaluation and may include sweat tests to measure chloride levels. Treatment options focus on symptom management and may include antiperspirants, oral medications, botulinum toxin injections, or surgical interventions in severe cases. Isolated hyperchlorhidrosis should be distinguished from other forms of hyperhidrosis, which may involve broader systemic causes or other symptoms.

Park Kil-woo(Korean: 박길우; born (1967-01-04)January 4, 1967) is a South Korean wheelchair curler and curling coach. He participated at the 2010 Winter Paralympics where South Korean team won a silver medal.

Park Kil-woo is a South Korean actor born on September 19, 1985. He gained prominence for his performances in various television dramas and films. Park made his acting debut in the early 2000s and has since appeared in notable series such as "The Great Show," "The Lady in Dignity," and "My Name." His acting style is characterized by its versatility, enabling him to portray a wide range of characters across genres. In addition to his television work, Park has also participated in theater productions and films, contributing to his reputation as a talented and dedicated performer in the South Korean entertainment industry.

On 23 August 1944, a United States Army Air Forces (USAAF) Consolidated B-24H Liberator crashed during a test flight into the centre of the village of Freckleton, Lancashire, England, killing all three crewmen aboard the aircraft and 58 individuals on the ground, including 38 children aged four to six. An official inquiry was unable to pinpoint an exact cause for the accident, although a sudden thunderstorm and the resultant reduced visibility immediately prior to the accident had caused the test pilot of the B-24H, First Lieutenant John Bloemendal, to be ordered to abandon the test flight and attempt to return to base. The report was unable to discount structural failure of the aircraft in such extreme weather conditions as a factor for the accident, and recommended that American pilots be warned how to respond to British thunderstorms. This aviation accident would prove to be the deadliest to occur in Britain during World War II, and remained the second worst aviation accident in the world (in terms of number of fatalities) until the 1950 Llandow air disaster.

The Freckleton Air Disaster occurred on August 23, 1944, when a military aircraft, a Douglas C-54 Skymaster, crashed in the village of Freckleton, Lancashire, England. The incident took place shortly after takeoff from nearby RAF Warton, resulting in the deaths of all 38 people on board and an additional 8 individuals on the ground. The aircraft was returning to the United States from a training exercise when it encountered a severe storm that led to the crash, which struck a local school and nearby homes. This disaster remains one of the deadliest aviation accidents in the UK during World War II and had a significant impact on the local community, prompting investigations into air safety and regulations.

Leonid Borisovich Alaev (Russian: Леонид Борисович Алаев; 20 October 1932 – 31 July 2023) was a Soviet and Russian historian and indologist, Doctor of Sciences in Historical Sciences, principal researcher at the Institute of Oriental Studies of the Russian Academy of Sciences, professor at the Moscow State Institute of International Relations, and honorary professor at the Pedagogical Institute of Irkutsk State University. Alaev was the editor of Oriens (1988–1999).

Leonid Alaev is a prominent figure known for his contributions to mathematics and computer science, particularly in the areas of graph theory and combinatorial optimization. He has authored numerous research papers and has been involved in various academic and industrial collaborations. Alaev's work is recognized for its innovative approaches to solving complex problems and enhancing algorithmic efficiency. His influence extends through mentorship and participation in conferences, promoting collaboration and advancements within the mathematics community.

Alfonso, Prince of Asturias (10 May 1907 – 6 September 1938), was heir apparent to the throne of Spain from birth until the abolition of the monarchy in 1931. He renounced his rights to the defunct throne in 1933. Alfonso was the eldest son of King Alfonso XIII of Spain and Victoria Eugenie of Battenberg. Alfonso's renunciation of his rights as heir to the Spanish throne in order to marry Cuban commoner Edelmira Sampedro caused controversy at the time. A similar situation would take place three years later in Britain with his second cousin Edward VIII, who would abdicate as King of the United Kingdom and Emperor of India to marry an American divorcee, Wallis Simpson. He died at the age of 31 as a result of a car crash. Though appearing to have sustained minor injuries, his haemophilia, inherited through his great-grandmother Queen Victoria, led to fatal internal bleeding.

Alfonso, Prince of Asturias (1907–1938), was the heir apparent to the Spanish throne from the House of Borbón. Born on November 10, 1907, he was the firstborn son of King Alfonso XIII of Spain and Queen Victoria Eugenie. Alfonso's childhood was marked by the political instability of Spain during the early 20th century, including the monarchy's challenges in facing republican sentiments. He became heir after the birth of his siblings and was educated in Spain and abroad. His life and prospects were drastically altered when the monarchy was abolished in 1931, leading to the establishment of the Second Spanish Republic. Following the rise of political tensions and the Spanish Civil War, Alfonso's life tragically ended when he died in a car accident in 1934. He is remembered for his short life and the disruptive historical context surrounding his upbringing.

Due to the increased vulnerability that lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender and queer (LGBTQ) youth face compared to their non-LGBTQ peers, there are notable differences in the mental and physical health risks tied to the social interactions of LGBTQ youth compared to the social interactions of heterosexual youth. According to the article "Social Media: A double-edged sword for LGBTQ+ Youth", the study showed that "in comparison to cisgender youth, transgender and gender binary youth had a higher risk of depression and anxiety" from social media exposure. Youth of the LGBTQ community experience greater encounters with not only health risks, but also violence and bullying, due to their sexual orientation, self-identification, and lack of support from institutions in society.

LGBTQ youth vulnerability refers to the heightened risks and challenges faced by young individuals who identify as lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, queer, or questioning (LGBTQ). This demographic often encounters discrimination, stigma, and isolation, which can lead to mental health issues, including anxiety, depression, and suicidal ideation. Factors contributing to this vulnerability include family rejection, bullying in schools, lack of supportive social networks, and limited access to affirming healthcare services. Additionally, LGBTQ youth are disproportionately affected by homelessness and substance abuse. Advocacy groups and policies aimed at improving the well-being of LGBTQ youth focus on creating inclusive environments, promoting acceptance, and providing resources that address their unique needs.

The Robert Guertin Centre ("The Bob") (French: Centre Robert Guertin) (formerly Robert Guertin Arena and Hull Arena) was a multi-purpose arena in the Hull sector of Gatineau, Quebec, with a capacity of 4,000 capacity (3,196 seated). It was built in 1957 and demolished in 2024. The original tenant hockey team was the Ottawa-Hull Canadiens from 1957 to 1961, until the team relocated to become the Montreal Junior Canadiens. The Ottawa-Hull Canadiens hosted the 1958 Memorial Cup between the Hull Arena and the Ottawa Auditorium across the river. The Canadiens defeated the Regina Pats four games to two to win the Memorial Cup. The Hull Arena hosted various teams in the Ottawa-Hull District Junior Hockey League during the 1960s, and briefly hosted the Ottawa 67's in 1967 and 1968, while the Ottawa Civic Centre was under construction. Since 1973, the arena has been home to Quebec Major Junior Hockey League teams. The Hull Festivals joined the QMJHL in 1973, and later became known as the Hull Olympiques in 1976, and finally the Gatineau Olympiques in 2003. The Hull arena hosted the 1982 Memorial Cup. The Gatineau Olympiques won the 1997 Memorial Cup on home ice, defeating the Lethbridge Hurricanes. The arena is also the site of an international midget hockey tournament held every January.

The Robert Guertin Centre is a multi-purpose arena located in Gatineau, Quebec, Canada. Opened in 1966, it serves as a venue for various sporting events, concerts, and community activities. The arena has a seating capacity of approximately 3,000 and has hosted numerous local hockey teams, including the Gatineau Olympiques of the QMJHL. Named after Robert Guertin, a prominent figure in the region, the facility is recognized for its contribution to the local sports culture and community events. Throughout its history, the Robert Guertin Centre has undergone several renovations to improve its amenities and services.
Laetesia mollita is a species of sheet weaver found in Western Australia. It was described by Simon in 1908.

Laetesia mollita is a species of beetle belonging to the family Cerambycidae, commonly known as longhorn beetles. First described by the entomologist Thomas Say in 1824, this species is characterized by its elongated body and long antennae, which can be several times longer than the body itself. Laetesia mollita is typically found in North America, inhabiting a variety of ecosystems, including forests and woodlands. Like many cerambycid beetles, it plays a role in the decomposition of wood, contributing to nutrient cycling within its habitat. The species is not considered to be of significant concern in terms of conservation status.
Chile competed at the 1996 Summer Olympics in Atlanta, United States. 21 competitors, 16 men and 5 women, took part in 18 events in 10 sports.

Chile competed at the 1996 Summer Olympics held in Atlanta, United States, from July 19 to August 4, 1996. The Chilean delegation consisted of 32 athletes, participating in 11 sports. The country did not win any medals during these Games, marking a continuation of its challenging Olympic history. Notable athletes included swimmer Kristel Köbrich and javelin thrower María José Moya. Chile's participation in the 1996 Olympics aimed to enhance its international presence and promote sports development within the nation.

Marrying the Mafia II is a 2005 South Korean comedy film, and the second installment of the Marrying the Mafia series. It was the most successful comedy film in South Korea the year of its release; its over 5 million tickets sold represented more than 10% of the population and contributed to the third straight year that more tickets were sold for local than for Hollywood films.

Marrying the Mafia II is a South Korean romantic comedy film released in 2005, directed by Jeong Yong-ki. It serves as a sequel to the 2002 film Marrying the Mafia, following the comedic adventures of a traditional family caught in the world of organized crime. The storyline focuses on the lead character, who must navigate the challenges of love, family dynamics, and the mob's influence while trying to maintain a normal life. The film features a blend of humor and romance, highlighting the cultural clash between conventional values and the chaotic lifestyle associated with the mafia. Notable for its comedic elements and engaging performances, Marrying the Mafia II received a positive response from audiences and contributed to the popularity of the initial film.
Vilvaarani is a panchayat village located in Tiruvannamalai district of Tamil Nadu, India. The village has a temple dedicated to the poet-philosopher Valluvar.

Vilvarani is a village located in the Tiruvannamalai district of Tamil Nadu, India. It falls within the jurisdiction of the Tiruvannamalai taluk and is part of the larger Kadaladi Panchayat. The village is characterized by its rural setting and traditional Tamil culture. Its proximity to the sacred town of Tiruvannamalai, known for the Annamalaiyar Temple and the Arunachala hill, contributes to its significance within the local context. The economy of Vilvarani primarily revolves around agriculture and small-scale local businesses. The village enjoys a community-centric lifestyle, with festivals and cultural events playing a vital role in its social fabric. As of the latest census, the population and demographic details reflect typical patterns found in other villages of the region.

The battle of Khiam, fought between Hezbollah and the South Lebanon Army (SLA) in April and May 2000, which became a crucial step in the disintegration of the South Lebanon Army, in light of the coming withdrawal of IDF troops from South Lebanon.

The Battle of Khiam was a military confrontation that occurred in May 2000 during the Israel-Lebanon conflict, specifically in the context of Israel's withdrawal from southern Lebanon. The battle took place near the Khiam prison, a site of significant historical and political importance, as it had been used for detention during Israel's occupation of southern Lebanon. The confrontation involved the Israel Defense Forces (IDF) and various Lebanese militant groups, notably Hezbollah. As Israel began its withdrawal, intense fighting erupted as Hezbollah sought to establish control over the area. The battle resulted in heavy casualties and marked a pivotal moment in the broader conflict, influencing the dynamics of resistance against Israeli presence in Lebanon. The withdrawal ultimately led to a shift in power in southern Lebanon, with Hezbollah emerging as a dominant force. The fallout from the battle and the subsequent Israeli withdrawal contributed to ongoing tensions in the region.

The Jardin St. Martin (Monégasque: Giardi̍n San Martin) are located in the Monaco-Ville ward of Monaco. The gardens are made up of a series of paths on the south west face of the Rock of Monaco. They have an estimated area of 11,200 square meters (1.12 hectares). Flora include Aleppo pine trees and yellow agaves in its terraces with medieval fortifications also featuring in the gardens. Archaeological finds in the gardens have been dated to the Holocene era. François Cogné's bronze statue of Prince Albert I as a sailor stands at the centre of the gardens.

St Martin Gardens is a public park located in the parish of St Martin, in the east of the island of Jersey. Established in the 20th century, the gardens feature a variety of horticultural displays, including flower beds, trees, and shrubs, providing a tranquil space for recreation and relaxation. The park is known for its picturesque views and well-maintained landscape, attracting both locals and tourists. Facilities within the gardens may include walking paths, seating areas, and picnic spots, making it a popular destination for families and nature enthusiasts.

The Utah Constitutional Convention was held from March 4, 1895 to May 8, 1895. The 1895 convention was the Utah Territory's seventh and final attempt to be admitted to the United States as a state. The Constitution of Utah was accepted by Congress and President Grover Cleveland, leading to Utah's admittance into the union as the 45th state on January 4, 1896.

The Utah Constitutional Convention of 1895 was a significant assembly held to draft a constitution for the proposed state of Utah. Convened in Salt Lake City from March 4 to May 8, 1895, the convention resulted in the creation of a constitution that included provisions for a bicameral legislature, a governor, and various civil rights. The proceedings were influenced by the political context of the time, particularly the federal government's push for Utah to establish a constitution that would renounce polygamy, a practice associated with the region's dominant religious group, the LDS Church. Following the completion of the draft, the constitution was submitted to the voters of Utah and subsequently ratified. It served as the foundation for Utah's admission into the Union as the 45th state on January 4, 1896.
Eulitoma is a genus of sea snails, marine gastropod mollusks in the family Eulimidae.

Eulitoma is a genus of small sea snails, marine gastropod mollusks, within the family Eulimidae. Members of this genus are characterized by their elongated, conical shells and their parasitic lifestyle, often living on or inside other marine organisms, such as mollusks or echinoderms. Eulitoma species are found in various marine environments, from shallow coastal waters to deeper offshore habitats. The classification of Eulitoma is based on both morphological characteristics and genetic studies, contributing to the broader understanding of gastropod diversity. Research on Eulitoma contributes to knowledge of marine ecosystems and the role of parasitic relationships in marine environments.

Saints Catherine and Florian Church in Gołąb, Poland, is an early seventeenth-century church. The church was founded by Chancellor Jerzy Ossoliński (1595–1650). Built between 1626–1634, the build was consecrated upon completion in 1634. The church's construction was influenced by different architectural movements which entered the Lublin Land (Lubelszczyzna) in the seventeenth century.
topic: Saints Catherine and Florian Church, Gołąb

text: Saints Catherine and Florian Church is a Roman Catholic church located in the village of Gołąb, Poland. Constructed in the 18th century, the church is known for its Baroque architectural style and its significant role in the local community. It is dedicated to Saints Catherine of Alexandria and Florian of Lorch, who are venerated as patrons of various causes, including protection against fire and persecution. The church features an array of religious artworks, including altarpieces and sculptures, which contribute to its cultural and historical value. Saints Catherine and Florian Church serves as both a place of worship and a landmark reflecting the area's religious heritage.
topic: Saints Catherine and Florian Church, Gołąb

text: The Maury Channel (75°41′N 094°30′W) is a natural waterway through the central Canadian Arctic Archipelago in Qikiqtaaluk Region, Nunavut. It separates Baillie-Hamilton Island (to the north) from Cornwallis Island (to the south). To the west it opens into Queens Channel and to the east into Wellington Channel.

The Maury Channel is a waterway located in the western part of the North Atlantic Ocean, acting as a connection between the eastern United States and the Caribbean Sea. It is situated to the east of the North Carolina coast and serves as an important maritime corridor for shipping and navigation. Named after the American oceanographer Matthew Fontaine Maury, the channel is characterized by its deep waters and significant oceanographic features, including various currents that influence regional climate and marine ecosystems. The Maury Channel is utilized for both commercial and recreational boating, with its waters supporting diverse wildlife and fisheries.

Squadism (or sometimes "Squaddism") was the practice of physical, anti-fascist direct action. The term, often used pejoratively by liberal anti-fascists eschewing violence, originated in the Anti-Nazi League, an anti-fascist campaigning organisation dominated by the heterodox Trotskyist Socialist Workers Party (SWP). The SWP formed "squads", fighting units, in 1977, initially to defend and steward meetings against violent attacks from the fascist National Front. However, other anti-fascist squads emerged separately from the SWP, such as the Sari Squad. The name associated eventually with all of the fighting groups of that era, i.e., "Squads" originated with the already established Manchester-based anti fascist fighting group, drawn from many groups on the left and non-aligned anti fascists which first adopted the name "The Squad" for its, previously very ad hoc, fighting unit in 1977. The adoption of the name, "Squad", had only an accidental connection with the very similar "Peoples Squads" in Italy in the early 1920s. Squads were active in the North West of England and then Glasgow, the Midlands, Hatfield, and London in this period. The core of the fighting units were SWP members, but also included many members of other left groups and non-aligned anti fascists. The squads were formed to directly combat the rising tide of fascist "street" violence being experienced by the Left, and black communities, during this period, from the National Front in particular. The regionally-based direct action squads operated within, but also considerably beyond, the ANL. Squad members also used violence and intimidation to break up meetings, marches and other gatherings of far right groups such as the National Front and the British Movement The idea behind the tactic was to ensure the safety of ANL and general left meetings through efficient stewarding, and also to generally intimidate groups seen as fascist, without generating media publicity for the far right. Squadism became increasingly frowned upon by the ANL/SWP leadership when the National Front's expansion fell away in the late 1970s. By 1979/80, the ANL leadership had decided that the National Front was a broken force, and so militant campaigning anti-fascism was no longer a priority. Militant anti-fascists both within and without the SWP strongly disagreed with what they saw as a mistaken and opportunistic abandonment of militant activity. The SWP leadership had also for some time been concerned that in areas of London and Manchester the semi-clandestine combat groups had become a law unto themselves. A campaign led by the Central Committee within the SWP against "squadists" was organised in late 1981, on the back of an "operation" by the Manchester Squad which resulted in five of its members (and four non-Squad SWP students) receiving prison sentences. Many of the individual SWP members who had defended the Left from fascist attack across the UK for many years were expelled during late 1981 to early 1982. When the ANL was disbanded in the late 1970s, many ex-squad members went on to form Anti-Fascist Action and Red Action (AFA), whose "Stewarding Group" was active (along the same lines as the earlier "Squads") from the mid-1980s until the early 1990s. As a term of abuse, "squadism" is taken to mean vanguardist, secretive, adventurist, direct action against fascist organisations; isolated from mass anti fascist activities. This negative, pejorative labelling of any combat group based physical force action against fascism has since the 1980s been the political orthodoxy on most of the British Left. Anarchist groups and other anti fascists in particular however have continued the direct physical action, "squadist" approach to fascist street mobilisations to the present day, via a variety of nationally networked groupings over the years, such as "No Platform" and "Antifa" Similar currents have existed elsewhere, with analogous terminology. In Italy, for example, the Arditi del Popolo (people's squads) pursued a similar policy in the 1920s and were suppressed by the Communist Party of Italy.

Squadism is a term primarily associated with the Italian Fascist movement, particularly its militaristic factions during the early 20th century. It refers to the organized violence and paramilitary practices employed by groups known as "sqadristi," who were loyal to Benito Mussolini and aimed to intimidate opponents and consolidate power through aggressive tactics. The squadristi engaged in street fighting, physical assaults on leftist political adversaries, and acts of sabotage. This phenomenon played a crucial role in the rise of fascism in Italy, contributing to the establishment of Mussolini's regime in the 1920s. The tactics and ideology of squadism influenced later fascist movements and have been studied as examples of extremist nationalism and political violence.

The Boren-McCurdy intelligence reform proposals were two legislative proposals from Senator David Boren and Representative Dave McCurdy in 1992 (102nd Congress). Both pieces of legislation proposed the creation of a National Intelligence Director. Neither bill passed into law.

The Boren–McCurdy proposals are a set of legislative recommendations aimed at addressing the growing concerns regarding the U.S. national debt and entitlement spending. Named after U.S. Senators David Boren and John McCurdy, who introduced the proposals in the early 1990s, these initiatives sought to reform various aspects of federal budgetary policy and social safety net programs. The proposals emphasized fiscal responsibility, advocating for reforms to Medicare, Social Security, and tax policies to ensure long-term sustainability of government finances. While the proposals garnered interest and debate, they faced significant political challenges and have not been fully implemented.
Mercedes Moltó Contreras (born 21 February 1974, in Barcelona, Spain), known as Mercedes Molto, is a Spanish-born Mexican actress.

Mercedes Moltó is a Spanish television presenter and journalist, best known for her work in various broadcasting networks. She gained prominence for her engaging style and in-depth reporting, specializing in cultural and social topics. Born on December 3, 1978, in Valencia, Spain, Moltó began her career in regional television before moving to national networks, where she hosted multiple programs. Her contributions to the media have earned her recognition within the Spanish entertainment industry, and she is noted for her ability to connect with diverse audiences. In addition to her television career, Moltó is involved in advocacy work and various charitable initiatives.

Frank Miner Patterson (June 29, 1873 – April 10, 1939) was an American college football player and coach. He was the sixth head football coach for the University of Missouri in Columbia, Missouri, serving for one season, in 1896, and compiling a record of 7–5. Patterson was an alumnus of Yale University, where he played football as an end.

Frank Patterson was a professional American football player who played as a guard in the National Football League (NFL). Born on June 25, 1906, in Elkhart, Indiana, Patterson played college football at the University of Chicago. He was drafted in 1929 and went on to have a career spanning several seasons in the NFL, primarily with the Boston Braves. Known for his toughness and durability on the field, Patterson contributed to his team's offensive line. After retiring from professional play, he continued to be involved in football through coaching and mentoring younger players. Patterson's legacy in the sport is reflected in his contributions to the teams he played for and his influence on future generations of athletes. He passed away on July 9, 1968.
Joe Murray (born 1963) is a former British Army soldier and television personality from Glasgow, United Kingdom. He is best known for his role on ITV programme, Bad Lads' Army.

Joe Murray is a British Army soldier known for his service with the British Armed Forces. He gained recognition for his role in various military operations and has been awarded multiple commendations for bravery and excellence in service. Details of his deployments and contributions highlight his involvement in significant historical events and military campaigns. Murray's experiences have been documented in various media, showcasing his commitment to his duties and the impact of his service on both his unit and the larger military community.

Andricus mukaigawae is a species of gall wasp native to southeastern Asia. It creates galls on the buds and leaves of oak trees. The galls are sometimes used by other gall wasps unable to create galls of their own, with both species sharing the gall.

Andricus mukaigawae is a species of gall wasp in the family Cynipidae, first described by the Japanese entomologist Kiyoshi Mukaigawa in 1952. This species is known for inducing galls on the leaves of certain oak trees. A notable feature of Andricus mukaigawae is its complex life cycle, which includes both sexual and asexual reproduction phases. The galls produced by this wasp play a crucial role in the ecology of oak forests, providing habitat and resources for various other organisms. Research on Andricus mukaigawae contributes to the understanding of gall wasp diversity, plant interactions, and ecosystem dynamics.

The 2023–24 Kennesaw State Owls women's basketball team represented Kennesaw State University during the 2023–24 NCAA Division I women's basketball season. The Owls, led by third-year head coach Octavia Blue, played their home games at the KSU Convocation Center in Kennesaw, Georgia as members of the Atlantic Sun (ASUN) Conference. This was the Owls' last season as members of the ASUN Conference, as they will be moving to Conference USA, effective July 1, 2024.
topic: 2023–24 Kennesaw State Owls women's basketball team

text: The 2023–24 Kennesaw State Owls women's basketball team represents Kennesaw State University in the 2023–24 NCAA Division I women's basketball season. Led by head coach Kimsha Hatfield in her second year, the team plays its home games at the KSU Convocation Center in Kennesaw, Georgia. The Owls compete in the Atlantic Sun Conference (ASUN) and aim to improve upon their previous season's performance. The roster features a mix of returning players and new recruits, with the team focusing on developing their skills and building chemistry throughout the season. The Owls are determined to make a strong impact in conference play and secure a spot in postseason competitions.
