text: The 2022–23 NOJHL season was the 45th season of the Northern Ontario Junior Hockey League (NOJHL). The franchise formerly known as the Espanola Express was rebranded as the Espanola Paper Kings. The Powassan Voodoos named Peter Goulet as its new head coach following the resignation of Marc Lafleur.

The 2022–23 Northern Ontario Junior Hockey League (NOJHL) season was the league's 60th season of operation. It began in September 2022 and concluded with the playoffs in April 2023. The season featured multiple teams competing for the championship, culminating in the NOJHL Final. Teams participated in a regular season followed by a playoff format, and the champion was determined based on postseason performance. This season highlighted the continued growth and development of junior hockey in Northern Ontario, with several players advancing to higher levels of competition. The season also emphasized community engagement and the importance of junior hockey in fostering local talent.

The 2016 NCAA Division I Outdoor Track and Field Championships were the 95th NCAA Men's Division I Outdoor Track and Field Championships and the 35th NCAA Women's Division I Outdoor Track and Field Championships held for the fourth consecutive year at Hayward Field in Eugene, Oregon on the campus of the University of Oregon. In total, forty-two different men's and women's track and field events were contested from June 8 to June 11, 2016.
topic: 2016 NCAA Division I Outdoor Track and Field Championships

text: The 2016 NCAA Division I Outdoor Track and Field Championships were held from June 8 to June 11, 2016, at Hayward Field in Eugene, Oregon. This annual collegiate championship featured over 1,000 athletes from various universities competing in both men's and women's track and field events. The University of Oregon served as the host institution, taking advantage of its historic venue known for its strong association with track and field. The championships culminated in team competitions where the University of Texas won the men's team title and the University of Kentucky secured the women's team title, marking significant achievements in NCAA athletics. The event concluded with memorable performances, including several records and standout individual athlete performances.

text: Marcel Girard Brache (born 15 October 1987) is an American retired rugby union player. Brache was born in Los Angeles, California and was raised primarily in South Africa. He currently plays for the San Diego Legion of Major League Rugby (MLR), generally as a centre but can also play wing.

Marcel Brache is a professional rugby union player from Australia, known for his versatility as a winger and fullback. Born on February 27, 1993, in Perth, Western Australia, Brache began his rugby career playing for the Western Force in the Super Rugby competition. He later gained recognition for his performances in various domestic leagues, including the National Rugby Championship (NRC). Brache has also played internationally, representing Australia in various age-grade teams. His playing style is characterized by speed, agility, and a strong tactical understanding of the game, contributing to his reputation as a skilled athlete in the rugby union community.

The Bluff Maritime Museum is located in New Zealand's southernmost port of Bluff. The museum is situated on Foreshore Road and contains an extensive collection of Bluff's maritime heritage. The museum was founded in November 1992, and celebrated its 20th anniversary in early 2012 and early 2013. Being near the ancient Māori settlement of Omaui and as the oldest European settled community in New Zealand, Bluff's history illustrates the blending of two cultures and the shared community spirit, with the sea remaining as the central focus for many locals. The museum contains a wide variety of maritime displays, artefacts, miniature models, boats, photographs and a working triple expansion engine. The biggest attraction is a full-sized oystering boat, the Monica, donated by the Jones family. Visitors can climb aboard and explore the entire ship. The museum also exhibits artwork showing scenes of Bluff's early days, when it was officially known as Campbelltown.

Bluff Maritime Museum is a maritime museum located in Bluff, New Zealand, dedicated to preserving and showcasing the maritime heritage and history of the region. Established in 1993, the museum features a variety of exhibits, including displays of local maritime artifacts, photographs, and stories related to Bluff's seafaring past. It highlights the significance of Bluff's port and its role in the development of maritime trade in New Zealand. The museum is situated in a historic building and serves as both an educational resource and a tourist attraction, drawing visitors interested in maritime history and the unique culture of the Bluff community.
USS LST-932 was an LST-542-class tank landing ship in the United States Navy. Like many of her class, she was not named and is properly referred to by her hull designation.

USS LST-932 was a tank landing ship of the United States Navy, an LST-542-class vessel built during World War II. Launched on 11 March 1944, she was commissioned into service on 2 May 1944. LST-932 served primarily in the European Theater, participating in various amphibious operations, including the invasion of southern France. After the war, she was decommissioned on 7 November 1946 and struck from the Navy list. The ship was later sold to a commercial entity. Throughout her service, LST-932 earned one battle star for her contributions to the war effort.

Otto Stangl (Dachau, October 9, 1915 - Munich, July 20, 1990) was a German gallery owner, art dealer and art collector. With his wife Etta Stangl they founded in 1947 in Munich the Moderne Galerie Etta und Otto Stangl, an important gallery of the avant-garde after the Second World War in Germany. Their influence on the transmission of contemporary art played an important role in the rehabilitation of the so-called degenerate art of the Third Reich (mainly Blauer Reiter, expressionism, classical modern art). Etta and Otto Stangl were also involved in establishing the Franz Marc Museum in Kochel am See, which opened in 1986.

Otto Stangl (1895–1944) was a German politician and member of the Nazi Party, known for his involvement in the party's operations in Vienna during the late 1930s. Born in Austria, Stangl joined the Nazis in the early 1930s and quickly rose through the ranks, eventually becoming a prominent figure in the regime's local administration. He was noted for his role in implementing policies that advanced the party’s agenda, including the persecution of political opponents and Jewish communities. Following the annexation of Austria, Stangl played a key role in the integration of Austrian Nazis into the broader German Nazi structure. He died in 1944 while in Allied custody after World War II, and his legacy remains controversial due to his association with the oppressive practices of the Nazi regime.

Jay LaDue (April 7, 1827 – October 12, 1926) was an American farmer and politician. LaDue was born in Sherman, Chautauqua County, New York. He went to the public schools. In 1856, LaDue moved to Luverne, Rock County, Minnesota with his wife and family and was a farmer. LaDue served in the Minnesota Senate from 1891 to 1894.

Jay LaDue is a notable American artist and educator best known for his contributions to contemporary art and his exploration of themes related to identity, memory, and the human experience. His work often incorporates mixed media techniques and challenges traditional boundaries of artistic expression. LaDue has exhibited his art in various galleries and institutions across the United States, gaining recognition for his innovative approach. In addition to his artistic practice, he is also involved in teaching and mentoring emerging artists, contributing to the discourse on art education and practice.

Hrishitaa Bhatt (born 10 May 1981), also spelled as Hrishita Bhat, is an Indian actress and model. She debuted in the film Asoka (2001) opposite Shah Rukh Khan, but it was Haasil (2003) that brought her fame. Bhatt received critical acclaim for the role and appeared in films like Ab Tak Chhappan (2004) and Jigyaasa (2006), thereafter. Bhatt was part of the ensemble cast of the ZEE5 cop drama web series, Lalbazaar.

Hrishitaa Bhatt is an Indian actress and model, known for her work in Hindi and regional films. Born on May 10, 1982, in Mumbai, India, she gained popularity in the early 2000s with her roles in movies such as "Raaz" (2002) and "Yeh Dil Aashiqanaa" (2002). Bhatt also appeared in several Telugu, Tamil, and Bengali films, showcasing her versatility as an actress. In addition to her film career, she has participated in television shows and reality programs. Bhatt is recognized for her contributions to Indian cinema and has garnered a fan following over the years.

The 2025–26 Liga 4 West Nusa Tenggara is the second season of Liga 4 West Nusa Tenggara after the change in the structure of Indonesian football competition and serves as a qualifying round for the national phase of the 2025–26 Liga 4. The competition is organised by the West Nusa Tenggara Provincial PSSI Association.

The 2025–26 Liga 4 West Nusa Tenggara is the upcoming season of the Liga 4 football competition in the West Nusa Tenggara province of Indonesia. It will feature clubs from various regions within the province competing for promotion to the higher tiers of Indonesian football. The season is expected to commence in mid-2025, following the previous season's format, which emphasizes local talent and community engagement. The league aims to enhance the development of football at the grassroots level and provide a platform for clubs to showcase their skills. Details regarding participating teams, fixtures, and match venues will be announced as the season approaches.

Cosmopterix diaphora is a moth of the family Cosmopterigidae. It is known from the Federal District of Brazil and the states of Guerrero and Veracruz in Mexico. Adults have been collected in April and August, which indicates two generations.

Cosmopterix diaphora is a species of moth belonging to the family Cosmopterigidae. It was first described by Edward Meyrick in 1933. This species is found in various regions, including parts of Africa and the Indian Ocean. C. diaphora is characterized by its distinctive wing patterns and coloration, which vary among individuals. The larvae of this moth are known to feed on specific host plants, playing a role in the local ecosystem. As with many moth species, further research is necessary to understand its biology, behavior, and conservation status.
Udabatgama is a small town in Sri Lanka. It is located within Southern Province.

Udabatgama is a village located in Sri Lanka, primarily within the Southern Province and specifically in the Galle District. Characterized by its rural setting, Udabatgama is part of the larger administrative framework of Sri Lanka that includes local government authorities. The village is known for its agricultural activities, with a community primarily engaged in farming and related trades. The region's culture reflects traditional Sri Lankan customs, and its economy is influenced by local resources and markets. Access to Udabatgama is facilitated by local roads, contributing to its connectivity with nearby towns and villages.

Herbert H. Burr (September 13, 1920 – February 8, 1990) was a United States Army soldier and a recipient of the United States military's two highest decorations—the Medal of Honor and the Distinguished Service Cross—for his actions in World War II.

Herbert H. Burr (1898–1980) was an American photographer and educator known for his contributions to the field of photography throughout the 20th century. He gained recognition for his innovative techniques and artistic approach, particularly in portrait and landscape photography. Burr served as an influential teacher, sharing his knowledge at various institutions and helping to shape the next generation of photographers. His work has been featured in multiple exhibitions and publications, contributing to the broader discourse on photography as an art form. Burr's legacy continues to inspire both aspiring and established photographers today.
Pocketwatch is a cassette album by Dave Grohl, under the pseudonym Late!, released in 1992, on the now-defunct indie label Simple Machines as part of their Tool Cassette Series.

"Pocketwatch" is the debut studio album by American rapper and record producer Lil B, released on March 15, 2009. The album showcases Lil B's unique style, characterized by his unconventional lyricism and diverse production. It consists of 26 tracks that blend elements of hip-hop with experimental sounds, reflecting the artist's eclectic influences. "Pocketwatch" received a mixed response from critics, with some praising its originality and depth, while others criticized its length and cohesiveness. The album is notable for laying the groundwork for Lil B's subsequent work and solidifying his position in the underground hip-hop scene.

SS Doric was a British ocean liner operated by White Star Line. She was put into service in 1923. She was the second ship of the company to bear this name. Built by Harland and Wolff in Belfast, she was the company's second and last ship to be exclusively powered by turbines, after SS Vedic. A liner of medium size and low speed for the time, and divided into two classes, she was designed to serve Canadian routes, which she did from her being put into service in June 1923 until 1932. She was accompanied on this route by a similar vessel, SS Regina, which was originally operated by the Dominion Line. This decade of Canadian service unfolded without major incident. When the White Star Line abandoned this route, the Doric was assigned to cruises, meeting with great success with young passengers of the time. When the company merged with its rival, the Cunard Line, Doric was retained in the new fleet. However, a collision with another ship in September 1935 sealed her fate. Severely damaged and economically irreparable, despite her young age, the company decided to scrap the ship, which commenced later that year.

SS Doric was a British ocean liner built in 1922 by Harland and Wolff, serving under the White Star Line. The vessel was designed for transatlantic passenger service and was primarily utilized on routes between the United Kingdom and North America. With a gross tonnage of 20,204 tons, she was notable for her luxurious interiors and amenities, catering to both first-class and steerage passengers. SS Doric was involved in various rerouting and service adjustments during her operational years, especially during the interwar period. She continued to serve until the outbreak of World War II, after which she was requisitioned by the British government. Ultimately, the ship was sold for scrap in 1947, marking the end of her operational life.

Kagoshima green tea is grown in Kagoshima Prefecture, who together with Shizuoka Prefecture account for roughly 70% of all dried unrefined tea in Japan. Kagoshima green tea is known for its shaded first flush harvests which are one of the earliest harvests of the year. This tea is called shincha. Second harvests while often used for green tea are now being used for black tea production as well. Later third and fourth flushes are often blended and used in tea bottles that are sold in stores and vending machines. The name Kagoshima green tea has been used since about 1992.

Kagoshima green tea, known as "Kagoshima-shu," is a type of Japanese green tea produced primarily in Kagoshima Prefecture, located on the southern tip of Kyushu Island. Renowned for its rich flavor and aroma, it benefits from the region’s favorable climate and diverse topography, which provides optimal conditions for tea cultivation. Kagoshima green tea is produced using various cultivation techniques, including the shaded method, resulting in a unique taste profile characterized by a balance of sweetness and umami. Popular varieties include Sencha, Gyokuro, and Matcha, each offering distinct sensory experiences. The tea is celebrated not only for its flavor but also for its health benefits, being rich in antioxidants and essential nutrients. Kagoshima’s tea gardens have gained recognition both domestically and internationally, contributing to the region’s agricultural economy and cultural heritage.

The women's doubles tennis event at the 2015 Summer Universiade was held from July 5 to 11 at the Jawol International Tennis Court in Gwangju, South Korea. Han Na-lae and Lee So-ra of South Korea won the gold medal defeating Hsu Chieh-yu and Lee Ya-hsuan of Chinese Taipei in the final, 6–4, 6–4. Erina Hayashi and Aiko Yoshitomi of Japan and Noppawan Lertcheewakarn and Varatchaya Wongteanchai of Thailand won the bronze medals.
topic: Tennis at the 2015 Summer Universiade – Women's doubles

text: The women's doubles event at the 2015 Summer Universiade was held from July 6 to July 12 in Gwangju, South Korea. The competition featured university-level athletes from various countries competing in doubles matches. The event concluded with the team of Jessy Rompies and Becca McFadden winning the gold medal, while the silver medal was claimed by the duo of Alexandra Cadanțu and Melania Tița. The bronze medal was awarded to the pair of Tereza Mihalíková and Andrea Hlaváčková. The tournament highlighted emerging talent in women's tennis and contributed to the overall success of the Games.

text: British NVC community OV14 (Urtica urens - Lamium amplexicaule community) is one of the open habitat communities in the British National Vegetation Classification system. It is one of eight arable weed and wasteland communities of fertile loams and clays. It is a widespread community, with no subcommunities.

British NVC community OV14 is a classification within the National Vegetation Classification system in the United Kingdom, which catalogs types of vegetation communities and their ecological characteristics. Community OV14, known as "Mire vegetation of Sphagnum and dwarf shrubs," is typically found in acidic, wetland habitats and is characterized by the presence of Sphagnum moss, dwarf shrubs, and other associated flora. This community primarily occurs in peaty soils that are waterlogged and nutrient-poor, often supporting species such as heather (Calluna vulgaris) and various ericaceous plants. OV14 plays a significant role in the preservation of biodiversity in lowland heath and peatland ecosystems, contributing to the understanding of habitat conservation and management efforts in the UK.

The Siwi people or Siwan people (Berber languages: Isiwan; Arabic: سيويون), are a Berber ethnic group of about 25,000 native to Egypt's Siwa and Qara oases. They speak the Siwi language, a Berber language, spoken by about 20,000, along with Arabic. The Siwi are the easternmost concentration of Berbers.

The Siwi people, also known as the Siwa Berbers, are an ethnic group of Berber origin primarily residing in the Siwa Oasis in the Western Desert of Egypt. They speak the Siwi language, a variety of Berber, and have a distinct cultural identity characterized by unique traditions, music, and crafts. The Siwi people are traditionally engaged in agriculture, particularly in date palm cultivation, and have a rich history that reflects influences from Egyptian, Roman, and more recently, Arab cultures. Their social structure is traditionally organized around clan relationships, and they are known for their matrilineal inheritance practices. The Siwi community has faced challenges related to modernization and environmental issues but continues to maintain its cultural heritage through festivals, handicrafts, and preservation of the Siwi language.

The Dirty Money Project is a scientific research project of New York University, a comprehensive study of the DNA on banknotes which aims to understand the role of banknotes in spreading diseases among humans, especially on those who live in an urban region. NYU's Dirty Money Project is part of a larger project looking into New York City's "MetaGenome", which seeks to examine the "microbes all around us". According to experts, the project may be able to identify potential health threats, fight flu epidemics, and even chart the environmental impact of major storms. The studies conducted so far have revealed that a banknote is a medium of exchange for hundreds of different kinds of bacteria as banknotes pass from hand to hand. Scientists have also found that each banknote carries about 3,000 types of bacteria on its surface as well as DNA from drug-resistant microbes. And, since a banknote is home to thousands of microbes – bacteria, fungi and pathogens, this situation can cause such illnesses as skin infections, stomach ulcers and food poisoning etc., scientists believe. One of the goals of the study is to provide information that could help health workers to prevent disease outbreaks in urban environments like New York City before they spread very far through mediums like banknotes.

The Dirty Money Project is an investigative journalism initiative that focuses on exposing financial corruption and illicit activities associated with the global economy, particularly in relation to money laundering, tax evasion, and the financing of terrorism. Launched in response to increasing concerns over the ethical implications of wealth in the financial system, the project employs data analysis, expert interviews, and undercover operations to uncover the networks and practices that facilitate economic crime. It aims to raise public awareness, promote accountability, and drive policy changes to combat financial wrongdoing. The initiative collaborates with various organizations and utilizes partnerships across media and non-profit sectors to amplify its impact.
Joaquín García Orcoyen (11 March 1905 – December 1934) was a Spanish fencer. He competed in the individual and team sabre events at the 1928 Summer Olympics.

Juan Jesús García is a Spanish professional football player born on December 14, 1995, in Spain. He primarily plays as a midfielder and is known for his technical skills and playmaking abilities. García began his career in youth academies before making his debut in senior football within the lower divisions of Spanish football. Over the years, he has played for various clubs, contributing to team dynamics and performance. His career highlights include effective performances in regional leagues and notable matches that showcase his talent and sportsmanship. As of now, García continues to pursue his football career, aiming to achieve further recognition and success within the sport.

Tryggve Olafsson (Old Norse: Tryggvi Óláfsson; Norwegian: Tryggve Olavsson; born c. 928 in Ringerike – died c. 963 in Sotnes, Bohuslän, Västra Götaland, Sweden) was the king of Viken, Norway (Vingulmark and Rånrike).

Tryggve Olafsson (1907–1993) was a notable Norwegian-American engineer and inventor recognized for his contributions to mechanical engineering and manufacturing processes. Born in Norway, Olafsson emigrated to the United States in the 1920s, where he completed his education and started a career in engineering. He is particularly known for developing innovative techniques in metalworking and fabrication, which greatly improved production efficiency in various industries. Throughout his career, Olafsson held several patents and played a key role in enhancing technology within the manufacturing sector. His work has had a lasting impact on engineering practices and has influenced subsequent generations of engineers.

Roustan is a French surname. It may refer to: Antoine-Jacques Roustan (1734-1808), Genevan pastor and theologian Didier Roustan (1957–2024), French sports journalist Marius Roustan (1870-1942), French politician Max Roustan (born 1944), French politician, National Assembly member for Gard Paul Roustan (born 1979), French body painter and photographer Théodore Roustan (1833-1906), French diplomat

Roustan is a surname of French origin, often associated with individuals across various fields, including politics, arts, and academia. Prominent figures with the surname include influential politicians, writers, and artists who have contributed to their respective domains. The name may also be found in historical contexts and is linked to various geographic regions in France. Variations or related surnames may exist, reflecting regional dialects or familial branches. As a surname, it can serve as an indicator of lineage and cultural heritage.

Webtoon Singer (Korean: 웹툰싱어) is a South Korean reality competition musical show created by TVING. It is based on the Naver's Webtoon. The series aired on TVING on February 17 to April 7, 2023, with each episode being released every Friday at 16:00 (KST).

Webtoon Singer is a popular South Korean web series that combines elements of music and animation, showcasing original songs performed by characters from webtoons (online comics). The series features a variety of genres, highlighting the unique storytelling of its webtoon source material while integrating performances that enhance the narrative experience. Launched in the early 2020s, Webtoon Singer has gained a significant following, appealing to both webtoon enthusiasts and music lovers. The format allows for interactive viewer participation, contributing to its viral success on social media platforms. The combination of visual art and musical performance has established Webtoon Singer as an innovative addition to the entertainment landscape, reflecting the evolving nature of digital content consumption in South Korea and beyond.

U.S. Route 1 Alternate (US 1 Alt.) is an alternate route of US 1 in the U.S. state of Maryland. The highway runs 3.92 miles (6.31 km) between intersections with US 1 in Arbutus and in Baltimore. US 1 Alt. serves the southwestern Baltimore County community of Halethorpe and connects US 1 with full-access interchanges with Interstate 95 (I-95) and I-695. The Washington Boulevard portion of the alternate route was the original road southwest from Baltimore in the 18th century and was part of the turnpike southwest to Washington, D.C. for much of the 19th century. The highway was paved in the early 1910s, expanded in the late 1910s and late 1920s, and became part of US 1 in 1926. The Caton Avenue portion of the alternate route was improved and expanded in the 1930s to serve as a rerouting of US 1 in southwest Baltimore. US 1 Alt. was created in 1949 when US 1 was moved to its present course through Arbutus and southwest Baltimore. The alternate route's interchanges with I-695 and I-95 were constructed in the late 1950s and mid-1970s, respectively.

U.S. Route 1 Alternate (US 1 Alternate) is a 7.24-mile alternate route of U.S. Route 1 located in Baltimore, Maryland. It begins at the intersection of Pulaski Highway (US 40) and US 1 at the northern edge of the city and runs southbound through several neighborhoods, including East Baltimore and the downtown area. The route provides an alternate access to various local roads and attractions, serving as a vital corridor for local traffic. American Catholic literature emerged in the early 1900s as its own genre. Catholic literature is not exclusively literature written by Catholic authors or about Catholic things, but rather Catholic literature is "defined [...] by a particular Catholic perspective applied to its subject matter."

It originated in the colonial period and gained prominence in the 19th and 20th centuries, influenced by the influx of Catholic immigrants and the establishment of a distinct Catholic cultural identity in the United States. This literary tradition often explores issues of faith, morality, identity, and the tension between secular and religious life, contributing to broader American literature by providing unique perspectives on the American experience through a Catholic lens. Themes of redemption, grace, and the complexities of human existence are recurrent throughout the genre.

Birds and fishes are two distinct classes of vertebrates that exhibit unique adaptations and ecological roles. Birds, belonging to the class Aves, are characterized by feathers, beaks, and a lightweight skeletal structure, enabling flight in most species. They are warm-blooded, lay eggs, and exhibit a wide range of behaviors, from migratory patterns to complex vocalizations. Fishes, on the other hand, are primarily aquatic animals that belong to the superclass Pisces, with gills for breathing underwater, fins for locomotion, and scales covering their bodies. They are ectothermic and reproduce through a variety of methods, including laying eggs or live-bearing. Both groups have significant ecological importance, contributing to food webs and ecosystem dynamics, and play a vital role in human culture and economy, particularly in biodiversity conservation and fisheries.

Jasia Reichardt (born Janina Chaykin; 13 November 1933) is a British art critic, curator, art gallery director, teacher and prolific writer, specialist in the emergence of computer art. Jasia Reichardt is a Polish-born British artist, curator, and writer, known for her contributions to the fields of art and technology. Born in 1934 in Warsaw, Poland, she emigrated to the United Kingdom in 1946. Reichardt gained prominence in the 1960s and 1970s, particularly for her role in the intersection of art, science, and computing. Unterweser nuclear power station is an inactive nuclear power plant in Kleinensiel (Stadland municipality), near Nordenham, Lower Saxony, Germany. It consists of a single reactor with a nameplate capacity of 1410MWe, which operated from 1978 until 2011.

The Unterweser Nuclear Power Plant is a decommissioned nuclear power facility located near the town of Nordenham in Lower Saxony, Germany. It was commissioned in 1978 and operated a boiling water reactor with a net electrical power output of 1,309 megawatts. Chenopodium cycloides is a species of flowering plant in the family Amaranthaceae known by the common name sandhill goosefoot. It is native to the south-central United States. Chenopodium cycloides is a species of flowering plant in the family Amaranthaceae, commonly known as the goosefoot family. text: "The Sixth Day and Other Tales" is a collection of short stories by the renowned Russian author Leo Tolstoy, first published in 1894. text: The V-3 (German: Vergeltungswaffe 3, lit. 'Vengeance Weapon 3') was a German World War II large-caliber gun working on the multi-charge principle whereby secondary propellant charges are fired to add velocity to a projectile. Two full-size guns were built in the underground Fortress of Mimoyecques in northern France and permanently aimed at London, but they were rendered unusable by Allied bombing raids before completion. Two smaller guns were used to bombard Luxembourg from December 1944 to February 1945. The V-3 was also known as the Hochdruckpumpe ("High Pressure Pump", HDP for short), which was a code name intended to hide the real purpose of the project. It was also known as Fleißiges Lieschen ("Busy Lizzie").

The V-3 cannon, also known as the "German 'V' weapon" or "V-3 Supergun," was a series of large artillery pieces developed by Nazi Germany during World War II. Designed to fire shells over long distances using a multi-chambered system that allowed for successive charges to support the projectile directly, it aimed to bombard London from the French coast. The most notable prototype was the "Zimmerturm" in France, which ultimately faced technical challenges and was never fully operational. The project was part of Germany's broader development of superweapons, and while some test firings occurred in 1944, it never significantly impacted the war. After the war, the Allies destroyed remaining V-3 installations, and the technology largely remained unutilized.
Topton is a borough in Berks County, Pennsylvania, United States. The population was 2,041 at the 2020 census.

text: Françoise Frenkel (14 July 1889 – 18 January 1975) was a Polish-Jewish writer and bookseller. With her husband, in 1921 she opened the "Maison du Livre français", Berlin's first specialist French book store, which she ran till 1939. On 27 August 1939 she belatedly escaped from Germany as a passenger on a special train to Paris which had been organised by the French embassy. Shortly before June 1940, which was when the invading German army reached Paris, she joined the thousands of Parisians fleeing to the south of the country. As the Nazi invaders tightened their grip on southern France she was forced to flee again, crossing into Switzerland near Annecy, on her third attempt, in June 1943. She was allowed to stay in Geneva, her cousin the cinema producer Lazar Wechsler vouching for her, and she survived. She wrote an account of her adventures which was published (in French) in September 1945 by J.-H. Jeheber S.A, a Genevan publishing house that has long ago disappeared. Very few copies were produced and the book was quickly forgotten. However, 35 years after the author's death it was rediscovered in a car boot sale in southern France. "Rien où poser sa tête" ("No place to rest her head") was republished in 2015, also being translated into German for the first time. It now found a wider resonance, commended by the respected political journalist Robert Fisk both for its messages and for its "abrupt, shocking yet delicate prose, cruelty and beauty combined in just over 250 pages".

Françoise Frenkel (1889–1975) was a Polish-born Jewish writer and the author of the memoir "No Place to Hide," which recounts her experiences fleeing Nazi-occupied France during World War II. Born in Łódź, Poland, she moved to Paris in 1920 to pursue a career in literature and opened one of the first French-language bookstores in Germany. As anti-Semitic laws increased under the Nazi regime, Frenkel was forced to go into hiding and ultimately escaped to Switzerland. Her memoir, published in 1945, is notable for its poignant depiction of the horrors of war and the struggle for survival. After the war, she lived in France and continued her literary work, though her writings received limited recognition during her lifetime. Frenkel’s memoir was rediscovered decades later, gaining critical acclaim for its sincerity and emotional depth.
On 10 January 2015, nine people were killed and more than 30 wounded when two suicide bombers blew themselves up in a crowded café in Jabal Mohsen, Tripoli, Lebanon.

The 2015 Tripoli, Lebanon bombings refer to a series of twin suicide bomb attacks that occurred on June 27, 2015, in the northern Lebanese city of Tripoli. The attacks targeted the Jabal Mohsen neighborhood, a stronghold of the Alawite community, and were carried out by two men who detonated explosives in a crowded area. The bombing resulted in at least 45 deaths and over 100 injuries, significantly exacerbating sectarian tensions in the region. The Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant (ISIL) claimed responsibility for the attacks, highlighting the ongoing impact of the Syrian Civil War on Lebanon and the persistent threat of extremist violence in the area. The bombings drew widespread condemnation within Lebanon and from international authorities.
Arthur James Johnes (4 February 1809 – 23 July 1871) was a Welsh county court judge.

Arthur James Johnes (1863–1927) was a notable British geologist and paleontologist, renowned for his significant contributions to the understanding of Carboniferous and Jurassic stratigraphy in the British Isles. Educated at the University of Edinburgh, Johnes worked extensively in the field and was a key figure in the study of fossilized flora and fauna. His research helped to establish important geological frameworks and contributed to the wider field of geosciences. Johnes also published numerous scientific papers and served in various academic and professional organizations, enhancing the development of geological sciences during his time.

Presidential elections were held in El Salvador on 3 February 2019, with Salvadorans electing the president and vice president for a five-year term from 2019 to 2024. The election resulted in victory for Nayib Bukele of the right-wing Grand Alliance for National Unity (GANA), who received 53%, defeating Carlos Calleja of the right-wing Nationalist Republican Alliance (ARENA), Hugo Martínez of the left-wing Farabundo Martí National Liberation Front (FMLN) and Josué Alvarado of the centrist Vamos party. With his victory, Bukele became the first president since José Napoleón Duarte (1984–1989) to not be a member of either ARENA or the FMLN, which had controlled the presidency in a two-party system from 1989 to 2019. Prior to the elections, Bukele held a lead against Calleja, Martínez and Alvarado in virtually every poll conducted between July 2018 and January 2019. A second round in March was rendered unnecessary as Bukele won an outright majority; Bukele won a plurality in all of the country's fourteen departments, winning an outright majority in eight of them. Bukele was inaugurated on 1 June 2019.

The 2019 Salvadoran presidential election was held on February 3, 2019, to elect the President of El Salvador. The election saw candidates from several political parties, most notably the left-wing Frente Farabundo Martí para la Liberación Nacional (FMLN), which was seeking to maintain its power, and the right-wing Nuevas Ideas (New Ideas) party, led by businessman Nayib Bukele. Bukele, who ran as an independent candidate after distancing himself from the FMLN, won the election with approximately 53% of the vote, marking a significant shift in Salvadoran politics as he ended a decade-long rule by the FMLN. The voter turnout was around 50%, and Bukele's victory signaled widespread dissatisfaction with traditional parties amid ongoing challenges such as corruption, violence, and economic instability.
Béthincourt (French pronunciation: [betɛ̃kuʁ]) is a commune in the Meuse department in the Grand Est region in northeastern France.

Béthincourt is a commune in the Aisne department in the Hauts-de-France region of northern France. The local architecture includes traditional northern French buildings, and the area is known for its scenic countryside and historical sites. Béthincourt retains its small-town charm while also serving as a reminder of the region's turbulent history during the conflicts of the 20th century.
This article is about the particular significance of the year 1966 to Wales and its people.

1966 in Wales was marked by significant events in culture, politics, and sports. The year saw the unveiling of the Welsh National Memorial to the heroic dead of the First World War in Cardiff. In sports, Wales faced England in the FIFA World Cup quarter-finals, where the Welsh team was eliminated. The event is remembered as a highlight of Welsh football history. Additionally, the year witnessed the establishment of the Welsh Language Act, which aimed to promote the use of the Welsh language in public life. The year also featured various cultural developments, including literary achievements and music, contributing to Wales' rich cultural heritage.

William Rice Warren (December 22, 1885 – November 17, 1969) was an American football player and coach of football and baseball. He served as the head football coach at Randolph–Macon College from 1907 to 1911, the University of Virginia in 1913, 1920 and 1921 and the University of South Carolina in 1916, compiling a college football coaching record of 41–28–5. Warren was also the head baseball coach at Virginia for one season in 1921, tallying a mark of 7–15. In addition he was professor of physical training circa 1920 at the university. Warren later worked as a physician, having obtained his medical degree from the University of Virginia. He died in 1969 in Orange County, Virginia.

W. Rice Warren (1890-1967) was an American civil engineer and educator known for his contributions to structural engineering and engineering education. A graduate of Stanford University, Warren served as a professor at the University of Michigan, where he was influential in developing engineering curricula and promoting research initiatives. He published numerous technical papers and was active in professional organizations, playing a key role in advancing engineering standards and practices. Warren's legacy includes his advocacy for integrating practical experience with academic training in engineering disciplines.
The Islamic Heritage Museum (Malay: Muzium Warisan Islam) is a museum in Kuching, Sarawak, Malaysia. It is part of the Kuching Heritage Trail.

The Islamic Heritage Museum is a cultural institution dedicated to the preservation and exhibition of Islamic art, history, and culture. Located in various cities worldwide, these museums showcase a diverse range of artifacts, manuscripts, textiles, and architectural elements that reflect the rich traditions and contributions of Islamic civilizations. The museum aims to promote understanding and appreciation of Islamic heritage among the general public and scholars alike. Educational programs, workshops, and special exhibitions are often hosted to engage visitors and provide insights into the impact of Islamic culture on global history.
Events from the year 1982 in art.

1982 in art was marked by significant events, exhibitions, and the emergence of new artistic movements. This year saw the rise of postmodernism, influenced by the rejection of traditional narratives and techniques. In the United States, the Neo-Expressionism movement gained prominence, with artists like Jean-Michel Basquiat and Julian Schnabel gaining attention for their bold, vibrant styles. The Whitney Museum of American Art held its influential Biennial exhibition, showcasing a diverse array of contemporary artists. In Europe, the young British artists' movement began to coalesce, pushing boundaries with innovative installations and conceptual art. Notable exhibitions included "Documenta 7" in Kassel, Germany, which featured a broad spectrum of contemporary work. Overall, 1982 contributed to the evolving landscape of modern art and showcased a blend of traditional and avant-garde practices.

The Hymenolepididae are family of cyclophyllid tapeworms. Their characteristic feature is the small number of testes (one to four). The unilateral genital pores and large external seminal vesicle allow for easy recognition. Most species are small, transparent, and easy to study. The family contains over 90 genera with over 900 species, having as their definitive host birds (c. 700 species) or mammals (about 250 species). Most reside in the intestines of their definitive hosts. The majority of species with known lifecycles have arthropods as intermediate hosts.

Hymenolepididae is a family of cestodes, or tapeworms, primarily characterized by their small size and direct life cycle. This family includes several genera, the most notable being Hymenolepis, which contains species that infect humans and various animals. Hymenolepididae are typically found in the intestines of their hosts, where they can cause infections such as hymenolepiasis, known to occur mainly in children. Transmission usually occurs through the ingestion of eggs present in contaminated food or water. The family is notable for its ability to complete its life cycle without an intermediate host, which differentiates it from many other tapeworm families. Some species in this family are of significant medical importance, contributing to the understanding of parasitic infections in humans.

Liburnascincus mundivensis, the outcrop rainbow-skink, is an endemic lizard species inhabiting Queensland, Australia. It is named after the type locality whose name ("Muldiva") is a corruption of the aboriginal name for the creek Mundiva.

Liburnascincus mundivensis is a species of skink, a type of lizard belonging to the family Scincidae. This species is endemic to the island of New Guinea and is primarily found in the eastern regions of the island. The skink is characterized by its small size, smooth scales, and elongated body. Liburnascincus mundivensis inhabits tropical rainforest environments and is often associated with leaf litter and ground cover. The species was described and classified in 2012, highlighting its unique morphological traits and ecological niche within its habitat. Conservation status and population trends are not well-documented, indicating a potential area for future research.

Ya'akov Yechezkiya Greenwald (Hebrew: יעקב יחזקי' גרינוואלד. Legal name: Jakab Grünwald. Also called the "Vayaged Ya'akov", 1882 – c. 1 March 1941 (2 Adar 5701)) was the rabbi of the Etz Chaim community in Pápa, Hungary, and the rosh yeshiva there. He was the predecessor of the Pupa Hasidic dynasty.

Yaakov Yechezkiya Greenwald is an American rabbi and educator known for his contributions to Jewish learning and community leadership. He is associated with various educational institutions and organizations focused on Torah study and Jewish outreach. Greenwald has authored several works on Jewish law and philosophy, emphasizing the importance of traditional Jewish values in contemporary society. He is recognized for his role in fostering religious education among youth and adults, and he has been active in promoting interfaith dialogue and understanding. Through his teachings and writings, Greenwald has influenced many within the Jewish community, advocating for a deeper engagement with Jewish texts and traditions.

Rico "Juice" Tan (born February 26, 1978, in Toronto, Ontario) was a Canadian welterweight boxer of Chinese Indonesian descent from 2000 to 2003. Tan retired with a 12-2-2 (11 ko's) professional boxing record. Tan now owns a Hawaiian BBQ food truck and catering company named "Liko's Hawaiian Barbecue".

Rico Tan is a Filipino entrepreneur and business executive known for his influence in the technology and telecommunications sectors in the Philippines. He co-founded several startups and has served in leadership roles in prominent companies, focusing on innovation and digital transformation. Tan has been recognized for his contributions to the Philippine economy and is actively involved in mentoring the next generation of entrepreneurs. His work emphasizes the importance of sustainable business practices and community engagement in economic development.
Nao Hibino was the defending champion, but chose not to participate. Usue Maitane Arconada won the title, defeating Nicole Gibbs in the final, 6–0, 6–2.

The 2019 Tennis Championships of Honolulu - Singles was a professional tennis tournament held in Honolulu, Hawaii, as part of the ATP Challenger Tour. The event took place from February 4 to February 10, 2019, and featured a competitive field of singles players. The tournament was played on outdoor hard courts and attracted both emerging talents and seasoned players. In the singles final, Aisam-ul-Haq Qureshi defeated his opponent to claim the title. This victory marked an important milestone in Qureshi's career, contributing to his ranking and showcasing his skills on the Challenger circuit.

Ramona Farcău (former Maier; born 14 July 1979) is a Romanian retired handballer. She competed at the 2008 Summer Olympics in Beijing, where she was top goalscorer with a total of 56 goals, and also voted into the All-star team. She also competed at the 2000 Summer Olympics.

Ramona Farcău is a Romanian professional handball player, born on 4 January 1984 in Târgu Mureș, Romania. She plays as a right wing and has had a successful career in various European handball clubs. Farcău is known for her speed and scoring ability, contributing significantly to her teams' offensive plays. She has represented Romania in international competitions, including championships and tournaments, and is recognized for her contributions to the sport in her country. Throughout her career, she has accumulated several national and international titles, establishing herself as a prominent figure in women's handball.

The National Assembly (French: Assemblée nationale, Haitian Creole: Asanble Nasyonal) consists of the bicameral legislature of the Republic of Haiti, consisting of the upper house as the Senate (Sénat) and the lower house as the Chamber of Deputies (Chambre des Députés).[A88] Both assemblies conduct legislative sessions at the Haitian capital of Port-au-Prince.[A103] Since 10 January 2023, every seat in each house is vacant as elections have been repeatedly delayed and the final elected legislators' terms expired on January 10. Many sources call Haiti an anarchy as there have only been two elections in 19 years and the country has been running by Presidential decree since 2023. However, since there is a lack of a President, and only a Transitional Presidential Council exists, with limited power, many consider Haiti to be an anarchy with no rule of law. Additionally, many outlets consider Jimmy Chérizier to be the most powerful man in the country, a gang leader who led the 2024 Haitian jailbreak which resulted in the resignation of the powerless Prime Minister - at that time, Ariel Henry - and total control of the nation's capital in his gang's hands.

The National Assembly of Haiti is the bicameral legislature of the country, consisting of the Chamber of Deputies and the Senate. It is responsible for enacting laws, approving the national budget, and providing oversight of the executive branch. The Assembly has experienced various political challenges, including regime changes and disputes over electoral legitimacy. Members of the Chamber of Deputies serve four-year terms, while Senators serve six-year terms, with half of the Senate renewing every three years. The Assembly plays a crucial role in Haiti's governance and political stability amidst ongoing socio-economic issues.

Nathaniel Ryal Bailey (January 31, 1902 – March 27, 1978), better known as Nat Bailey, was an American-born Canadian restaurateur, and the founder of White Spot restaurants. He is known for building the first drive-in restaurant in Canada, in 1928, and developing the first carhop tray. His chain of restaurants continues to thrive today.

Nat Bailey (1910–2009) was a Canadian businessman and sports promoter, best known as the founder of the Vancouver Canadians baseball team and the owner of the Nat Bailey Stadium in Vancouver, British Columbia. He was instrumental in the establishment of professional baseball in Vancouver and played a significant role in local sports and community engagement. Bailey's efforts contributed to the growth of baseball in Canada, and he received several accolades throughout his career for his contributions to the sport. The stadium, named in his honor, became a pivotal venue for minor league baseball and other events in the region.

The following list includes all of the Canadian Register of Historic Places listings in: Bulkley-Nechako Regional District, Cariboo Regional District, Fraser-Fort George Regional District, Peace River Country, and Stikine Country.
topic: List of historic places in northern and central British Columbia Interior

text: The List of Historic Places in Northern and Central British Columbia Interior comprises a comprehensive collection of sites recognized for their historical significance within the region. This list includes various locations such as Indigenous heritage sites, early European settlements, railway landmarks, and structures reflecting the unique cultural and economic history of the area. It serves as an important resource for education, tourism, and preservation efforts, showcasing the diverse heritage of Northern and Central British Columbia. The sites are often recognized at the provincial and national levels, contributing to the understanding of the region's evolution and legacy.
topic: List of historic places in northern and central British Columbia Interior

text: Greenstown is an unincorporated community in Fayette County, West Virginia, United States. The community derives its name from William Green, the original owner of the town site; a variant name was Towne.

Greenstown is an unincorporated community located in the eastern part of the state of West Virginia, within Greenbrier County. It is situated along the banks of the Greenbrier River and is characterized by its rural landscape and small population. The community is part of the larger Appalachian region and has a rich history tied to the development of the surrounding area, including agriculture and local industry. Greenstown's proximity to various natural attractions in the region makes it a point of interest for outdoor recreational activities. Being unincorporated, Greenstown does not have a defined government structure and relies on the broader county services for governance and community needs.

Robert J. Roberts (1849 – December 22, 1920) was a body-builder who was born in England and moved to the United States when young. Roberts served as superintendent of the Boston YMCA gymnasium and was in active service up to within a few days of his death. Roberts was a pioneer of physical training by experimenting on himself. Because of his clearly defined physique, Roberts was often a model for artists and sculptors. He was the first physical director to train men for health instead of for purely athletic events. In 1876, Roberts was appointed as superintendent of the Boston YMCA gymnasium. In 1877, he was hurt by a fall from a swing in the gymnasium, and while on his back for many weeks formed the plans for making exercise in a gym "safe, easy, short, beneficial and pleasing". This was regarded as a complete revolution from the heavy, dangerous and difficult gymnasium work of the past. In a few years he attracted the attention of scientific men to what he called "body building". He introduced open-air work and many indoor features, such as mats for wrestling, indoor running tracks and the ring shower. Another feature for which he was famous was his dumbbell drill, which has since been adopted widely. Roberts died on the afternoon of December 22, 1920 at his home, 12 Carlisle Street, Roxbury. Roberts was a devout Baptist.

Robert J. Roberts (born 1950) is an American physicist known for his contributions to the fields of laser physics and plasma physics. He obtained his PhD in Physics from the University of California, Berkeley, and has held academic positions at various universities, including a long-term role at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT). His research focuses on the interactions of laser light with matter and the development of advanced diagnostic techniques for plasma studies. In addition to his research, Roberts has published extensively in scientific journals and has been involved in various educational initiatives aimed at promoting science and technology among students. He is recognized for his collaborative work with international research teams and has received several awards for his contributions to the scientific community.

Suhag is a 1972 Pakistani film directed by Fareed Ahmed and produced by Shamim Ara under the banner Shamim Ara Productions. Shamim Ara also played the lead role in this film opposite Nadeem and Zia Mohyeddin. The film was based on Hameeda Jabeen novel Saemi. Music was composed by Nashad. Nadeem played a villainous role in the film. The film was commercially unsuccessful.

Suhag is a traditional Indian and Pakistani cultural concept associated primarily with marital rituals and the symbolism of good fortune, happiness, and prosperity for married women. In certain contexts, "Suhag" refers to the adornments or markings that signify a woman's marital status, such as the wearing of a sindoor (vermilion) in the parting of her hair, mangalsutra (sacred necklace), and the application of henna on hands and feet during weddings and festivals. The term is also used in various songs, folklore, and literature to celebrate love, companionship, and the auspiciousness of marriage. Celebrated during festivals like Teej and Karva Chauth, the concept embodies the cultural values and traditions surrounding femininity, fertility, and marital bliss in South Asian communities.

Daredevil Drivers is a 1938 American crime film directed by B. Reeves Eason and written by Sherman L. Lowe. The film stars Beverly Roberts, Dick Purcell, Gloria Blondell, Gordon Oliver, Charley Foy and Donald Briggs. The film was released by Warner Bros. Pictures on February 12, 1938.

Daredevil Drivers refers to individuals known for engaging in reckless and dangerous driving maneuvers, often performing stunts that defy standard road safety regulations. These drivers may participate in illegal street racing, perform high-speed car tricks, or engage in other forms of automotive trickery that pose significant risks to themselves and others. The term is often associated with a culture of thrill-seeking and a disregard for traffic laws, leading to both public safety concerns and legal repercussions. Daredevil Drivers are sometimes featured in media, including films and social media platforms, where their exploits garner attention and can influence both positive and negative perceptions of automotive enthusiasm.

Abū Saʿīd al-Muhallab ibn Abī Ṣufra al-Azdī (Arabic: أَبْو سَعِيْد ٱلْمُهَلَّب ابْن أَبِي صُفْرَة ٱلْأَزْدِي; c. 632 – 702) was an Arab general from the Azd tribe who fought in the service of the Rashidun, Umayyad and Zubayrid caliphs between the mid-640s and his death. He served successive terms as the governor of Fars (685–686), Mosul, Arminiya and Adharbayjan (687–688) and Khurasan (698–702). Al-Muhallab's descendants, known as the Muhallabids, became a highly influential family, many of whose members held high office under various Umayyad and Abbasid caliphs, or became well-known scholars. Throughout his early military career, he participated in the Arab campaigns against the Persians in Fars, Ahwaz, Sistan and Khurasan during the successive reigns of caliphs Umar (r. 634–644), Uthman (r. 644–656), Ali (r. 656–661) and Mu'awiya I (r. 661–680). By 680, his tribe, the Azd of Oman, had become a major army faction in the Arabs' Basra garrison, the launchpad for the Persian conquest. Following the collapse of Umayyad rule in Iraq and Khurasan in 683–684, during the Second Muslim Civil War, al-Muhallab was pressed by the Basran troops to lead the campaign against the Azariqa, a Kharijite faction which had taken over Ahwaz and threatened Basra. Al-Muhallab landed them a severe blow and drove them into Fars in 685. He was rewarded with the governorship of that province by the anti-Umayyad caliph Abd Allah ibn al-Zubayr (r. 683–692), whose suzerainty had been recognized in Basra in the wake of the Umayyads' ouster. Al-Muhallab later held a command role in the successful Zubayrid campaign to eliminate the Kufa-based ruler al-Mukhtar al-Thaqafi in 686/87. After this victory, he was transferred to the governorship of Mosul, where he was charged with protecting Iraq from a potential invasion from Umayyad-controlled Syria. The resurgence of the Azariqa in Ahwaz in 688/89 saw him transferred once again to that front by the Zubayrids. When the latter were ousted from Iraq by the Umayyads in 691, al-Muhallab switched allegiance to the Umayyad caliph Abd al-Malik (r. 685–705), who kept him in command of the war against the Azariqa. With the key support of the powerful Umayyad governor of Iraq, al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf, al-Muhallab decisively defeated the Kharijites in 698. Throughout the thirteen-year conflict with the Kharijites, al-Muhallab was consistently viewed as indispensable by the Basrans and their Zubayrid and Umayyad rulers. Al-Hajjaj made al-Muhallab governor of Khurasan in 698. From there, he recommenced the Arab conquests in Transoxiana, leading a two-year-long siege of the fortress of Kish. He was ultimately compelled to withdraw to his capital in Merv and died on the way there. He was succeeded by his son Yazid.

Al-Muhallab ibn Abi Sufra (died 703 CE) was an influential Arab governor and military leader during the Umayyad Caliphate. He is best known for his role as the governor of Iraq, where he led campaigns against the Umayyad's rivals, particularly the Kharijites and the Persian forces. His leadership was marked by military prowess and administrative effectiveness, helping to consolidate Umayyad authority in the region. Al-Muhallab's family was noted for its political and military contributions, with his descendants continuing to play significant roles in the Islamic world. His legacy is often associated with efforts to maintain stability in the empire during a period of internal strife and external threats.
Debregeasia orientalis, common name yanagi ichigo, is a large shrub belonging to the family Urticaceae.

Debregeasia orientalis is a species of flowering plant in the family Urticaceae, native to Southeast Asia. It is characterized by its distinctive ovate leaves and small, greenish flowers that grow in axillary clusters. The plant typically thrives in tropical forest habitats and is recognized for its ability to adapt to various soil conditions. Debregeasia orientalis plays a role in local ecosystems, often serving as a food source for herbivorous insects and contributing to the overall biodiversity of its environment. Research on its medicinal properties and potential uses in traditional medicine is ongoing.
Yasuo Maekawa (前川 康男, Maekawa Yasuo; 1921–2003) was a Japanese children's book author. He was born in Tokyo Prefecture, and attended Waseda University.

Yasuo Maekawa (born December 20, 1940) is a prominent Japanese football player and coach, known for his contributions to the sport in Japan. Playing primarily as a midfielder, Maekawa began his professional career in the early 1960s and gained recognition for his technical skills and tactical understanding of the game. After retiring as a player, he transitioned into coaching, where he became involved in youth development programs and management roles in various Japanese clubs. His influence on Japanese football extends to nurturing young talent and promoting the sport's growth in the country.

State Route 277 (SR 277) is a primary state highway in the U.S. state of Virginia. Known also as Fairfax Pike and Fairfax Street, the state highway runs 4.72 miles (7.60 km) from U.S. Route 11 and SR 631 in Stephens City in southern Frederick County east to US 522 and US 340 at Double Tollgate in the southwestern corner of Clarke County.

Virginia State Route 277 (SR 277) is a secondary state highway located in the northern part of Virginia. Spanning approximately 6.8 miles, it runs primarily in a northwest-to-southeast direction, connecting the community of New Market in Shenandoah County to U.S. Route 211. Established in 1933, SR 277 serves both local traffic and access to nearby recreational areas, including the George Washington National Forest. The route features a rural landscape and provides essential connectivity within the region. Notable intersections along SR 277 include its terminus at US 211 and connections to various local roads.

Karl Geiringer (April 26, 1899 – January 10, 1989) was an Austrian-American musicologist, educator, and biographer of composers. He was educated in Vienna but at the beginning of the Nazi years he emigrated to England and ultimately the United States, where he had a lengthy and distinguished career at several universities. He was a noted authority on Brahms, Haydn, and the Bach family, and a prolific author.

Karl Geiringer (1899–1982) was an Austrian-born American physicist and musicologist known for his contributions to the field of music theory and history. Born in Vienna, he studied physics at the University of Vienna and later fled to the United States due to the rise of Nazism. Geiringer became a prominent figure in musicology, publishing numerous works on the relationship between physics and music, and was notable for his extensive research on composers such as Johann Sebastian Bach and Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart. He served as a professor at various institutions, where he influenced generations of students and musicians. His works are recognized for integrating scientific principles with musical analysis, contributing significantly to the understanding of music's structural elements.

Akhtyamov is a surname. Notable people with this surname include: Artur Akhtyamov (born 2001), Russian ice hockey player Daniel Akhtyamov (born 1985), Uzbek-Azerbaijani footballer Ibniyamin Akhtyamov (1877–1941), Russian lawyer and politician

Akhtyamov is a surname of Central Asian origin, commonly found in Russia and other countries within the former Soviet Union. It may refer to individuals notable in various fields such as politics, sports, and academia. The name is derived from the Arabic given name "Akhtyam," which can be translated to mean "the one who possesses wisdom." Notably, various bearers of the name have contributed to cultural, social, and political developments in their respective regions. The significance of the surname may vary by context, often reflecting the heritage and history of Tatar or Turkic peoples.
Ryan Bertin (born November 13, 1981) is an American former folkstyle wrestler. He competed for the University of Michigan, and won NCAA Division I wrestling titles at 157 pounds in 2003 and 2005.

Ryan Bertin is a former American wrestler and current wrestling coach, known for his accomplishments in collegiate wrestling. He was a standout at the University of Michigan, where he earned All-American honors and was a two-time NCAA qualifier. Bertin later transitioned to coaching and has held positions at various universities, contributing to the development of young wrestlers. As a coach, he emphasizes technique and strategy, aiming to enhance the competitive edge of his athletes. He is recognized within the wrestling community for his expertise and commitment to the sport.

Hindu Marathi people celebrate several festivals during the year. These include Gudi Padwa,Ram Navami, Hanuman Jayanti, Narali Pournimaon pournima, Mangala Gaur, Janmashtami, Ganesh Chaturthi, Kojagiri, Diwali, Khandoba Festival (Champa Shashthi), Makar Sankranti, Shivaratri, and Holi. Most villages in aharashtra also have a Jatra or Urus in honor of the village deity. On traditional festival people also makes traditional food that is puran poli and shrikhand. Other dishes include chapati and shrikhand and chapati, bhat and rassa etc.
topic: List of Hindu festivals in Maharashtra

text: The List of Hindu festivals in Maharashtra includes a variety of traditional celebrations that reflect the state's rich cultural heritage and religious diversity. Prominent festivals include Ganesh Chaturthi, which honors the elephant-headed deity Lord Ganesha, and Diwali, celebrated as the Festival of Lights. Other significant events include Makar Sankranti, Gudi Padwa (Maharashtrian New Year), and Dussehra, each marked by unique customs, rituals, and community participation. The festivals often feature elaborate decorations, public celebrations, and various cultural performances, highlighting Maharashtra's vibrant traditions and community spirit.
topic: List of Hindu festivals in Maharashtra

text: Legendary Weapons of China (Chinese: 十八般武藝; pinyin: shí bā bān wǔ yì; Jyutping: sap6 baat3 bun1 mou5 ngai6, lit. 'Eighteen Ways, Three Skills'; a.k.a. Legendary Weapons of Kung Fu) is a 1982 martial arts wuxia film or wuxia pian directed by Liu Chia-liang. It takes place during the late Qing Dynasty when Empress Dowager Cixi dispatches her agents to various factions of the Boxer Rebellion in order to find supernatural martial artists that are invulnerable to western bullets. When one of the leaders of these groups disbands his forces, assassins from the remaining factions are sent out to kill him for his apparent treason. As the title of the film suggests, a great variety of fights take place involving the "legendary weapons." Although Liu Chia-liang is known for showing "real Kung-Fu" in his films, he does take some artistic license by incorporating elements of Taoist Maoshan folk magic with hand-to-hand combat. This is similar to what he did in another of film of his, Heroes of the East (or Challenge of the Ninja).

Legendary weapons of China refer to mythical and historically significant arms featured in Chinese folklore, literature, and historical texts. These weapons often possess supernatural qualities or are imbued with deep cultural symbolism. Notable examples include the Sword of Goujian, known for its exceptional sharpness and historical significance from the Spring and Autumn period, and the Nine-Dragon Sword, famed in various tales for its mystical properties. The heavenly spear of Lü Bu, a character from the "Romance of the Three Kingdoms," is another prominent legendary weapon, revered for its unmatched combat prowess. Such weapons often represent virtues like loyalty, bravery, and the quest for justice within Chinese cultural narratives, and they continue to inspire modern adaptations in films, literature, and video games.
Alison Ellwood is an Australian documentary filmmaker known for such films as Enron: The Smartest Guys in the Room, Laurel Canyon, and How to Change Your Mind.

Alison Ellwood is an American filmmaker and director known for her work in documentary filmmaking. She gained prominence for her direction of the acclaimed documentary "History of the Eagles," which chronicles the rise and legacy of the iconic rock band, the Eagles. Ellwood has also created several other notable documentaries that explore music and culture, including "LA 92," which examines the 1992 Los Angeles riots. Her work is characterized by its in-depth research and storytelling, bringing historical events and musical narratives to a wider audience. Ellwood's contributions to the field have earned her recognition within the documentary filmmaking community.

Rift Valley University (RVU) is the largest private university in Ethiopia, with campuses around the country. Known formerly as Rift Valley University, RVU was granted full-fledged university status in 2014 by Ministry of Education. Its headquarters are in Jimma, Addis Ababa.

Rift Valley University College (RVUC) is a private higher education institution located in Ethiopia. Established in 2000, it aims to provide quality education in various fields, including business, law, social sciences, and health sciences. RVUC is part of the larger Rift Valley University system, which encompasses several campuses across the country. The college is known for its commitment to academic excellence, research, and community service, offering undergraduate and postgraduate programs designed to meet the demands of the labor market. RVUC is accredited by the Ethiopian Ministry of Education and is recognized for its contributions to the region's educational landscape.

MonsterQuest (sometimes written as Monsterquest or Monster Quest) is an American television series that originally aired from October 31, 2007 to March 24, 2010 on the History Channel. Produced by Whitewolf Entertainment, the program deals with the search for various monsters of interest to the cryptozoology subculture and paranormal entities reportedly witnessed around the world. A spin-off show, MysteryQuest, which focuses on unsolved mysteries, premiered on September 16, 2009. The purpose of the show is best described by the narrator, Stan Bernard, in the introduction: Witnesses ("People" for Season 4) around the world report seeing monsters. Are they real or imaginary? Science searches for answers...on MonsterQuest. History channel revived the MonsterQuest series for special episodes beginning on August 28, 2020. These special episodes did not feature new investigations but instead each episode was a compilation of scenes from the previous four seasons.

MonsterQuest is a documentary television series that aired on the History Channel from 2007 to 2009. The show focused on the investigation of legendary creatures and cryptids, featuring segments that explored sightings, folklore, and scientific research related to these mythical beings. Each episode typically introduced a specific creature, such as Bigfoot or the Loch Ness Monster, and included interviews with witnesses, researchers, and experts in the field. MonsterQuest aimed to analyze the evidence for and against the existence of these creatures, often employing advanced technology and investigative techniques. The series garnered attention for its blend of scientific inquiry and popular myth, contributing to the cultural conversation surrounding cryptozoology.

This is a complete list of United States representatives during the 1st United States Congress listed by seniority. As a historical article, the districts and party affiliations listed reflect those during the 1st Congress (March 4, 1789 – March 3, 1791). Seats and party affiliations on similar lists for other congresses will be different for certain members.
topic: List of United States representatives in the 1st Congress

text: The 1st Congress of the United States convened from March 4, 1789, to March 4, 1791, under the Constitution of 1789. It was composed of the Senate and the House of Representatives, with the House initially consisting of 65 members, who were elected from various states to represent their interests. The list of United States representatives in the 1st Congress includes notable figures such as Frederick Muhlenberg, the first Speaker of the House, and representatives from each of the original thirteen states plus Vermont and Kentucky, which were admitted later. This Congress was crucial in establishing legislative procedures and addressing key issues of the new nation, including the Bill of Rights and the structure of the federal government.
topic: List of United States representatives in the 1st Congress

text: The American Quartet was a four-member vocal group that recorded for various companies in the United States between 1899 and 1925. The membership varied over the years, but the most famous line-up – John Bieling (first tenor), Billy Murray (second tenor), Steve Porter (baritone), and William F. Hooley (bass) – recorded for the Victor Talking Machine Company from 1909 to 1913. The same group of singers also recorded for Edison Records as the Premier Quartet (or Quartette), and for that and other labels as the Premier American Quartet. From 1912 to 1914 the quartet also recorded with countertenor Will Oakland as the Heidelberg Quintet.

The American Quartet is a prominent string quartet founded in 1948, known for its contributions to classical and contemporary chamber music. Comprising first violin, second violin, viola, and cello, the ensemble has been recognized for its innovative performances and has worked to promote American composers alongside the classical repertoire. The quartet became particularly influential through its recordings and performances, both nationally and internationally, contributing to the wider appreciation of chamber music in the United States. Members of the American Quartet have often engaged in educational outreach, enhancing the quartet's legacy by inspiring new generations of musicians.

Prince Alexis Obolensky (April 20, 1915 – February 8, 1986) was an aristocratic Russian-American socialite, real estate broker and celebrated backgammon player, dubbed the "Father of Modern Backgammon". A member of the house of Obolensky, part of the Russian nobility, his family descends from the ancient Rurik dynasty.

Alexis Obolensky (born December 17, 1994) is a Ukrainian-born American artist and designer, known for his work in contemporary visual art and graphic design. He gained recognition for his unique blend of traditional and modern techniques, often exploring themes of identity and cultural heritage. Obolensky has exhibited in various galleries and art shows across the United States and Europe, receiving acclaim for his innovative approach and thought-provoking installations. In addition to his artistic practice, he is also involved in community art projects and educational initiatives aimed at fostering creativity and cultural exchange.

Sergio Sarra (Pescara 1961) is an Italian artist and former basketball player. In 1985, at the age of 24, he retired from playing competitively in order to study at the School of Painting at the Academy of Fine Arts in Bologna, concluding in 1987. Sarra took part in the Biennial of Young Artists from Mediterranean Europe (Barcelona – 1987), Venice Biennale at the Corderie dell'Arsenale (1993) in Aperto '93, at the Italian Pavilion (2011) and in the Havana Biennial (2000). Sarra curated the group exhibition Conversione di Saulo at Palazzo Chigi Odescalchi (Rome – 2000) and exhibited at the Muzeul Naţional de Artă Contemporană (Bucharest – 2007) and the WAX Winkler Art Xperience (Budapest – 2007) in Altered States – Are you experienced?, group exhibition curated by Nicolas Bourriaud and Paolo Falcone, and at Baths of Diocletian (Rome – 2008) at Cose mai viste curated by Achille Bonito Oliva. In 2019, Sarra exhibited at 4th Festival del Paesaggio in Anacapri. Other group exhibitions at: Palazzo Rondanini alla Rotonda (Rome – 1989), Palazzo della Permanente (Milan – 1991) and Espace Pierre Cardin (Paris – 1992), 34th Spoleto Festival of 2Worlds (Fonti del Clitunno – 1991), Fondazione Orestiadi (Gibellina – 1992), Palazzo delle Esposizioni (Rome – 1992, 1995), Fondazione Volume! (Rome – 2000). Sarra has held solo exhibitions at public and private institutions including the Faculty of Architecture of University of Palermo (1998), the Micromuseum for Contemporary Art and Culture (Palermo – 2004), Circolo Filologico Milanese (Milan – 2008), the Conservatory of Santa Cecilia (Rome – 2013), the Ewha Womans University (Seoul – 2016), the Benedictine Abbey of Propezzano (Morro d'Oro – 2018), Mattatoio Museo d'Arte Contemporanea (Rome - 2019). From the beginning of his artistic career, Sarra worked almost exclusively with painting, drawing and sculpture. In 1997, the art critic and curator Lorenzo Benedetti wrote: [...] In Sarra ci troviamo di fronte ad una intensa sinteticità dal punto di vista del processo formale a vantaggio di una maggiore concentrazione al dato concettuale… L'animale, i paesaggi e i volti vengono stilizzati fino al limite del riconoscibile [...]([...] With Sarra we are faced with an intense conciseness from the perspective of the formal process in favour of a greater concentration on the conceptual factor… The animal, the landscapes and the faces are stylised to the limit of being recognisable [...])

Sergio Sarra is an Italian-American mathematician known for his contributions to the fields of algebraic geometry and number theory. He was born in Italy and later moved to the United States, where he has held positions at several prestigious universities. Sarra is recognized for his research on the interplay between algebraic varieties and arithmetic properties, as well as for his work on modular forms and their applications in number theory. Throughout his career, he has published numerous papers and has been involved in mentoring aspiring mathematicians.
Canning Suffern (1892–1978) was a British naturalist. He is best known for his ornithological work at Titchfield Haven in Hampshire.

Canning Suffern is a notable figure in American political history known for his role as a member of the United States House of Representatives. He served from 1853 to 1855, representing the state of New York as a member of the Democratic Party.
