Born on June 17, 1818, in the town of Suffern, New York, he was educated at the local schools and later engaged in various business ventures. Following his congressional term, Suffern continued to be active in his community and local politics. He passed away on January 15, 1899. His contributions are recognized within the context of 19th-century American politics, particularly during a time of significant national change.

The 2020–21 FIS Alpine Ski Continental Cup (AOC) was a season of the FIS Alpine Ski Continental Cup, a series of second-level alpine skiing competitions arranged by the International Ski Federation (FIS).

The 2020–21 FIS Alpine Ski Continental Cup was a series of alpine skiing competitions held during the 2020–2021 season, organized by the International Ski Federation (FIS). It served as a developmental circuit for emerging athletes, featuring multiple events across different disciplines, including slalom and giant slalom. The competition took place in various locations, primarily in Europe, and included both men’s and women’s categories. The season's results contributed to the overall standings, determining the athletes eligible for promotion to higher-level competitions, including the World Cup. The season was impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic, leading to adjustments in the schedule and safety protocols.

Paul Grigson is an Australian diplomat and public servant. He is currently the deputy secretary of Infrastructure, Transport Security and Customs Group and the deputy comptroller-general of customs. He has served as the Australian ambassador to Indonesia, Thailand and Myanmar.

Paul Grigson is an Australian biochemist and academic known for his contributions to the field of molecular biology and protein biochemistry. He has conducted extensive research on protein structure and function, particularly in relation to the mechanisms of enzyme activity and regulation. Grigson has published numerous scientific papers and has been involved in teaching and mentorship at various universities. His work has advanced the understanding of biochemical pathways and has implications in areas such as drug development and biotechnology.
Qarabagh is a town located in Afghanistan. It is the administrative center of Qarabagh District, Ghazni Province. Also a district in Kabul is named Qarabagh too.

Qarabagh is a district located in Ghazni Province, Afghanistan. It is primarily inhabited by ethnic Pashtuns and has been historically significant due to its strategic location along important trade routes. The district has faced substantial challenges, including conflict and instability, particularly during the ongoing Afghan conflict. Qarabagh comprises several villages and is characterized by its rugged terrain and agricultural economy, with residents engaging in farming and livestock rearing. The district has seen various developments in infrastructure and education but continues to contend with the impacts of war and political turmoil.

The Pyrohoshcha Dormition of the Mother of God Church (Ukrainian: Церква Успіння Богородиці Пирогощої, romanized: Tserkva Uspinnia Bohorodytsi Pyrohoshchoyi) or simply Pyrohoshcha Church (Ukrainian: Церква Пирогощі [ˈtsɛrkwɐ pɪroˈɦɔʃtʃi]) is an Orthodox church in Kyiv in the historical neighbourhood Podil. The original church was built in 1130s by the Mstyslav I the Great of Kyiv. It was the main church of Podil, and was a temporary cathedral of Kyiv Metropolitanate in the early 17 century. In 1613 the church was reconstructured in Renaissance style, and then in 18th–19th centuries was rebuilt in Ukrainian Baroque and Neoclassicism styles. In 1934, the church became the cathedral of Ukrainian Autocephalous Church when its center moved from Kharkiv to Kyiv together with the capital of Ukrainian SSR. But it was in that status less than a year, being destroyed in 1935 by the Soviet administration for the reason of "reconstructing the square". Then, for some time the church was largely forgotten by the time the research on its remains began in 1976. At that time the idea of rebuilding of the church appeared, but the project was completed only in 1997, providing the restoration in the hypothetic Medieval Rus' style, which was made in 1997–1998. However, the historical originality of the reconstruction is still in discussion. On the Easter of 1998, the rebuilt Pyrohoshcha was consecrated as a church of the Ukrainian Orthodox Church of the Kyivan Patriarchate. On 11 November 2012 Patriarch Filaret consecrated the church religious paintings.

The Pyrohoshcha Church, also known as the Church of the Pyrohoshcha, is an Eastern Orthodox church located in the historic center of Kyiv, Ukraine. Built in the early 18th century, it is renowned for its stunning Baroque architecture and intricate interior decorations, which include frescoes and iconostasis. The church is dedicated to the Holy Virgin and is notably associated with the Cossack period. Its name, "Pyrohoshcha," translates to "of the Burning," referencing a local legend. The church serves as a significant cultural and religious site, reflecting the rich heritage of Ukrainian Orthodox Christianity and attracting both worshippers and tourists alike.

After the 1969 classical / rock fusion Concerto for Group and Orchestra, Jon Lord was commissioned to write a follow-up. This was Gemini Suite, five long movements inspired by the members of Deep Purple, and performed live in September 1970 at the Royal Festival Hall with The Light Music Society Orchestra (the album of the concert was issued in 1993 as Gemini Suite Live). Jon Lord then recorded it in the studio as his first solo project in 1971, with the London Symphony Orchestra conducted by Malcolm Arnold and soloists drawn from the rock world. Gemini Suite was an important step for Lord and led to albums such as Windows (1974) and Sarabande (1976). The album was originally released in Europe and America in 1971 with different cover artwork. The 1973 US reissue featured a third version of the cover artwork. In 1983 the album was re-released on LP in Germany and four years later also for the first time on CD. The digital remastered version of the album premiered on CD in 2008.

Gemini Suite is a software application developed by Gemini Technologies, designed for use in the field of automation and control systems. It provides a comprehensive environment for designing, monitoring, and managing complex control processes in various industries such as manufacturing, pharmaceuticals, and energy. Featuring user-friendly interfaces and robust functionality, Gemini Suite supports a range of tasks including process simulation, data acquisition, and real-time system monitoring. The suite is recognized for its flexibility and scalability, allowing customization to meet specific operational needs. Gemini Suite integrates with various hardware platforms and communication protocols, making it suitable for diverse applications in industrial automation.

David "Aki" Collins is an American basketball coach. Collins currently serves as the director of recruiting for Overtime Elite. Collins is originally from Brooklyn and is a 1997 graduate of Clark Atlanta University. Collins spent time as an assistant coach at University of Colorado (1997-2000), Howard University (2000-2003), Marshall University (2003-2006), and Fairfield University (2006-2008) before accepting a position at Marquette under Buzz Williams. While at Marquette, Collins gained a reputation as a good recruiter, assisting the Golden Eagles to two top-25 ranked recruiting classes during his tenure. On May 27, 2012, Collins accepted an assistant coach position at the University of Memphis. In Collins' first few months with the program, he assisted the Tigers in securing the commitment of highly ranked frontcourt player Kuran Iverson.

Aki Collins is a fictional character primarily known from the young adult novel series "Dawn of the World" by author J.L. Hughes. Introduced as a central figure, Aki is depicted as a resourceful and determined teenager navigating a post-apocalyptic landscape ravaged by environmental disasters. The character's journey encompasses themes of survival, friendship, and self-discovery, capturing the struggles of youth in a transformed world. Aki's development throughout the series is marked by her resilience and leadership qualities, making her a relatable protagonist for readers exploring issues of climate change and societal collapse. The character has garnered positive reviews for her depth and relatability, contributing to the series' appeal among young adult audiences.

Edwin H. McConkey is an American biologist. As of 2004, he is a professor emeritus at the department for Molecular, Cellular, and Developmental Biology at the University of Colorado at Boulder, Colorado. His contributions to taxonomy include the original description the northern subspecies of mole skink, Plestiodon egregius similis.

Edwin H. McConkey (1874–1945) was an American architect known for his contributions to the design of commercial and residential buildings in the early 20th century. Based in New York, McConkey established a successful architectural practice, creating structures characterized by their innovative use of materials and attention to detail. His work is noted for blending architectural styles, particularly in the Beaux-Arts and Colonial Revival movements. McConkey was also involved in urban planning and served on various architectural boards, influencing the development of architecture in his region. His legacy includes numerous buildings that remain significant in the historical landscape of American architecture.

Marguerite Pearson (Tesseine) (September 6, 1932 – January 4, 2005) was a utility player who played in the All-American Girls Professional Baseball League between the 1948 and 1954 seasons. Listed at 5 ft 5 in (1.65 m), 125 lb., Pearson batted and threw right-handed. She was nicknamed ″Dolly". During her seven-year tenure in the AAGPBL, Dolly Pearson moved around for a while, playing for seven different clubs in seven different cities as the league shifted players as needed to help weak teams stay afloat. A versatile utility, she played all positions except catcher before becoming a regular shortstop. Pearson never had the opportunity to play for a pennant contender or a champion team. After her baseball career was over, Pearson made a name for herself promoting youth sports activities to provide a safe and family-oriented environment on the field, which gained her induction in several Halls of Fame. Born in the Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania neighborhood of Hazelwood, Pearson was the daughter of William and Retha (Hayes) Pearson. She was playing sandlot baseball with the boys and was gaining a reputation. And a AAGPBL scout caught wind, and signed Pearson at the age of 14 nearing 15, the youngest player ever to play in the league. Pearson entered the league in 1948 with the Muskegon Lassies, playing for them for one year before joining the Peoria Redwings (1949), Racine Belles (1950), Battle Creek Belles (1951), Kalamazoo Lassies (1951–1952), South Bend Blue Sox (1953) and Grand Rapids Chicks (1954). On the last day of her rookie season, Pearson celebrated her 16th birthday to everybody's surprise, because everyone thought she was already 16 (the minimum age according to the rules), so she had played illegally all year. In addition to Dolly, she also received one of the more unusual nicknames in the AAGPBL – "Buttons". Pearson received her curious nickname on a trip with the club, when she wanted to play with all the push-buttons in the train. Overall, Pearson appeared in 82 games and hit .182 with 15 runs and 12 RBI. In 1949, Pearson batted .216 with Peoria, playing only in 30 games at various positions. At Racine the next year, she started at center field and posted a .235 average with 41 runs and 47 RBI in a career-high 110 games. Then in 1951 she found herself on the move again, this time to Battle Creek, where she started at shortstop, the position she would play for the rest of her career. In addition, she was sent to Kalamazoo in the midseason, combining for a .190 average in 100 games for both teams. Pearson hit .185 in 44 games for Kalamazoo in 1952 and moved to South Bend in 1953. She appeared in 90 games for the Blue Sox, batting .185 with 29 RBI and 38 runs scored, being selected for the All-Star Team in 1953. Then she joined Grand Rapids in 1954, during what turned out to be the AAGPBL's final season. The league reduced the size of the ball 10 inches to major league size that year. As a result, Pearson started hitting the ball with authority and posted a .327 average w/272 at bats, 18 home runs, 10 stolen bases, a slugging percentage of .544, driving in 57 runs while scoring 68 times, setting career-marks. In 85 games, she committed 29 errors in 350 chances for a .917 fielding average. Once the league folded, Dolly settled in Grand Rapids, Michigan for 15 years before moving to Mount Pleasant, Michigan. She started playing slow-pitch softball, playing until the age of 65, where her club won 18 championships. She married Edward Tesseine in 1955. The couple raised four children: Retha, Sam, Ron and Ed, and had five grandchildren; Taylon, Kaedria (Sam), Stacy, Rodney and Russell (Retha). She worked in the Central Michigan University athletics department for several years, while helping her husband run the bar they owned at Mount Pleasant, E.J.'s Lounge, until 1981. Widowed in 1991, Pearson retired in 1994 and was involved in the Foster Grandparents Program. Children were always an integral part of her life, as she started the first T-ball program in Mt. Pleasant. She also trained and coached boys' baseball and girls' softball at various levels in the Michigan area, and coached senior girls' bowling for many years. She was the Grand Rapids, Michigan City Woman's singles' bowling Champion in 1968.

Marguerite Pearson (1896–1984) was an American painter and educator known for her contributions to the art community in the United States, particularly during the mid-20th century. Born in Iowa, she studied at the Art Institute of Chicago and continued her artistic education in Europe. Pearson's work spans various mediums, including oil painting, watercolor, and prints, with a focus on landscapes and still lifes. Throughout her career, she exhibited in numerous solo and group shows, receiving recognition for her expressive style and use of color. In addition to her art practice, Pearson was dedicated to teaching, influencing a generation of artists through her positions at various institutions. Her works are part of several public and private collections, reflecting her significant impact on American art.

The Shepard Energy Centre is a combined cycle power plant located on the east side of Calgary, Alberta. It is powered by two gas turbine generators, with two HRSGs capturing the waste heat from the exhaust-gases and producing steam for a single steam turbine. ENMAX claims the Shepard Energy Centre emits less than half of the carbon dioxide per MW compared to traditional coal power-plants.

The Shepard Energy Centre is a natural gas-fired power plant located in Calgary, Alberta, Canada. Operated by TransAlta Corporation, it was commissioned in 2014 and has a total generating capacity of approximately 880 megawatts. The facility utilizes combined-cycle technology, which increases efficiency by using both gas and steam turbines to generate electricity. The Shepard Energy Centre plays a significant role in providing reliable power to the Alberta electrical grid and incorporates various environmental measures to minimize its carbon footprint. It is one of the largest power plants in Alberta and represents a key part of TransAlta's transition toward more sustainable energy solutions.

Tundra is an unincorporated community in Van Zandt County, Texas, United States. According to the Handbook of Texas, the community had a population of 34 in 2000. It is located within the Dallas/Fort Worth Metroplex.

Tundra is an unincorporated community located in Willacy County, Texas, United States. Situated within the South Texas region, Tundra is characterized by its rural landscape and proximity to agricultural activities. Historically, the area developed alongside local farming and ranching industries, with limited infrastructure and population. As an unincorporated area, Tundra lacks a defined government structure and relies on the governance of Willacy County. The community is part of the larger region that is influenced by the subtropical climate typical of South Texas.

Jaroměř (Czech pronunciation: [ˈjaromɲɛr̝̊]; German: Jermer) is a town in Náchod District in the Hradec Králové Region of the Czech Republic. It has about 12,000 inhabitants. It is known for the Josefov Fortress. Josefov is well preserved and is protected as an urban monument reservation, the historic town centre of Jaroměř is procected as an urban monument zone.

Jaroměř is a town located in the Hradec Králové Region of the Czech Republic. Situated at the confluence of the Elbe and Metuje rivers, it has historical significance dating back to the 13th century when it was founded by the Czech king Přemysl Otakar II. Jaroměř is known for its well-preserved medieval architecture, including the Church of St. Jacob the Greater and the remains of a fortress. The town serves as a cultural and administrative center, with various local events and festivals celebrating its rich history. Additionally, Jaroměř is positioned near the significant historical site of Josefov, a former fortification built in the 18th century. Its economy is supported by services and small industries, contributing to the overall development of the region.

Chesterfield Highlands Historic District is a national historic district located at Colonial Heights, Virginia. The district encompasses 305 contributing buildings and 1 contributing structure. The district was developed in the early decades of the 20th century as a carefully planned middle-class neighborhood in a gridiron plan. The primarily residential district includes dwellings in a variety of popular late 19th and early 20th century architectural styles. Located in the district are the Highland Methodist Episcopal Church (1920), Immanuel Baptist Church (1932), and Colonial Heights Presbyterian Church (1950). It was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2013.

The Chesterfield Highlands Historic District is a designated historic district located in the city of Chesterfield, Missouri. It encompasses a collection of residential buildings that reflect the architectural styles and historical significance of the early to mid-20th century. The district features a variety of architectural designs, including Craftsman, Colonial Revival, and Mid-Century Modern styles, which highlight the evolution of suburban development in the area. Established to preserve the cultural heritage of Chesterfield, the district is listed on the National Register of Historic Places. It serves as a testament to the historical and aesthetic values of the community, promoting awareness of its unique architectural character and history.

Muhammad Zaid Alam (Urdu محمد زید عالم born 24 December 1999) is a Pakistani cricketer. He made his List A debut for Lahore Whites against National Bank in the 2018–19 Quaid-e-Azam One Day Cup on 13 September 2018. Prior to his List A debut, he was named in Pakistan's squad for the 2018 Under-19 Cricket World Cup. He made his Twenty20 debut for Lahore Blues in the 2018–19 National T20 Cup on 10 December 2018.

Muhammad Zaid is a prominent figure known for his contributions in a specific field, though details on his exact profession and notable achievements are not widely documented or available. He may be recognized in various contexts, including politics, academia, or arts, depending on regional significance. Information about his background, major works, and impact varies and may require further research for a comprehensive understanding.
Digamasellus australis is a species of mite in the family Digamasellidae.

Digamasellus australis is a species of mite belonging to the family Digamasellidae. It was first described in 1993 by researchers Joe M. P. de Moraes and T. L. De Lima. This species is primarily found in southern regions, with its habitat predominantly in soil ecosystems where it plays a role in the decomposition process and nutrient cycling. Digamasellus australis is characterized by its distinctive morphological features, which aid in its identification and classification within the broader group of soil-dwelling arthropods. The ecological significance of this mite, along with its contributions to soil health, underlines its importance in entomological and ecological studies.

The following article is a list of characters who have appeared in various media across the Action Man franchise, including the original Palitoy toyline, the subsequent Hasbro toy line, the Panini Comics series, various video games, and the two Action Man TV shows, from USA in 1995 and from Canada in 2000.

The "List of Action Man characters" details the various fictional characters associated with the Action Man toy line, originally launched by Hasbro in the 1960s. Action Man, a British counterpart to G.I. Joe, featured a range of characters that represented different military and adventure archetypes, including soldiers, pilots, and explorers. Over the decades, the brand has introduced a diverse lineup of characters, each with unique backstories, skills, and specialties. Notable figures include General Joe Colton, a military leader, and Action Man himself, who embodies an idealized soldier. The list also includes supporting characters and adversaries, contributing to the rich narrative and expanding the Action Man universe in various media, including television series, comic books, and animated adaptations.
Dişlibaşak is a neighbourhood in the municipality and district of Çınar, Diyarbakır Province in Turkey. Its population is 837 (2022).

Dişlibaşak is a village located within the Çınar district of Diyarbakır Province in Turkey. It is characterized by its rural lifestyle and is part of a region known for its agricultural activities. The village is home to a small population and features typical Anatolian architecture. The local economy primarily revolves around farming and livestock. Dişlibaşak, like many villages in the area, reflects the cultural heritage and traditions of the Kurdish and Turkish communities.

Hushai (hus'-sha-i) or Chusai (Hebrew: חוּשַׁי or חוּשַׁי הָאֲרְכִּי 'Hushai the Archite') was a friend of David and a spy according to the Hebrew Bible. During Absalom's rebellion, as described in the Second Book of Samuel, he agrees to act as an advisor to Absalom to sabotage his plans while secretly sending information to David. It was on his advice that Absalom did not immediately pursue the retreating David, thus giving David time to regroup and gather his forces. Hushai's sabotage helped to ensure Absalom's rapid defeat.

Hushai was a significant figure in the biblical narrative found in the Second Book of Samuel in the Hebrew Bible. He served as a loyal advisor to King David during the time of Absalom’s rebellion. Appointed as David's confidant, Hushai played a crucial role in countering the counsel of Ahithophel, Absalom's chief adviser. By strategically providing advice that appealed to Absalom’s ego, Hushai aimed to delay the pursuit of King David and gather time for him to escape. His loyalty and cunning ultimately contributed to David's restoration to the throne after Absalom's defeat. Hushai's actions are noted for their implications on the dynamics of power and loyalty within the biblical narrative.

Sreedharan Champad (1938 – 14 June 2024) was an Indian circus artist, circus historian and Malayalam-language writer from Kerala. He worked in a number of fields, including working as circus manager, flying trapeze artist, circus company public relations officer, auto driver, bill collector, journalist and writer. Champad wrote over 20 books including novels, more than 100 short stories, biographies and articles. His book An Album of Indian Big Tops chronicles the history of the circus industry in India from 1880 to 2010.

Sreedharan Champad, commonly known as Sreedharan, is an Indian civil engineer and a pivotal figure in the development of rapid transit systems in India. Born in Kerala, he is best recognized for his leadership in the construction of the Delhi Metro, which significantly improved urban transportation in the Indian capital. Champad's engineering career spans several decades, during which he has received numerous awards for his contributions to civil engineering and infrastructure development. His innovative approaches and emphasis on timely project delivery have made him a respected authority in the field. Champad is also known for his advocacy of sustainable transport solutions and has been involved in various infrastructure projects across India and abroad.

Atropanthe (Chinese name 天蓬子 tian peng zi) is a monotypic genus of flowering plants belonging to tribe Hyoscyameae of subfamily Solanoideae of the family Solanaceae. The single species, Atropanthe sinensis (Hemsl.) Pascher is native to the temperate forests of S. Central China (to Hunan). It is a herbaceous perennial (specifically a rhizomatous hemicryptophyte) which bears a marked similarity (particularly in regard to the form of the corolla) to the related genus Atropa - whence the genus name Atropanthe ( meaning ‘having a flower resembling that of Atropa’). Unlike Atropa, however (and in common with the other genera belonging to subtribe Hyoscyaminae of Hyoscyameae) Atropanthe bears a dry, pyxidial fruit, resembling a pot with a lid (Atropa, the sole member of subtribe Atropinae, bears, by contrast, a fruit taking the form of a juicy, glistening berry).

Atropanthe is a genus of flowering plants belonging to the family Solanaceae, commonly known as the nightshade family. It is characterized by its herbaceous perennial species that are primarily found in subtropical and tropical regions. Members of this genus are known for their distinctive flowers and fruits, often exhibiting a range of colors. Atropanthe species typically thrive in well-drained soils and require adequate sunlight for optimal growth. This genus has garnered interest for its potential medicinal properties, as some species contain alkaloids that may have therapeutic applications. However, certain members of the genus may also possess toxic qualities, necessitating caution in handling and consumption.

Holly Mae Muller (born 26 August 1997) is an English singer. She rose to prominence in 2021 after releasing the single "Better Days", a collaboration with Swedish music collective Neiked and American rapper Polo G, which went on to chart in the top 40 in both the United Kingdom and United States. She represented the United Kingdom in the Eurovision Song Contest 2023 in Liverpool, with top 10 hit, "I Wrote a Song". In the competition final, the song finished in 25th place with 24 points. She released her debut studio album Sorry I'm Late in September 2023.

Mae Muller is a British singer-songwriter known for her distinctive pop sound and candid lyrics. Born on August 26, 1997, in London, England, she gained prominence in the music industry with her debut EP, "After Hours," released in 2019. Muller's music often explores themes of love, heartbreak, and empowerment. She has collaborated with various artists and achieved chart success, particularly with her singles "Anticipation" and "Better Days." In 2023, Muller represented the United Kingdom in the Eurovision Song Contest with her song "I Wrote a Song." Her style blends elements of pop, R&B, and electronic music, earning her a dedicated fanbase and critical acclaim.
Jean-Albin Emile Régis (19 February 1912 – 12 December 1981) was a French sports shooter. He competed in the 100 m running deer event at the 1952 Summer Olympics.

Jean-Albin Régis (1798–1859) was a French painter and lithographer known for his contributions to the art movements of the 19th century. Born in La Rochelle, Régis specialized in portraits and historical scenes, often capturing the essence of contemporary social issues. His works were influenced by Romanticism, and he became associated with various art salons in Paris. Régis's lithographs gained popularity for their detailed representation and accessibility, bringing art to a broader audience. Throughout his career, he contributed to the aesthetic discourse of his time, aligning with notable figures in the French art scene. Despite a relatively limited oeuvre, his impact on lithography and portrait painting remains recognized in art history.

Escape Me Never is a 1947 American melodrama film directed by Peter Godfrey, and starring Errol Flynn, Ida Lupino, Eleanor Parker, and Gig Young. It is the second film adaptation (the first was in 1935) of the 1934 play Escape Me Never by Margaret Kennedy, which was based on her 1930 novel The Fool of the Family. That book was a continuation of her story of the fictional Sanger family of musical geniuses introduced in The Constant Nymph, but there is a disjunct among the books and the films: The Sanger brothers are never mentioned in the 1943 film of The Constant Nymph, and their names are changed in this picture.

Escape Me Never is a 1947 British romantic comedy film directed by Paul L. Stein and starring Margaret Lockwood, Michael denison, and Marius Goring. The film follows the story of a celebrated playwright who becomes entangled in a complex love triangle involving an aspiring actress. It explores themes of love, ambition, and artistry against the backdrop of the theater world. Escape Me Never is noted for its witty dialogue and engaging performances, particularly by Lockwood, and has maintained a cult following among fans of classic British cinema. The film was produced by Gainsborough Pictures and features a soundtrack by composer Benjamin Frankel.

Ait Boudaoud (Arabic: أيت بوداود, Ayt Bu-Dāwud; Tamazight: ⴰⵢⵜ ⴱⵓⴷⴰⵡⴷ) is a small rural commune in Zagora Province. The Ait Boudaoud commune is located within the Agdz district, 80 kilometers from the city of Zagora. It's home to a population of around 5,344 residents

Ait Boudaoud is a village located in the Ait Ziri commune of the Béjaïa Province in Algeria. Nestled in the Kabylie region, it is characterized by its mountainous terrain and Berber culture. The village is part of a region known for its rich history, traditional architecture, and agricultural practices. Ait Boudaoud serves as a hub for local community activities and cultural heritage, contributing to the preservation of Berber customs and lifestyle. The surrounding environment is typical of the Mediterranean climate, fostering diverse flora and fauna, making it significant for both ecological and cultural tourism.

The United States of America vs. Oswald Pohl, et al., commonly known as the Pohl trial, was the fourth of the twelve "subsequent Nuremberg trials" for war crimes and crimes against humanity after the end of World War II between 1947 and 1948. The accused were SS-Obergruppenführer Oswald Pohl and 17 other SS officers employed by the SS Main Economic and Administrative Office charged for their administration of, and active involvement in, the Nazi concentration camp system. The Pohl trial was held by United States authorities at the Palace of Justice in Nuremberg in the American occupation zone before US military courts, not before the International Military Tribunal. Four of the accused, including Pohl, were sentenced to death by hanging, three to life imprisonment, eight to prison sentences from 10 to 25 years, and three were acquitted. The judges in this case, heard before Military Tribunal II, were Robert M. Toms (presiding judge), Fitzroy Donald Phillips, Michael A. Musmanno, and John J. Speight as an alternate judge. The chief of counsel for the prosecution was Telford Taylor; James M. McHaney and Jack W. Robbins were the principal prosecutors. The indictment was presented on January 13, 1947; the trial began on April 8, and sentences were handed down on November 3, 1947. At the request of the judges, the court reconvened on July 14, 1948, to consider additional material presented by the defense. On August 11, 1948, the tribunal issued its final sentences, confirming most of its earlier sentences, but slightly reducing some of the prison sentences and changing the death sentence of Georg Lörner into a sentence of life imprisonment.

The Pohl trial was a series of proceedings held by the United States military against officials of the Nazi regime after World War II, specifically focused on the administration of the concentration camps and the operations of the SS. Conducted from December 1947 to April 1948 in Nuremberg, Germany, it was part of the Subsequent Nuremberg Trials, which pursued justice for war crimes and crimes against humanity. The trial was named after Ernst Pohl, a former SS leader, and included 18 defendants who were charged with various offenses including murder, enslavement, and participation in a criminal organization. The proceedings highlighted the role of the SS in the Holocaust and resulted in several convictions, including death sentences for key defendants. The Pohl trial contributed to the legal foundations addressing war crimes and set precedents for international law regarding human rights and accountability.

Campo de Mirra (Spanish: [ˈkampo ðe ˈmira]) or El Camp de Mirra (Valencian: [el kam(b) de ˈmira]), officially El Camp de Mirra / Campo de Mirra, is a municipality in the comarca of Alt Vinalopó in the Valencian Community, Spain.

Campo de Mirra is a municipality located in the province of Alicante, in the Valencian Community of Spain. It is situated in the interior region of the province, characterized by its mountainous terrain and rural landscape. The town has a small population and is known for its agricultural activities, primarily the cultivation of olives and almonds. Campo de Mirra is also notable for its historical architecture, including traditional Spanish styles, and its local festivals, which reflect the cultural heritage of the area. The municipality is part of the comarca of Alcoià and is surrounded by natural parks, making it a destination for outdoor enthusiasts.

"Days" (デイズ, Deizu) is the 42nd single by Japanese entertainer Akina Nakamori. Written by Nakamori and Tetsurō Oda, the single was released on April 30, 2003, by Universal Music Japan. It was also the lead single from her 21st studio album I Hope So.

"Days" is a single by Japanese singer Akina Nakamori, released on February 21, 1986. The song is part of her album "Cross My Palm" and showcases a blend of pop and rock influences characteristic of Nakamori's style during the mid-1980s. "Days" received significant acclaim, further solidifying Nakamori's status as a prominent figure in the J-pop genre. The track features emotive lyrics and a memorable melody, contributing to its popularity and enduring legacy in Japanese music culture.

The 2020 African U-17 Women's World Cup Qualifying Tournament was the 7th edition of the African U-17 Women's World Cup Qualifying Tournament, the biennial international youth football competition organised by the Confederation of African Football (CAF) to determine which women's under-17 national teams from Africa qualify for the FIFA U-17 Women's World Cup. Players born on or after 1 January 2003 were eligible to compete in the tournament. Three teams would have qualified from this tournament for the 2021 FIFA U-17 Women's World Cup (originally 2020 but postponed due to COVID-19 pandemic) in India as the CAF representatives. However, FIFA announced on 17 November 2020 that this edition of the World Cup would be cancelled. As a result, all remaining qualifying matches were cancelled.
topic: 2020 African U-17 Women's World Cup qualification

text: The 2020 African U-17 Women's World Cup qualification was the process through which national teams in Africa aimed to secure a spot in the inaugural FIFA U-17 Women’s World Cup, scheduled to take place in 2020 in Uruguay. The qualification matches involved several African nations competing in various rounds, culminating in a final tournament to determine the continent's representative. The qualification format included group stages followed by knockout rounds, with teams competing for a chance to showcase young female talent on an international stage. The process was marked by the participation of numerous teams, showcasing the growth and development of women's football in Africa. However, the FIFA U-17 Women’s World Cup was eventually canceled due to the COVID-19 pandemic, impacting the outcomes of the qualification rounds.
topic: 2020 African U-17 Women's World Cup qualification

text: Mike Moher (born March 26, 1962) is a Canadian former professional ice hockey right winger. He played junior hockey for the Kitchener Rangers with whom he won the Memorial Cup in 1982. He also won a gold medal with the Canadian junior team at the 1982 World Junior Ice Hockey Championships. Selected 106th overall by the New Jersey Devils in the 1982 NHL Entry Draft, Moher made his NHL debut February 27, 1983, and played nine games with the team in 1982–83, recording one assist. He spent the rest of the season with the Wichita Wind, and played one more season with the Maine Mariners before retiring in 1984 at the age of 22.

Mike Moher is a Canadian politician who served as a member of the Legislative Assembly of Prince Edward Island for the Liberal Party. Elected in the 2015 provincial election, he represented the district of Montague-Kilmuir. Moher has been involved in various community initiatives and has focused on issues related to economic development and social services during his tenure in office. Prior to his political career, he had experience in the business sector and community organization. Moher is known for his commitment to local governance and enhancing the quality of life for residents in his constituency.

The Bravo Territory (Spanish: Territorio del Bravo) was a federal territory of Mexico that existed between 1914 and 1917. It was created by the decree of 17 June 1914 issued by the Congress of the United Mexican States which amended articles 43 and 44 of the Constitution of 1857, with the districts of Galeana, Bravos, and a fraction of the district of Iturbide, from the state of Chihuahua; the capital was Ciudad Juárez. The decree also created the territories of Jiménez and Morelos. With the promulgation of a new constitution on 5 February 1917, the territory was reintegrated into the state of Chihuahua.

Bravo Territory is a historic region located in the southwestern United States, primarily associated with the development of various wildlife and environmental conservation efforts. Established in the early 20th century, it served as a crucial area for the preservation of diverse ecosystems and endangered species. The territory is characterized by its unique geographical features, which include mountains, rivers, and deserts, providing habitats for a wide array of flora and fauna. Over the decades, Bravo Territory has become a focal point for research in ecology and conservation biology, attracting scientists, educators, and tourists interested in its natural beauty and ecological significance. The region is overseen by various federal and state agencies focused on environmental protection and sustainable use of natural resources.

The former Land Register in Amsterdam is a well-known building situated on the Prins Hendrikkade in the center of Amsterdam, opposite Amsterdam Central Railway Station. The former land register building and the Lloyd Building on the Oostelijke Handelskade in Amsterdam serve as reminders of the Royal Dutch Lloyd in their name and details in both the interior and exterior. Opened in 1920, the building initially served as the headquarters of the Royal Dutch Lloyd shipping company. Later, it housed the communal land register before relocating, at which point the building was rented out for office space. Long-standing plans exist to convert the building into a 4-star hotel. The hotel would have approximately 100 guest rooms; the basement area designated for cultural and social activities. Some individuals oppose additional hotel space in central Amsterdam, arguing thatthere are enough hotels in the area. However, the hotel project remains on track for construction, as no other tenants could be found for the building.

The Land Register building in Amsterdam, known as the "Kadastergebouw," is a historical structure that serves as the headquarters for the Dutch Cadastre, responsible for land registration and property rights in the Netherlands. Completed in 1914 and designed by architect J. H. de Roos, the building reflects a blend of Neo-Renaissance and traditional architectural styles. It is characterized by its imposing façade, elaborate detailing, and symmetrical layout, making it a significant example of early 20th-century architecture in Amsterdam. The building's preservation as a registered monument highlights its cultural and historical importance within the city.
Jacob Joseph Pugsley (January 25, 1838 – February 5, 1920) was an American lawyer and politician who served two terms as a U.S. representative from Ohio from 1887 to 1891.

Jacob J. Pugsley (b. 1990) is a notable American entrepreneur and technology innovator known for his work in software development and artificial intelligence. He co-founded several successful tech startups, focusing on developing solutions that leverage machine learning and data analytics. Pugsley earned a degree in Computer Science from a prestigious university, where he conducted research on algorithm optimization. He has contributed to multiple publications in the tech field and is regarded as a thought leader in ethical AI practices. In addition to his business pursuits, he is an advocate for STEM education and frequently speaks at industry conferences.

Every Dog Has His Day is the third and final studio album by the American band Let's Active, released in 1988. The title track peaked at No. 17 on Billboard's Modern Rock Tracks chart. The band promoted the album by touring with Velvet Elvis.

"Every Dog Has His Day" is a popular idiom that suggests that everyone, regardless of their situation, will have a moment of success or recognition at some point in their life. The phrase implies that even those who may feel overlooked or undervalued will eventually experience a time when they are appreciated or able to shine. While the origins of the saying are uncertain, it has been widely adopted in English-speaking cultures and is often used in various contexts, from personal achievements to moments of good fortune. The expression is commonly utilized in literature, music, and popular media, reflecting the universal theme of hope and redemption.
This article details the Bradford Bulls rugby league football club's 2023 season. This is the Bulls' fifth consecutive season in the Championship.

The 2023 Bradford Bulls season is the club's 132nd in professional rugby league and their 27th consecutive season in the second tier of English rugby league, the Betfred Championship. Under the leadership of head coach Mark Dunning, the Bulls aimed to secure promotion to Super League following a challenging 2022 season. The team's home games are played at the Odsal Stadium, and they compete against various teams within the Championship. The season saw a mixture of strong performances and setbacks as the Bulls sought to improve their standings in the league, attracting attention for their playing style and key player contributions. The campaign also focuses on community engagement and initiatives to strengthen the club's ties with its supporters.

The Rosenstrasse protest was the only mass public demonstration by Germans in the Third Reich against the deportation of Jews. The protest on Rosenstraße ("Roses street") took place in Berlin during February and March 1943. This demonstration was initiated and sustained by the non-Jewish wives and relatives of Jewish men and Mischlinge, (those of mixed Jewish and so-called Aryan heritage). Their husbands had been targeted for deportation, based on the racial policy of Nazi Germany, and detained in the Jewish community house on Rosenstrasse. The protests, which occurred over the course of seven days, continued until the men being held were released by the Gestapo. The protest by the women of the Rosenstrasse led to the release of approximately 1,800 Berlin Jews.

The Rosenstraße protest was a significant demonstration that took place in Berlin, Germany, between February and March 1943 during World War II, where the Aryan wives of Jewish men organized a public protest against the deportation of their husbands. The protest occurred outside the Rosenstraße, a detention center where Jewish men were held by the Gestapo. The women, many of whom were married to non-Jewish German citizens, demanded the release of their spouses, challenging Nazi racial policies. The protest lasted several days and garnered increasing public support, ultimately resulting in the release of the detained men. It is often cited as one of the few instances of open resistance against the Nazi regime where non-Jewish people stood up for the rights of Jewish individuals. The event is remembered for its display of solidarity and is considered a poignant example of civil disobedience in Nazi Germany.
The 2015 European Junior Badminton Championships were held at the Regional Sport Centrum Hall in Lubin, Poland, between 26 March and 4 April 2015.

The 2015 European Junior Badminton Championships were held from April 3 to April 12, 2015, in Mulhouse, France. This biennial tournament featured competition in multiple categories, including boys' and girls' singles, doubles, and mixed doubles, for athletes aged 19 and under. The event attracted participants from various European nations and served as a platform for emerging badminton talents. Denmark won the most medals, securing both team and individual titles, solidifying its dominance in junior badminton on the continent. The championships highlighted the growing popularity of badminton in Europe and contributed to the development of young players.

"Calm Down" is a song by Australian rock band Killing Heidi, released as the second single from their third studio album, Killing Heidi (2004). Released as a single on 27 September 2004, the song reached number 23 on the Australian Singles Chart and is Killing Heidi's most recent top-40 hit as of 2026. The video was shot in the open spaces of Broken Hill, New South Wales.

"Calm Down" is a song by Australian rock band Killing Heidi, released in 2000 as part of their debut album, "Reflector." The track was written by band members Ella Hooper and Jesse Hooper, and features a mix of alternative rock and pop elements. It achieved commercial success, reaching the top of the Australian singles chart and earning acclaim for its catchy chorus and energetic sound. The song contributed to the band's rise in popularity during the early 2000s and has since been regarded as one of their signature tracks. "Calm Down" showcases themes of youth and emotional expression, reflecting the band's distinct style and musical influences.
František Tázler (1898 – May 1930) was a Czech wrestler. He competed at the 1920 and the 1924 Summer Olympics.

František Tázler (born 19 September 1942) is a Czech former professional football player and coach, known for his position as a forward. Throughout his career, he played for various clubs in Czechoslovakia, including his notable tenure at Sigma Olomouc. After retiring from professional play, Tázler transitioned into coaching, contributing to the development of young football talent in the region. He is recognized for his impact on Czech football during a period of significant change in the sport, both nationally and internationally.

Luciano do Valle Queirós (4 July 1947 – 19 April 2014) was a Brazilian sports commentator, television presenter, journalist and entrepreneur. He worked on several broadcasts on television—Rede Globo from 1971 to 1982, Rede Record from 1982 to 1983 and 2003 to 2006, and Rede Bandeirantes from 1983 to 2003 and 2006 to his death. He was born in Campinas, São Paulo. Luciano do Valle died on the afternoon of 19 April 2014 in Uberlândia, Minas Gerais, aged 66.

Luciano do Valle (1938–2018) was a prominent Brazilian sports commentator, television presenter, and journalist known for his extensive work in sports broadcasting, particularly in football and boxing. He gained widespread recognition for his passionate commentary style and was instrumental in popularizing sports on Brazilian television. Do Valle began his career in the 1960s and became a significant figure in Brazilian media, working for various networks, including Rede Globo and Bandeirantes. His contributions to sports journalism earned him several awards and he is remembered as a pivotal voice in Brazilian sports history. Do Valle passed away in April 2018, leaving behind a legacy of influence in sports media.

Tapan Sikdar (20 September 1944 – 2 June 2014) was a Union minister of state in the National Democratic Alliance government of India and a Bharatiya Janata Party politician. He was born on 20 September 1944 in Jessore. His father Dr. D. N. Sikdar was a physician. His mother's name was Bela Rani Sikdar. He was member of 12 and 13 Lok Sabha representing Dum Dum (Lok Sabha constituency) in West Bengal. Tapan Shikdar died on 2 June 2014 in Delhi, AIIMS due to respiratory problems.

Tapan Sikdar is an Indian politician and prominent member of the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP). Born on May 16, 1945, in West Bengal, he has served in various capacities within the party and government. Sikdar was a Member of Parliament representing the Dum Dum constituency in North 24 Parganas, West Bengal, from 1998 to 2004. During his political career, he has been involved in various initiatives and has focused on development issues in his region. He is known for his contributions to the party's growth in West Bengal and his advocacy for Hindutva values. Additionally, Sikdar has experience in local governance and has been active in various social and cultural organizations.

Mayet is a surname. Notable people with the surname include: Carl Mayet (1810–1868), German chess player Jean-François Mayet (born 1940), French politician Muhammed Mayet (born 1998), South African cricketer Valéry Mayet (1839–1909), French entomologist

Mayet is a surname of French origin, which is believed to derive from the Old French word "maiet," meaning "little May." The surname is relatively uncommon and may be associated with individuals of French heritage or those with ties to regions in France where the family name has historical roots. Notable bearers of the Mayet surname may include individuals in various fields such as the arts, sciences, or business, although specific notables are not widely recognized. The surname is part of a wider tradition of French surnames that often derive from geographical, occupational, or descriptive origins.
Pompiliu Constantinescu (May 17, 1901 – May 9, 1946) was a Romanian literary critic.

Pompiliu Constantinescu (1938–2021) was a renowned Romanian physicist and educator, recognized for his contributions to the fields of theoretical physics and applied mathematics. He served as a professor at the University of Bucharest, where he significantly influenced the academic landscape through his innovative research and teachings. Constantinescu published numerous papers and was involved in various scientific projects that advanced the understanding of complex physical systems. His work earned him respect in the scientific community, both in Romania and internationally. In addition to his research, he was dedicated to mentoring students and promoting science education.

There are Unicode typefaces which are open-source and designed to contain glyphs of all Unicode characters, or at least a broad selection of Unicode scripts. There are also numerous projects aimed at providing only a certain script, such as the Arabeyes Arabic font. The advantage of targeting only some scripts with a font was that certain Unicode characters should be rendered differently depending on which language they are used in, and that a font that only includes the characters a certain user needs will be much smaller in file size compared to one with many glyphs. Unicode fonts in modern formats such as OpenType can in theory cover multiple languages by including multiple glyphs per character, though very few actually cover more than one language's forms of the unified Han characters.

Open-source Unicode typefaces are fonts that are publicly available for use and modification under open-source licenses, supporting a wide range of Unicode characters, scripts, and languages. These typefaces facilitate accessibility, interoperability, and inclusivity in digital communication by ensuring that diverse linguistic representations can be displayed accurately. Examples include Google’s Noto fonts, which aim to cover all the scripts encoded in the Unicode standard, and other projects like DejaVu, Liberation Fonts, and Hack. The open-source nature allows designers and developers to collaborate, improve, and adapt typefaces for various applications, contributing to a broader cultural exchange and language preservation.

Syncletica of Alexandria (Ancient Greek: Συγκλητική, romanized: Synkletikḗ) was a Christian saint, ascetic, anchorite, and Desert Mother from Roman Egypt in the 4th century AD. She is the subject of The Life of Syncletica, a Greek hagiography purportedly by Athanasius of Alexandria (d. 373) but not published until 450; and the Alphabetical and Systematic Apophthegmata (probably compiled in the 6th century), which included 28 of her sayings and teachings. She died at the age of 80, after a three-year-long illness from mouth cancer. Girls and young women, despite Syncletica's resistance, were drawn to her and to her teachings, and chose to live near her in the desert. Her teachings and sayings were full of Biblical quotes, allusions, and metaphors, especially about sailing, the sea, domestic chores, and female tasks. Unlike male ascetics of the time, domesticity for Syncletica was connected with spirituality and "richly capable of expressing the ascetic's spiritual growth". Her teachings "promote spiritual life as a dynamic, embodied reality and they do this in a way that unusually appropriates women's bodies and women's work as useful means by which one might think about and speak of spiritual life".

Syncletica of Alexandria was a 4th-century Christian saint and one of the earliest known desert mothers, recognized for her ascetic life and teachings in the Egyptian desert. Born to a wealthy family, she chose a life of poverty and solitude, dedicating herself to prayer and spiritual contemplation. Syncletica is often regarded as a pioneer of female monasticism, emphasizing the importance of inner strength and the struggle against temptation in her teachings. Her sayings and life exemplify the virtues of humility, resilience, and faith, and she is venerated in both the Eastern Orthodox and Roman Catholic traditions. Her legacy continues to inspire the monastic community and those seeking spiritual guidance.

Bernard Pertuiset (1920-2000) was a French neurosurgeon, born in Paris on 28 October, 1920. He spent four years of internship in a service of neurology at La Pitié-Salpêtrière Hospital with T. Alajouanine. He got his medical diploma in 1949, writing a thesis on cranioplasty. He was an assistant of Dr Petit-Dutaillis in La Pitie Hospital neurosurgical service where he developed his career until retirement on 1 October 1990. In 1949-1950, he acted at the Montreal Neurological Institute as a research fellow in neurophysiology under the direction of H. Jasper, and also was allowed by W. Penfield to analyze the records of operated epileptic patients and involvement in a paper on bitemporal epilepsy. From 1949 to 1990, he wrote, as senior author, 356 papers on all aspects of neurosurgery. Moreover, he wrote: 4 books on epidermoids (1957), Cranio cerebral topography (1959), Acoustic neuromas (1970) and Ruptured saccular aneurysms (1987). Most of his clinical and fundamental research was devoted to cerebral vascular malformations. He delivered 3 international lectures: Olivecrona in Stockholm (1981), European in Warsaw (1986) and at Henrich Kluver in Chicago (1990). He developed the profound arterial hypotension technique to make open aneurysmal surgery in a safer way. He developed a neurosurgical service of 90 beds, with a staff of 2 Associate Professors and 3 neurosurgeons. Co-founders of Advances and Technical Standards in Neurosurgery.

Bernard Pertuiset (born 1944) is a French astronomer known for his contributions to the study of asteroids and comets. He gained prominence for his research in celestial mechanics and has been involved in significant projects related to space observation. Pertuiset's work has helped advance the understanding of near-Earth objects and their potential impact on Earth. In addition to his research, he has been active in public outreach and education concerning astronomy and space science. His contributions have been recognized within the scientific community, and he continues to participate in various international collaborations.

Carbon Design System is a free and open-source design system and library created by IBM, which implements the IBM Design Language, and licensed under Apache License 2.0. Its public development initially started on June 10, 2015. Their components have multiple implementations, which includes a vanilla JS and CSS implementation and React (maintained by the Carbon Core team), while the community maintains the frameworks developed in Svelte, Vue.js, and Web Components. The official typeface to be used according to the guidelines is the IBM Plex typeface, with alternative typefaces for CJK scripts are Noto Sans CJK SC, Noto Sans CJK TC, and Noto Sans JP.

The Carbon Design System is a comprehensive design framework developed by IBM to create cohesive user experiences across digital products. Launched in 2017, it provides a set of guidelines, components, and tools that emphasize accessibility, usability, and consistency in interface design. Carbon aims to streamline collaboration between designers and developers by offering a unified visual language and a library of reusable components suited for enterprise applications. The system includes resources such as typography, color palettes, icons, and responsive layouts, enabling teams to build applications that are both functional and visually appealing. Carbon also promotes best practices in design and development, supporting the rapid evolution of digital experiences in the enterprise context.

Dimitrie Drăghicescu (or Dumitru Drăghicescu) (4 May 1875 – 14 September 1945) was a Romanian politician, sociologist, diplomat, and writer. Dimitrie Drăghicescu was born on 4 May 1875 in the village of Zăvoieni, Vâlcea County, Romania. After finishing grammar school in his native village, he attended Carol I High School in Craiova and thereafter the Law Faculty of the University of Bucharest. In 1901, he left for Paris where he studied at the Collège de France with Emile Durkheim, Gabriel de Tarde, Henri Bergson, and Theodule Armand Ribot. He also attended the lectures organized by the Académie des Sciences Morales et Politiques. In 1903, he published Le problème du déterminisme social: Déterminisme biologique et déterminisme social, which argued for biological determinism. On 17 May 1904, he obtained his doctor's degree in Sociology. In 1905, he was appointed associate professor of Sociology at the University of Bucharest. During World War I, from 1916 to 1918 he lived in France where he was active in promoting the ideas of a national Romanian state. On 9–12 April 1918 he attended the "Congress of Nationalities" in Rome with Nicolae Lupu and Simion Mândrescu, as members of the group supporting the right of Romanians to a state within their national ethnic borders and demanding the recognition of Romania as a cobelligerent country. From 1934 to 1936, he was minister plenipotentiary in Mexico. He is said to have committed suicide on 14 September 1945.

Dimitrie Drăghicescu (1875–1949) was a Romanian philosopher, sociologist, and politician, notable for his contributions to the development of sociological theory in Romania. He is particularly recognized for his work in social psychology and group dynamics, emphasizing the role of social factors in shaping individual behavior. Drăghicescu's scholarship included studies on the relationship between society and individual identity, as well as the influence of social structures on human interactions. A prominent figure in the Romanian intellectual landscape, he also held various political positions and advocated for social reforms during a time of significant change in Romania. His legacy includes the promotion of sociological thought and the establishment of academic institutions focused on social sciences in the country.

O Mur Apunar Desh (Assamese pronunciation: [o mʊɹ apʊnaɹ dex]) is the state and traditional song of Assam, India. It was written by Lakshminath Bezbarua and the tune was made by Kamala Prasad Agarwala. It was first published in 1909 in an Assamese magazine named Bahi ("flute"). It was adopted as the Assam's state song in 1927 at asom chatrô sônmilôn ("Assam Student Conference") held in Tezpur, and was officially adopted by the state government on 15 December 2013. This song is also used as the anthem of the secessionist, United Liberation Front of Asom, as an anthem for a sovereign Assam. The song was translated to Mising by Tabu Taid.

"O Mur Apunar Desh" is a renowned Assamese patriotic song composed by the celebrated poet greater than (Bishnu Prasad Rabha) in the early 20th century. The title translates to "Oh, My Land" in English, and the song expresses deep affection for Assam, highlighting its beauty, culture, and heritage. It reflects themes of pride, longing, and a call for unity among the Assamese people. The song has become an emblem of Assamese identity and is often sung during cultural events, social gatherings, and political movements, encapsulating the spirit of Assamese nationalism. Its music and lyrics have left a lasting impact on the cultural landscape of Assam, resonating with sentiments of love for the homeland.

Minden City is a village in Sanilac County of the U.S. state of Michigan. The population was 165 at the 2020 census. The village is located within Minden Township. It has possessed a post office since 1862 (named Minden until 1883).

Minden City is a village in Sanilac County, Michigan, United States. As of the 2020 census, it has a population of 199 residents. Minden City was incorporated as a village in 1907, initially serving as a lumber town due to its access to timber resources. The village is located within both Minden Township and the eastern part of Sanilac County, approximately 18 miles southwest of the city of Bad Axe. The local economy is primarily based on agriculture and small businesses, with a rural character and community-focused atmosphere. Minden City is served by Minden Township's public services and is known for its close-knit community and local events.
Officer Down is the debut novel by crime writer Theresa Schwegel. It was published in 2005 by Minotaur Books and won the Edgar Award for Best First Novel.

"Officer Down" is a novel by Theresa Schwegel, published in 2007. The story revolves around a police officer who is shot and the subsequent investigation into the incident. The narrative delves into the personal and professional challenges faced by law enforcement, exploring themes of loyalty, trauma, and the complexities of police work. The novel received critical acclaim for its authentic representation of police life and character development, contributing to Schwegel's reputation as a notable author in the crime fiction genre. "Officer Down" is part of a broader trend in contemporary literature that examines societal issues through the lens of law enforcement.

Cornelis Nicolaas Hin (6 October 1869 – 21 October 1944) was a sailor from the Netherlands, who represented his native country at the 1920 Summer Olympics in Ostend, Belgium. During the second race one of the marks was drifting and the race was abandoned. Since the organizers did not have the time to re-sail the race that week the two remaining races were rescheduled for September 3 of that year. Since both contenders were Dutch, the organizers requested the Dutch Olympic Committee to organize the race in The Netherlands. With his son Johan Hin as crew Hin won the first race. His son Frans Hin crewed the remaining races in The Netherlands on the Buiten IJ, in front of Durgerdam near Amsterdam. Hin took the gold over the combined series with his boat Beatrijs III.

Cornelis Hin (c. 1620-1670) was a Dutch painter known for his still lifes and genre scenes. Born in Amsterdam, he became associated with the prominent artistic community of the Dutch Golden Age. Hin's works often depicted intricate arrangements of everyday objects, employing a meticulous attention to detail and a rich color palette. Although not as widely recognized as some of his contemporaries, his contributions to the genre of still life painting have been noted for their technical skill and thematic depth. Hin's art reflects the cultural and economic vibrancy of 17th-century Dutch society. His works can be found in various museums and private collections, illustrating the enduring legacy of this period in art history.

Two ships of the United States Navy have borne the name USS Strong, in honor of Rear Admiral James H. Strong (1814–1882), who distinguished himself at the Battle of Mobile Bay. The first, USS Strong (DD-467), was a Fletcher-class destroyer, launched in 1941 and sunk in action in 1943. The second, USS Strong (DD-758), was an Allen M. Sumner-class destroyer, launched in 1944 and struck in 1973.

USS Strong (DD-758) was a Gearing-class destroyer of the United States Navy, named after Rear Admiral Herbert Strong. Laid down on 24 April 1944 at the Federal Shipbuilding and Dry Dock Company in Kearny, New Jersey, she was launched on 28 January 1945 and commissioned on 12 May 1945. Strong served during World War II, participating in operations in the Pacific Theater, and continued with peacetime service through the late 20th century. After being decommissioned on 1 December 1970, she was struck from the Naval Vessel Register on 1 December 1976 and later sold for scrap. Strong earned multiple battle stars for her service, reflecting her active role during significant naval engagements.

The 1990–91 Xavier Musketeers men's basketball team represented Xavier University from Cincinnati, Ohio in the 1990–91 season. Led by head coach Pete Gillen, the Musketeers finished with a 22–10 record (11–3 MCC), won the MCC regular season and conference tournament titles, and received an automatic bid to the NCAA tournament as the #14 seed in the Midwest region. In the NCAA tournament, the Musketeers defeated #3 seed Nebraska, then lost to #11 seed Connecticut in the second round.

The 1990–91 Xavier Musketeers men's basketball team represented Xavier University during the 1990–91 NCAA Division I men's basketball season. Under the leadership of head coach Pete Gillen, the team achieved a strong season with a record of 24 wins and 8 losses. The Musketeers finished third in the Midwestern Collegiate Conference (MCC) and earned a bid to the NCAA Tournament, where they advanced to the Round of 32 before being eliminated. This season solidified the Musketeers' position as a competitive program in college basketball and laid the groundwork for future successes. Key players included All-MCC selections who contributed significantly to the team's performance.

Art's Famous Chili Dogs was a hot dog stand located in Los Angeles, California. It was founded in 1939 by Art Elkind, a frankfurter entrepreneur, who was its owner until 1990. It was well known for its chili dogs, which have been praised by a variety of Los Angeleno media outlets, and proclaimed by aficionados as the finest in the city.

Art's Famous Chili Dog Stand is a popular fast-food establishment known for its signature chili dogs, located in various cities across the United States. Tharindu Gamage (born 30 May 1996) is a Sri Lankan cricketer. He made his Twenty20 debut for Nugegoda Sports and Welfare Club in the 2019–20 SLC Twenty20 Tournament on 14 January 2020. He made his List A debut on 3 April 2021, for Kandy Customs Cricket Club in the 2020–21 Major Clubs Limited Over Tournament.

Tharindu Gamage is a Sri Lankan cricketer known for his all-round capabilities, primarily as a right-handed batsman and right-arm medium-fast bowler. The 1989 USSR Federation Cup was the fourth edition of the USSR Federation Cup and was officially known as Cup of the USSR Football Union. It took place between 26 February to 5 November. Its final was played at Meteor Stadium in Dnepropetrovsk.

The 1989 USSR Federation Cup was the 28th edition of the annual football cup competition in the Soviet Union. It featured teams from across the republics, competing in a knockout format. The tournament began in the spring of 1989 and concluded in late fall of the same year. The final match was held on November 1, 1989, where Torpedo Moscow emerged as the champions, defeating Dynamo Moscow. This edition was notable for the increasing visibility of football as a major sport in the USSR, amidst the political and social changes occurring during the period of Perestroika. The tournament also reflected the growing competitiveness of various clubs across the federation.

Alan G. Spoon (born June 4, 1951) is a business executive who left the Washington Post Company after 18 years, his final position being president and chief operating officer, to become a general partner at Polaris Ventures. Spoon served as Chief Operating Officer and a director of The Washington Post Company from March 1991 through May 2000 and President from September 1993 until May 2000. At Polaris, he was General Partner and Partner Emeritus from 2011 to 2018 and Managing General Partner from 2000 to 2010. He became a director of IAC in February 2003. Spoon graduated from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology in 1973.

Alan Spoon is an American businessman and venture capitalist known for his work in the media and technology sectors. He served as the president of The Washington Post Company from 1991 to 1997, where he played a pivotal role in the company's transition to digital media. After leaving The Washington Post, Spoon became a partner at the investment firm Graham Holdings Company and has also been involved in numerous startup ventures as an investor and advisor. He is recognized for his expertise in media innovation and entrepreneurship, and has served on various boards in both non-profit and private sectors.

Tapulous, Inc. was an American software and video game developer and publisher headquartered in Palo Alto, California. It was a wholly owned subsidiary of The Walt Disney Company as part of Disney Interactive's Disney Mobile unit. The company's most profitable products were the Tap Tap series of music games, which surpassed thirty-five million downloads. Tapulous was founded in February 2008 with the specific intention of developing software applications for the iOS platform. The company's Tap Tap Revenge gained at least one million players in less than one month of availability, and was the most-downloaded free game of the App Store in 2008. Building on the success of the game, Tapulous then heavily focused on the Tap Tap Revenge series by expansion and partnership with Universal Records. As of December 2009, the company's sales totaled nearly $1 million per month. In July 2010, the company was acquired by The Walt Disney Company. On January 9, 2014, the company shut down and removed all its remaining games from the App Store.

Tapulous was an American mobile application development company best known for creating the popular music game series "Tap Tap Revenge" for iOS devices. Founded in 2008 by Tapulous developers, including co-founders Ilan Klages and Andrew Karam, the company gained significant attention for its innovative use of licensed music tracks and engaging gameplay mechanics that incorporated rhythm and timing. "Tap Tap Revenge" became one of the best-selling apps on the iPhone, contributing to the growth of the music game genre. Tapulous was acquired by Disney in 2010, after which it operated as part of Disney Interactive. The company eventually discontinued its major products, and many of its titles were removed from app stores.

Galeazzo da Sanseverino (French: Galéas de Saint-Séverin'), known as the son of Fortuna, (c. 1460 – 24 February 1525) was an Italian-French condottiere and Grand Écuyer de France; Marquis of Bobbio, Count of Caiazzo, Castel San Giovanni, Val Tidone and Voghera. He was first the favorite of Ludovico il Moro and Beatrice d'Este, then of Louis XII and Francis I of France, as well as a sworn enemy of Gian Giacomo Trivulzio. On the other hand the Duke of Milan called and gave the general cane to Maria Galeazo, and captain did it of his people on the saddle, who riding then from hand to hand, with the banner in the wind of the snake, honor and glory of Lombardy, with many great gentlemen in company.

Galeazzo Sanseverino (circa 1430 – 1495) was an Italian military leader and nobleman, best known for his role as a condottiero (a leader of mercenary soldiers) in the service of various Italian states during the late 15th century. Born into the powerful Sanseverino family in Lombardy, he became prominent under the Duke of Milan, Francesco Sforza, and later served Sforza's son, Ludovico il Moro. Sanseverino played a key role in several military campaigns, including those against the Venetians and in the conflicts surrounding the Italian Wars. His career was marked by both successes and defeats, and he was eventually captured and exiled, spending his final years in foreign service. Sanseverino's legacy is reflective of the tumultuous political landscape of Renaissance Italy, characterized by shifting allegiances and the rise of military leadership.

The Beijing–Zhangjiakou Railway or Jingzhang Railway (Chinese: 京张铁路; pinyin: Jīngzhāng Tiělù), also known as the Imperial Peking–Kalgan Railway, is China's first railway that has been designed and built solely by Chinese, situated in the nation's capital Beijing and Zhangjiakou City in Hebei Province. It was built in 1905–1909 under the proposal of Viceroy of Zhili Yuan Shikai and Assistant Director-General of Railways Hu Yufen (胡燏棻), with Zhan Tianyou as engineer-in-chief. When building the railway, Zhan reduced the length of Badaling Tunnel by using a switchback—or “人”-shaped rail track called in China as the shape of it resembles the Chinese character “人” (pinyin: rén; literally: people); he also used vertical shafts to facilitate the excavation of the tunnel. The railway formally opened in 1909, with a total length of 201.2 km (125.0 mi). Starting from Liucun Village in Fengtai, it connected Beijing to Zhangjiakou via the Guan'gou Valley with 14 stations, 4 tunnels and 125 bridges. In 1912, four stations with passing loops were built in the Guan'gou section. The railway was merged into Beijing-Suiyuan Railway in 1916, and later into Beijing-Baotou Railway after the founding of People's Republic of China (PRC). The rail tracks located within the city area of Beijing were gradually dismantled to meet the need of urban traffic. In 1995, the former Xizhimen Station was designated as a Major Historical and Cultural Site of Beijing, and in 2013, the section between Nankou and Badaling was listed as Major Historical and Cultural Site Protected at the National Level.

The Beijing–Zhangjiakou Railway is a major railway line in northern China, connecting the capital city of Beijing with Zhangjiakou, a city in Hebei province. Completed in 1909, the original line was significant for its role in facilitating transportation and trade in the region. The railway has since undergone several upgrades, particularly to accommodate high-speed trains, with the modernized Beijing–Zhangjiakou High-Speed Railway opening in December 2019 in preparation for the 2022 Winter Olympics. The high-speed line drastically reduces travel time between the two cities to approximately 50 minutes, enhancing connectivity and promoting tourism in the area. The railway is a vital part of China's broader transportation network and plays an essential role in the economic development of Zhangjiakou as a winter sports destination.

Janez Stanič (4 January 1937 – 28 October 1996) was a Slovene journalist and translator. He was considered one of the best socio-political analysts of his generation and was often outspoken and critical of the Soviet regime. Stanič was born in Ljubljana in 1937. He studied Slovene and Russian at the University of Ljubljana and graduated in 1961. He worked for the newspaper Delo, first as their Moscow correspondent and later as an editor. From 1975 he worked at the Slovene National Broadcaster and from 1991 as head of the Cankarjeva Založba publishing house. He died in Ljubljana in 1996. He won the Levstik Award in 1968 for his book Onkraj Kremlja (The Other Side of the Kremlin).

Janez Stanič is a Slovenian politician and member of the Social Democrats (SD) party. He has served in various capacities within local and national government, promoting social democratic ideals and policies. Stanič's work has focused on issues such as economic development, social welfare, and environmental sustainability. He has been an advocate for democratic governance and civic engagement in Slovenia, contributing to debates on key national issues. His political career reflects a commitment to progressive change and enhancing the quality of life for citizens in Slovenia.
Greensboro Day School is a private, day, college preparatory school located in Greensboro, North Carolina, United States. It enrolls students from age 2 through grade 12.

Greensboro Day School (GDS) is a private, coeducational college preparatory school located in Greensboro, North Carolina. The school emphasizes academic excellence, character development, and community engagement, with a diverse curriculum that includes advanced placement courses, arts, and athletics. GDS is a member of several educational associations, including the National Association of Independent Schools (NAIS) and the Southern Association of Independent Schools (SAIS).
