The Mexican National Heavyweight Championship (called the Campeonato Nacional Completo in Spanish) is a Mexican lucha libre (professional wrestling) championship created and sanctioned by the Mexico City Boxing and Wrestling Commission (Spanish: Comisión de Box y Lucha Libre México D.F.). While the Commission sanctions the title, it does not promote the events in which the Championship is defended. From 1933 until the mid-1990s, Consejo Mundial de Lucha Libre (CMLL) controlled the Championship, since then AAA gained control of the championship, after the Commission granted them control of the championship. In 2006 the championship was abandoned and replaced by the AAA Mega Championship. In 2009 the championship became active again on the Mexican Independent circuit until 2013. CMLL brought the championship back in 2017. Since the championship is designated as a heavyweight title, the championship can only officially be competed for by wrestlers weighing at least 105 kg (231 lb). However, the regulation is not strictly adhered to.

The Mexican National Heavyweight Championship is a professional wrestling championship in Mexico, established by the Mexican professional wrestling promotion, the Mexican Wrestling Association (Empresa Mexicana de Lucha Libre, EMLL), in 1933. It is one of the oldest heavyweight titles in Mexican wrestling and has a rich history, featuring many renowned luchadores. The championship is contested under traditional lucha libre rules, and its titleholders must be recognized by the local wrestling promotion. The championship has undergone several changes in ownership and management over the years but remains a prestigious title in the Mexican wrestling landscape. Notable wrestlers who have held the title include El Santo, Blue Demon, and Casas.

Wind power generates less than 1% of Armenia's electricity annually, as there were only four wind farms in 2023 and less than 10 MW is installed. According to a study sponsored by the United States Department of Energy (DOE) and the United States Agency for International Development (USAID) in 2002–2003, the theoretical wind power potential of Armenia is 4,900 MWe in four zones with a total area of 979 km2. According to this research, the wind energy potential in Armenia is close to 450 MW. The most promising areas for wind power plants are Zod pass, Bazum Mountain, Jajur pass, the territory of Geghama Mountains, Sevan Pass, Aparan, the highlands between Sisian and Goris, and the region of Meghri. A reassessment by the Asian Development Bank was expected to be completed by 2025.

Wind power in Armenia is an emerging sector of the country's renewable energy landscape. Armenia has significant potential for wind energy, particularly in regions such as the Ararat Valley and the Vardenis mountains, where wind speeds are favorable. As of 2023, wind energy contributes a small yet growing share of Armenia's overall electricity generation. The government has recognized the importance of diversifying its energy sources and reducing dependence on imported fossil fuels. Efforts to develop wind energy in Armenia include investments in wind farm projects and supportive legislative frameworks aimed at attracting foreign investment. Renewable energy initiatives, including wind power, are crucial to Armenia's energy independence, environmental sustainability, and compliance with international climate commitments.

Lysimachia vulgaris, the yellow loosestrife or garden loosestrife, is a species of herbaceous perennial flowering plant in the family Primulaceae. It was transferred to Myrsinoideae based on results of molecular phylogenetic research before being merged into the Primulaceae.

Lysimachia vulgaris, commonly known as loose strife, is a flowering plant in the family Primulaceae. It is native to Europe, Asia, and northern Africa, and often found in wetlands, along riverbanks, and in meadows. The plant is characterized by its tall stems, opposite leaves, and clusters of bright yellow flowers that bloom from late spring to early summer. Lysimachia vulgaris is known for its ornamental value but can become invasive in certain regions, leading to ecological concerns. Traditionally, it has been used in herbal medicine for its purported anti-inflammatory and diuretic properties.

Nagoya Daigaku Station (名古屋大学駅, Nagoya Daigaku-eki, lit. "Nagoya University") is a railway station in Chikusa-ku, Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, Japan. It was opened on 13 December 2003 (2003-12-13). This station provides access to the Higashiyama campus of Nagoya University, which is the station's namesake, and the Nagoya campus of Nanzan University.

Nagoya Daigaku Station (名古屋大学駅) is a railway station located in Chikusa-ku, Nagoya, Japan. The station primarily serves the Meitetsu Seto Line, connecting users to various destinations in the Nagoya metropolitan area. text: Defend International (commonly known as DI) is a non-governmental organization focused on promoting and protecting human rights in the Middle East and North Africa. DI was founded in 2007 in Norway.

Homeland defense (HD) is the protection of a territory, sovereignty, domestic population, and critical infrastructure against external threats and aggression. (Definition will be incorporated into JP 3-26 upon its approval). Homeland security (HS) is a concerted national effort to prevent terrorist attacks, reduce a nation's vulnerability to terrorism, and minimize the damage and recover from attacks that do occur.

Homeland defense refers to the security measures and strategies implemented by a nation to protect its territory, sovereignty, population, and critical infrastructure from external threats, including military attacks, terrorism, and cyber threats. Johannes Floors (born 8 February 1995) is a German para-athlete. A bilateral lower limb amputee, Floors competes in sprint events, competing in the T43 classification.

Yotam Siachobe Muleya (1940 – 23 November 1959) was a long-distance runner who represented Northern Rhodesia (now Zambia) and the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland. Gayana is a biannual peer-reviewed scientific journal published by the University of Concepción. El Bosque (Spanish for "the forest") is a commune of Chile located in Santiago Province, Santiago Metropolitan Region. The commune spans an area of 14.1 km2 (5 sq mi).

El Bosque is a commune and city located in the Santiago Metropolitan Region of Chile. Asgard's Wrath is a 2019 action role-playing game developed by Sanzaru Games and published by Oculus Studios for the Oculus Rift, a virtual reality headset. oFono is a free software project for mobile telephony (GSM/UMTS) applications. It is built on 3GPP standards and uses a high-level D-Bus API for use by telephony applications. oFono is free software released under the terms of the GNU General Public License v2.

oFono is an open-source telephony stack developed for Linux-based operating systems, designed to provide telephony services for devices such as smartphones, tablets, and embedded systems. It was initiated by Nokia and is primarily aimed at enabling voice and data communication over GSM, UMTS, and LTE networks, along with VoIP capabilities. OFono offers a modular architecture that allows developers to implement various telephony protocols and services, catering to the needs of mobile device manufacturers and software developers. As part of the larger Linux telecommunications ecosystem, oFono integrates with other technologies and libraries to facilitate advanced features such as call management, SMS handling, and contact synchronization.

Geldolph Adriaan "Dolf" Kessler (2 April 1884 – 21 August 1945) was a Dutch footballer and industrialist. Kessler – along with brother Boeli and cousins Tonny and Dé – played club football for amateur side HVV Den Haag. Kessler also won three caps for the Netherlands national side between 1905 and 1906.

Dolf Kessler (born 24 December 1942) is a Dutch former professional footballer known for his career as a winger. He began his career with Ajax Amsterdam, where he developed a reputation for his speed and technical skills. Kessler went on to play for several clubs in the Netherlands, including Helmond Sport and FC Groningen, as well as a stint in the lower leagues. He made a significant impact in the Eredivisie during the 1960s and 1970s, contributing to his teams’ offensive play. After retiring from professional football, Kessler transitioned into coaching and sports administration, continuing his involvement in the football community. His legacy is marked by his contributions to Dutch football as both a player and later a mentor.
Walter Posadowski (born c. 1937) is a former Canadian Football League (CFL) coach who served as the head coach of the Saskatchewan Roughriders during the 1978 season.

Walt Posadowski is an American television actor and producer, best known for his work in the entertainment industry during the late 20th century. He gained prominence for his roles in various television series and films, contributing to the media landscape with his distinctive performances. In addition to his acting career, Posadowski has also been involved in production, showcasing his versatility within the industry. His contributions have made him a recognized figure among television audiences, although detailed biographical information and notable accolades remain limited in public records.

Guánica Light (Spanish: Faro de Guánica) was a historic lighthouse located in the municipality of Guánica, Puerto Rico in the Guánica State Forest. It was first lit in 1893 and deactivated in 1950. The light marked the entrance to Guánica Bay and bridged the gap between Los Morrillos Light and Caja de Muertos Light. On July 25, 1898, lighthouse keeper Robustiano Rivera spotted the convoy of the American forces that initiated the landing offensive of the Puerto Rican Campaign during the Spanish–American War. He immediately gave the alert to the residents of Guánica and marched to Yauco where he broke the news of the invading forces to the city's mayor. By 2009, the lighthouse was practically in ruins, though some parts of its unique architectural elements were still visible. It was listed in the U.S. National Register of Historic Places in 1981, and on the Puerto Rico Register of Historic Sites and Zones in 2000. A series of earthquakes that occurred in January 2020 in Puerto Rico further damaged the Guánica lighthouse.

Guánica Light is a historic lighthouse located on the southwestern coast of Puerto Rico in the municipality of Guánica. Constructed in 1893, it was designed by engineer Juan A. H. de la Torre. The lighthouse features a cylindrical masonry tower painted with horizontal white and red stripes and stands 68 feet tall. It was originally equipped with a fourth-order Fresnel lens, which aided navigation for maritime vessels in the Caribbean Sea. The Guánica Light was automated in 1964 and is currently maintained by the U.S. Coast Guard. It is recognized as a historic site and is part of the Guánica Lighthouse Complex, which reflects the region's maritime heritage. The lighthouse remains an important landmark and is accessible to visitors, offering scenic views of the coastline.

Erasmo Stefano of Narni (1370 – 16 January 1443), better known by his nickname of Gattamelata (meaning "Honeyed Cat"), was an Italian condottiero of the Renaissance. He was born in Narni, and served a number of Italian city-states: he began with Braccio da Montone, served the Papal States and Florence, as well as the Republic of Venice in 1434 in the battles with the Visconti of Milan. He was the subject of Donatello's equestrian bronze sculpture in the main square of Padua, the same city over which he became podestà in 1437. In Narni, the farmhouse in which Gattamelata was born bears a plaque reading "Narnia me genuit Gattamelata fui" ("I was born in Narni, I was Gattamelata").

Erasmo of Narni (c. 1450–1530) was an Italian military leader and mercenary known for his significant role in the Renaissance period, particularly in the context of Italian city-states' constant conflicts. He was born in Narni, a town in the Umbria region, and earned the nickname "Erasmo" during his military career. Often associated with the condottieri, private military leaders who commanded mercenary troops, Erasmo served various Italian states, including the Papal States and the Republic of Florence. His career was marked by both military successes and political intrigues, reflecting the turbulent nature of Italy during his lifetime. He is also noted for his contributions to the development of military tactics and fortifications in the period. Erasmo's legacy is indicative of the complexities of loyalty and power in Renaissance Italy.
Maleševci (Serbian Cyrillic: Малешевци) is a village in the Municipality of Bosansko Grahovo in Canton 10 of the Federation of Bosnia and Herzegovina, an entity of Bosnia and Herzegovina.

Maleševci is a village located in the municipality of Bosansko Grahovo, which is part of the Federation of Bosnia and Herzegovina, a political entity in Bosnia and Herzegovina. The village is situated in a mountainous region characterized by its natural beauty and rural landscape. Maleševci has a small population, predominantly comprised of Bosniaks, reflecting the demographic trends of the area. The village's economy is primarily based on agriculture and livestock. Like many areas in Bosnia and Herzegovina, Maleševci has experienced historical challenges, particularly during the Bosnian War in the 1990s, impacting its demographic and sociocultural environment.

Thomas Jefferson High School for Advanced Studies, more commonly known as simply Thomas Jefferson High School, is a public high school in the city of Gretna in Jefferson Parish, Louisiana. It has consistently ranked among the state's best public high schools and is also among the most diverse It is part of the Jefferson Parish Public Schools system. The school had 367 students in 2016. It has 67 percent minority enrollment. The school is at 910 Gretna Boulevard. The school mascot is a jaguar and the colors are California blue, gray, and black. In 2023 the school was to move to the former Gretna Middle School.

Thomas Jefferson High School is a public secondary school located in Gretna, Louisiana. Established in 1958, it serves grades 9 through 12 and is part of the Jefferson Parish Public School System. The school is named after Thomas Jefferson, the third President of the United States. It is known for its commitment to academic excellence and offers a variety of educational programs, including Advanced Placement courses. The school emphasizes a well-rounded education with extracurricular activities in athletics, arts, and various clubs. Thomas Jefferson High School has received recognition for its performance and contributions to the local community.
Rafael Alcázar is a Spanish screenwriter, film director and producer.

Rafael Alcázar was a Spanish footballer born on March 10, 1990, in Alcalá de Henares, Spain. Primarily playing as a midfielder, Alcázar began his professional career in the lower divisions of Spanish football before moving on to play for several clubs in Spain's Segunda División and Tercera División. His playing style was characterized by strong tactical awareness and versatility on the field. Throughout his career, Alcázar garnered attention for his work ethic and leadership qualities, contributing to his teams both offensively and defensively. After retiring from professional football, he transitioned into coaching, where he continued to impact the sport.

Amy Mainzer (born January 2, 1974) is an American astronomer, specializing in astrophysical instrumentation and infrared astronomy. She was the deputy project scientist for the Wide-field Infrared Survey Explorer and is the principal investigator for its NEOWISE extension to study minor planets and for the future Near Earth Object Surveyor space telescope mission. She also hosted segments for and served as science curriculum consultant and executive producer for the PBS Kids series Ready Jet Go!

Amy Mainzer is an American astronomer and professor known for her work in infrared astronomy and her role as a principal investigator for NASA's NEOWISE mission, which surveys near-Earth objects. She earned her Ph.D. in Astronomy from the University of California, Los Angeles, and has contributed significantly to the study of asteroids, comets, and planetary science. Mainzer has also been involved in various educational outreach initiatives, promoting science and technology to the public. In addition to her research, she has appeared in documentaries and media programs to discuss topics in space and astronomy, making her a prominent figure in the field.
Cesar Javier Montiglio (born August 22, 1984, in Yerba Buena, Tucumán) is an Argentine footballer who currently plays as right winger for Juventud Antoniana.

César Montiglio is a prominent Argentine astronomer known for his contributions to the field of astrophysics and space science. He gained recognition for his research in stellar dynamics and the study of cosmic phenomena. Montiglio has published numerous papers in peer-reviewed journals and has collaborated with various international space agencies on projects related to celestial observation and data analysis. In addition to his research work, he has been involved in educational outreach programs aimed at increasing public awareness of astronomy and science. His efforts have earned him accolades within the scientific community, making him a notable figure in contemporary astronomy.
Glossosphecia is a genus of moths in the family Sesiidae.

Glossosphecia is a genus of moths belonging to the family Sesiidae, commonly known as clearwing moths. Found primarily in various regions of Africa and parts of Asia, species within this genus are characterized by their resemblance to wasps, featuring transparent wings and a robust body. The larvae typically feed on the roots and stems of herbaceous plants, contributing to their ecological role as herbivores. Glossosphecia moths are often identified by their distinctive wing patterns and coloration, which exhibit mimicry to evade predation. The genus includes several species, each adapted to specific environments and host plants.

Groß-Umstadt Wiebelsbach station is a station on the Odenwald Railway (German: Odenwaldbahn) in the town of Groß-Umstadt in the German state of Hesse. The station is classified by Deutsche Bahn as a category 5 station. The Odenwald Railway running from Eberbach branches at the station towards Darmstadt and Hanau. The station is located in the area administered by the Rhein-Main-Verkehrsverbund (Rhine-Main Transport Association, RMV).

Groß-Umstadt Wiebelsbach station is a railway station located in the district of Wiebelsbach in the municipality of Groß-Umstadt, Hesse, Germany. It is situated on the Odenwaldbahn line, which connects the cities of Frankfurt and Erbach. The station primarily serves regional train services, providing connectivity to the surrounding areas. Notable features include a single platform and basic passenger amenities, making it a local stop for residents and visitors. The station is operated by the Hessische Landesbahn and plays a role in the regional transportation network.

Tienerklanken (English: Teen Sounds) was a Belgian Dutch language pop music television programme. It was broadcast from 1963 to 1973, running on the Vlaamse Radio- en Televisieomroeporganisatie Belgian television network. It showcased European and American stars of the pop, rock and rhythm and blues genres, such as Jacques Brel, The Rolling Stones, Queen, having their first appearance outside of the UK, The Jimi Hendrix Experience and Pink Floyd. The show also had segments dealing with controversial counterculture revolution topics, such as drugs and sex.

Tienerklanken is a Dutch youth radio station that primarily focuses on music aimed at teenagers and young adults. Founded in the early 2000s, the station is known for featuring a mix of contemporary pop music, chart-topping hits, and emerging artists. Tienerklanken also provides engaging content tailored to its audience, including interviews, podcasts, and discussions on relevant youth culture topics. The station aims to create a community atmosphere for its listeners, encouraging interaction through social media and events. Tienerklanken contributes to the Dutch youth media landscape by offering an accessible platform for music and cultural expression.

Cane Tago ("The Dog Tago") is a statue erected in Bologna, Italy, in 1777. It was the work of Luigi Acquisti, commissioned by the marquis Tommaso dè Buoi, to celebrate the fidelity of his German Weimaraner hound. The dog had fallen from the windowsill in joy at seeing its master returning from a long journey. The terracotta statue was placed on the spot where the dog was supposed to have fallen, at the de’ Buoi’House, now the seat of the Cultural Department of the Municipality of Bologna in via Oberdan 24. After 200 years, atmospheric damage necessitated restoration, and the statue was subsequently displayed at the local Museum of Archeology. It is now at the Collezioni Comunali d’Arte, inside Palazzo d'Accursio.

Cane Tago is a notable figure in the field of music and performance, known for his innovative contributions to contemporary dance and choreography. He began his career in the late 20th century, gaining recognition for his unique style that blends traditional dance with modern elements. Tago has collaborated with various artists and ensembles, showcasing his work in numerous international festivals. His performances often explore themes of cultural identity and social issues, employing a mix of music, visual art, and physical expression. Throughout his career, Tago has received several awards for his artistic achievements and has been influential in inspiring a new generation of dancers and choreographers.

Chinese Internet slang (Chinese: 中国网络用语; pinyin: zhōngguó wǎngluò yòngyǔ) refers to various kinds of Internet slang used by people on the Chinese Internet. The categories given below are not exclusive and are used distinguish the different kinds of Chinese internet slang. Some phrases may belong in more than one category.

Chinese Internet slang refers to the colloquial language and expressions popularized on Chinese social media platforms and forums. This slang often combines elements of traditional Chinese language with phonetic plays, humor, and cultural references. Abbreviations, acronyms, and homophones are commonly used, allowing for creative and concise communication. Notable examples include "666" (liù liù liù), meaning "cool" or "impressive," and "duang," a nonsensical word popularized by a viral video. The use of emojis and memes further enriches the online lexicon. Chinese Internet slang reflects youth culture, societal trends, and the rapid evolution of language in digital communication.
Dandapur is a village in Belagavi district in the southern state of Karnataka, India.

Dandapur is a village located in the Bhadrak district of the Indian state of Odisha. It is part of the Tihidi block and is known for its agricultural activities, with the local economy primarily based on farming. The village features a rural landscape typical of the region, with rice and various other crops cultivated by the inhabitants. Dandapur is also home to a small population that engages in traditional practices and community-based events. The village is accessible by local roads and is characterized by its close-knit community and cultural heritage.

Kareem Ignacio López Contreras (born January 1, 1990), better known by his stage name, Karim Lopez, is a Mexican musician and singer who became widely known as a former member of the musical group AK-7.

Kareem Lopez is a fictional character known for his dynamic role in contemporary literature and multimedia. Often depicted as a resourceful and determined individual, he represents themes of resilience and social justice. Within various narratives, Kareem's character typically navigates challenging environments, facing issues related to identity, community, and personal growth. His storyline has resonated with audiences, leading to discussions around cultural representation and the impact of storytelling on societal perceptions. While specific works featuring Kareem Lopez vary, he is frequently celebrated for his relatable struggles and triumphs that highlight the complexities of modern life.

Juanita Tamayo Lott (born 1948) is a Filipina-American author and activist. A chronicler of the Filipino experience in America, Lott has authored several popular and scholarly works on Asian Americans. She has also contributed to the establishment of several Asian American studies departments. As a college student in 1969, Lott co-founded the first U.S. Filipino American Studies Program at San Francisco State; in 2007, she developed the Filipino American Studies Program at the University of Maryland, College Park. Trained as a statistician and demographer, she spent her career as a policy analyst for the federal government.

Juanita Tamayo Lott (born 1943) is an American educator, author, and activist known for her work in promoting multicultural education and social justice. Born in Manila, Philippines, she moved to the United States and has since contributed to various educational initiatives focused on cultural diversity and equity. Lott has written extensively on issues related to race, gender, and education, advocating for the inclusion of underrepresented voices in academic curricula. She has served in various teaching and administrative roles, emphasizing the importance of critical pedagogy in empowering marginalized communities. Lott's contributions have made her a prominent figure in discussions around educational reform and multiculturalism in the United States.
The Snow Girl (Spanish: La chica de nieve) is a Spanish mystery thriller television series based on the novel by Javier Castillo which stars Milena Smit.

"The Snow Girl" is a Spanish thriller television series adapted from the 2012 novel "La chica de nieve" by Javier Castillo. The series, which premiered on Netflix in January 2023, follows the story of a young girl named Amaya who mysteriously disappears during a holiday parade in Málaga. Her distraught parents, along with an investigative journalist, embark on a desperate search to uncover the truth behind her abduction. The show intertwines themes of media sensationalism, parental desperation, and the complexities of crime investigation. It gained attention for its gripping narrative and compelling character development, contributing to the growing popularity of Spanish-language series on global streaming platforms.

A pellet is a non-spherical projectile designed to be fired from an air gun, and an airgun that fires such pellets is commonly known as a pellet gun. Air gun pellets differ from bullets, shells and shot used in firearms and artillery in terms of the pressures encountered; airguns operate at pressures as low as 50 atmospheres, while firearms and artillery operate at thousands of atmospheres. Airguns generally use a slightly undersized projectile that is designed to obturate upon shooting so as to seal the bore, and engage the rifling; firearms have sufficient pressure to force a slightly oversized bullet to fit the bore in order to form a tight seal. Since pellets may be shot through a smoothbore barrel, they are often designed to be inherently stable, much like the Foster slugs used in smoothbore shotguns.

A pellet is a small projectile designed for use in air guns, including air rifles and air pistols. Typically made from lead or a lead alloy, pellets are often shaped as diabolo, featuring a waist and a wider head to improve aerodynamic stability. They come in various calibers, with .177 (4.5 mm) and .22 (5.5 mm) being the most common. Pellets are favored for their accuracy and efficiency in target shooting, pest control, and small game hunting. The design and weight of pellets can vary, impacting their speed and trajectory, which makes them suitable for different shooting applications.
Peter Johnson (died 1672, real name possibly Pieter Janszoon) was a Dutch pirate active in the Caribbean. He is best known for the circumstances surrounding his trial.

Peter Johnson was a notorious English pirate active during the early 18th century, primarily known for his raiding activities in the Caribbean Sea and along the American colonial coast. Little is documented about his early life, but he is believed to have started his career in piracy around the 1710s. Johnson gained a reputation for his cunning tactics and brutality, often targeting merchant ships. He operated primarily out of the Bahamas, which served as a haven for pirates during this period. Johnson's reign as a pirate came to an end when he was captured by naval forces in the mid-1720s. Following his apprehension, he was tried and executed, contributing to the efforts against piracy in the region. His exploits and eventual downfall are reflective of the turbulent era of piracy that characterized the Golden Age of Piracy.
Kankuria is a census town in the Samserganj CD block, under the Dhulian Circle Area in the Jangipur subdivision of the Murshidabad district in the Indian state of West Bengal.

Kankuria is a village located in the Purulia district of West Bengal, India. It falls under the administrative jurisdiction of the Barabazar police station and is characterized by its rural setting and agricultural economy. The village has a diverse population, comprising various ethnic communities. Kankuria is known for its traditional practices and local festivals, which reflect the cultural heritage of the region. Accessible by road, it connects with nearby towns and cities, contributing to its gradual development in terms of infrastructure and education. The surrounding landscapes include natural features typical of the Chota Nagpur plateau, offering opportunities for agriculture and local craft industries.

New Nation Movement NPC and Others v President of the Republic of South Africa and Others, ZACC 11, is a decision of the Constitutional Court of South Africa, handed down on 11 June 2020, which declared that the Constitution requires that citizens be allowed to stand for election to the National Assembly and provincial legislatures as independents without having to join or form a political party. The declaration was suspended for 24 months to allow Parliament to modify the electoral laws to comply. The majority judgment was written by Justice Madlanga and a concurring opinion was written by Justice Jafta; these opinions were supported by eight of the nine judges hearing the case. Justice Froneman filed a dissenting opinion.
topic: New Nation Movement NPC v President of the Republic of South Africa

text: New Nation Movement NPC v President of the Republic of South Africa is a landmark case decided by the Constitutional Court of South Africa in 2020. The case addressed the constitutionality of the electoral system in the country, specifically the requirement for political parties to contest elections in order to secure representation in the National Assembly. The New Nation Movement, a non-profit organization, challenged the system, arguing that it limited democratic participation and was inconsistent with the constitutional principles of inclusivity and proportional representation. The court ultimately ruled that the electoral system was unconstitutional and ordered the government to reform it, affirming the importance of a more inclusive and representative democracy in South Africa. This decision has significant implications for electoral reform and the political landscape of the country.
topic: New Nation Movement NPC v President of the Republic of South Africa

text: The Dark Circle is a criminal organization appearing in media published by DC Comics, primarily as an enemy of the Legion of Super-Heroes. They first appeared in 1968, created by Jim Shooter as a criminal organization founded by five members and consisting of them and their clones. The Dark Circle concept was later modified to consist of members from five core worlds instead of clones. A later version, introduced following the Zero Hour: Crisis in Time! reboot, was led by Brainiac 4 and had several known Legion enemies among their ranks instead of generic masked henchmen.

Dark Circle is a term used in various contexts, most commonly in the fields of literature, film, and comic books. In the realm of fiction, it often refers to a group or organization characterized by secrecy and malevolence. In comic books, particularly in the works published by Archie Comics, Dark Circle refers to a superhero line that includes characters like The Shield and The Black Hood. This line reimagines classic characters, blending traditional superhero themes with modern storytelling. Additionally, the term can denote visual phenomena, such as shadows beneath the eyes, commonly associated with fatigue or poor health.
The men's discus throw event at the 2014 African Championships in Athletics was held on August 11 on Stade de Marrakech.
topic: 2014 African Championships in Athletics – Men's discus throw

text: The men's discus throw at the 2014 African Championships in Athletics took place on July 12, 2014, at the Estadio Olímpico, in Marrakech, Morocco. This event featured top male discus throwers from various African nations competing for medals. The competition was notable for showcasing the continent's athletic talent in this discipline, with numerous national records being challenged. The final results included performances that contributed to the overall medal tally for the championships, highlighting the significance of the event in the African athletic calendar.
topic: 2014 African Championships in Athletics – Men's discus throw

text: Lesbian, gay, bisexual, and transgender (LGBT) people in Montenegro face significant challenges not experienced by non-LGBT residents. Both male and female same-sex sexual activity are legal in Montenegro, but households headed by same-sex couples are not eligible for the same legal protections available to opposite-sex married couples. Same-sex couples are unable to marry, and the Constitution of Montenegro bans same-sex marriage. Since 15 July 2021, same-sex couples may register their relationship as a Life Partnership, which gives them almost the same legal rights and protections available to opposite-sex married couples, except adoption. Discrimination on the basis of both sexual orientation and gender identity is banned in employment, the provision of goods and services, education and health services. Montenegro also possesses hate crime and hate speech laws which include sexual orientation and gender identity as grounds of non-discrimination. The association ILGA-Europe has ranked Montenegro 8th out of 49 European countries in terms of LGBT rights legislation. Despite this, Montenegrin society has yet to reach a high level of acceptance, and discrimination against LGBT people often goes unreported.

LGBTQ rights in Montenegro have experienced gradual progression since the country gained independence in 2006. Although homosexuality was decriminalized in 1977, societal acceptance has lagged behind, with widespread discrimination and prejudice reported. Montenegro's legal framework includes anti-discrimination laws and a civil partnership law enacted in 2020, which provides some legal recognition for same-sex couples. Despite these advancements, LGBTQ individuals often face challenges, including social stigma and violence, highlighting the need for greater protections and advocacy. International organizations and local activists continue to work towards improving the rights and visibility of the LGBTQ community in Montenegro.
Charles Edmund de Trafford (21 May 1864 – 11 November 1951) was an English aristocrat and a first-class cricketer.

Charles de Trafford (1825–1899) was a British soldier, landowner, and notable figure in 19th-century England. He was born into a prominent family with roots in the military and local governance. De Trafford served as an officer in the British Army and achieved the rank of Lieutenant Colonel. He was recognized for his contributions during various military campaigns. In addition to his military career, he was involved in local politics and land management, owning significant estates in Lancashire. De Trafford's legacy includes his role in the development of the region and his participation in social and charitable activities. He passed away in 1899, leaving behind a reputation for service and community involvement.

East Arm is a suburb in the Northern Territory of Australia located in Darwin about 8 kilometres (5.0 mi) east of Darwin City overlooking a part of Darwin Harbour known as the East Arm. It is the traditional land and waterways of the Larrakia people.

East Arm is a suburb located in the Northern Territory of Australia, situated approximately 20 kilometers south of Darwin's central business district. It is primarily industrial, hosting a number of transport and logistics facilities, including the East Arm Port, which is a key shipping hub for the region. The suburb is also adjacent to the Larrakeyah and the Glyde Point military precincts and has seen significant development due to its strategic location. East Arm is characterized by its mixed-use zoning, with residential areas emerging alongside its industrial landscape. The suburb has access to various amenities and services catering to both workers and residents.

The Soure train crash occurred on 31 July 2020 when a high-speed passenger train collided with a track maintenance vehicle at Soure, Portugal. Two people were killed and 44 were injured, three seriously.

The Soure train crash refers to a railway accident that occurred on October 18, 2020, in Soure, a municipality in the state of Pará, Brazil. A passenger train operated by the Ferrovia Norte-Sul derailed while traveling at high speed, resulting in multiple injuries and fatalities. The incident drew attention to safety regulations and maintenance practices within the Brazilian railway system. Investigations were launched to determine the cause of the derailment, focusing on potential mechanical failures and track conditions. The crash prompted widespread discussions about railway safety and infrastructure improvement in the region.

Yasmin Louise Sooka (born 10 October 1957) is a South African human rights lawyer and activist. She is the Executive Director of the Foundation for Human Rights in South Africa and the International Truth and Justice Project (ITJP), an independent non-profit organisation based in London working since 2013 to protect and promote justice and accountability in Sri Lanka. She was born in Cape Town, South Africa, and got a law degree from the University of the Witwatersrand. During the Apartheid, she was a member of the World Conference on Religion and Peace (South African Chapter) and served as the President of the WCRP. She was a member of the National Repatriation Committee for South African exiles. In 1995, she was appointed to the South African Truth and Reconciliation Commission as the Deputy Chair of the Human Rights Violations Committee and was responsible for finalizing the Commission’s final report handed over to President Mbeki in March 2003. She chaired the Commission’s legal sub-committee between 1995 and 2001. Since 2000, she has also been a member of the Advisory Body on the Review of Resolution 1325. She was appointed to the Sierra Leone Truth and Reconciliation Commission by Mary Robinson severing from 2002 to 2004. In July 2010, Ms. Sooka was appointed to the three-member Panel of Experts advising the UN Secretary General on accountability for war crimes committed during the final stages of the war in Sri Lanka. She was appointed as Chair of the United Nations Commission on Human Rights in South Sudan in 2016.

Yasmin Sooka is a prominent South African lawyer and human rights advocate known for her work in transitional justice and international human rights law. She co-founded the Foundation for Human Rights in South Africa and has served as a commissioner on the Truth and Reconciliation Commission (TRC), where she played a crucial role in addressing the human rights violations committed during apartheid. Sooka has been involved in various roles within international organizations, including the United Nations, where she has worked to advance accountability for war crimes and promote human rights globally. Her work is characterized by a commitment to justice for victims and the promotion of democratic governance in post-conflict societies.

The 2011 Nigerian Senate election in Osun State was held on April 9, 2011, to elect members of the Nigerian Senate to represent Osun State. Olusola Adeyeye representing Osun Central, Christopher Omoworare Babajide representing Osun East and Mudasiru Oyetunde Hussein representing Osun West all won on the platform of Action Congress of Nigeria.
topic: 2011 Nigerian Senate elections in Osun State

text: The 2011 Nigerian Senate elections in Osun State were held on April 9, 2011, as part of the national general elections. Voters in Osun State elected their representatives for the Senate, part of the National Assembly of Nigeria. The elections saw the emergence of various political parties, with the Action Congress of Nigeria (ACN) and the People's Democratic Party (PDP) being the most prominent. The election results led to the ACN securing the Senate seat, reflecting the party's strong influence in the region at the time. The elections were characterized by a significant voter turnout and were part of a broader electoral process that sought to enhance democratic governance in Nigeria.
topic: 2011 Nigerian Senate elections in Osun State

text: Nichols and Shepard Co. was an American partnership company which manufactured farm machinery, steam engines and mill machinery. In 1848, John Nichols opened a blacksmith shop in Battle Creek, Michigan, where he began making various farm tools for local farmers. He built his first thresher/separator in 1852. The business was successful, so in the 1850s he joined with David Shepard to form a partnership known as Nichols, Shepard and Company which manufactured farm machinery, steam engines and mill machinery. The first thresher/separator of small grains (largely wheat and oats) was developed in about 1831 by the Pitts brothers—Hiram and John Pitts of Buffalo, New York. However, this early thresher, called the "ground hog," was quite unlike the conventional thresher/separators that developed since that time. For instance, the ground hog's separating unit was largely a slatted apron which pulled the grain across a screen. John Nichols and David Shepard realized that the apron style separator was not a technology that was going to work. Consequently, in 1857, the Nichols and Shepard Company developed the first "vibrator" separating unit for the small grain thresher. This vibrator-style of separator soon became universally adopted by all other thresher/separator manufacturers. The Nichols and Shepard Company received a patent from the United States government for their "Vibrator" grain separator on January 7, 1862. The company also obtained a number of other patents for other advances in the thresher/separator technology, for original improvements in steam engine traction technology. During the 1920s, the Nichols and Shepard Company developed a successfully functioning corn picker. In 1929 the Nichols and Shepard Company was acquired by the Oliver Farm Equipment Company. Thus the 'corn picker' became the direct ancestor of the Oliver cornpicker.

Nichols and Shepard was an American manufacturer of agricultural machinery, particularly known for its innovations in hay and forage equipment. Founded in 1881 in Battle Creek, Michigan, the company initially specialized in the production of horse-drawn hay machinery. Over the years, Nichols and Shepard expanded its product lineup to include steam engines and, later, gas-powered tractors. The firm became notable for its contributions to the mechanization of farming and played a significant role in advancing agricultural technology during the late 19th and early 20th centuries. The company was eventually acquired by the Oliver Farm Equipment Company in the mid-20th century, leading to its integration into a larger agricultural machinery ecosystem. Nichols and Shepard's legacy is marked by its emphasis on innovation, quality, and its impact on the agricultural industry.

Hugh Dillon (born May 31, 1963) is a Canadian singer, actor, and television producer. The lead vocalist of the rock bands Headstones and Hugh Dillon Redemption Choir, his acting roles include Albert Manning in Degrassi: The Next Generation, Mike Sweeney in Durham County, Ed Lane in Flashpoint, Nick in Left 4 Dead 2, Duncan Sinclair in X Company, Donnie Haskell in Yellowstone, and Ian Ferguson in Mayor of Kingstown. He is also a co-creator, executive producer, and series regular of the Paramount+ series Mayor of Kingstown.

Hugh Dillon is a Canadian actor, musician, and filmmaker, best known as the lead vocalist for the rock band The Headstones. Born on May 31, 1967, in Kingston, Ontario, Dillon gained prominence in the 1990s as a key figure in the Canadian music scene. The Headstones are recognized for their energetic performances and contributions to alternative rock, particularly with hits like "Cemetery" and "Unsafety Suit." In addition to his music career, Dillon has made a significant impact in television and film, with notable roles in series such as "Durham County" and "Flashpoint." His work has earned him critical acclaim for his acting versatility and compelling performances. Dillon continues to influence both the music and entertainment industries in Canada.

Michael F. Suarez, S.J. is an American academic and Jesuit priest. He is the executive director of Rare Book School (RBS) at the University of Virginia (UVA), where he is also a professor of English, a university professor, and an honorary curator of special collections. In addition to serving as Rare Book School’s executive Director, Suarez teaches multiple courses on bibliography and book history at the school. He is also PI for $5.4 million in grants from the Andrew W. Mellon Foundation to support the study of bibliography and book history at Rare Book School and beyond.

Michael F. Suarez is an American bibliographer, scholar, and professor known for his expertise in the fields of bibliography, book history, and digital humanities. He serves as the director of the Rare Book School at the University of Virginia and has held various academic and administrative positions in institutions dedicated to the study of books and literary culture. Suarez has contributed to numerous scholarly publications and is recognized for his work on the history of the book, manuscript studies, and the impact of digital technologies on the humanities. His research often explores the intersections between traditional bibliographic methodologies and contemporary digital practices.
Özlem Ceren Dursun (born 8 December 2003) is a Turkish Olympian cross-country skier.

Özlem Ceren Dursun is a Turkish politician and member of the Republican People's Party (CHP). She was born on November 4, 1984, in Turkey. Dursun has served in various capacities within the CHP and has been an advocate for social justice, women's rights, and democratic reforms. She gained prominence in regional politics and has been involved in initiatives aimed at improving local governance and community engagement. Dursun's political career reflects a commitment to progressive values and active participation in civic affairs.

Same-sex marriage has been legal in Norway since 1 January 2009 when a gender-neutral marriage law came into force after being passed by the Storting in June 2008. Norway was the first Scandinavian country, the fourth in Europe, and the sixth in the world to legalize same-sex marriage, after the Netherlands, Belgium, Spain, Canada and South Africa. Polling suggests that a majority of Norwegians support the legal recognition of same-sex marriage. In 2024, a British wedding planning website named Norway the best marriage destination in the world for same-sex couples. From 1993 to 2008, Norway allowed same-sex couples to enter into registered partnerships, which provided virtually all the protections, responsibilities and benefits of marriage. Norway was the second country in the world to provide some form of recognition to same-sex couples, after Denmark.

Same-sex marriage in Norway was legalized on January 1, 2009, following the passage of a law that allowed same-sex couples to marry and enjoy the same legal rights as heterosexual couples. Norway had previously enacted a registered partnership law in 1993, which provided limited rights but did not equate to marriage. The 2009 legislation marked a significant advancement in LGBTQ+ rights in Norway, reflecting changing societal attitudes towards same-sex relationships. The law allows same-sex couples to adopt children and has been supported by a majority of the Norwegian population, as well as by various political parties. Norway's acceptance of same-sex marriage is seen as part of a broader trend of increasing recognition of LGBTQ+ rights across Europe.
A HEENT examination is a portion of a physical examination that principally concerns the head, eyes, ears, nose, and throat.

The HEENT examination is a clinical assessment that focuses on the Head, Eyes, Ears, Nose, and Throat of a patient. It is a routine part of a physical examination performed by healthcare providers to evaluate signs and symptoms related to these regions. The examination involves visual inspection, palpation, and various tests to assess functions and detect abnormalities such as infections, allergies, or structural issues. The HEENT exam can help diagnose a wide range of conditions, including sinusitis, conjunctivitis, and otitis media. It is commonly conducted in outpatient settings and is crucial for a comprehensive understanding of a patient's health.

The Drury Panthers are the athletic teams that represent Drury University, located in Springfield, Missouri, in NCAA Division II intercollegiate sports. The Panthers compete as members of the Great Lakes Valley Conference for all 21 varsity sports. Drury has been a member of the GLVC since 2005.

The Drury Panthers are the athletic teams representing Drury University, a private institution located in Springfield, Missouri. Competing in NCAA Division II, the Panthers are part of the Great Lakes Valley Conference (GLVC). The university offers a variety of sports, including basketball, soccer, swimming, and tennis, among others. The Drury Panthers have a reputation for strong performances, particularly in men’s and women’s basketball, with both teams achieving significant success in conference and national championships. The school colors are navy blue and gold, and the mascot is a panther. Drury's athletics program focuses on fostering teamwork, sportsmanship, and community involvement.
Jane Donnelly is a British writer. During period of 1965 to 2000, she wrote more than 60 romance novels from Mills & Boon. Her novels are characterized as "happy escapist reading".

Jane Donnelly is a notable figure in the field of social activism and human rights advocacy, primarily known for her work in promoting educational and social equity. Active in various campaigns for over two decades, she has focused on issues related to poverty, women's rights, and access to education. Donnelly has collaborated with numerous non-governmental organizations and community groups, leveraging her expertise to raise awareness and drive policy changes. Her efforts have garnered recognition from both local and international bodies, highlighting her influence in advancing social justice initiatives.
Gare is a village in the municipality of Zavidovići, Bosnia and Herzegovina.

Gare is a village located in the municipality of Zavidovići, within Bosnia and Herzegovina. It is situated in the central part of the country and is part of the Federation of Bosnia and Herzegovina entity. Historically known for its natural beauty and local agriculture, Gare has a small population that contributes to the rural character of the area. The village is characterized by its traditional Bosnian architecture and communal lifestyle, reflecting the cultural heritage of the region. Zavidovići, the larger municipality, is known for its industrial activities and has played a significant role in the economic development of the area. The surrounding landscape features hills and forests, attracting visitors interested in outdoor activities.
Yekimovo (Russian: Екимово) is a rural locality (a village) in Voskresenskoye Rural Settlement, Cherepovetsky District, Vologda Oblast, Russia. The population was 15 as of 2002.

Yekimovo is a rural locality (a village) situated in the Cherepovetsky District of Vologda Oblast, Russia. It is part of the rural settlement of Poverhovo. According to the 2002 census, the village has a small population, indicative of the area's rural character. The locality is characterized by its agricultural landscape and proximity to other villages within the district, reflecting the typical demographic and economic conditions of rural life in this region of Russia.
Arcobacter canalis is a bacterium from the genus Arcobacter.

Arcobacter canalis is a species of bacteria belonging to the genus Arcobacter, which is part of the family Campylobacteraceae. It was first isolated from the intestinal tract of various animals and is known for its rod-shaped, spiral morphology. Arcobacter canalis is microaerophilic and capable of oxygen metabolism, thriving in environments with low oxygen levels. It has garnered attention due to its potential role in foodborne illnesses and its presence in water systems, indicating possible implications for public health and food safety. Research on Arcobacter canalis is ongoing to better understand its ecology, pathogenicity, and antibiotic resistance profiles.

Warm Dust were a British progressive jazz rock band of the early 1970s. Although a relatively obscure group, they featured Paul Carrack and Terry "Tex" Comer, who later formed Ace. They recorded for the Trend and BASF labels. They also had releases on the Uni, Stateside and Metronome labels.

Warm dust refers to the small particles found in space that are heated by nearby stars and other celestial objects. This type of dust, typically composed of silicate minerals, carbon compounds, and ice grains, plays a crucial role in various astrophysical processes, including star formation and the thermal balance of galaxies. Warm dust emits infrared radiation, allowing astronomers to study the cosmic structure and composition of the universe in the infrared spectrum. Observations made with space telescopes, such as the Spitzer Space Telescope, have significantly advanced the understanding of warm dust and its influence on the interstellar medium and galactic evolution.

Iraq and Pakistan established diplomatic relations in 1947. Cultural interaction and economic trade between Mesopotamia and the Indus Valley date back to 1800 BCE. In 1955, both Iraq and Pakistan joined the Baghdad Pact, a military alliance against the Soviet Union. However, when the king of Iraq was assassinated in 1958, Iraq pulled out of the Baghdad Pact, which was subsequently renamed to the Central Treaty Organization (CENTO). During the Ba'athist era, relations were at times cordial and sometimes hostile. However, following the 2003 invasion of Iraq that toppled the Ba'athist government, bilateral relations have stabilized; Pakistan has supported Iraq in its fight against the Islamic State and other militant groups active in the Iraqi conflict. Iraq maintains an embassy in Islamabad while Pakistan maintains an embassy in Baghdad.

Iraq–Pakistan relations refer to the bilateral diplomatic, cultural, and economic interactions between Iraq and Pakistan. Both countries established formal diplomatic ties shortly after Pakistan's independence in 1947. Historically, their relationship has been influenced by shared political interests and regional dynamics, such as their stance on various Middle Eastern issues and the impact of external powers in the region. Economic cooperation has included trade in commodities like oil and textiles. Diplomatic visits and exchanges have taken place periodically, emphasizing solidarity in mutual interests, particularly related to security and counter-terrorism. However, relations have fluctuated due to broader geopolitical changes in the region and varying domestic political landscapes in both countries.
Belgique is an unincorporated community in eastern Perry County, Missouri, United States. It is located approximately five miles south of Chester, Illinois in Perry County's Bois Brule Township.

Belgique is an unincorporated community located in Cape Girardeau County, Missouri, United States. Situated near the convergence of Route 34 and Route 25, it is part of the larger Cape Girardeau-Jackson Metropolitan Statistical Area. The community was established in the late 19th century and named after Belgium, reflecting the influence of Belgian immigrants who settled in the area. Although it does not have a formal government structure or defined boundaries, Belgique is noted for its rural character and historical significance in the region.

Singapore FA was a representative team of the Football Association of Singapore in competitions run by the Football Association of Malaysia, separate from the Singapore national team. Despite dissolving in 1994, it is still by far the second most successful team in Malaysia with 65 honours (only surpassed by its rival Selangor). Its derby with Selangor was known as the most North-South Rivalry.

The Singapore Football Association (SFA) is the governing body for football in Singapore, responsible for overseeing the development, regulation, and promotion of the sport within the country. Founded in 1892, it is one of the oldest football associations in Asia. The SFA organizes the national leagues and tournaments, conducts training programs for players and coaches, and manages the national teams at various levels. It is a member of the Asian Football Confederation (AFC) and the Fédération Internationale de Football Association (FIFA), representing Singapore in international football competitions. The association has played a key role in promoting football as a popular sport in Singapore, contributing to its growth and the establishment of a competitive local football scene.

United Nations Security Council resolution 1170, adopted unanimously on 27 May 1998, after considering the situation across the African continent, the Council decided to establish an ad hoc Working Group to review the Secretary-General Kofi Annan's recommendations concerning the maintenance of international peace and security in Africa.

United Nations Security Council Resolution 1170, adopted on June 28, 1998, addresses the situation in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC). The resolution emphasizes the need for a peaceful resolution to the ongoing conflict and calls for the cessation of hostilities and respect for human rights. It expresses concern over the humanitarian crisis resulting from the conflict and encourages international support for humanitarian efforts. The resolution also urges all parties involved to engage in dialogue and to cooperate with regional organizations in peacebuilding initiatives. It reaffirms the commitment of the Security Council to monitor the situation in the DRC and calls for a report from the Secretary-General on developments in the country.
Wilfrid Caithness or Wilfred Caithness (1883–1954) was a British stage and film actor. He played the role of Sebastian Moran in the 1935 film The Triumph of Sherlock Holmes.

Wilfrid Caithness (1917-2006) was a British artist known for his contributions to the fields of painting and printmaking. Born in England, he studied at the Royal Academy Schools and developed a distinctive style that blended traditional techniques with contemporary themes. Caithness's work often explored the interplay of light and shadow, and he was influenced by the natural landscapes of his surroundings. His paintings have been exhibited in various prestigious galleries across the UK and are included in several public and private collections. In addition to his artistic practice, Caithness was an educator, teaching at multiple institutions and inspiring a new generation of artists. His legacy continues to be celebrated in the art community for both his creative output and his dedication to artistic education.

Ars et Labor Ferrara, formerly known as SPAL (Italian pronunciation: [spal]), is a professional football club based in Ferrara, Emilia-Romagna, Italy. During the 2024–25 season the team played in Serie C, the third tier of the Italian football league system. Founded in 1907, since 1928 they have played their home matches at Stadio Paolo Mazza, named after Paolo Mazza (chairman of the club between 1946 and 1977). In total, SPAL have participated in 24 top-tier, 28 second-tier, 43 third-tier, 7 fourth-tier and 1 fifth-tier league seasons. The club's best finish was when they came fifth in the 1959–60 Serie A; they also reached the 1961–62 Coppa Italia final. The club was chaired by the American lawyer and businessman Joe Tacopina and the manager was Francesco Baldini until the 2025 liquidation, after which the team's legacy is carried on by Ars et Labor Ferrara.

SPAL, or Società Polisportiva Ars Et Labor, is a professional football club based in Ferrara, Italy. Founded in 1907, the club's colors are white and blue. SPAL has experienced various periods of success, notably winning three Serie B titles and one Serie C title, and has had several stints in Italy's top-flight Serie A. The club's home games are played at the Stadio Paolo Mazza, which can accommodate over 16,000 spectators. SPAL has a passionate fan base and has developed a reputation for its youth academy, producing several notable players throughout its history. The club's nickname, "Gli Estensi," reflects its historical ties to the House of Este, which was influential in Ferrara.
Ieuan Lloyd (1914–1989) was a professional rugby league footballer who played in the 1930s. He played at club level for Barrow, as a stand-off.

Ian Lloyd is a former professional rugby league player from England who played primarily as a fullback during the 1990s. He began his career with the London Broncos, making his debut in the early 1990s. Lloyd had stints at various clubs, including the Halifax Blue Sox, where he gained recognition for his scoring ability and tactical play. Known for his agility and speed on the field, he contributed significantly to his teams throughout his career. After retiring from professional play, Lloyd remained involved in rugby league, contributing to the sport through coaching and local club activities.

Koah-pau or gua bao or cuapao also known as a pork belly bun, ambiguously as bao, or erroneously as bao bun, is a type of lotus leaf bun originating from Fujianese cuisine in China. It is also a popular snack in Taiwan, Singapore, Malaysia, the Philippines, and Nagasaki Chinatown in Japan. It consists of a slice of stewed meat and condiments sandwiched between flat steamed bread known as lotus leaf bread (荷葉餅; héyèbǐng). The lotus leaf bread is typically 6–8 centimetres (2.4–3.1 in) in size, semi-circular and flat in form, with a horizontal fold that, when opened, gives the appearance that it has been sliced. The traditional filling for gua bao is a slice of red-cooked pork belly, typically dressed with stir-fried suan cai (pickled mustard greens), coriander, and ground peanuts.

Koah-pau is a traditional Hawaiian practice involving the ceremonial use of a conch shell (pū) to signal various events, such as the beginning and end of significant activities or gatherings. The term "koah-pau" can refer specifically to the action of blowing the conch shell to call people together or to mark ceremonial moments. This practice is deeply rooted in Hawaiian culture and history, symbolizing communication, respect, and the spiritual connection among the community. The sound of the pū is often associated with important cultural rituals, celebrations, and observances, underscoring its role as a cultural emblem in Hawaiian society.

The Azawagh (alias Azaouagh or Azawak) is a dry basin covering what is today the northwestern Niger, as well as parts of northeastern Mali and southern Algeria. The Azawagh is mainly made up of Sahelian and Saharan flatlands and has a population that is predominantly Tuareg, with some Arabic-speaking and Wodaabe minorities and a recent influx of Hausa and Zarma.

Azawagh is a semi-arid region located in the northeastern part of Mali, extending into parts of Niger and Burkina Faso. It is characterized by its unique desert landscape, including sand dunes, rocky plateaus, and sparse vegetation. The area is primarily inhabited by nomadic pastoralist communities, including the Tuareg and Arab peoples, who rely on transhumance for livestock herding. Azawagh has a rich cultural heritage, with traditions that include music, poetry, and handicrafts. The region has experienced various socio-political challenges, including conflicts driven by ethnic tensions and competition over resources. Climate change and desertification have also impacted livelihoods in Azawagh, exacerbating existing vulnerabilities among its population.

Krishna Shrine is a 6,131-foot-elevation summit located in the eastern Grand Canyon, in Coconino County of northern Arizona, US. The landform is attached at the southwest to the Vishnu Temple massif, about 1.0 mi distant. Krishna Shrine is about 3.0 mi south of the Cape Royal overlook, Walhalla Plateau (southeast Kaibab Plateau, North Rim). A twin landform occupies the southeast of Vishnu Temple, the Rama Shrine. Krishna Shrine towers about 4,000 ft above the Colorado River, about 2.0 miles south. Both Shrines, east and west, and Vishnu Temple, center, are at the headwater drainage of Asbestos Canyon. At the southwest of Krishna, on an extending arm of Redwall Limestone is a short unnamed drainage; (at the southwest is Newberry Butte at the Colorado River, and Granite Gorge). The west flank of Krishna Shrine drains into the long southwest-trending Vishnu Canyon and Creek, which comes from Freya Castle and the Walhalla Plateau, South Rim. Like Rama Shrine (at east), Krishna Shrine is composed of the same geology units: a massif of 4-unit Supai Group, upon the platform of the cliff-former (and platform-former) Redwall Limestone. The upper surface of the landform is triangle-shaped-(northwest), and a small eroded peak-(southeast); the high-point prominence is at the northwest and is a remainder, fractured cliff-peak of the Supai, unit 4, the cliff-forming Esplanade Sandstone famous in the Grand Canyon for forming platforms. (The prominence-platform of Rama Shrine is an extensive Esplanade layer (with slope-former debris of soft Hermit Shale).)

The Krishna Shrine, often associated with Hindu worship, is a sacred space dedicated to Lord Krishna, a major deity in Hinduism revered as the eighth avatar of Vishnu. These shrines can be found in various forms across India and other countries with Hindu populations, including temples, home altars, and festival sites. Typically, a Krishna Shrine features an idol or image of Krishna, often depicted as a young cowherd playing the flute, surrounded by offerings such as flowers, fruits, and incense. Devotees engage in rituals, prayers, and singing of devotional songs (bhajans) to express their reverence. Significant pilgrimage sites, such as the Dwarka temple and Vrindavan, are particularly known for their elaborate Krishna Shrines, attracting millions of followers annually. The importance of such shrines reflects Krishna's fundamental role in devotional practices and the cultural heritage of Hinduism.
Caecum cooperi, common name the Cooper's Atlantic caecum, is a species of small sea snail, a marine gastropod mollusk or micromollusk in the family Caecidae.

Caecum cooperi is a species of marine gastropod mollusk belonging to the family Eulimidae. This species is characterized by its small, elongated, and glossy shell, which typically exhibits a conical shape. Caecum cooperi is primarily found in sandy or muddy substrates in shallow waters, commonly associated with marine environments. It has a wide distribution across the North Atlantic region, particularly along the coasts of the United States and Canada. As a member of the Eulimid family, it is part of a group known for their parasitic associations, often with echinoderms. Research on Caecum cooperi contributes to a better understanding of marine biodiversity and the ecological roles of gastropods in their habitats.
Miguel Câmara Machado (born 29 June 1999) is a Portuguese footballer who plays for CD Operário as a midfielder.

Miguel Machado is a Portuguese professional footballer born on September 18, 1994, in Vila Nova de Gaia, Portugal. Primarily playing as a midfielder, he began his career in the youth system of C.D. Aves before making his senior debut with the club. Throughout his career, he has played for several teams in the lower leagues of Portuguese football, including a notable stint with Varzim S.C. Machado is known for his technical skills, vision on the pitch, and ability to control the tempo of the game. As of October 2023, he continues to compete at the professional level, contributing to his team's performance in domestic competitions.

Derrick Shaqueill Green (born February 21, 1994) is an American former football running back. He was rated by Rivals.com and Scout.com as the No. 1 running back in the country and by Rivals.com as the No. 8 overall player in the Class of 2013. He played for the Michigan Wolverines football football team from 2013 to 2015, amassing 898 yards and scoring seven touchdowns on 212 carries. In January 2016, the University of Michigan granted Green a transfer release, enabling him to continue his football career at another institution following his graduation in spring 2016.

Derrick Green is a former American football running back born on December 16, 1992, in Richmond, Virginia. He played college football at the University of Michigan, where he was a prominent player from 2013 to 2015. Green was known for his powerful running style and ability to break tackles. He was highly regarded as a recruit out of high school and joined Michigan as part of their 2012 recruiting class. After completing his collegiate career, Green went undrafted in the 2016 NFL Draft but signed with the Indianapolis Colts, later spending time with the team’s practice squad. He had a brief career in professional football and has since moved on from the sport.
Leonid Labzov (born October 23, 1973) is a Russian and Soviet former professional ice hockey defenceman. He is a one-time Russian Champion.

Leonid Labzov (born 12 January 1960) is a Russian mathematician known for his contributions to functional analysis and topology. He received his doctorate from Moscow State University and has held various academic positions, including teaching at institutions in Russia and abroad. Labzov's research focuses on topological vector spaces and their applications in analysis. He has published numerous papers and is recognized for his work on the structure and properties of functional spaces. In addition to his academic pursuits, Labzov has been involved in mathematical education and fostering international collaborations in the field.

Dominic Holden is an American journalist. He was National Lesbian and Gay Journalists Association's 2016 Journalist of the Year Award awardee, and one of The Advocate's 50 most influential LGBTs in America in 2017. He was director of Seattle Hempfest and an editor at Seattle's The Stranger alternative newspaper for six years. From 2015 until June 2020 he wrote for BuzzFeed News. Holden appeared in the 2013 documentary Evergreen: The Road to Legalization. In 2019, The New York Times reported that he was one of the leaders of an effort to unionize employees at Buzzfeed. His father, Ronald Holden, is a Seattle food writer, who worked at KING-TV and was executive editor at the other Seattle alt-weekly, Seattle Weekly.

Dominic Holden is an American journalist and editor known for his work in digital media, particularly focusing on issues related to LGBTQ+ rights, social justice, and politics. He gained prominence as a reporter for BuzzFeed News, where he covered significant stories surrounding the U.S. Supreme Court's rulings on same-sex marriage and the broader implications of LGBTQ+ rights in American society. Holden has received multiple accolades for his journalism, including recognition from the GLAAD Media Awards. In addition to his reporting, he has contributed to discussions on the role of media in shaping public perception of marginalized communities.

Castore e Polluce (Castor and Pollux) is an opera seria by Francesco Bianchi. The libretto was one translated by Carlo Innocenzo Frugoni, from Pierre-Joseph Bernard's French text for Rameau's Castor et Pollux. The opera was extravagantly in the French style. As Marita P. McClymonds explains, "Castore contains all the elements that had been purged from Italian serious opera before the turn of the century: gods appearing in machines, miraculous scene changes, arias without exit, much use of chorus, and an infernal scene in the underworld with dancing."

Castore e Polluce, known as Castor and Pollux in Greek mythology, are the twin brothers who symbolize friendship and bravery. They are the sons of Leda and, according to different accounts, Zeus, who fathered Pollux, or Tyndareus, who fathered Castor. The twins are often associated with the constellation Gemini, representing their close bond. Castor is famed for his skills in horsemanship and boxing, while Pollux is celebrated as a boxer and a capable warrior. In Roman mythology, they are revered as protectors of sailors and were worshipped with various festivals. The brothers’ story intertwines themes of loyalty and sacrifice, highlighted by Pollux's decision to share his immortality with Castor after his untimely death.

The Ministers of the French National Convention were appointed on 10 August 1792 after the French Legislative Assembly suspended King Louis XVI and revoked the ministers that he had named. On 12 Germinal year II (1 April 1794) Lazare Carnot proposed to suppress the executive council and the six ministers, replacing the ministers with twelve Committees reporting to the Committee of Public Safety. The proposal was unanimously adopted by the National Convention.
topic: Ministers of the French National Convention

text: The Ministers of the French National Convention were a group of officials who served during the National Convention, the governing body of France from 1792 to 1795 during the French Revolution. The Convention was established after the fall of the monarchy and was responsible for significant political and social transformations in France, including the declaration of the First French Republic. The ministers, appointed by the Convention, executed its policies and managed various government departments. Key figures included Maximilien Robespierre, Georges Danton, and Jean-Paul Marat, who played prominent roles in shaping revolutionary legislation and directing the Revolution's anti-monarchical policies. The period was marked by intense political factionalism, leading to conflicts such as the Reign of Terror, during which the ministers often faced immense pressure for their decisions. The National Convention ultimately dissolved in 1795, paving the way for the establishment of the Directory, which signified a shift in governance following the revolutionary upheaval.
topic: Ministers of the French National Convention

text: Sphenophryne stenodactyla is a species of frog in the family Microhylidae. It is endemic to Papua New Guinea and known from the New Guinea Highlands in the Western Highlands, Chimbu, Eastern Highlands Provinces at elevations between 2,490 and 4,000 m (8,170 and 13,120 ft) above sea level. The specific name stenodactyla is derived from the Greek words stenos meaning "narrow" and dactylos meaning "digit".

Sphenophryne stenodactyla, commonly known as the narrow-fingered frog, is a species of frog in the family Microhylidae. It is endemic to Papua New Guinea and is primarily found in forest habitats, particularly near streams and wetlands. The species is characterized by its small size and distinctive morphology, with slender fingers adapted for its environment. Sphenophryne stenodactyla is known for its relatively secretive behavior and can often be found concealed among leaf litter or vegetation. Its conservation status is currently listed as of concern due to habitat loss and degradation in its natural range. Research on its ecology and life history is limited, emphasizing the need for further studies to understand its population dynamics and environmental threats.

Soyuz 11 (Russian: Союз 11, lit. 'Union 11') was the only crewed mission to board the world's first space station, Salyut 1. The crew, Georgy Dobrovolsky, Vladislav Volkov, and Viktor Patsayev, arrived at the space station on 7 June 1971, and departed on 29 June 1971. The mission ended in disaster when the crew capsule depressurised during preparations for re-entry, killing the three-person crew. The three crew members of Soyuz 11 are the only humans to have died in space.

Soyuz 11 was a Soviet space mission that launched on June 6, 1971, as part of the Soyuz program. It was notable for being the first crewed mission to dock with a space station, specifically the Salyut 1. The crew consisted of commander Georgi Dobrovolski, flight engineer Viktor Patsayev, and research engineer Vladislav Volkov. The mission successfully spent 23 days in space, during which the crew conducted scientific experiments aboard Salyut 1. Tragically, the mission ended in disaster on June 30, 1971, when the crew died during re-entry due to cabin depressurization caused by a design flaw in the Soyuz capsule's re-entry system. Soyuz 11 remains the only crewed mission to have resulted in multiple fatalities in space, leading to significant design changes and safety improvements in subsequent missions. The incident highlighted the dangers of space travel and the need for rigorous safety protocols.

CER (Serbian: Цифарски Електронски Рачунар – Digital Electronic Computer) model 12 was a third-generation digital computer developed by Mihajlo Pupin Institute (Serbia) in 1971 and intended for "business and statistical data processing" (see ref. Lit. #1 and #4). However, the manufacturer also stated, at the time, that having in mind its architecture and performance, it can also be used successfully in solving "wide array of scientific and technical issues" (ref. Lit.#2 and #3). Computer CER-12 consisted of multiple modules connected via wire wrap and connectors.

CER-12 is a synthetic compound classified as a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) and is primarily investigated for its potential antidepressant and anxiolytic properties. It operates by modulating serotonin levels in the brain, which may help alleviate symptoms of depression and anxiety disorders.
