Research into CER-12 includes preclinical studies that evaluate its efficacy, pharmacokinetics, and safety profile. As of October 2023, CER-12 has not yet reached widespread clinical use, and its development is ongoing within pharmaceutical research.

The Royal Pump Room Gardens is a popular open space found in the centre of Leamington Spa, Warwickshire, England, next to the Royal Pump Rooms and just north of the River Leam. Despite being named "gardens" there is only one 5.4-acre (22,000 m2) area divided by footpaths with an ironwork bandstand in the centre. Opened in 1814 with the Pump Rooms themselves, to begin with, the gardens were only for the use of patrons of the Pump Rooms "to afford them pleasant promenades." The original bandstand was later erected and bands played in the afternoon and evening during the summer (and other public holidays) for those paying to use the baths. During these early days the famed tightrope walker Charles Blondin crossed the area in July 1851. However in 1875 the gardens were opened to the public. In 1881 plans were made to build the town hall on the gardens but at the last minute a change of site was agreed and it was built further north near The Regent Hotel. In 1893 a pedestrian bridge called York Bridge was built over the river. The second bandstand was designed by Walter MacFarlane & Co and founded in Glasgow. It was installed in 1896. Also the only remaining examples of the original Leamington cast iron gas lamps can be found alongside the south side of the gardens. The gardens originally contained decorative flower beds but with the decline in fortunes of the Pump Rooms themselves these have been grassed over. Bands still play in the bandstand, although very infrequently but the gardens host the annual Leamington Peace Festival, a fun fair once a year and farmers markets once a month. It is also a popular place for young people, especially those from the nearby Warwick University to sunbathe and play football. As it lies so close to the river it is part of the town that is most frequently flooded. The last two times such floods occurred were at Easter 1998 and in the summer of 2007. In March 2012 new lights in iron arches (commonly known as the Linden Arches) were erected along the pathway furthest from the river. They were officially unveiled by Prince Richard, Duke of Gloucester. This is the first major alteration to the park's superficial appearance since 1896. This was followed later in the decade when the bandstand was removed in autumn 2018 and returned re-furbished and painted different colours in spring 2019. The paths were slightly re-routed and totally re-paved and a new seating area with rock features and a small pond was constructed at the western end of the gardens. The £1.4 million project also included new railings and new trees being planted.

The Royal Pump Room Gardens are a public park located in Harrogate, North Yorkshire, England. Established in the 19th century, the gardens are adjacent to the Royal Pump Room, which originally served as a spa facility receiving visitors seeking the therapeutic benefits of mineral water. The gardens feature a mix of ornamental flowerbeds, pathways, and mature trees, along with sculptures and water features, contributing to their Victorian charm. They are a significant part of Harrogate's heritage, providing a recreational space that attracts both tourists and locals. The gardens are also the site for various events and festivals throughout the year.
Manganism or manganese poisoning is a toxic condition resulting from chronic exposure to manganese. It was first identified in 1837 by James Couper.

Manganism is a neurological disorder associated with prolonged exposure to manganese, a naturally occurring metal. It primarily affects individuals in industries involving manganese mining, processing, or welding. Symptoms of manganism include tremors, changes in mood and behavior, muscular rigidity, and difficulties with balance and coordination, resembling Parkinson's disease. The condition is the result of manganese accumulation in the brain, particularly in the basal ganglia, which disrupts dopaminergic function. Diagnosis typically involves clinical evaluation, history of exposure, and imaging studies. Prevention strategies focus on reducing occupational exposure, while treatment may include medications to manage symptoms and supportive care.

Cyril C. Perera (3 June 1923 – 4 September 2016) was a Sri Lankan author of Sinhala literature who was well known for his translations of world literature into Sinhalese. His translations included novels, short stories, poems, stage plays, and children's literature. Perera was born in Colombo and attended various schools there. He studied art, architecture, and building engineering at Colombo Technical College and later joined government service. He worked as a building designer in the Ministry of Health.

Cyril C. Perera is a Sri Lankan-born British businessman and entrepreneur known for his contributions to the fields of technology and telecommunications. He has held various leadership roles in multinational companies and has been instrumental in promoting innovations within the industry. Perera is recognized for his work in developing digital communication systems and has spoken at numerous conferences about the impact of technology on business practices. His career is marked by a commitment to enhancing productivity through technological advancements and fostering international business relationships.

Liu Lunxian (simplified Chinese: 刘伦贤; traditional Chinese: 劉倫賢; pinyin: Liú Lúnxián; December 1943 – 4 December 2017) was a Chinese politician and military officer. Liu was promoted to the rank of major general (shaojiang) in September 1988 and lieutenant general (zhongjiang) in July 1993. He was a delegate to the 13th and 14th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party and a deputy to 7th and 9th National People's Congress.

Liu Lunxian (刘伦羡) was a prominent Chinese painter born in the late 19th century, renowned for his contributions to traditional Chinese landscape painting. He is celebrated for his ability to blend classical techniques with innovative approaches, influencing a generation of artists in modern China. Liu's work often reflects the aesthetics of nature and philosophical undertones typical of Chinese art, making him a key figure in the revival of traditional styles during a period of Western influence in China. His paintings have been exhibited in numerous galleries and are part of several prestigious collections, contributing to his legacy in the art world.

The London, Midland and Scottish Railway (LMS) Fowler Class 4F is a class of 0-6-0 steam locomotive designed for medium freight work. They represent the ultimate development of the Midland Railway's six-coupled tender engines. Many trainspotters knew them as "Duck Sixes", a nickname derived from their wheel arrangement.

The LMS Fowler Class 4F is a British locomotive class designed by William Fowler for the London, Midland and Scottish Railway (LMS) in the 1920s. This class is a type of 0-6-0 freight locomotive, characterized by its six driving wheels and was primarily used for freight services. A total of 155 units were built between 1925 and 1935, featuring an all-steel construction and simple steam technology. The Class 4F was known for its robustness and versatility, becoming a workhorse for the LMS in various regions. Many of the locomotives remained in service during the British Railways era, and some have been preserved for heritage railways, reflecting their historical significance in British railway history.
Jayson Scott Musson is an artist who lives and works in Brooklyn, NY. He was born in Bronx, NY.

Jayson Musson is an American artist, writer, and curator known for his work that explores themes of identity, race, and socio-political issues. He gained prominence through his multimedia projects, including video, performance, and visual arts, often incorporating humor and personal narrative. Musson is also recognized for his role as an educator and for his contributions to contemporary art discussions, particularly through platforms such as his web series "Art Thoughtz," which critiques the art world and its conventions. His work has been exhibited in numerous galleries and institutions, contributing to conversations about the intersections of art and culture.

Isao Obata (小畑 功, Obata Isao; 1904–1976) was a pioneering Japanese master of Shotokan karate. He was a senior student of Gichin Funakoshi, who is widely recognized as the founder of modern karate, and was a key figure in the establishment of the Japan Karate Association (JKA) under Funakoshi in 1949. Obata also helped introduce karate to the United States of America through his demonstrations of the art to United States Air Force personnel, and through his students, most notably Tsutomu Ohshima.

Isao Obata (born February 12, 1945) is a renowned Japanese artist and designer, primarily recognized for his contributions to the fields of environmental art and installation. He gained prominence for his innovative approaches that blend traditional Japanese aesthetics with contemporary techniques. Obata's work often explores themes of nature and human interaction within urban settings, leading to significant installations in both public and private spaces. His art has been featured in numerous exhibitions worldwide, and he has received several awards for his contributions to contemporary art. Additionally, Obata is involved in art education, promoting interdisciplinary approaches and sustainability in artistic practices.

The 76th United States Congress was a meeting of the legislative branch of the United States federal government, composed of the United States Senate and the United States House of Representatives. It met in Washington, D.C., from January 3, 1939, to January 3, 1941, during the seventh and eighth years of Franklin D. Roosevelt's presidency. The apportionment of seats in the House of Representatives was based on the 1930 United States census. Both chambers had a Democratic majority - holding a supermajority in the Senate, but a greatly reduced majority in the House, thus losing the supermajority there. With President Roosevelt, the Democrats maintained an overall federal government trifecta. The 76th is also the most recent Congress to have held a third session.

The 76th United States Congress convened from January 3, 1939, to January 3, 1941, during the presidency of Franklin D. Roosevelt. This Congress was notable for its response to the lingering effects of the Great Depression and the rising tensions through the lead-up to World War II. Comprising the Senate and the House of Representatives, it included 48 Democratic Senators, 48 Republican Senators, and 1 Progressive Senator in the Senate, while the House featured a majority of Democrats. Significant legislation passed during this session included the Fair Labor Standards Act, which established minimum wage and overtime pay standards, and various measures aimed at economic recovery and social reform. The congressional composition reflected a closely divided political landscape, particularly in the Senate. The 76th Congress faced challenges including debates over foreign policy and military preparedness as global conflicts escalated.

"Bumpy Ride" is a soca song by Swedish-Congolese R&B singer-songwriter Mohombi from his debut studio album, MoveMeant (2011). It was released as his debut single in the United States on 24 August 2010. Written by RedOne, Bilal "The Chef" Hajji, AJ Junior, and Mohombi, and produced by RedOne, it is the first release on RedOne's label 2101 Records, a joint venture with Universal Music. He also released a popular bilingual French/English version for the Francophone markets. The song became a number-one hit in The Netherlands, and a top ten hit in Belgium, Czech Republic, Denmark, Finland, France, Norway, Poland, Slovakia, and Sweden.

"Bumpy Ride" is a term that typically refers to a rough or uncomfortable journey, either literally or metaphorically. In transportation, it can describe a vehicle’s unstable movement due to poor road conditions or mechanical issues. In a figurative sense, it often denotes challenging experiences or tumultuous situations in various contexts, such as personal relationships, business ventures, or artistic endeavors. Additionally, "Bumpy Ride" has been used as a title for various songs, films, and other media, often emphasizing themes of struggle and resilience.

Dan Inosanto (born July 24, 1936) is an American martial arts instructor and actor. Inosanto holds instructor or black belt level ranks in several martial arts. He has studied traditional Karate, Judo, Jujutsu, Kung Fu, Shooto, Muay Thai, Tai Chi, Arnis, and Jeet Kune Do. He was one of three people who were appointed to teach at one of the three Jun Fan Gung Fu institutes under Bruce Lee, the other two being Taky Kimura and James Yimm Lee. After Bruce Lee's death, Inosanto became the principal spokesperson and historian for Jeet Kune Do. Inosanto is credited for training martial arts to a number of Hollywood actors including Bruce Lee, Chuck Norris and others. He has had minor roles in a number of films, including Lee's uncompleted last film Game of Death (1972), and Steven Seagal's Out for Justice (1991).

Dan Inosanto is an American martial artist, instructor, and actor, known for his expertise in Filipino martial arts and his significant influence on the development of mixed martial arts (MMA). Born on July 24, 1936, in Stockton, California, Inosanto was a student of Bruce Lee and later became one of the few individuals to be certified as a full instructor in Jeet Kune Do, the martial art developed by Lee. He is the founder of the Inosanto Academy of Martial Arts in Los Angeles and has contributed to promoting Filipino martial arts, including Eskrima and Silat. Inosanto has appeared in various films and television series, often showcasing his martial arts skills. He is regarded as a prominent figure in the martial arts community for his teaching and contributions to the evolution of combat sports.

A tablao (colloquial term for the Spanish "tablado", floorboard) is a place where flamenco shows are performed and also tablao is the term used for the platform floor in which a flamenco dancer dances. Tablao venues were developed during the 1960s throughout Spain replacing the cafés cantantes (cabarets).

A tablao is a venue specifically designed for the performance of flamenco music and dance, primarily in Spain. These establishments offer a stage for artists to showcase their talents in an intimate setting, often accompanied by traditional Spanish cuisine. Tablaos typically feature a mix of professional flamenco performers and musicians and are known for their vibrant atmosphere, providing audiences with an immersive cultural experience. Originating in the late 20th century, these venues have become popular tourist attractions, contributing to the global appreciation of flamenco as an art form. Notable tablaos include Casa Patas in Madrid and El Palacio Andaluz in Seville.

The 1930–31 Buffalo Bisons men's basketball team represented the University of Buffalo during the 1930–31 NCAA college men's basketball season. The head coach was Art Powell, coaching his sixteenth season with the Bisons.

The 1930–31 Buffalo Bisons men's basketball team represented the University of Buffalo during the 1930-31 NCAA basketball season. The team was a member of the Western New York Basketball Conference and played its home games at the Buffalo Memorial Auditorium. Coached by J. Edward Boor, the Bisons recorded a season with a competitive performance, contributing to the growing popularity of college basketball in the region. The team's roster included several standout players, and they competed against various local and regional opponents throughout the season. The 1930–31 season is noted as a significant period in the history of the Buffalo Bisons basketball program.

Rosalie Verner (16 December 1880 Avanduse Parish (now Väike-Maarja Parish), Kreis Wierland – ?) was an Estonian politician. She was a member of III Riigikogu. On 21 May 1926, she resigned her position and she was replaced by August Luik.

Rosalie Verner is a notable Canadian artist and educator known for her contributions to contemporary visual arts. Born in 1985 in Toronto, Ontario, she studied fine arts at OCAD University, where she developed her distinctive style that often incorporates themes of identity, community, and social engagement. Throughout her career, Verner has participated in numerous exhibitions across Canada and internationally, receiving acclaim for her innovative use of mixed media and her commitment to socially relevant topics. In addition to her artistic practice, she holds teaching positions at various institutions, where she emphasizes the importance of creativity and critical thinking in art education. Verner's work has been featured in both public and private collections, further solidifying her influence within the Canadian art scene.
Gerriadai was a town of ancient Ionia, near Teos. Its site is located near the modern Sığacık Liman, Asiatic Turkey.

Gerridae, commonly known as water striders or pond skaters, is a family of insects that belong to the order Hemiptera. They are notable for their ability to walk on the surface of water due to their elongated bodies and specialized legs, which distribute their weight and exploit surface tension. Gerridae predominantly inhabit freshwater environments such as ponds, streams, and marshes. They are characterized by their predatory behavior, feeding on other small insects and aquatic organisms. Water striders are found globally, except in extreme polar regions, and play an essential role in aquatic ecosystems as both predators and prey. Their unique locomotion and adaptations for life on water have made them subjects of interest in studies of biomechanics and ecology.
Elwill is a given name and surname. Notable people with the name include: Elwill M. Shanahan (1912–1983), American politician Elwill baronets John Elwill (disambiguation), multiple people

Elwill is a notable name or term that may refer to various subjects, including individuals, brands, or fictional characters, although specific information is limited. There is no widely recognized entry under this name as of the latest updates in 2023. Further context is required to provide a detailed summary, as "Elwill" could pertain to different domains or cultures.
Helena Stetkiewicz or Olena Vyhovska (Ukrainian: Олена Виговська; died 1664) was the second wife of Ivan Vyhovsky, the hetman of the Zaporozhian Host (1657–1659) in the Cossack Hetmanate.

Helena Stetkiewicz (born June 8, 1990) is a Polish contemporary artist known for her innovative work in mixed media and installation art. She explores themes of identity, memory, and cultural heritage, often incorporating elements of traditional Polish craftsmanship into her pieces. Stetkiewicz studied at the Academy of Fine Arts in Warsaw and has exhibited her work in various galleries across Europe and the United States. Her contributions to the contemporary art scene have garnered critical acclaim, and she is recognized for her unique approach to engaging audiences with thought-provoking narratives.

Brendan Keogh (born 8 December 1970) in Penrith, New South Wales is an Australian Paralympic swimming coach who has over 20 years of swimming coaching experience. He has been an Australian coach at five successive Summer Paralympic Games - 2000, 2004, 2008, 2012 and 2016. As of 2015, he is the lead coach of the Australian Paralympic Swimming Team along with Swimming Australia Paralympic performance manager Adam Pine. He was appointed head coach for the Australian Swim Team at the 2016 Rio Paralympics.

Brendan Keogh is an Australian professional football (soccer) coach known for his contributions to youth development and coaching in the sport. He gained recognition for his work with various clubs and academy programs, focusing on nurturing talent and implementing effective training regimes. Keogh's coaching philosophy emphasizes the importance of technical skills, tactical understanding, and the personal growth of players. Throughout his career, he has held positions in multiple football organizations, contributing to the advancement of coaching standards in Australian football. His impact on the game continues to be felt through the players he has mentored and the programs he has developed.
Mark Richards Clapp (March 3, 1803 – April 23, 1891) was an American farmer and territorial legislator.

Mark R. Clapp is an American biostatistician and academic recognized for his contributions to statistical methods in public health and epidemiology. He holds a faculty position at the University of Kentucky, where he engages in research involving statistical modeling and analysis of health data. Clapp's work often focuses on improving methodologies for assessing health risks and outcomes, contributing to both theoretical advancements and practical applications in health sciences. He has published numerous articles in peer-reviewed journals and is a member of various professional organizations in the fields of statistics and epidemiology.
Skrova is a small island group and village area in Vågan Municipality in Nordland county, Norway.

Skrova is a small island located in the Lofoten archipelago of Norway, primarily known for its scenic beauty and fishing heritage. The island is part of the Vågan municipality in Nordland county and is situated near the larger island of Flakstadøya. Skrova has a population of around 200 residents, who are engaged in various activities including fishing, tourism, and artisanal crafts. The island is famous for its picturesque landscapes, white sandy beaches, and a vibrant marine ecosystem, making it a popular destination for outdoor enthusiasts and photographers. Transport links to Skrova are provided by ferries connecting it to the mainland and nearby islands.

Miriam Matthews (August 6, 1905 – June 23, 2003) was an American librarian, advocate for intellectual freedom, historian, and art collector. In 1927, Matthews became the first credentialed African American librarian to be hired by the Los Angeles Public Library.

Miriam Matthews (born 1915, date of death unknown) was an American artist known for her contributions to the field of painting and drawing. She gained recognition for her use of color and innovative techniques in her artwork, which often drew from themes of nature and personal experiences. Matthews was active in various art communities throughout her career, participating in exhibitions and collaborations that showcased her talent. While specific details about her life and career remain limited, she is remembered for her impact on local art scenes and her influence on emerging artists. Her works are part of several private collections and have been displayed in local galleries.

Carlos Alberto Vives Restrepo (born 7 August 1961) is a Colombian singer, songwriter and actor. One of the best-selling Latin music artists ever, with over 20 million records sold worldwide, Vives is regarded as one of the most influential artists in the world as he has progressively helped vallenato gain popularity globally and for his interpretation of traditional music styles, such as cumbia, champeta, bambuco and porro, with Latin pop, rock, reggaeton, dance-pop and tropical music. Through his career he was honored with two Grammy Awards and eighteen Latin Grammy Awards, being awarded with the Latin Recording Academy Person of the Year in 2024. Vives has been honored with several accolades for his songwriting and producer contribution including the BMI President’s Award, the ASCAP Founders Award, and an induction into the Billboard Latin Music Hall of Fame. In 2019, Vives was selected as number 45 on both the Greatest of All Time Latin Artists and Top Latin Artists 2010s by Billboard. He worked with some of the most influential artists in the Latin music scene, including Ricky Martin, Shakira, Wisin, Daddy Yankee, Alejandro Sanz, Camilo, Sebastián Yatra, Mau y Ricky, Michel Teló, Gente de Zona, Carlos Rivera, Silvestre Dangond and Juanes. Vives is also a successful actor. His roles as the titular character in the soap opera Gallito Ramírez and as Rafael Escalona in Escalona, a story about the famous Colombian composer of the same name, are among his most important and well-remembered appearances.

Carlos Vives is a Colombian singer, songwriter, and actor, born on August 7, 1961, in Santa Marta, Colombia. He is renowned for his contributions to Colombian music, particularly in the genres of Vallenato and pop. Vives gained popularity in the 1990s with hits such as "La Gota Fría" and "Fruta Fresca," incorporating traditional Colombian rhythms into contemporary music. His career has been marked by numerous awards, including Grammy and Latin Grammy Awards. In addition to his musical career, Vives has also appeared in telenovelas and is known for his philanthropic work in education and cultural preservation in Colombia. He remains an influential figure in Latin music, advocating for Colombian culture on a global scale.

Hectobrocha adoxa, the unadorned footman, is a species of moth of the subfamily Arctiinae first described by Edward Meyrick in 1886. It is known from the Australian states of New South Wales and Victoria.

Hectobrocha adoxa is a species of moth belonging to the family Noctuidae, commonly known as owlet moths. It was first described by the entomologist Alfred E. G. McDunnough in 1940. The species is primarily found in the United States, with a distribution that includes various regions across the country. Hectobrocha adoxa is known for its distinct wing patterns and colors, which assist in camouflage against predators. Like other moths in its family, it is nocturnal and attracts to artificial lights at night. The ecology, behavior, and life cycle of Hectobrocha adoxa reflect typical characteristics of nocturnal lepidopterans.
BaadAsssss Cinema is a 2002 TV documentary film directed by Isaac Julien. Julien looks at the Blaxploitation era of the 1970s in this hour-long documentary.

BaadAsssss Cinema is a 2002 independent film directed by Mario Van Peebles, which serves as both a biographical portrayal of the life and career of his father, Melvin Van Peebles, and a homage to the genre of blaxploitation cinema. The film follows Melvin's journey to create the groundbreaking film "Sweet Sweetback's Baadasssss Song" in the early 1970s, navigating challenges such as financial struggles, racial tensions, and the innovative spirit of the era. It intertwines personal anecdotes with historical context, highlighting the influence of the Van Peebles family and the broader cultural implications of the blaxploitation movement. The film features a mix of dramatization and documentary elements, showcasing the legacy of Melvin Van Peebles as a pivotal figure in American cinema.

Withius is a genus of pseudoscorpions in the Withiidae family. It was described in 1911 by English zoologist Harry Wallis Kew. The generic name Withius honours Danish arachnologist Carl Johannes With (1877–1923).

Withius is a historical figure known primarily in the context of medieval scholarship and theology. He was a scholar of the 7th century, often recognized for his contributions to ecclesiastical literature, particularly through his writings on theological matters and his role in the preservation and transmission of classical knowledge during the early Middle Ages. His works, although not extensively preserved, played a part in the development of Christian thought in Europe. Withius is often studied in discussions about the intellectual climate of the early medieval period and the impact of monastic scholarship in the Carolingian Renaissance.
Cyrtomostoma is a genus of picture-winged flies in the family Ulidiidae.

Cyrtomostoma is a genus of parasitic flatworms belonging to the class Trematoda, specifically within the family Fasciolidae. These trematodes are primarily known for their lifecycle, which typically involves a snail as an intermediate host, before infecting vertebrate hosts. Members of the genus are characterized by their flattened bodies and complex reproductive systems, which include both sexual and asexual reproduction stages. Cyrtomostoma species have been studied for their ecological importance and potential impacts on host populations, as well as their implications in veterinary and medical parasitology. The genus is notable for its adaptations to parasitism, including specialized attachment structures and the ability to evade host immune responses.
Liugongli station (Chinese: 六公里站; pinyin: Liùgōnglǐ zhàn) is a station on Line 3 of Chongqing Rail Transit in Chongqing Municipality, China. It is located in Nan'an District. It opened in 2011.

Liugongli station is a railway station located in the Haidian District of Beijing, China. It serves as part of the Beijing Subway system, specifically on Line 10. The station opened to the public on December 30, 2009, enhancing access to various areas within the city. It features standard subway amenities and is designed to facilitate the transit of passengers in the rapidly growing urban environment of Beijing. The station is situated near important local landmarks, contributing to its significance in the city's public transportation network.

The IBM System/32 (IBM 5320) introduced in January 1975 was a midrange computer with built-in display screen, disk drives, printer, and database report software. It was used primarily by small to midsize businesses for accounting applications. RPG II was the primary programming language for the machine.

The IBM System/32 was a midrange computer system introduced by IBM in 1975, primarily designed for small to medium-sized businesses. It featured a 16-bit architecture and utilized the IBM System/3 operating system, supporting both batch and timesharing processing. The System/32 was notable for its integrated hardware and software, including disk storage and peripherals, which facilitated ease of use and maintenance. It was aimed at providing businesses with more affordable computing solutions compared to larger mainframe systems. The hardware architecture allowed for a variety of applications, ranging from payroll to inventory management. The System/32 was eventually succeeded by the IBM System/34 and later models, reflecting the evolution of IBM’s midrange computing strategy. Production of the System/32 continued until 1982, during which time it helped establish IBM’s presence in the small business computer market.

Barry Pyle (born June 17, 1965) is an associate professor at Eastern Michigan University in the United States who has written on the American judicial system. Along with Elaine Martin he has written "Gender and Racial Diversification of State Supreme Courts". This article was noted by Political Science Quarterly as "one of the best and most comprehensive works on ... the jurisprudence and gender stratification pervasive in the United States legal system." Pyle attained his undergraduate education at Indiana University Bloomington receiving his degree in political science. He then received his Masters and Ph.D. from University of Missouri–St. Louis.

Barry Pyle is an American swimmer known for his achievements in competitive swimming during the 1970s. He specialized in butterfly and individual medley events and gained recognition as a member of the United States national team. Pyle competed in several prestigious swimming competitions, including the Pan American Games and NCAA championships, earning multiple medals and setting records in his discipline. His contributions to the sport and noteworthy performances have made him a significant figure in American swimming history.
Foxhills is a golf club and resort located in Ottershaw, Surrey, United Kingdom. It was established in 1975 and named for Charles James Fox on whose former estate it's located.

Foxhills Golf Club is a prestigious golf and country club located in Ottershaw, Surrey, England. Established in 1975, it features two 18-hole championship courses, the Longcross and the Bernard Hunt course, catering to golfers of varying skill levels. The club offers a range of facilities including a golf academy, a health and fitness center, and accommodation options. Foxhills has hosted numerous tournaments, including qualifying events for The Open Championship, and is known for its commitment to golf development and sustainability initiatives within the sport. The club also provides members with a vibrant social environment, various dining options, and recreational amenities beyond golf.

Thyine wood is a 15th-century English name for a wood from the tree known botanically as Tetraclinis articulata (syn. Callitris quadrivalvis, Thuja articulata). The name is derived from the Greek word thuon, "fragrant wood," or possibly thuein, “to sacrifice”, and it was so called because it was burnt in sacrifices, on account of its fragrance. In Rome, wood from this tree was called citrum, "citrus wood". It was considered very valuable, and was used for making articles of furniture by the Greeks and Romans. Craftsmen who worked in citrus wood and ivory had their own guild (collegium). Thyine wood is mentioned in the King James Version of the Bible at Revelation 18:12 as being among the articles which would cease to be purchased when Babylon fell. The New International Version translates the passage "citron wood"; the Amplified Bible translates it as "scented wood". This wood is also mentioned in the 1st Book of Kings, chapter 10, in a list of items brought to Solomon by the navy of Hiram. The resin from this wood is used as the basis for euparal, a mounting medium used in microscopy.

Thyine wood, also known as Thuyra wood, is a rare and valuable timber derived from the tree species of the genus Tetraclinis, commonly referred to as thuya. Primarily found in the Mediterranean region, particularly in Morocco, this wood is notable for its durability, attractive grain, and aromatic properties. Historically, thyine wood was highly prized in ancient times for its use in luxury items, furniture, shipbuilding, and religious artifacts. Despite its historical significance, the timber is now less commonly used due to overexploitation and the conservation status of the thuyra tree. Its distinctive qualities continue to attract interest from artisans and woodworkers seeking unique materials.

Por Intharapalit (Thai: ป. อินทรปาลิต) was the pen name of Pricha Intharapalit (Thai: ปรีชา อินทรปาลิต; 12 May 1910 – 25 September 1968), a Thai humorist and writer. Among his works was the Samgler (Thai: สามเกลอ, lit. 'three chums') series of comic short stories, of which he wrote nearly 2,000. He also wrote a novelisation of the battle of Bang Rajan. These works have been cited by Thai scholars among the 100 books that must be read by Thais. The first book in the Samgler series, Ai Phuying (Thai: อายผู้หญิง; lit. 'shy around women'), was written in 1938, and immediately became a best-seller, selling 20,000 copies in its first week. The stories all revolve around three playboy friends and their families. The series seen by historians and literary experts as providing witty glimpses of Bangkok high society from 1938 to 1968, making the mention over the decades of actual restaurants, nightclubs, and celebrities.

Por Intharapalit is a renowned Thai film director and screenwriter, known for his significant contributions to the Thai cinema industry since the late 20th century. Born on February 21, 1974, in Bangkok, Thailand, Intharapalit gained recognition for his innovative storytelling and visual style, often incorporating elements of Thai culture and social themes into his works. His filmography includes a mix of commercial hits and critically acclaimed films, earning him accolades at various film festivals. Intharapalit's works are noted for their unique perspective on contemporary issues and their ability to connect with both Thai and international audiences.

Kart Food Industries Sdn Bhd (doing business as Kart's) is a Malaysian halal-certified frozen food suppliers company established since 1988, specialising in Malaysian cultures food and Western-snacks. The company started as a small home-based industry and now expands globally. It is one of the Malaysia's largest chilled and frozen snacks companies.

Karts, often referred to as go-karts, are small, low-slung vehicles designed for racing on tracks or for recreational purposes. They typically feature an open design with a simple chassis, four wheels, and a variety of engine types, from electric to small gasoline engines. Karts are commonly used in motorsport for beginner training, competitive racing events, and leisure activities. They vary in size and speed, with some specialized models designed for professional racing and others for casual driving. Karting has become a popular sport worldwide, serving as a stepping stone for drivers aspiring to progress to higher levels of motorsport, including Formula racing. The sport is governed by various organizations, and numerous karting championships are held globally.

The Feast of Orthodoxy (or Sunday of Orthodoxy or Triumph of Orthodoxy) is celebrated on the first Sunday of Great Lent in the Eastern Orthodox Church and other churches using the Byzantine Rite to commemorate, originally, only the final defeat of iconoclasm on the first Sunday of Lent in 843, and later also opposition to all heterodoxy.

The Feast of Orthodoxy, celebrated on the first Sunday of Great Lent in the Eastern Orthodox Church, commemorates the restoration of the veneration of icons in 843 AD following the period of Byzantine Iconoclasm. This feast highlights the significance of icons in Orthodox Christian worship and doctrine. It marks the triumph of the Orthodox faith and the decisions made by the Seventh Ecumenical Council, which affirmed the use of religious images. The celebration typically includes a procession with icons, prayers, and a liturgical service emphasizing the teachings and traditions of the Orthodox faith.

The 2002–03 Colorado Buffaloes Men's basketball team represented the University of Colorado as a member of the Big 12 Conference during the 2002–03 season. Led by 7th-year head coach Ricardo Patton, the Buffaloes played their home games at the Coors Events Center in Boulder, Colorado. After finishing 4th in the conference regular season standings, Colorado reached the semifinal round of the Big 12 tournament. The team did receive an at-large bid to the NCAA tournament as No. 10 seed in the South region – the program's first NCAA tournament selection in six seasons. Colorado would lose to Michigan State in the opening round to finish the season with a 20–12 record (9–7 Big 12).

The 2002–03 Colorado Buffaloes men's basketball team represented the University of Colorado in the 2002–03 NCAA Division I basketball season. Under the leadership of head coach Ricardo Patton, the team played its home games at the Coors Events Center in Boulder, Colorado. The Buffaloes finished the season with an overall record of 19 wins and 12 losses, and they achieved a conference record of 10 wins and 6 losses in the Big 12 Conference. The team participated in the 2003 NCAA Tournament after a successful season but was eliminated in the first round. Key players included David Harrison, who was an impactful center for the team, contributing significantly to their performance throughout the season.
Rushden & Diamonds F.C. were a Football Conference club based in Irthlingborough, Northamptonshire, England.

Rushden & Diamonds F.C. was an English football club formed in 1992 through the merger of Rushden Town and Irthlingborough Diamonds. The club, based in Rushden, Northamptonshire, achieved rapid success, progressing from the United Counties League to the Football Conference within a decade. They reached the peak of their success in 2000, winning the Conference and earning promotion to the Football League Third Division. However, financial difficulties and mismanagement plagued the club in the late 2000s, leading to its eventual dissolution in 2011. A community-based club, AFC Rushden & Diamonds, was subsequently formed by supporters, continuing the legacy of the original club at a lower level in the English football pyramid.

The Austrian Volley League Men is a men's volleyball competition organized by the Austrian Volleyball Federation (ÖVV), it was created in 1952. It is currently a 10 teams league running from September/October to April/May. The season starts with a Supercup match between the League and the Cup champions. The regular season is followed by playoffs, with the winner crowned as Austrian champions.

The Austrian Volley League Men (AVL) is the top professional men's volleyball league in Austria, established in 1979. It operates on a system of promotion and relegation with the lower division, known as the Austrian Volleyball Second League. The league comprises several teams competing annually for the national title, and it typically runs from October to May. Dominant clubs in the AVL's history include SK Posojilnica Aich/Dob, Hypo Tirol Innsbruck, and TV Melk. The league plays a crucial role in promoting volleyball within Austria and serves as a platform for players to showcase their skills at a national level.

"Kids Again" is a 2014 song by British recording artist Example. It was released as the second single from his fifth studio album, Live Life Living, on 16 March 2014 in the United Kingdom by Epic Records. The song is written and produced by Example, Alf Bamford, and Fraser T Smith.

"Kids Again" is a song by American singer-songwriter A.J. McLean, released as part of his solo project. Colobogynium variegatum is a species of flowering plant in the arum family, Araceae. It is the sole species in genus Colobogynium. It is a perennial or rhizomatous geophyte native to Borneo and the Riau Islands.

Colobogynium is a genus of botryoidal, freshwater diatoms belonging to the family Caloneis, characterized by unique morphological features including a distinct valve structure. These diatoms are typically found in various aquatic environments, including rivers, lakes, and wetlands, and play a significant role in the ecosystem as primary producers. Their siliceous cell walls and intricate patterns contribute to the biodiversity of microalgae in freshwater systems. Colobogynium contributes to the study of diatom taxonomy and ecology, aiding in the assessment of water quality and environmental monitoring. The genus was first described in the early 20th century and continues to be of interest for both ecological research and paleolimnology.

The U visa is a United States nonimmigrant visa which is set aside for victims of crimes (and their immediate family members) who have suffered substantial mental or physical abuse while in the U.S. and who are willing to assist law enforcement and government officials in the investigation or prosecution of the criminal activity. It permits such victims to enter or remain in the US when they might not otherwise be able to do so. An advantage that comes along with the acceptance of a U-visa is the individual will have deportation protection which is important when they are collaborating with law enforcement. The US Congress created the U nonimmigrant visa with the passage of the Victims of Trafficking and Violence Protection Act (including the Battered Immigrant Women’s Protection Act) in October 2000. The legislation was intended to strengthen the ability of law enforcement agencies to investigate and prosecute cases of domestic violence, sexual assault, trafficking of people, and other crimes while, at the same time, offer protection to victims of such crimes. The legislation also helps law enforcement agencies to better serve victims of crimes.

The U visa is a non-immigrant visa in the United States designed to provide legal status to foreign nationals who are victims of certain crimes and have suffered substantial physical or mental abuse. It permits them to assist law enforcement in the investigation or prosecution of criminal activities. The U visa is available for victims of crimes such as domestic violence, sexual assault, human trafficking, and other specified offenses. It allows eligible individuals to remain in the U.S. for up to four years, with the possibility of applying for lawful permanent residency after three years of continuous presence. The program aims to strengthen the cooperation between law enforcement and immigrant communities while offering protection and support for vulnerable victims.

Jewish meditation includes practices of settling the mind, introspection, visualization, emotional insight, contemplation of divine names, or concentration on philosophical, ethical or mystical ideas. Meditation may accompany unstructured, personal Jewish prayer, may be part of structured Jewish services, or may be separate from prayer practices. Jewish mystics have viewed meditation as leading to devekut (cleaving to God). Hebrew terms for meditation include hitbodedut (or hisbodedus, literally "self-seclusion") or hitbonenut/hisbonenus ("contemplation"). Through the centuries, meditation practices have been developed in many movements, including among Maimonideans (Moses Maimonides and Abraham Maimonides), Kabbalists (Abraham Abulafia, Isaac the Blind, Azriel of Gerona, Moses Cordovero, Yosef Karo and Isaac Luria), Hasidic rabbis (Baal Shem Tov, Schneur Zalman of Liadi and Nachman of Breslov), Musar movement rabbis (Israel Salanter and Simcha Zissel Ziv), Conservative movement rabbis (Alan Lew), Reform movement rabbis (Lawrence Kushner and Rami Shapiro), and Reconstructionist movement rabbi (Shefa Gold).

Jewish meditation refers to various contemplative practices rooted in Jewish traditions that seek spiritual connection, self-awareness, and mindfulness. Historically, meditation in Judaism has been influenced by Kabbalistic mysticism and Hasidic spirituality, often involving techniques such as visualization, repetition of sacred texts, and focused prayer. Key forms of Jewish meditation include Hitbodedut, a form of spontaneous prayer and dialogue with God, and the contemplative practices outlined in texts such as the "Tanya" by Rabbi Schneur Zalman of Liadi. Modern adaptations continue to explore the integration of mindfulness concepts with traditional Jewish practices, promoting a deeper spiritual experience. Jewish meditation is often viewed as a means to enhance personal growth, connect with divine principles, and foster communal spiritual practices.
Jonas Suyderhoef (1613 in Haarlem – 1686 in Haarlem), was a Dutch Golden Age engraver.

Jonas Suyderhoef (1600–1681) was a Dutch painter, known primarily for his contributions to the genre of still life and flower painting during the Dutch Golden Age. "Back Where You Belong" is a song by American rock band 38 Special and the second single from their sixth studio album Tour de Force (1983).

"Back Where You Belong" is a 1985 single by the American rock band .38 Special, featured on their album "Rock & Roll Strategy." The song showcases the band's signature blend of Southern rock and arena rock, characterized by its catchy melodies and anthemic chorus. Count Vladimir Alexandrovich Sollogub (Russian: Влади́мир Алекса́ндрович Соллогу́б; German: Woldemar Graf Sollogub (Sollohub); 20 August 1813 – 17 June 1882) was a minor Russian writer, author of novelettes, essays, plays, and memoirs. Born in Saint Petersburg, his paternal grandfather was a Polish aristocrat, and he grew up in the midst of high society. He graduated from the University of Dorpat in 1834 and was attached to the Ministry of Internal Affairs the following year in Vienna. His literary career began in 1837 in the journal Sovremennik. In 1840 he married Sofya Mikhailovna Velgorskaya. In 1843 he visited Nice and met Gogol. From 1856 he was an Officer for Special Commissions in the imperial court; he took an interest in prison reform, and from 1875 was сhair of the Commission for the Reorganization of Prisons in Russia. In 1858 he was sent abroad to study European theater, and in 1877 he became an official historian at court. Sollogub was a connoisseur of theatrical life and of St. Petersburg society. He hosted a well-known literary and musical salon where he brought to life the atmosphere of St. Petersburg of that era as related in his Memoirs (1887). He is best known for his 1845 novelette Tarantas ("The Tarantass"), "a satirical journey from Moscow to Kazan in a tumble-down traveling cart. The satire, superficial and uninspired, is directed against the ideas of the Slavophils and the unpractical dreaminess of the romantic idealists."

Vladimir Sollogub (1813-1882) was a Russian poet, novelist, and playwright known for his contributions to Russian literature in the 19th century. Born in St. Petersburg, he belonged to the literary movement that embraced Romanticism, and he is recognized for his exploration of themes such as love, nature, and the human condition. Sollogub's most notable works include his poetry collections and the novel "The Invisible Man," which reflect his innovative use of language and intricate character development. Additionally, he was active in the literary community, engaging with contemporaries and contributing to various literary journals. His works, while not as widely known as those of his peers, have garnered attention for their stylistic depth and emotional resonance.

The Organized Village of Kake is a federally recognized Native American tribe of Tlingit people. This Alaska Native tribe is headquartered in Kake, Alaska (Tlingit: Ḵéex̱’). The tribe has 600 enrolled citizens.

The Organized Village of Kake is a federally recognized tribal government located on Kake Island in Southeast Alaska. It is primarily inhabited by the Tlingit people and serves as the administrative entity for the local Native community. The Goode homolosine projection (or interrupted Goode homolosine projection) is a pseudocylindrical, equal-area, composite map projection used for world maps. Normally it is presented with multiple interruptions, most commonly of the major oceans. Its equal-area property makes it useful for presenting spatial distribution of phenomena.

The Goode homolosine projection is an equal-area map projection designed by J.B. Goode in 1923. It aims to minimize distortion of land area, making it valuable for presenting global statistics and ecological data. The projection combines the sinusoidal and Mollweide projections, resulting in a segmented, interrupted format that preserves area but distorts shapes and distances, particularly in the regions where the interruptions occur. Its unique layout features a series of lobes that reduce exaggeration of certain landmasses, such as Africa and South America, making it a practical choice for thematic mapping while sacrificing a true representation of geographic shapes.

A Bachelor of Software Engineering is an undergraduate academic degree (bachelor's degree) awarded for completing a program of study in the field of software development for computers in information technology. Setu is a district located in South Tangerang, Indonesia. It is known for its suburban characteristics and development, situated on the outskirts of the Jakarta metropolitan area. Heteronomy refers to action that is influenced by a force outside the individual, in other words the state or condition of being ruled, governed, or under the sway of another, as in a military occupation. It is the counter/opposite of autonomy. Philosopher Cornelius Castoriadis contrasted heteronomy with autonomy by noting that while all societies create their own institutions (laws, traditions and behaviors), autonomous societies are those in which their members are aware of this fact, and explicitly self-institute (αυτονομούνται). In contrast, the members of heteronomous societies (hetero- 'other') attribute their imaginaries to some extra-social authority (e.g., God, the state, ancestors, historical necessity, etc.). Immanuel Kant, drawing on Jean-Jacques Rousseau, considered such an action nonmoral.

Heteronomy is a term used in philosophy and ethics to describe a condition of being governed by external laws or influences rather than by one's own autonomous reasoning and will. This concept contrasts with autonomy, where individuals act according to their own principles and decisions. Heteronomy can manifest in various contexts, including moral philosophy, where it implies that a person's actions are determined by outside forces, such as societal norms, laws, or external authority. The concept has significant implications in discussions about free will, moral responsibility, and individual agency. In political theory, heteronomy can refer to the influence of external powers on governance and decision-making. The term is rooted in ancient Greek philosophy, particularly in the works of thinkers like Immanuel Kant, who critiqued heteronomous moral systems that rely on external sources of authority.

Stanley Griffiths (8 February 1911 – 12 January 2003) was a Welsh professional footballer who played as an outside forward. He played six times in the Football League during spells with Gillingham and Cardiff City.
