Sleen is a village in Drenthe, Netherlands of about 2,500 people. Sleen has been inhabited for centuries. Much ancient history can be found in the area, particularly in the forests (which are planted, though). At birth and during the Republiek der Zeven Verenigde Nederlanden (Republic of the Seven United Netherlands (1581–1795)). Sleen became the capital of one of the six “dingspels” (administrative areas) of Drenthe. When Drenthe was recognised as a province, Sleen became a municipality capital, before it merged into the municipality of Coevorden. Sleen has a 450-year-old church. At about 68 meters, it is the highest church in the entire province. Nowadays, it functions as a Dutch Reformed Church. There is also another Protestant church in the village. Sleen has its own supermarket (behind the former police station), library, fish stand (every Wednesday), cafés, a few other shops, hair salons, petrol station, sports park (soccer, tennis, multipurpose indoor complex), horse riding school, houses for the elderly, and other facilities. There are also two schools: CBS “de Fontein” (Christian primary school) and OBS “de Akker” (public primary school). “De Brink” is the “centre” and the old part of the village, with some historic houses and a brick road. This is also where the two churches are, and other facilities like the former municipal centre. New houses were constructed over time, and an area known as “de nieuwbouw” is where the most recently constructed houses can be found. The famous Pieterpad (a 498 kilometres (309 mi) long hiking route that runs from Pieterburen in the north of the country to the St Pietersberg in Maastricht in the south) goes straight through Sleen. The windmill De Hoop has been restored to working order.

Sleen is a fictional character from the comic book series "The Adventures of Tintin," created by Belgian artist Hergé. He is depicted as a skilled but unscrupulous antagonist in the story "Tintin in the Land of the Soviets," where he is involved in espionage and other nefarious activities. Sleen's character contributes to the narrative's themes of conflict and intrigue, showcasing the political climate of the time. Despite being a minor character, he reflects the broader issues of deception and manipulation that Tintin encounters throughout his adventures.

I Saw the Sun (Turkish: Güneşi Gördüm) is a 2009 Turkish drama film, written and directed by Mahsun Kırmızıgül, which tells of a Kurdish family who are forced from their village in southeastern Turkey by the conflict there. The film, which was released on 13 March 2009 (2009-03-13), was one of the highest grossing Turkish films of 2009, prompting its re-release on 18 September 2009 (2009-09-18). The film was Turkey's official submission for the Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film at the 82nd Academy Awards, but it was not nominated.

"I Saw the Sun" is a 2006 Turkish drama film directed by A. Tamer Acar. The film centers around the personal and political struggles of a young Kurdish girl living in a rural village in southeastern Turkey during the 1990s. It highlights issues related to ethnic identity, social injustice, and the impact of the Kurdish-Turkish conflict on local communities. Received positively at several international film festivals, "I Saw the Sun" is noted for its poignant storytelling and strong character development, as it seeks to shed light on the resilience of its characters amidst adversity.

Marc Bloch (6 July 1886 – 16 June 1944) was a French historian. He was a founding member of the Annales School of French social history. Bloch specialised in medieval history and published widely on Medieval France over the course of his career. As an academic, he worked at the University of Strasbourg (1920 to 1936), the University of Paris (1936 to 1939), and the University of Montpellier (1941 to 1944).

Marc Bloch was a prominent French historian and one of the founders of the Annales School, which emphasized social history and interdisciplinary approaches. His bibliography includes several influential works that explore medieval history, economic history, and cultural studies. Notable publications include "The Historian's Craft" ("Apologie pour l'histoire"), where he discusses the methodology of historians, and "Feudal Society," which analyzes the social and political structures of the Middle Ages. Additionally, Bloch's "Strange Defeat" reflects on France's fall to Nazi Germany in World War II, offering insights into national identity and historical interpretation. His writings remain foundational in the field of history and are celebrated for their depth and critical approach to historical study.

"Eddie" Wu Kuang-yu or Wu Guangyu (born 1946) is a Chinese-Canadian tai chi teacher. He is the eldest son of the late Wu Ta-k'uei and senior instructor of the Wu family and "Gatekeeper" of the Wu-style as taught in the Wu's tai chi Academies internationally since the death of his uncle, the late Wu Daxin, in January 2005.

Wu Kuang-yu (born September 30, 2001) is a Taiwanese professional racing driver known for his participation in various single-seater categories, including the Eurocup Formula Renault and the FIA Formula 3 Championship. He began his racing career in karting before moving up to open-wheel racing in 2018. Wu has gained recognition for his skillful driving and competitive performances, representing Taiwan on international racing stages. He is affiliated with multiple racing teams and has aspirations to advance to higher levels of motorsport, including Formula 1.

The Short Strand (Irish: an Trá Ghearr) is a working class, inner city area of Belfast, Northern Ireland. It is a mainly Catholic and Irish nationalist enclave surrounded by the mainly Protestant and unionist East Belfast. Short Strand is located on the east bank of the River Lagan in the townland of Ballymacarrett, which is part of County Down. The borders of the Short Strand are Albertbridge Road (to the south), Short Strand Road (to the west), Newtownards Road (to the north) and Bryson Street/Clandeboye Gardens (to the east). At the Short Strand's northeast corner is St Matthew's Catholic church. For decades, Protestants and Catholics have regularly clashed at the edges of the Short Strand. This has led to fierce rioting and, occasionally, gun battles. Much of the Short Strand is surrounded by peace lines.

Short Strand is a residential area in East Belfast, Northern Ireland, known for its strong working-class community and significant historical context related to the Troubles, a period of conflict in Northern Ireland. The area has a predominantly Catholic population and has been the site of various social and political activities. Short Strand is characterized by its close-knit neighborhoods and local culture, with institutions such as schools and community centers playing vital roles in its social fabric. The area also features a mix of historical and contemporary architecture, reflecting its development over the decades. Short Strand has a reputation for resilience, having experienced significant challenges and changes throughout its history.

Yang Hormat Dato' Yasmin Yusuff, DIMP (born 1955), is a former beauty queen, veteran actress and singer in Malaysia. Yasmin is one of the few well-known Malay pageant contestants who won the Miss Universe Malaysia title back in 1978, she competed in Miss Universe 1978.

Yasmin Yusuff is a recognized Malaysian entrepreneur, philanthropist, and motivational speaker known for her work in promoting women's empowerment and entrepreneurship in Southeast Asia. She is the founder of several initiatives aimed at fostering business skills among young women and underprivileged communities. Yusuff has been involved in various social impact projects, focusing on education, mentorship, and financial literacy. Her contributions to empowering women in business have garnered her multiple awards and recognition within the region. In addition to her entrepreneurial endeavors, she frequently speaks at conferences and events, advocating for gender equality and sustainable development.
Star Feminine Band are a band consisting of eight female musicians from Benin, born between 2003 and 2010. The group sing songs on social topics, often with a feminist message.

Star Feminine Band is a musical group from the Republic of Benin, formed in 2016. The band comprises young female musicians who play a blend of traditional Beninese music styles, including Zanku and Afrobeat, while incorporating modern influences. Their music often addresses social issues, particularly gender equality and empowerment. Star Feminine Band gained international recognition for their unique sound and performances, which highlight the talents of women in a male-dominated industry. They released their debut album, "Féminin," in 2021, receiving praise for both its musicality and its message. The band continues to advocate for women's rights and representation in the music industry.

Trygve Henrik Lindeman (November 3, 1896 – October 24, 1979) was a Norwegian cellist and the head of the Oslo Conservatory of Music for two generations. Lindeman was born in Kristiania (now Oslo). After passing his university qualifying exam, he studied civil engineering at the Norwegian Institute of Technology, and then he switched to studying music in 1916 at the Oslo Conservatory of Music under Gustav Fredrik Lange. He also studied at the Royal Danish Academy of Music in Copenhagen under Carl Nielsen. He debuted as a cellist in 1925, and in 1928 he took over leadership of the Oslo Conservatory of Music from his father, Peter Brynie Lindeman. He headed the conservatory until 1969. Lindeman and his wife, Marie Louise née Swensen, had no children and so they established the Lindeman Foundation (Norwegian: Lindemans Legat) and turned over the directorship of the conservatory to Anfinn Øien, who headed the school until it was closed and succeeded by the Norwegian Academy of Music in 1973. The "Lindeman tradition" in Norwegian music was cultivated by Trygve Lindeman, who believed that everyone is capable of playing, composing, and understanding music theory and practicing pedagogy. Lindeman was a Freemason and held the position of master mason in his lodge for many years.

Trygve Lindeman (1886–1974) was a prominent Norwegian composer, violinist, and music educator known for his contributions to Norwegian folk music and contemporary classical music. He studied at the Norwegian Academy of Music and furthered his education in Germany and France. Lindeman was a key figure in the early 20th-century Norwegian music scene, blending traditional Norwegian folk elements with modern techniques. His work included orchestral compositions, chamber music, and educational pieces. In addition to his compositions, Lindeman was influential as a teacher, mentoring a generation of Norwegian musicians and composers. His legacy is celebrated for its role in shaping the national musical identity of Norway.

The Sukwa people are an ethnic group in Malawi. According to the 2018 census, they make up 0.5% of Malawi's population. The Sukwa people mainly live in Northern Malawi in the district of Chitipa and Karonga.

The Sukwa people are an ethnic group primarily residing in the central region of Zambia. They are known for their distinct cultural traditions, languages, and agricultural practices. The Sukwa speak the Sukwa language, which belongs to the Bantu language family. Traditionally, their economy is based on subsistence farming, with staple crops including maize, cassava, and millet. They also engage in fishing and hunting, utilizing the natural resources available in their environment. Social structure within Sukwa communities is typically organized around extended family units, with strong emphasis on kinship ties and communal activities. The Sukwa have a rich oral tradition, which encompasses folklore, proverbs, and songs that convey their history and cultural values.
Baire Benítez was a Cuban chess player. He won the Cuban Chess Championship in 1921.

Baire Benítez (born August 8, 1992) is a Paraguayan professional footballer who plays as a midfielder. He began his career in the youth system of Club Libertad in Paraguay before making his debut for the senior team. Known for his technical skills and versatility on the field, Benítez has played for various clubs in both Paraguay and abroad. Throughout his career, he has gained recognition for his contributions in domestic leagues and international competitions, and he has also represented Paraguay at various youth levels, showcasing his potential on the international stage.

Yuzhnoye Izmaylovo (Russian: Ю́жное Изма́йлово, Southern Izmaylovo) is an area located in the eastern part of Moscow, Russia, just north of the Shosse Entuziastov (шоссе Энтузиастов) Route and west of the Moscow Ring Highway (МКАД). It borders Izmaylovo District in the south and Izmaylovsky Park in the north and west. In 1960, it became a part of Moscow, after all territories inside the Moscow Ring Highway were incorporated into Moscow proper. Yuzhnoye Izmaylovo is in one of the green areas of Moscow. Izmaylovsky Park, one of the largest forested urban areas in Europe, is right across the street. Yuzhnoye Izmaylovo was built in the mid-1970s, initially as an Olympic Village for the 1980 Moscow Olympics. Yuzhnoye Izmaylovo would later be converted into a bedroom community, a microdistrict for the fast-growing population of the city. Yuzhnoye Izmaylovo is less than an hour away from the center of the city by public transportation (bus and Moscow Metro).

Yuzhnoye Izmaylovo is an urban locality (a microdistrict) in the eastern part of Moscow, Russia. It is situated within the Izmaylovo District, known for its residential areas and parks. The microdistrict features a mix of Soviet-era apartment buildings and modern developments. Yuzhnoye Izmaylovo is well-connected to the rest of the city through public transportation, including the Moscow Metro. The locality offers various amenities, including schools, shops, and recreational spaces, making it a residential choice for many families and individuals in the capital.

"The Dicks Hate the Police" (usually shortened to "Hate the Police") is the debut release and 7-inch single from the American hardcore punk band the Dicks, released in 1980. The record was released on the band's own Radical Records imprint. Mudhoney included a cover of the song on Superfuzz Bigmuff Plus Early Singles.

"The Dicks Hate the Police" is a 1983 benefit album by the American punk band The Dicks, aiming to raise awareness against police brutality and discrimination. Known for its raw sound and confrontational lyrics, the album incorporates a mix of punk rock and post-punk elements, reflecting the band's social and political activism. The Dicks, originating from Austin, Texas, were influential in the hardcore punk movement and often addressed issues such as LGBTQ+ rights and anti-establishment sentiments in their music. The album's title track and other songs encapsulate the band's disdain for oppressive law enforcement practices, contributing to the broader punk discourse on authority and resistance.

The Canterbury Railway Society is an organisation of railway enthusiasts based in the Canterbury region of New Zealand's South Island, best known for their operation of The Ferrymead Railway at the Ferrymead Heritage Park.

The Canterbury Railway Society (CRS) is a railway enthusiast organization based in Christchurch, New Zealand. Established in 1960, the society promotes interest in rail transport and heritage railways through activities such as organizing excursions, hosting meetings, and producing publications related to rail history. CRS also engages in the preservation of railway heritage and operates a collection of historical railway memorabilia. The society serves as a platform for railway enthusiasts to share knowledge, experiences, and passion for railways in New Zealand.
Sebt is a town and commune in Tiaret Province in north-western Algeria.

Sebt is a geographic term used in North Africa, particularly in Morocco, to refer to a traditional rural settlement or village, often characterized by agricultural practices and communal living. The term is derived from Arabic, where it connotes a sense of belonging to a community and is associated with the cultivation of land and local customs. Sebts may vary in size and structure, typically comprising houses built from local materials. Many Sebts serve as cultural and social hubs, preserving local traditions and providing a sense of identity for their inhabitants. The concept emphasizes the relationship between people and their environment, as well as the importance of community cohesion in rural lifestyles.

The Broken Yolk Cafe is an American restaurant chain serving American breakfast foods mixed with Tex-Mex cuisine. Founded in 1979, it has forty locations mainly in Southern California, with others in other areas of the Western United States. The restaurant is most known for its Eggs Benedict.

Broken Yolk Cafe is a restaurant chain based in the United States, known for its breakfast and brunch offerings. Founded in 1985 in San Diego, California, the café specializes in a variety of breakfast dishes, including omelets, pancakes, and sandwiches, alongside a selection of lunch items. The brand emphasizes a casual dining experience and has gained a reputation for its extensive menu that caters to diverse dietary preferences. As of 2023, Broken Yolk Cafe has expanded to multiple locations across several states, establishing itself as a popular destination for breakfast lovers.

The 1924 East Tennessee State Normal School football team was an American football team that represented East Tennessee State Normal School—now known as East Tennessee State University (ETSU)—as an independent in the 1924 college football season. They were led by third-year head coach James Karl Luck.
topic: 1924 East Tennessee State Normal football team

text: The 1924 East Tennessee State Normal football team represented East Tennessee State Normal School (now East Tennessee State University) during the 1924 college football season. The team competed as an independent and did not join any conference. Under the leadership of head coach John B. "Jack" Dempsey, the team played a total of 6 games, finishing the season with a record of 2 wins and 4 losses. The team was notable for its emphasis on sportsmanship and development of character, reflecting the educational mission of the institution. Despite facing challenges throughout the season, the players and coaches contributed to the establishment of a growing football tradition at the school.
topic: 1924 East Tennessee State Normal football team

text: The Dazai Osamu Prize (太宰治賞) is a Japanese literary prize named for novelist Dazai Osamu (1909–1948). The prize was established in 1965 by the Chikuma Shobō publishing company, discontinued in 1978, and resumed again in 1999 with co-sponsorship of the Mitaka, Tokyo municipal government. It is awarded annually to an outstanding, previously unpublished short story by an unrecognized author; the winner receives a commemorative gift and a cash award of 1 million yen.

The Dazai Osamu Prize is a prestigious Japanese literary award named after the renowned author Osamu Dazai, established in 1968. It is awarded annually to outstanding works of fiction by new or emerging authors in Japan, typically focusing on novels or short stories. The prize aims to promote and recognize innovative literary talent, fostering the development of contemporary Japanese literature. Recipients receive a monetary award and recognition within the literary community, contributing to their professional careers. The award is considered highly significant in Japan's literary landscape and has a notable impact on the careers of its winners.
Trochus cariniferus is a species of small sea snail, a marine gastropod mollusc in the family Trochidae, the top snails.

Trochus cariniferus, commonly known as the ridged top shell, is a species of sea snail in the family Trochidae, which comprises marine gastropod mollusks. This species is characterized by its distinctive conical shell adorned with prominent ridges, which can vary in color from pale brown to dark purples and greens. Trochus cariniferus is typically found in shallow coastal waters of the Indo-Pacific region, including areas around Australia, Southeast Asia, and various Pacific islands. It inhabits rocky substrates and is often associated with coral reefs. The shell of Trochus cariniferus is prized for its aesthetic qualities and is sometimes harvested for decorative purposes. However, overexploitation and habitat destruction have raised concerns about its conservation status in certain regions.
Lithacodia is a genus of moths of the family Noctuidae.

Lithacodia is a genus of extinct bivalve mollusks belonging to the family Pectinidae, commonly known as scallops. First described in the scientific literature in the late 19th century, Lithacodia lived during the Late Jurassic to Early Cretaceous periods, with fossil remains primarily found in marine sedimentary deposits across Europe and North America. The genus is characterized by its unique shell morphology, featuring distinctive ribbing and radial ornamentation. Lithacodia provides important insights into the evolutionary history of scallops and the paleoecology of ancient marine environments.

Graciela Alfano (born 14 December 1952) is an Argentine actress and model. She is best known for her appearances in comedies between the late 1970s and early 1980s which made her a sex symbol. She has worked as a judge on Bailando por un sueño in Argentina.

Graciela Alfano is an Argentine actress, model, and television personality, born on December 14, 1952, in Buenos Aires, Argentina. She rose to fame in the 1970s and 1980s through her roles in various films and television series. Alfano is known for her appearances in significant Argentine productions, including soap operas and comedy shows, establishing herself as a prominent figure in the country's entertainment industry. In addition to her acting career, she has participated as a judge on talent competitions and reality shows, showcasing her versatility and influence in Argentine media. Throughout her career, Alfano has been recognized for her contributions to television and film, earning numerous awards and nominations.

90 Bisodol (Crimond) is the twelfth studio album by UK rock band Half Man Half Biscuit. It was released on 26 September 2011 by Probe Plus. The inner sleeve includes a modified version of the painting Christ's Entry into Jerusalem by William Gale (1823–1909), in which one onlooker holds a sign with the words "Dirk Hofman Motorhomes". This is a reference to a man who holds such a sign at the finish of European cycling races.

90 Bisodol (Crimond) is a designation used for a specific oilfield located in the North Sea, near the eastern coast of Scotland. The field is situated approximately 130 miles northeast of Aberdeen, and is part of the Bisodol oilfield complex. Discovered in the late 20th century, 90 Bisodol (Crimond) has been of interest for its hydrocarbon reserves, contributing to Scotland's energy production. The field's development involves the use of advanced drilling techniques and infrastructure to extract and process crude oil. As part of the broader North Sea oil industry, it plays a role in the local economy but also faces scrutiny related to environmental impacts and sustainability practices within the fossil fuel sector.

The 1968 Copa Perú season (Spanish: Copa Perú 1968), the promotion tournament of Peruvian football. In this tournament after many qualification rounds, each one of the 24 departments in which Peru is politically divided, qualified a team. Those teams plus the team relegated from First Division on the last year, enter in two more rounds and finally 6 of them qualify for the Final round, staged in Lima (the capital). The winning team, Carlos A. Mannucci, was promoted to play in 1968 Torneo Descentralizado.

The 1968 Copa Perú was the sixth edition of the Copa Perú, a national football tournament in Peru. Organized by the Peruvian Football Federation, the tournament featured teams from various regional leagues competing for promotion to the top tier of Peruvian football, the Primera División. The tournament ran in a format that included multiple stages, starting with regional rounds that culminated in a national stage. Atlético Grau emerged as the champion, securing their promotion to the Primera División for the subsequent season. The 1968 edition is noted for its role in developing football talent and providing opportunities for smaller clubs across the country.
The Zira FK 2021-22 season was Zira's seventh Azerbaijan Premier League season, and eighth season in their history.

The 2021–22 season was the 31st season in the history of Zira FK, a professional football club based in Zira, Azerbaijan. Competing in the Azerbaijan Premier League, the team aimed to improve upon its previous performances. Throughout the season, Zira FK participated in domestic league matches and was involved in the Azerbaijan Cup. The club's performance was marked by notable player contributions and strategic developments under its coaching staff. The season concluded with Zira FK finishing mid-table in the league standings, and their journey in the Azerbaijan Cup ended at the quarter-finals stage. This season reflected the club's ongoing efforts to establish itself within Azerbaijani football.

Wolfgang Thüne (born 8 October 1949) is a German former gymnast. He competed at the 1972 Summer Olympics in all artistic gymnastics events and won a bronze medal with the East German team. Individually his best achievement was seventh place in the horizontal bar. He won two more bronze team medals at the world championships in 1970 and 1974 as well as individual silver in the horizontal bar in 1974. At the 1975 European championships in Bern, he fled to West Germany with the help of his rival Eberhard Gienger. In 1977 he won the national all-round title in West Germany competing for TuS 04 Leverkusen. After retirement he worked as a gymnastics coach at the same club.

Wolfgang Thüne (born 24 October 1944) is a German economist and politician, known for his work in regional economic development and his involvement in environmental policy. He served as a member of the German Bundestag from 1990 to 1994, representing the Green Party. Thüne has also been active in various national and international economic organizations, advocating for sustainable development and ecological responsibility. Throughout his career, he has published several papers and articles on economic theory and practice, particularly focusing on the intersection of economics and environmental issues.
The 1990 Nobel Prizes were awarded by the Nobel Foundation, based in Sweden. Six categories were awarded: Physics, Chemistry, Physiology or Medicine, Literature, Peace, and Economic Sciences.

The 1990 Nobel Prizes were awarded in the fields of Physics, Chemistry, Medicine (Physiology or Medicine), Literature, Peace, and Economic Sciences. The Nobel Prize in Physics was awarded to Jerome I. Friedman, Henry W. Kendall, and Richard E. Taylor for their contributions to the understanding of the deep inelastic scattering of electrons on protons and bound neutrons. The Chemistry Prize went to Ahmed Zewail for his work on femtochemistry. The Physiology or Medicine Prize was jointly awarded to Joseph E. Murray and E. Donnall Thomas for their pioneering work in organ transplantation. The Nobel Prize in Literature was awarded to Octavio Paz for his extensive and diverse literary works. The Peace Prize was given to Mikhail Gorbachev for his role in ending the Cold War. Lastly, the Sveriges Riksbank Prize in Economic Sciences was awarded to Harry M. Markowitz, Merton H. Miller, and William F. Sharpe for their contributions to the theory of financial economics.

The 1997 SWAC men's basketball tournament was held March 6–8, 1997, at the Riverside Centroplex in Baton Rouge, Louisiana. Jackson State defeated Mississippi Valley State, 81–74 in the championship game. The Tigers received the conference's automatic bid to the 1997 NCAA tournament as No. 16 seed in the Southeast Region.

The 1997 SWAC men's basketball tournament was the postseason conference tournament for the Southwestern Athletic Conference (SWAC) held from March 6 to March 8, 1997, in Grand Prairie, Texas. The tournament featured eight teams competing in a single-elimination format to determine the conference champion. Jackson State claimed the title by defeating Texas Southern in the championship game with a score of 72-67. This victory secured Jackson State's automatic berth to the 1997 NCAA Tournament. The tournament's Most Valuable Player was Caleb Smith of Jackson State, who played a pivotal role in his team's success throughout the event.

St Thomas' Church is a parish church in the Groves area of York, a city in England. It is sometimes known as St Thomas' in the Groves. The Groves area, in the parish of St Olave's Church, York, was built up in the mid 19th century. It was decided to construct a new church in the area. A building was designed by George Fowler Jones, and constructed between 1853 and 1854. The work cost £2,500, and the completed church, in the Gothic revival style, could seat 500 worshippers. The church was given its own parish in 1855, and was enlarged in 1899. The church was grade II listed in 1997. In 2014, it was added to the Heritage at Risk Register due to severe erosion to some of its stonework. The church is built of sandstone with slate roofs. It consists of a three-bay nave, two-bay north and south transepts, and a two-bay chancel. The west end has a gable and its central bay projects slightly, supporting a bellcote and cross. There is an arched doorway, with windows either side, and a two-light window above. The nave has buttresses with windows in each bay, while the transepts have two-light windows and a circular window in the gable end. The east window has three lights.

St Thomas' Church is a parish church located in the city of York, England. It was built in 1853, designed by the architect William Henry Crossland in the Gothic Revival style. The church is part of the Church of England and serves the local community with regular worship services, events, and community activities. Notable features include its stained glass windows and a distinctive tower, which contribute to the local architectural heritage. The church is also involved in various outreach programs and maintains a commitment to serving the spiritual needs of its parishioners. St Thomas' Church is recognized as a Grade II listed building, reflecting its historical and architectural significance.

Danielle Fournier (born 1955) is a Quebec educator and writer. She was born in Montreal and received a PhD in literature from the Université de Sherbrooke. She also studied German at the University of New Brunswick. She has taught at the college and university level at various institutions including the Université de Sherbrooke, the University of New Brunswick, at McGill University, the Université du Québec à Montréal, at Concordia University and at the Collège Jean-de-Brébeuf, where she held a permanent position. Fournier has written poetry, fiction and critical essays for various magazines such as Exit, Arcade, Estuaire, Moebius, Spirale, Urgences, Québec français and Voix et Images. Her work has also appeared in a number of anthologies. In 2003, she was awarded the Prix Alain-Grandbois for her poetry collection Poèmes perdus en Hongrie. In 2005, Il n'y a rien d'intact dans ma chair was a finalist for the Governor General's Award for French-language poetry and, in 2010, her collection effleurés de lumière won that award.

Danielle Fournier is a Canadian politician and member of the New Democratic Party (NDP). She has served as a Member of the Legislative Assembly (MLA) in the province of Quebec, representing the electoral district of Mauricie. Fournier's political career has been marked by her advocacy for social justice, environmental issues, and workers' rights. Throughout her tenure, she has actively participated in various committees and has been involved in community initiatives aimed at improving the quality of life for her constituents. Her work reflects the NDP's principles of equity and sustainability.

Kotiliesi (Finnish: Fireside) is a bimonthly family and women's magazine published in Helsinki, Finland. Founded in 1922, it is one of the oldest magazines in the country and is the first Finnish magazine addressing housewives.

Kotiliesi is a Finnish magazine focused on home and lifestyle, primarily targeting women. Founded in 1922, it is one of Finland's oldest and most popular publications, known for its content on cooking, home decoration, and family life. The magazine features recipes, practical tips, and articles on various topics related to domesticity and personal well-being. Kotiliesi is published by the Sanoma Media Finland, a major media company in the Nordic region, and has a strong online presence alongside its print edition. The magazine aims to inspire and support its readers in managing their households and nurturing family relationships.

The Taikang Cultural Center or Taijiang Cultural Center (traditional Chinese: 臺江文化中心; simplified Chinese: 台江文化中心; pinyin: Táijiāng Wénhuà Zhōngxīn) is a cultural center in Annan District, Tainan, Taiwan.

The Taikang Cultural Center, located in Beijing, China, is a contemporary cultural complex that showcases a blend of art, design, and architecture. Opened in 2018, it serves as a hub for cultural exchange and artistic engagement, featuring spaces for exhibitions, performances, and public events. The center was designed by the renowned architectural firm Studio Other Spaces, co-founded by artists Olafur Eliasson and Sebastian Behmann, and it emphasizes sustainability and community involvement. It aims to foster creativity and dialogue among artists and the public, offering a platform for both local and international talent.

The Church of the Holy Trinity is a Church of England parish church in Bosbury in the English county of Herefordshire. Its bell tower stands well apart from the church. Both are Grade I listed buildings.
topic: Church of the Holy Trinity, Bosbury

text: The Church of the Holy Trinity is a historic parish church located in Bosbury, Herefordshire, England. It is part of the Church of England and is recognized for its architectural significance, which includes elements from the Norman period and later medieval modifications. The church features a distinctive tower and stained glass windows, attracting visitors for both its religious function and its heritage value. The building is a grade I listed structure, indicating its exceptional historical importance. The church serves the local community and is involved in various religious and social activities.
topic: Church of the Holy Trinity, Bosbury

text: Homo luzonensis, also known as Callao Man and locally called "Ubag" after a mythical caveman, is an extinct, possibly pygmy, species of archaic human from the Late Pleistocene of Luzon, the Philippines. Their remains, teeth and phalanges, are known only from Callao Cave in the northern part of the island dating to before 50,000 years ago. They were initially identified as belonging to modern humans in 2010, but in 2019, after the discovery of more specimens, they were placed into a new species based on the presence of a wide range of traits similar to modern humans as well as to Australopithecus and early Homo. In 2023, a study found that the fossilized remains were 134,000 ± 14,000 years old, much older than previously thought. Their ancestors, who may have been Asian H. erectus or some other even earlier Homo, would have needed to have made a sea crossing of several miles at minimum to reach the island. Hominin presence on Luzon dates to as early as 771,000 to 631,000 years ago. The inhabitants of the cave dragged in mainly Philippine deer carcasses, and used tools for butchering.

Homo luzonensis is an extinct species of hominin that was discovered in the Callao Cave on Luzon, the largest island in the Philippines. The species is believed to have lived approximately 50,000 to 67,000 years ago. Fossil evidence, including teeth and limb bones, indicates that Homo luzonensis exhibited a unique combination of primitive and derived traits, suggesting a complex pattern of hominin evolution in Southeast Asia. Its discovery, announced in 2019, has significant implications for our understanding of human migration and the diversity of ancient human relatives in the region. The name "luzonensis" refers to the island where the fossils were found, highlighting the geographic significance of this species in the study of human evolution.

Pigato is a white Italian wine grape planted primarily in Liguria. The grape is found in the Riviera di Ponente zone in Italy's region of Liguria which makes sturdy, aromatic wines with plenty of fruit. DNA evidence proves that Pigato, Vermentino and Favorita are closely related. It gains its name, which means "spotted" from the appearance of the ripe grapes.

Pigato is a white wine grape variety indigenous to the Liguria region of northwest Italy, predominantly cultivated in the provinces of Savona and Imperia. It is known for producing aromatic wines with a characteristic floral and fruity profile, often exhibiting notes of peach, apricot, and almonds. Recognized for its vibrant acidity and mineral qualities, Pigato is often associated with the local cuisine, particularly seafood dishes. The grape is sometimes confused with other varieties such as Vermentino, but Pigato is distinctive due to its unique flavor and growing conditions. Wines made from Pigato can be enjoyed young or aged, and the variety has gained recognition both domestically and internationally for its quality.

Ånderdalen National Park (Norwegian: Ånderdalen nasjonalpark) is located on the large island of Senja in Troms county, Norway. The 134-square-kilometre (52 sq mi) park lies within Senja Municipality. The park was established by royal decree on 6 February 1970 and it was enlarged in 2004. The national park preserves this Northern Norwegian coastal landscape. The bedrock consists of hard granite rocks and the landscape image gives exciting impressions of the glacial forces that have worked on Senja. The coastal pine forest in Ånderdalen has in some areas primeval forest with over 600 years old trees and countless marble pines and lower. Along the river there are many lush hawked birch forests and floodplain forests. The national park had no moose before 1940, but it now has a permanent population. It is also an important calving and grazing area for semi-domesticated reindeer. The most common smaller animals are red foxes, stoats, hares, small rodents, and two species of shrews. Seals occur at the heads of the fjords and otters are often seen along rivers. Trout and char are common, and salmon run right up the river to the lake Åndervatn.

Ånderdalen National Park is a protected area located on the island of Senja in Troms og Finnmark county, Norway. Established in 1970, the park covers approximately 146 square kilometers and is characterized by its diverse landscapes, including deep valleys, rugged mountains, and lush forests. It is known for its rich biodiversity, including populations of elk, reindeer, and various bird species, as well as unique flora. Ånderdalen serves as a recreational area for hiking, fishing, and wildlife observation, attracting outdoor enthusiasts and nature lovers. The park's remote wilderness offers a glimpse into the natural beauty of Northern Norway, making it an important site for conservation and ecological research.
Cephalota maura is a species of tiger beetle. This species is found in Portugal, Spain, Italy, Morocco, Algeria and Tunisia. The habitat consists of salt marshes and river banks.

Cephalota maura is a species of beetle belonging to the family Cerambycidae, commonly known as longhorn beetles. This species was first described by the entomologist Franz von Paula Schrank in 1789. Cephalota maura is primarily found in various regions of Europe, where it inhabits forests and wooded areas. The adult beetles are characterized by their elongated bodies and long antennae, typical of the longhorn beetle family. Their larvae are wood-borers, feeding on decaying or dead wood, which plays a role in the ecological decomposition processes. As with many beetle species, their presence can be an indicator of the health of their environment.
Gary Mule Deer (born Gary C. Miller; November 21, 1939) is an American comedian and country musician.

Gary Mule Deer is an American comedian and musician best known for his stand-up comedy and appearances on television. Born on July 1, 1939, in Los Angeles, California, Mule Deer gained prominence in the 1970s and 1980s for his clean, family-friendly humor, which often includes storytelling and observational comedy. He has performed on various TV shows, including "The Tonight Show" and "The Merv Griffin Show," and has released several comedy albums. Mule Deer is also recognized for his work as a country music singer, blending his comedic talents with musical performances. Throughout his career, he has become a beloved figure in the comedy circuit for his light-hearted and engaging style.

The following outline is provided as an overview of and topical guide to Christianity: Christianity is a monotheistic religion centered on the life and teachings of Jesus of Nazareth as presented in the New Testament (see Jesus in Christianity). The Christian faith is essentially faith in Jesus as the Christ (or Messiah), the Son of God, the Savior, and, according to Trinitarianism, God the Son, part of the Trinity with God the Father and the Holy Spirit.

The Outline of Christianity is a structured overview of the Christian faith, encompassing its beliefs, practices, history, and influence on culture. Christianity, centered on the life and teachings of Jesus Christ, is based on the Bible, which includes the Old and New Testaments. Key doctrines include the belief in one God, the Trinity, salvation through faith in Jesus, and the resurrection. Major branches of Christianity include Roman Catholicism, Orthodoxy, and Protestantism, each with distinct practices and theological principles. Christianity emphasizes sacraments, particularly baptism and communion, and has a rich tradition of liturgy and worship. The religion has significantly shaped Western civilization, contributing to art, philosophy, law, and ethics, while also expanding globally through missionary work and cultural exchange.
KIAA0999 protein is a protein that in humans is encoded by the SIK3 gene. It is part of the salt-inducible kinase family.

KIAA0999 is a human gene located on chromosome 6, which encodes a protein that is implicated in various cellular processes, including neuronal development and regulation of synaptic functions. It is part of the KIAA gene family, characterized by being identified through large-scale sequencing efforts. Research has suggested a potential role for KIAA0999 in neurodegenerative diseases, although its precise functions and mechanisms are not fully understood. Further studies are ongoing to elucidate its biological significance and potential implications in health and disease.
Xyloskenea grahami is a species of sea snail, a marine gastropod mollusk, unassigned in the superfamily Seguenzioidea.

Xyloskenea grahami is a species of moth belonging to the family Erebidae, first described by entomologist William James Kaye in 1932. This species is primarily found in various regions of South America, particularly in tropical and subtropical environments. The moth is characterized by its distinctive wing patterns and coloration, which provide it with camouflage against predators. Xyloskenea grahami plays a role in its ecosystem as a pollinator and as a part of the food chain, contributing to the ecological balance. Its conservation status is not well-documented, highlighting the need for further research into its population dynamics and habitat requirements.

Bath and North East Somerset (commonly referred to as BANES or B&NES) is a unitary authority created on 1 April 1996, following the abolition of the County of Avon, which had existed since 1974. Part of the ceremonial county of Somerset, Bath and North East Somerset occupies an area of 220 square miles (570 km2), two-thirds of which is green belt. It stretches from the outskirts of Bristol, south into the Mendip Hills and east to the southern Cotswold Hills and Wiltshire border. The city of Bath is the principal settlement in the district, but BANES also covers Keynsham, Midsomer Norton, Radstock and the Chew Valley. The area has a population of 170,000, about half of whom live in Bath, making it 12 times more densely populated than the rest of the area. In the United Kingdom, the term listed building refers to a building or other structure officially designated as being of special architectural, historical, or cultural significance; Grade II* structures are those considered to be "particularly significant buildings of more than local interest". Listing was begun by a provision in the Town and Country Planning Act 1947. Once listed, strict limitations are imposed on the modifications allowed to a building's structure or fittings. In England, the authority for listing under the Planning (Listed Buildings and Conservation Areas) Act 1990 rests with Historic England, a non-departmental public body sponsored by the Department for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport; local authorities have a responsibility to regulate and enforce the planning regulations. Bath and North East Somerset has 212 Grade II* listed buildings.
topic: Grade II* listed buildings in Bath and North East Somerset

text: Grade II* listed buildings in Bath and North East Somerset refer to structures that are recognized by Historic England as being particularly significant and of more than special interest, falling under the category of Grade II* within the UK's system of protected heritage. This designation is assigned to approximately 5.5% of listed buildings, indicating a higher level of historical or architectural importance. In Bath and North East Somerset, the selection includes a diverse range of buildings, such as Georgian architecture, churches, and monuments that reflect the area's rich history and architectural heritage. Notable examples include the Royal Crescent and the Bath Assembly Rooms, which are celebrated for their design and historical context. The preservation of these buildings is crucial for heritage conservation and contributes to the character and identity of the region.
topic: Grade II* listed buildings in Bath and North East Somerset

text: Tony Delaney (born 27 December 1975) is a former Australian rules footballer. He played as a midfielder. During his nine years in the Australian Football League he played for three clubs, Essendon, Fremantle and St Kilda. Injuries restricted him to only 76 games in this time. He is most notable as being traded by Essendon to Fremantle along with Dale Kickett and Todd Ridley which enabled the Bombers to recruit star players Scott Lucas and Matthew Lloyd. After he retired from the AFL he returned to the WAFL and played for Claremont from 2004 until 2006. He had previously played for South Fremantle during his time with Fremantle.

Tony Delaney is a former professional footballer from England, known for his role as a midfielder. Born in 1971 in Manchester, Delaney began his career in the youth system of Manchester City before progressing to the first team. He went on to play for various clubs throughout the 1990s, including Stockport County, where he made a significant impact. Delaney's playing style was characterized by his passing ability and tactical awareness. After retiring from professional football, he transitioned into coaching and has been involved in youth development in football. His contributions to the sport have been recognized at local levels, highlighting his influence on upcoming players.
St. Lucas or Saint Lucas is a city in Fayette County, Iowa, United States. The population was 167 at the time of the 2020 census.

St. Lucas is a city located in Fayette County, Iowa, United States. Established in the late 19th century, it is known for its agricultural roots and small-town charm. The population, as of the 2020 census, was 239 residents. St. Lucas features several local businesses, community events, and recreational opportunities, reflecting its close-knit community culture. The city is part of the Decorah metropolitan area and is characterized by its rural landscape and historic architecture.

Kheeston Randall (born May 7, 1990) is an American former professional football defensive tackle. He was a member of the Miami Dolphins, Cincinnati Bengals, and Minnesota Vikings of the National Football League (NFL). Prior to that he played college football for the Texas Longhorns.

Kheeston Randall is a former American football offensive lineman who played college football at the University of Texas at Austin. Born on April 20, 1991, in Killeen, Texas, he gained recognition for his performance as a guard and tackle during his collegiate career. Randall was selected by the Miami Dolphins in the seventh round of the 2013 NFL Draft. He had a brief tenure with the Dolphins and later spent time on the practice squads of various teams, including the New York Jets and the San Diego Chargers, before transitioning from professional football to other pursuits. Known for his versatility on the offensive line, Randall contributed to his teams' blocking schemes and depth through his work ethic and adaptability.

Breanna Mackenzie Stewart ( bree-ANN-ə; Born, August 27, 1994), nicknamed "Stewie", is an American professional basketball player for the New York Liberty of the Women's National Basketball Association (WNBA) and for the Mist of Unrivaled. She is one of the most accomplished basketball players in history. Stewart is a founder of the Unrivaled basketball league together with Napheesa Collier. In high school, Stewart was the National Gatorade Player of the Year, the Gatorade Female Athlete of the Year, and a McDonald's All-American. She led the University of Connecticut Huskies to four consecutive national championships, was named the Final Four's most outstanding player a record four times, and was a three-time consensus national player of the year. She was the first overall pick in the 2016 WNBA draft and was named the 2016 WNBA Rookie of the Year. She was named the WNBA MVP in 2018 and 2023, and was named an All-Star in 2017, 2018, 2021, 2022 and 2023. She led the Storm to two championships in 2018 and 2020, and received the WNBA Finals MVP award both times. In 2021, Stewart was named to The W25 as one of the top 25 players of the WNBA's first 25 years. Stewart is a two-time EuroLeague Women champion and was MVP of the league in 2019, as well as winning national league titles in Russia and Türkiye. As a member of the U.S. women's national team, Stewart has won gold medals in the 2016, 2020, and 2024 Olympics and at the 2014, 2018, and 2022 FIBA World Cups.

Breanna Stewart is an American professional basketball player, widely regarded as one of the best female athletes in the sport. Born on August 27, 1994, in Syracuse, New York, she played college basketball for the University of Connecticut (UConn) from 2012 to 2016, where she won four consecutive NCAA championships and earned numerous awards, including the Naismith College Player of the Year. Stewart was selected first overall in the 2016 WNBA Draft by the Seattle Storm and has since led her team to multiple championships, earning WNBA Finals MVP honors. In addition to her success in the WNBA, she has represented the United States in international competitions, winning gold medals at the Olympics and FIBA World Championships. Stewart is known for her versatility, scoring ability, and defensive skills, and has significantly contributed to the growth of women's basketball.
Vladan Adžić (Serbian Cyrillic: Владан Аџић; born 5 July 1987) is a Montenegrin professional footballer who plays as a centre-back for Budućnost.

Vladan Adžić is a Serbian professional football coach and former player, recognized for his contributions to various clubs in the Serbian league. Born on January 6, 1974, in Belgrade, Serbia, he played as a midfielder before transitioning to coaching. Adžić began his managerial career in the early 2000s and has held positions at several clubs, where he is noted for his tactical acumen and ability to develop young talent. His coaching philosophy emphasizes teamwork and discipline, which have garnered him respect within the football community. As of 2023, he continues to be active in the coaching sphere, contributing to the development of football in Serbia.

The Warrington Cavaillé-Coll Organ is housed in Parr Hall in Warrington (UK). It is one of the few surviving pipe organs in the UK that were built by the French organ builder Aristide Cavaillé-Coll (1811–99).

The Warrington Cavaillé-Coll Organ is a historic pipe organ located in the Parish Church of St. Elphin in Warrington, England. Built by the renowned French organ builder Aristide Cavaillé-Coll in 1884, it is one of the few surviving examples of his work in the UK. The instrument is notable for its rich tonal palette and innovative design, reflecting Cavaillé-Coll's influence on the development of symphonic organ music. The organ underwent restorations in the 20th century to preserve its integrity and functionality, and it remains an important cultural and musical asset within the local community, frequently featured in concerts and recitals.

Akuri is a spicy scrambled egg dish eaten in Parsi cuisine of India. Akuri is cooked until almost runny; the eggs are never overcooked. The main flavouring is fried onions and the spices used are ginger, coriander, chopped chilis, and black pepper. Akuri is traditionally eaten with pav or double roti (types of Indian bread). A less common version of akuri is bharuchi akuri, which contains nuts and dry fruits like cashews, almonds and raisins in addition to the other spices. This dish supposedly originated from the city of Bharuch in Gujarat, hence the name. Egg bhurji is a similar egg dish eaten in many parts of the Indian subcontinent. Connoisseurs of these Indian scrambled egg varieties would argue that egg bhurjee and akuri are almost identical but distinct in taste.

Akuri is a traditional Parsi dish originating from the Indian subcontinent, specifically associated with the Zoroastrian community. It is a spicy scrambled egg dish characterized by the use of eggs, onions, tomatoes, and a variety of spices, often including turmeric, chili powder, and coriander. Akuri is typically served with bread, such as paav or toast, and is commonly enjoyed for breakfast or as a light meal. The dish reflects the culinary practices of the Parsi community, which combines Persian heritage with Indian flavors, showcasing their unique gastronomic culture. Akuri is often associated with social gatherings and is a popular choice in Parsi households.

Švajcarnica (Hungarian: Őrhely, Serbian Cyrillic: Швајцарница) is a settlement in the region of Baranja, Croatia. Administratively, it is located in the Darda municipality within the Osijek-Baranja County. Population is 196 people (2011 census).

Švajcarnica is a peak in the western part of the Košuta mountain range, located on the border between Slovenia and Austria. It stands at an elevation of 2,132 meters (6,998 feet) and is known for its distinctive triangular shape. The peak is a popular destination for hikers and climbers, offering scenic views and access to various trails. Its name, derived from the Slovene word for "Swiss," reflects its alpine characteristics reminiscent of the Swiss Alps. The area around Švajcarnica is also characterized by diverse flora and fauna, contributing to its ecological significance.

Chasing Shadows is the third studio album by American heavy metal band Black Tide. It was released on October 16, 2015. This is the first official studio release to not feature original bassist Zakk Sandler and drummer Steven Spence. The album's first single titled "Angel in the Dark" was released on September 15, 2015.

"Chasing Shadows" is the third studio album by American heavy metal band Black Tide, released on March 5, 2016. The album features a blend of traditional heavy metal and modern rock elements, showcasing the band's signature sound and musicianship. Produced by Josh Wilbur, the album received generally positive reviews from critics. It includes singles such as "My Darkest Hour" and "Barbarians," which highlight the band's evolving musical style. "Chasing Shadows" reflects themes of struggle and perseverance, continuing the band's exploration of personal and societal issues established in their previous works.

The Human Microbiome Project (HMP), completed in 2012, laid the foundation for further investigation into the role the microbiome plays in overall health and disease. One area of interest is the role which delivery mode plays in the development of the infant/neonate microbiome and what potential implications this may have long term. It has been found that infants born via vaginal delivery have microbiomes closely mirroring that of the mother's vaginal microbiome, whereas those born via cesarean section tend to resemble that of the mother's skin. One notable study from 2010 illustrated an abundance of Lactobacillus and other typical vaginal genera in stool samples of infants born via vaginal delivery and an abundance of Staphylococcus and Corynebacterium, commonly found on the skin surfaces, in stool samples of infants born via cesarean section. From these discoveries came the concept of vaginal seeding, also known as microbirthing, which is a procedure whereby vaginal fluids (and hence vaginal microbes) are applied to a new-born child delivered by caesarean section. The idea of vaginal seeding was explored in 2015 after Maria Gloria Dominguez-Bello discovered that birth by caesarean section significantly altered the newborn child's microbiome compared to that of vaginal birth. The purpose of the technique is to recreate the natural transfer of bacteria that the baby gets during a vaginal birth. It involves placing swabs in the mother's vagina, and then wiping them onto the baby's face, mouth, eyes and skin. Due to the long-drawn nature of studying the impact of vaginal seeding, there are a limited number of studies available that support or refute its use. The evidence suggests that applying microbes from the mother's vaginal canal to the baby after cesarean section may aid in the partial restoration of the infant's natural gut microbiome with an increased likelihood of pathogenic infection to the child via vertical transmission.

Vaginal seeding is a process in which newborns are exposed to their mother’s vaginal microbiota shortly after birth, typically through the application of vaginal fluid to the infant's skin or mouth. This practice has emerged as a response to concerns about the potential negative effects of cesarean section deliveries on an infant's microbiome, as these babies are not exposed to the maternal vaginal and gut bacteria during birth. Proponents suggest that vaginal seeding may help establish a healthy microbiome and reduce the risk of certain health issues, including allergies and autoimmune disorders. However, the practice is controversial and raises concerns regarding safety, ethics, and the lack of comprehensive scientific evidence to support its benefits. As of now, medical guidelines emphasize that more research is needed before vaginal seeding can be widely recommended.

The rotatores muscles (rotatores spinae muscles) lie beneath the multifidus and are present in all spinal regions but are most prominent in the thoracic region. Each muscle is small and somewhat quadrilateral in form and each segment contains a rotatores brevis and a rotatores longus portion; they arise from the superior and posterior part of the transverse process, and are inserted into the lower border and lateral surface of the lamina of the two vertebra above, the fibers extending as far as the root of the spinous process for the rotatores longus. The first thoracic rotatores muscle is found between the first and second thoracic vertebrae; the last, between the eleventh and twelfth. Sometimes the number of these muscles is diminished by the absence of one or more from the upper or lower end. The rotatores muscles have a high density of proprioceptors and have been implicated in postural control.

The rotatores muscles are a group of small, deep muscles located in the back, primarily positioned between the vertebrae of the spine. They are part of the transversospinalis muscle group and can be found spanning one to two vertebrae. Their primary function is to facilitate the rotation and stabilization of the spinal column, contributing to movements such as twisting and bending. The rotatores are innervated by the dorsal rami of spinal nerves and play a significant role in maintaining posture and facilitating complex spinal motion. They are divided into the rotatores breves and rotatores longi, with the breves attaching to the adjacent vertebrae and the longi connecting to the vertebrae two levels above.

Scotts Head Lighthouse is a small lighted navigational aid in Scott's Head, Dominica. It is a cylindrical metal post approximately 5 meters (16.4 ft) tall, with the lamp at the top of the post. It emits a quick-flashing white (Q.W.) light. It is located at the top of the Scotts Head peninsula (or Cachacrou peninsula), in southwestern Dominica. The peninsula is considered a tied island, due to its being connected to the mainland by a tombolo.

Scotts Head Lighthouse is a maritime navigation aid located on Scotts Head, New South Wales, Australia. Commissioned in 1859, the lighthouse was built to improve safety for vessels navigating the nearby coast. Featuring a cylindrical design with a height of 22 meters, it stands on a rocky outcrop overlooking the Pacific Ocean. The original light source was a kerosene lantern, which has since been replaced by an automated electric light. The lighthouse is a significant heritage site, not only for its historical importance but also for its scenic views, and it is accessible to the public, contributing to local tourism.
The 1989–90 Primera División season was the second category of the Spanish basketball league system during the 1989–90 season. It was the second played with the name of Primera División.

The 1989–90 Primera División de Baloncesto, also known as Liga ACB, was the 7th season of Spain's top-tier professional basketball league. The season featured 18 teams competing for the championship title. Real Madrid emerged as the league champions, securing their title after a competitive season. The league, which commenced in October 1989 and concluded in June 1990, showcased notable players and teams, contributing to the growth of basketball in Spain during this period. The competition format included a regular season followed by playoffs to determine the champion. The season is remembered for its intense matchups and the development of Spanish basketball talent.
The Swoboda Bakery was built in 1888 in the Little Bohemia neighborhood of Omaha, Nebraska. It was listed on the National Register of Historic Places on July 19, 1996.

Swoboda Bakery is a family-owned bakery located in Chicago, Illinois, known for its artisanal bread, pastries, and specialty baked goods. Founded in 1924 by Czech immigrants, the bakery emphasizes traditional baking techniques and high-quality ingredients. Over the decades, Swoboda Bakery has garnered a reputation for its sourdough, rye bread, and a variety of seasonal pastries, serving both individual customers and local businesses. The bakery has maintained a loyal customer base and has expanded its offerings to include gluten-free options, reflecting changing dietary preferences. Swoboda Bakery is recognized for its contributions to the local food scene and its commitment to preserving traditional baking methods.

L'Aplec del Caragol is a meeting that is held annually, on a weekend in May, on the banks of the river Segre and the Champs Elysees in Lleida since 1980. It is a gastronomic event where snails are the protagonists and which incorporates music, brass bands and parades through the city. In 2010 there were 200,000 visitors and twelve tons of snails were consumed. These are shipped in from Spain, North Africa and South America. Apart from the snail feast, the event includes numerous other activities such as concerts and charanga music, castells, performances, and competitions.

L'Aplec del Caragol is an annual gastronomic festival held in the city of Lleida, Catalonia, Spain, dedicated to the celebration of snails and local cuisine. Established in 1980, the festival typically takes place during the first weekend of May and attracts thousands of visitors. The event features a variety of culinary activities, including cooking competitions, tastings, and music performances. Participants often set up communal dining areas to enjoy traditional dishes that highlight snails, alongside other regional foods. Over the years, L'Aplec del Caragol has become a significant cultural event in the area, promoting local gastronomy and fostering community engagement.
Rauksi is a village in Lääneranna Parish, Pärnu County, in southwestern Estonia. It has a population of 15 (as of 1 January 2011).

Rauksis are unique geological formations found primarily on the island of Gotland in Sweden, characterized by their limestone structures. Formed during the Silurian period, these large pillars or blocks of rock typically rise dramatically from the surrounding landscape. Rauksis are often situated along coastlines and are known for their distinct shapes, resulting from centuries of erosion and weathering. They are not only of geological interest but also serve as popular tourist attractions, offering scenic views and opportunities for photography. The presence of rauks contributes significantly to the natural heritage and biodiversity of Gotland, making them a subject of study for geologists and ecologists alike.

The Belgium men's national ice hockey team is the national men's ice hockey team of Belgium, and has been a member of the International Ice Hockey Federation (IIHF) since 1908. They have played in four Olympic Games, the last in 1936. The team also participated in the European Championships from 1910 to 1932. They have won 2 bronze medals (1910, 1911), 1 silver medal (1927), and 1 gold medal (1913).
topic: Belgium men's national ice hockey team

text: The Belgium men's national ice hockey team represents Belgium in international ice hockey competitions. Governed by the Royal Belgian Ice Hockey Federation, the team is a member of the International Ice Hockey Federation (IIHF). Belgium has participated in various international tournaments, including the IIHF World Championships, where they have competed at different levels since the late 20th century. The team has traditionally faced stiff competition from more established ice hockey nations in Europe. Despite this, the Belgian national team has made significant progress in developing the sport domestically, with a growing number of local leagues and developmental programs aimed at fostering talent. The team's colors are red, yellow, and black, reflecting the national flag of Belgium.
topic: Belgium men's national ice hockey team

text: Comendador is the capital of the Elías Piña province of the Dominican Republic. It has a border crossing to the Haitian town Belladère.

Comendador is the capital of the Elías Piña province in the Dominican Republic, situated in the southwestern part of the country near the border with Haiti. Known for its agricultural activities, the town's economy is largely based on farming, particularly the cultivation of crops such as coffee and cacao. Comendador serves as a cultural and commercial center for the surrounding rural areas and is characterized by its small-town atmosphere and vibrant local traditions. The town is also notable for its historical significance and community-focused lifestyle.
Hubert Saint-Macary (born 18 May 1949) is a French actor. He is the brother of Xavier Saint-Macary.

Hubert Saint-Macary is a prominent French-born artist known for his contributions to contemporary art, particularly in the medium of painting and sculpture. Based in the United States since the late 20th century, Saint-Macary's work often explores themes of identity, culture, and the human experience. He has exhibited his art in various galleries and museums, gaining recognition for his unique style that combines abstract techniques with elements of realism. In addition to his artistic practice, Saint-Macary is involved in educational initiatives, promoting art appreciation and fostering creativity among emerging artists. His work has been included in numerous exhibitions and he is regarded as a significant figure in the evolution of modern art.

Marhanets urban territorial hromada (Ukrainian: Марганецька міська територіальна громада, romanized: Marhanetska miska terytorialna hromada) is one of the hromadas of Ukraine, located in Nikopol Raion within Dnipropetrovsk Oblast. The administrative center is the city of Marhanets. The area of ​​the territory is 201.32 square kilometres (77.73 mi2), the population of the hromada is 50,051 (2018).

Marhanets urban hromada is a municipal formation in the Dnipropetrovsk Oblast of Ukraine. It encompasses the city of Marhanets and several adjacent villages, serving as an administrative unit that allows for localized self-governance. The hromada was established during Ukraine's administrative reform aimed at decentralizing power and enhancing local governance. It is part of the larger Dnipro Raion and contributes to regional economic, cultural, and social activities. The governance structure is led by a mayor, and the hromada focuses on community development, infrastructure improvement, and service delivery to its residents.
Pseudolatirus pallidus is a species of sea snail, a marine gastropod mollusc in the family Fasciolariidae, the spindle snails, the tulip snails and their allies.

Pseudolatirus pallidus is a species of sea snail belonging to the family Eucyclidae, which is characterized by marine gastropod mollusks. This species was first described by Melvill & Standen in 1896. Pseudolatirus pallidus is found in the Indian Ocean, particularly off the coasts of Madagascar and Mozambique. The shell of this species is notable for its distinctively pale coloration and specific morphological features that distinguish it from related species within the genus Pseudolatirus. Like other members of its family, Pseudolatirus pallidus is part of the diverse marine ecosystems where it plays a role in the ecological balance of its habitat.
William James Beal (March 11, 1833 – May 12, 1924) was an American botanist. He was a pioneer in the development of hybrid corn and the founder of the W. J. Beal Botanical Garden.

William James Beal (1833–1924) was an American botanist and a pioneering figure in the field of plant ecology. He is best known for his work on plant propagation and his efforts in the development of the Michigan Agricultural College (now Michigan State University). Beal conducted significant research in the area of seed dormancy and is renowned for the establishment of the "Beal's seed experiment," which sought to determine the longevity of seeds buried in the soil. His contributions to botany, particularly in understanding plant behavior and adaptation, have had a lasting impact on agricultural science and ecology. Beal served as a professor and was involved in various professional organizations throughout his career, influencing both education and research in the botanical sciences.

Oliver Barker-Vormawor is a Ghanaian lawyer, activist, and academic known for his role as a co-founder and lead convener of the #FixTheCountry movement, a social and political activism group in Ghana advocating for government accountability and systemic reforms.

Oliver Barker Vormawor is a Ghanaian lawyer and political activist, known for his involvement in social justice and governance issues within Ghana. He gained prominence as a leading figure in the advocacy for democratic reforms and the rule of law, often challenging governmental practices through legal avenues and public discourse. Vormawor is also recognized for his role in various civil society organizations, promoting transparency and accountability in governmental operations. His activism has led to notable public engagement and debate over human rights and political freedom in Ghana.

Jordanita graeca is a moth of the family Zygaenidae. It is found from southern Slovakia through Hungary, the Balkan Peninsula, Rhodes and Cyprus to southern Russia, Ukraine, Transcaucasia, Turkey, Iran and northern Iraq. The length of the forewings is 7.5–12.5 mm for males and 7–12 mm for females. Adults are on wing from May to July. The larvae of subspecies graeca feed on Centaurea melitensis and Cirsium creticum, while the larvae of subspecies sultana feed on Centaurea solstitialis, Centaurea salonitana, Carduus arabicus, Carduus uncinatus, Jurinea sordida and Xeranthemum annuum.

Jordanita graeca is a species of moth belonging to the family Cossidae, commonly known as the carpenter moths. It was first described by the entomologist Alpheus Spring Packard in 1885. This species is primarily found in Greece and other parts of southern Europe. Jordanita graeca is notable for its distinctive wing patterns and coloration, which vary among individuals. Adults typically exhibit a wingspan of approximately 30 to 40 millimeters, and they are often observed in habitats that include grasslands and open woodlands. The larvae of J. graeca are known to feed on grasses, contributing to their ecological role in the ecosystem. As a member of the Cossidae family, J. graeca shares characteristics with other species within this group, including their wood-boring behavior during the larval stage.

Dirgantara Monument (Indonesian: Monumen Patung Dirgantara), also known as Gatot Kaca Monument after the Javanese wayang figure, is a monument located in Jakarta, Indonesia. It is also known as Tugu Pancoran, after tugu a word for statue and pedestal, and the South Jakarta subdistrict of Pancoran, where it is located. The monument was commissioned by President Sukarno in 1964 as a tribute to the Indonesian Air Force and early Indonesian aviators who flew against the Dutch to achieve independence.

The Dirgantara Monument is a prominent landmark located in Yogyakarta, Indonesia, dedicated to the history of Indonesian aviation. It was inaugurated on July 29, 1986, and is part of the larger Diponegoro Park complex. The monument features a large statue of an Indonesian pilot, symbolizing the nation's commitment to air transportation and the advancement of aviation technology. Inside, it houses a museum showcasing various aviation artifacts, aircraft models, and historical exhibits related to the Indonesian Air Force and the nation's aviation development. The Dirgantara Monument serves as a site of education and reflection on Indonesia's aerospace achievements and is a popular tourist attraction in the region.
Sari Amro (Arabic: ساري عمرو; born 15 November 1989) is a professional Saudi Arabian football player who currently plays as a left back.

Sari Amro is a Palestinian-American artist and activist known for her work in promoting Middle Eastern culture and women's rights through various media, including painting, photography, and performance art. Born in Palestine and raised in the United States, Amro's art often reflects themes of identity, displacement, and resilience, drawing on her experiences as a member of a diasporic community. She has exhibited her work in galleries and cultural institutions across the United States and internationally, and is recognized for her commitment to social justice and empowerment of marginalized voices. Amro also engages in community-based initiatives, teaching art to youth and advocating for cultural awareness.

Benjamin Eicher (born 31 March 1974 in Tübingen, Germany) is a film director famous for his cult film sequel Dei Mudder Sei Gesicht II and further feature-length gangster comedies. His company Los Banditos Films, founded in 2000 together with Timo Joh. Mayer, produces controversial movies with stylistic influences from American and French film. Lately they concentrate on filming in Street Gangster Movies. Dei Mudder Sei Gesicht I – III and Schaffe schaffe Scheiße baue are said to be Germany's most-watched underground movies. In addition to his feature-length movies Benjamin Eicher has directed numerous shorts, television commercials and music videos since 1996.

Born on [birth date], he has been instrumental in [describe relevant achievements, projects, or roles]. Eicher's work has significantly influenced [mention areas impacted or specific communities], and he is often cited for his expertise in [specializations or topics]. In addition to his professional pursuits, he is engaged in [any relevant community involvement or publications].

Bamuni Dam Tang-e Divan-e Mahtab (Persian: بموني دم تنگ ديون مهتاب, also Romanized as Bamūnī Dam Tang-e Dīvan-e Mahtāb) is a village in Tayebi-ye Sarhadi-ye Sharqi Rural District, Charusa District, Kohgiluyeh County, Kohgiluyeh and Boyer-Ahmad Province, Iran. At the 2006 census, its population was 38, in 6 families.

Bamuni Dam Tang-e Divan-e Mahtab is a significant water reservoir located in the Alborz Province of Iran. The dam was constructed primarily for irrigation and water supply purposes in the surrounding agricultural areas. It is named after the Bamuni region and the nearby Tang-e Divan-e Mahtab, reflecting the local geography. The dam plays a crucial role in managing the water resources in the region, contributing to the sustainability of local agriculture, and supporting local ecosystems. Its construction has also facilitated the development of recreational opportunities and enhanced the landscape's aesthetic value.

The 2003 UAW-DaimlerChrysler 400 was the third stock car race of the 2003 NASCAR Winston Cup Series season and the sixth iteration of the event. The race was held on Sunday, March 2, 2003, in North Las Vegas, Nevada at Las Vegas Motor Speedway, a 1.5 miles (2.4 km) permanent D-shaped oval racetrack. The race took the scheduled 267 laps to complete. At race's end, Matt Kenseth of Roush Racing would dominate the late stages of the race to win his seventh career NASCAR Winston Cup Series win and his first and only win of the season. To fill out the podium, Dale Earnhardt Jr. and Michael Waltrip of Dale Earnhardt, Inc. would finish second and third, respectively.

The 2003 UAW-DaimlerChrysler 400 was a NASCAR Sprint Cup Series race held on March 2, 2003, at the Las Vegas Motor Speedway in Las Vegas, Nevada. This marked the fifth race of the 2003 NASCAR season. The event was notable for its significant attendance and high-speed action on the 1.5-mile intermediate track. Jimmie Johnson won the race, marking his first career victory in the NASCAR Cup Series.
