The race featured 267 laps and showcased competitive performances from various drivers, with notable incidents and strategic pit stops influencing the outcome. The UAW-DaimlerChrysler 400 was part of a larger sponsorship and promotional effort between the United Auto Workers and DaimlerChrysler, aimed at highlighting the intersection of motorsports and auto manufacturing.
Alain Voss (29 April 1946 – 13 May 2011), also known as Al Voss, was a French-Brazilian comics artist.

Alain Voss is a French artist and illustrator known for his distinctive style that often incorporates elements of surrealism and fantasy. His work includes illustrations for children’s books, graphic novels, and various commercial projects. Voss has gained recognition for his ability to blend whimsical characters with intricate landscapes, appealing to both young audiences and adult collectors. His contributions to the field of illustration have been showcased in multiple exhibitions, and he has collaborated with various publishers and authors, solidifying his reputation in the modern visual arts landscape.

The Planters Bank Building is a historic commercial building at 200 East Hale Street in downtown Osceola, Arkansas. It is a Classical Revival brick and mortar structure, designed by Missouri architect Uzell Singleton Branson and built c. 1920. It is one of the most architecturally sophisticated buildings in the city, which is otherwise dominated by vernacular early 20th-century commercial architecture. It has housed banks for most of its existence, although it briefly served as city hall in 1943–44. The building was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1987.

The Planters Bank Building is a historic commercial structure located in Osceola, Arkansas. Built in 1914, it showcases a blend of Classical Revival and Commercial architectural styles, characterized by its brick facade and prominent ornamental details. The building served as a hub for local banking and commerce throughout the early 20th century. It is notable for its cultural and historical significance, reflecting the economic growth of Osceola during that period. The Planters Bank Building was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1997, recognizing its importance in the region's architectural and economic history.
Jean Deutz (November 29, 1618 in Amsterdam, -August 1673) was a Dutch merchant, banker and financier of his brother-in-law Grand pensionary Johan de Witt.

Jean Deutz (1851–1936) was a prominent French banker and businessman, known for his significant contributions to the banking industry and economic development in France. He played a crucial role in the establishment and growth of various financial institutions during the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Deutz was an advocate for financial reform and modernization, promoting innovative banking practices that shaped the landscape of French finance. His legacy includes the expansion of credit systems and investment in industry, which contributed to France's economic progress during a period of industrialization.

Shaun Marquase Lovings (born c. 1984) is an American college football coach. He is the defensive ends coach for the University of Louisiana at Lafayette. He previously coached for Dunbar High School, P. K. Yonge Developmental Research School, Mississippi State, Louisiana–Lafayette / Louisiana, Nicholls State,, Austin Peay and Arkansas State University. He played college football as a walk-on tight end for Howard. In 2020, following the resignation of Mark Hudspeth, Lovings was named interim head coach for the 2020 season. Due to COVID-19 the season was postponed after three games into the spring of 2021. He was replaced by Scotty Walden prior to the start of the spring season.

Marquase Lovings is an American entrepreneur and technology innovator known for his contributions to the development of artificial intelligence and machine learning applications. He gained prominence as the founder of several tech startups that focus on leveraging AI to enhance user experiences in various industries, including healthcare and finance. Lovings is also an advocate for diversity and inclusion in the tech sector, actively mentoring underrepresented groups in STEM fields. His work has garnered recognition through various awards and features in industry publications, highlighting his impact on the technology landscape.
Plectroglyphidodon sagmarius is a species of Perciformes in family Pomacentridae.

Plectroglyphidodon sagmarius is a species of marine fish belonging to the family Pomacentridae, commonly known as damselfishes. This species is native to the Indo-Pacific region, particularly found around coral reefs and rocky substrates, where it prefers shallow waters. Plectroglyphidodon sagmarius exhibits a distinct coloration with blue and yellow hues, which aid in camouflage and social interactions. Like other damselfishes, it is known for its territorial behavior and forms symbiotic relationships with coral reefs. The species plays a significant role in the reef ecosystem, contributing to the maintenance of coral health by promoting algae grazing. Conservation status assessments indicate that Plectroglyphidodon sagmarius is currently not considered threatened, although habitat degradation poses potential risks to its populations.

Patta (Hindi: पट्टा) is a type of land deed issued by the government to an individual or organization. The term is used in India and certain other parts of South Asia for a small piece of land, granted by the government to an approved cultivator with a land revenue exemption. The period of exemption is what is required to bring the land under cultivation. These land deeds were associated with the process of land reform in India, after the colonial period. A person holding a patta is known as a pattadar.

A Patta is a legal document that signifies ownership of land, primarily used in India. It serves as evidence of title and is issued by the government to confirm that an individual or entity holds rights to a specific piece of land, often after a survey or verification process. Patta is particularly significant in agricultural contexts, enabling farmers to establish their rights over the land they cultivate. The document includes details such as the land's boundaries, agricultural classification, and ownership status. Obtaining a Patta can facilitate access to various government benefits and schemes related to agriculture and land use.

Khanrud (Persian: خانرود, also Romanized as Khānrūd; also known as Eslām Rūd) is a village in Torqabeh Rural District, Torqabeh District, Torqabeh and Shandiz County, Razavi Khorasan Province, Iran. At the 2006 census, its population was 374, in 122 families.

Khanrud, also known as Khānroud, is a river located in the central region of Iran, primarily flowing through Yazd Province. It originates from the mountains of the central plateau and travels southeastward, eventually merging into the Brahimi River. The river serves as an essential water source for agricultural activities in the arid regions it traverses. Historically significant for its role in supporting local communities, Khanrud has been pivotal in the development of irrigation systems in the area. Despite its importance, the river faces challenges such as water scarcity and pollution, largely due to over-extraction and rapid urbanization.

Amara Raja Group is an Indian multinational conglomerate, headquartered in Tirupati. The group has a presence in the automotive battery business, packaged foods and beverages, electronics products manufacturing, infrastructure sector, power system production and fabrication of sheet metal products and fasteners. The Amara Raja Group is known for its automotive battery brand Amaron. Amara Raja Group employs a workforce of more than 15,216 employees. Amara Raja Batteries was named on Asia's 'Best Under A Billion' 2010 list of companies compiled by Forbes magazine.

Amara Raja Group is an Indian multinational conglomerate headquartered in Tirupati, Andhra Pradesh. Founded in 1985 by Ramachandra N. Galla, it initially focused on battery manufacturing and has since expanded into various sectors such as electronics, renewable energy, and food and agro-processing. The group is best known for its flagship company, Amara Raja Batteries, which is one of the largest manufacturers of lead-acid batteries in India. The group emphasizes sustainable development and innovation, contributing to India's energy needs through alternative energy solutions. Additionally, it is involved in CSR activities, particularly in education and community development initiatives.

Marooned in Iraq (Persian: گم‌گشته‌ای در عراق/Gomgashte i dar Aragh, and also known as Songs of My Motherland Persian: آوازهای سرزمین مادری‌ام) is a 2002 Iranian (Kurdish/Persian) film written and directed by Bahman Ghobadi and produced in Iran. It was screened in the Un Certain Regard section at the 2002 Cannes Film Festival.

"Marooned in Iraq" is a 2002 documentary film directed by David LaChapelle that chronicles the experiences of a group of individuals who become stranded in Iraq during the aftermath of the 2003 invasion. The film features interviews and footage that highlight the challenges and complexities faced by those remaining in the region, including local residents and foreign nationals caught in the conflict. It addresses themes of isolation, survival, and the human condition amidst geopolitical turmoil. The documentary was well-received for its raw portrayal of life in war-torn Iraq and contributes to the broader conversation about the impact of war on civilian populations.

Dave's Picks Volume 43 is a three-CD live album by the rock band the Grateful Dead. It was recorded on November 2, 1969 at the Family Dog at the Great Highway in San Francisco, California, and on December 26, 1969 at McFarlin Memorial Auditorium in University Park, Texas. It was released on July 29, 2022, in a limited edition of 25,000 copies. The two concerts were taped by audio engineer and LSD chemist Owsley "Bear" Stanley. They document the band in a transition period between the recording of the psychedelic rock album Live/Dead and the folk rock album Workingman's Dead. Upon release, the album debuted on the Billboard 200 at number 11 on the week of August 13, 2022, becoming the group's second-highest charting album, behind 1987's In the Dark. The third song of the second set of the December 26 concert, "Cold Rain and Snow", was omitted due to lack of space. It was included as a bonus track on Dave's Picks Volume 44.

Dave's Picks Volume 43 is a live album by the American rock band Grateful Dead, released on February 3, 2023. It is the 43rd installment in the Dave's Picks series, which features archived concert recordings selected by band archivist Dave Lemieux. This volume captures a concert performed on June 10, 1974, at theRFK Stadium in Washington, D.C. The release includes remastered versions of the band's performances, highlighting their improvisational style and musical variety. It features popular tracks and extended jams, showcasing the band's evolution during the early 1970s. Dave's Picks Volume 43 is part of an ongoing effort to preserve and disseminate the Grateful Dead's extensive live recordings.

A kugelblitz (German: [ˈkuːɡl̩ˌblɪt͡s] ) (English: ball lightning) is a theoretical astrophysical object predicted by general relativity. It is a concentration of heat, light, or radiation so intense that its energy forms an event horizon and becomes self-trapped. In other words, if enough radiation is aimed into a region of space, the concentration of energy can warp spacetime so much that it creates a black hole. This would be a black hole the original mass–energy of which was in the form of radiant energy rather than matter; however, there is currently no uniformly accepted method of distinguishing black holes by origin. (See the no-hair theorem.) John Archibald Wheeler's 1955 Physical Review paper entitled "Geons" refers to the kugelblitz phenomenon and explores the idea of creating such particles (or toy models of particles) from spacetime curvature. This paper coined the term kugelblitz. A study published in Physical Review Letters in 2024 argues that the formation of a kugelblitz is impossible due to dissipative quantum effects like vacuum polarization, which prevent sufficient energy buildup to create an event horizon. The study concludes that such a phenomenon cannot occur in any realistic scenario within our universe. While the intensity needed to directly verify this calculation is 50 orders of magnitude higher than the level of technology allows as of 2024, the spontaneous disintegration of a high-energy photon into an electron-positron pair (the Schwinger effect) only requires 1000 times more energy than the most advanced lasers can produce. It has been speculated that the kugelblitz could be the basis interstellar engines (drives) for future black hole starships.

In astrophysics, a kugelblitz refers to a theoretical phenomenon where a region of space becomes so densely concentrated with energy that it forms a black hole. The term, originating from the German words "Kugel" meaning ball and "Blitz" meaning lightning, suggests that light or radiation can generate enough gravitational pull to collapse into a singularity, similar to how mass does. This concept explores the interplay between energy and gravity, indicating that it is not mass alone that can create a black hole, but also extreme concentrations of energy. Kugelblitzes are often discussed in the context of general relativity and speculative cosmology, particularly concerning the nature of black holes and the limits of our understanding of physics at high-energy scales.

In genetics, loss of heterozygosity (LOH) is a type of genetic abnormality in diploid organisms in which one copy of an entire gene and its surrounding chromosomal region are lost. Since diploid cells have two copies of their genes, one from each parent, a single copy of the lost gene still remains when this happens, but any heterozygosity (slight differences between the versions of the gene inherited from each parent) is no longer present.

Loss of heterozygosity (LOH) refers to the loss of one allele at a locus in an organism's genome, resulting in the organism being homozygous at that locus. This phenomenon is significant in the context of cancer biology and genetics, as it can lead to the inactivation of tumor suppressor genes, thereby contributing to tumor progression. LOH can occur through various mechanisms, including chromosomal deletions, mitotic non-disjunction, or uniparental disomy. It is often used as a biomarker for identifying regions of the genome that may harbor critical mutations associated with certain diseases, particularly in oncology. LOH is detected using techniques such as microsatellite analysis and single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) arrays.
Tommy Hayes (born 13 December 1973 in Tokoroa, New Zealand) is a Cook Island international rugby union player. He plays as a fly-half.

Tommy Hayes (born 27 March 1973) is a retired Irish rugby union player known for his position as a lock. He played for Leinster between 1995 and 1999, making a notable contribution to the team during his tenure. Hayes also represented the Irish national team, earning caps and participating in international competitions. After retiring from professional rugby, he has been involved in coaching and development within the sport. His legacy includes inspiring future generations of rugby players in Ireland.
Gusztáv Nemeskéri (born 1960), known as The Katóka Street Killer, is a Hungarian serial killer who killed 4 people between 1996 and 1999 in order to settle his debts.

Gusztáv Nemeskéri (1873–1949) was a Hungarian archaeologist and historian known for his contributions to the study of the prehistoric and ancient history of Hungary. He played a significant role in the development of modern archaeological practices in Hungary and was instrumental in the establishment of various museums and archaeological programs. His research focused on the Neolithic and Bronze Age cultures of the Carpathian Basin, and he published numerous works that advanced the understanding of early human habitation in the region. Nemeskéri's legacy includes a greater appreciation for the cultural complexities of ancient Hungarian societies.

Pearcea is a genus of tropical herbaceous plants in the family Gesneriaceae native to western South America. It is classified in tribe Gloxinieae and is closely related to the genus Kohleria, in which some of its species were previously included. The genus Parakohleria has recently been synonymized under Pearcea, a conclusion later supported by molecular analyses that showed that Pearcea hypocyrtiflora was nested within the former Parakohlerias. The best-known and most widely cultivated species is Pearcea hypocyrtiflora, a low-growing herb with attractively marked leaves and unusual bubble-like red or orange flowers.

Pearcea is a genus of flowering plants belonging to the family Orchidaceae, which comprises a group of orchids primarily found in the tropical regions of Central and South America. The genus was established to classify a variety of species characterized by their distinctive floral structures and adaptations to specific ecological niches. Pearcea orchids are notable for their unique morphology, including flattened leaves and intricate flower arrangements, often exhibiting a range of colors and patterns. These plants typically thrive in humid environments, such as cloud forests and rainforests, where they can access adequate moisture and light. Due to habitat loss and environmental changes, some Pearcea species face threats in their natural habitats, leading to conservation efforts aimed at preserving their populations and ecosystems.

The Aba Nigeria Temple is a temple of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints located on the outskirts of Aba, Abia State, Nigeria, north of the Ogbor River. Announced on April 2, 2000, by church president Gordon B. Hinckley during general conference, it became the first temple in Nigeria, the second in West Africa, and the third in Africa, following those in Johannesburg and Accra. The single-spire building is 11,500 square feet, has Namibian pearl granite on its exterior, and includes two instruction rooms, two sealing rooms, and a baptistry. The temple complex covers 6.3 acres and also includes patron housing, a stake center, and an administration office building.

The Aba Nigeria Temple is a sacred building of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (LDS Church), located in Aba, Nigeria. Announced in April 2019, the temple serves as a place of worship, reflection, and community for members of the LDS Church in Nigeria and the surrounding regions. The temple's construction reflects the church's growth in Africa and its commitment to serving its members in the region. The facility provides various religious services, including sacred ordinances and community events, promoting faith and fellowship among local church members. The Aba Nigeria Temple was dedicated on June 1, 2023, by Elder David A. Bednar of the Quorum of the Twelve Apostles, marking it as a significant addition to the church's infrastructure in Africa.

The 1996 season was the first season in New York/New Jersey MetroStars's existence, in the first season of American Major League Soccer. They played their home games at Giants Stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey. The team played 38 matches in the season including 35 Major League Soccer games and 3 friendlies. Eddie Firmani coached the team for their first eight games before being replaced by Carlos Queiroz. The team is now known as the New York Red Bulls; the entire organization is known as Red Bull New York.

The 1996 New York/New Jersey MetroStars season was the inaugural season for the Major League Soccer (MLS) team, which played its home games at Giants Stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey. The MetroStars were coached by Carlos Alberto Torres and finished the regular season in 8th place in the Eastern Conference. Throughout the season, they recorded a total of 8 wins, 20 losses, and 4 draws, scoring 35 goals and conceding 56. Despite failing to qualify for the playoffs, the season marked the beginning of professional soccer in the New York/New Jersey area, setting the stage for the team's future and the league’s development in the United States. Key players included goalkeeper Tony Meola and forward Roberto Donadoni, who were pivotal to the team's performance.

Bill Nilsson (17 December 1932 – 25 August 2013) was a Swedish professional motocross racer. He competed in the FIM Motocross World Championships from 1952 to 1967. Nilsson won the inaugural FIM Motocross World Championship in 1957 becoming the first official Motocross World Champion. He won a second World Championship in 1960 and was the first in a long line of Swedish motorcyclists who dominated the sport of motocross in the late 1950s and early 1960s including; Sten Lundin, Rolf Tibblin and Torsten Hallman. During a five year period from 1957 to 1961, Nilsson ranked first or second in the World Championships. He was a fiery, hot-tempered competitor known for his intimidating and aggressive riding style.

Bill Nilsson (1918–2008) was a prominent Swedish-American engineer and entrepreneur known for his contributions to the telecommunications industry. Born in Sweden, he emigrated to the United States in the mid-20th century, where he played a key role in the development of various communication technologies. Nilsson co-founded several technology companies and was instrumental in advancing satellite communication systems. His innovative work earned him numerous patents and recognized him as a leader in the field. In addition to his engineering accomplishments, he was an advocate for education in engineering and technology, mentoring young professionals throughout his career. Nilsson's legacy continues to influence the telecommunications sector.

Keith Joseph Andrews (born 13 September 1980) is an Irish football coach and former player who played as a defensive midfielder. He is the head coach of Premier League club Brentford. Andrews began his career at Wolverhampton Wanderers, where he was their youngest captain in over a century. His club career also involved stints at Hull City and Milton Keynes Dons, as well as loan spells at Oxford United, Stoke City and Walsall while he was at Wolves. While at Milton Keynes Dons he was club captain, and helped secure promotion for his team with a vital goal, helped win his team the Football League Trophy by scoring in the final at Wembley and they were named in the PFA Team of the Year. He joined Blackburn Rovers in September 2008 and spent three seasons at Ewood Park which included a loan spell at Ipswich Town. After a short stay with West Bromwich Albion Andrews joined Bolton Wanderers and after loans at Brighton & Hove Albion, Watford and a return to Milton Keynes Dons he retired in the summer of 2015. A full international from 2008 to 2012, Andrews gained 35 caps for the Republic of Ireland and was selected for UEFA Euro 2012.

Keith Andrews is a retired Irish professional footballer, born on 13 April 1980 in Dublin, Ireland. Primarily a midfielder, he began his career at Wolverhampton Wanderers before moving on to play for several clubs, including Kidderminster Harriers, and Brighton & Hove Albion, and achieving notable stints at Ipswich Town and Blackburn Rovers. Andrews is renowned for his leadership qualities, technical skills, and ability to score from midfield. On the international stage, he represented the Republic of Ireland, earning over 30 caps and participating in the UEFA Euro 2012 tournament. After retiring from professional play, he transitioned into coaching and media analysis.

The Solca (in its upper course also: Solcuța) is a right tributary of the river Suceava in Romania. It discharges into the Suceava in Gura Solcii. Its length is 34 km (21 mi) and its basin size is 165 km2 (64 sq mi).

The Solca is a river located in Romania, primarily flowing through the Suceava County in the northeastern part of the country. It is a tributary of the Moldova River, contributing to the hydrology of the region. The river is approximately 40 kilometers (25 miles) long and is known for its scenic landscapes and rich biodiversity, including various fish species. It originates in the Eastern Carpathians and flows into the Moldova, serving as a natural resource for local communities, facilitating activities such as fishing and recreational pursuits. The Solca is also significant for its historical and cultural context, as well as its role in the local ecosystem.

Lloyd Garrison "Chick" Davies (March 6, 1892 – September 5, 1973) was an American professional baseball player who played for the Philadelphia Athletics (1914–1915) and the New York Giants (1925–1926) as a pitcher and outfielder. He led the National League in saves (6) and games finished (29) in 1926. Davies was an alumnus of the University of Massachusetts Amherst. He died September 5, 1973, in Middletown, Connecticut.

Chick Davies (born 25 March 1898, date of death unknown) was a Welsh professional footballer known for his role as an inside forward. He began his career with Cardiff City before moving to play for several clubs, including Newport County and QPR. Davies was recognized for his technical skills and goal-scoring ability. He enjoyed a brief period in the English Football League and later played in the Welsh leagues. His contributions to football during the early 20th century are remembered by sports historians and fans.

Radio Edge was a Bangladeshi FM radio station, the headquarters of which radio is situated in Dhaka. It started broadcasting on 30 October 2017. Sometime in 2018 or 2019 the station shutdown, but the website is still online.

Radio Edge is a digital radio platform that provides streaming services for various music genres, talk shows, and live broadcasts. Launched in 2020, it aims to connect audiences with diverse content produced by independent artists and broadcasters. The platform features user-friendly navigation and personalized playlists, enabling listeners to discover new music and programs tailored to their preferences. Radio Edge often collaborates with local talents, promoting emerging voices in the industry, and hosts exclusive events and live performances. Its commitment to providing quality content and supporting creators has gained it a loyal listener base in the digital radio community.

Prospere is a surname. Notable people with the surname include: Alleyn Prospere (born 1979), Saint Lucian cricketer Alfred Prospere, Saint Lucian politician Jean Prosper Guivier (1814–1862), French musician, was known as Prospère

Prospere is a fictional character from the television series "The Real World," known for seeking personal growth and transformation throughout the show's narrative. Introduced in the show's later seasons, Prospere exemplifies themes of resilience and self-discovery, often facing challenges related to mental health, relationships, and societal expectations. The character has resonated with audiences for their relatable struggles and journey towards authenticity, making a significant impact in discussions around representation and personal narratives in reality television.

The 2010 Supertaça Compal was the first edition of the Supertaça Compal, taking place in Luanda, Angola from April 2 to 4, 2010. It was contested by four teams from Portugal and Angola in a single round robin system. Benfica of Portugal was the winner and Miguel Lutonda from Primeiro de Agosto was the tournament's MVP.

The 2010 Supertaça Compal was the annual Portuguese football Super Cup match contested between the Primeira Liga champions, Benfica, and the Taça de Portugal winners, Porto. Held on August 7, 2010, at the Estádio Municipal de Aveiro, Porto triumphed with a 2-0 victory, securing their 18th Supertaça title. The match featured goals from Falcão and Hulk, highlighting Porto's dominance in Portuguese football during that period. The Supertaça Compal is traditionally played at the beginning of the football season, serving as a showdown between the winners of the league and cup competitions from the previous season.

Princess Consort Munseong (25 August 1576 – 21 October 1623), of the Munhwa Yu clan, was the wife and queen consort of Yi Hon, King Gwanghae, the 15th Joseon monarch. She was queen consort of Joseon from 1608 until her husband's deposition in 1623, after which she was known as Deposed Queen Yu.

Deposed Queen Yu was a historical figure in ancient China, associated with the Zhou Dynasty. She is often regarded as a notable example of political intrigue during the period. Initially married to King Zhou of the Shang Dynasty, her reign was marked by the eventual overthrow of her husband due to his tyrannical rule. Although she was deposed, Yu's story is significant in understanding the transitions between dynasties and the role of women in power dynamics of ancient China. Her life reflects the complexities of royal lineage and the impact of political decisions on individual fates. References to Queen Yu can be found in various historical texts, which explore themes of power, betrayal, and the sociopolitical landscape of her time.

Rockin' Through the Decades is a live-action/animated TV special based on characters from Alvin and the Chipmunks. It was directed by Steve Karman, produced by Bagdasarian Productions, and premiered on NBC on December 9, 1990. The special was released on VHS by Buena Vista Home Video in 1992 as Rockin' with the Chipmunks and aired on the Disney Channel the same year. On September 8, 2009 it was released on DVD as part of the Paramount Home Entertainment release The Very First Alvin Show, which also included the first episode of The Alvin Show and the 1985 special A Chipmunk Reunion.

"Rockin' Through the Decades" is a musical variety television special that premiered on November 26, 1992, on the NBC network. The program features performances by various artists who pay tribute to the evolution of rock music from the 1950s through the 1990s. The show showcases iconic rock songs from each decade, with celebrated musicians recreating or covering classic hits, interspersed with commentary and historical context about rock music's influence on popular culture. The special was well-received for its nostalgic appeal and vibrant performances, highlighting the genre's lasting impact over the years.

Neopterygii (from Ancient Greek νέος (néos), meaning "new", and πτέρυξ (ptérux), meaning "wing, fin") is a subclass of ray-finned fish (Actinopterygii). Neopterygii includes the Holostei and the Teleostei, of which the latter comprise the vast majority of extant fishes, and over half of all living vertebrate species. While living holosteans include only freshwater taxa, teleosts are diverse in both freshwater and marine environments. Many new species of teleosts are scientifically described each year. The potentially oldest known neopterygian is the putative "semionotiform" Acentrophorus varians from the Middle Permian of Russia; however, one study incorporating morphological data from fossils and molecular data from nuclear and mitochondrial DNA, places this divergence date at least 284 mya (million years ago), during the Artinskian stage of the Early Permian. Another study suggests an even earlier split (360 myr ago, near the Devonian-Carboniferous boundary).

Neopterygii is a clade of bony fish that encompasses most modern ray-finned fishes, distinguishing them from other fish groups such as traditional actinopterygians. Originating in the late Paleozoic Era, Neopterygii includes diverse species that are characterized by various adaptations allowing for efficient locomotion and a wide range of habitats. Key subgroups within Neopterygii include Holostei, which encompasses gars and bowfin, and Teleostei, the most dominant group of fish in number and diversity today. Neopterygii are noted for their evolutionary innovations, such as a mobile predatory feeding mechanism and a highly flexible swim bladder that assists in buoyancy and movement. This clade plays a critical ecological and economic role in aquatic ecosystems and fisheries worldwide.
New Visions Academy is a chain of charter schools in Payson and St. Johns, Arizona.

New Visions Academy is an educational institution focused on providing an innovative learning environment for students. Founded to meet the needs of diverse learners, the academy emphasizes a curriculum that integrates technology, critical thinking, and social-emotional learning. It offers various programs aimed at fostering academic excellence and personal growth, catering to different educational levels. New Visions Academy aims to prepare students for future challenges by promoting creativity, collaboration, and community involvement. The academy is part of a broader movement towards reforming traditional education systems to enhance student engagement and achievement.

Tōtara Park is a suburb of Upper Hutt, New Zealand, located 2 km northeast of the city centre. It is accessed via the Tōtara Park Bridge which crosses the Hutt River, connecting it to State Highway 2 and the main Upper Hutt urban area. It was popular in the 1970s and 1980s for families moving into the Upper Hutt area. Most of the streets in Tōtara Park are named after states, towns and cities of the United States, with the main road running through the suburb being California Drive. Tōtara Park is built on alluvial gravel. The Wellington Fault runs through the suburb—one section of California Drive straddles the fault, and the road lanes are built on either side of the fault with a large central reservation between them. This was designed so houses would be offset at least 20 metres from the fault, hopefully limiting damage if the fault were to rupture. The Māori names for this area are Te Hau-karetu and for the Hutt River itself; Heretaunga, and Awa Kairangi. In December 2019, the official name of the suburb was gazetted as "Tōtara Park".

Tōtara Park is a large recreational park located in the suburb of Manurewa, Auckland, New Zealand. Spanning approximately 130 hectares, it features a mix of natural bush, open spaces, and sports facilities. The park is named after the native tōtara tree, significant in Māori culture. It includes walking and cycling paths, picnic areas, playgrounds, and sports fields, catering to various outdoor activities. Tōtara Park is also recognized for its ecological importance, providing habitat for native wildlife and offering educational opportunities for visitors about New Zealand's natural environment.

Martino da Canal was a 13th-century Venetian chronicler, whose only known work is the so-called Les estoires de Venise, a French chronicle of the history of Venice from its beginnings to 1275. It is particularly valuable for Canal's depiction of events during his own lifetime, particularly during the years 1267–1275, when he was at Venice.

Martino da Canal was a Venetian merchant and chronicler known for his travel writings in the 13th century. He is best recognized for "Il Milione" (The Million), a text that provides a detailed account of his travels to the East, including regions of the Byzantine Empire and the Mongol territories. Although often overshadowed by Marco Polo, da Canal's work offers valuable insights into the cultural and commercial exchanges of the period. His narrative reflects the intertwining of trade and exploration during a time of significant geopolitical change in Eurasia. The authenticity and accuracy of his accounts have been the subject of scholarly debate, but they remain an important part of the literary and historical record of medieval travel literature.

Vice Admiral Thomas J. Moore (born 1959) is a former senior officer in the United States Navy. He retired from active duty on June 19, 2020, after 39 years of service. He currently serves as Vice President of Customer Relations for the Mission Technologies Division at Huntington Ingalls Industries.

Admiral Thomas Moore (born July 12, 1870 – died September 4, 1940) was a prominent officer in the United States Navy known for his significant contributions during the early 20th century. He graduated from the United States Naval Academy in 1893 and served with distinction in various naval capacities, including during the Spanish-American War and World War I. Moore held several important commands, including command of USS Pennsylvania and the Navy's Bureau of Navigation. His leadership and expertise were instrumental in naval operations and personnel management during a transformative period for the U.S. Navy. He was promoted to rear admiral in 1935 and retired from active duty in 1938. Moore's legacy includes a commitment to naval education and modernization, influencing the Navy's developments in the years leading up to World War II.

Nordlinger is a surname. Notable people with the surname include: Jay Nordlinger (born 1963), American journalist Joseph Yuspa Nördlinger Hahn (died 1637), German rabbi Rachel Nordlinger, Australian linguist and academic Zelda Nordlinger (1932–2008), American feminist and women's rights activist

Nordlinger is a surname of German origin, commonly associated with individuals in various fields, including journalism, literature, and academia. Notable people with the surname include John Nordlinger, a prominent American journalist known for his work in political commentary and analysis. The name may also refer to various places or institutions named after individuals bearing the surname. The usage of Nordlinger reflects cultural and historical ties to German-speaking regions, where surnames can denote ethnic, occupational, or geographical roots.
Scythris potentillella is a moth of the family Scythrididae first described by the German entomologist Philipp Christoph Zeller in 1847. It is found in Asia Minor and Europe.

Scythris potentillella is a species of moth belonging to the family Scythrididae. It was first described by the entomologist Edward Meyrick in 1931. This moth is primarily found in regions of Europe and is associated with certain plant species, particularly from the genus Potentilla, which serves as a food source for its larvae. The adult moths exhibit a distinctive wing pattern that aids in camouflage among their natural habitat, enhancing their survival against predators. Like other members of its family, Scythris potentillella plays a role in its ecosystem as both a herbivore and a prey species.
Roger Huckfeldt (born August 22, 1958) is an American politician who served in the Wyoming House of Representatives from the 4th district from 1995 to 2003.

Roger Huckfeldt is an American political scientist renowned for his research on political communication, public opinion, and voting behavior. He is particularly noted for his work on the social context of political behavior, analyzing how interactions with peers, social networks, and political environments influence individual political attitudes and choices. Huckfeldt's contributions include numerous publications and studies that examine the dynamics of political discussions among voters and how these discussions shape electoral outcomes. His scholarship has been influential in both theoretical and empirical approaches within the field of political science, and he has served in various academic roles, including faculty positions at several universities.
Don Lawrence (born 1945) is an American vocal coach and musician from New York City.

Don Lawrence is a noted vocal coach and educator, renowned for his expertise in vocal technique, performance, and artistry. With a career spanning several decades, he has worked with a diverse range of clients, including aspiring singers, professional performers, and industry professionals. Lawrence is celebrated for his innovative teaching methods, which emphasize the integration of vocal health, artistry, and emotional expression. He has contributed to various music programs and workshops, helping numerous individuals develop their vocal abilities and achieve their artistic goals. Lawrence also shares his knowledge through online platforms and instructional materials, influencing a new generation of vocalists.
Nygellidae is a family of nematodes belonging to the order Dorylaimida. Genera: Nygellus Thorne, 1939

Nygellidae is a family of insects belonging to the order Hemiptera, specifically within the suborder Heteroptera. This family comprises small, flattened bugs known for their unique morphology and behavioral adaptations. Nygellidae is characterized by their piercing-sucking mouthparts and often exhibits a variety of colors and patterns that help with camouflage and predator evasion. Members of this family are typically found in diverse habitats, including forests, grasslands, and wetlands, where they feed on plant sap or fungi. They play a crucial role in the ecosystem as both herbivores and prey for various predators. Research on Nygellidae is ongoing, focusing on their taxonomy, ecology, and potential agricultural relevance.

The Commission on Growth and Development (informally known as the Growth Commission) was an independent body set up by the World Bank chaired by American economist Michael Spence that brought together 22 policy-makers, academics, and business leaders to examine various aspects of economic growth and development. Launched in 2006, the Commission set out to take stock of the state of theoretical and empirical knowledge on economic growth with a view to drawing implications for policy for the current and future policymakers. Its work culminated in two publications – The Growth Report: Strategies for Sustained growth and Inclusive Development in May 2008 – and Post-Crisis Growth in Developing Countries : A Special Report of the Commission on Growth and Development on the Implications of the 2008 Financial Crisis in October 2009. Five thematic volumes and nearly 70 working papers were also published by the Commission. The Growth Commission’s work was sponsored by the governments of Australia, the Netherlands, the United Kingdom, and Sweden, the William and Flora Hewlett Foundation, and the World Bank Group. The group's activities formally ended in June 2010.

The Growth Commission, formally known as the International Commission on Financing Global Education Opportunity, was established in 2016 to identify and promote innovative financing strategies for global education. Chaired by former UK Prime Minister Gordon Brown, the commission aimed to address the financing gap in education, particularly in developing countries, and to enhance the quality and accessibility of education for all. It brought together leaders from various sectors, including government, business, and civil society, to seek solutions that would increase investment in education and improve outcomes for children and youth worldwide. The commission's work culminated in a report presenting recommendations for achieving the United Nations Sustainable Development Goal on education.

KPUA (670 AM) is an American radio station licensed to serve the community of Hilo, Hawaii. The station, established in 1936 as "KHBC", has been owned and operated by New West Broadcasting Corporation since 1992. KPUA broadcasts a variety of local and syndicated talk radio programming, including some specialty Japanese language programs.

KPUA is an AM radio station located in Hilo, Hawaii, broadcasting on the frequency of 670 kHz. The station, which is owned by Pacific Radio Group, Inc., primarily features a talk radio format, along with programming that includes local news, sports, and community events. KPUA has been a key source of information and entertainment for residents in the Big Island area since its establishment in 1947. The station's signal covers a wide area of the Hawaiian Islands, making it an important platform for reaching various audiences with relevant content.

Good Food, known as BBC Good Food until 2024, is a global food media brand established in 1989, with a monthly magazine, website, app, live events and series of books. Keith Kendrick is Head of Magazines along with Dr. Keith Rowley, with Christine Hayes as BBC Good Food's first brand editorial director. Natalie Hardwick is the editor of bbcgoodfood.com. In November 2014, the BBC Good Food brand celebrated its 25th anniversary with a new logo, designed by international branding agency Lambie-Nairn. BBC Good Food magazine was awarded Food and Drink Magazine of the year at the DMA 2013 Digital Magazine Awards and Digital Magazine Of The Year at the PPA Digital Publishing Awards 2013. In 2018, rights to the brand were acquired from BBC Studios by Immediate Media. In 2024, the continued use of the BBC name ended and the brand was renamed Good Food starting with April 2024 issue.

Good Food is a meal kit delivery service based in Canada, offering customers the convenience of preparing fresh, chef-designed recipes with pre-portioned ingredients. Launched in 2014, the brand emphasizes sustainable sourcing and aims to simplify cooking at home while promoting healthy eating habits. Good Food provides a variety of meal plans catering to different dietary preferences and household sizes, and operates primarily through an online platform that allows users to customize their orders. The company has gained popularity for its user-friendly service and commitment to quality, contributing to the growing trend of meal kit subscriptions in the food industry.

In object-oriented programming, an interface or protocol type is a data type that acts as an abstraction of a class. It describes a set of method signatures, the implementations of which may be provided by multiple classes that are otherwise not necessarily related to each other. A class which provides the methods listed in an interface is said to implement the interface, or to adopt the protocol. Interfaces are useful for encapsulation and reducing coupling. For example, in Java, the Comparable interface specifies the method compareTo. Thus, a sorting method only needs to take objects of types which implement Comparable to sort them, without knowing about the inner nature of the class (except that two of these objects can be compared via compareTo()).

In object-oriented programming, an interface is a defined contract within which classes can implement specific methods without dictating how these methods should be carried out. An interface can contain method signatures and properties but does not provide the implementation details. This concept promotes a separation of concerns, allowing different classes to be developed independently while still ensuring that they adhere to specific expectations. Interfaces support polymorphism, enabling objects of different classes to be treated uniformly if they implement the same interface. Languages such as Java and C# utilize interfaces extensively, allowing developers to define multiple behaviors and interactions in a flexible manner.
Kurt Friedrich Werner Ludwig Wilhelm Haseloff (1894–1978) was a German general during World War II.

Kurt Haseloff (born 12 October 1945) is a German biochemist and molecular biologist known for his contributions to the fields of plant biology and biotechnology. He is notably recognized for his work on gene expression in plants and the development of techniques for plant transformation. Haseloff's research has advanced the understanding of gene regulation in response to environmental stimuli, leading to practical applications in agriculture and horticulture. He has held various academic positions, including a professorship at the University of Cambridge, where he has published extensively and mentored numerous students in the field. His innovative approaches have made significant impacts on the science of plant development and genetic engineering.

Peter Joseph Cattaneo MBE (born 1 July 1964) is a British filmmaker. He is best known for directing the comedy film The Full Monty, for which he won the MTV Movie Award for Best New Filmmaker and was nominated for the Academy Award for Best Director.

Peter Cattaneo is an English film and television director, best known for his work on the acclaimed 1997 film "The Full Monty," which received several awards and nominations, including an Academy Award nomination for Best Original Screenplay. Born on July 27, 1964, in London, Cattaneo studied at the University of Warwick and later at the Royal College of Art. He began his career directing short films and commercials, transitioning to feature films in the late 1990s. In addition to "The Full Monty," Cattaneo has directed projects such as "Lucky Break" (2001) and the television series "Rev" (2010-2014). His work often combines humor with poignant social themes, earning him recognition in the British film industry.
Pantopholis (meaning "more love") is a genus of prehistoric fish.

Pantopholis is a genus of extinct reptiles belonging to the family Sphenodontidae, which is closely related to modern tuataras. These prehistoric reptiles lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 100 million years ago. Fossil remains of Pantopholis have been discovered in North America, providing insight into the evolutionary history of reptiles. Characterized by their distinctive skull and dental features, Pantopholis is an important subject of study for paleontologists, as it helps to elucidate the diversity and adaptation of lepidosaurians during the Mesozoic era.

Opportunistic mobile social networks are a form of mobile ad hoc networks that exploit the human social characteristics, such as similarities, daily routines, mobility patterns, and interests to perform the message routing and data sharing. In such networks, the users with mobile devices are able to form on-the-fly social networks to communicate with each other and share data objects.

An opportunistic mobile social network is a type of decentralized social networking system that enables users to connect and share information directly with one another based on their physical proximity and similar social interests. These networks leverage opportunistic communication protocols, allowing data to be exchanged intermittently without relying on a continuous internet connection. Users can serve as nodes in a mesh network, facilitating communication through devices like smartphones and tablets. The design of opportunistic mobile social networks encourages the discovery of new social connections while overcoming limitations of conventional networks in areas with poor connectivity. Applications can vary from event coordination and community engagement to location-based services and emergency communication.

Two ships of the United States Navy have been named USS Ponchatoula or USNS Ponchatoula, after the Ponchatoula Creek in Louisiana. USS Ponchatoula (AOG-38) was a tanker commissioned in 1944. USNS Ponchatoula (T-AO-148) was a fleet oiler in service from 1956 to 1992.

USS Ponchatoula (AO-148) was a fleet oiler of the United States Navy, named after the city of Ponchatoula, Louisiana. Launched on 12 September 1944 and commissioned on 25 December 1944, the ship served during World War II and the Korean War, primarily providing underway refueling to naval vessels. Ponchatoula operated in the Pacific Theater, supporting operations by supplying fuel to ships and submarines. Following the Korean War, she continued her service into the 1970s, participating in various naval exercises and operations. The vessel was decommissioned in 1970 and stricken from the Naval Vessel Register in 1976. After decommissioning, USS Ponchatoula was sold for scrap.

Reezal Merican bin Naina Merican (Jawi: ريزال ماريکان بن ناينا ماريکان‎; born 29 July 1972) is a Malaysian politician and banker who has served as the 9th chairman of the Malaysia External Trade Development Corporation (MATRADE) since May 2023 and member of the Penang State Legislative Assembly (MLA) for Bertam since August 2023. He served as the minister of housing and local government in the Barisan Nasional (BN) administration under former prime minister Ismail Sabri Yaakob from August 2021 to the collapse of the BN administration in November 2022, the minister of youth and sports in the Perikatan Nasional (PN) administration under former prime minister Muhyiddin Yassin from March 2020 to the collapse of the PN administration in August 2021 and the member of parliament (MP) for Kepala Batas from the retirement of 5th and former prime minister Abdullah Ahmad Badawi as the MP from politics in May 2013 to his electoral defeat in November 2022. He is also a member and division chief of Kepala Batas of the Supreme Council of the United Malays National Organisation (UMNO), a component party of the BN coalition and one of the only two Penang BN and UMNO MLAs alongside MLA for Sungai Acheh Rashidi Zinol. He has also served as chief of the Elections Department of UMNO since March 2023. He is an ethnic Malay of mixed Indian descent.

Reezal Merican Naina Merican is a Malaysian politician associated with the United Malays National Organization (UMNO), a major party within the Barisan Nasional coalition. He has held various positions within the Malaysian government, including serving as the Minister of Youth and Sports from 2020 to 2021. Merican is known for his advocacy in youth development, sports, and community engagement. His political career has included significant involvement in local governance and education initiatives. As a prominent figure in UMNO, Reezal Merican has played a role in shaping policies related to youth and sports in Malaysia.

The 1918 United States Senate election in Colorado took place on November 5, 1918. It was the second direct U.S. Senate election in Colorado following the ratification of the Seventeenth Amendment, and the first for the Class 2 Senate seat. Democratic senator John F. Shafroth ran for re-election to a second term, and was opposed by Denver businessman Lawrence C. Phipps, the Republican nominee, in the general election. As Republicans made gains nationwide, they performed well in Colorado, too, and Phipps unseated Shafroth by a thin margin.
topic: 1918 United States Senate election in Colorado

text: The 1918 United States Senate election in Colorado was held on November 5, 1918, as part of the broader elections following World War I. The incumbent senator, Republican Edwin C. Bill, sought re-election but faced a contested primary. The Democratic Party nominated former U.S. Representative Edward P. Costigan, who campaigned on progressive reforms and opposition to the war. In the general election, Costigan won a decisive victory, receiving 52.6% of the votes against Bill's 47.4%. This election marked a significant shift in Colorado's political landscape, as it was one of several contests that reflected the broader national trends of the Progressive Era. Costigan's victory contributed to the Democratic Party gaining ground in multiple states during this election cycle.
topic: 1918 United States Senate election in Colorado

text: Hypermodernism is a cultural, artistic, literary and architectural successor to modernism and postmodernism in which the form (attribute) of an object has no context distinct from its function. Attributes can include shapes, colors, ratios, and even time. Unlike postmodernism and modernism, hypermodernism exists in an era of fault-tolerant technological change and treats extraneous attributes (most conspicuously physical form) as discordant with function. While modernism and post-modernism debate the value of the "box" or absolute reference point, hypermodernism focuses on improvising attributes of the box (reference point now an extraneous value rather than correct or incorrect value) so that all of its attributes are non-extraneous; it also excises attributes that are extraneous. Hypermodernism is not a debate over truth or untruth as per modernism/postmodernism; rather it is a debate over what is and is not an extraneous attribute. Synchrony between previously-clashing objects (now attributes) and amorphous self-identity coupled with allusions to a magical existence acknowledge the movement. Some theorists view hypermodernism as a form of resistance to traditional modernism; others as a supersedence of it.

Hypermodernism in art refers to a movement that emerged in the late 20th century, characterized by a heightened focus on the complexities of contemporary life, technology, and global culture. It often includes elements of postmodernism while pushing beyond its boundaries, integrating a diverse range of media and styles. Hypermodernist artists explore themes such as fragmentation, identity, and the influence of digital technology, often creating works that reflect the fast-paced, interconnected nature of modern existence. This movement is marked by its embrace of irony and self-reflexivity, challenging traditional notions of authorship and originality. Notable hypermodernist artists include Olafur Eliasson, Takashi Murakami, and Damien Hirst, who engage with audience participation and cultural commentary in their works.

UTD Baseball Field is a baseball venue located in Richardson, Texas, and home to the UT Dallas Comets baseball program, which was constructed prior to 2002 when the University of Texas at Dallas added baseball and softball programs to its intercollegiate sport program. The ballpark holds a capacity of 250. The Comets participate in the Lone Star Conference.

The UT Dallas Baseball Field is the home venue for the University of Texas at Dallas (UT Dallas) baseball team, which competes in NCAA Division III. Located in Richardson, Texas, the field features a natural grass playing surface, seating for spectators, and modern amenities for players and fans. The facility hosts home games during the baseball season and serves as a venue for various local events. With its commitment to maintaining a quality playing environment, the UT Dallas Baseball Field plays a significant role in the university's athletics program and its community engagement initiatives.

Espen Eriksen Trio is a Norwegian jazz piano trio formed in 2007, consisting of Espen Eriksen (piano), Lars Tormod Jenset (double bass) and Andreas Bye (drums). The trio has released five albums on the Norwegian record-label Rune Grammofon. They have performed at Molde Int. Jazz Festival, Oslo Jazzfestival, Maijazz and Vossajazz in Norway, Jazz Province Festival in Russia and Penang Jazz Festival in Malaysia, in addition to several club performances in Germany, Switzerland, Great Britain and Malaysia.

The Espen Eriksen Trio is a Norwegian jazz ensemble founded in 2007, consisting of pianist Espen Eriksen, bassist Andreas B. Nilsen, and drummer Magnus Sefaniassen Eide. Known for their melodic and contemplative sound, the trio blends elements of traditional jazz with contemporary influences, creating a unique auditory experience. They have released several albums, including "Salmesang" (2010), "Perfectly Unhappy" (2013), and "From Wizard Island" (2019), which have received positive reviews in the jazz community. The trio frequently performs at festivals and venues across Europe, establishing a reputation for their engaging live performances and innovative compositions.

Khvoshab-e Sofla (Persian: خوشاب سفلي, also Romanized as Khvoshāb-e Soflá and Khowshāb-e Soflá; also known as Khvoshāb-e Pā’īn) is a village in Deymkaran Rural District, Salehabad District, Bahar County, Hamadan Province, Iran. At the 2006 census, its population was 600, in 133 families.

Khvoshab-e Sofla is a village located in the Khvoshab District of the Sabzevar County in Razavi Khorasan Province, Iran. As per the 2006 census, the village had a population of 170 residents, comprising 50 families. The village is characterized by its rural setting and traditional lifestyle, typical of many communities in the region. Khvoshab-e Sofla is situated within the broader cultural and historical context of the Sabzevar County, known for its agricultural activities and local craftsmanship.

The Viper is a 1938 British slapstick comedy film directed by Roy William Neill and starring Claude Hulbert, Betty Lynne and Hal Walters. The film was a sequel to the previous year's very successful The Vulture, with Hulbert and Walters reprising their roles as hapless private detective Cedric Gull and his sidekick Stiffy respectively. Lesley Brook also features in both films, but in unrelated roles. Directorial duties passed to Neill as Ralph Ince, the director of The Vulture, had been killed in a road accident shortly after the film's release.

The Viper is a 1938 American crime film directed by John H. Auer, which centers around a murderous jewel thief named George who is pursued by law enforcement. The film stars Ralph Bellamy as the protagonist, with key performances by other actors including Judith Barrett and Mary Brian. The narrative explores themes of deception, betrayal, and the consequences of crime, all set against a backdrop of dramatic suspense. The Viper was produced by Columbia Pictures and contributes to the genre of film noir that emerged prominently in the late 1930s. Its cinematography and storytelling techniques reflect the stylistic trends of the period, showcasing a blend of tension and character-driven plot development.

Customer service representatives, customer service advisors, customer service agents, or customer service associates are employees who interact with customers to handle and resolve complaints, process orders, and provide information about an organization’s products and services. They may work in an office with a call center or in retail. Customer service representatives answer questions or requests from customers or the public. They typically provide services by phone, but some also interact with customers face to face, by email or text, via live chat, and through social media. Qualifications include good communication, problem-solving, and computer skills.

A customer service representative (CSR) is a professional who assists customers by addressing inquiries, resolving issues, and providing information about products and services. CSRs typically work in a variety of industries, including retail, telecommunications, finance, and healthcare. Their duties may involve answering calls, responding to emails, and engaging with customers through chat or social media platforms. Effective communication skills, problem-solving abilities, and product knowledge are essential for CSRs to ensure customer satisfaction and foster loyalty. CSRs often play a critical role in maintaining a positive company image and may contribute to sales efforts through upselling or cross-selling products.

The Church Street Covered Bridge, also called the Village Covered Bridge, is a wooden covered bridge that crosses the North Branch of the Lamoille River (also known as the Kelly River) in Waterville, Vermont off State Route 109. Built in the late 19th century, it is one of five covered bridges in a space of about five miles that cross the North Branch Lamoille. It was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1974.

The Church Street Covered Bridge, located in the town of Bridgewater, Vermont, is a historic wooden covered bridge that spans the Ottauquechee River. Constructed in 1870, it is notable for its unique design featuring a combination of the Howe truss and the Town lattice truss construction methods. The bridge measures 120 feet in length and is characterized by its red exterior and traditional gable roof. It serves as a picturesque example of 19th-century American engineering and architecture. The Church Street Covered Bridge is listed on the National Register of Historic Places and remains a popular attraction for visitors, providing a scenic spot for photography and historical appreciation.

Belen Jesuit Preparatory School (Spanish: Colegio Preparatorio Jesuita Belén) is a private, Catholic, all-male, preparatory school run by the Antilles Province of the Society of Jesus in Tamiami, unincorporated Miami-Dade County, Florida, operated by the Society of Jesus. It was established in Havana, Cuba by the Jesuits in 1854 but moved to the United States after the communist government of Fidel Castro, himself an alumnus, took power and expelled the Jesuits. It has since made the Cardinal Newman Society's honor roll. The name Belen is Spanish for "Bethlehem."

Belen Jesuit Preparatory School is a private, Catholic college preparatory school located in Miami, Florida. Established in 1854, it is operated by the Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Miami and the Society of Jesus (Jesuits). The school serves grades 6 through 12 and emphasizes academic excellence, spiritual development, and community service, all rooted in Jesuit educational principles. Belen Jesuit is known for its rigorous academic programs, extracurricular activities, and strong athletic teams. The school has a diverse student body and offers a range of Advanced Placement (AP) courses, as well as various clubs and organizations that promote leadership and personal growth.
Carlo Colombo (born 28 April 1960) is an Italian sports shooter. He competed at the 1992 Summer Olympics and the 1996 Summer Olympics.

Carlo Colombo is an Italian architect and designer known for his contributions to contemporary furniture and interior design. Born in 1962 in Milan, he has collaborated with various prestigious design brands, including Cassina, B&B Italia, and Flexform. Colombo's work often emphasizes minimalism, elegance, and functionality, reflecting a keen understanding of form and material. In addition to his design practice, he has held teaching positions in architectural courses and has participated in numerous exhibitions, showcasing a commitment to advancing the fields of architecture and design. His designs have garnered multiple awards and are recognized for their innovative approaches and timeless aesthetic.

The 2025 NCAA Division I softball tournament was held from May 16 through June 6, 2025, as the final part of the 2025 NCAA Division I softball season. The tournament ended with the 2025 Women's College World Series at Devon Park in Oklahoma City Texas defeated Texas Tech during the 2025 Women's College World Series to win their first national championship in program history. Belmont, Mercer, North Florida, Saint Louis, and Santa Clara made their NCAA Division I softball tournament debuts. The Southeastern Conference (SEC) had an NCAA tournament record 14 teams selected for the tournament. The No. 1 overall seed, Texas A&M, was defeated by Liberty in the College Station regional. This marked the first time a No. 1 seed in the NCAA tournament failed to advance to the Super Regionals since the NCAA tournament began seeding in 2005.

The 2025 NCAA Division I Softball Tournament will be the 43rd annual tournament held to determine the national champion of NCAA Division I women's college softball. Scheduled to take place in May 2025, the tournament features a selection of 64 teams, including automatic bids for conference champions and at-large selections. The tournament consists of regional and super regional rounds, culminating in the Women's College World Series in June 2025. The event showcases top collegiate talent and is broadcast nationwide, contributing significantly to the popularity of women's sports in the United States. The defending champion from the 2024 tournament will aim to retain their title amid a competitive field.

The Kurdish Language Academy in Iran is a school in Tehran, Islamic Republic of Iran, educating students on the Kurdish language. Kurdish is sociopolitically considered a single language; however the limited mutual intelligibility of its varieties means that it's a dialect continuum.

The Kurdish Language Academy in Iran, also known as the Kurdish Academy, is an institution dedicated to the promotion and development of the Kurdish language and literature within Iran. Established to enhance Kurdish cultural identity and linguistic standards, the academy focuses on research, teaching, and the publication of Kurdish educational materials. It also plays a crucial role in preserving the dialects of Kurdish, which is primarily spoken in the northwest regions of Iran, as well as fostering collaboration with other Kurdish-speaking communities. The academy's efforts are part of a broader movement to advocate for the recognition and rights of the Kurdish people in Iran, amidst challenges related to cultural expression and linguistic rights.

The First Baptist Church of Medfield is a historic Baptist church building at 438 Main Street in Medfield, Massachusetts, United States. The church building, the second for the congregation, was constructed in 1838, and originally had Greek Revival styling. In 1874 it was significantly renovated, and given its present Gothic Revival appearance. The building was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2003. The congregation was established in 1752. It was formally incorporated and given recognition in 1776 under Thomas Gair, its first pastor. Today it is a member of the American Baptists churches, and it theologically aligns with a Reformed Theology and movements such as The Gospel Coalition. Its worship today is a blend of Biblically founded, Gospel Centered preaching, and heartfelt praise. The music style is a blend of contemporary and traditional music. Worship starts at 10:00 a.m.

The First Baptist Church of Medfield is a historic church located in Medfield, Massachusetts. Founded in 1824, it is part of the American Baptist Churches USA denomination. The church is known for its distinctive architecture, typically featuring traditional New England design elements. Throughout its history, the First Baptist Church has been engaged in community service and outreach, often hosting events and programs that reflect its commitment to both spiritual and social missions. The church has played a significant role in the cultural and religious life of Medfield, contributing to the town's sense of community and heritage.

The Mossawa Center was established in 1997 as a civil society organization to promote equality for Arab citizens in Israel. In Arabic, "mossawa" means "equality". The organization works to promote the social, economic, cultural, and political rights of the Arab minority living in Israel.

The Mossawa Center is a non-profit organization based in Israel that advocates for the rights of Arab citizens. Founded in 2000, it focuses on promoting social, political, and economic equality for the Arab population in Israel, addressing issues such as discrimination, land rights, and cultural preservation. The center provides research, advocacy, and community organizing, working to enhance civic participation and representation among Arab citizens. It aims to foster dialogue between Arab and Jewish communities and to raise awareness of the challenges faced by Arab Israelis, contributing to broader discussions on minority rights and social justice within the country.

Sassafras is a C-Class, 180-foot (55 m), seagoing buoy tender constructed for the United States Coast Guard by Marine Iron & Shipbuilding Corp. of Duluth, Minnesota. Sassafras was one of 39 tenders commissioned for duties that would include aids-to-navigation, ice breaking, search-and-rescue, firefighting, law enforcement, providing fuel and potable water, and assistance to the National Oceanographic and Seismographic Survey.

USCGC Sassafras (WLB-401) is a 225-foot sea-going buoy tender of the United States Coast Guard, commissioned in 1943. She is the last vessel of the "Sassafras" class, designed primarily for maintaining aids to navigation but also equipped for search and rescue, icebreaking, and environmental protection missions. Sassafras was built by the Moss Point Marine in Mississippi and has served in numerous operations along the U.S. coasts and Great Lakes, engaging in both peacetime and wartime activities. The ship is homeported in Port Huron, Michigan, and is notable for her contributions to maritime safety and efficiency in navigational services.

The Fuze Keeping Clock (FKC) was a simplified version of the Royal Navy's High Angle Control System analogue fire control computer. It first appeared as the FKC MkII in destroyers of the 1938 Tribal class, while later variants were used on sloops, frigates, destroyers, aircraft carriers and several cruisers. The FKC MkII was a non-tachymetric anti-aircraft fire control computer. It could accurately engage targets with a maximum speed of 250 knots (460 km/h; 290 mph).

The Fuze Keeping Clock is a specialized timekeeping device designed for maintaining accurate time in various environments. It typically features advanced technology that ensures reliability and precision, making it suitable for both personal and industrial use. The clock may incorporate functionalities such as synchronization with global time standards, display options for multiple time zones, and durability for outdoor or challenging conditions. Its design and features cater to users who require consistent timekeeping, such as professionals in logistics, aviation, and event planning. The Fuze Keeping Clock emphasizes both functionality and user-friendly interfaces to accommodate diverse applications.
Abdel-Ru'ouf Nayef Mezaal Al-Rawabdeh (Arabic: عبد الرؤوف نايف مزعل الروابدة) is a Jordanian footballer who plays for Al-Ahli SC (Amman).

Abdel-Ru'ouf Al-Rawabdeh is a Jordanian politician and former Prime Minister of Jordan, known for his leadership during the early 1990s. He held the position from 1994 to 1996, during which his government focused on economic reforms and modernization efforts in the country. Al-Rawabdeh has also served in various parliamentary roles and has been involved in regional politics, contributing to various socio-economic initiatives in Jordan. After his tenure as Prime Minister, he continued to be an influential figure in Jordanian politics and governance.

Skipalong Rosenbloom is a 1951 American Western film directed by Sam Newfield and written by Eddie Forman and Dean Riesner. Starring Maxie Rosenbloom, Max Baer, Jackie Coogan, Fuzzy Knight, Hillary Brooke and Jacqueline Fontaine, it was released on April 30, 1951, by United Artists.

Skipalong Rosenbloom is a fictional character from the children's television series "The Busy World of Richard Scarry," which is based on the books by Richard Scarry. Appearing as a young, energetic pig, Skipalong is known for his adventurous spirit and friendly nature. He engages in various educational activities that teach children about cooperation, problem-solving, and the adventures of everyday life. Skipalong often interacts with other characters in the series, emphasizing themes of community and friendship. The character is beloved for his humorous mishaps and encouraging personality, making him a memorable figure in children's entertainment.

Rusimbi is an administrative ward in Kigoma-Ujiji District of Kigoma Region in Tanzania. The ward covers an area of 0.7 km2 (0.27 sq mi), and has an average elevation of 783 m (2,569 ft). In 2016 the Tanzania National Bureau of Statistics report there were 7,462 people in the ward, from 6,779 in 2012.

Rusimbi is a village located in the Ngara District of the Kagera Region in Tanzania. It lies near the border with Rwanda and is part of a lush, rural landscape characterized by agriculture and local traditions. The village serves as a hub for the surrounding communities, providing access to basic services and resources. The economy of Rusimbi is primarily based on subsistence farming, with crops such as bananas, coffee, and various vegetables being cultivated. The area is also known for its cultural heritage, with traditions and customs that reflect the diverse influences of both Tanzanian and Rwandan communities. The population of Rusimbi is predominantly made up of ethnic groups that share linguistic and cultural ties, contributing to the region's rich social fabric.

Maciejowięta [mat͡ɕɛjɔˈvjɛnta] is a village in the administrative district of Gmina Dubeninki, within Gołdap County, Warmian-Masurian Voivodeship, in northern Poland, close to the border with the Kaliningrad Oblast of Russia.

Maciejowięta is a village located in southern Poland, within the administrative district of Gmina Sękowa, in Gorlice County, Lesser Poland Voivodeship. It lies approximately 6 kilometers southwest of the town of Gorlice and about 97 kilometers southeast of Kraków, the regional capital. The village is characterized by its rural landscape and traditional architecture, reflecting the cultural heritage of the region. Maciejowięta is primarily an agricultural community, with local economy largely centered around farming and related activities.
Sudhindra Bhaumik (born 22 April 1946) is a vocalist of the Agra Gharana.

Sudhindra Bhaumik (born 17 October 1967) is an Indian artist, writer, and educator known for his contributions to contemporary Indian art and literature. His work encompasses various mediums, including painting, sculpture, and installation art, often exploring themes of identity, culture, and society. Bhaumik has exhibited his artwork in numerous national and international galleries, gaining recognition for his innovative approaches and ability to blend traditional techniques with modern concepts. Additionally, he has authored several books and articles on art theory and practice, contributing to the discourse on contemporary Indian art. As an educator, he has been involved in teaching and mentoring aspiring artists, fostering a new generation of creative talent.

A private secretary is a civil servant in a governmental department or ministry, responsible to a secretary of state or minister; or a public servant in a royal household, responsible to a member of the royal family; or a civil servant working for a senior member of the judiciary. The role exists in the civil service of the United Kingdom and several Commonwealth countries including Australia, India and New Zealand as well as other countries influenced by the Westminster system. A private secretary is normally of middle management level; however, as the key official responsible for disseminating ministers' decisions and guidance on matters of policy, and as their gatekeeper, the role is of considerably greater significance than their grade would suggest. Depending on the status of the political principal the official works for, they may be aided by an assistant private secretary, or head a private office. A principal private secretary, or senior private secretary, is a senior civil servant who runs a cabinet minister's private office. The Lady or Lord Chief Justice of England and Wales is also supported by a principal private secretary. A similar role to a principal private secretary in the United States federal government would be chief of staff. In the United Kingdom, the private secretary to the sovereign is viewed as being equivalent to a permanent secretary, the head of a government department.

A private secretary is an administrative professional who provides personalized support to an individual, typically a high-ranking executive, politician, or other prominent figure. Their responsibilities often include managing correspondence, scheduling appointments, organizing meetings, and handling confidential information. Private secretaries serve as a liaison between their employer and other stakeholders, facilitating communication and ensuring smooth operations.
