This role requires strong organizational skills, discretion, and the ability to multitask effectively. The position can vary significantly based on the employer's needs and the specific industry, with some private secretaries specializing in areas such as legal or medical fields. The role has evolved with technology, incorporating digital tools for communication and organization.
Sosnka [ˈsɔsŋka] is a village in the administrative district of Gmina Stara Kamienica, within Jelenia Góra County, Lower Silesian Voivodeship, in south-western Poland.

Sosnka is a village located in Poland, within the administrative district of Gmina Stawiguda, in Olsztyn County, Warmian-Masurian Voivodeship. It lies approximately 12 kilometers northwest of Olsztyn, the regional capital. The village is characterized by its rural setting and agricultural landscape, contributing to the local economy. Sosnka is part of a region known for its natural beauty, including forests, lakes, and a variety of recreational opportunities. The area reflects a blend of cultural influences, shaped by its history and the changing borders of Poland.

Audiosurf is a 2008 music-based puzzle video game created by Invisible Handlebar, a company founded by Dylan Fitterer. Its track-style stages visually mimic the music the player chooses, while the player races across several lanes collecting colored blocks that appear in sync with the music. The game was released on February 15, 2008, over Steam, a few days after winning the Independent Games Festival 2008 Excellence in Audio Award, heavily influenced by the soundtrack composed by Pedro Macedo Camacho. The full version was for a long time only available for purchase through Steam, but was later released as a retail product in Europe, by Ascaron. Audiosurf was the first third-party game to use Valve's Steamworks technology. The Zune HD version was also released as Audiosurf: Tilt. A sequel, Audiosurf 2, was released in May 2015.

Audiosurf is a rhythm-based puzzle video game developed by Dylan Fitterer and published by Valve Corporation, released in 2008. The game allows players to control a customizable spaceship that rides on a track generated by the player's own music, creating a unique experience with each song. Players collect colored blocks while avoiding obstacles, with gameplay influenced by the tempo and rhythm of the music. Audiosurf received positive reviews for its innovative concept, engaging mechanics, and the seamless integration of music into the gameplay experience. It is available on various platforms, including Windows and macOS, and has garnered a dedicated following within the gaming community. The game has also inspired a sequel, Audiosurf 2, released in 2014.
Víctor Fabián Pacheco Techera (born September 12, 1972 in Montevideo, Uruguay) is a former Uruguayan footballer who played for clubs of Uruguay, Chile and Mexico.

Víctor Pacheco (born December 15, 1972) is a retired Argentine footballer who played primarily as a midfielder. He began his professional career in the early 1990s with Club Atlético Vélez Sarsfield, where he contributed to the team's success in domestic competitions. Over the course of his career, Pacheco played for several clubs in Argentina and abroad, including stints in Bolivia and Chile. Known for his dribbling skills and tactical intelligence, he was respected for his work ethic on and off the field. After retiring from professional football, Pacheco transitioned into coaching, where he has aimed to develop young talent in youth academies.

"I Drink Wine" is a song by the English singer Adele from her fourth studio album, 30 (2021). Adele co-wrote the song with its producer, Greg Kurstin. It became available as the album's seventh track on 19 November 2021, when it was released by Columbia Records. It is a ballad with gospel influences reminiscent of church music and incorporates a piano and organ in its instrumentation. It is about letting go of one's ego and addresses Adele's divorce from Simon Konecki, comprising arduous realisations about the condition of her marriage and life. "I Drink Wine" generally received positive reviews from music critics, some of whom viewed it as one of Adele's best songs and a career highlight. It reached the top 10 in the United Kingdom, Ireland, Iceland, New Zealand, South Africa, Australia, Canada, and Sweden and entered the charts in various other countries. Joe Talbot directed the music video for "I Drink Wine", which depicts Adele floating through a river and exploring a forest while drinking a glass of wine. Adele performed the song for her television specials and at the Brit Awards 2022, which was positively received. In addition, "I Drink Wine" impacted radio airplay in Italy on 4 November 2022 as the album's third single.

"I Drink Wine" is a popular song by British singer-songwriter Ed Sheeran, released in 2022 as part of his album "Equals." The track features Sheeran's signature blend of acoustic and pop influences, with lyrics that express themes of romance, introspection, and the complexities of relationships. The song garnered widespread acclaim for its catchy melody and relatable content, contributing to its success on various music charts worldwide. Sheeran's emotive performance and lyrical storytelling are notable features, solidifying his position as a leading figure in contemporary music.
Roberto Carlos Jiménez Jiménez (born 17 April 1983) is a Peruvian former professional footballer who played as a forward.

Roberto Jiménez, born on 10 February 1983 in Madrid, Spain, is a retired professional footballer who played as a goalkeeper. He began his career in the youth system of Real Madrid before making his senior debut with the club's B team, Real Madrid Castilla. Over his career, Jiménez played for several clubs across Spain, including a notable spell at Atlético Madrid, where he won the UEFA Europa League in 2010. He also played for various other teams including Sporting de Gijón, and served as a backup goalkeeper for top-tier clubs. Jiménez had a brief international career, earning caps with the Spanish U21 national team. Known for his shot-stopping abilities and strong presence in the box, he retired from professional football in 2020.

The Dutch Association of Publishers categorizes the magazines in the Netherlands into four classes: (1) general-interest magazines, (2) opinion magazines, (3) TV and radio guides, and (4) professional and scientific magazines. The listing here is topical, without making a value judgment if, for example, a music magazine is professional or not.
topic: List of magazines in the Netherlands

text: The List of magazines in the Netherlands includes a diverse array of periodicals published in the country, covering various genres such as news, lifestyle, fashion, sports, and culture. Prominent Dutch magazines include "Elsevier," a renowned newsweekly; "Canvas," focusing on arts and culture; and "Vogue Nederland," representing the fashion genre. The magazine landscape in the Netherlands features both Dutch-language publications and international titles available in translation. This listing reflects the broad interests of the Dutch readership and highlights the contributions of the Netherlands to print media. Many magazines also have online versions, adapting to the changing media environment.
topic: List of magazines in the Netherlands

text: The Mandolin Society of Peterborough (MSOP) was a non-profit community mandolin orchestra based in the City of Peterborough, Ontario, Canada. The MSOP was one of three such groups in Ontario (as of 2013), and the only one composed completely of mandolinists. In 2019, the society disbanded.

The Mandolin Society of Peterborough is a musical organization based in Peterborough, England, dedicated to the promotion and enjoyment of mandolin music. Founded in 1996, the society provides opportunities for musicians of all skill levels to participate in performances, workshops, and social events centered on the mandolin family of instruments. The society aims to foster a community of musicians, encourage collaboration, and preserve the cultural heritage of mandolin music through a variety of genres. Members often engage in public performances and outreach activities to raise awareness and appreciation of mandolin music within the local community.

Margaret Dows Herter Norton Crena de Iongh (née Herter; 1894–1985), known as Mary D. Herter Norton when she co-founded W. W. Norton & Company with her first husband, William Warder Norton, was an American publisher, violinist, and translator. She was known as M. D. Herter Norton for her work as a violinist and translator. Under the name M. D. Herter Norton, she translated from German about a dozen volumes of the poetry of Rainer Maria Rilke. Norton is noted for her dedication to giving "the closest idiomatic parallel" in her translation of Rilke poems. Among the translators working in her time, she was on the extreme end of being faithful to the "image, symbol, and elements of language" to the loss of the "technical elements of form" that would make a text a "living poems". Instead she offers the reader a literary aid to understand Rilke's words that accompany her line by line translation. Her second husband, Daniel Crena de Iongh, was an Executive Director and treasurer of the World Bank. Mary Dows Herter Norton was also known as “Polly”. She and her husband William Warder Norton had one child, Anne Aston Warder Norton Jones, born 1928 -1977.

Mary D. Herter Norton (1914–2004) was an American librarian, educator, and historian known for her contributions to the field of library science and her advocacy for library access. She served as the director of several public and academic libraries and was a prominent figure in the American Library Association. Norton focused on promoting literacy and improving library services for underserved populations. Her work emphasized the importance of libraries in fostering community engagement and lifelong learning, impacting library practices and policies across the United States. In recognition of her contributions, she received numerous awards throughout her career, solidifying her legacy in the library profession.
Idalus multicolor is a moth of the family Erebidae first described by Walter Rothschild in 1909. It is found in French Guiana and Guyana.

Idalus multicolor is a species of moth belonging to the family Erebidae, first described by the entomologist George Hampson in 1898. This nocturnal moth is primarily found in various regions of Central and South America, including countries like Costa Rica and Brazil. Characterized by its striking coloration, I. multicolor features distinct patterns and hues that aid in camouflage and mate attraction. The species plays a role in the local ecosystem, serving as both a pollinator and a food source for predators. Research on its life cycle and ecological significance continues to contribute to the understanding of moth biodiversity and conservation.
Aenictegues is the only genus of mites in the family Aenicteguidae, in the order Mesostigmata.

Aenictegues is a genus of spiders belonging to the family Thomisidae, commonly known as crab spiders. These arachnids are primarily found in tropical regions and are characterized by their flattened bodies and ability to ambush prey by blending into their surroundings. Aenictegues species typically exhibit various colorations and patterns, which aid in camouflage. They are known for their unique hunting techniques, utilizing their long legs to grasp prey rather than spinning webs. Research on Aenictegues contributes to the understanding of spider behavior, ecology, and evolution within the Thomisidae family.

Thomas Hinckley Robbins Jr. (11 May 1900 – 12 December 1972) was a rear admiral of the United States Navy. A naval aviator, his career included command of an aircraft carrier during World War II, service as a key advisor to the United States Secretary of the Navy, and a tour as President of the Naval War College. Robbins' ancestors included William Bradford (1590-1657), the first governor of the Massachusetts Bay Colony, and Thomas Hinckley (1618-1706), a governor of Plymouth Colony. His great-great-grandfather was Fisher Ames (1758-1808), a Massachusetts politician who served in the United States House of Representatives.

Thomas H. Robbins Jr. (born March 6, 1937) is an American mathematician and educator known for his contributions to the field of combinatorial mathematics and graph theory. He has published numerous research papers and co-authored several textbooks aimed at advancing mathematical education at various levels. Robbins is recognized for his role in promoting mathematics through workshops and conferences, fostering collaboration among educators and researchers. Throughout his career, he has held faculty positions at multiple universities and has been actively involved in curriculum development initiatives. His work has significantly impacted both theoretical mathematics and practical applications in education.
Şeyhçoban is a neighbourhood in the municipality and district of Çınar, Diyarbakır Province in Turkey. Its population is 181 (2022).

Şeyhçoban is a village located in the Çınar district of Diyarbakır Province in southeastern Turkey. It is known for its rural character and agricultural activities, with a population that reflects the demographic trends of the region. The village is situated in a geographic area that has been influenced by historical migrations and cultural exchanges. The local economy primarily relies on farming and livestock, typical of many villages in this part of Turkey. Şeyhçoban, like many small settlements, reflects the traditions and lifestyle of its inhabitants, while also facing modern challenges and changes.
Daniela Graglia (born 26 May 1976 in Fossano) is a former Italian sprinter and middle distance runner.

Daniela Graglia is an Italian-American mathematician and professor, recognized for her contributions to the fields of applied mathematics and mathematical biology. She has conducted significant research on mathematical modeling in biological systems, particularly in epidemiology and population dynamics. Graglia has held academic positions at various institutions, where she has published numerous peer-reviewed articles and co-authored several textbooks. Additionally, she is noted for her involvement in mentoring students and promoting interdisciplinary approaches in mathematical research. Graglia's work often emphasizes the application of mathematical techniques to solve real-world problems in health and ecological contexts.

Pierre-Hugues Herbert and Michał Przysiężny were the defending champions, but Prysiężny chose not to participate. Herbert played alongside Nicolas Mahut, but lost in the quarterfinals to Aisam-ul-Haq Qureshi and Gilles Simon. Raven Klaasen and Marcelo Melo won the title, defeating Juan Sebastián Cabal and Robert Farah in the final, 7–6(7–5), 3–6, [10–7].
topic: 2015 Rakuten Japan Open Tennis Championships – Doubles

text: The 2015 Rakuten Japan Open Tennis Championships – Doubles was a professional tennis tournament held in Tokyo, Japan, as part of the ATP World Tour 500 series. The event took place from October 5 to October 11, 2015, and featured a doubles competition. The defending champions were the duo of Jean-Julien Rojer and Horia Tecău, who successfully retained their title by defeating the pair of Raven Klaasen and Marcelo Melo in the final with a score of 6–3, 6–4. The tournament showcased high-level performances from various international teams and contributed to the players' rankings and careers on the ATP tour.
topic: 2015 Rakuten Japan Open Tennis Championships – Doubles

text: Paij Lagnachi is a 1997 Indian Marathi-language drama film directed by Yashwant Bhalkar. It stars Varsha Usgaonkar, Avinash Narkar, and Prateeksha Lonkar in lead roles.

Paij Lagnachi is a traditional Indian wedding ceremony predominantly practiced in the state of Maharashtra among the Marathi community. The term "Paij" refers to the auspicious moment when the bride and groom are united, and "Lagnachi" denotes the marriage or wedding event. This ceremony typically involves various rituals, including the exchange of vows, blessings from family elders, and the tying of the "mangal sutra," which symbolizes the couple's union. The Paij Lagnachi is characterized by cultural significance, vibrant celebrations, and adherence to local customs, making it a vital aspect of Marathi social life. The event often includes music, dance, and elaborate feasting, emphasizing the joyous occasion for family and friends.
Bonäs is a locality situated in Mora Municipality, Dalarna County, Sweden with 457 inhabitants in 2010.

Bonäs is a locality situated within the municipality of Hällefors in Örebro County, Sweden. It is characterized by its rural setting and is part of the wider historical and geographical context of the region. The area is known for its natural landscapes and small-community atmosphere, typical of Swedish villages. Bonäs's population dynamics and local culture reflect the trends of rural life in Sweden, contributing to the overall demographic and cultural diversity of Hällefors municipality.

The Fundo Soberano de Angola (lit. 'Angola Sovereign [Wealth] Fund', FSDEA) is the sovereign wealth fund of Angola. It is a member of the International Forum of Sovereign Wealth Funds, and therefore has signed up to the Santiago Principles on best practice in managing sovereign wealth funds. The FSDEA is meant to play an important role in promoting Angola’s social and economic development and generating wealth for its people. The fund was rated by the SWFI in February 2015 with a ranking of 8 out of 10.

The Fundo Soberano de Angola (FSDEA) is the sovereign wealth fund of Angola, established in 2012 to manage the country’s surplus revenues, primarily derived from oil exports. The fund aims to promote economic diversification, support development projects, and generate sustainable returns for future generations of Angolans. FSDEA invests in various sectors, including agriculture, infrastructure, and real estate, both domestically and internationally. It operates under the oversight of the Angolan government and adheres to principles of transparency and accountability in its operations and investments.
Ilya Grigorevich Chashnik (1902, Lucyn, Russian Empire, currently Ludza, Latvia – 1929, Leningrad) was a suprematist artist, a pupil of Kazimir Malevich and a founding member of the UNOVIS school.

Ilya Chashnik (1903–1946) was a prominent Russian painter and graphic artist associated with the avant-garde movement. Born in Moscow, he initially studied at the Moscow School of Painting, Sculpture and Architecture, where he became influenced by Constructivism and Suprematism. Chashnik's work is characterized by a unique blend of geometric abstraction and symbolic elements, reflecting his interest in the intersection of art and ideology. He was involved in various exhibitions and groups, including the UNOVIS (Affirmers of the New Art) collective, which promoted radical artistic innovation in the early Soviet period. Chashnik's contributions to Russian modern art are recognized for their experimental approach and exploration of color, form, and composition. His legacy is preserved through his artworks, which continue to be studied and celebrated in contemporary art discourses.
Eunidia duplicata is a species of beetle in the family Cerambycidae. It was described by Francis Polkinghorne Pascoe in 1864.

Eunidia duplicata is a species of beetle belonging to the family Cerambycidae, commonly known as longhorn beetles. This species is native to various regions in Africa and is characterized by its distinctive elongated body and long antennae, which can be longer than the body itself. E. duplicata typically inhabits forested areas where it is associated with wood and foliage. Like other members of its family, it undergoes a lifecycle that includes a larval stage where it typically develops within wood, contributing to its ecological role in decomposition and nutrient cycling. The species is recognized by entomologists for its unique morphological features, and it plays a role in the biodiversity of its habitat.

The 2018 Rugby Challenge – known as the SuperSport Rugby Challenge for sponsorship reasons – was the second edition of the Rugby Challenge, the secondary domestic rugby union competition in South Africa. The competition was organised by the South African Rugby Union and was played between 21 April and 15 July 2018. The competition featured all fourteen South African provincial unions plus Namibian side the Welwitschias. The competition was won by the Pumas for the first time, after they beat Griquas 32–30 in the final in Oudtshoorn. Pumas fly-half Chris Smith was the top scorer in the competition with 131 points, while Griquas wing Enver Brandt was the top try scorer with 11 tries.

The 2018 Rugby Challenge was the sixth season of the Rugby Challenge, a professional rugby union competition in the Southern Hemisphere. It featured teams from various regions, including South Africa, Namibia, and Zimbabwe, competing in a structured format. The season kicked off in March 2018 and concluded in October 2018. The tournament aimed to provide a competitive platform for franchises from the Currie Cup, as well as teams from other countries, enhancing player development and fan engagement. The championship was ultimately claimed by the Free State Cheetahs, who defeated the Griffons in the final. The season contributed to the growth of rugby in the region and showcased emerging talent in the sport.

Myracle Brah is an American indie pop/power pop band primarily centered on singer-songwriter Andy Bopp. It is based out of Baltimore, Maryland. The group consists of Bopp, Paul Krysiak (bassist, piano player, and vocalist), Marty Canelli (organist), Joe Parsons (drummer), and Greg Schroeder (drummer). It was previously signed to Universal Music Group.

Myracle Brah is an American power pop band formed in the late 1990s, originating from Orlando, Florida. The group was led by singer-songwriter and multi-instrumentalist Kool Karlo (real name: J. K. Hsfried) and is known for its catchy melodies and layered harmonies. Myracle Brah gained prominence with their self-titled debut album released in 1998, followed by several other albums, including "Life on the Wires" (2000) and "Nights of the Modern Age" (2004). The band has received recognition within the power pop genre and is noted for its DIY approach to music production and distribution, particularly in the era of the internet. Their style blends elements of rock, pop, and alternative music, and they have been associated with various independent labels throughout their career.
The Very Merry Widows (French: Mariées mais pas trop) is a 2003 Franco-Belgian black comedy film directed and co-written by Catherine Corsini.

The Very Merry Widows is a musical comedy with music by David Friedman and book and lyrics by Janet Hood. The show centers around a support group for widows who learn to cope with their loss and embrace their new lives. The narrative weaves together themes of love, friendship, and humor, showcasing the journey of acceptance and empowerment as the women navigate their grief. The musical features a blend of upbeat and poignant songs, reflecting the emotional highs and lows faced by the characters. It has been produced in various regional theaters and is noted for its uplifting message and engaging performances.

In chemistry, transition metal silyl complexes describe coordination complexes in which a transition metal is bonded to an anionic silyl ligand, forming a metal-silicon sigma bond. This class of complexes are numerous and some are technologically significant as intermediates in hydrosilylation. These complexes are a subset of organosilicon compounds.

Transition metal silyl complexes are coordination compounds formed between transition metals and silyl ligands, which are derived from silanes (compounds containing silicon-hydrogen bonds). These complexes play a crucial role in various catalytic processes, including hydrosilylation, polymerization, and silica synthesis. Silyl ligands can enhance the reactivity of transition metal centers and are valuable in organometallic chemistry. The study of these complexes contributes to the development of new materials and the advancement of techniques in synthetic chemistry. Their unique properties emerge from the interplay between the metal center and the silyl groups, influencing electronic characteristics and steric hindrance. Transition metal silyl complexes have applications in fields such as catalysis, materials science, and the synthesis of silicon-based compounds.

The Chicago Blitz were a professional indoor football team based in Villa Park, Illinois. The Blitz were members of American Indoor Football (AIF) in 2015 and 2016 and the Continental Indoor Football League (CIFL) in 2014. They played their home games at the Odeum Expo Center. The Blitz briefly joined the regional Midwest Professional Indoor Football (MPIF) for the 2018 season, but ceased operations once again after three games.

The Chicago Blitz was a professional indoor football team based in Chicago, Illinois, that played in the Arena Football League (AFL) during its inaugural season in 1987. Founded as one of the league's original franchises, the Blitz played their home games at the Rosemont Horizon. The team was known for its high-scoring offensive style and featured notable players, including quarterback Bobby Hebert. Despite early promise, the Blitz struggled financially and competed in a challenging environment. After the 1987 season, the franchise was sold and relocated, leading to the team's dissolution. The Chicago Blitz is remembered as part of the early history of indoor football in the United States.
Yeşilbağ is a neighbourhood in the municipality and district of Manavgat, Antalya Province, Turkey. Its population is 461 (2022).

Yeşilbağ is a village located in the Manavgat district of Antalya Province in Turkey. Situated near the Mediterranean coast, it is part of a region known for its tourism, agriculture, and natural beauty. The village features typical Mediterranean architecture and is surrounded by lush landscapes and agricultural fields. Yeşilbağ has a small population that relies primarily on farming and tourism for its economy. The area is accessible from larger towns and cities, making it a point of interest for visitors seeking a quieter, rural experience in Turkey's scenic countryside.

The Gunnister Man is the remains of a late 17th- or early 18th-century man found by two Shetlanders in a peat bog not far from the junction of the A970 road in Gunnister, Shetland, Scotland. The bog body was found on 12 May 1951 as the men were digging peat for fuel. A stone placed by the Northmavine History Group now marks the find location.

Gunnister Man refers to a well-preserved prehistoric human remains discovered in a peat bog near the village of Gunnister in Shetland, Scotland, in 1951. The body, estimated to be around 2,000 years old, is believed to belong to a male individual who lived during the Iron Age. The remains were notable for their state of preservation, with skin, hair, and clothing largely intact, providing valuable insights into the burial practices and lifestyles of ancient populations in the region. The discovery helped enhance the understanding of Iron Age cultures in the Scottish Isles and is significant in the study of archaeology and bioarchaeology. Gunnister Man is currently housed in the National Museum of Scotland, where it serves as an important artifact in understanding prehistoric life in northern Britain.

Czesław Dźwigaj (born 18 June 1950 in Nowy Wiśnicz) is a Polish artist, sculptor, and professor. Creator of numerous monuments, he is most often associated with monuments of Pope John Paul II, almost 50 of which have left his workshop.

Czesław Dźwigaj (born January 18, 1948) is a Polish sculptor and academic known for his contemporary works that blend traditional techniques with modern themes. He has created numerous sculptures, including public art installations and monuments, which often draw inspiration from religious and philosophical motifs. Dźwigaj is also recognized for his work in education, having taught at various art institutions in Poland. His contributions to the field of sculpture are celebrated both nationally and internationally, making him a prominent figure in contemporary Polish art.

Guerra de Titanes (1998) ("War of the Titans") was the second Guerra de Titanes professional wrestling show promoted by AAA. The show took place on December 13, 1998 in Chihuahua, Chihuahua, Mexico. The Main event featured a Steel Cage Match that highlighted two storyline feuds between Octagón and his "Evil twin" Pentagón and the feud between Heavy Metal and Kick Boxer as Octagón and Heavy Metal teamed together to take on Pentagón and Kick Boxer.

Guerra de Titanes (1998) is a professional wrestling event produced by the Mexican wrestling promotion Consejo Mundial de Lucha Libre (CMLL) that took place on December 18, 1998, at the Palacio de los Deportes in Mexico City. The event is notable for featuring a multi-man lucha libre match known as a "lucha de apuestas" where competitors wager their masks or hair. The main event showcased a match for the masks of iconic luchadores, contributing to the event's prestige and drawing significant attention from fans and media. Guerra de Titanes is considered one of the key annual events in the CMLL calendar, highlighting notable rivalries and showcasing top talent in the lucha libre scene.
Asiapistosia stigma is a moth of the subfamily Arctiinae first described by Cheng-Lai Fang in 2000. It is found in the Chinese provinces of Sichuan, Shaanxi, Hubei and Guangdong.

Asiapistosia stigma is a species of moth belonging to the family Erebidae. It was first described by entomologist Thomas A. McMillan in 2003. This species is endemic to regions in Southeast Asia and is noted for its distinctive coloration and wing patterns, which play a role in its camouflage. As with many moths, A. stigma is primarily nocturnal and engages in behaviors typical of the Erebidae family, such as seeking nectar from nocturnal flowers. Its habitat typically includes tropical and subtropical environments, where it contributes to the local ecosystem as a pollinator. Further studies on its life cycle and ecological role are ongoing to better understand its contributions to biodiversity.

John Duncan Forsyth (1886 or 1887–1963) was a Scottish-American architect who became prominent in Oklahoma. Based in Tulsa and working in a variety of styles, he was connected with a number of significant buildings around the state.

John Duncan Forsyth (1910–1998) was a prominent American naturalist, ornithologist, and conservationist known for his contributions to wildlife conservation and education in the United States. He served as a professor at various universities and conducted extensive research on bird populations and their habitats. Forsyth played a pivotal role in advocating for environmental policies and the protection of endangered species. His work included authoring significant publications in ornithology and participating in initiatives that raised public awareness about biodiversity and conservation. Forsyth's legacy continues to influence contemporary conservation efforts and educational programs in the field of wildlife studies.

Kobulov is a masculine surname, its feminine counterpart is Kobulova. Notable people with the surname include: Amayak Kobulov (1906–1955), Soviet politician Bogdan Kobulov (1904–1953), Soviet politician, brother of Amayak

Kobulov is a surname of Uzbek origin, commonly associated with notable individuals in Central Asia. One prominent figure bearing this name is Zulfiya Kobulova, a distinguished poet and writer known for her contributions to Uzbek literature. The term "Kobulov" may also refer to various locations or institutions in Uzbekistan, and its cultural significance is linked to the historical and artistic heritage of the region. The Kobulov family lineage may have connections to various historical events, individuals, and cultural traditions within Uzbekistan and neighboring areas.

The GCR Class 1 was a class of steam locomotives designed by John G. Robinson for the Great Central Railway, and introduced to service between December 1912 and 1913. In the 1923 grouping, they all passed to the London and North Eastern Railway which placed them in class B2. Their classification was changed to B19 in 1945, and all had been retired by the end of 1947.

The GCR Class 1, also known as the "Large Wheeled" locomotives, was a class of steam locomotive designed by the Great Central Railway (GCR) in the early 20th century. Built between 1903 and 1911, the Class 1 locomotives were primarily used for passenger services on the GCR network. Characterized by their 4-4-2 wheel arrangement, these locomotives featured large driving wheels which provided high speeds and efficient performance. The Class 1 was designed by the noted engineer Edward Thompson and later had a significant influence on the development of British locomotive design. A total of 40 units were constructed, and they were widely regarded for their reliability and speed. The Class 1 was gradually phased out in the 1950s as diesel locomotives became more prevalent. While none survive in operational condition today, their legacy remains significant in the history of British railways.

The Gorges du Tarn (Occitan: Gòrjas de Tarn) is a canyon formed by the Tarn (river) between the Causse Méjean and the Causse de Sauveterre, in southern France. The canyon, mainly located in the Lozère département, and partially in the Aveyron département, is about 53 kilometres (33 mi)-long (from the village of Quézac to Le Rozier, from 44°22′30″N 3°31′15″E to 44°11′36″N 3°12′20″E) and 400 m to 600 m deep.

The Gorges du Tarn is a stunning river canyon located in southern France, primarily within the Lozère department in the Occitanie region. Carved by the Tarn River, the gorge spans approximately 53 kilometers and features dramatic cliffs, deep valleys, and picturesque villages such as Sainte-Enimie and La Malène. The area is renowned for its natural beauty, making it a popular destination for outdoor activities such as hiking, kayaking, and rock climbing. The Gorges du Tarn is part of the Cévennes National Park, which is recognized for its biodiversity and geological significance. The region also boasts several UNESCO World Heritage Sites, highlighting its cultural and historical importance.

Geoffrey Baumann is an American visual effects artist. He was nominated for an Academy Award in the category Best Visual Effects for the film Black Panther: Wakanda Forever. At the 72nd British Academy Film Awards, he won a BAFTA Award for Best Special Visual Effects for the film Black Panther. His win was shared with Jesse James Chisholm, Craig Hammack and Dan Sudick.

Geoffrey Baumann is an American author and historian known for his works on American history and culture. He has contributed to various historical journals and publications, focusing on topics such as early American society, colonial history, and the evolution of cultural practices in the United States. Baumann's research often emphasizes the interconnectedness of history and contemporary social issues, making his works relevant for both academic and general audiences. His contributions to the field have been recognized through various awards and speaking engagements at historical forums.

Andrew James Lock OAM (born 26 December 1961) is an Australian mountaineer. He became the first, and still remains the only, Australian to climb all 14 "eight-thousanders" (the peaks over 8,000-metres above sea level) on 2 October 2009, and is the 18th person to ever complete this feat. He climbed 13 of the 14 without bottled oxygen, only using it on Mount Everest, which he has summited three times. He retired from eight-thousander climbing in 2012.

Andrew Lock is an Australian mountaineer and adventurer known for his significant achievements in high-altitude climbing. He gained international recognition for being one of the few climbers to successfully summit all 14 of the world's 8,000-meter peaks without supplemental oxygen, achieving this feat in 2007. Lock began his climbing career in the 1990s and quickly established himself in the global climbing community. He is also noted for his contributions to the sport through motivational speaking and writing about his experiences in high-altitude expeditions. In addition to his mountaineering, Lock has been involved in outdoor education and has actively promoted outdoor safety practices.

The Barrhead Branch was a branch line built by the Glasgow and South Western Railway in Scotland. It connected Potterhill railway station on the south side of Paisley with a new Barrhead Central railway station. The line was sometimes known as the Barrhead Central Railway. It was made in reaction to competition for local passenger traffic, and for a time a circular service operated from Glasgow via Paisley and Barrhead. However income from the line was disappointing, and the passenger service was cut back, finally closing in 1917. As a goods-only route it remained open until 1970. None of the line is open now.

The Barrhead Branch is a railway line in Scotland that connects the towns of Barrhead and Neilston in East Renfrewshire. Originally part of the Glasgow and South Western Railway, it was opened in stages between 1874 and 1910 to facilitate transportation and commerce in the region. The line serves predominantly passenger traffic, providing access to Glasgow and surrounding areas. The Barrhead Branch features several intermediate stations and has undergone various upgrades over the years to improve service efficiency. It remains a vital part of the local transport network, promoting connectivity and supporting regional economic activity.

The 2023 Syed Modi International (officially known as the Syed Modi India International 2023) was a badminton tournament that took place at Babu Banarasi Das Indoor Stadium in Lucknow, India, from 28 November to 3 December 2023 and had a total prize of $210,000.

The 2023 Syed Modi International is a badminton tournament that took place in Lucknow, India, from January 24 to January 29, 2023. It is part of the BWF World Tour and was organized by the Badminton Association of India. The tournament featured various categories including men's singles, women's singles, men's doubles, women's doubles, and mixed doubles. As a significant event in the badminton calendar, it attracted participants from multiple countries and contributed to the global rankings in the sport. The event was named in honor of Syed Modi, a notable Indian badminton player.

"Vande Mātaram" is a poem that was adopted as the national song of the Republic of India in 1950. It was written in Sanskritised Bengali by Bankim Chandra Chatterjee in the 1870s, and was first published in 1882 as part of Chatterjee's Bengali novel Anandmath. The poem is an ode to the motherland, it was officially adopted by the Indian National Congress in 1905 to serve as a pan-Indian, nationalist salutation to Mother India during the Indian independence movement, culminating in its 1950 designation as the National Song of the Republic of India, sharing equal honour with India's National Anthem. The first two verses of the song make abstract reference to the "mother" and "motherland", without any religious connotation. However, later verses mention Hindu goddesses such as Durga. The references to Hindu gods became a point of contention in 1937, after which the Indian National Congress, on suggestion of Mahatma Gandhi and Rabindranath Tagore, adopted the two stanzas of the song. Unlike the national anthem, there are no rules or decorum to be observed when reciting Vande Mataram.

"Vande Mataram" is a renowned Bengali poem composed by Bankim Chandra Chatterjee in 1870, later adapted into a song. It serves as a significant cultural and political symbol in India, especially during the Indian independence movement, representing nationalistic sentiment. The poem expresses reverence for the motherland, personified as a goddess, and illustrates the beauty and richness of India. It is featured in Chatterjee's novel "Anandamath" and was later set to music by Rabindranath Tagore. "Vande Mataram" was adopted as the national song of India, reflecting its importance in India's struggle for freedom and its ongoing resonance in Indian culture and identity.

Aleh Slautsin (Belarusian: Алег Слауцін; Russian: Олег Слаутин; born 21 April 1986) is a Ukrainian and Belarusian former professional football, beach soccer and futsal player. He was a member of Belarus national beach soccer team.

Aleh Slautin is a Belarusian-American mathematician known for his contributions to number theory and algebra. He earned his Ph.D. from the University of Washington in 1998, focusing on algebraic structures and their applications. Slautin has held academic positions at various institutions, where he has published numerous articles in peer-reviewed journals. His research interests include modular forms, arithmetic geometry, and the interplay between number theory and cryptography. In addition to his academic work, Slautin is active in mathematics education and outreach, promoting mathematical literacy and engagement among students and the broader community.

Gabriel J. Alberro (born March 31, 1976) is an American underground artist, writer, songwriter, and filmmaker. He was born in Miami, Florida, and began his indie career writing short stories and poems and making his own comic books. Alberro served as writer, artist and vocalist of the classic rock hip hop band UBNAWKSHIS (sounded Obnoxious) in its various incarnations from 2000 to 2010.

Gabe Alberro is an American television writer and producer known for his work in the entertainment industry. He gained prominence for his contributions to various television series, showcasing a talent for storytelling and character development. Alberro's writing is characterized by its wit and emotional depth, often exploring themes of personal growth and interpersonal relationships. His career includes significant collaborations with well-known networks and production companies, contributing to both scripted and unscripted content. Alberro has garnered recognition within the industry for his work, earning accolades and a dedicated following among audiences.

Frank Kitchen (c. 1931 – December 1992) was an English professional rugby league footballer who played in the 1950s as a wing. He played at club level for Leigh, and at representative level for Great Britain, where he was part of the 1954 Rugby League World Cup winning team.

Frank Kitchen is a notable American entrepreneur, author, and public speaker, recognized for his work in personal development and career coaching. He gained prominence for promoting resilience and leadership principles through workshops and motivational speaking engagements. Kitchen has authored multiple books that focus on transforming adversity into success and is known for his engaging storytelling style. He has also contributed to various media outlets, sharing insights on business strategy and personal growth. Through his initiatives, Kitchen aims to inspire individuals and organizations to overcome challenges and achieve their goals.

The Decembrist revolt (Russian: Восстание декабристов, romanized: Vosstaniye dekabristov, lit. 'Uprising of the Decembrists') was a failed coup d'état led by liberal military and political dissidents against the Russian Empire. It took place in Saint Petersburg on 26 December [O.S. 14 December] 1825, following the death of Emperor Alexander I. Alexander's brother and heir-presumptive Grand Duke Konstantin Pavlovich privately renounced his claim to the throne two years prior to Alexander's sudden death on 1 December [O.S. 19 November] 1825. The next in the line of succession therefore was younger brother Nicholas, who would ascend to the throne as Emperor Nicholas I. Neither the Russian government nor the general public were initially aware of Konstantin's renunciation, and as a result, parts of the military took a premature oath of loyalty to Konstantin. A general swearing of loyalty to the true emperor Nicholas was scheduled for 26 December [O.S. 14 December] 1825 in Senate Square, Saint Petersburg. In the midst of this confusing transition into Nicholas' reign, the Northern Society, a secret society of liberal revolutionaries, nobles, and military officials, organized a conspiracy to replace the Russian Empire's autocratic regime with a constitutional monarchy. To seize control of the government and implement a regime change, it sought to convince the military that Nicholas was usurping the throne from Konstantin. On 26 December, Northern Society members led a force of approximately 3,000 troops into Senate Square to prevent the loyalty-swearing ceremony and to rally additional soldiers and officers to their cause. This group of rebels, although disorganized due to indecision and dissension among its leaders, confronted troops loyal to Nicholas outside the Senate building in the presence of a large civilian crowd. A standoff ensued, during which Nicholas' envoy, Mikhail Miloradovich, was assassinated. The loyalists eventually opened fire with heavy artillery, scattering the rebels. In the aftermath of the coup attempt, five rebellion leaders were sentenced to hanging; many other participants were imprisoned, or exiled to Siberia. The individuals who participated in the conspiracy and coup attempt became known as the Decembrists (Russian: декабристы, romanized: dekabristy).

The Decembrist revolt was a failed uprising that took place in Imperial Russia on December 14, 1825. It was led by a group of army officers and noble revolutionaries who sought to challenge the autocracy of Tsar Nicholas I and promote constitutional reform. The movement was characterized by its liberal ideals, advocating for civil liberties, the abolition of serfdom, and the establishment of a constitutional monarchy. The revolt was prompted by the confusion over succession following the death of Tsar Alexander I and the desire for political change among the Decembrists. The uprising was suppressed by loyalist troops, resulting in the execution of several leaders and the exile of many participants to Siberia. The Decembrist revolt is often viewed as a precursor to later revolutionary movements in Russia and remains a symbol of the struggle for reform and democracy in the country.

This is a bibliography of U.S. congressional memoirs by former and current U.S. representatives. The United States House of Representatives is one of the two houses of the United States Congress, the bicameral legislature which also includes the Senate. The composition and powers of the House are established in Article One of the Constitution. The major power of the House is to pass federal legislation that affects the entire country, although its bills must also be passed by the Senate and further agreed to by the president before becoming law (unless both the House and Senate re-pass the legislation with a two-thirds majority in each chamber). Each state receives representation in the House in proportion to its population but is entitled to at least one representative. The total number of voting representatives is fixed by law at 435. Each representative serves for a two-year term. The Speaker of the United States House of Representatives, traditionally the leader of the majority party, is the presiding officer of the chamber, elected by the members of the House.

The U.S. representative bibliography, particularly focusing on congressional memoirs, comprises a collection of autobiographical accounts written by members of the United States Congress. These memoirs provide insights into the personal experiences, legislative processes, and political environments faced by legislators during their time in office. The works vary in style and substance, often reflecting the authors' perspectives on significant political events, their motivations for public service, and their roles within the broader historical context of American governance. Many congressional memoirs are published as books or articles, contributing to the understanding of U.S. political history and the workings of the legislative branch. Notable figures in this genre include former presidents, senators, and representatives who have documented their journeys, challenges, and contributions to national policy.
The Curtis Counce Group (later released as Landslide) is an album by American jazz bassist Curtis Counce recorded in 1956 and released on the Contemporary label.

The Curtis Counce Group was a prominent American jazz ensemble formed in the early 1950s, led by bassist Curtis Counce. Based in Los Angeles, the group was known for its innovative blend of hard bop and cool jazz styles, featuring talented musicians such as tenor saxophonist Harold Land, trumpeter Jack Sheldon, and pianist Elmo Hope. The Curtis Counce Group gained recognition for its sophisticated arrangements and improvisational skills, releasing several albums that contributed to the West Coast jazz movement. Their work was characterized by a focus on ensemble playing and intricate harmonies, influencing future generations of jazz artists. The group disbanded in the mid-1950s, but its recordings continue to be celebrated for their artistic significance in the jazz genre.
